However, my original intention was to unite inside and outside, to be widely favored and honored, to bring glory to our kind. Now that we have become enemies, why should I pretend to be clean and honest, not wanting to flatter others to gain something? What I received from you, I wish to return it to your wife. My task is complete." In the blink of an eye, he turned back into a scholar and had intercourse with Zhang. Zhang could only accept it passively. The scholar was also extremely tired, glancing at his face. The couple looked at each other, each with a look of shame.

    After a long time, the scholar put on his clothes and got off the bed, clapped his hands and laughed loudly, raised his hand in a high bow and said, "I'm leaving!" He turned into a wild fox, leaped out, and never came again.

    Originally, the scholar did not return from a clear and bright journey. The next day, the scholar visited his home. Zhang saw him and was pleased, and after seeing him three times, they became intimate. Neighbors all heard and found it shameful, but the scholar did not know about it. Until then, the fox revealed the truth.

    44

    非非子曰：子舆氏有言：“杀人之父者，人亦杀其父。杀人之兄者，人亦杀其兄。”当生之如河上也，邂逅粲者，得遂其媾，将赋《蔓草》焉。岂知振万于其宫者，即此抱衾于其室者哉一身两役，报亦巧矣。嗟乎！依古以来，定娄猪猪于闺中，活秦宫于花里而曾不顾者，又何多也！
    　　梦中宾主
    　　客有以事造主人言者，值主人有他故、末遽出。客坐厅事待，久之，偶倦而伏几。主人既出，见客睡正酣，不欲惊之，乃亦伏几。少顷客醒，见主人于旁鼻声鼾然，因复睡而不相呼。顷之，主人觉，而客睡如故，乃亦复睡。比客再醒，日巳昃矣，恐主人觉而延之，趁门而归。既而主人见客去，亦趋入内。
    　　宾主竟日相对，未晤一面，未交一言。闻者谓之“梦中宾主”。
    　　西村颜常
    　　谚云：“钱有眼，毂有鼻，飞来飞去无定地。”盖至言也。

    Explanation

    44

    Feifeizi said: Ziyu once said, "He who kills another's father, his own father will also be killed. He who kills another's brother, his own brother will also be killed." When he was born on the riverbank, he chanced upon a beautiful person, consummated his union, and intended to compose a poem called "Wancao" (Mossy Grass). How could he know that the one who is active in his palace is the same as the one who holds the quilt in his room? One person in two roles, the retribution is also ingenious. Alas! Since ancient times, how many women have settled down in the inner chambers and lived in the Qin palace amidst flowers, yet remained unaware of their dual lives?

    Dream Guests and Hosts

    A guest came to speak to the host about something. The host had other matters and did not immediately come out. The guest sat in the hall waiting, and after a while, he became tired and leaned on the desk. The host came out and saw the guest sleeping soundly, not wanting to wake him, he also leaned on the desk. After a while, the guest woke up and saw the host beside him snoring, so he fell asleep again without calling out. After a while, the host woke up, and the guest was still asleep, so he also fell asleep again. By the time the guest woke up again, the sun had already moved to the west. Afraid that the host would wake up and invite him, he left through the door. Afterward, when the host saw the guest leave, he also hurried inside. The guest and host faced each other all day without meeting face-to-face or speaking a word. Those who heard about it called it "guest and host in a dream."

    Proverb of the West Village

    A proverb says, "Money has eyes, and the axle has a nose, flying around without a fixed place." It is indeed a profound saying.

    45

    尝有一贫儿，年二十馀，落拓不事产业。一日，有青衣人导一白衣人至其家，谓之曰：“某等为人所幽闭，幸得脱，今来就君。数日内黄兄亦至矣。”径入其内室。贫儿颇骇，促视之，寂无所有。见地上有物积甚厚，皆青钱、白金也，乃悟为银钱之神。数日后，又有黄衣者造其宅而灭。得黄金百镒，遂暴富。
    　　心念财神为幽闭而来，必乐为人用，于是聘妻买妾，造华屋，营田产。奴仆充庭，宾客踵座。出则肩舆驷马，炫赫道路，俨然如长官贵族焉。豪富甲乡里，奢侈闻都邑。其子暴殄尤甚于父。或搥金为簿，以大旗数十卷之。俟风起，命仆飏飐，灿烂满空，日以为常。或以绮罗席地，厚数寸，令婢妾数十裸卧于地，以金珠贵重之物迢掷之，约：中其私处者即与狎。又或以珍珠瑟瑟数升撒地上，令裸相抢夺，而观其颠仆，以为笑乐。其他淫侈亵越之事，不可枚举，而一饮千绢，一食万钱，又不足道也。

    Explanation

    45

    Once upon a time, there was a poor young man in his twenties who was carefree and did not engage in productive work or business. One day, a person dressed in green led another person dressed in white to his home and said to him, "We were imprisoned by someone, but luckily we managed to escape. We have come to you. In a few days, a person named Huang will also arrive." They directly entered his inner room. The poor man was greatly startled and hurriedly looked around, but saw nothing. He noticed that the ground was piled high with objects, all of which were green coins and white gold. He then realized that they were the gods of silver and money. A few days later, another person dressed in yellow arrived at his house and disappeared. He found a hundred catties of gold and suddenly became very wealthy.

    Thinking to himself that the gods of wealth had come to him after being imprisoned, he believed they would be pleased to be used by him. He then married a wife and bought concubines, built magnificent houses, and acquired land and property. His courtyard was filled with servants, and guests followed each other to sit. When he went out, he rode in a sedan chair pulled by four horses, showing off his wealth and status, appearing like a high-ranking official or noble. He became the wealthiest in his village, and his luxurious living became known in the city.

    His son, however, was even more reckless and wasteful than his father. Sometimes, he would hammer gold into a ledger and roll up dozens of large flags, waiting for the wind to rise before ordering his servants to wave them, making them shine brightly across the sky. This became a daily routine. Other times, he would lay luxurious silk and brocade on the ground, several inches thick, and order dozens of concubines to lie naked on the ground. He would throw gold and precious jewels at them, allowing those who hit their private parts to have intimate relations with him. Sometimes, he would scatter a handful of pearls and jade on the ground, ordering the concubines to fight over them nakedly, watching them tumble and fall, finding amusement in their misfortune. There were many other lewd, extravagant, and indecent acts that could not be listed, but drinking a thousand pieces of silk in one go and eating a meal worth ten thousand coins were also not worth mentioning.

    46

    一日，其父出游，见道上粪中有毂数粒，忽瞿然曰：“积农人三时之勤，为人生日食之需者，奈何令弃污秽中？”即命仆拾取，以水涤之。
    　　归至家，其于迎谓曰：“午晌时，有数人衣服鲜楚，成队自室中出，语我曰：“尔家逐我。今去至西村颜常家。”遂冉冉出户。视室中财物，尽亡矣。复见黄蚊亿万出仓中，顷刻蔽空，望西而去，而仓中无粒榖存矣。”父子跌足懊叹，其家顿贫。

    Explanation

    46

    One day, the father went out for a walk and saw some grains of rice in the excrement on the road. Suddenly startled, he said, "The labor of farmers during three seasons, which is essential for the daily sustenance of people, how can it be allowed to be discarded in filth?" He immediately ordered his servant to pick it up and wash it with water.

    On returning home, his son greeted him and said, "At noon, several people dressed in bright and neat clothes came out in a group from the room and told me, 'Your family drove us away. Now they have gone to the house of Yan Chang in the western village.' They gradually left the house. Looking at the valuables in the room, they were all gone. Then I saw billions of yellow mosquitoes coming out of the granary. In an instant, they filled the sky, flying westward, but there was not a single grain of rice left in the granary." The father and son stamped their feet and sighed in regret. Their family suddenly became poor.

    47

    数年间，田产鬻尽，死亡相继，其子竟以饥寒委沟壑。盖华侈素习，不能复以勤俭持其后，天祸又从而施之。以至于斯也！
    　　其父复梦人告曰：“我榖神也，感尔昔日秽中相救，念之不忘，当以尔身之食给尔。”明日，乃有黄蚊亿万飞来其家，尽化为榖，食尽复来，至死乃已。
    　　呜呼！天地生财，本为人用，必撙节流通而后不弊。近世窖镪之徒，至于父借耕锄，母取箕帚，犹不肯与。其贪吝之意，若取钱神而囚之。钱神岂可囚哉而纨袴祷膏粱之子弟，承袭馀荫，腴田万顷，广厦千楹，至于举手一掷，辄费中人十家之产，是钱神既来，而斧钺逐之。钱神又岂可逐哉夫囚钱者不智。逐钱者不仁。不仁不智，直不可以为人，区区用财之道，又无足论矣。
    　　上床鬼
    　　吾乡有夫妇谇语者，夫怒而出。
    　　其夜妇独寝，半掩其扉。烛既息，有人入室，若特上床寝者。以为其夫归，亦并不知问，但敛身向内，虚其外以待之。

    Explanation

    47

    Over several years, all the land and property were sold out, and death followed one after another. His son eventually died of hunger and cold, leaving his body in the ditches. This was due to their long-standing habit of extravagance, which they could not abandon even in their later years, and divine retribution came upon them. Until it reached this point!

    His father then dreamed someone told him, "I am the god of grain. I am grateful for your past help in a filthy place, and I cannot forget it. I will provide food for you with your own body." The next day, countless yellow mosquitoes flew into his house, all transforming into grain. After being eaten, they came again, until death.

    Alas! The wealth created by heaven and earth is originally for human use, and it must be saved and circulated to avoid waste. In recent times, those who hoard money go so far as to borrow plows and hoes from their fathers and take brooms and shovels from their mothers, yet they still refuse to give. Their greedy and stingy intention is like capturing the money god and imprisoning it. How can the money god be imprisoned? Yet the wealthy and pampered children who inherit the legacy of their ancestors, with thousands of acres of fertile land and thousands of rooms in large houses, spend the wealth of ten middle-class families with a casual gesture. The money god comes, but is driven away with axes and halberds. How can the money god be driven away? Those who imprison money are unwise, and those who drive it away are unrighteous. Unrighteous and unwise, one cannot be a person. The way of using wealth is so trivial that it is not worth discussing.

    The Ghost Who Climbs into Bed
    In my hometown, there were a couple who quarreled, and the husband became angry and left. That night, the wife slept alone, half-closing the door. After the candle went out, someone entered the room, as if they were just going to bed. Thinking it was her husband returning, she did not ask. She just huddled inward, leaving the outside empty to wait for him.

    48

    既而其人上床寝，绝无声息，若不似其夫平昔者，妇不忍，因诘之曰：“君夜归，悄不发语，得毋犹有憾于日中耶闺阁口语何常，乃不宏如此！”亦绝不应诺。
    　　妇乃以身相偎傍，以手扪其臂，则体冷如冰而软如絮，乃知其鬼而非人也，大惊呼救。同舍者急持灯来，其物乃徐滚下床，色黑而形肥，似人似兽，扑簌一声，寂无所见。
    　　其夫方寄宿他所，驰召以归。群谓夫妇不和，故鬼物乘其隙也。自是伉俪有加。
    　　红裳女子
    　　常德有士人，客滇中归。一仆负担以从。一日向夕，不及旅店，过一小村，向村人假宿。村人曰：“此地他无馆舍，惟一古庙，然紊有妖怪杀人，不敢宿客。”士入度日暮途远，不得已，乃曰：“吾不畏。”乞以一几一灯，为达旦之备。村人与之。士人入庙，下榻一室，命仆宿其耳房。因张灯读书，并取行箧中朱笔砚，陈于几。澄心息虑，以待其变。

    Explanation

    48

    Afterward, the man went to bed, utterly silent, as if it were not like his husband usually was. The wife, unable to bear it, questioned him, saying, "You return at night, not speaking a word. Is it not that you still harbor resentment from noon? What kind of words are usually spoken in the women's quarters, to be so restrained?" He also did not respond.

    The wife then leaned against him with her body, and touched his arm with her hand, finding that his body was as cold as ice and as soft as cotton. She realized that he was a ghost and not a human, and greatly alarmed, she cried for help. The fellow lodger quickly brought a lamp. The ghost slowly rolled off the bed, black in color and fat in shape, resembling a human or a beast. With a rustling sound, it disappeared, and nothing could be seen.

    His husband was staying elsewhere, and was hurriedly called back. The crowd said the couple was not harmonious, and therefore the ghost took advantage of the rift. From then on, the couple became more harmonious.

    The Woman in Red Clothing

    A scholar from Changde returned from a trip to Yunnan. A servant carrying a burden followed him. One evening, he did not reach the inn and passed through a small village, asking the villagers for lodging. The villagers said, "There are no other inns here, only an ancient temple, but it is said to have ghosts that kill people, and they dare not host guests." The scholar considered the late hour and the long journey, unwillingly, and said, "I am not afraid." He asked for a table and a lamp, to prepare for the night. The villagers agreed. The scholar entered the temple, settled in a room, and ordered the servant to stay in the side room. He lit the lamp and began to read, also taking out the red ink and brush from his travel bag and arranging them on the table. He calmed his mind and ceased thoughts, to await any changes.

    49

    二更之后，仆已熟睡。有红裳女子，年可十八九，婀娜而来，顾之而笑。士人揣知妖魅，殊不顾。女乃延伫而歌曰：
    　　“昔伴笙歌队，今居土木旁。铜丸埋汉殿，谁是定陶王”
    　　低昂断续，音节颇妙。既毕，笑曰：“郎识之乎”士人答言不识。因复少近，曰：“更有新歇，敢献于君子乎”士人曰：“第歌之。”女乃拂袖搴裳，料眸欹足，缓声而歌。柔曼缠绵，夭媚百出。其一章曰：
    　　白月尘生暗铁星，漆床孤卧蛮冥冥。都昙答腊无消息，肠断花奴空泪零。
    　　二章云：
    　　闻道萧郎爱细腰，齐娘薛姊颤声娇。
    　　自怜不及双飞鹭，犹伴行人宿丽谯。
    　　歇罢，立近几旁，含情欲发。士人取笔濡朱，戏书其颊。女大惊，失声而走，遂不复至。
    　　次日，以状告村人。令穷其迹。遍索庙中。见殿角一败鼓朱书宛然，遂破之，得血数升及人骨若干。魅遂绝。

    Explanation

    49

    After the second watch, the servant had already fallen into a deep sleep. A woman in a red dress, about eighteen or nineteen years old, approached gracefully and smiled at him. The scholar suspected that she was a demon and paid no heed. The woman then lingered and sang:

    "Once I was part of a troupe of musicians, now I dwell beside a mound of earth and wood. A copper ball was buried in the Han palace, who was the King of Dingtao?"

    Her melody rose and fell, intermittent and beautifully rhythmic. After finishing, she smiled and asked, "Do you recognize this?" The scholar replied that he did not. She then moved a little closer and said, "I have another new song. Dare I offer it to the gentleman?" The scholar said, "Just sing it." The woman then waved her sleeves, lifted her skirt, tilted her gaze, and leaned her feet, singing softly. Her voice was soft, gentle, and lingering, full of charm and beauty. The first verse went:

    "In the moonlight, dust rises from the dark iron star. Alone on a black bed, in a dark and mysterious place. No news from the distant lands of Duta and Dalaba, the heartbroken flower slave sheds tears in vain."

    The second verse went:

    "Hearing that Xiao Lang loves a slender waist, the Qin and Xue sisters with trembling and charming voices. Feeling sorry for herself, not as good as the pair of flying herons, still accompanying travelers to stay in the beautiful tower."

    After finishing, she stood near the table, holding her feelings, about to speak. The scholar took a brush and dipped it in red ink, playfully writing on her cheek. The woman was greatly surprised, screamed, and ran away, never to return again.

    The next day, he reported the incident to the villagers. They ordered an investigation into her traces. They searched the temple thoroughly and found a broken drum at the corner of the hall with red writing clearly visible. They broke it open and found several liters of blood and some human bones. The demon was finally eliminated.

    50

    牛豕瘟鬼
    　　余年十六读书涂坊村，距家三里许。师松岩先生，族叔也。
    　　族祖某招之饮酒，席散已二更矣。时孟秋既望，月明如洗。
    　　先生爱其凉景，因独步来塾。遥见田畔一黑团，如气球而大，以为荆丛。行渐近，隔丈许，觉其物左右转动，促视之，遂旋滚入林箐中而灭。先生至塾，为诸人述其状，莫知何物也。
    　　数日后，闻附林小村牛豕瘟死殆尽，得非此物为之欤
    　　雪媒
    　　康熙己丑冬，祟仁有两姓同日娶妇者。一富室贾姓，一士族谢姓。新妇一姓王，名翠芳，一姓吴。吴贫而王富。两家香车遇于陌上。时彤云布空，飞霰如掌，郊原溪谷之间一望皎然，几不辨途径。车上各饰彩缯，覆以油幕，积雪封之一二寸，绚烂略相似。同行二三里，共憩于野亭。舆夫媵仆辈体寒欲僵，共拾枯薪，薰火亭中。久之而雪愈甚，恐日暮途远，各拥香车分道而去。

    Explanation

    50

    Ghost of Cattle and Pig Plague

    When I was sixteen, I was studying in the village of Tu Fang, which is about three li from my home. My teacher was Mr. Song Yan, my cousin. One of my clan elders invited him to drink, and the banquet ended around the second watch of the night. It was the 15th day of the first month of the lunar calendar, and the moon was as bright as washed silver.

    Mr. Song Yan enjoyed the cool scenery and walked alone to the school. Far away, he saw a black mass in the field that looked like a large balloon and mistook it for a thicket. As he approached, about ten paces away, he noticed the object turning left and right. He hurriedly looked closer and saw it spin and roll into the dense forest, where it disappeared. When Mr. Song Yan arrived at the school, he described what he had seen to everyone, but no one knew what it was.

    A few days later, he heard that a small village near the forest had almost all its cattle and pigs die of the plague. Could this have been the work of that thing?

    Snow Matchmaker

    In the winter of 1679 during the reign of Emperor Kangxi, two families in Sui Ren county married their daughters on the same day. One was a wealthy merchant family named Jia, and the other was a scholarly family named Xie. The new brides were both named Wang Cuifang and Wu. Wu was from a poor family, while Wang was from a rich one. Their perfumed carriages met on the road. At the time, red clouds covered the sky, and snowflakes fell like hands. The fields, streams, and valleys were all clear and bright, almost indistinguishable. The carriages were decorated with colorful silk and covered with oil cloths, with snow accumulating an inch or two, creating a similar yet splendid scene. They traveled together for two or three li before stopping at a pavilion in the wilderness. The servants and attendants were so cold that they were almost frozen, so they gathered dry wood and lit a fire in the pavilion. After a while, the snow became even heavier, and they were afraid it would get dark and the road would be long, so they each took their perfumed carriages and went their separate ways.

    51

    是夜，翠芳将寝，环视室内，奁具甚薄，且非己物，疑婿家质而易之。怪叹不能忍，乃问婿：“吾紫檀镜台安在可令婢将来，为我卸妆也。”婿笑曰：“卿家未有此物来，今从何处觅？”翠芳曰：“贾郎何必相诳。”婿又笑曰：“吾真郎，非假郎也。”翠芳曰：“谓郎姓贾耳。”婿曰：“某姓谢。”翠芳闻言大骇，乃啼呼“贼徒卖我”。婿大惊，不知所措。家人尽集问故，翠芳唯啼呼不止。谢母怒叱曰：“家本儒素，谁会作贼汝父母厌我贫薄，教汝作此伎俩耶谁能畏汝”翠芳曰：“吾闻汝家本姓贾，今姓谢，何也”母曰：“拙婢岂有临婚而易姓者乎然则汝家亦不姓吴乎”翠芳悟曰：“我知之矣，汝妇自姓吴，吾自姓王。吾来时，途次遇一嫁娘，同避雪亭下。微闻旁人言此妇吴氏，其婿家吾亦闻之，不能记忆，殆汝家妇也。而吾乃贾氏之妇。雪甚寒极，两家车从仓卒而行，其必两误而互易之矣。速使人觇于贾氏，当得其故。”

    Explanation

    51

    That night, Cui Fang was about to sleep and surveyed the room. The trousseau was very simple, and not her own possessions, leading her to suspect that her husband's family had pawned and replaced them. Unable to bear her frustration, she asked her husband, "Where is my rosewood mirror stand? Can you have the maid bring it to help me take off my makeup?" Her husband laughed and said, "Your family didn't have this item. Where could we find it now?" Cui Fang replied, "Why would you lie to me, Mr. Jia?" Her husband laughed again and said, "I am the real husband, not a fake one." Cui Fang said, "I mean the husband's surname is Jia." Her husband said, "My surname is Xie." Upon hearing this, Cui Fang was greatly startled and cried out, "Thieves have sold me!" Her husband was greatly surprised and at a loss. All the family members gathered to ask what happened, but Cui Fang continued to cry and call out. Mother Xie angrily scolded and said, "Our family is originally scholarly and modest. Who would do such a thing? Your parents are dissatisfied with my poverty and simplicity. Are they teaching you to do such tricks? Who would be afraid of you?" Cui Fang said, "I heard that your family originally had the surname Jia. Now you have the surname Xie. Why is that?" Mother Xie said, "How could a simple servant change her surname on the eve of marriage? Then does your family not have the surname Wu?" Cui Fang understood and said, "I understand now. Your wife originally had the surname Wu, and I originally had the surname Wang. When I came, I met a bride on the road, and we took shelter together under a snow pavilion. I faintly heard someone say that this woman was from the Wu family, and I also heard about her husband's family. I can't remember, but it must be your wife. And I am the wife of the Jia family. The snow was extremely cold, and the two families' carriages hurriedly left. It must be that both sides were mistaken and exchanged each other. Quickly send someone to check with the Jia family, and we will find out the reason."

    52

    众咸以为然。而贾氏相距三十里，使者明日乃达，则延陵季女已共贾大夫射雉如皋矣。盖吴女凝视妆奁，略闻姓氏，亦颇知有误，而心艳其富，姑冒昧以从之。至是知之，徉为怨怒而盆水之覆，已不可收。即贾氏之子，亦不欲其别抱琵琶也。使者反报，翠芳欲自尽。或劝之曰：“王谢之婚，本由天定。殆姻缘簿上偶尔错注，合有此颠倒。今贾氏已婚于吴，则阿卿自宜归谢，尚何负哉”翠芳不可。谢氏乃驰介诣王公，告以故。王公深异曰：“非偶然也。”即遣媒者来告：“愿为秦晋。”翠芳以父母之命，乃始拜见姑嫜，同牢合卺，成夫妇之礼。

    Explanation

    52

    Everyone agreed. However, the Jia family was thirty li away, and the messenger would only arrive the next day. By then, the daughter of the Yancheng family had already married the Jia doctor at Rugao. The girl from Wu was looking intently at her makeup box, heard the surname briefly, and also knew there was a mistake. She was attracted by his wealth and temporarily went along with it. By then she knew, and pretended to be angry, but the overturned water pot could not be undone. Even the son of the Jia family did not want her to marry someone else. The messenger returned to report, and Cui Fang wanted to commit suicide. Someone advised her saying, "The marriage of the Wang and Xie families was destined by heaven. Perhaps there was a mistake in the marriage register, that's why this happened. Now the Jia family has married someone from Wu, then your ladyship should marry into the Xie family. What's there to regret?" Cui Fang refused. The Xie family then sent a messenger to the Wang family, to tell them the reason. The Wang family was very surprised and said, "It's not accidental." They immediately sent a matchmaker to propose, "To become in-laws." Cui Fang, following her parents' command, then began to meet her in-laws, shared a meal and drank from the same cup, and completed the wedding ceremony.

    53

    厥后贾氏陵替，吴女愤恚而卒，谢氏子补诸生，终身伉俪，儿女成行，而翠芳以顺妇称焉。
    　　是事也，时人谓之雪媒。
    　　非非子曰：余观于画屏红叶之事，未尝不叹，曰：巧哉天道，不意幻化滕六，直解作冰人也。夫男女之道，纳果为定，直于亲迎之日而交臂易之，可不谓奇妙者乎！然君子于此觇世态矣。
    　　英巨山神
    　　金溪喻公步高晓堂先生，幼孤，为人慵牧。饭牛于野，失足坠深渊，人无知者。闻下有人云：“此封君也。”乃以版承其足，捧之出水，则牛方垂尾岸下，遂攀而上。后经商景德镇，腰金以归，年八十馀卒。长君南屏先生大任，领乾隆庚子乡荐。封君之说，当有验矣。

    Explanation

    53

    After the Jia family's decline, the Wu woman died of anger, and the Xie family's son took the place of a student. The couple lived as a lifelong married couple, with grown children, and Cui Fang was known for her obedience.

    This event was called a "snow agent" by the people of the time.

    The commentator, Non-Nonzi, said: "I have never ceased to marvel at the story of the painted screen and red leaves, saying: How ingenious is the way of heaven! Unexpect

    54

    卒后数年，其戚属徐氏为扶鸾之戏。乩书曰：“英巨山神至。”英巨山者，金溪北境之名山。山之阴，即徐氏居也。因叩神姓名，乩书“喻步高”三字。徐氏惊曰：“公得毋即吾姻乎”乩曰：“然。”
    　　时公次孙云圃在侧，问：“识之否”乩曰：“吾孙耳，乌得不识”云圃喜而跽请曰：“祖何以得主兹山”乩曰：“冥王谓我无欺，故膺此封典。”云圃曰：“祖既神矣，必知休咎。子孙科名何如”乩曰：“尔但读书，自可致功名。何问为”既而题诗一首，复自书曰：“生平未尝读书，故作诗不能佳。”历叩以家中旧事及家人所在，莫不符合。久之，辞去，乩不复动矣。后请之，亦不再至。

    Explanation

    54

    Several years after his death, a relative of his family named Xu performed the practice of communicating with the spirits through a medium. The spirit tablet wrote, "The deity of Mount Yingju has arrived." Mount Yingju is a famous mountain in the north of Jinx溪. The north side of the mountain is where Xu's family resides. They then asked the deity for his name, and the spirit tablet wrote "Yu Buhao." Xu was surprised and said, "Could it be that he is a relative of ours?" The deity replied, "Yes."

    At that time, the second grandson of the deceased, Yunpu, was present. He asked, "Do you recognize him?" The deity replied, "He is my grandson, how could I not recognize him?" Yunpu was delighted and bowed down to ask, "Why did my ancestor become the god of this mountain?" The deity replied, "The King of the Underworld said I am not deceitful, so I received this title." Yunpu said, "Since my ancestor has become a god, he must know good or bad fortune. How are the descendants' scholarly achievements?" The deity replied, "Just study, and you can achieve fame and success. Why ask?" After that, the deity composed a poem and wrote, "I never studied in life, so I cannot write good poetry." They then asked about old family matters and the whereabouts of family members, and everything matched perfectly. After a while, the deity departed, and the spirit tablet no longer moved. When they asked for further communication, the deity did not appear again.

    55

    云圃为余姊婿，故能悉也。公居家孝友，富而节俭，好施与。余十二岁时，曾登堂拜公。宽衣缓带，蔼然可亲，真长者也。长者而神焉，谁曰不宜
    　　佑清寺僧
    　　豫章某生秋试，僦居于佑清寺侧。
    　　一夜，月光透窗。闻有排闼而入者，穴窗窥之。见一人纬帽纱衣，左手提壶，右手挈榼，心讶其异，初不敢问。
    　　其人既至窗外，置壶榼于地，以指击窗者再。生不解所谓，聊亦击窗应之。其人低语曰：“可启窗。”生亦试启之。其人取壶、榼入窗，生漫受之。复以其帽入，生接之，而谛视其人，顶光孺然，乃一憎也。心计髡奴夜至，作如此狡狯，必有所私；益隐跃向之，以观其动。
    　　既而僧以手引生手，使探其私处。生心恶其汙而不欲使觉，亦捉僧手入窗，令下按己阴，则翘然者乃与已类。僧大骇，叫绝狂弃，从断垣跃出。生启关追之，佯为不及而返。

    Explanation

    55

    Cloud Garden is my sister's husband, hence I know him well. He was filial and friendly at home, wealthy yet thrifty, and generous in his giving. When I was twelve years old, I once went to his house to pay my respects. He was dressed in loose robes and wide sashes, his demeanor was warm and approachable, truly a noble person. A noble person who is also divine, who would say it's inappropriate?

    Yousheng Temple Monk

    A certain student from Yuzhang took the autumn examination and rented a room near Yousheng Temple.

    One night, moonlight shone through the window. He heard someone push open the door and enter. Peering through the window, he saw a man wearing a straw hat and a silk robe, holding a pot in his left hand and a jar in his right. He was surprised by the unusual sight and did not dare to ask what it was.

    The man reached the window, placed the pot and jar on the ground, and knocked on the window twice. The student did not understand what it meant, so he lightly knocked back in response. The man whispered, "Open the window." The student cautiously opened it. The man brought the pot and jar inside, and the student accepted them. Then the man brought his hat inside, and the student took it, looking closely at the man. The man's head was shining brightly, and he was a monk. The student thought that a shaved servant had come at night and was acting so cunningly, he must have some secret purpose. He became more curious and watched the monk's actions more closely.

    Soon, the monk led the student's hand and made him touch his private area. The student felt disgusted by the monk's filth and did not want to let him know, so he took the monk's hand and placed it on his own private area. The monk was greatly startled, cried out in shock, and ran away over a broken wall. The student opened the door to chase him, but pretended not to catch up and returned.

    56

    乃引壶酹之。良醖也，启榼尝之，佳肴也。鼓掌狂笑，大恣饮啖。
    　　盖居停主人之妇向与僧期，数日前徙以寓客，僧犹未之识云。
    　　无赖子
    　　信州某村民入市镇买谷。肩之过一村，从姑之夫家在焉。民念中途饥渴，盍进谒以博一餐至则姑父他出，姑出见之，甚悦，命置谷于前厅，邀入后室，为设酒食。饭毕，出厅求谷，已亡矣。民大号，诉于姑曰：“家有老父，待此朝食。今无谷归，将逐我矣。吾宁死此，不忍见老父之饥且怒也！”姑恻然，给偿之，民负以归。
    　　至半途，有无赖子阻之，曰：“若盗某氏谷耶某氏使吾要夺，宜速舍而奔；不然，且执尔。”民不得已，置谷而去。
    　　无赖子取以归，复造其姑，责之曰：“尔大不良！尔夫不在。乃以谷与私人耶吾已夺之，将待尔夫而告之。”姑曰：“吾侄也。买谷经此，以探吾故而亡之，惧不敢归。故偿之耳。”无赖子复诬以秽词。姑无以自明，恚甚，投缳死，未敛也。

    Explanation

    56

    He poured wine to honor her. It was fine wine, and when he opened the container to taste it, it was delicious food. He clapped and laughed loudly, indulging freely in drinking and eating.

    The wife of the host had previously made an appointment with a monk. A few days ago, she moved to accommodate a guest, but the monk had not yet met her.

    A rascal.

    A villager from Xinzhou went to the market town to buy grain. Carrying the grain, he passed through a village where the wife of his aunt lived. The villager thought about his hunger and thirst along the way and decided to pay a visit to secure a meal. When he arrived, his aunt's husband was away. His aunt came out and was very pleased to see him. She ordered him to place the grain in the front hall and invited him into the back room, where she prepared wine and food for him. After finishing the meal, when he went out to look for the grain, it was gone. The villager cried out loudly and complained to his aunt, saying, "My father at home is waiting for this morning meal. Now that I have no grain to return with, he will cast me out. I would rather die here than see my old father starving and angry!" His aunt felt pity and gave him compensation. The villager carried the compensation back home.

    Halfway back, a rascal blocked his way and said, "Are you stealing grain from someone? Someone sent me to seize it, so you should quickly drop it and run; otherwise, I will arrest you." The villager had no choice but to abandon the grain and leave.

    The rascal took the grain and returned home. He went back to his aunt and accused her, saying, "You are very bad! Your husband is not here. Did you give the grain to a private person? I have already taken it, and I will wait for your husband to tell him." The aunt replied, "He is my nephew. He bought grain and passed by here, discovering my reason and taking it. He was afraid to return. So I gave him compensation." The rascal further accused her with foul words. The aunt could not defend herself, became extremely angry, and hanged herself, but she was not yet buried.

    57

    民闻而往哭之，谓“姑之死乃以我故也”。无赖子执而缚之，声其以奸致死，将诣有司。顷之，雷雨大作，黯黑不见人。比雨霁，无赖子震死户外矣，其姑复活。无赖子者，姑之从叔，居于前厅者也。搜其室中，前谷并在，民仍肩之以归。乡里共传，以为天理昭然也。
    　　——此庚戌春间事。东乡王肖山来都，于途决闻之，为余言。
    　　余老人
    　　余老人者，逸其名，东乡之西塘里人，善推测之术。
    　　初，闻某帅好招纳异人，往投之。逆旅遇一人，谈甚合，盖同道者，遂请试其术。命主人以碗覆一物于灯下，各卜之。余曰：“铁物也。”其人曰：“铁是矣。究是何铁物”余不能知。其人曰：“必断钉也。”启之果然。余惊服，固叩其所往。其人曰：“适至某帅府，欲售吾术。彼处胜我者甚多，念无所用，故归耳。”余闻之，亦废然而返。

    Explanation

    57

    The people heard and went to mourn her, saying, "The death of my aunt was due to me." The rascal seized and bound him, accusing him of causing her death through adultery, and intended to take him to the authorities. Soon, a great thunderstorm broke out, and it was so dark that people could not be seen. When the rain stopped, the rascal was found dead outside, and his aunt came back to life. The rascal was the aunt's cousin, who lived in the front hall. Searching his room, the grain was still there, and the people carried it back. The village spread the story, believing that divine justice was evident.

    This is an event that occurred in the spring of the Geng Xu year. East Village Wang Xiaoshan came to the capital, and on the way, he heard about it and told me.

    Old Man Yu, whose name is unknown, is from Xitang Village in East Village and is skilled in divination.

    Initially, he heard that a certain general liked to recruit extraordinary people and went to offer his services. At an inn, he met someone with whom he had a very good conversation, and they were likely of the same kind, so he asked to test his skills. He ordered the innkeeper to cover an object under a bowl by the lamp and each of them tried to guess it. I said, "It's an iron object." The other person said, "It is iron. But what kind of iron object is it?" I couldn't know. The other person said, "It must be a broken nail." When they opened it, it was indeed a broken nail. I was amazed and impressed, and I firmly asked where he was going. The other person said, "I just went to the general's mansion to sell my skills. There were many better than me there, so I thought there was no use and returned." Hearing this, I also returned disappointed.

    58

    越数年，其人来访。余辞以他出，以观其能。其人笑曰：“正在枣树下观书，何诳也”阍者惊报。相见大笑，盘桓而去。
    　　余晚年术益进，家居闭户，不肯衒于人。间露数事，皆神验。有村人修屋，问当以何日毕工。余期以某日某时，且属勿后。及期工毕，大雨踵至，淋漓旬日。又尝薄暮游某氏园，见瓜棚上瓜甚夥，向某氏求二枚。某氏许之。余请自携去，某氏曰：“公老人，焉能挟此重物明日当遣人送至。”余曰：“若是，则空言矣。”某氏笑曰：“公岂疑我食言耶”余亦笑而归。是夜，某氏瓜为偷儿盗尽，始悟余请自携之故，盖预知之也。

    Explanation

    58

    After several years, the person came to visit. I excused myself by saying I was out, to see his ability. The person laughed and said, "I was just reading under the jujube tree, how is this a lie?" The gatekeeper was surprised and reported. We met and laughed, staying for a while and then left.

    In my old age, my skills advanced further, and I stayed at home, not flaunting it to people. Occasionally, I revealed a few things, all of which were divinely accurate. A villager was building a house and asked when it would be completed. I predicted a specific date and time, and instructed him not to be late. When the predicted date arrived, the work was completed, and heavy rain followed, lasting for ten days.

    Once, late in the afternoon, I visited Mr. X's garden and saw many melons on the trellis. I asked Mr. X for two. Mr. X agreed. I asked to carry them myself, and Mr. X said, "You are an old man, how could you carry such a heavy load? I will send someone to deliver them tomorrow." I said, "If so, it would be empty words." Mr. X laughed and said, "Do you doubt that I would go back on my word?" I also laughed and went home. That night, the melons of Mr. X were stolen by thieves. I realized the reason I asked to carry them myself, as I had foreseen it.

    59

    余后不知所终。
    　　文慧禅师
    　　先族祖四桥公，官嵩明时，与僧文慧相善。僧没后，公解任家居。数年染疾，百治不效。忽僧来诊视，相见如平生欢，袖出医方，一药而瘳。僧既去，公始悟其死也。族人相讶以为神，为建寺栖之，称为文慧禅师。遇水旱之灾，祈祷甚应。能降乩示药方，治人疾病，无不立愈，其不治者，乩不答。
    　　有族人好斗，刃饬其腕，乞方于乩，乩曰“不治”。某忧泣，固请不已。乩曰：“出寺门，随手摘一草傅之。”如其言，痛立止，经三日，创已合矣。惟傅草处凝血为痂，附于肉。某厌其赘，以手爪去之，血涌出不止，须臾昏绝。知乩言“不治”者，终不治也。

    Explanation

    59

    I later did not know what became of him.
    Master Wen Hui
    My ancestor, the Righteous Bridge Master, served in Songming during which time he befriended a monk named Wen Hui. After the monk passed away, Master Righteous Bridge retired to his home. After several years, he contracted an illness that was not cured despite many treatments. Suddenly, the monk came to diagnose him; they met as if they had always been close friends. The monk took out a medical prescription from his sleeve, and with a single medicine, the illness was cured. After the monk had left, Master Righteous Bridge realized that the monk had died. The family was astonished by this and regarded it as miraculous, building a temple to house him and calling him Master Wen Hui. When faced with floods and droughts, prayers were very effective. The monk could descend via a medium to show medical prescriptions, treating people's illnesses, and all were cured instantly. If the illness was not curable, the medium would not respond.
    There was a family member who loved to fight, and his wrist was cut by a knife. He asked for a remedy from the medium, which said, "It is not curable." He was distressed and cried, persisting in his request. The medium said, "Go out of the temple gate and pick a plant nearby and apply it." Following its instructions, the pain stopped immediately, and after three days, the wound had healed. Only the place where the plant was applied had a clot of blood forming a scab attached to the flesh. He disliked the scab and removed it with his fingernails, causing blood to gush out uncontrollably. In a moment, he fainted. Knowing that the medium had said "not curable," he understood that it was ultimately not curable.

    60

    土寇杨益茂剽剠村落。族众谋避兵，请乩问所向。乩曰：“还走何方。”三问，答如初。佥谓“我族当赤，无所逃命矣”，相与号哭。无何，寇大至，村里为墟。寇氛既靖，存者尚半。盖族之西南有村曰“何坊”，寇所未经，凡避此方者皆免，始悟乩之隐告也，其不得兔者数也。
    　　今其乩失传，灵亦少替。
    　　耳食录卷三
    　　蜀商
    　　蜀有商人某甲，居货汉口。性诚朴，而不善持筹，每为同伙者欺蔽，商知之，亦不较。
    　　一日，独立店门，有美人翩然而入，直上其楼。商疑为娼女，而同伙者之私之也，将召而诘之。美人从楼上语曰：“君勿疑，吾乃狐也，欲僦此楼，故来耳。幸日以白饭一器饷我，当有以报。”商诺之，不复言。即以饭往，寂无所见，信其果狐也，设饭而下。抵暮往取器，则磊磊者在碗中。视之，白金也，商惊喜。次日复设饭，复得金如前。日以为常。

    Explanation

    60

    The Bandit Elites and the Fox Spirit Merchant

    Bandit leaders Yang Yimao and his men raided and plundered the villages. The clan members planned to flee from the soldiers and asked a spirit medium to ask for a direction. The spirit medium said, "Where should we return?" They asked three times, and the answer was the same each time. All said, "Our clan is doomed; there is no way to escape our fate," and they wept together. Soon, the bandits came in large numbers, and the villages became ruins. Once the bandit threat was quelled, half of them still survived. It turned out that to the southwest of the clan, there was a village called "He Fang," a place the bandits had never been. Those who fled to this place were all spared, and they finally understood the hidden message of the spirit medium; those who did not escape were few.

    Now, the spirit medium's method is lost, and the spirit's power has also diminished.

    Ear Food Record, Volume Three

    A Merchant from Sichuan

    There was a merchant named Jia from Sichuan, who lived in Hankou and dealt in goods. He was honest and simple but not good at managing accounts, and he was often cheated by his partners. The merchant knew this, but did not care.

    One day, standing alone at the store door, a beautiful woman entered gracefully and went directly to his upstairs room. The merchant suspected she was a prostitute, and thought his partners might have something to do with her, so he was going to call her down and question her. The woman spoke from the upstairs, saying, "Do not doubt me, I am a fox spirit. I want to rent this room, so I came. Please give me one bowl of white rice every day, and I will reward you." The merchant agreed and did not say anything else. He set up the rice and went downstairs. At dusk, he went to take the bowl, and found something in it. He looked inside and saw white gold (silver). The merchant was surprised and happy. The next day, he set up the rice again, and again found the silver. He made it a daily routine.

    61

    同伙询知其事，因先往取器，冀得金，至则碗中饭如故。乃笑谓商诳己，倾其饭而下。及商往，则金也。同伙恚曰：“金自楼出，公物也，当均分之。”商未应，而楼上语曰：“吾以金予某，赏其朴也。若辈盗贼其行，每私其囊橐以欺某，不罚幸矣，复望得赏耶？敢言析金者，尝吾石！”语毕，有石掷地上，地为之裂。伙惭且惧，乃不敢言。
    　　后伙众谋欲杀商而分取其金，置毒酒中，邀商饮，商未识也。忽楼上叱伙曰：“跪！”伙不觉皆跪。又叱曰：“拜！”伙皆向商亟拜。商诧甚，急扶之起，则皆膝屈不可伸。楼上又叱曰：“好自陈其罪！”伙皆涕泣向商曰：“偶萌恶念，利君财，实欲图君，设毒酒待君矣。”又闻楼上叱曰：“有毒酒，何不自饮”于是数人趋起取酒，将分饮之，商亟夺覆地，火光星爆。楼上大笑曰：“公诚长者，姑为公贷此数人死，令长跪三日谢罪。然此辈不可与居，公宜亟去，吾亦从此逝矣。”

    Explanation

    61

    Accomplices inquired about the matter and then went to take the vessel, hoping to obtain gold. When they arrived, the rice in the bowl was as before. They laughed and said the merchant had deceived them, pouring out the rice and leaving. When the merchant arrived, it was gold. The accomplices were angry and said, "The gold came from the tower; it is public property, and it should be divided equally." The merchant did not respond, but the voice from upstairs said, "I gave the gold to someone as a reward for their simplicity. You lot are thieves in your actions, always privately taking your bags to deceive me. You should be lucky not to be punished, and do you still expect a reward? Anyone who dares to suggest dividing the gold, taste my stone!" After speaking, a stone was thrown to the ground, and the ground split open. The accomplices were ashamed and afraid and dared not speak.

    Later, the accomplices plotted to kill the merchant and divide the gold, putting poison in the wine and inviting the merchant to drink. The merchant did not recognize it. Suddenly, the voice from upstairs shouted at the accomplices, "Kneel!" Unaware, they all knelt. Then it shouted, "Bow!" The accomplices all hurriedly bowed to the merchant. The merchant was very surprised and quickly helped them up, but they all had bent knees that could not be straightened. The voice from upstairs shouted again, "Admit your own crimes!" The accomplices wept and said to the merchant, "We suddenly had evil thoughts, coveting your wealth, actually wanting to harm you, preparing poisoned wine for you." Then they heard the voice from upstairs shout, "You have poisoned wine, why not drink it yourself?" Several people hurriedly got up to take the wine, about to drink it together, when the merchant quickly grabbed it and spilled it on the ground. Flames burst like stars. The voice from upstairs laughed and said, "You are indeed a noble person. I will temporarily pardon these people's deaths, make them kneel for three days to apologize. However, these people cannot be kept around; you should leave quickly, and I will also leave from here."

    62

    于是见美人缘梯而下，含倩流睐，徐徐出户而去。商追谢之，不复见矣。伙果跪三日而后能起。
    　　狐居楼凡三年，商得金无算，遂返成都为富人，立狐仙祠焉。
    　　非非子曰：快哉狐也，侠哉狐也，神哉孤也！商何以得此于狐哉忠厚之报也。呜呼！中孚可及豚色，况狐之灵者乎
    　　毛生
    　　前明熹庙时，天下多故，盗贼充斥，锦帆绿林之徒所在多有。
    　　洪州数举子入都，挟资颇重。道淮徐之间，一少年求附舟。
    　　叩其所自，自云施姓，盖亦应春官试者，为独行恐盗，故来。
    　　语作吴音，窥其行李衣冠，似是乌衣子弟。既入舟，取笥中佳茗，煎以江水，遍钦同袍，俊语名谈倾一座。众皆悦之，以为良友，恐不得当也。

    Explanation

    62

    In the end, a beautiful woman descended the ladder gracefully, her charming smile and flirtatious glances captivating everyone. She slowly left the house, and the merchant chased after her to thank her, but she was never seen again. The servant, upon hearing this, knelt for three days before being able to stand up.

    The fox lived in the tower for three years, during which the merchant accumulated vast wealth. He returned to Chengdu and became a wealthy man, building a temple for the fox spirit. The commentator, Nononzi, remarked: "How swift and brave the fox is! How divine it is!" He wondered how the merchant received such rewards from the fox, concluding that it was a reward for his loyalty and kindness. Alas! Even the middle faith can reach the color of a pig, how much more so the fox, which is even more spiritual.

    Mao Sheng

    During the reign of Emperor Xizong of Ming, there were many troubles in the world, and bandits were rampant. Several scholars from Hongzhou traveled to the capital, carrying a lot of money. On their journey through Huai and Xu, a young man asked to join their boat. When asked about himself, he said his surname was Shi and that he was also taking the spring official examination. He was traveling alone and feared bandits, so he came to join them. He spoke in the Wu dialect, and upon looking at his belongings and attire, they seemed to be members of the aristocracy. After entering the boat, he took out good tea from his box, brewed it with river water, and offered it to his fellow travelers. His witty and famous conversations impressed everyone. Everyone liked him and considered him a good friend, but they were afraid they might not be worthy of him.

    63

    已而江岸夕阳，乱流明灭，孤舟泊芦苇间。少年进曰：“江天暮景殊佳，某有短笛，愿为诸君一奏。”遂摩管倚篷吹之，悠扬数弄，直使鱼龙惊飞、蟾兔欲跃。众皆击节曰：“桓伊李牟今复生矣！”
    　　语未毕，忽一豪客跃入舟中，持一铁柄伞，奋击少年堕水死，呵曰：“忤奴不丐食村落，来此奚为”众视其人，形容怪伟，鬚髪林林如竖戟，皆骇极仆跌，结舌重呼曰：“贼贼……”客曰：“公等非赴试者耶”曰：“然。”“有重资耶？”曰：“有之。愿献贼，贼毋杀我。”客笑曰：“余不杀贼，贼真且杀公。适吹笛号众者是也。”众皆起谢。客曰：“贼众且悍，夜将报余。畏者可暂去前三里村高翁店一宿，无患也。不畏者留，更看余杀赋。”于是去者半，留者半。客戒留者先寝，闻呼即起视。自引酒狂饮，连飞数十斛不醉。饮罢，取铁柄伞枕之，卧，鼾声如雷霆。众假寐俟之。

    Explanation

    63

    Afterward, the sunset on the riverbank, the turbulent and flickering river, and a solitary boat moored among reeds. The youth stepped forward and said, "The evening scenery on the river is exceptionally beautiful. I have a short flute and wish to play for everyone." He then polished the flute and leaned against the boat's railing to play, his music melodious and graceful, so that even the fish and dragons were startled and leaped, and the moon and rabbits seemed to jump. Everyone clapped in rhythm and exclaimed, "It's as if the great musicians Huan Yi and Li Mou have been reborn!"

    Before he finished speaking, suddenly a rough person leaped onto the boat, holding an iron-handle umbrella, and fiercely struck the youth, causing him to fall into the water and drown. He shouted, "You insolent wretch, why not beg for food in the village, what are you doing here?" Looking at the man, he had an extraordinary and imposing appearance, with thick, bristly hair and beard, like spears standing upright. Everyone was too frightened to speak, only able to cry out, "Bandit, bandit..." The man said, "Are you all going to take an exam?" They replied, "Yes." "Do you have a lot of money?" "We do, we are willing to give it to you, don't kill us." The man laughed and said, "I won't kill you, but the real bandits will kill you. It was the one who just played the flute and called out to the crowd."

    The man said, "The bandits are numerous and fierce, they will come at night. Those who are afraid can temporarily go to the village and inn of Gao Wang, three miles ahead, and there will be no harm. Those who are not afraid can stay, and watch me finish off the bandits." Thus, half of them left, and half stayed. The man told those who stayed to go to sleep first, and to wake up if they heard a call. He then drank wine wildly, consuming dozens of measures without getting drunk. After drinking, he took the iron-handle umbrella and used it as a pillow, lying down, his snoring like thunder. The others took a brief nap and waited.

    64

    夜半，忽闻客呼曰：“贼至矣。”挟伞踞船头，时月黑星繁，微辨人影。一赋持刀奔客曰：“若杀吾弟，我今取若头。”客不答，即举伞格之，贼应手而仆。刀槊环进，客从容挥伞，呼呼作风声，与芦苇琴瑟相应。贼左右扑刺落水，馀贼奔逃。客已夺得贼弓矢，连发射之，尽告毙。观者股栗，汗流浃衣裾。
    　　客忽挟伞入舱坐，神气洒然。众酗酒劳客。复飞敷十觥，掀髯谓众曰：“公等穷年占毕，足迹不出三里外。宁知世路之巉巘哉！”众唯唯。又曰：“国家求才待用，自惟有其具则进。苟平平，宁坐床头弄稚子，无以父母之身轻饫虎狼之口也！今弟行无畏。”众罗拜曰：“向者不敢启问，今将军活我恩厚矣，愿闻姓名，以图报效。”客悉扶之起，举伞扣舷曰：“余亦非将军，亦无姓名，亦不望报。吾去矣！”一跃而逝。

    Explanation

    64

    Midnight, suddenly a guest shouted, "Thieves have arrived!" The guest held an umbrella and sat on the prow of the boat. At that moment, the moon was obscured and the stars were numerous, and faint human figures could be discerned. A thief rushed towards the guest with a knife and said, "If you killed my brother, I will take your head today." The guest did not respond and immediately raised the umbrella to block the attack; the thief fell immediately. Swords and spears surrounded and advanced, but the guest calmly waved the umbrella, making a whistling sound like the wind, which echoed with the rustling of the reeds. The thieves on both sides fell into the water, and the remaining thieves fled. The guest had already taken the thieves' bows and arrows, continuously shooting them, and all were killed. The spectators were so terrified that they shivered and their sweat soaked their clothes.

    Suddenly, the guest entered the cabin with the umbrella and sat down, looking calm and composed. The crowd drank and served the guest. They drank ten more cups of wine, and the guest lifted his beard and said to the crowd, "You all spend your lives studying books and never venture beyond three miles from here. How can you know the dangers of the world?" The crowd nodded in agreement. The guest continued, "The state seeks talents and waits to use them, but if one has the ability, one should advance. If one is mediocre, one might as well sit at home playing with children, and one should not lightly sacrifice one's parents to feed the mouths of tigers and wolves! Now my brother will go without fear." The crowd knelt and bowed, saying, "Before, we dared not ask. Now the general has saved us with great kindness. We wish to know your name to repay your kindness." The guest helped them all up, lifted the umbrella, and tapped the side of the boat, saying, "I am not a general, I have no name, and I do not expect any reward. I am leaving!" He leaped away.

    65

    既而春闱，一举子逢客于号舍，心讶此君能挽两石弓，复能识丁字，真异人也！趋前问无恙，客睨视若不相识，亦不答，即入号熟寝。窥其舍，铁砚斑管各一，别无长物，初不敢呼问。客直睡一昼夜，不少寤。次日午响，举子文己毕，将缮写，心德客，虑其沈睡将不克终卷，欲以己馀勇贾之。遂呼客，客大（圭心）曰：“竖子败吾事，断送会元矣！”举子踧踖，不知所对。既而客叹曰：“毛生毛生，岂非命也？夫千金之璧，当首贡王廷，安能随行逐队，自居牛后，为渴睡汉椰揄哉今以吾文与公，可获亚名，亦不负公数千里冒险跋涉也。”索纸书之，风行海涌，三艺立成。掷于举子之前，曰：“吾去矣，”即挟空卷投有司，称疾而去。

    Explanation

    65

    During the spring metropolitan examination, a scholar encountered a guest in the examination room. He was astonished in his heart that this man could draw a bow capable of shooting two stones and could also read and write, truly an extraordinary person! He approached and asked if all was well, but the guest looked askance as if not recognizing him, did not answer, and immediately went to sleep soundly. Peering into his room, he saw only a single iron inkstone and a single inkstick, with no other possessions. Initially, he dared not call out or ask. The guest slept straight through the day and night, not waking up at all. The next day at noon, the scholar had already completed his essay and was about to transcribe it. He was grateful to the guest, fearing that his deep sleep would prevent him from completing the test, and wanted to lend his remaining energy to the task. He called out to the guest, who angrily said, "This boy has ruined my plans, has ruined my chances of being the top scorer!" The scholar was embarrassed and did not know what to say. The guest then sighed, "Mao Sheng, Mao Sheng, is it not fate? A precious jade worth a thousand gold pieces should be presented first to the imperial court; how can it follow in the crowd, placing itself behind the ox, to be ridiculed by the sleepy ones? Now I will give my essay to you, and you can achieve second place, which does not辜负 your journey of thousands of miles and the risks taken." He took paper and wrote swiftly, like wind and waves, completing three sections in an instant. He tossed the essay before the scholar and said, "I am leaving," then left with an empty scroll, claiming illness and departing.

    66

    举于阅其文，允称杰构，书法亦矫健非常，嗟叹不已。因弃己作，书客文以进，果成进士第二名。
    　　非非子曰：余闻乡先生述毛生事甚悉，惜失其名字。嗟乎！
    　　天地奇气，必有所钟。畸人杰士，宜不绝于世，顾有幸有不幸，斯隐显异焉。使毛生建高牙、拥大纛，虎奋鹰扬。立功万里外，则班，卫之勋，岂多让哉即不然，以彼其文掇撮巍科而冠多士，秉笔词翰之林，亦足与枚、马，邹、扬辈争烈，何至雾鳞云爪、首尾不详若是哉！昔宋景濂录秦士，余纪毛生，文虽不逮，有同慨云。

    Explanation

    66

    The selection was made after reviewing his literary work, which truly deserves to be called a masterpiece; his calligraphy was also exceptionally strong, and I cannot stop admiring it. Therefore, he abandoned his own work and wrote the guest's text to submit, ultimately becoming the second-place winner in the imperial examination.

    Nonnonzi said: I heard from a local scholar who described the story of Mao Sheng in great detail, but regrettably, I lost track of his name. Alas!

    The extraordinary energy of heaven and earth must surely manifest in someone. Outstanding individuals should not be absent from the world, but there is luck and misfortune, and thus differences in visibility and recognition. If Mao Sheng had established a high banner and held a large flag, and acted boldly and fiercely, achieving great deeds far away, his accomplishments would not be inferior to those of Ban Chao and Wei Qing. Even if he did not achieve military success, with his writing, he could have topped the list in the imperial examination and competed with figures like Mei Cheng, Ma Rong, Zou Yan, and Yang Xiong. How could it come to this, where his beginning and end are so obscure?

    In the past, Song Jinglian recorded the story of Qin scholars, and I record the story of Mao Sheng. Though my writing may not match up to theirs, we share the same sentiment.

    67

    卖酥饼者
    　　有卖酥饼者某，行山僻中。会日暮，恐遇鬼物，汗下疾驰。
    　　遥见前一人彳亍而行，某甚喜，以为有伴矣，追而谓之曰：“闻此地素多鬼，君可少待，同行也。”其人且行且应曰：“但速来，无恐。”
    　　既及，某抚其肩，曰：“脱不遇君，吾恐怖欲死矣！”其人转头应曰：“大是！大是！以一饼啖我，何如？”某取饼与之，忽见其口大如箕、面蓝色、牙长数寸垂口外，嚼饼嘻笑曰：“甚佳。”
    　　某骇绝，弃饼，狂吼而奔。
    　　张小姐
    　　桐城张小姐，初从其父督学江苏，居江阴署中之大雅楼。窗外桂树一株，高与楼齐。每日梳冼毕，从窗间倾盆水其下。
    　　一日，亡金耳环一，遍觅不得。穷诘女使，亦不得。终疑女使匿之也。
    　　后小姐归某巨公为夫人，复从夫督学江苏，亦居大雅楼。
    　　一夕，倚窗看月，见树上一小枝金光烂然，心甚异之。次日寻视，则所失耳环在焉。始悟向者环落水盆中，倾水时挂于枝上也。

    Explanation

    67

    The Baker and the Ghost

    There was a baker named Mr. So who was traveling through a remote mountain area. Just as evening fell, he feared encountering ghosts or spirits, and he was sweating and galloping quickly.

    From afar, he saw a man walking slowly and hesitantly. Mr. So was very happy, thinking he had found a companion, and he chased after him, saying, "I've heard this place is usually haunted by ghosts. You might want to wait a bit; let's walk together." The man continued walking and responded, "Just come quickly, there's nothing to be afraid of."

    When they caught up, Mr. So patted the man's shoulder and said, "If I hadn't met you, I would have been terrified to the point of death!" The man turned his head and replied, "Very much so! Very much so! Give me a cake to eat, how about that?" Mr. So took a cake and gave it to him. Suddenly, he saw the man's mouth as large as a winnowing basket, with a blue face and teeth several inches long hanging out of his mouth. The man chewed the cake and laughed, saying, "Very good."

    Mr. So was terrified to the point of shock, dropped the cake, and screamed wildly before running away.

    Lady Zhang

    Lady Zhang from Tongcheng initially followed her father to supervise education in Jiangsu and lived in the Dàyálóu (Grand Elegance Pavilion) in the Jiangyin government office. There was a single osmanthus tree outside the window, as tall as the building. Every day after washing and combing her hair, she would pour a bucket of water from the window down below.

    One day, she lost a gold earring. She searched everywhere but could not find it. She interrogated the female servant thoroughly, but the servant also could not find it. Finally, she suspected the female servant had hidden it.

    Later, Lady Zhang married a prominent official as his wife and again followed her husband to supervise education in Jiangsu, living in the Dàyálóu. One night, leaning against the window to watch the moon, she saw a small branch on the tree with golden light shining brightly. She found it very strange. The next day, she searched and looked, and then found the lost earring there. She finally realized that the earring had fallen into the water bucket and hung on the branch when she poured water.

    68

    旧地重来，珠还璧合，事亦巧矣！
    　　三官神
    　　临川吴甲，贾黔中，尝与黔人之女私。女曰：“郎家距此数千里，一旦远归，别选淑配，妾如残秋败叶矣！”甲曰：“某虽归，必旋来，谋与卿偕老，何乃出此言”女转呜咽。甲亦怆然，无以慰谕之，乃曰：“卿不信，立誓可乎？”女颔之。地有三官庙，神素灵显。共诣庙，焚香矢之，曰：“男某女某，为结私缘，愿偕佳偶。千里同心，九原共穴。有渝此盟，神明殛之！”
    　　既立誓，情好愈笃，将图偕奔。女曰：“无庸也。君乡人客黔中，与妾父善者不下十馀人。妾父素重君，若因乡人为蹇修，致礼而求娶，其谁曰不然又何必以迁贿之行，为人吱笑哉”甲曰：“甚善。然顷得严君手谕，令某暂回，以慰思念，某不敢违。计往返不及期年。某更得衔父命而通姻好，不亦善乎”女然之。

    Explanation

    68

    Returning to the old place, the pearls returned and the jade reunited, and the situation was indeed ingenious!

    The Three Officials

    Linchuan's Wu Jia, who traded in Guizhou, once had an illicit affair with a woman from Guizhou. The woman said, "Your home is thousands of miles away from here. If you return one day, you might choose a virtuous spouse, and I would be like a fallen leaf in late autumn!" Wu Jia replied, "Even if I return, I will surely come back to marry you and grow old together. Why do you say such things?" The woman then wept. Wu Jia was also moved and could not comfort her, so he said, "If you do not believe me, can we take an oath?" The woman nodded. There was a temple dedicated to the Three Officials, and the deities were particularly powerful and responsive. They went to the temple together, burned incense, and vowed, saying, "Man Jia and woman from Guizhou, we are bound by a private connection. We wish to be matched with a good partner. Though we are thousands of miles apart, our hearts are united, and we will share a grave in the afterlife. If this oath is broken, may the gods punish us!"

    After taking the oath, their affection became even stronger, and they planned to run away together. The woman said, "There is no need for that. Your fellow townspeople are guests in Guizhou, and there are at least ten who are close to my father. My father has always respected you. If we let them act as matchmakers, present gifts, and seek marriage, who would say otherwise? Why engage in such corrupt actions that would be ridiculed by others?" Wu Jia said, "That is very good. However, I recently received a letter from my father, ordering me to return temporarily to comfort his thoughts. I dare not disobey. I estimate that the round trip will not take more than a year. I will then carry out my father's command and establish a marital alliance, which is not a bad plan." The woman agreed.

    69

    临行之前夕，谓甲曰，“始终之盟，固不忒矣。但早至一日，则多受一日之赐，若稽以岁月，将有子晰委禽之衅，妾虽捐骨泥沙，其如三生饮恨何”甲曰，“吾己寓意乡人为某斧柯，当无他虞。某亦克期至矣。”洒泪而别。
    　　既归，甲父为聘封氏女，甲不能违。已复得黔中乡人书，言为君议昏某翁，可不辱命，但当速来完其好事。甲素畏父严，不敢以闻，进退狼狈，而背誓之念遂决，不复税黔中之驾矣。
    　　将逆女于封氏，先三日，召屠者割牲，将告祖庙。忽有三人从南来，形表伟异，遽夺屑者刀，擒甲而割其阴，掷于地而去。旁人奔救，不及也。甲既阉，昏绝数四，傅药半年，乃能起。亲迎不果，封氏之女亦别蘸焉。

    Explanation

    69

    The night before his departure, he said to Jia, "Our lifelong vow will surely not be broken. But arriving one day earlier will bring one day more of benefit. If delayed by days or months, there will be the scandal of Zi Xi abandoning his wife. Even if I sacrifice my bones to the mud and sand, how can I bear the three lifetimes of regret?" Jia replied, "I have already instructed the villagers to prepare something for me. There will be no other worries. He will definitely arrive on time." They parted with tears.

    After returning, Jia's father arranged for him to marry a girl from the Feng family, and Jia could not refuse. Later, he received a letter from a villager in Qianzhong, which said that they had arranged a marriage for him with a certain old man, and he would not disgrace the mission, but he should come quickly to complete this good deed. Jia always feared his father's strictness and dared not inform him, finding himself in a difficult position, unable to decide. He decided to break his vow and no longer planned to return to Qianzhong.

    He was going to marry the girl from the Feng family, and three days before, he summoned a butcher to slaughter an animal to offer to the ancestral temple. Suddenly, three men came from the south, their appearance was extraordinary. They suddenly took the butcher's knife and castrated Jia, then threw him on the ground and left. Others tried to save him, but it was too late. Jia was castrated, he fainted several times, and after half a year of medicine, he could stand up again. The wedding did not take place, and the girl from the Feng family also remarried.

    70

    后父母物故，家道凋落，甲以腐疾不任远商。年六十馀，遂至乞食。尝挈瓢来余族，儿童见之，无不诮谑。寻以创发死。
    　　非非子曰：桑濮之行，君子所耻。然始之以情，继之以礼，终之以义，虽不可训，犹有取焉。若唐元九所为《会真记》，始乱终弃，饰曰补过，幸崔氏寡廉鲜耻，转眼怜人，不然，璧之破也，岂能再璞崔氏之过，又将焉补忍人之言何其佞也！而人不之非，神莫之殛，甚矣其漏网矣，于甲也又何诛
    　　邹忠介公
    　　崇仁大华山，名山也。其神甚灵异，祷疾病、祈福祥者，数郡毕至，趾相接也。有不诚者，辄被谴。
    　　赵元坛、王灵官为左右二元帅，轮流值岁。相传赵宽而王严。赵值岁，来者有小过，多不较。其绳之峻而祸之速者，必王值岁也：甚者被其击立死，或蹉跌伤折肢体，邪视者痛目，妄言者卷舌焦唇，手足不戒者伤指及趾，其轻者亦或得狂疾，自道生平私隐事。以故诸为不善者，闻其风不敢往。

    Explanation

    70

    After the death of his parents, Jia's family fortune declined, and he was afflicted with a chronic illness that prevented him from traveling far to engage in commerce. At the age of over sixty, he was reduced to begging. Once, carrying a gourd, he came to my clan, where children saw him and mocked him mercilessly. Soon after, he died from a sudden onset of illness.

    Non-Nonzi remarked: The behavior of the Sang and Pu people is a disgrace to the noble. However, if such behavior begins with emotion, is followed by propriety, and ends with righteousness, it may still have some redeeming value, even if it cannot be fully taught. But consider the case of Tang Yuanjiu's The Tale of the Pillow, where the story begins with infidelity and ends with abandonment, with the excuse of correcting a mistake. Fortunately, Cui Yingying is so shameless that she can turn around and show compassion to others. Otherwise, once a jade is broken, can it ever be restored to its original purity? How can one make up for the wrongs of a heartless person? The flattery is so insincere! Yet people do not condemn him, and the gods do not punish him; how much has he escaped punishment? What then is there to blame Jia for?

    Zou Zhongjie (Zou Yiwu)

    Mount Dahua in Chongren is a famous mountain. Its deities are extremely powerful and mysterious, and those who pray for healing or seek blessings from neighboring prefectures come in great numbers, their footsteps overlapping. Those who are not sincere are immediately punished.

    Zhao Yuantan and Wang Lingguan are the left and right generals, taking turns to oversee the year. It is said that Zhao is lenient while Wang is strict. When Zhao is in charge, those who come with minor offenses are often not reprimanded. However, when Wang is in charge, those who are harshly disciplined and suffer swift misfortune are those who have come. Some are struck dead on the spot, others stumble and injure or break their limbs, those who look askance suffer pain in their eyes, those who speak falsely have their tongues curled and lips scorched, those who are not careful with their hands and feet injure their fingers and toes, and even those with minor offenses may suffer from madness, revealing their private secrets. Therefore, those who do evil are afraid to come, even upon hearing of their reputation.

    71

    尝有谒者至山下，见一女足甚纤，心羡之。因以二指遥度其长短，指乃僵；至神前悔罪，乃复。又有一人言牛肉之美，遂喑哑，恳祷而后能言。又有男女二人中途相悦，夜苟合，两体连牵不能脱，遂羞痛俱死，死犹相粘，同棺而葬之。其灵异如此。
    　　邹忠介元标微时，三至而三不得上。询其故于人，人曰：“子之靴，革物也，神必恶此。盍易之”易布靴，乃登。既谒庙，见神旁有鼓，革鼓也。忠介诘神曰：“我革其靴，神恶之，宜矣。神胡革其鼓，庸有说乎”言未已，鼓即从架上滚下，直至山脚。闻者异之。后每岁供布鼓焉。
    　　忠介既以理屈神，弥自持慎，虽旷途暗室，不敢或苟。神使灵官随之三年，欲伺其短。每闻鞭声铮然，或日中灯下见其举鞭欲击状，而卒莫之击。后偶过井旁，见修绠系一胆瓶，有水甚清洁，盖新汲之井中者，而汲者不在。忠介方渴，掬饮之。乃见水中有灵官鞭影，急取钱投于水，乃灭。遂不复见。

    Explanation

    71

    Once upon a time, a visitor arrived at the foot of a mountain and saw a woman's foot that was extremely slender, and he envied it. He used his two fingers to measure its length and width at a distance, and his fingers then became stiff. He went before the deity to repent, and then they returned to normal. There was also a man who spoke of the beauty of beef, and he became mute. After fervently praying, he was able to speak again. There were also two men and women who met and fell in love in the middle of the road. They had a secret tryst at night, and their bodies became entangled and they could not separate. They both died in shame and pain, and even after death, they remained stuck together. They were buried in the same coffin. Such were the miraculous and strange occurrences.

    When Zou Zhongjie Yuanbiao was of lowly status, he went to the mountain three times and failed to ascend each time. He asked someone about the reason, and the person said, "Your boots are made of leather, and the deity must surely dislike them. Why not change them?" He changed to cloth boots, and then he ascended. After he had visited the temple, he saw a drum beside the deity, which was made of leather. Zou Zhongjie questioned the deity, saying, "I changed my leather boots, and the deity disliked them, which is appropriate. Why did the deity change its leather drum? Is there any explanation for this?" While he was still speaking, the drum rolled off the stand and down to the foot of the mountain. Those who heard about it found it strange. Afterwards, every year, a cloth drum was provided.

    Zou Zhongjie, having been defeated by the deity with reason, became even more cautious and self-disciplined. Even in remote paths and dark rooms, he dared not act carelessly. The deity sent a spirit official to follow him for three years, to watch for any faults. He often heard the sound of a whip cracking, or saw the figure of the spirit official raising a whip to strike, in the middle of the day or under the lamp, but he never actually struck him. Later, he happened to pass by a well. He saw a long rope tied to a gourd-shaped jar, with very clean water, which had just been drawn from the well, but the person who had drawn the water was not there. Zou Zhongjie was thirsty, and he scooped up some water to drink. And then he saw the shadow of the spirit official's whip in the water. He quickly took some coins and threw them into the water, and the shadow disappeared. And he never saw it again.

    72

    市中小儿
    　　昔长安市中有二小儿：一红衣，一白衣。红衣者过人门前，则以一红球抛掷地上。白衣者随而拾之，以为笑乐。红衣者抛掷益急，球落纷纷，白衣者不能尽拾，遂相连面去，馀球亦不见。
    　　次日，市中火大作。红球所掷之家，荡为灰烬。惟经白衣拾取者，房舍参差并存。
    　　又尝有一道士，露其胸，大书一“人”字于两乳之间。行市中三日，人无识者，——乃“火”字也。已而遭火。
    　　长春苑主
    　　友人余君，自号乌虚子，为余述元生事甚悉。元生，不知何许人也，弱冠补诸生，才名甚噪。三战秋闱，不捷，遂结庐山间，绝意进取。淡营寡虑，诗文之外，唯嗜花若性命，然未尝手折一枝、戕一萼也。

    Explanation

    72

    Children in the Market

    Once upon a time, in the market of Chang'an, there were two children: one wearing a red robe, the other a white robe. The child in red would pass by people's doors and throw a red ball onto the ground. The child in white would follow and pick up the ball, finding amusement in it. The child in red threw the ball more vigorously, causing them to fall one after another, and the child in white could not pick them all up, so they left together, and the remaining balls were no longer seen.

    The next day, a great fire broke out in the market. The house where the red ball was thrown became ashes. Only the houses where the white child had picked up the balls remained, with varying sizes and styles still standing.

    There was also once a Taoist priest who exposed his chest and wrote a large "person" character between his nipples. He walked in the market for three days, and nobody recognized him—it was actually the "fire" character. Later, he suffered a fire.

    The Master of Changchun Garden

    A friend of mine, Yu Jun, who calls himself Wu Xu Zi, told me a very detailed story about Yuan Sheng. Yuan Sheng, whose origins are unknown, became a student in his early twenties and gained a well-known reputation. He took the provincial examinations three times but failed each time. He then built a hut in the mountains and gave up the desire for advancement. Living a simple and carefree life, he loved flowers as if they were life itself, but he never picked a single branch or harmed a single petal.

    73

    一日独坐，有舆马旌节，倥偬门外。二青衣进谒曰：“夫人敬遣相迓。”元茫然，问夫人云谁，青衣曰：“长春苑主也。”即促登道。元漫许之。
    　　不半里，便有香气来迎，泽衣沁骨。又三四里许，城郭袤延，望如锦绮。青衣曰：“此苑城也。皆落花砌筑，久而胶结，固于金石。”抵一城门，榜曰“管春”，谓天下之春皆从此出。城内辉煌烂漫，不可目穷，皆四时之花同时开放，颜色相间，掩映而蔽亏也。行万花中，径甚缭曲，觉神明爽发，肤髪皆香。
    　　有顷而至，则宫阙亭台，朱甍碧瓦，鳞次而带续也。阍者皆韶艳女郎，驰报夫人。元降舆以待。第见红英翠叶之间，飘轻裙、曳广袖，美丽闲都者无虑千百，私语匿笑，往来窥客。以问，青衣曰：“花神也。花一种，即有一神。丰约秾纤不一，花亦如之。”

    Explanation

    73

    One day, Xuan was sitting alone when a carriage, horse, and ceremonial flags appeared hurriedly outside the door. Two attendants in green robes paid their respects and said, "Madam respectfully sent me to welcome you." Xuan was bewildered and asked, "Who is Madam?" The attendant replied, "The master of the Garden of Eternal Spring." He then urged Xuan to mount the carriage. Reluctantly, Xuan agreed.

    After not half a mile, fragrant breezes greeted them, and the dew-soaked clothes permeated his bones. Another three or four miles later, the city walls stretched out, looking like brocade. The attendant said, "This is the city of the Garden. It is all built from fallen petals, over time they have bonded, more solid than metal and stone." They reached a city gate with a sign that read "Controller of Spring," indicating that all the spring in the world comes from here. Inside the city, it was brilliant and colorful, too much to see at once, with all the flowers of the four seasons blooming at the same time, colors intermingling, overlapping and partially hidden. Walking among the myriad flowers, the path was very winding, and Xuan felt refreshed and invigorated, his skin and hair perfumed.

    After a while, they arrived at palaces, pavilions, red eaves, and green tiles, arranged in a fish-scale pattern and connected. The gatekeepers were all beautiful young women who rushed to report to Madam. Xuan stepped down from the carriage to wait. Among the red blossoms and green leaves, he saw women dancing in light skirts and wide sleeves, beautiful and elegant, numbering in the hundreds, whispering and giggling, peeking at the guests. He asked the attendant, who replied, "They are the flower goddesses. Each type of flower has its own goddess, varying in fullness and slimness, just like the flowers."

    74

    已而阍者出，传夫人请见，元肃容而入。历门闼十数重，达一大殿。珠帘乍卷，翟葆斜开，嫔从如云，左右环簇。夫人冠华玉之冠，履彩云之舄，衣裳霞艳，环佩鸾鸣，神影仙姿，惊绝人世，降阶而迎。元下拜，夫人命侍女曳起，赐坐。夫人南向，元东向。夫人敛容曰：“此地众美所归，群芳所出。秀才以惜花之心，成爱花之癖，故特迎致，盖奇绿也。”元逊谢。
    　　俄而设享，自殿中至于廊庑，绮席相次。凡诸花神，悉令陪宴。进飞英红露之酿，陈词香和玉之羞。酒肴数周，众乐并作。间歌叠舞，遏雪停云。风调各殊，容华并绝。夫人曰：“惜弄玉、飞琼请姊妹方宴瑶池，来暇邀致，使今日管弦未臻极盛。”元捧觞避席，曰：“今日宴会，色艺极天地之选，声容尽古今之妙。一介鲰生，躬际斯盛，皆夫人辱命之宠也。愿借芳尊，为夫人寿。”夫人起而受之，亦酌酒酬元，日昃罢宴，以笙歌羽卫，送居丛芳馆。

    74

    After a while, the gatekeeper emerged and announced that the lady wished to see him. The subject entered with a solemn and respectful demeanor, passing through dozens of gates and reaching a grand hall. The pearl curtains suddenly lifted, and the ceremonial canopy slightly opened. Concubines and attendants were like clouds, surrounding him on all sides. The lady wore a jade crown and colorful cloud-patterned shoes, her clothes as colorful as the evening clouds, and her jade pendants made a tinkling sound. Her divine appearance and celestial beauty were astonishingly beyond the world. She descended the steps to greet him. The subject bowed deeply, and the lady ordered her attendants to help him up and offered him a seat. The lady faced south, and the subject faced east. The lady composed herself and said, "This place is where all beauty gathers and where all flowers bloom. The scholar cultivated a love for flowers due to his appreciation of their beauty, and thus I specially invited you, considering it a rare and precious encounter." The subject humbly thanked her.

    Soon after, a feast was set up, with luxurious tables arranged from the main hall to the corridors. All flower deities were invited to join the feast. They served flying-pearl red-dew wine and fragrant jade delicacies. The feast lasted several rounds of wine and food, and various entertainments began. Intermittent songs and dances were performed, stopping the falling snow and clouds. Each performance had its own unique style, and the beauty of the performers was unparalleled. The lady said, "The flower deities of Jade and Flying Pearl, along with their sisters, are enjoying a feast in the Jade Pool. They are too busy to be invited, so today's music and dance have not reached their peak." The subject stood up and offered a toast to the lady's longevity, saying, "Today's banquet features the most beautiful and talented, with performances that are the finest of all time. A humble scholar like me has had the great fortune to witness such a splendid event, all thanks to the lady's gracious invitation. I wish to offer a toast to the lady's longevity." The lady stood up to accept the toast and then offered a drink in return. The feast ended when the sun was high in the sky, and they were escorted by music and a retinue to the Fragrant Flower Pavilion.

    75

    明日令下，以元为长春苑催花使者，并赐青幡绛节，彩仗云骈，披服宝玉，出入府第。花神闻之，皆来贺。夫人命侍儿送花名玉册至。元阅其姓字，皆往古美人，乃悟花神即美人也，出世为美人，离世为花神。元欲识其尤者，因谓众美曰：“诸卿芳名，心写久矣。今鄙人愿有以观焉，敢请。”众皆笑曰：“谨受命。”
    　　元问：“苏若兰来乎？”一女迟回而出，意度闲稚。元揖谓之曰：“卿织锦璇玑图，八百四十字，宛转循环，有诗三千余首，古今才妙莫或右之。”若兰逊谢。次问红拂，红拂应曰：“妾是也。”元视其貌，修然绝尘，叹曰：“识药师于晋谒，结虬髯于逆旅，红妆俊眼，真希有也！”红拂退，元目送久之。忽见一女于稠人中俯而摩其足，若不胜其楚者，元戏问为谁，众曰：“窅娘也。”元曰：“以纤月之勾，妙凌云之舞，真不让掌上人矣，”窅颊赤。

    Explanation

    75

    The next day, the order was issued, appointing Yuan as the envoy to the Changchun Garden to hasten the blooming of flowers, and granting him a green banner, a red scepter, and colorful flags in a cloud-like formation, as well as precious jade ornaments to wear, allowing him to come and go in and out of the mansion. When the flower spirits heard of it, they all came to congratulate. The lady-in-waiting was ordered to bring the jade-bound register of flower names. Yuan read their names, and they were all ancient beauties. He realized that the flower spirits were the same as the ancient beauties—they became beauties in this world and flower spirits after leaving it. Yuan wanted to recognize the most outstanding among them, so he said to the beautiful ones, "Your noble names have been long cherished in my heart. Now, I humbly wish to see you, if I may ask." They all smiled and said, "We accept your command."

    Yuan asked, "Is Su Ruolan here?" A woman hesitated and then emerged, with a leisurely and innocent demeanor. Yuan bowed and said to her, "You wove the Xuanji Tu with 840 characters, which is intricately interwoven and contains over 3,000 poems, unmatched in talent and skill throughout history." Su Ruolan modestly declined the praise. Next, he asked about Hong Fu, and Hong Fu replied, "I am here." Yuan looked at her appearance, which was elegant and transcendent, and he sighed, "You recognized the Buddhist master during his visit and befriended the man with the curly beard in an inn. Your beauty and sharp eyes are truly rare!" Hong Fu stepped back, and Yuan watched her for a long time. Suddenly, he saw a woman in the crowd who was bending down and rubbing her foot, as if in pain. Yuan playfully asked who she was, and they replied, "It is Yan Niang." Yuan said, "With her slender waist like a crescent moon and her graceful dance that soars to the clouds, she is truly no less than a dancer on the palm of a hand," and Yan Niang blushed.

    76

    而西阶一美人轻盈纤弱，拂袖而唾，颇有不平之色。旁一女手梅花一枝，眉目明秀，不可描画，睨之而晒曰：“燕儿燕儿，又作昭阳故本矣。”美人闻言，惭且怒曰：“何与尔梅精事，”盖飞燕与采萍相诮也。杨妃亦谓楼东寂寥，则珍珠可慰，奈何以舌锋刺人采萍未及答，一女姗姗而前曰：“肥婢何知，欲为祸水兴波，不记环上系罗衣时耶”元问之，乃班婕妤也，恐其攻轧无已，亟为解之，曰：“诸卿往事，何足深论譬诸花枝纠结，花片相扑，无害于花，徒令人可怜耳。”于是诸女谢而退。

    Explanation

    76

    On the western steps stood a graceful and delicate woman, who waved her sleeve and spat, showing a look of dissatisfaction. Beside her was another woman holding a plum branch, with beautiful and refined features that were beyond description. She glanced at the first woman with a sarcastic smile and said, "Ah, Ah, you're back to your old tricks at the Zhaoyang Palace again." The first woman, upon hearing this, felt both ashamed and angry and retorted, "What business do you have with my affairs?" This exchange was between Zhao Feiyan and Cai Ping. Yang Guifei also felt lonely in the east wing of the palace, thinking that Pearl could provide comfort, but she was upset by the sharp words. Cai Ping did not have time to respond before another woman approached slowly and said, "How can you, a fat maid, understand? Are you trying to stir up trouble as a disaster? Don't you remember when you were wearing a silk robe tied with a ring?" Yuan asked who she was, and it turned out to be Ban Jieyu. Worried that the conflict would continue, Yuan quickly intervened and said, "What happened between you all in the past is not worth discussing in detail. It's like when flower branches get entangled and petals collide; it doesn't harm the flowers, it just makes people feel sorry for them." With these words, the women all apologized and left.

    77

    他日，夫人命元定花神甲乙，元辞让再三，不获。乃甄别其香，艳，各为三品：香曰奇香、名香、幽香；艳曰雅艳、秾艳、狂艳。视其神以定品，而花隶焉。如西施、王嫱、卓文君、崔双文之属，咸列狂艳。
    　　西施闻之，请见曰：“妾虽鄙陋，君何至以狂艳见目”元谓：“卿泛湖之役，固当小贬。”西子辩曰：“沼吴之后，妾实从伍相于江流。陶朱何人，妾宁俪之，以负君恩而丧妇节也”元矍然曰：“微卿言，吾几误矣！”有顷，王嫱亦来，泣告曰：“妾以薄命，为画工所误，远嫁沙漠。以君命故，不敢违，未尝一日忘汉也。而佞臣秉笔，诬以聚麀之行，妾饮恨黄沙，末由昭雪，故使冢草独青，以明区区之志。而僧孺《周秦行记》乃敢肄为狂言，深相汙蔑，此妾之所痛心而茹愤者也，君其察之！”元再三引咎，乃列二人于幽香，始悦而去。

    Explanation

    77

    On another day, the lady ordered Yuan to rank the flower deities from first to last. Yuan repeatedly declined but was not successful in his refusal. He then distinguished between fragrance and beauty, each into three grades: fragrance was divided into peculiar fragrance, famous fragrance, and subtle fragrance; beauty was divided into elegant beauty, rich beauty, and wild beauty. He determined their grade based on their spirit, and the flowers followed suit. Figures such as Xi Shi, Wang Zhaojun, Zhuo Wenjun, and Cui Shuangwen were all listed under the category of wild beauty.

    Xi Shi heard this and requested to see him, saying, "Although I am humble and unremarkable, why would you classify me as wild beauty?" Yuan replied, "Your role in the lake incident should indeed be slightly downgraded." Xi Shi argued, "After the fall of Wu, I actually followed Wu Zixu in the river. Who is Fan Li, and would I ever associate with him, for it would betray your kindness and my virtue?" Yuan was startled and said, "Without your words, I almost made a mistake!" Soon after, Wang Zhaojun also came and wept, saying, "Because of my ill fate, I was misled by a court painter and married far away to the desert. Because of your command, I dared not disobey, and I have never forgotten Han for a single day. Yet a sycophantic minister, wielding the pen, defamed me with the charge of having relations with multiple partners. I drink bitterness in the yellow sand, without a chance to clear my name, which is why the grave grass remains uniquely green, to show my loyalty. And Bai Xingjian in his Journey Through the Zhou and Qin Dynasties dared to speak wildly, deeply defaming me, which deeply pains me and fills me with resentment. Please investigate this!" Yuan repeatedly acknowledged his fault and then listed the two under subtle fragrance, and they were pleased and left.

    78

    元既以六品位花神，报于夫人。夫人嘉叹。自是花神岁时，各以花之颜色数目具书于册，闻于使者，使者以上之夫人。如是三年，苑花愈茂。故事：每岁立春先三日，花使请夫人登春台，陈天下林池苑囿之图，乞令诸女散花，以应春令。夫人乃召众花神对于台下，使按图布散，仍考其成于所司。众花神衔命，岁出管春门一度，五日而返。亦有不返，遂留人间者，即出世而为美人者也。

    Explanation

    78

    The Yuan Dynasty had classified the flower gods into six ranks and reported this to the lady. The lady praised and sighed at this. From then on, each year, the flower gods would record the colors and numbers of the flowers in a book, which was then reported to the messenger, who in turn reported it to the lady. In this way, for three years, the garden flowers became more lush. According to tradition, three days before the beginning of spring each year, the flower messenger would invite the lady to ascend the spring platform, where the map of all forests, ponds, and gardens in the world was presented. The messenger would then request the ladies to scatter flowers to comply with the spring command. The lady would then summon the flower gods to gather below the platform and instruct them to distribute the flowers according to the map, while also having the officials examine the results. The flower gods, carrying out the order, would each leave the spring gate once a year, returning after five days. Some, however, did not return and stayed in the human world, becoming beautiful women.

    79

    元以久别山居，欲乞假暂归，遂上书于夫人。其略曰：“窃惟芳草相思，王孙惆帐；垂杨惹恨，游子踯踌。故富贵有乡国之思，即神仙多别离之感。臣本蒭荛，辱在草莽。芝无田而蕙无囿，税驾何乡；岸有芷而汀有兰，寻芳迷路。蓬心不改，自分老于蒿莱，夸包系无闻，谁忆采其葑菲柴关昼掩，忽惊小玉来敲；蓉苑春开，竟使飞琼下请。鹭羽成行，鸾笙作队。现空中之城郭，十二楼台，讶世外之婵娟，三千粉黛。身如桂树，许近嫦娥；命占桃花，更依王母。既陪麟脯之宴，复长蛾眉之班。千古奇逢，三生异数。葵藿倾阳而已遂，藜葭倚玉以何求然而玉枕游仙，梦里之蘼芜莫采，金丹换骨，胸中之荆棘难除。忆阮客之曾归，笑庞公之不返。东篱晚菊，定忆陶潜；南国秋药，尚思张翰。值此风迎杏靥，暂假归鞭；会待雪绽梅妆，重开旧阁。振衣华表，争传化鹤之人；坠舄雪端，窃比飞凫之吏。”

    Explanation

    79

    Mr. Yuan, having been long separated from his mountain retreat, desired to request leave to return temporarily, and thus submitted a letter to his wife. The gist of the letter reads as follows: "I secretly ponder the longing for fragrant grass, the melancholy of a noble youth; the willow trees stirring up resentment, the wanderer hesitating. Therefore, even those of wealth and honor have thoughts of their hometown, and even immortals experience feelings of separation. I am originally a commoner, disgraced in the wilderness. Without fields for the orchid and gardens for the sweet-scented plant, where shall I rest my carriage? With fragrant herbs on the shore and orchids on the sandbank, I seek beauty but get lost. My humble heart remains unchanged, and I consider myself old among the wild plants. Who remembers to pick the humble vegetables? The simple gate is closed during the day, and suddenly I am startled by the knock of Xiao Yu; the beautiful garden of lotus blooms in spring, and even the fairy Fei Qiong comes to invite. The herons' feathers form rows, and the luan sheng instruments play in groups. The city walls appear in the air, the twelve celestial palaces, and I am surprised by the beautiful women beyond the world, the three thousand beauties. My body is like a cinnamon tree, allowed to approach Chang'e; my fate is to occupy the peach blossom, and further rely on the Queen Mother. Having already accompanied the deer meat feast, I again lead the group of beautiful women. A rare encounter throughout history, an extraordinary fate in three lives. The sunflower and lily lean towards the sun and have already achieved their purpose, the thistle and reed lean on jade, what do they seek? However, with the jade pillow of the immortal, the fragrant grass in dreams should not be picked, the golden elixir transforming bones, the thorns in the heart are difficult to remove. Remembering the guest from the state of Ruan who once returned, laughing at Pang Gong who did not return. The late chrysanthemums by the eastern fence, certainly remembering Tao Qian; the autumn medicine of the southern country, still thinking of Zhang Han. At this time of the wind welcoming the cherry cheeks, temporarily borrowing the returning whip; waiting for the snow blooms of plum blossom makeup, reopening the old pavilion. Shaking off clothes on the ornamental pillar, competing to spread the story of the person who transforms into a crane; the fallen shoes at the snow tip, secretly comparing myself to the official who flies like a duck."

    80
