    Afterwards, he rose to the position of prefect, often thinking of Lady Fu's words, and then retired from office, practicing the art of daoyin. One day, he bid farewell to his family, laughed loudly, and threw himself into the Luo River, dying. Several years later, someone saw him floating on the water with several beautiful women.
    Zhou Sisheng
    Zhou Sisheng was a scholar from Jinxi, known for his good deeds. At night, he often dreamed of being the city god of a certain county. One night, as he was folding the records of the underworld, he suddenly heard that the heavenly list had been issued. He hurried to see it, finding white paper with black writing, resembling a human provincial list. He saw his name listed as the top candidate, with a red seal over his surname, though it was somewhat blurry and not very clear. Also, his fellow townspeople were all listed. After waking up, he remembered it and told others about it. Later, when the provincial examination results were announced, there was no news of his success. He later learned that the top candidate was Yan Sisheng, and thus understood the reason behind the red seal. Everything else matched what was said.
    The Cross-Over
    Scholar Chen from the east was reading at night when he heard the quarreling between a woman and her mother-in-law from the neighboring house. The neighbor returned home and slapped his wife, who cried softly and continuously. Soon after, there was a splash with a sound outside the door. He suspected that the woman had drowned herself in anger, and hurriedly called his family to rescue her, indeed pulling someone up from the water.

    118

    视之，乃数年前其家佣工也，犹呓语曰：“聘金五十，已在腰缠。但欲一见新妇，何久不出？”既而恚曰：“谁败吾事耶？”既而则又拱手作揖别状，鼾然睡去。乃挞其面而醒之，则四顾彷徨，惊起骇走曰：“何遽至此此何处耶”陈语之故，且诘其自。
    　　佣恍然曰：“吾昨过艾家桥，见友人某乙跨黑卫而来，盖去冬溺死桥下者，吾殊忘之。渠问我：‘欲得妇否？有少妇欲嫁，须聘若干。吾为君媒之。’遂与俱至一人家。而跨卫者忽怒曰：‘竖子败吾事！’叩其故，不答，愤恨而去。吾逐而送之，乃竟问诸水滨，殊不觉耳。忆所聘者，即君东邻某妇也。”陈亟使人觇之，则妇已投缳垂绝久矣，救之得苏。
    　　是夜陈既寝，闻跨卫者来窗外，且哭且詈。陈怖不敢出。鸡鸣始去。
    　　非非子曰：君子之恶鬼，宜哉，溺而求代，几死者二人，不已名乎吾闻古有鬼媒，以人为之，其事甚怪，殆谲者托以射利耳。今鬼也，而媒人使自委禽焉。谲斯极矣！

    Explanation

    118

    A Man Encountering a Ghostly Servant

    Upon seeing him, it turned out to be a servant from several years ago in his family, still muttering incoherently, he said, "The dowry of fifty was already in my waistband. But I just want to see the bride, why has she not come out?" Afterwards, he angrily said, "Who ruined my plan?" Then he made a gesture of bowing and parting, and fell asleep snoring. The man slapped his face to wake him up, and the servant looked around in confusion, jumped up in fright, and ran away, saying, "Why am I here? Where am I?" The man explained the situation to him and asked where he had come from.

    The servant suddenly realized, "I passed by Ai Family Bridge yesterday and saw my friend Mr. Yi riding a black steed. He was the one who drowned in the bridge last winter, and I had completely forgotten about him. He asked me, 'Do you want a wife? There is a young woman who wants to marry, and she requires a dowry of a certain amount. I will be your matchmaker.' So we went together to a house. But the one riding the steed suddenly became angry and said, 'You ruined my plan!' When I asked him why, he didn't answer, and left in anger. I followed him and sent him off, but I didn't realize that I had reached the water's edge. Now I remember that the woman I was supposed to marry was the lady who lives east of your house." The man quickly sent someone to check on her, and it turned out that the woman had already hanged herself and was nearly dead, but she was saved.

    That night, after the man had gone to bed, he heard the one riding the steed come to the window, crying and cursing. The man was too frightened to get out of bed. The ghostly figure only left at cockcrow.

    Non-Nonzi said: It is indeed appropriate for gentlemen to hate ghosts. To drown and seek a replacement, nearly two people died. In ancient times, there was a ghost matchmaker, and people would act as matchmakers for them. The story is quite strange, and it is likely that a trickster used it to seek profit. Now, the ghost is making the matchmaker himself arrange the marriage. This is the ultimate form of deceit.

    119

    白衣妇人
    　　临汝之东，一水湛然，游鳞可数。然深浅冀测，虽善泅者不能穷其底也。
    　　尝有少年偕数人出游，见二白衣妇人甚娟雅、立于水旁鼓掌大笑，因往就之，欲诘其故。二妇人遂推少年入水，妇人亦入。众皆惶惑，半晌，少年复奋坟波而出，妇人亦出，皆鼓掌大笑。少年遍体淋漓，妇人白衣略不沾濡。
    　　又推少年之兄入，妇人亦复入。入之，竟不复出臭。众骇甚，皆大哭。少年独笑不止，谓众曰：“彼甚乐，尚何哭为”问其状，终身不肯言。
    　　葛衣人
    　　江宁江进士之京师，止逆旅小饮。时密雪严寒，折绵冰酒。忽见户外一葛衣人过，頎然而长，跣足行雪中，了无寒色。江异之，前叩其姓氏，不答。又问“客寒乎”，亦不答。又问“客饮酒乎”，乃点首者再。遂引入旅舍。饮至无算，不醉。复进食，食至无算，亦不饱。而终席都无一语，状类喑哑。江愈奇之。次日将行，请客俱，摇首勿许。遂辞别。

    Explanation

    119

    A White-Clothed Woman

    To the east of Linru, there is a clear stream where fish can be counted. However, despite its apparent shallowness, even skilled swimmers cannot reach the bottom.

    Once, a young man went out with several companions. They saw two white-clad women standing by the water, clapping and laughing heartily. Curious, the young man approached them, wanting to ask why they were laughing. The two women then pushed the young man into the water, and they entered the water as well. Everyone was startled, and after a while, the young man emerged from the water, followed by the women, all of whom clapped and laughed. The young man was drenched, but the women remained dry.

    The women then pushed the young man's brother into the water, and they entered the water again. This time, the young man's brother never came out of the water. Everyone was terrified and began to weep. The young man alone continued to laugh, saying to the others, "They were very happy, so why should we cry?" When asked about what had happened, he never spoke of it again.

    The Hemp-Clothed Man

    A scholar from Jiangning went to the capital city and stayed at an inn for a light drink. It was a time of heavy snow and severe cold, and they drank wine that was as cold as ice. Suddenly, they saw a man wearing a hemp robe passing by outside. He was tall and slender, walking barefoot in the snow without showing any signs of cold. The scholar was amazed and asked him his name, but the man did not respond. The scholar then asked, "Are you cold?" The man did not answer. The scholar asked again, "Would you like to drink?" The man nodded twice. The scholar then invited him into the inn. The man drank a lot but did not get drunk. He also ate a great deal but did not feel full. Throughout the meal, he remained silent, appearing as if he were mute. The scholar found this even more remarkable. The next day, when the man was about to leave, he asked the guests to join him, but they shook their heads and refused. The man then took his leave.

    120

    行三日，至一处，葛衣人忽至，渭江曰：“君见大宽衣大笠、短棒荷笼性、遥立道旁者乎？”江曰：“见之，一僧也。彼何为”曰：“今夜三鼓，飞刃取君首者，即渠也。”江胆丧，伏地求救。客曰：“吾在，固无畏。渠果来，膏吾斧矣。”乃戒江熟寝勿惊。
    　　至夜半，客提僧头掷地上，曰：“莽髡无礼，吾已杀之。然亦君挟资太重，为渠所觊耳。”江初讳之。客曰：“君囊中白金若干，黄金若干，封识何状，藏置何所，胡乃欺也”江大惊失色，连曰唯唯。客曰：“挟此何为？”江曰：“欲往投某公门下，以此为贽耳。”客艴然怒曰：“咄！汝固蝇营若此哉！吾目眯，误识尔，悔不教和尚杀尔”言罢，提僧头越屋而去。时星光黯淡，顷刻无踪。

    Explanation

    120

    After three days of travel, they arrived at a place where a man in a coarse cloth garment suddenly appeared. Wei Jiang asked, "Have you seen the person with a large loose robe, a wide hat, carrying a short stick and a basket, standing far off by the roadside?" Jiang replied, "Yes, he is a monk. What is he doing?" The man said, "At the third watch of the night, the one who will come to take your head with a flying blade is him." Jiang was terrified and knelt on the ground to beg for help. The guest said, "I am here, so there is no need to be afraid. If he does come, I will cut him with my axe." He then instructed Jiang to sleep soundly and not be alarmed.

    At midnight, the guest pulled the monk's head and threw it on the ground, saying, "This rude and disrespectful monk has been killed by me. However, it is also because you carry too much wealth, which he coveted." Jiang initially tried to deny it. The guest said, "How much white gold and how much gold do you have in your bag? What is the condition of the seal, and where is it stored? Why are you lying to me?" Jiang was greatly alarmed and said, "Yes, yes." The guest asked, "What are you carrying this for?" Jiang replied, "I want to go and serve under a certain official, and this is as a gift or offering." The guest became angry and said, "Hmph! You are so greedy for such small gains! I mistakenly recognized you, and I regret not having the monk kill you." After saying this, he pulled the monk's head over the house and left. The stars were dim, and in an instant, he was gone.

    121

    江惭且惧，遂不复至京师而返。
    　　过阴
    　　野老常言：阴牒勾人，往往有生人为之者，谓之过阴。其人言语饮食，了不异人，但就睡则嗒焉若丧，呼之不能觉，盖其过阴时也。榻下双履，必一仰一覆，尽仰其履则觉，尽覆其履则死不复返。故每寝必扃其户。惧为人所弄也。后一月谁当死者，辄先知之，预见阴牒也。
    　　有村民周氏，役此有年矣。一日独坐，汪然出涕。家人怪问之。曰：“吾弟莱日死矣。吾欲生之而不能也。”弟媳跽而请曰：“伯固忍勾其弟耶盍谋所以代之”民曰：“诺。然必得生辰符合者，使冥司不及察，则弟可生而吾不获谴。顾安所得其人乎？”数日，喜曰：“得之矣，同邑某生是也，方读书山中。吾弗能入其室，闻某日夜归，当俟于途而要之。”
    　　数日，又报曰：“无能为矣！吾俟之南山之阴、亭础之右。某生过焉，吾三前而三踣，重为所困，吾惫矣，无能为矣！”期至而弟果死。

    Explanation

    121

    Final Translation

    Jiang was ashamed and fearful, and thus did not return to the capital but instead went back.

    Overcoming the Shadow Realm

    Elder villagers often say: The shadow decree summons people, and it is often done by living individuals, which is called overcoming the shadow realm. The person's speech and food consumption are no different from others, but when they sleep, they suddenly seem lost, and calling them cannot wake them up; this is when they are being summoned by the shadow realm. Under the bed, a pair of shoes must be one facing up and one facing down. If all the shoes are facing up, they will wake up; if all the shoes are facing down, they will die and not return. Therefore, every time they sleep, they must lock the door, fearing that they will be played with by others. Those who will die in the following month are always known beforehand, as they can predict the shadow decree.

    There was a villager named Zhou who had been performing this for many years. One day, he sat alone, shedding tears. His family members found it strange and asked him. He said, "My younger brother will die tomorrow. I want to save him but cannot." His younger brother's wife knelt and pleaded, "Uncle, how can you summon your younger brother? Why not plan to replace him?" The villager replied, "Yes, but I must find someone with matching birthdates. If the underworld cannot detect it, then my younger brother can live, and I will not be punished. But where can I find such a person?" After several days, he happily said, "I have found him! It is a certain student from the same county, who is studying in the mountains. I cannot enter his room, but I heard that he returns home at night. I will wait on the road and intercept him."

    After several more days, he reported, "I cannot do anything! I waited for him on the north side of the south mountain, at the right side of the pavilion. The student passed by, and I stepped forward three times and fell three times, heavily trapped. I am exhausted, and I cannot do anything!" When the deadline arrived, his younger brother indeed died.

    122

    有知其事者，举以叩某生。生曰：“诚有是。畴昔之夜，吾行亭下而寒噤者三，毛髪皆竖立，其殆是欤”
    　　后十馀年，生补弟子员，今年五十馀矣。友人徐君能举其姓名，姑逸之。
    　　沈翘翘
    　　河南韩生游都下，薄暮独行。见高楼隐隐，粉壁纱窗。有妇女笑语其中，驻步听之。
    　　一女曰：“前日叶子戏，阿姊获几何”一女曰：“三百缗耳。昨复为阿翠掇去。”一女曰：“渠是钱树子，偏耐著花。”一女曰：“连日张公子不来，想醉眠郑九娘处矣。”一女太息曰：“锦绮缠头，金钱买笑，大都冷烟寒月，不足复记忆。近有薄悻郎赠吾二诗，尤可笑也。岂知吾辈多情，乍离眼前，已置脑后，况于形销骨化乎！不然，墓门相思之树，行且成林矣！”诸女皆失笑，问其诗云何。女乃诵诗曰：
    　　“舞衫如蝶鬓如鸦，醉倒城南碧玉家。
    　　一霎红楼嫌梦短，酸风苦雨送梨花。”
    　　“眉敛秋霜冷画屏，崔娘卷里太零丁。

    Explanation

    122

    A person who knew the matter asked it of a certain scholar. The scholar replied, "Truly, it is so. On that night of old, as I walked beneath the pavilion, I shivered three times, and my hair stood on end. Was it perhaps this?"

    More than ten years later, the scholar was enrolled as a student in the Imperial Academy. This year, he is over fifty years old. A friend named Xu could name his surname, but he chose to omit it for now.

    Shen Qiaoqiao

    A man from Henan named Han was traveling in the capital. As dusk fell, he walked alone. He saw a tall building in the distance, with white walls and gauze windows. There were women laughing and talking inside, and he stopped to listen.

    One woman said, "How much did my sister win from the card game the other day?" Another replied, "Three hundred strings of cash. Yesterday, Ah Cui took it away from her." A third woman said, "She is a money tree, especially good at bearing fruit." Another woman sighed and said, "Adorned with fine silk and bought with money, most of the time, it's just cold smoke and cold moon, not worth remembering. Recently, a fickle lover gave me two poems, which are especially laughable. How could we, who are so emotional, forget them as soon as they are out of sight? How much more so, when they are gone forever! Otherwise, the trees of longing at the tomb gate would soon form a forest!" The other women all laughed, and they asked her to recite the poems. She then recited:

    "Dancing dress like a butterfly, hair like a raven, drunk and fallen in the home of the jade maiden of the southern city.
    A moment in the red building, the dream is too short, the bitter wind and rain send away the pear blossoms."

    "Brows gathered like autumn frost, the cold painting screen, in Cui's scroll, too lonely."

    123

    紫萝红杜都寻遍，何处空山墓草青”
    　　韩闻大惊，即其吊亡妓沈翘翘诗也。瞬息间，高楼已失所在，惟白杨萧瑟，夕照熏林。叹息而返，亟搜筪中二诗稿焚之，终身不复幸妓。斯亦迷香洞之闭门羹也。
    　　耳食录卷六
    　　南野社令
    　　桃江之滨有渔者，一人一舟，往来烟水，卖鱼得钱，沽酒独酌。
    　　一夕，明月满江，欤乃既息，有客造舟求饮，渔即引与共酌。问其姓名，客诡以对。于是谈风说雨，相得甚欢。天将曙，客始辞去。至夜复来。渔是日得鱼，倍于往日，沽酒亦倍之。复与客畅饮，无少吝色。客笑曰：“君可谓得鱼而不忘筌矣。虽然，君贶吾酒而不费，我贡君鱼而不劳，可谓相须亦复相济。”渔愕然不解所谓，客从容曰：“君勿怖，吾溺鬼也。今日之鱼我所致，所以报昨夕之惠也。此后当日日为之，少佐壶觞耳。”渔素豪旷，闻而乐之。自是捕鱼辄盈网罟，皆鬼力也。昼则捕鱼买酒，夜则与鬼豪饮，鸡鸣而罢。近半载桌。

    Explanation

    123

    In search of purple roses and red peonies, where can one find the green grass of an empty mountain tomb?
    Han was greatly surprised upon hearing this, for it was the poem he had written in memory of the deceased courtesan Shen Qiaoqiao. In an instant, the tall building had vanished, leaving only the sound of the white poplar trees rustling in the breeze and the evening sun warming the forest. He sighed and returned, urgently searching for and burning the two poetry drafts in the bamboo tube, vowing never to visit courtesans again. This was indeed the "closed-door soup" of the misty fragrance cave.
    Ear Shí Lù, Volume Six
    Southern Wilderness Social Commander
    By the banks of the Tao River, there was a fisherman who lived alone in a boat, coming and going through the misty waters, selling fish to earn money and buying wine to drink alone.
    One night, the full moon shone over the river, and the fisherman had just rested. A guest came to the boat asking for a drink, and the fisherman invited him to join in the drinking. When asked his name, the guest gave a false answer. They then engaged in idle conversation and got along very well. As dawn approached, the guest finally took his leave. That night, he returned again. On that day, the fisherman caught more fish than usual and bought twice as much wine. He again drank heartily with the guest, showing no signs of stinginess. The guest laughed and said, "You can be said to have caught the fish and not forgotten the basket. Although you gave me wine without spending, I gave you fish without labor, we can be said to need each other and also help each other." The fisherman was startled and did not understand what he meant. The guest calmly said, "Do not be afraid, I am a drowned ghost. The fish today were brought to you by me, to repay the kindness of last night. From now on, you will catch fish every day, and I will help you a little with your drinking." The fisherman, who was usually bold and unrestrained, was delighted to hear this. From then on, whenever he fished, his nets were always full, all by the power of ghosts. During the day, he fished and bought wine, and at night, he drank heartily with the ghost until the rooster crowed. Almost half a year had passed.

    124

    一夕，饮半酣，鬼色不豫。诘之，乃曰：“明日受代，行与君别矣。”词甚凄恻，渔亦惘然。
    　　明日伺之，有一妇人携幼子而来，既及河干，自投于水。子恋母，亦从之。渔心知鬼之所为，殊为之悲恻，欲救之面无从也。少顷，妇人复携子冲波而出，迤逦上岸去，若有自下捧之者。心转讶之，谓鬼之不能祸也。
    　　比夜，鬼复来，曰：“吾今日本当得代，然毙一妇人，并戕其子，吾不忍为，宁终处水国，隶于波臣之籍耳。故复得盘桓于君前。”渔益敬之，谓其已死而仁心特厚也。因纵酒欢呼，订交莫逆。
    　　又数年，鬼复辞去，曰：“吾前者一念之善，冥王嘉之，已为转奏上帝。得授南野某村社令，明日走马赴任矣。君倘念故人，宜来相访。虽不能复见，然必有以待君也。”渔许之，且问不复见之故。鬼曰：“此非吾所能主也。”遂殷勤晒泪而别。

    Explanation

    124

    One night, after drinking to half-intoxication, the ghost's appearance was unhappy. When questioned, he said, "Tomorrow I will be replaced, and I will be leaving you." His words were very sad, and the fisherman felt dazed.

    The next day, the fisherman watched as a woman came with her young child. They reached the riverbank and the woman threw herself into the water. The child clung to his mother and followed her. The fisherman knew that the ghost was responsible, and he was greatly saddened, wanting to save them but unable to do so. In a short while, the woman and child swam against the waves and emerged, as if someone from below was supporting them. The fisherman was surprised and believed that the ghost could not harm them.

    By night, the ghost returned and said, "Today I should have been replaced, but I killed a woman and her child. I could not bear to do it, so I decided to stay in the water realm, belonging to the register of water spirits. Therefore, I can linger before you again." The fisherman respected him more, believing that even though he was already dead, he had a particularly kind heart. They drank freely and cheered, making a pact of inseparability.

    After several years, the ghost said his farewells, saying, "My former good deed was praised by the Lord of the Underworld, who has already reported it to the Heavenly Emperor. I have been granted the position of village official in a village in the south field, and I will ride to take office tomorrow. If you miss an old friend, you should come to visit. Although we cannot see each other again, I will certainly have something waiting for you." The fisherman agreed and asked why they would not see each other again. The ghost said, "This is not something I can control." He then bid farewell with tears.

    125

    越数日，渔棹舟龙南。至某村，求社令之祠而造焉，则村民相待于路，闻渔至，则皆讶且喜。渔问故，村民皆曰：“昨梦社公言：“明日吾故人来访，尔当迎于郊，为我作东道主人，慎毋慢客也。”故先俟于此。”亦叩渔所以访社令之故。渔具告之，莫不嗟异。
    　　既引渔至祠，设香楮蜡炬茶酒鸡鱼之供。渔捧香醁酒，拜祝曰：“故人别来无恙今受祀兹乡，不忧馁而，故人仁厚爱物，亦宜有大造于兹乡也。惟是澄江静夜，孤岸扁舟，无复素心人来共杯杓矣！”言讫，不觉泣下。忽有香风起于神座，拂渔衣袂，飘飘举动，他人则否。
    　　于是观者咸异之，竞邀至家，劳以酒食，数日不能周，且各有钱帛之赠，皆体神意也。渔将归，辞于神。复有香风送之，至舟而后散。
    　　渔每数年一往，神异如初。
    　　廊下物

    Explanation

    125

    After several days, the fisherman steered his boat south to Longnan. Upon arriving at a certain village, he sought out the shrine of the village god and proceeded there. The villagers were awaiting him on the road, and upon hearing that the fisherman had arrived, they were all surprised and delighted. The fisherman asked for the reason, and the villagers all said, "Last night, in a dream, the village god said, 'Tomorrow, my old friend will visit. You should welcome him at the outskirts and serve as the host. Be careful not to treat the guest poorly.' That is why we waited here beforehand." They also inquired as to why the fisherman had come to visit the village god. The fisherman explained everything to them, and no one failed to marvel.

    Once the fisherman was led to the shrine, they set up offerings of incense, paper money, candles, tea, wine, chicken, and fish. The fisherman held the incense and wine, bowed, and prayed, saying, "Old friend, I hope you are well since our last meeting. Now that you are honored in this village and no longer worry about hunger, it is fitting that you bring great benefit to this village. Yet, in the clear river at night, on a lone boat on the shore, there is no longer anyone with pure intentions to share cups with!" After finishing his prayer, he could not help but cry. Suddenly, a fragrant wind arose from the god's seat and touched the fisherman's clothes, moving him gently, but not others.

    The onlookers were all amazed, and they all invited him to their homes, treating him to food and drink. For several days, they could not exhaust their invitations, and each gave him money and cloth as gifts, all acting according to the god's will. When the fisherman was about to leave, he bid farewell to the god. Again, a fragrant wind accompanied him, reaching his boat and then dispersing.

    Every few years, the fisherman would return, and the god's wonders remained as before.
    Objects under the eaves.

    126

    某甲晨起，见一物状如人，一身两首，自项而分，卧于阶下，鼻声鼾然。甲大惊而呼，家众毕集，以杖击之。物惊觉而起。视其两首，耳目口鼻并与人同，但一面衰老，一面夭少，老者惨但，少者欢愉。众咸骇异。物两口并言曰：“我不足异。落瓠山有馗馗者乃异耳，我当迎以来。”言罢，自檐间跃去。
    　　半晌，偕一物至，立于阶前，人身而九面环肩而生，大如拳，状亦不一。有嘻笑者，有哭泣者，有喜者，有怒者，有愁者，有闭目睡者，有倾耳听者，有言语者，有静默若凝思者。既见人，亦不惊避。亦不近身。而两首者立其旁，伺之甚恭，若厮役然。九面者谓众人曰：“我不足异，何不请吾頵頵来”于是俱去。
    　　众方嗟讶间，见前二物导一物自门而入。其首乃多至无数，丛生侧出若花瓣。或仰或俯，或侧或欹，悉大如桃核，妍媸杂见，奇正互出。语言嘈杂不能辨，少顷，变形异相，则众人之貌悉具，无异纤毫。众相顾各错愕，喧传一里。

    Explanation

    126

    A certain man named Jia woke up in the morning and saw a creature that looked like a human, with one body and two heads, split from the neck, lying on the steps, snoring. Jia was greatly startled and shouted, and his family members gathered, striking the creature with a stick. The creature woke up and stood up. Looking at its two heads, its ears, eyes, mouth, and nose were all the same as those of a human, but one side was old and withered, while the other side was young and vibrant. The old side appeared sad, while the young side seemed joyful. Everyone was startled and amazed. The creature spoke with both mouths, saying, "I am not extraordinary. The creature named Kuikui in Luo Hu Mountain is extraordinary, I will welcome it here." After speaking, it jumped away from under the eaves.

    After a while, it arrived with another creature, standing in front of the steps, with a human body and nine faces growing around its shoulders, each as large as a fist, and each face had a different appearance. Some were laughing, some were crying, some were happy, some were angry, some were sad, some were sleeping with closed eyes, some were listening intently, some were speaking, and some were silent as if deep in thought. After seeing people, it did not startle away or approach them. The two-headed creature stood by its side, waiting respectfully, like a servant. The nine-faced creature said to everyone, "I am not extraordinary, why not invite my creature Kuikui?" Then they all left.

    Just as everyone was exclaiming in surprise, they saw the two creatures leading another creature into the house through the door. Its head was so numerous that it could not be counted, growing in clusters and sprouting sideways like flower petals. Some were upright, some were downward, some were sideways, and some were leaning. All were as large as peach pits, beautiful and ugly mixed together, unusual and ordinary alternating. The speech was noisy and indistinguishable. A short while later, it changed form and appearance, and everyone's likeness was perfectly present, not differing even by a hair. Everyone looked at each other, each astonished, and the news spread by word of mouth for a li.

    127

    忽廊下有双足自地伸出，须臾及手，又须臾及肩，跃然立起，首大于瓮，并无七孔。于是三物见之惊走，此物逐之，疾于隼鸟，顷刻失所往。竟不知为何怪。
    　　秋心山人
    　　吕生者，名并柏，维扬人。少贫而孤，邃于学。有中表余高甫，其妹玉簪者，国色也，且善属文，与生同岁。两人自幼相处，为儿嬉戏，聪颖绝人，而相得亦迥异于他儿。玉母尝笑曰：“好一对能言鹦鹉！”时两人方九岁，闻而解之，相爱因愈笃，俨然雌雄之依倚也。十二三岁，便各能诗。唱和酬答之间，每有多情语。年十五，玉父约之甚严，生至其家，不使复见。两人之怨自此始。玉每于帘下窥生，动至永日，生亦为之神魂寓舍也。屡托媒者致辞，而余氏以生困弱，坚不许。

    Explanation

    127

    Suddenly, two feet emerged from the ground, soon followed by hands and then shoulders. It leaped up, its head larger than a jar, and it had no seven apertures. The three objects saw it and fled in terror. This creature chased them, faster than a falcon, and they vanished in an instant. No one knew what kind of creature it was.

    Autumn Heart Hermit

    Lu Sheng, whose name is Binbai, is from Weiyang. He was poor and orphaned but deeply learned. He had a cousin named Yu Gaofu, whose sister, Yu Zanzan, was of extraordinary beauty and skilled in writing. They were the same age, and they grew up together, playing as children, both exceptionally intelligent and very different from other children. Yu Zanzan's mother once laughed and said, "What a pair of talking parrots!" At the age of nine, they understood the joke and became even more devoted to each other, like a pair of birds. By the age of twelve or thirteen, they could each write poetry. In their exchanges of poems, they often used affectionate language. At the age of fifteen, Yu Zanzan's father strictly forbade Lu Sheng from visiting her home. Their relationship began to deteriorate from this point. Yu Zanzan would often secretly watch Lu Sheng through the curtains, sometimes for entire days, and Lu Sheng was also captivated by her. Lu Sheng repeatedly asked matchmakers to propose marriage, but Yu Zanzan's family refused because they considered him too weak and poor.

    128

    积半载，生以文会过高甫。玉徘徊帘下，乘间以函书投。生于密处拆视之，其略曰：“自二年来，会绝踪疏，眼中千里。每恨岁月淹驰，妹将笄而兄且冠，不复如垂髫时旦夕左右、形影无猜也。向使此身常童年稚齿，则相见相依，亦何至避若仇雠、视同行路豹以文章而深隐，翠以毛羽而高飞。吾两人者，何以异此哉乃音临风独叹，向月孤吟，吊影无端，赏音谁是诗思逐槁木同枯，人面与落花俱瘦。回忆簸钱堂下。总髻床前，言笑宛转之时，殆恍然如隔世矣使妹而虎头麟角，便可订笔砚之交，兄而蝉鬓蛾眉，亦可作闺房之侣。安见韩张不可并称，而莘昭不可嗣响乎奈何势异松萝，嫌防瓜李，天实限制，夫复何言彼谗言间北山之鸟，精卫填东海之波，亦可以喻此素抱也。愿与有心人共鸣之。小妹玉簪敛衽言。”

    Explanation

    128

    After six months, I used my writing to meet with Gao Fu. Jade (my sister) lingered by the curtain, taking advantage of an opportunity to slip a letter through. I secretly opened and read it, and it roughly said: "Since two years ago, our meetings have become sparse, and it feels like we are separated by a thousand miles. I often lament the passage of time, as my sister is about to come of age and I am about to come of age, no longer able to be together as we were in childhood, trusting each other without suspicion. If only I could remain a child, then we could meet and rely on each other, and why would we avoid each other like enemies or treat each other like strangers? I hide away through writing, while you soar high with your talents. What is different about us compared to this? I sigh alone in the wind, sing alone to the moon, look at my shadow without purpose, and wonder who is the true appreciator of my talent. My poetic thoughts wither along with dry wood, and my face and the falling flowers both wither. Recalling the courtyard where we played with coins and the bed where we braided our hair, when we laughed and spoke so gently, it feels like it happened in another life. If only my sister had a tiger's head and a unicorn's horn, we could have become close friends through writing; if only I had a lady's delicate hair and eyebrows, we could have been companions in the inner chambers. How can we not compare ourselves to Han and Zhang, and not follow in the footsteps of Shen and Zhao? Why are our circumstances different from those of the pine and vine, and why are we suspicious of each other like the melon and plum trees? It is truly the will of heaven that limits us, what more can I say? Those slanderous words are like the birds of the North Mountain, and like Jingwei filling the East Sea with stones, they can also be used to describe my persistent feelings. I hope to resonate with someone who understands. My younger sister, Jade Hairpin, respectfully says."

    129

    生省书挥涕，亟具回笺。其词不记，大约以徐田之言相慰喻，而凄激之音亦复形露腕下也。后屡以诗词自帘下掷递。其家微觉之，防愈密。生不得意，快怏而归，而玉乃自此病矣。生闻之，愈增怆切，愁绪百端，臞鹤哀鸿，强支风日。托故造余氏，访其病状，则已沉睡衾帷，断浆绝粒矣。生悲恻之情，形于颜面，而玉父母兄弟颇厌之。于是晦迹不复去。

    Explanation

    129

    Sheng Xingshu wiped his eyes with tears, quickly prepared a reply. The exact words are not remembered, but they were intended to comfort and console using the words of Xu Tian, and the mournful and intense tone was also revealed in his writing. Later, he would often throw poems and verses from behind the curtain. Her family noticed this subtly and became more cautious. Sheng Xingshu left in a gloomy mood, and it was then that Yu fell ill. Upon hearing this, Sheng Xingshu felt even more sorrowful and anxious, overwhelmed by a hundred worries, like a thin crane and a grieving swan, forcing himself through the wind and sun. He excused himself to visit the Yu family to inquire about her condition, and found her deeply asleep in bed, refusing food and water. His sorrowful feelings were visible on his face, and Yu's parents and siblings were quite repulsed by him. Thus, he concealed his tracks and did not go again.

    130

    闻有秋心山人者，善君平之术，能前知，因往卜之。山人曰：“尔两人皆散仙也，偶涉花月之戒，上帝怒谪于人间，相慕悦已三世矣。第一世，君为临海人，姓白氏，玉为邯郸人，姓伍氏。两家同官于粤西，伍因上巳踏青郊外，与君相见，两人俱感情而亡。第二世，君与玉同生于豫章，君姓元业商，玉为卖粉者王某之女。君尝市粉其家，见而相悦。屡过之，得通辞焉。玉坐是而殒，君亦以郁结死。今两人之算，亦垂尽矣，然尚有一见之缘。若忍而不见，君可延三年之寿。”生曰：“吾宁惮速死而割情于彼美乎顾何由得见，愿先生教之。”山人曰：“三日后，巳午之间，待于某村佛寺。玉当以祷病而来，可得一晤。此后即长别耳。”

    Explanation

    130

    A fortune-teller known as Autumn Heart was skilled in the techniques of Zou Yan, able to foretell the future. The man sought him out for a reading. The fortune-teller said, "You two are both dispersed immortals who, having accidentally violated the taboo of flowers and moon, were exiled to the human world by the Supreme Deity. You have been mutually attracted to each other for three lifetimes. In your first life, you were both from Linhai, and you bore the surnames Bai and Wu, respectively. Your families served together in Guangxi. Wu, on the Upper Yuan Festival, went out into the countryside for a spring outing and met you. Both of you died of love. In your second life, you were both born in Yuzhang. You bore the surnames Yuan and Wang, respectively. You were a merchant, and she was the daughter of a woman who sold flour. You often visited her home to buy flour, and you fell in love. After many visits, you managed to communicate with each other. She died because of this, and you also died of heartbreak. Now, your lifespans are nearly exhausted, but you still have one chance to meet. If you endure and do not see each other, you can extend your life by three years." The man replied, "I would rather die quickly and sever ties with that beauty. How can I meet her? I wish the Master would instruct me." The fortune-teller said, "In three days, between 9:00 AM and 11:00 AM, wait at a temple in a certain village. She will come to pray for a cure and you can meet. After this, it will be a long farewell."

    131

    生如言，粟见玉偕母同舆而至，羸质娇姿，神韵酸楚。生前拜母曰：“柏亦以祈病至此，讵意相遇。”因揖玉曰：“阿妹珍重”不觉相视涕横，言词呜咽。母大怪怒，促女归，遂吞声登车而去。生肝肠寸裂，饮泣归家。沈晕数四，卧不能起。
    　　已而传玉讣音至，不觉抚膺大恸曰：“玉妹玉妹，一至此乎！妹先我而死，我何可以后妹而生虽然，一息尚存，不可无一言以告妹也。”乃力疾揽笔，书《忆秦娥》一阙云：
    　　准能补，蝉鱼烛断姻缘簿。姻缘簿，珠楼人去。彩云黄土。此身不作芙蓉主，三生石上相思苦。相思苦，琵琶凄断，数声鹦鹉。

    Explanation

    131

    According to the narrative, "粟" and "玉" arrived together with their mother in a carriage. "玉" had a delicate and graceful appearance, but her demeanor was melancholic. Before "生" (a person's name) bowed to his mother, he said, "柏 has come here to pray for illness, and I did not expect to meet you." He then bowed to "玉" and said, "Take care, sister." Unable to hold back his tears, they looked at each other, and their words were choked with sobs. The mother was greatly surprised and angry, urging "玉" to return home, so "玉" left silently in the carriage. "生" was heartbroken, weeping silently as he returned home. He fainted several times and was unable to get up.

    Later, the news of "玉"'s death reached him. He could not help but clutch his chest in great sorrow, saying, "玉妹, how could it come to this? You died before me, how can I live after you? Although I am still alive, I cannot go without telling you one thing." He then, despite his illness, picked up a pen and wrote a poem titled "忆秦娥":

    Who can make up for it? The register of fates is broken by the death of a cicada and a fish. The register of fates, the jade tower is empty. Colorful clouds turn to yellow earth. This body is not the master of the lotus, the stone of three lives bears the pain of longing. The pain of longing, the lute is broken by sorrow, and the sound of a parrot echoes.

    132

    投笔而绝。
    　　两人赠答之诗，不下数十首。不能尽记，今录其数章。玉赠生七律云：
    　　万丈愁丝一寸心，暮云庭院冷花阴。
    　　纵横玉筋灯前泪，怨乱霓裳月下琴。
    　　紫燕巢孤春寂寂，青鸾信杳夜沈沈。
    　　上宫独处东邻老，命薄神伤自古今。
    　　生和之曰：
    　　娇莺啼乱惜花心，日转西楼落晚阴。艳曲未调风月笛，朱弦欲断凤凰琴。那堪路远天难问，只觉情深海可沉。悔恨十年空倚玉，岂知辽阔似如今。
    　　生之七绝二首云：
    　　隔面垂帘两地孤，洞庭云水渺苍梧。
    　　分明帘是湘妃竹，犹带当年血泪枯。
    　　自从冷泪化啼鹃，倏忽黄花九月天。
    　　一枕新愁不成梦，秋风秋雨夜如年。
    　　五言律云
    　　才子休名玉，佳人莫姓苏。泪浇白雪曲，尘汙璇玑田。自昔愁如此，于今恨更殊。盈盈伤一水，牛女望河乌。

    Explanation

    132

    Throwing down the pen and severing ties.

    The two exchanged poems, not fewer than dozens, cannot all be remembered. Now, a few chapters are recorded. Jade gave a seven-character regulated verse to Life:

    A thousand-foot-long sorrow thread in an inch of heart,
    A courtyard shrouded in evening clouds and cold flower shadows.
    Tears streaming like jade before a lamp,
    A discordant melody played on a moonlit night.

    A solitary swallow's nest in spring, empty and desolate,
    A distant message from a mythical bird, the night deep and profound.
    Alone in a high palace or eastern neighbor, aged and sorrowful,
    A thin fate and divine sorrow since ancient times.

    Life responded with:

    A nightingale's lament for a withered heart,
    The sun setting westward in the west pavilion, shadows lengthening.
    Unplayed wind and moon flute,
    A zither's strings about to break, a phoenix's lament.

    How can one ask about distant paths and difficult questions,
    Only feeling that the depth of emotion can sink into the sea.
    Regretting ten years of empty jade,
    How could I have known it was so vast now.

    Life's two seven-character absolute verses:

    Behind the curtain, two hearts separated,
    The Dongting Lake and the Cangwu mountains, vast and distant.
    The curtain is clearly made of bamboo associated with the Xiang River goddess,
    Still bearing the traces of past blood and tears.

    Since cold tears transformed into the cries of cuckoos,
    Suddenly, yellow flowers in September.
    A pillow of new sorrow preventing sleep,
    Autumn wind and rain, a night like a year.

    A five-character regulated verse:

    A talented man should not be named Yu,
    A beautiful woman should not be surnamed Su.
    Tears watering a white snow melody,
    Dust polluting the celestial fields.
    Since ancient times, sorrow has been like this,
    Today's hatred is even more unique.
    The river's separation, a pain like the Cowherd and the Weaver Girl,
    Looking across the river, the river bird as a bridge.

    133

    又有《忆玉满庭芳》词云：
    　　粉蝶韩凭，金蝉齐女，飞来梓泽平泉。春花秋树，伴侣过年年。一旦缚蝉驱蝶，伤离恨，冷露凄烟。空惆怅，阑干曲处，魂梦永相牵。堪怜！犹得记画眉低偃，恰趁花钿。更一捻纤腰，斜倚风前。别后知他愁绝，娇模样，曾否依然休肠断，昔时儿戏，也算好姻缘。
    　　玉五绝二首云：
    　　郎莫牵红丝，妾莫题红叶。媒妁倩滕王，画为双蛱蝶。
    　　消恨桃花红，忘忧萱草绿。翻惊忧恨多，站在相思木。
    　　绝笔七言绝句云：
    　　芙蓉凋谢可怜秋，一霎西风下土游。认得旧时王母鹤，来迎侍女返瀛洲。
    　　又有警句，如：“花缘才子落，月向美人残。”“佳句兼愁写，深情倩梦传。”“鬓丝燕草乱，眉黛楚山攒。”“世间大恨归儿女，天上飞仙怕别离。”“心似沉檀炉内火，人如桃李镜中花。”云云，皆可喜。

    Explanation

    133

    There is another poem titled "Remembrance of Yu in the Full Courtyard of Fragrance" which goes:
    Butterflies, like Han Peng, and golden cicadas, like Qi girls, flew to Zizi Ze and Pingquan. Spring flowers and autumn trees, companions for year after year. One day, the cicadas were bound and the butterflies driven away, causing a heart-wrenching separation, cold dew and mournful smoke. In vain, one laments, at the curve of the railing, the soul and dreams forever intertwined. Pity! Yet, one can still remember the delicate beauty of the painted eyebrows, fitting with the floral decorations. Even more, the slender waist, slightly leaning against the wind. After parting, one knows his sorrow is endless, his charming appearance, has it remained the same? Do not break your heart, the childish play of old, was also a good match.

    Two five-character poems by Yu go:
    The young man should not entangle with red threads, the young woman should not inscribe on red leaves. The matchmakers, like Prince Teng, painted two butterflies. The red peach blossoms, to dispel hatred, the green daylilies, to forget worries. Suddenly, one is shocked by the multitude of sorrows and worries, standing at the tree of longing. The final seven-character poem goes:
    The lotus has withered, pitifully in the autumn, a sudden west wind, sweeping down to the earth. Recognizing the old white crane of the Queen Mother, coming to welcome the attendant girl back to Penglai. There are also cautionary phrases, such as: "Flowers fall for talented men, the moon wanes for beautiful women." "Beautiful lines are written with sorrow, deep emotions are conveyed through dreams." "The hair and eyebrows are disheveled, the eyebrows gathered like the mountains of Chu." "The greatest sorrows belong to children, even celestial beings fear separation." "The heart is like the fire in the incense burner, the person like the reflected flowers in the mirror." All of these are delightful.

    134

    非非子曰：古称才于数奇、佳人薄命。未有甚于两人者也，积三生之遇，无一笑之缘。而世之愚夫愚妇共衾同穴者，比比皆是。岂天之厄仙人欤抑厄才子佳人欤
    　　倚户女子
    　　有商人日暮间行，见一女子倚户而立，丰态楚楚。商注目久之，女遂招之入。
    　　旁有过者见之，以为私期也，告其邻里，图执之。邻里讶曰：“此实空宅，安有女子其必魅也。”
    　　率数人入宅索之，果无人迹。唯东偏一室，古棺在焉，尘埃封积。觉棺盖旁缝开裂。微露衣襟。共启视之，则一人覆卧枯骨上；乃所见商人也，已绝矣。求其亲属而归之。瘗古棺于土。
    　　秦少府
    　　蒲郡秦少府，官常州。其子秀，少而能文，与常州殷生者相善。
    　　殷妻美而早卒，葬之郭外。殷感伤不己，冀其魂见于梦中，祷之三年而不应。
    　　秦少府卒于官，宦囊萧索，秀无力奔丧，移榇佛寺，托家口于殷生，而自往求亲旧之官姑苏者。

    Explanation

    134

    Non-Nonzi said: Anciently it was said that talented people often encountered misfortune, and beautiful women had short lives. There has never been anyone more unfortunate than these two, accumulating three lifetimes of encounters, yet lacking even a single smile of affection. Yet in the world, foolish men and women share a bed and a grave, abundant and plentiful. Is it heaven that is afflicting sages, or is it afflicting talented men and beautiful women?

    A Woman Leaning Against a Door

    There was a merchant who walked alone at dusk and saw a woman leaning against a door, graceful and charming in appearance. The merchant stared at her for a long time, and the woman then invited him inside.

    A passerby saw this and thought it was a secret rendezvous, so he told the neighbors, who planned to arrest them. The neighbors were surprised and said, "This is actually an empty house. How could there be a woman here? It must be a demon!"

    They led several people into the house to search for her. Indeed, there was no trace of anyone. Only in the eastern room was an ancient coffin present, covered with dust and dirt. They noticed that the lid of the coffin was slightly open. A small part of her clothing was visible. Together they opened it to look inside, and they found one person lying face down on top of dried bones; it was the merchant they had seen, already dead. They sought out his relatives and returned him to them, and buried the ancient coffin in the ground.

    The Prefect of the Imperial Household

    The Prefect of the Imperial Household from Pu County served as an official in Changzhou. His son, Qin Xiù, was young and skilled in writing, and was friends with a man named Yin in Changzhou.

    Yin's wife was beautiful but died young. She was buried outside the city walls. Yin grieved deeply and continuously, hoping that her spirit would appear to him in a dream. He prayed for three years without any response.

    The Prefect of the Imperial Household died in office, and his official income was meager. Qin Xiù was unable to afford the funeral expenses, so he moved the coffin to a Buddhist temple and entrusted his family to Yin, and went himself to seek out old friends who were officials in Suzhou.

    135

    长洲令张，秦之内兄也，于秀为舅氏。张女名小慧，有姿容，年十七岁死，旅榇未归。
    　　武弁黄某，秀之从姑之夫也。黄子纶与秀同生时年月，故自幼投契。是时年俱弱冠矣，并豪宕不羁。
    　　自秦登仕藉，秀与两家眷属不谋面者凡八九载。至是相见，益亲昵。秀往来两家，住无常所。久之，渐与纶为北里游。黄知之，召纶切责，并责秀。张闻之，亦谓秀奈何与贱倡为伍，秀与纶俱不听也。
    　　一日薄暮，共游隘巷。见数女姝酾，倚门盼客，两人私语曰：“必倡之居也。”试挑之，女郎皆笑呼客入。纱灯锦幛，华丽迷人。饮酒既醉，各选一女就东西室寝，皆诸妓之尤者。夜半，纶大怒呼秀。秀惊问，则曰：“贱婢谲诈，乃敢以纪信诳我！”于是残灯映帷，秀未及答，忽谛视共寝者，亦一丑女，殊非寝时所选者也。将应纶，丑女急掩其口曰：“慎勿声，祸及矣！”秀惧，不敢再言，纶亦为其妓所止，寂然不相问。

    Explanation

    135

    Zhang, the magistrate of Changzhou, was the brother-in-law of Qin, and he was the maternal uncle of Xiu. Zhang's daughter, named Xiaohui, was beautiful and died at the age of seventeen, and her coffin had not yet returned home.

    Huang, a military officer, was the husband of Xiu's maternal aunt. Huang's son, Luan, was born in the same year and month as Xiu, so they had been close since childhood. By this time, they were both in their twenties and were both unrestrained and free-spirited.

    Since Qin entered officialdom, Xiu had not met with the family members of both households for about eight to nine years. When they finally met, they became even closer. Xiu frequently visited both households and did not have a fixed place to stay. After a while, he gradually became involved in activities in the northern part of the city with Luan. Huang found out and severely scolded Luan, and also reprimanded Xiu. Zhang also criticized Xiu for associating with lowly prostitutes, but both Xiu and Luan refused to listen.

    One day in the late afternoon, they wandered through narrow alleys and saw several beautiful women leaning on the door, waiting for customers. The two whispered to each other, "This must be the place of a prostitute." They tried to flirt with them, and the women laughed and called them in. The lighting and decorations were luxurious and attractive. After getting drunk, they each chose a woman to sleep with in the east and west rooms, and these women were the best among the prostitutes. In the middle of the night, Luan suddenly became very angry and called out to Xiu. Xiu asked in surprise, and Luan said, "The lowly servant was deceitful and dared to deceive me with the loyalty of Ji Xin!" Under the dim light reflected on the curtain, Xiu did not have time to answer, but he suddenly looked closely at the person sharing the bed with him and found that it was an ugly woman, not the one he had chosen earlier. When he was about to respond to Luan, the ugly woman quickly covered his mouth and said, "Be careful not to make a sound, or trouble will come!" Xiu was afraid and dared not speak again. Luan was also stopped by the prostitute and they remained silent, not asking each other anything.

    136

    秀私叩丑女祸及之故，答曰：“郎君年少，当大用，故特相告。主人异姓兄弟五六人，皆犷狺无赖。常以诸美诱客，醉后就寝则易之，妾所以得侍君子也。玉杯之诈有年矣。觉而嚷者，则杀之。坐是死者不可胜数。今幸主人未闻，不然，危矣！少顷辞别，宜若为勿知也者，以示彼弗疑，则可免于难。明宵主人饮他所，若再来，彼美可图也。”秀谢而起，呼纶出，语之。而纶之丑女亦云。须臾主人出，果五六人，皆身长七尺，状貌狰狞，各怒色相向。纶、秀心悸，辞以有事，欲早归，解金而予之，略不及夜来易女之事。主人乃喜，且却其金曰：“两公一宿，拜惠多矣。余无所用金。”纶、秀强之受，时诸女在旁，咸以目相视。纶、秀会意，乃怀金而出。门皆扃锔，主人启钥，乃得出。

    Explanation

    136

    秀私下询问丑女为何会遭遇这样的不幸，她回答说：“郎君年轻，将来必大有用处，所以我特别告知你。主人有五六位非亲兄弟，都凶狠无赖。他们常常以各种美女来引诱客人，喝醉后就替换客人，所以我得以侍奉郎君。主人用玉杯设下的圈套已经持续多年了。那些发现并大声喊叫的人，就会被杀死。因此，死的人不计其数。现在幸好主人还没有察觉，否则我们就会有危险了！一会儿我们就告辞，应该装作什么都不知道的样子，以显示他们没有怀疑，这样我们就可以避免灾祸。明天晚上主人会去别的地方饮酒，如果你们再来，那位美女是可以追求的。”秀感谢她后起身，叫纶出来，把情况告诉了他。纶的丑女也这么说。不久主人出来了，果然是五六个人，个个身高七尺，面目狰狞，彼此怒目而视。纶和秀心里害怕，以有事为由，想要早点回去，解下金子给了他们，但没有提到昨晚替换女子的事。主人很高兴，还推辞着不肯接受金子，说：“两位先生昨晚的款待已经让我受益良多，我没有必要再收金子了。”纶和秀勉强让他收下，当时其他女子在一旁，都用眼神互相示意。纶和秀心领神会，于是带着金子离开了。门都被锁上了，主人开门后，他们才得以出去。

    137

    纶谓秀曰：“夜来美人，吾见亦罕。当再来，以毕夙愿，且觇其实。”秀然之。薄暮又往焉。诸女仍延客于门，问主人，果赴饮他所。两人喜，各私所选妓而幸之。秀将寝，女忽自陈，乃常州殷生之妇。秀惊曰：“殷生吾友也，其妇死已久，何妄乎”女曰：“君勿怖，吾鬼也。吾娣妹辈皆鬼也。芳魂无恢，为强鬼所劫，望乞救援。”言罢呜咽，双泪阑干。秀不觉凄然，为之悲怆，反忘其为异物也，因问：“鬼亦能淫乎”女曰：“甚于人。

    Explanation

    137

    Lun told Xiu, "The beautiful woman who came last night, I have rarely seen her. She should come again, to fulfill my long-held wish, and also to see if she is real." Xiu agreed to it. At dusk, he went there again. The other women still invited guests to the door, and they asked the host, who confirmed that he was going to drink somewhere else. The two of them were happy, each privately chose a prostitute, and enjoyed themselves. As Xiu was about to sleep, the woman suddenly spoke up, revealing that she was the wife of Yin Sheng from Changzhou. Xiu was startled and said, "Yin Sheng is my friend, his wife has been dead for a long time, how can this be false?" The woman replied, "Do not be afraid, I am a ghost. My sisters and I are all ghosts. My fragrant soul has no hope, I was taken by a powerful ghost, I hope you will help me." After speaking, she wept, her tears streaming down her face. Xiu could not help but feel sad, he was moved to sorrow, and forgot that she was an unusual creature. He then asked, "Can ghosts also engage in sexual acts?" The woman answered, "Even more than humans."

    138

    ”秀问：“妍媸有择乎”女曰：“鬼之好与人同，鬼之淫与人异。”秀问其异云何，女曰：“鬼纯阴而无阳，不能行人道。恒使丑者媚人，取精而食之，然后选色而御之。其具倍于人，而妾辈之辱且虐，亦甚于有生之日。”秀不觉怒而起，曰：“嫂氏勿忧，誓当召武士提利剑，断此数贼头！”女曰：“如是则无济，君第走告殷生，使投牒吴县城隍，则此辈齑粉矣。城隍非他，君之府君也，生平居官廉洁，上帝嘉之，故有是命。”秀悲喜交集。

    Explanation

    138

    Translation:

    Xiu asked, "Is there a choice between beauty and ugliness?" The woman replied, "The preferences of ghosts are the same as those of humans, but their sexual behavior is different." Xiu asked how they were different. The woman said, "Ghosts are purely yin and lack yang, so they cannot engage in human sexual practices. They constantly use ugly beings to seduce humans, consume their essence, and then choose beautiful beings to mate with. Their sexual organs are double that of humans, and the humiliation and abuse suffered by us is even worse than during our lifetime." Xiu could not help but rise in anger and said, "Sister-in-law, do not worry. I swear to summon warriors with drawn swords to cut off the heads of these few bandits!" The woman said, "If you do that, it will be futile. Just go and inform Yin, and have him submit a petition to the city god of Wuxian. These ghosts will then be reduced to dust. The city god is none other than your deceased father, who was upright and廉洁 in office. The supreme deity favored him, so he was given this appointment." Xiu was filled with mixed emotions of sorrow and joy.

    139

    方絮语间，纶忽启门呼曰：“君知其异乎”秀曰知之。纶曰：“君知张妹小蕙在此乎”秀闻言，急呼：“慧妹安在”蕙淹泣而出，娇魂楚楚，欲诉欲语，愁怨难明。秀亦洒涕曰：“别来多时，竞不识尔。妙龄弱质，不意遭此暴横，良可悯怜！”亦出殷生妇以语纶。四人相对，悲哀哽咽，至于失声。纶、秀复相与私幸，虽冒李下之嫌，未涉桑中之刺也。
    　　诸女闻之，咸来自诉，且曰：“宜速去，迟将有变。”殷妻嘱秀曰：“必告殷生！阴律：非亲昵，不得控私事。君慎勿置词，授狡童以返噬之隙也。”蕙亦以告父母牒城隍，嘱秀并嘱纶。纶亦知秦少府官城隍之事，蕙先告之也。二人别而行。迨晓，验宿处，数冢累累，白杨衰草，信夜来之非谬矣，
    　　秀即以白张，张叱其妄。纶又来言之，张夫妇乃不胜悲痛。秀复遣介赍书，驰召殷生至，告以故。殷抢地长号，悟三年之无梦者，强鬼之制其妻也。遂与张共诣城隍首之，纶、秀亦往，井宿庙中。

    Explanation

    139

    As they were chatting, Lun suddenly opened the door and called out, "Do you know its strangeness?" Xiu replied, "I know it." Lun then said, "Do you know that Zhang's sister Xiao Hui is here?" Upon hearing this, Xiu shouted urgently, "Where is my sister Hui?" Xiao Hui came out weeping, with a delicate and sorrowful appearance, wanting to speak but unable to, full of sorrow and resentment that was hard to express. Xiu also wept, saying, "It has been a long time since we last met, and I don't even recognize you. In her prime and delicate, she unexpectedly suffered such violence, truly pitiable!" He also mentioned the wife of Yin Sheng to Lun. The four of them sat facing each other, their grief so profound that they choked on their sobs, even to the point of losing their voice. Lun and Xiu then privately enjoyed each other's company, even though it carried the suspicion of being under a plum tree, without touching on the thorns of the mulberry bush.

    The other girls heard about it and came forward to complain, saying, "You should leave quickly, for if you stay too long, things may change." Yin's wife instructed Xiu, "You must tell Yin Sheng! The rules of the underworld: only close friends can reveal private matters. You must be careful not to speak, for you might give a cunning youth an opportunity to bite back." Xiao Hui also reported to her parents to the City God, instructing Xiu and also Lun. Lun also knew about the City God's affairs, as Xiao Hui had told him first. The two of them parted ways and went their separate paths. By dawn, they verified the place of rest, finding numerous graves piled up, with white poplar and withered grass, confirming that what happened last night was not a mistake.

    Xiu immediately reported to Zhang, who scolded him for being delusional. Lun came again to tell him, and Zhang and his wife were overwhelmed with grief. Xiu then sent someone to deliver a message, rushing to summon Yin Sheng, and told him the reason. Yin fell to the ground and wailed, realizing that the three years of no dreams were due to a powerful ghost controlling his wife. So he went with Zhang to report to the City God, and Lun and Xiu also went, staying overnight in the temple.

    140

    是夜乃闻城隍升厅事，锒铛钮镣诸具掷地声，次闻胥役吆喝声、唱到声、书吏点名声。城隍问曰：“诱良善，侵孤弱，淫横不逞者，谁为首”秀察其声音，宛然父也，因幽咽。俄闻群鬼供曰：“是万德为首。”一鬼辨曰：“小鬼非毛三、周喜儿等怂恿，亦不敢肆行。”一女鬼诉曰：“鬼妇是赶七所劫。”一女鬼曰：“辱妾者，余小猴、金午及万德也，而万德为甚。”声似殷生之妻，殷生泣下。旋闻城隍曰：“蕙儿可言状。”蕙曰：“儿为众贼共虐，儿苦也愿置贼极刑！”城隍叹息，命至后堂与阿姑相见，盖张之姊，秀之母，先少府没三年矣。张及秀皆暗哭，纶亦哭。其时众鬼纷辨，鞠讯杂沓，听不甚彻。已闻拷掠声、众鬼呼痛楚声、女鬼感谢声、毕谳声、累犯入狱声，久之遂寂。

    Explanation

    140

    On that night, the city god ascended to the hall, and the sounds of shackles and chains being thrown to the ground were heard. Next came the shouts of officials, the chanting of names, and the calls of clerks. The city god asked, "Who is the leader among those who entice the innocent, oppress the weak, and act wantonly?" Xiu recognized the voice and realized it was his father, and he sobbed softly. Soon, the ghosts confessed, "Wan De is the leader." One ghost defended himself, saying, "If it were not for the encouragement of Ma San, Zhou Xier, and others, I would not have dared to act so boldly." A female ghost recounted, "The ghost woman was kidnapped by Gang Qi." Another female ghost said, "Those who dishonored me were Yu Xiaohou, Jin Wu, and Wan De, with Wan De being the worst." Her voice resembled that of Yin Sheng's wife, and Yin Sheng wept. The city god then said, "Hui'er, can you describe the situation?" Hui'er replied, "I was mistreated by many bandits, and I wish for them to receive the harshest punishment!" The city god sighed and ordered her to go to the back hall to meet with Auntie. Auntie was Zhang's sister, Xiu's mother, who had died three years ago as a minor official's wife. Zhang and Xiu both cried silently, and Lun also wept. At that moment, the ghosts were in a state of confusion, and the questioning was chaotic, making it difficult to hear clearly. The sounds of torture, the cries of pain from the ghosts, the gratitude of the female ghosts, the final verdict, and the sound of repeat offenders being sent to jail were heard. After a long time, it became silent.

    141

    于是秀上堂呼父，张呼女，殷生呼妻，纶呼舅氏及蕙妹，惨语千端，哭声一片。卒亦无有隔窗而应者。
    　　后殷生梦妇来日：“狂徒虽则授首，妾已蒙垢，羞见郎君。且近奉秦公约束，艳妇不得外行，虑招谤辱。情不能已，暂得请命来见，为谢秦公子及黄生，蒙其仗义相授，他生图报。蕙妹亦寄声张府，言己得所依，嗣当寝门问安也。郎君好自重，妾从此别矣！”言毕，涕泣而去。殷生挽之，倏然已觉，惟残缸荧荧射枕上泪光而己。自是杳不复至。小蕙屡见梦于张少府，亦屡梦于秀焉。秀遂反常州，奉父榇葬之姑苏。

    Explanation

    141

    At that time, Show went upstairs and called his father, Zhang called his daughter, Yin called his wife, and Lun called his uncle and his sister Hui. They spoke in sorrowful words and cried in unison. In the end, no one answered from behind the window.

    Later, Yin dreamed that his wife came to him and said, "The fierce man has been beheaded, but I have already been defiled and am ashamed to see my husband. Moreover, I recently received instructions from Mr. Qin, who forbids a beautiful woman to go out, fearing slander and disgrace. Unable to hold back my feelings, I temporarily obtained permission to come and see you, to thank Mr. Qin and Mr. Huang for their kindness and assistance. I will repay them in the future. Miss Hui also sent a message to Mr. Zhang's residence, saying she has found a place to rely on and will visit to pay respects in the future. Please take good care of yourself, I will part from you now!" After finishing speaking, she wept and left. Yin tried to stop her, but suddenly woke up, only to see a faint light from the lamp shining on the tears on the pillow. From then on, she never came again. Miss Hui often dreamed of Mr. Zhang and also often dreamed of Show. Show then returned to Changzhou and brought his father's coffin to bury in Suzhou.

    142

    竹连环
    　　邑中八九月之间，四乡赛神演剧，农商士女及游方乞食之徒咸集焉，谓之神会。
    　　有一道人来趁会，敝衣跣足，以竹杖荷竹连环二，盖截竹为之，互挽而无端，人不能为也。独坐场隅三日，而众莫之识。有一士人察其异，俟其去，潜尾之。
    　　行二十里许，至一山，岩谷耸然，石壁千仞。道人以杖叩石壁，石即洞开。石屋数重，户牖阶墀悉具。道人趋入内，士人随之入。至前庭，窥其内昏黑不辨物，徘徊不敢前，亦不去。
    　　其前庭一柱联云：“云藏石室虚无地，风卷泉帘小有天。”字皆石镂。吟诵不已，不觉饥甚，呼道人觅食。道人出，诃曰：“谁教尔来此此岂有食耶无已，山我丸啖尔。”探袖出一丸与之，道人则仍趋入内。

    Explanation

    142

    The Bamboo Rings
    Between the eighth and ninth months in the county, festivals in the four districts where gods are worshipped and plays are performed, with farmers, merchants, scholars, women, and wandering beggars all gathering there, called a divine assembly.
    A Taoist came to join the festival, wearing tattered clothes and barefoot, carrying two bamboo rings on a bamboo staff, made by cutting bamboo, interlocking and without ends, no one can make them. Sitting alone in a corner for three days, and no one recognized him. A scholar noticed his strangeness, waited for him to leave, and secretly followed him.
    Walking about twenty miles, they reached a mountain, with towering cliffs and thousand-foot stone walls. The Taoist knocked on the stone wall with his staff, and the stone opened. Several stone houses, with doors, windows, steps, and thresholds all complete. The Taoist hurried inside, and the scholar followed him. Reaching the front courtyard, he peered into the darkness where he could not see anything, hesitated, and did not dare to go forward, but also did not leave.
    One column in the front courtyard had a couplet that read, "Clouds hide the stone room in a void, the wind rolls up the spring curtain to reveal a small heaven." The characters were all carved in stone. He recited it continuously, unaware that he was very hungry, and called out to the Taoist to find food. The Taoist came out, scolded, "Who told you to come here? There is no food here. If you insist, I will give you a pill to eat." He pulled out a pill from his sleeve and gave it to him, then the Taoist hurried back inside.

    143

    丸大如山查，坚如石，深黑色。士人嚼之，齿力既尽，不能碎。念必仙药，强吞之，咽中如物噎，而饥仍不止。乃复呼道人，百呼不一应。
    　　将入内，视之，第见一巨蛇据内户，双目闪闪如电光。士人骇绝辟易，汗流气喘，迴视石壁己闭，孤松倒挂，萝蔓垂垂，野鸟咿嘤其上而已。士人叹曰：“叔夜未应得道也！”叹罢，咽中丸哽，倏已消下，还家数月不饥。
    　　大赤蛇
    　　丰城有娶妻而美者，甚恋之。数年之间，足迹未尝离房帷。其妻亦情好至笃。琴瑟伉俪，至有长生夜半之约。
    　　友人好事者，故以酒招之，强之醉而止之宿，必再三而后可。既宿则终夜不寐，呻吟床榻，辄有忆妻诗及词，以示友人。虽诮谑之，不顾也。坐是招饮常不赴，人知其如此，亦无有过而问者。虽其至亲，终岁不获一面焉。

    Explanation

    143

    The pill was as large as a mountain peach, hard as stone, and deep black in color. The scholar chewed it, but his teeth could not break it. Thinking it must be an elixir of immortality, he forced it down, and it felt as if something were stuck in his throat, yet he still felt hungry. He called out again, but after a hundred calls, there was no response.

    As he was about to enter, he saw a giant snake blocking the inner door, its eyes flashing like lightning. The scholar was so terrified that he stepped back, drenched in sweat and gasping for breath. Looking back, he saw that the stone wall had closed, with a solitary pine tree hanging upside down and vines cascading down. Only the sounds of wild birds above broke the silence. The scholar sighed, "Shuyue must not have attained enlightenment!" After his sigh, the pill in his throat suddenly disappeared, and he returned home, not feeling hungry for several months.

    The Large Red Snake

    In Fengcheng, there was a man who married a beautiful wife and was very attached to her. For several years, he never left her room. His wife also loved him deeply. They were a harmonious couple, even making a secret pact for immortality at midnight.

    A friend, who enjoyed socializing, often invited him to drink, forcing him to get drunk and stay overnight. He would only agree after being asked repeatedly. Once he stayed, he could not sleep all night, groaning on the bed, and would write poems and lyrics expressing thoughts of his wife to show his friends. Despite being teased, he paid no attention. Because of this, he often avoided drinking invitations. People knew of his behavior and did not bother to ask. Even his closest relatives did not see him for an entire year.

    144

    后病瘵将死，执妻之手涕泣曰：“本图百年完聚，岂意中道解携君尚青春，能终居燕子楼否”妻泣而誓之，且曰：“羽族失雄，犹不再匹，可以人而不如鸟乎”夫乃诀而瞑。妻哭之三日，尽哀，屡欲自经。舅姑救而免，稍稍劝慰之，乃不哭。
    　　有傅生者吊其家。傅，夫友也，美丰仪，妻窥而悦之。傅请见，妻缟素而出，虽衔哀惨语，四目往来，如流星之相照也。
    　　时傅方丧偶，妻遂有去帷之意。往往勃豀于舅姑之前，或无故虐其婢，使夜施华帐，旦饰明妆，风流顾盼，自好也。舅姑不能堪，告其父母，父母亦不能制，知其贰心，命之再醮。傅闻之，旋以媒来，遂归于傅。
    　　成欢之夕，有大赤蛇出于床下，绕床三匝。夫妇惊呼，家人破尸视之，已垂死矣，蛇啮妇心食之，须臾而灭。

    Explanation

    144

    After the man fell ill with consumption and was near death, he held his wife's hand and wept, saying, "I had originally intended for a lifelong union, how could I have expected to part ways in the middle of the journey? You are still in your prime, can you remain in the '燕子楼'?" The wife wept and vowed, and added, "Birds that lose their male partners do not remarry, can humans be less loyal than birds?" The husband then bid farewell and passed away. The wife cried for three days, expressing her grief fully, and repeatedly attempted to hang herself. Her in-laws saved her and gently comforted her, and she stopped crying.

    There was a man named Fu who came to mourn at her home. Fu was the husband's friend, handsome and well-appearing, and the wife secretly admired him. Fu asked to meet her, and she came out dressed in white, though she was still grieving and speaking sadly, their eyes meeting as if they were stars shining upon each other.

    At that time, Fu had just lost his wife, and the wife began to have thoughts of leaving her current situation. She often quarreled with her in-laws and sometimes would abuse her maidservants, ordering them to set up a beautiful canopy at night and adorn themselves with bright makeup in the morning, displaying a charming demeanor, as if she were enjoying herself. Her in-laws could no longer tolerate this behavior and reported it to her parents. Her parents also could not control her, knowing that she had second thoughts about her marriage, and arranged for her to remarry. Fu heard of this and soon sent a matchmaker, and she was then betrothed to Fu.

    On the night of their consummation, a large red snake emerged from under the bed, circling the bed three times. The couple cried out in shock, and the family broke open the bed to look, finding her already on the verge of death, with the snake having bitten into her heart and consumed it, disappearing in an instant.

    145

    非非子曰：吾邑李甲未娶时，读书外舅家，与妻兄同学。戏请曰：“令妹可得一见否？”妻兄曰：“吾家惟妹寝最后。吾夜半归，呼其启门，可从吾后窃窥之。非是则无由矣。”如其言，果见之，盖国色也。次日驰归告父母：“明日必迎妇！”父母怜而从之。请于妇家。以期太迫辞。使者返，甲曰：“后日亦可也。”又请之，则又辞。次日又请之，妇家曰：“至近亦须一月后。恶有请期三日而亲迎者乎”使者既返，甲已病。父母忧之，将复请次日而告山病。甲曰：“无庸也，疾不能待矣”次日遂死。其妻末期年，竟别适。余尝闻而恶之，然以视丰城之妇，其轻重盖迥不侔矣。
    　　绣鞋
    　　王屋之阴，有童子采樵入山。雪后失径，饥甚。林壑幽翳中，忽闻麦饭香。即之，甲第一区，金环朱户，蓬头小婢方炊麦西庑，乞食得饱。复引入后院，倚檐高树一株，花大如盎。数美人宴集花下，被服奇艳，饮馔芳烈，并非人间所有。婢令叩头谢讫，随出。

    Explanation

    145

    非非子曰：吾邑李甲未娶时，读书外舅家，与妻兄同学。戏请曰：“令妹可得一见否？”妻兄曰：“吾家惟妹寝最后。吾夜半归，呼其启门，可从吾后窃窥之。非是则无由矣。”如其言，果见之，盖国色也。次日驰归告父母：“明日必迎妇！”父母怜而从之。请于妇家。以期太迫辞。使者返，甲曰：“后日亦可也。”又请之，则又辞。次日又请之，妇家曰：“至近亦须一月后。恶有请期三日而亲迎者乎”使者既返，甲已病。父母忧之，将复请次日而告山病。甲曰：“无庸也，疾不能待矣”次日遂死。其妻末期年，竟别适。余尝闻而恶之，然以视丰城之妇，其轻重盖迥不侔矣。

    绣鞋

    王屋之阴，有童子采樵入山。雪后失径，饥甚。林壑幽翳中，忽闻麦饭香。即之，甲第一区，金环朱户，蓬头小婢方炊麦西庑，乞食得饱。复引入后院，倚檐高树一株，花大如盎。数美人宴集花下，被服奇艳，饮馔芳烈，并非人间所有。婢令叩头谢讫，随出。

    146

    告其家，寻路同入，屋舍杳然。咸以为诳。回过竹林中，拾得绣鞋一只，精丽异常。山中故无此物，心知其仙，携归什袭笥中。准杨妃锦袜之例，诣观者辄与百钱。
    　　予亲见之，瘦不盈握，镂绣嵌珠，异香袭人，非兰非麝，把玩不能去手。因邀至拾鞋处，馀雪未消，莲印宛然，类石家香尘，绝无沽涴之迹。怅惘良久。
    　　后数日，闻其家启笥，鞋失所在。
    　　一此篇得之吴兰雪。
    　　异石
    　　河南修武县，去城三十里许有清真观。观外有阁临溪；老松数十株，荫映如画。溪水作绿玉色，清彻鉴人；浅不盈尺，灌田数顷，虽大旱不竭。中产异石，能行。拾置磁斗中，养以清泉，五色陆离，终日浮漾若游鱼然。偶碎其一，中有小虫，状类蛾蠓，去之，遂不能自运。盖虫生于石，以石为屋，载而行之，殆蜗之属欤

    Explanation

    146

    A Fairy's Shoe

    A man told his family about an experience he had. They followed him to the place, but the houses were nowhere to be found. Everyone thought it was a lie. However, as they retraced their steps through the bamboo grove, he picked up a single embroidered shoe, extraordinarily exquisite. Knowing that there was no such thing in the mountains, he believed it must be a fairy's. He carefully wrapped and stored it in a box. Following the example of Yang Guifei's brocade socks, he gave a hundred coins to anyone who visited to see it.

    I personally saw the shoe. It was slender, not even the width of a fist, inlaid with embroidery and jewels, and had a strange fragrance that permeated the air, unlike any other scent. It was so captivating that I could not stop playing with it. I invited others to the spot where I had found the shoe. The remaining snow was still there, and the lotus imprint was clearly visible, like the dust of the Shih family, untouched and pristine. I gazed at it for a long time, filled with longing.

    A few days later, I heard that the family had opened the box, but the shoe was nowhere to be found.

    This piece was obtained from Wu Lanxue.

    Strange Stone

    In the County of Xiuwu, Henan Province, about thirty miles from the city, there is a Pure and True Temple. Outside the temple, there is a pavilion overlooking a stream; dozens of old pine trees cast a picturesque shade. The stream water is a green jade color, clear enough to see one's reflection; it is shallow, not even a foot deep, but it irrigates several acres of fields, and even during a severe drought, it does not run dry. In the middle of the stream, there is a strange stone that can move. When picked up and placed in a porcelain bowl, nourished with clear spring water, it appears variegated in five colors, all day floating and swimming like a fish. Once, when one of the stones was accidentally broken, a small insect, looking like a fly or mosquito, was found inside. After it was removed, the stone could no longer move on its own. It seems that the insect was born in the stone, using it as a house, and carried it along, probably a type of snail.

    147

    观为元长春子邱处机同邑人刘志敏所建，土人以石之异，传为刘海戏蟾处，遂讹为海蟾宫云。
    　　——此篇亦吴兰雪笔记。
    　　绀霞
    　　吴仲子兰雪，少多诗梦。今记其一：
    　　年十五时，梦行溪上。春水揉蓝，落花如绣，心艳之，欲穷其源。行里许，见短垣一带、篱门半掩，侧足而入。园中桃花数百株，芳菲弥望。穿树而行，径甚缭曲。花片打人，绛雪满衣。已而乱红深处，露一六角亭，画帘蜿地，隐隐有坠钗声。吴故文怯，止不敢前，觉鬓云眉翠，依约可接。方徘徊间，有搴帘微笑者，望之如朝霞和雪、与桃花争丽。吴痴立久之，闻帘钩璙然，始惊其去。启帘窥之，竟杳矣。亭中绿窗斐几，笔砚甚设，衣香缕缕，尚萦画屏绣幔间也。惆怅如失，感成绝句，于几上拾得浅碧笺书之。诗曰：

    Explanation

    147

    Viewed as constructed by Liu Zhimin, a fellow townsman of Qiu Chuji, the Yuan Chang Chunzi. The locals attributed the stone's uniqueness, passed it down as the place where Liu Hai played with the toad, and thus it became known as the Sea Toad Palace.

    ——This piece is also from Wu Lanxue's notes.

    Purple mist

    Wu Zhongzi Lanxue, in his youth, had many poetic dreams. Now, I will record one:

    At the age of fifteen, he dreamed of walking along a stream. Spring water was as blue as kneaded dye, and fallen flowers were like embroidery. He was captivated by it and wanted to explore its source. After walking for a short distance, he saw a stretch of low walls and a half-open gate. He stepped sideways inside. In the garden, there were hundreds of peach trees, their fragrance filling the air. He walked through the trees, the path being very winding. Flower petals hit him, leaving a red snow on his clothes. Soon, deep among the scattered red petals, a hexagonal pavilion appeared. The painted curtain lay on the ground, and there was a faint sound of a fallen hairpin. Wu, being naturally timid, stopped and did not dare to go forward. He felt that the cloud-like hair and green eyebrows were almost within reach. While hesitating, someone pulled back the curtain and smiled. She looked like a blend of morning glory and snow, competing in beauty with the peach blossoms. Wu stood there in a daze for a long time. When he heard the sound of the curtain hook, he was startled to realize she had left. He opened the curtain to look, but she was nowhere to be seen. Inside the pavilion, there was a green window, a colorful table, and writing utensils neatly arranged. The scent of her clothes lingered in the air, still hanging around the painted screens and embroidered curtains. Feeling melancholic and lost, he composed a quatrain. He picked up a light blue note paper and wrote on it. The poem reads:

    148

    新绿重重树，莺啼自在春。
    　　桃花红作雨，愁杀隔帘人。
    　　掷笔遂觉，心异而志之。
    　　他日，又梦至其处。遥闻亭中笑语，遂隐身花丛中。俄见丽人从数鬟，自亭中出，绡衣玉佩，向之帘间人也。数鬟争拾落花，以衣襟裹之，聚而较量，拔簪摘翠，小语呶呶。丽人绰约临风，对花不语，恍若有思。数蝶依依，绕其左右。吴于是目眩魂消，殊有化蝶之想，遽折花枝遥掷之。丽人觉，谓鬟曰：“人间刘阮再来矣。盍归乎”遂迁延避去。吴怅然，尾至亭中，竟不复见。几上词笺一幅，取观之，墨渖尚湿，俨如前诗意相答，末署“绀霞”二宇。词不尽记，有“溪上桃花红奈何，春风吹又多”之句。方拟袖之，为啼鸟所惊，惘然而悟。

    Explanation

    148

    The lush green trees, their foliage dense and vibrant, and the carefree spring, filled with the cheerful songs of orioles, create a serene and joyful atmosphere. The blooming peach blossoms, their petals falling like a red rain, evoke a profound sense of sorrow in someone behind a curtain. The narrator, moved by this scene, throws down his brush and feels a sudden change in his heart, resolving to pursue this newfound inspiration.

    On another day, he dreams again of this place. From a distance, he hears laughter and conversation coming from a pavilion. He hides among the flowers, and soon sees a beautiful woman accompanied by several maidservants emerge from the pavilion. She is dressed in a light, gauze-like garment and adorned with jade ornaments, the same person who was behind the curtain earlier. The maidservants compete to pick up the fallen flowers, gathering them in their sleeves and comparing them, plucking hairpins and picking up green leaves, their soft voices chattering playfully.

    The woman stands gracefully in the breeze, her eyes fixed on the flowers, as if lost in deep thought. Several butterflies flutter around her, showing their affection. The narrator, captivated by her beauty, is almost overcome, and he has a sudden desire to transform into a butterfly. He quickly breaks a branch and throws it far away.

    The woman notices this and tells her maidservants, "It seems that Liu and Ruan have come again. Let's go back." They delay their departure, possibly due to embarrassment or reluctance. The narrator, feeling disappointed, follows them to the pavilion but finds them gone. On a table, he discovers a piece of paper with a poem, the ink still wet, written in a style similar to the earlier poem, and signed with the name "Ganxia" at the end.

    As he is about to tuck the poem into his sleeve, he is startled by the sound of a crying bird and suddenly realizes the true nature of the experience.

    149

    耳食录卷七
    　　彭公子
    　　东乡艾生善卜日。凡乡里婚嫁丧葬，及一切动作琐碎事多就卜焉。遵其所谓宜者则吉，犯其所谓忌者则凶，往往奇验。乡之人以是神之。
    　　有彭公子者，豪而愎，素不信艾。买得好绢将制衣，伪卜期于艾，艾造某日可，某日必不可。公子心嗤之，故以不可之日制焉。且多召衣工，务观成于一日。艾闻而往戒之，曰：“公衣犯大忌，必不可服。即服，当以明年之某月某日某时，庶无患。公宜信之，毋谓鄙人之言略不验也。”公子阳诺，次日即衣之。方挈领，忽有人影出衣中，歎然遂灭。公子始疑惧，然转念一衣之微，何关休咎或目眩所致。卒衣之。

    Explanation

    149

    Ear-Eating Record, Volume Seven

    • Mr. Peng
      • In the eastern village, there was a man named Ai who was skilled in choosing auspicious dates. Whenever there were weddings, funerals, and other important events in the village, people often consulted him for guidance. If they followed his advice on what was auspicious, things went well; if they violated what he deemed inauspicious, misfortune followed. His predictions were often remarkably accurate, and the villagers revered him for this.
      • There was a man named Mr. Peng, who was proud and stubborn and did not believe in Ai. He bought some fine silk to make a new set of clothes. Pretending to seek a favorable date from Ai, Ai said a certain day was good, and another day was definitely not good. The young master secretly ridiculed him and therefore made the clothes on the day that was said to be inauspicious. He also called in many tailors, aiming to complete it in one day.
      • Ai heard about it and went to warn him, saying, "Your clothes violate a major taboo. If you wear them, they should be worn on a specific date and time next year, perhaps there will be no harm. You should believe me; do not think that my words are entirely without merit." The young master pretended to agree and wore the clothes the next day. Just as he was pulling up the collar, a human shadow suddenly emerged from the clothes, sighed, and then disappeared. The young master began to doubt and fear, but then thought, "Such a small matter—could it have any relation to good or bad fortune, or was it just an illusion?" He finally wore the clothes.
    150

    以事如冈上。冈上者，小市廛名也。事毕而归，道逢担者负棘薪过之甚疾。棘端勾其衣，衣为之裂。乃止担者而呵之。担者不逊，公子怒，三掌其面而去之。
    　　是夜但者归，号痛语妻子曰：“彭公子击我，伤甚，我死矣。
    　　尔曹不可忘！”言毕遂死。妻子鸣于里正，夜奔公子，声以毙命，明发将诣县诉之。当公子之事担者也，里正在焉，乃谓公于曰：“日间之事，余实见之。公子拳之，亦太甚，动中窾会，是以速毙。”公子知其诬证而莫能辨也，私许里正金，求其排难，愿以万缗归担者妻子，且经纪其丧，以求毋讼。里正主之，遂息讼。计公子所费，不下三万金，而家藏顿尽。
    　　或以语艾，谓不听公言，果至于此。艾叹曰：“未也，犹有甚时。”

    Explanation

    150

    Young Master Peng went to the hilltop for an errand. The hilltop was the name of a small market. After finishing his errand, he returned home. On the way, he met a man carrying thorny firewood passing by very quickly. The thorn at the end caught his clothes, causing them to tear. He stopped the man and scolded him. The man was not respectful, and the young master became angry, slapping him three times before leaving.

    That night, the man returned home and cried out in pain to his wife and children, saying, "Young Master Peng hit me, I am badly injured, I am going to die. You must not forget!" After finishing speaking, he died. His wife and children reported to the village head, and at night, they rushed to the young master, claiming that he had killed him. They planned to go to the county office to sue him the next day. The village head was present, and he said to the young master, "I saw what happened during the day. Your punches were also too harsh; you struck the vital point, which is why he died quickly." The young master knew the evidence was false but could not prove it. He secretly offered money to the village head to resolve the issue, offering to give ten thousand strings of coins to the man's wife and children and to handle the funeral arrangements, in order to avoid litigation. The village head took charge, and the lawsuit was dropped. The cost to the young master was no less than three thousand strings of coins, and his family's wealth was depleted.

    Someone told Ai that if he had listened to his advice, it would not have come to this. Ai sighed and said, "Not yet, there will be worse to come."

    151

    公子愤怨不已，乃过担者家，抚其棺而大哭曰：“吾与若何仇尔裂吾衣，不吾偿而薄责尔，亦其宜也。尔之死，病也，命也。尔妻子因以为利，破吾家，荡吾产。尔死有知，宁独无愧于心乎”其妻闻之，大恚曰：“尔本吾杀夫之仇，仅倾尔产，亦大幸。今既若此，吾岂以夫之死为市者乎且尔非实毙吾夫，心有所恧，伺为输吾金而营其丧事”即命其子诉之县。令验尸。得死伤五六处，乃抵公子法。里正亦以受贿蔽辜，比于同谋，降一等议罪。
    　　夫破吾衣而不逊，担者则有罪矣，从而薄责之，亦人情所时有，未应遂得惨报。然必其平日暴横不逞，有以取鬼神之怒，逃于彼而偿于此也。不然，亦其前生业冤也。至卜者之言，本不足信。然人生有吉康，亦有凶悔，跬步不谨，祸机伏焉。虽无人言，亦当随地自警，况有卜者之言哉！此与陆次云《北墅奇书》所载推车者破衣事，初相类而后相反，盖彼能忍而不较，此屡警而不悟也，

    Explanation

    151

    The young nobleman, still filled with anger and resentment, went to the porter's house and, touching the coffin, wept loudly. "What wrong have I done to you that you tore my clothes, did not compensate me, and lightly scolded me? It was fitting. Your death was due to illness and fate. Your wife and children took advantage of this to profit, breaking up my family and ruining my property. If you know of your death, would you not feel ashamed in your heart?" Upon hearing this, the porter's wife was greatly enraged and said, "You are the enemy of my husband, who I killed. Even if you had destroyed my property, it would still be considered fortunate. Now that things have come to this, would I use my husband's death as a means to gain something? Moreover, you did not actually kill my husband, and you have something to be ashamed of. You are waiting for me to give you money to handle the funeral arrangements." She immediately ordered her son to file a complaint with the county magistrate. The magistrate ordered an examination of the body, which revealed injuries in five or six places. The nobleman was then punished according to the law. The village headman was also found guilty of accepting a bribe and covering up the crime, and was treated as a co-conspirator, receiving a reduced sentence by one degree.

    Breaking someone's clothes rudely is a fault, and the porter was guilty. Lightly scolding him was also a common human reaction, but it should not necessarily lead to severe retribution. However, if he had been violent and rebellious in his daily life, it might have brought the wrath of the spirits, leading to retribution in this life. Otherwise, it could be a karmic debt from a previous life. As for the words of the fortune-teller, they are not worth believing. Yet, life has its good and bad fortunes, and one can fall into misfortune at any moment. One should always be cautious, even without warnings, especially when there are words from fortune-tellers. This is similar to the story in Lu Ci Yun's The Strange Book of the North Villa about a man who broke someone's clothes while pushing a cart, which initially seemed similar but ended differently. The reason is that the man in the story could endure and not argue, whereas this person repeatedly ignored warnings and did not realize the danger.

    152

    又尝有一人出游通市，一无赖汉迎而殴之，非醉非颠，不可理解。其人猛悟，此汉无故而辱我，非前生仇，即今生业也。再拜而谢之，拂衣竟去。市中见者无不服其量之宏，而怒此汉之狂也。次日，此汉无故死。其人以是故，得免于累。善乎哉！君子克己以全身，达人见几而远祸，斯人有焉，此可以为法矣。
    　　三元
    　　乾隆癸酉科江西乡试，分宜令天门陈公大经典房考官。入闱前一夕，梦迎天榜，众鼓乐送一匾额至其家，书曰“三元及第”。已而本房取中七人。内三人为乐平胡羽尧先生，名翘元，大庾戴莨圃先生，名第元，南昌彰芸楣先生，名元瑞；后皆登进士第，所谓“三元及第”也。胡官至光禄寺卿，，戴至太仆寺卿，彭以乾隆庚戌官至协办大学士。
    　　清河令
    　　清河令王君名城，大兴人。其所著里衣，通身皆有火，视之不见。以手批之，则火星灿然。间抖其两袖，则火从袖中爆出，其他性体，略不异人。

    Explanation

    152

    Once there was a man who went out into the bustling market. Suddenly, a ruffian approached and attacked him, though the man was neither drunk nor insane, and the attack was inexplicable. The man suddenly realized that the ruffian had wronged him without cause, either out of a grudge from a past life or a karmic debt from this one. He bowed twice in gratitude and left, waving his sleeves. All who witnessed this admired his great tolerance and were angry at the ruffian's madness. The next day, the ruffian died unexpectedly. Because of this, the man was spared from trouble. Indeed! The gentleman restrains himself to preserve his body, and the wise person sees the signs and avoids disaster. This person possesses these qualities, and thus he can be a model.

    Three Yuan

    During the 1753 provincial examination in Jiangxi under Emperor Qianlong, Chen Da Jing, the magistrate of Fenxi, Tianmen, served as the examiner. The night before entering the examination hall, he dreamed of receiving the heavenly list, and a crowd of musicians and drummers carried a plaque to his home, inscribed with "Three Yuan Jidi." Subsequently, his examination selected seven candidates. Among them were Hu Yu Yao, courtesy name Qiao Yuan from LePing; Dai Langpu, courtesy name Di Yuan from DaYu; and Zhang Yunmei, courtesy name Yuan Rui from Nanchang. All three later passed the imperial examination, achieving the highest honors known as "Three Yuan Jidi." Hu became an official in the Imperial Household Department, Dai became an official in the Department of Imperial Steeds, and Peng reached the position of Assistant Grand Secretary in 1760.

    Commander of Qinghe

    Wang Cheng, the magistrate of Qinghe, was from Daxing. He wore a special garment that was covered in fire, though it was invisible to the eye. When he touched it with his hand, flames appeared brightly. When he shook his sleeves, flames burst out from them. Otherwise, his nature and body were no different from others.

    153

    盱眙令罗君恬庵亲试之，为余言。其故殊不可解。
    　　方先生
    　　明万历间，有方先生者，西江人也。性颇端严。举孝廉，至京师，馆于宗室府第。一日，偕主人遍游诸宅，见后苑一室华丽轩敞，栋椽指云，而扃钥甚固。时方盛夏，炎威炽人，心念此地清旷，欲避暑其中，请于主人。许之，为扫榻焉。先是，宅素传有异，人不敢居，亦绝无所见闻。主人雅不欲拂方，故未之告也。
    　　方秉烛观书，至更馀就寝。将欲寐矣，忽闻箫管杂奏，环佩之声镠然发于户外。户既启，历游诸房，达于寝所，则女郎数辈，容态妖冶，舞衣歌扇，虹灯翠葆，共拥一丽人。方意必主人诸姬，为长夜之游，偶至于此；又念直达寝所，何无闺阃礼得无以己新迁，而诸姬故未之识耶嗽以惊之。丽人遣问嗽者谁，方以姓名对。丽人谓侍从曰：“方先生，正人也，安得扰之”笑语而去。

    153

    The Magistrate of Xuyi, Lord Luo, the Tranquil Hermit, personally tested it and told me. The reason is utterly inexplicable.

    Mr. Fang

    During the Wanli era of the Ming dynasty, there was a man named Mr. Fang from the Western River region. He was very upright and serious. He was recommended as a filial and upright official and came to the capital, where he stayed in a noble family's mansion. One day, he accompanied the host on a tour of all the houses. He saw a room in the rear garden that was splendid and spacious, with its beams and rafters reaching the clouds, yet the door was locked very securely. It was the height of summer, and the heat was intense and oppressive. He thought this place was cool and spacious and wanted to escape the heat there. He asked the host, who agreed and had the bed prepared. Before this, the house was always said to have something strange; no one dared to stay there, nor had anyone seen or heard anything. The host did not want to offend Mr. Fang, so he did not tell him.

    Mr. Fang was reading by candlelight until past midnight when he went to bed. He was about to fall asleep when suddenly he heard a mix of flutes and pipes playing, and the sound of jade pendants clinking came from outside the door. The door was opened, and he visited all the rooms, reaching his bedroom. Then several young ladies appeared, with alluring appearances, dressed in dance clothes and holding fans, with colorful lights and green canopies, together escorting a beautiful lady. Mr. Fang thought they must be the host's concubines, engaged in a night-long revelry, accidentally coming here; he also thought about how they could directly enter his bedroom, why were they not following the proper etiquette of the inner chambers? Could it be because he had recently moved in, and the concubines had not yet met him? He coughed to startle them. The beautiful lady sent someone to ask who had coughed. Mr. Fang replied with his name. The beautiful lady said to her attendants, "Mr. Fang is a proper gentleman. How could they disturb him?" They laughed and left.

    154

    翼日，欲以所见语主人，恐冒瓜李之嫌而贻诸姬罪，乃托他词还旧居，而泄于其戚属某。
    　　戚，匪人也，窃幸之，潜往宿焉，冀有所遇。果闻鼓乐声自楼而下，急于门隙间窥之。见簇仗至厅事，一丽人中坐。奏乐既阕，两行班卫其肃，无敢哗者。中坐丽人忽怒曰：“闻方先生已去，谁何至此邪气逼人乃尔，速为勾致！”须臾，二女郎破门而入，以锒铛系戚颈，牵出堂下。丽人数之曰：“穴居鼠子，欲随公房鸡犬不可得，敢犯神仙眷属耶其心可诛，亟斩以恂！”戚叩头乞哀悔罪，丽人曰：“若杀尔，实污吾刃。今贷尔以死，尔宜举以告人也。若秘之，是尔心叵测，终当杀尔矣！”乃命杖一百而去。

    Explanation

    154

    The next day, he wanted to tell the master what he had seen, but feared the scandal of being seen together and thus causing trouble for the concubines. So, he used another excuse to return to his old residence and revealed it to a certain relative.

    The relative was not a good person, secretly hoping for it, went there to stay overnight, expecting to encounter something. Indeed, he heard the sound of music and drums coming down from the building, peering through the gap in the door. He saw a group of attendants arriving at the hall, with a beautiful woman sitting in the middle. After the music had finished, the guards were very solemn, and none dared to make a noise. The beautiful woman in the middle suddenly became angry and said, "I heard that Mr. Fang has already left, who is here with such a foul presence, quickly bring him here!"

    In a moment, two young ladies broke in through the door, chained around his neck, and dragged him out to the courtyard. The beautiful woman counted his offenses and said, "You, a rat living in a hole, could not even follow the chickens and dogs in the official's house, how dare you offend the divine couple, your heart is worthy of punishment, quickly beheaded to show respect!"

    The relative knelt and begged for mercy and repentance. The beautiful woman said, "If I kill you, it would really dirty my sword. Now I pardon you from death, you should tell others about it. If you keep it secret, it shows your heart is unpredictable, and eventually I will kill you!"

    She then ordered a hundred strokes of the cane and left.

    155

    次日人来，见戚宛转于地，血肉狼藉，气息仅属，病一月而后瘥焉。每自述其状云云。
    　　市中丐者
