    　　昔通州市一丐者，一瓢一杖，衣不襟，鞋不底，腹患疮臭恶，一市皆掩鼻。逢人则呼曰：“肚里饥，肚里矶！”人与之钱则辞，与之食则不受。如是三日，人咸怪之，谓其饥而不受饷，殆狂人也。及其再呼，则呵之，且恶其臭，议逐之境外。丐者笑曰：“我自肚里饥耳，与公等何与”于是呼更急。忽米肆一少年跪于丐者之前，曰：“师度我，师度我！”丐者大笑，举手对众曰：“我今真度李机矣”遂挟少年凌空而去。少年姓李名机也，其隐语云。丐者去后，市中香三日。

    155

    The next day, people came and found Qī lying on the ground, twisted and distorted, covered in blood and flesh, barely clinging to life. After a month of illness, he finally recovered. He often recounted his condition in detail.

    In the market, there was a beggar.

    In the past, in Tongzhou, there was a beggar who carried a gourd and a staff, wearing clothes that did not cover his chest and shoes without soles. He suffered from a foul-smelling abdominal wound, causing everyone in the market to cover their noses. Whenever he met people, he would call out, "Hunger in the belly, hunger in the belly!" When offered money, he would politely refuse, and when offered food, he would also refuse. After three days of this behavior, people began to find it strange, thinking that the beggar was refusing food despite being hungry, and thus must be crazy. When he called out again, people would scold him, and because of the foul smell, they discussed expelling him from the city. The beggar laughed and said, "I am just hungry in my belly, what does it have to do with you?" Then he called out even more urgently. Suddenly, a young man from a rice shop knelt before the beggar and said, "Master, save me, master, save me!" The beggar laughed loudly and raised his hand to the crowd, saying, "I am now truly saving Li Ji!" He then took the young man and flew away into the sky. The young man was named Li Ji, and this was a hidden saying. After the beggar left, the market was filled with fragrance for three days.

    156

    又，宛陵市一丐者，衣百结之衣，袒其腹。腹患痈溃，脓血被踵，腥秽不可近。大呼市中曰：“谁人舔我肚”人咸怒骂曰：“贱乞，谁舔尔肚者”丐仍呼不止。一判官肩舆而出，遇于市，即降舆跪而舔之。丐及判官皆失所在。
    　　仙人游戏，往往如此。以正道论之，殊不近人情。吁！此其所以为仙人欤
    　　李齐娘
    　　桃源罗敬之，弱冠客岳州。夜有女子款关而入，年可十八九。敬之惊问所由。曰：“妾，君之妇也。念君独处，情不能已，故来相视。”敬之茫然不解。既同寝，向晨而去。次夜，又一女子来，年更少于前女。相见之际，一如前女之言。敬之愈疑，意必非人也。

    Explanation

    156

    In the market of Wanling, there was a beggar who wore a patched-up robe and bared his belly. His belly was suffering from a festering abscess, with pus and blood covering his feet, making it foul and unbearable to approach. He shouted loudly in the market, asking, "Who will lick my belly?" People all angrily scolded him, saying, "Wretched beggar, who would lick your belly?" But the beggar continued to call out. Then, a magistrate emerged in a sedan chair and met the beggar in the market. He immediately got off the sedan chair, knelt down, and licked the beggar's belly. Both the beggar and the magistrate then vanished.

    Immortal games often manifest in this way. From a conventional perspective, it is far from human nature. Ah! This is why they are called immortals.

    — Li Qin娘

    Lu Jingzhi from Taoyuan, a young man in his early twenties, was visiting Yuezhou. One night, a woman knocked on the door and entered. She was around eighteen or nineteen years old. Jingzhi was startled and asked why she had come. She said, "I am your wife. Thinking of your solitude, I could not bear it, so I came to see you." Jingzhi was confused and did not understand. After they slept together, she left at dawn. The next night, another woman came, younger than the previous one. When they met, it was just as the previous woman had said. Jingzhi became even more suspicious, thinking it must not be a human.

    157

    序届残秋，太守李公召客张宴。敬之与焉。厅廨菊花盛开，座客各赋菊花词。敬之诗最佳，太守爱之，欲妻以女而未言。敬之酩酊归。
    　　是夜二女同至，敬之大恐，女曰：“吾姐妹与君，皆订百年之契。虽未及结缡，遽隔泉壤，安忍自疎？君既不安，便当晦迹。李齐娘者，君之佳偶也。彼已有意，宜求之。”凄恨而别。敬之独坐凝思，不得其故。孤灯短榻，泫然不寐。
    　　迨晓而父手书至，乃知作客之后，曾聘同邑崔氏女，小字松翠，年十八而卒。崔不欲与罗断婚，复以次女篁翠字焉。旬日而篁翠又亡。夜来二女，盖其魂也。
    　　敬之悲恸。遗书别太守。即日束装归，求二女之墓而哭焉。阴霾冷雾中，二女形见。他人不见，惟敬之见之，宛然岳阳晤对时也，有顷而灭。敬之感其情挚，不欲再娶，即娶亦必李齐娘，而又不知其何许人也。父亦令访之。遂浪游吴越。

    Explanation

    157

    When the late autumn season arrived, Governor Li invited guests for a banquet. Jingzhi was among the guests. The hall's courtyard was filled with blooming chrysanthemums, and each guest composed a poem about chrysanthemums. Jingzhi's poem was the best, and the governor admired him and wanted to marry his daughter to him, but did not say anything. Jingzhi returned drunk.

    That night, two women arrived together, and Jingzhi was greatly frightened. The women said, "My sisters and I have all made a pact for a lifetime with you. Although we have not yet been married, we are now separated by death; how could we bear to be distant from you? If you are uncomfortable, you should hide your identity. Li Qiniang is your ideal match. She has already shown interest; you should pursue her." They left with sorrow and resentment. Jingzhi sat alone, pondering, and did not understand the reason. Alone with a dim lamp and a short bed, he shed tears and could not sleep.

    In the morning, a letter from his father arrived, and he learned that after becoming a guest, he had previously been betrothed to a local Cui family girl named Songcui, who was eighteen and had died. The Cui family did not want to break the engagement and instead betrothed their second daughter, Huangcui, to him. After ten days, Huangcui also died. The two women who came last night were their spirits.

    Jingzhi was moved by their sincere feelings and did not want to remarry. Even if he were to marry, it would only be Li Qiniang, but he did not know who she was. His father also ordered him to seek her out. He then wandered through Wu and Yue.

    In the dim light of dawn, Jingzhi received a letter from his father, which revealed that after his time as a guest, he had once been betrothed to a local Cui family girl named Songcui, who had passed away at the age of eighteen. The Cui family did not wish to dissolve the engagement and instead betrothed their second daughter, Huangcui, to him. However, Huangcui too had died within ten days. The two women who had visited him the previous night were the spirits of these deceased betrothed girls.

    Overwhelmed with grief, Jingzhi wrote a farewell letter to the governor and immediately prepared to return home. He sought out the graves of the two girls and wept over them. Amidst the gloomy mist and cold fog, the spirits of the two girls appeared. Others could not see them, only Jingzhi saw them, just as they had appeared in Yueyang; after a while, they disappeared. Moved by their sincere feelings, Jingzhi did not want to remarry. Even if he were to marry, it would only be Li Qiniang, but he did not know who she was. His father also ordered him to seek her out. Thus, he wandered through Wu and Yue.

    158

    李太守，越人也。闻已罢官归，敬之以旧谊往谒。太守甚欢，询知敬之犹未娶，夜使媒者道意焉。敬之固辞，因以齐娘之说告。媒者笑曰：“若是，则君为求婚来也，又何辞焉齐娘，李公之仲女，我所执柯者是也。”敬之喜跃，遂允之。盖大守在岳州时，实欲以长女妻敬之，至是长女已他适，故及齐娘云。即其家成婚。
    　　婚之夕，既寝，闻妆台之畔有相对叹息之声。敬之惊问，则答曰“翠、翠”。敬之知为二女，乃谓曰：“二卿既来，何不登吾床吾不复畏也。”一女应曰：“宴尔新婚，岂得相扰”齐娘闻之，悸怖浃汗，急抱敬之于衾中，略不敢动。又一女曰：“阿妹且去，薄命之人，在此奚为郎即不弃，庸不取憎新人乎？”言罢寂然。再问之，则不应矣。由是遂不复见。

    Explanation

    158

    Li Taishou, a native of Yue, had heard that he had been dismissed from his post and returned home. Jingzhi visited him out of old friendship. The governor was very happy and inquired to find out that Jingzhi was still unmarried. At night, he sent a matchmaker to express his intentions. Jingzhi firmly refused, and then explained the story of Qi Niang. The matchmaker smiled and said, "If so, then you came to propose marriage, what reason to refuse? Qi Niang, the second daughter of Mr. Li, is the one I am mediating for." Jingzhi jumped with joy and agreed. It turns out that when the governor was in Yuezhou, he actually wanted to marry his eldest daughter to Jingzhi, but by then, the eldest daughter had already married someone else, so they settled on Qi Niang. They immediately got married in her family.

    On the night of the wedding, after they had gone to bed, Jingzhi heard voices of mutual sighing by the dressing table. He asked in surprise, and they replied, "Cui, Cui." Jingzhi knew that they were two daughters and told them, "Since you've come, why not come to my bed? I no longer fear." One of the daughters replied, "Your new marriage is a joyous occasion, how could we disturb you?" Hearing this, Qi Niang was frightened and broke out in a cold sweat, quickly embracing Jingzhi in the bedding, slightly daring not to move. Another daughter said, "Sister, go away, a person with a short life, what good would it do to be here? If you do not abandon me, would you not hate the new wife?" After speaking, it became silent. When asked again, they did not respond. From then on, they never saw them again.

    159

    囦默真人
    　　囦默真人姓徐氏，金溪菖蒲塘人。妇人孺子能道之。曩阅其家传，粗记其略，追录于此。
    　　真人将生之前夕，异香满室。母梦八人造其堂，类所传八仙状，互相推拥，最后推一跛足者入卧内，盖李铁拐也。既寤而真人生，貌奇丑。数岁颇愚鲁，以是失爱于父母。年十三四，始能言。
    　　值大旱，父命灌苗田间，则高卧树下，竟日不醒。见者以告，父怒，将挞之。真人曰：“父欲灌苗，则苗已灌矣，复何求”往视，则水已盈畦。盖结草为人，置水车上，车自运转以致水也。于是父始知其异。
    　　正月十五夜，与诸昆弟坐谈，忽假寐。既醒，则称曰：“苏州灯戏颇隹。”众嗤之曰：“汝梦游耶”真人曰：“即真去亦何难！”众试求与俱。真人曰：“欲去则当如吾教。”乃肩一伞，令昆弟闭其目，坐于上，共三人。戒之曰：“慎勿开目，开则堕矣。”其从兄素黠，真人于其左掌画一钱，曰：“呵之则钱出。”遂启行。

    Explanation

    159

    囦默真人
    　　囦默真人姓徐氏，金溪菖蒲塘人。妇人孺子能道之。曩阅其家传，粗记其略，追录于此。
    　　真人将生之前夕，异香满室。母梦八人造其堂，类所传八仙状，互相推拥，最后推一跛足者入卧内，盖李铁拐也。既寤而真人生，貌奇丑。数岁颇愚鲁，以是失爱于父母。年十三四，始能言。
    　　值大旱，父命灌苗田间，则高卧树下，竟日不醒。见者以告，父怒，将挞之。真人曰：“父欲灌苗，则苗已灌矣，复何求”往视，则水已盈畦。盖结草为人，置水车上，车自运转以致水也。于是父始知其异。
    　　正月十五夜，与诸昆弟坐谈，忽假寐。既醒，则称曰：“苏州灯戏颇隹。”众嗤之曰：“汝梦游耶”真人曰：“即真去亦何难！”众试求与俱。真人曰：“欲去则当如吾教。”乃肩一伞，令昆弟闭其目，坐于上，共三人。戒之曰：“慎勿开目，开则堕矣。”其从兄素黠，真人于其左掌画一钱，曰：“呵之则钱出。”遂启行。

    Translation:

    Figure of Silence and Stillness
    　　Figure of Silence and Stillness, surnamed Xu, was from Changputang in Jinxī. Women and children could recount his story. Once upon reading his family tradition, I roughly recorded its general outline and transcribed it here.
    　　On the eve of his birth, the room was filled with strange fragrance. His mother dreamed of eight people entering her hall, resembling the descriptions of the Eight Immortals, pushing and supporting each other, and finally pushing a lame one into the inner room, which was Li Tieguai. After waking up, Figure of Silence and Stillness was born, with an extremely ugly appearance. For several years, he was very dull and slow, and thus lost favor with his parents. At the age of thirteen or fourteen, he began to speak.
    　　During a severe drought, his father ordered him to irrigate the crops in the field. Instead, he lay under a tree, sleeping all day. Those who saw it reported it, and the father became angry, intending to beat him. Figure of Silence and Stillness said, "If the father wanted to irrigate the crops, they were already irrigated, so what more is needed?" When he went to see, the water had already filled the fields. It turned out that he tied grass into the shape of a person, placed it on the water wheel, and the wheel turned on its own to bring water. Thus, the father began to recognize his extraordinary nature.
    　　On the Lantern Festival night, he sat talking with his brothers. Suddenly, he fell into a light sleep. After waking up, he said, "The lantern plays in Suzhou are quite good." The others laughed at him and said, "Are you dreaming?" Figure of Silence and Stillness replied, "If it's true, then it's not hard to go!" They tried to go with him. Figure of Silence and Stillness said, "If you want to go, you must follow my instructions." He shouldered an umbrella, ordered his brothers to close their eyes, sat on it, with three people in total. He warned them and said, "Be careful not to open your eyes, if you do, you will fall." His older brother was usually clever. Figure of Silence and Stillness drew a coin on his left palm and said, "If you say 'ah' to it, the coin will come out." They then set off.

    160

    三人坐伞上，如坐椅桌，略不摇撼，但闻耳畔风声呼呼，如百万金甲衔枚赴关，巨浪洪涛汹涌而澎湃也。俄顷已至，便令开目。果见鲸鳞雁足，绮树缯楼，辉煌烂漫十馀里。妙女踏歌，游人如蚁，语言嘈杂，皆作吴音。真人曰：“扬州天津二处，亦不减此盛。宜并观之。”亦次第携之而至。风景不同，语音亦异，赏心悦目。使人忘返。
    　　时夜漏将残，真人笑曰：“可以归矣，”复令闭日，坐伞上如前。从兄中途私计：必腾空也。试开目下视，则已堕地矣。宛转至晓，乃在广信人家茅屋上，扳缘而下。将乞食，忽记掌中画有钱，如真人教，每呵之，辄得一钱以市食，得不饥。五日至家，而画钱乃灭。后有人自苏、扬返者，叩其所见，果不谬也。于是昆弟各异之。

    Explanation

    160

    Three people sat on an umbrella, as if sitting on a chair or table, slightly not shaken. They only heard the sound of the wind whistling by their ears, like a million armored soldiers marching silently towards a gate, with huge waves surging and surging. In a moment, they arrived, and the true person ordered them to open their eyes. Indeed, they saw fish scales and goose feet, embroidered trees and silk towers, splendid and colorful for more than ten miles. Beautiful girls were dancing and singing, tourists like ants, the language was noisy, all speaking the Wu dialect. The true person said, "The two places of Yangzhou and Tianjin are also not less magnificent. It is advisable to visit them together." They visited these places in turn, and the scenery was different, the language was also different, pleasing to the eye and heart, making one forget to return.

    When the night was almost over, the true person laughed and said, "It is time to return," and ordered them to close their eyes and sit on the umbrella again as before. My elder brother privately thought in the middle of the journey, "We must be flying in the air." When he opened his eyes to look down, they had already fallen to the ground, winding and turning until dawn. Then they were on a thatched roof of a house in Guangxin, climbed down. Suddenly, he remembered the drawing of money in his hand, as the true person taught, every time he called out to it, he would get one coin to buy food, so he did not starve. After five days, they returned home, and the drawing of money disappeared. Later, someone who returned from Suzhou and Yangzhou asked about what they saw, and it turned out to be true. Thus, the brothers parted ways.

    161

    又尝游邻耶，大署居停之门曰：“出卖风云雷雨。”见者骇焉。
    　　时五月之交，数郡旱甚。太守闻而召之，真人曰：“野人安可召”
    　　竟不往。使者反报，守怒曰：“妖人惑众，犹敢尔”欲捕之。或劝守姑就之，买雨不效，乃治之。守诺，往见焉。真人命结坛郊外，官吏斋戒，后三日午初当致雨。守归，遵其言，禁内外屠宰。
    　　其日辰牌，真人不至。使二胥视诸其旅，竟不知所往。遍索之，得于东门之酒肆，则烧刀一壶，犬一器，饮啖将尽矣。胥诃曰：“野道不洁如此，乃使官长斋戒耶！当呜于宫，惩尔罪状。”真人以犬耳二枚啖二胥，求秘之。既至，胥以告，守怒甚。真人曰：“是何伤哉”乃张口吐出一犬，缺其两耳，守问耳安在，真人曰：“二胥食之矣。”守乃笞二胥。

    Explanation

    161

    Once upon a time, he traveled to a nearby place and boldly inscribed on the host's door: "Selling wind, clouds, rain, and lightning." Those who saw it were startled.

    It was during the fifth month, and several counties were suffering from severe drought. The governor heard of this and summoned him. The true person said, "How can a rustic person be summoned?" He refused to go. The messenger returned to report, and the governor angrily said, "This sorcerer deceives the masses, yet still dares to do so!" He wanted to capture him. Someone advised the governor to go to him first, buy the rain, and if it was ineffective, then deal with him. The governor agreed and went to see him. The true person ordered an altar to be set up in the outskirts, and the officials fasted and purified themselves. He promised that rain would be brought at noon three days later. The governor returned and followed his words, prohibiting slaughtering inside and outside.

    On that day, at the third watch, the true person did not arrive. The governor sent two officials to look among his followers, but they did not know where he went. They searched everywhere and found him in a wine shop at the east gate, where he had a pot of strong liquor and a dog in a vessel, almost finished drinking and eating. The official scolded and said, "This rustic person is so unclean, yet you made the officials fast and purify themselves! You should report to the palace and punish his crimes." The true person fed two dog ears to the two officials, asking them to keep it secret. After arriving, the official reported, and the governor was very angry. The true person said, "What harm is there in this?" He then opened his mouth and spat out a dog, which was missing its two ears. The governor asked where the ears were, and the true person said, "The two officials ate them." The governor then whipped the two officials.

    162

    将午，真人命官吏拜坛下，戒之曰：“雨至亦勿起。”复取片瓦，覆县令之顶，然后畅快登坛。时赤日当天，晴空万里。真人向东而嘘，则黑云一片起于东。复向西、南、北三方嘘之，云皆随其嘘而起。须臾四合弥天，雷电交作，雨集如矢，自午迄未，甘霖三尺矣。真人拍掌高歌，坛上声乃高于雷。官吏长跪泥泞中，俯伏不敢起，起则雷声震足下。县令以片瓦之覆，周身方丈，雨竟不及焉。盖诸吏多贪刻，惟县令廉明，故真人以瓦相庇也。已而众欢呼“雨足”，真人举袖一挥，则滂沱顿止，阳乌跃出，更无纤云。下坛作别，衣冠洒然，略不沾濡。

    Explanation

    162

    Approaching noon, the immortal commanded the officials to bow at the foot of the altar, admonishing them, "Even if it rains, do not rise." He then took a tile and covered the magistrate's head, after which he ascended the altar with ease. At that time, the sun was directly overhead, and the sky was clear and vast. The immortal exhaled towards the east, and a black cloud rose from the east. He exhaled again towards the west, south, and north, and clouds rose wherever he exhaled. In a moment, the clouds gathered and filled the sky, with thunder and lightning clashing, and rain fell like arrows. From noon until the sixth hour, the sweet rain had fallen three feet. The immortal clapped his hands and sang loudly, and the sound on the altar was louder than thunder. The officials knelt in the mud, cowering and dared not rise, for if they rose, thunder would shake their feet. The magistrate, protected by the tile, remained dry within a square yard, while the rain did not reach him. Because the other officials were mostly greedy and harsh, only the magistrate was upright and fair, and thus the immortal protected him with the tile. Afterward, the crowd cheered, "The rain is sufficient," and the immortal raised his sleeve and waved it, and the torrential rain stopped immediately. The sun bird leaped out, and there were no more thin clouds. Descending from the altar to bid farewell, his attire was neat and dignified, and he was not even slightly wet.

    163

    守大服其抻，将酬以金币，饮以酒。真人曰：“风云雷雨，不须价也。”掉臂径行，追之莫及。
    　　又尝客金陵书肆。市中一人过其前，真人拱之曰：“公何来”其人曰：“来散布红盐。”真人曰：“吾所居乞相庇。”其人诺而去。闻者询其说，真人私语曰：“此荧惑星也。金陵火矣！此宅当无恙。”已而万家焦土，惟书肆岿然。
    　　真人从母贫，以卖酒为生。素爱真人，真人感之。家有井，投以米七粒，令汲之，则酒也。卖之三月，得钱数百千。真人问曰：“获利否”从母曰：“善则善矣，惜无槽滓以饲豚耳。”真人叹曰：“白水为酒，犹憾无糟，甚矣，人心之无厌也！”复投米七粒，而井水如故。
    　　人以其种种奇幻，咸知其为仙，多以“仙”呼之。真人终托术士以自晦，平居孝父母，畜妻子，和协乡里，不失乎人道之常，故人卒莫得以仙名之也。

    Explanation

    163

    Final Translation

    The governor was impressed by the real person's demeanor and intended to reward him with gold and coins, offering him wine. The real person said, "The wind, clouds, thunder, and rain do not need to be paid for." He waved his arm and walked away, and no one could catch up with him.

    Once, he was a guest at a bookstore in Jinling (Nanjing). A person in the market passed by in front of him, and the real person greeted him with a bow and said, "Where are you going?" The man replied, "I am here to spread red salt." The real person said, "I ask for your protection at my place." The man agreed and left. When others asked about this, the real person whispered, "This is the planet Mars. Jinling is on fire! This house will be safe." Indeed, afterward, the entire city was reduced to ashes, but only the bookstore remained intact.

    The real person's mother was poor and made a living by selling wine. She always loved the real person, and he was grateful to her. At home, they had a well. She threw in seven grains of rice and told him to draw water, and it would be wine. Selling it for three months, she earned several thousand coins. The real person asked, "Did you profit?" His mother said, "It's good, but it's a pity there is no lees to feed the pigs." The real person sighed, "Even if white water becomes wine, it is still a pity there is no lees. How insatiable human hearts are!" He threw in seven more grains of rice, but the well water remained unchanged.

    People knew him for his many magical feats and called him a "god" often. The real person ultimately concealed himself as a sorcerer. In daily life, he was filial to his parents, provided for his wife and children, and maintained harmony in the village, never straying from the norms of human conduct. Therefore, people could not ultimately call him a god.

    164

    一日无疾而死，家人殡而葬之矣。适有友人自成都返者，造其家访之，闻已死，恍然曰：“彼殆真仙矣！前日晤我成都市，谓我曰：“尊慈寿期已近，曷不归”吾以为道远莫及，则笑曰：“果欲归，已买舟候君。但去，保无误。”吾素闻其术，慨从之。黄昏解缆，比天晓，已达文昌桥下矣。不挂席，不蚊棹，舟过万重山，直如天上坐，不谓从水中行也。登岸相别，今来致谢耳。夫神妙若此，而岂其死哉”急发其墓，果空棺而己。
    　　后蜕化于武夷山。牧童每出其蜕戏弄之，忽大风摄蜕而去。幼时牧牛山中，偶以鸡卵掷石上，黄白相间，历久弥鲜，凿之不能去。又尝与诸仙试法游戏，或以网兜风，或以篮盛水，或以纸包火，真人以茅担石，其迹犹在焉。武宗召使求雨，辄称旨，封为真人。自称囦默道人云。

    Explanation

    164

    One day, he died without any illness, and his family buried and interred him. Just as a friend who returned from Chengdu arrived, he visited his home and heard that he had died, saying in amazement, "He must be a true immortal! The previous day, we met in Chengdu, and he told me, 'Your mother's lifespan is nearly over, why not return?' I thought the distance was too great to reach, so I laughed and said, 'If you really want to return, I have already bought a boat waiting for you. Just go, and it will be fine.' I have always heard of his skills, so I followed him with enthusiasm. At dusk, we untied the anchor, and by dawn, we had already reached under the Wenchang Bridge. Without raising the sail, without paddling, the boat passed through countless mountains, straight as if sitting in the sky, not knowing it was traveling on water. We parted on the shore, and now I come to thank you. Such divine and wondrous things, how could he be dead?" Quickly opening his tomb, indeed, the coffin was empty.

    Later, he transformed in Wuyi Mountain. Shepherds often took his skin and played with it, and suddenly a strong wind carried the skin away. As a child, herding cattle in the mountains, I accidentally threw a chicken egg onto a stone, and the yellow and white intermingled, becoming more fresh over time, impossible to remove by chiseling. Also, once I played magical games with various immortals, sometimes using a net to catch the wind, sometimes a basket to hold water, sometimes paper to wrap fire, and the true person using straw to carry stones. His traces are still there. Emperor Wuzong summoned him to pray for rain, and he always pleased the emperor, bestowing the title of True Person. He called himself the Silent Taoist.

    165

    今其村有神仙楼，祷祀不绝，其故宅也。尝遗一求雨牌，族人宝之。遇旱面祷，无不立应。后为邻村郭氏借而易之，遂不应。郭氏出真牌祷雨，风雷之中，牌亦不知所在。
    　　吴士冠
    　　吴生杰，字士冠，豫章人。僦居沈氏别业。院有小池，池上桃柳各一株。淡日微风，吟咏其下，帐然有碧云日暮。佳人未来之思。
    　　一夕，镜月初悬，遥见人影徘徊桃花下。促视之，乃一丽人，云鬟霞脸，衣浅绎衣。见生，欲避去，生引其裾曰：“天风吹来，复任其吹去耶？”绛衣曰：“妾西邻某氏之女也。爱此夜景弥佳，故来游赏。”生求与俱。至室中，绛衣曰：“妾非能无意宋玉者。然此时羞颜所不能及，且恐家人见迹，当俟诸他日。”生不得已，与之盟而纵之去。自是日扫榻整裀，以待佳期矣。

    Explanation

    165

    In his village, there stands a Fairy Pavilion, where prayers and sacrifices never cease; it is his former residence. He once left behind a rain-seeking tablet, which the clan treasures. When faced with drought, they pray, and there is no failure to respond immediately. Later, it was borrowed by a neighboring village's Guo family and replaced, and then it no longer responded. The Guo family took out the real tablet to pray for rain, in the midst of wind and thunder, the tablet also disappeared.

    Wu Shiguan
    Wu Shengjie, styled Shiguan, is a person from Yuzhang. He rents a residence in Shen's subsidiary estate. The courtyard has a small pond, with a peach tree and a willow tree each on it. Under the dim sun and gentle breeze, he recites poetry, feeling as if the sky is filled with green clouds at dusk. He longs for the thought of a beautiful lady who has not yet come.

    One night, the mirror-like moon hangs in the sky. He saw a shadow moving about under the peach blossoms from a distance. He looked closer and saw it was a beautiful woman, with hair like clouds and a face like a sunset, wearing a light purple robe. Seeing him, she wanted to avoid him and leave. He pulled her skirt and said, "The heavenly wind blew, but should we let it blow away again?" The woman in red said, "I am the daughter of someone from the west side of my neighbor's house. I love this night scene even more, so I came to enjoy it." He asked her to go together. When they reached the room, the woman in red said, "I am not one who can be indifferent to Song Yu. But at this moment, my blush cannot reach. And I am afraid that my family will notice my traces, so I should wait for another day." Wu Shiguan had no choice but to swear an oath with her and let her go. From then on, he swept the bed and arranged the bedding every day, waiting for the good time.

    166

    越三日，夜初，有扣环声。急启门，一女郎径入，绿衣翠袖，并非前日所期者，面容态冶艳不相下。生讶之，方欲启问，而女郎遽骇曰：“此非阿姨之家耶吾误耶”即欲去。生持之，笑曰：“谁为卿阿姨即此是也。”女且怒且笑曰：“此真冤苦！”生闭门迫之，女不得已，从焉，谓生曰：“妾家去此伊迩，因阿姨遣婶相召，误至君所，殆亦夙缘。今当赴阿姨招矣。”生请后期，女答以伺便当至，遂送之出门。时生僦居未久，且足迹不甚出门户，固未悉邻氏之谁何也。但觉馀情剩馥，描写中怀，竟不成寐。

    Explanation

    166

    After three days, at the beginning of the night, there was a sound of knocking on the door. The man quickly opened the door, and a woman entered directly, dressed in green with jade-green sleeves, not the person he had expected. Her face and demeanor were equally beautiful and captivating. The man was surprised and was just about to ask her who she was, but the woman suddenly started and said, "Is this not the aunt's house? Did I make a mistake?" She immediately wanted to leave. The man held her and smiled, saying, "Who is your aunt? It's me." The woman both scolded and laughed, saying, "This is truly unfair." The man closed the door and forced her to stay, and she reluctantly agreed. She said, "My house is nearby. My aunt sent me to be summoned, but I mistakenly came here. Perhaps it is a predestined connection." She said she must now go to the aunt's call. The man asked for a future meeting, and she agreed to come when convenient. He then escorted her out the door. At that time, the man had only recently rented the house, and he did not often leave his home, so he did not know who his neighbors were. But he felt lingering emotions and could not sleep.

    167

    少顷，又闻扣环声，窃意绿衣复至，喜而纳之。映烛而观，则宛转低鬟，绛衣长袖，向者之花下人矣。生益喜，私心窃谓一时顿得两玉人，从容抚之曰：“侍卿日久，今夕乃来，然真信人也。”绎衣不语，而眉黛间微有愁怨之色。叩之，亦不言，终宵而去。
    　　次夕，绿衣者复至，曰：“昨得恃君于，归而心醉，因成拙诗一首以志幸，可呈教否？”生狂喜索观。绿衣袖出一碧笺，字画端丽，诗曰：

    Explanation

    167

    A short while later, he heard a knock on the door and suspected that the green-robed woman had returned. Delighted, he let her in. By the candlelight, he saw her gracefully lowering her hairpin, dressed in a red robe with long sleeves, the same woman who had been under the flowers before. The scholar was even more delighted and whispered to himself, "I have served you for a long time, but she came tonight. Yet, she is indeed a trustworthy person." Wu Yi remained silent, and a slight look of sorrow or resentment appeared in her eyebrows. He asked her, but she also did not speak, and she left at the end of the night.

    The next night, the green-robed woman returned and said, "Yesterday, I was able to rely on you. I returned feeling intoxicated in spirit, and therefore composed a humble poem to record my happiness. May I present it for your instruction?" The scholar was ecstatic and asked to see it. She took out a blue note from her sleeve. The writing was elegant and beautiful. The poem read:

    168

    “小院春愁听子规，风前舞断小腰肢。
    　　韩郎忽走章台马，炯散红楼月上时。”
    　　生赞赏不已，笥而藏之，若获至宝。
    　　是夜绸缪缱绻，倍觉风流。绿衣临去，谓曰：“妾父母颇不戒，得恣往来。然恐过扰君子，当定期而至。”生正念两女频来，必且相值，岂得晏然乃订以越宿一至。
    　　次夜绛衣复来，妖娆谐谑，不复如前之缄默，而舌锋铦利，多含讥刺，若知有绿衣之事者。虽百端隐秘，终不释。将晓，临去，亦请期。生阴幸其言，因伪请连夕。而女不许，遂亦订以越宿，盖奇日也。而绿衣之约乃偶日，故偎红倚绿无虚夕，而竟不相值。
    　　生一日昼坐无聊，出绿衣诗观之，即于纸尾属和。既毕，压置砚匣下。是夜绛衣至，谈次，屡翻阅案头书册，复玩弄其笔墨不休。生曰：“美人亦解吟咏乎”绛衣曰：“诚恐贻羞大雅。然鄙人之志，不可默也。”遂索笺书二绝云：

    Explanation

    168

    In the small courtyard, spring sorrow is heard in the cuckoo's call, and in the wind, the slender waist is broken in graceful dance.
    The young man suddenly rides off on a spirited horse, leaving behind the bright, scattered light of the moon rising over the red building.
    The protagonist admires it endlessly, storing it away in a box as if gaining a treasure.
    That night, they are tender and affectionate, feeling even more romantic. As the green-clad woman departs, she says, "My parents are quite disapproving, so I can freely come and go. But I am afraid of disturbing you too much, so I will come at regular intervals." The protagonist, thinking that if both women visit frequently, they might meet, agrees to a visit the next night.
    The following night, the red-clad woman returns, flirtatious and teasing, no longer as silent as before, but with a sharp tongue, often containing sarcasm, as if aware of the green-clad woman's situation. Despite many attempts to conceal it, it remains revealed. Just before dawn, as she departs, she also asks for a future meeting. The protagonist secretly delights in her words and feigns a request for consecutive nights. But the woman refuses, so they agree to meet the next night, which is an odd day. The green-clad woman's agreement is on an even day, so they alternate visits without meeting each other.
    One day, during the day, the protagonist sits idly, takes out the green-clad woman's poem to read, and writes a response at the bottom of the paper. After finishing, he places it under the inkstone box. That night, the red-clad woman arrives, during the conversation, she frequently flips through the books on the table, and plays with the pen and ink incessantly. The protagonist says, "Can the beautiful woman also compose poetry?" The red-clad woman replies, "I am afraid it would be a shame to the refined and elegant. But my humble aspirations cannot remain silent." She then asks for two short poems to be written on a note.

    169

    “镇日无言忆玉真，天台明月是前身。
    　　芳声孤负襄阳赋，偏让灵和殿里人。”
    　　“为谁消恨助谁娇红雨丹霞自寂寥。惆怅刘郎并阮客，断魂翻在灞陵桥。”
    　　生览之，虽觉讽己，而惊其才藻，乃虽赞曰：“雅有唐音，真扫眉才子矣”绛衣笑曰：“谬赏所不敢当，第比章台柳何如”生愕然曰：“何谓也”绛衣即于砚匣下取绿衣诗读之，曰：“谓此耳。”生不胜惭，遂告之，巳求相容。绛衣曰：“非有他意，直以此赋诗者非入耳。恐伤郎君，宜远之。”
    　　生犹未信。忽有排闼而入者，乃绿衣也，指绛衣骂曰：“汝本妖妄，乃间我乎！”绛衣亦骂曰：“颠狂婢子，只合向长安道上，牵行人衣袂，何得撞入武林源诱人渔郎耶”绿衣曰：“吾先人九烈君好奖士类，曾以蓝袍赠李秀才，李遂登第。词人学士往往称之。即清风亮节如陶彭泽，犹心折焉。安所谓颠狂，为汝轻薄随流者口实也？且即有是，于汝何与而妒若此，岂犹谓阮宣之妇剑锋不利耶”

    Explanation

    169

    For the entirety of the source text provided, the final translation would be:

    "Day after day, without a word, I recall the true essence of Yu Zhen; the bright moon of Tiantai Mountain is my former life.
    The good reputation fails to live up to the Xiangyang assignment, and it is especially let to the people inside Linghe Hall."
    "For whom to dispel anger, to help whom to be charming, the red rain and red clouds naturally lonely. Melancholy Liu Lang and Ruan Guest, the broken spirit unexpectedly at Bailin Bridge."
    The student read it, though it felt like it was mocking him, but was amazed by its talent and style, and thus praised, saying, "Elegant and has the style of the Tang dynasty, truly a talented man with a graceful appearance." The red-clothed woman smiled and said, "I dare not accept such praise, but how does it compare to the willow by Changtai?" The student was startled and asked, "What do you mean?" The red-clothed woman then took the green-clothed poem from under the inkstone box and read it, saying, "It means this." The student was deeply ashamed and confessed, seeking her forgiveness. The red-clothed woman said, "There is no other intention, it's just that this poem isn't appealing to my ears. I'm afraid it will hurt you, you should stay away from it."

    The student still did not believe. Suddenly, someone burst in, it was the green-clothed woman, pointing at the red-clothed woman and cursing, "You are originally an illusion, why are you meddling in my affairs!" The red-clothed woman also cursed, "You crazy servant, you should be on the road to Chang'an, pulling people's clothes, how could you enter the source of Wulin and lure fishermen!" The green-clothed woman said, "My ancestor, the Nine Loyal Lord, loved to encourage scholars, once gave a blue robe to Li Xiucai, Li then passed the imperial examination. Poets and scholars often praised him. Even if he is as virtuous and upright as Tao Yuanming, he still admires him. How can you call it crazy, it's just what those who are frivolous and follow the crowd say. Even if there is something like that, what does it have to do with you that you are so jealous? Are you still saying that the sword of Ruan Xuan's wife is not sharp?"

    170

    是时生意惊魄骇，莫所知云，但曰：“不佞之罪，不佞之罪！”
    　　二女争辩殊不息，久之，乃相谓曰：“郎君何罪！皆汝我之孽。既已言泄，安可复留自后当相戒绝迹，再至者，宜尝斧刃！”生凄然曰：“二卿何相轧之深也？鄙人方田联芳，乃遽作此断肠语，吾将何以为情哉”二女曰：“君勿恋，缘尽矣。世间繁华，无不撒手，而况娇花弱絮乎”遂趋出，俱失所在。
    　　后微叩邻人，并无所谓二女者。但见桃花带雨，狼藉残红，柳线含烟，飘摇惨绿，尚有洒泪含颦之态，二女殆桃柳之精也。叹息永日，祷请终宵。每当淡月微风，双影摇动，辄疑娇魂丽魄翩然而来，卒亦无有搴帘而入者，而生亦自此病矣。思念之诚，至于心死，乃赋《醉春风》一阕以自伤，云：

    Explanation

    170

    At that moment, the protagonist was deeply shocked and confused, unable to understand what was happening. He simply said, "It is my fault, it is my fault!" The two women argued fiercely, and after a long time, they finally said to each other, "What has the young master done wrong? It is all our sins. Since the secret is out, how can we stay? From now on, we will warn each other and disappear. Those who come again should face the blade!" The protagonist spoke sadly, "Why do the two of you quarrel so deeply? I am just a simple farmer, and now you speak such heart-wrenching words. What will I do with my feelings?" The two women replied, "Do not cling to the past, the connection is over. Everything in the world is fleeting, and how much more so delicate and fragile things like beautiful flowers and soft fluff?" They then hurriedly left, and were nowhere to be seen. Later, he asked the neighbors, but no one had seen the two women. He only saw peach blossoms with raindrops, scattered petals, willow branches hanging in mist, swaying in a sad green hue, still showing signs of tears and sorrow. The two women must be the spirits of the peach and willow trees. He sighed for the rest of the day and prayed all night long. Whenever there was a gentle moon and a soft breeze, he would see two shadows moving and suspect that the beautiful spirits had come. But in the end, no one pulled the curtain and entered, and the protagonist fell ill from this. His sincere longing led him to the point of heartbreak, and he composed a poem titled "Drunk by the Spring Wind" to mourn his own sorrow, which goes as follows:

    171

    “柳外仓庚唤，花间蝴蝶散。东风吹老艳阳天，叹叹叹！前度刘郎，当年张绪，一般凄断。独倚雕栏畔，情根谁剖判相思相见定何时算算算！除是来生，现身花柳，才完公案。”
    　　久之，移疾归。
    　　耳食录卷八
    　　章琢古妻
    　　友人朱青谷述一事：有林甲者，素有心疾，心之所向，魂辄随之。
    　　一日，仰视飞雁，见其翱翔自得，心羡之。入宣而病，忽觉魂游舍外，旋有一人导之去。见一王长颈鸟噱，旁立者皆肖其形。王谓甲曰：“闻有狮天之志。凌云之想，宁欲羽化乎”因命一人持羽衣衣之。甲方逊让，自视其身则已雁矣。遂与群雁俱翔，海碧天青，唯其所向，写彭蠡之姻沙，宿潇湘之芦苇。忽有持弓挟弹、追而弋之者，群雁皆善避，唯甲不习，遂中左翅，嗷然而坠。

    Explanation

    171

    The Oriole Calls Beyond the Willow Trees, Butterflies Disperse Among the Flowers. The East Wind Blows the Bright Sunny Day Old, Sighing! The Former Liu Lang, Zhang Xu of Old, Both Are Equally Heartbroken. Alone, I Stand by the Carved Railing, Who Can Dissect the Root of Emotion? When Will We Meet in Longing, Calculate, Calculate! Only in the Next Life, Appearing Among Flowers and Willows, Can the Case Be Resolved.

    After a long time, he moved to a new place and returned home due to illness.
    Ears of Grain Record, Volume Eight
    The Wife of Zhang Zhuo
    A friend named Zhu Qinggu recounted a story: There was a man named Lin Jia who had always suffered from a heart ailment. Whenever his heart desired something, his soul would follow it.

    One day, he looked up at flying geese, seeing them soar freely and happily, and he envied them. He fell ill and suddenly felt his soul wandering outside his house. Soon, a person led him away. He saw a king with a long neck and a bird-like appearance, with others standing beside him who resembled his form. The king said to Lin Jia, "I have heard of your ambition to soar to the sky and your desire to transcend the clouds. Would you like to become a bird?" He then ordered someone to dress Lin Jia in a feathered robe. Lin Jia hesitated and looked at himself, realizing that he had already turned into a goose. He then flew with the flock of geese, the sea was blue and the sky was clear, and they flew wherever they pleased, writing in the sand of Pengli and lodging in the reeds of Xiaoxiang. Suddenly, there was someone holding a bow and shooting arrows, chasing and trying to shoot them. The geese all knew how to avoid danger, but Lin Jia was inexperienced and was shot in the left wing, falling down with a cry.

    172

    昏痛之际，倏已魂返，呻吟床褥，跃然而起。问诸家人，言已死半日，唯气尚未绝耳。犹记弋者为族子某，急使人告其故，则主人之雁，已为其不呜而烹之矣。
    　　又一日，临渊羡鱼，既归而魂离，遂往浴于渊。有一鱼头人引之至一处，宫殿皆水晶所构。其中人语曰：“子非鱼，何以知鱼之乐今当使尔为鱼也。”甲已惩羽族之苦，不欲更为异物，急辞不愿。忽一人持一衣覆之，投之深池，觉五官百骸都非其故，悟己身已鱼服矣。游泳清湾，依跃浅渚，侣虾蟹而戏萍藻，乘风雨而驾波涛，颇谓潜鳞差胜飞翼。然苦饥无食，唯淰水吞沙耳。间遇岸上垂纶，纶端之物芳香可味，熟视猛省，知其饵也，即掉尾不顾。后馋甚垂涎，聊一吞之，则钩挂其腮，已上七尺竿头矣。

    Explanation

    172

    In the midst of pain and darkness, suddenly the soul returned, moaning on the bed, and leapt up abruptly. Asking the family members, they said he had been dead for half a day, but his breath was not yet gone. He still remembered that the hunter was a cousin named [name], who hurriedly sent someone to inform the reason, and the master's goose had already been cooked because it did not cry.

    On another day, standing by the water, envying the fish, after returning, the soul departed, and he went to bathe in the water. A fish-headed person led him to a place where the palaces were all made of crystal. The person there said, "You are not a fish, how can you know the joy of fish? Now I will make you into a fish." Having been punished by the suffering of the feathered race, he did not want to become another creature, and quickly declined. Suddenly, a person held a robe over him and threw him into the deep pool, feeling that all his senses and bones were no longer as before, realizing that his body had transformed into that of a fish. Swimming in the clear bay, leaping on the shallow shoal, playing with shrimp and crabs among the duckweed and algae, riding the wind and rain, driving the waves, he thought that the hidden scales (fish) were slightly better than the flying wings (birds). However, suffering from hunger with no food, only swallowing water and sand. Occasionally encountering the line of a fisherman on the shore, the object at the end of the line smelling fragrant and tasting good, looking closely, suddenly realizing it was the bait, shaking the tail and not looking back. Later, being very hungry, licking the lips, and swallowing it, then the hook caught its gill, already on the top of a seven-foot pole.

    173

    视垂钓者，乃邻人之仆，因大呼：“舍我我乃林某也。”仆略不顾，欣然有喜色，脱其钩，以杨柳贯之。复大呼“勿贯”，即又不闻，提之以归。遇邻人于门，遂呼“公速救我”。邻人殊不识，但曰：“尚鲜尚鲜，速剖而烹之，可用佐饮膳。”甲窘甚，大骂曰：“我与尔比邻有年，今不相救，反烹我乎何凶残若此！”亦无应者。乃取以畀其妻。甲又连呼曰：“我也！奈何烹我！”其妻即又不答，乃携之庖厨。百端呼号，皆不省。既被刃，大叫一声，乃从床上惊觉耳。视诸邻人，鱼固俨然在釜也，云：“向见鱼口唼唼不已，实不闻声。”
    　　甲因自思，一心之动，便已易形，致受弓刀汤火之苦，以后遇物，绝不敢生歆羡想。然而化龟化鹤化牛化犬，仍不一而止，盖用心既滑，略动则应之，不必羡也。而所化无不被祸者，被祸乃得返。唯无知之物，虽羡之亦不能化。家人知其如此，每见其淹淹欲绝，亦殊不经童。因是或数日，或数月，似死非死，而卒亦不死。

    Explanation

    173

    Seeing the one who was fishing, it was the servant of a neighbor. He then shouted loudly, "Leave me, I am Lin." The servant barely paid attention and showed a joyful expression. He removed the hook and threaded it with a willow branch. He then shouted again, "Do not thread it," but it was not heard. He took it and went back home. Meeting the neighbor at the door, he then called out, "Sir, save me quickly!" The neighbor did not recognize him at all, but said, "It is still fresh, quickly cut and cook it, it can be used to accompany the meal." Jia was very embarrassed and shouted, "I have been neighbors with you for years, now you do not save me, instead cook me? How cruel you are!" No one responded. He then took it and gave it to his wife. Jia shouted again, "I am me! Why are you cooking me!" His wife did not answer and took it to the kitchen. He shouted in many ways, but no one understood. After being cut, he shouted once and woke up from his bed. Looking at the neighbors, the fish was still in the pot. They said, "We saw the fish's mouth moving constantly, but we did not hear any sound."

    Jia then thought to himself, that a single thought could change his form, leading to the suffering of arrows, knives, boiling water, and fire. From then on, whenever he encountered something, he would never dare to harbor any desires. However, he still transformed into a turtle, a crane, a cow, or a dog, one after another, because his mind had become slippery, and any slight movement would trigger a transformation, without needing to desire it. And whatever he transformed into, he would always suffer misfortune, and only after suffering would he return to his original form. Only those who were ignorant could not transform, even if they desired it. His family knew this, and every time they saw him lying there on the verge of death, they were not surprised. Because of this, he would sometimes be like this for several days, or sometimes for several months, neither dead nor alive, but ultimately he did not die.

    174

    其友人章琢古妻陶氏，丽色也。以病死，经日忽活，亲爱有加，而验其性情嗜好。声音举动，绝不类向时。闺阁中多作友朋契阔语，而床笫之情或寡。章每谓重订三生，便成隔世也。妻亦言不自知其故，并不记有向时情事者。时或束带加冠，作男子容状，见宾客常不避，或见他姓女流，反避焉。章颇患之。
    　　一日，甲之弟乙来访章。妻见乙，急前抱持痛哭，呼：“吾弟无恙”乙甚骇。而章甚怒，意其病狂也，牵而闭之室中。妻仍呼不止。乙恐犯嫌，即辞去。妻恸哭至暮。章素怜之，寻常不敢忤其色，至是怒其辱已，切责之，声色俱厉。妻略不悔，亦不辩，唯求死不已。章无如何也，反以温言慰之。妻曰：“我死志已决。欲我不死，须共如林氏，乃可。”诘其故，仍不肯言。章不得已，从之。既至林氏室，妻忽僵仆于地，气已绝矣。章惊悼而呼，观者环集，共相嗟讶。章既不知其妻暴死为何故，众又不知暴死之人为何人，莫不以为奇绝矣。

    Explanation

    174

    His friend Zhang Zugu's wife, Tao Shi, was a woman of great beauty. She died from an illness, but after a day, she suddenly revived. Her affection for him increased, but upon examining her temperament and preferences, they were completely different from before. Her voice and actions were unlike those of the past. In the inner chambers, she often spoke of parting and reunion among friends, while the intimacy of the marital bed was perhaps sparse. Zhang often said that even if they were to reconfirm their fate across three lives, it would become like another world. The wife also said she did not know the reason for it and did not remember any of the past events. Sometimes, she would tie up her hair and wear a hat, appearing in a male guise. She often did not avoid seeing guests, but when she saw women of other surnames, she would avoid them. Zhang was greatly troubled by this.

    One day, Yi, the younger brother of Jia, came to visit Zhang. The wife saw Yi, rushed forward to embrace him and wept bitterly, crying out: "My brother is safe!" Yi was greatly startled. But Zhang was very angry, thinking she had gone mad. He dragged her into the room and locked her in. The wife continued to call out. Yi feared causing suspicion, so he immediately took his leave. The wife wept bitterly until dusk. Zhang usually pitied her and dared not go against her wishes, but now, angered by her disgrace to himself, he severely reprimanded her, his voice and expression stern. The wife showed no regret, nor did she defend herself, only insisting on death. Zhang had no choice but to comfort her with gentle words. The wife said: "My resolve to die is firm. If you want me not to die, you must let me be like Lin Shi." When asked for the reason, she still refused to speak. Zhang had no choice but to comply. Once they arrived at Lin Shi's room, the wife suddenly collapsed on the ground, her breath gone. Zhang was shocked and mournful, calling out, and onlookers gathered around, all expressing astonishment. Zhang did not know why his wife suddenly died, and the crowd did not know who the person who died suddenly was, all of them thinking it was extremely strange.

    175

    忽一人自内鼓掌而出，曰：“吾友欺人太甚！乃使友人荐枕耶”视之，乃甲也。章亟叩其说，甲笑曰：“君妻久死，其复活者，乃我也。我向尝见君妻，心惊其艳。一日昼寝，略忆之，则魂已离壳，直至君家。见其尸在地，遂凭之而起，至为君帐中人数月，亦宿缘也。向所以不自言者，惧相对怀惭衾影，且惑吾友耳。今乃得免是役矣。”言讫大笑，章亦失笑。时甲死已数月，至是复苏云。
    　　章闻其向有是疾，信其言之不谬也，舁妻而归。是夜妻乃见于梦曰：“妾死之后，不知竟有替人。虽身有生死之分，而人无新故之别。妾亦克领其情，故久不欲泄。今行与郎长别矣！”恸哭而去。
    　　身没数月之后，始赋永诀，亦可异也。甲疾自是亦顿愈，以他疾终。

    Explanation

    175

    Suddenly, a man came out from inside, clapping, and said, "My friend has been too cruel to others! Has he made his friend a pillow?" Looking at him, it was Jia. Zhang quickly asked him about it, and Jia laughed and said, "Your wife has been dead for a long time, but the one who revived her is me. I once saw your wife and was shocked by her beauty. One day, while taking a nap, I briefly recalled her, and my soul had already left my body, arriving at your house. I saw her corpse on the ground and relied on it to rise, staying in your tent for several months, which is also a predestined connection. The reason I did not speak before was that I was afraid of being ashamed in front of each other and under the quilt's shadow, and to confuse my friend. Now I am finally free from this duty." After saying this, he laughed loudly, and Zhang also laughed. At that time, Jia had been dead for several months, and now he had revived.

    Zhang heard that Jia had had this illness before and believed his words were not false, so he carried his wife back home. That night, his wife appeared in a dream and said, "After I died, I did not know there would be a substitute. Although there is a difference between life and death, there is no distinction between new and old for people. I also understood the situation, so I did not want to reveal it for a long time. Now I will part with you forever!" She wept bitterly and then left.

    After the body had been dead for several months, a final farewell was finally uttered, which is also quite unusual. After that, Jia's illness suddenly improved, and he eventually died from other illnesses.

    176

    青巾儒士
    　　昔乡人某生，以名士自负。白谓能读万卷书，而最小服艾千于，至焚毁其遗集，唾骂不已。他人置之案头，见之亦必掷之地、投之火。每为人言：“时无英雄，使竖子成名。脱千于遇我，当奴仆命之，犹惧其无能为役。”
    　　一日，有青巾儒士来访，谈论古今文献，辩若悬河。生舌战屡屈。最后及艾千子，生作色曰：“此仆平生所最恶，奈何齿之”儒士曰：“古今才人如海，支派不同，安能尽投吾所好但随其性之所近、才之所逮，以为宗尚耳。安见溯江者必废河，渡淮者必轻汉哉！千子即不足学，置之可耳。灭裂诟詈，毋乃不广”生复攻击不已。
    　　时案上有《春秋》一部，儒士因指而问曰：“子之学赡矣。《春秋》见于经传者一百二十四国，能枚举以相示否”生茫然，转叩儒士，儒士因历数如指诸掌。生亟称其博，儒士笑曰：“此特艾公之馀事，君已不能。由此观之，古人亦末可轻也。”即拂衣而出，欻然不见。

    Explanation

    176

    A Scholar in Blue Headband

    Long ago, there was a man from the countryside who considered himself a distinguished scholar. He boasted that he could read ten thousand scrolls of books, but he was the least tolerant of Ai Qianyu, to the point of burning his remaining works and cursing him without end. Others who placed his works on their desks would also throw them on the ground or into the fire upon seeing them. Whenever he spoke to others, he would say, "There are no heroes in these times; it is only the likes of this boy who gain fame. If Ai Qianyu were to meet me, he would be treated as a servant, and I would still fear that he might be unable to serve me."

    One day, a scholar in a blue headband came to visit. They discussed ancient and modern literature, and the scholar spoke as if a river were flowing from his mouth. The man was repeatedly defeated in verbal debates. When the topic turned to Ai Qianyu, the man changed his expression and said, "This is the person I hate the most in my life. Why mention him?" The scholar replied, "The talents of ancient and modern times are like the sea, with different schools and styles. How can they all be to my liking? One should follow their natural inclinations and abilities, taking them as role models. How can one see that those who follow the Yangtze must abandon the Yellow River, and those who cross the Huai must belittle the Han? If Ai Qianyu is not worth learning from, he can be set aside. To tear apart and curse him is not broad-minded." The man continued to attack him without stopping.

    At that time, there was a copy of the Spring and Autumn Annals on the desk. The scholar pointed to it and asked, "Your learning is extensive. There are 124 states mentioned in the Spring and Autumn Annals in the classics and histories. Can you list them all for me?" The man was at a loss and turned to the scholar, who then listed them as if pointing to his palm. The man immediately praised his breadth of knowledge. The scholar smiled and said, "This is just a minor achievement of Mr. Ai. You are already unable to do it. From this, we can see that ancient people should not be underestimated." He then waved his sleeves and left, suddenly disappearing.

    177

    生且惭且惊，遂得狂疾，七窍流血死。
    　　阿惜阿怜
    　　萧生者，词人也，僦居金陵。有拉胡媚娘，甚丽，与生素好。生为赋《媚娘曲》，有
    　　“南国佳人娇于玉，摆乱风前腰一束。
    　　娥眉轻点黛螺新，照得秦淮春水绿。”
    　　“小楼西角断云飞，豆蔻春香犹在衣。
    　　东风乍起庭莺唤，杨花一曲送郎归。”
    　　云云。
    　　一日，有少年造访，飘巾丽服，丰采翩然。自言胡姓，盛称生此诗之佳。坐谈久之，颇相契洽。少年谓生曰：“君独处无绪，敝庐伊迩，屈往住几时，早晚促膝，更为深幸。”生慨然从之。既至，房宇不甚高敞，而缭曲精雅，颇称幽居：有一园，屋数十楹，不施丹垩；而花木之盛，几与平泉金谷埒。处生其中，设供具甚备。少年日至生所，剧谈酣酌，风雅横生。亦能诗，诗多俊语，生由是益爱之。

    Explanation

    177

    Sheng was filled with both shame and surprise, and soon fell into a mad frenzy, bleeding from all seven orifices and dying.
    　　Ah Xi and Ah Lian
    　　Xiao Sheng was a poet, renting a residence in Jinling. There was a woman named Hu Meiniang, who was very beautiful, and he had always been friendly with her. He composed a poem titled "Méiniáng Qu" for her, which included:
    　　"A beautiful woman from the southern lands, more delicate than jade, her waist swaying gracefully in the wind.
    　　Her eyebrows lightly painted, fresh and new, illuminating the green spring waters of Qinhuai."
    　　"At the western corner of a small pavilion, clouds break and fly away; the lingering fragrance of spring still lingers on her clothes.
    　　The east wind rises suddenly, and the courtyard is filled with the calls of orioles; a song of willow flowers sends the beloved back home."
    　　And so on.
    　　One day, a young man visited him, wearing a flowing scarf and elegant attire, with a charming and graceful demeanor. He introduced himself as a member of the Hu family and highly praised Xiao Sheng's poem. They talked for a long time and found themselves very compatible. The young man said to Xiao Sheng, "You are alone and seem listless; my humble dwelling is nearby. Please stay for a while, and we can sit close together, which would be even more fortunate." Xiao Sheng readily agreed. Upon arriving, the house was not very grand but was intricately and elegantly arranged, suitable for seclusion: there was a garden with dozens of houses, no painted decorations, but with abundant flowers and trees, comparable to the famous gardens of Li Feng. Xiao Sheng stayed there, and the young man prepared a lot of supplies for him. The young man visited Xiao Sheng daily, engaging in lively conversation and drinking, with a sense of elegance and refinement. He could also compose poetry, with many clever and elegant phrases, and Xiao Sheng grew more fond of him as a result.

    178

    一日，有小婢诣生，出片纸书曰：“愿录《胡媚娘曲》一读。”
    　　字画端丽。生问此何人书，婢笑曰：“主人之妹阿惜也。年十七，爱诗词。朝来主人探亲城南，故命至此。”生喜极，取碧笺亟书以进。且附一律云：
    　　“落拓单衫客，羁栖小玉家。
    　　遥情牵旅梦，旧句感春华。忽听能言鸟，来传解语花。愿调湘水瑟，弹和洞庭霞。”
    　　付婢持以去。颇为萦念，延颈至暮，音耗不至。
    　　次日，午窗独坐，遥闻窗外低吟，潜步出听。见绯桃花下，一女郎背花而立，手折花枝而嗅之，且嗅且咛，闻末二语云：
    　　“英夸颜色好，能伴阮郎无”
    　　生戏谓曰：“谁家玉人在此羞花乞以手中一枝见赠，看他能伴阮郎否”女郎回身，斜睇微笑，即以花掷之，低鬟转避。生索狂荡，径前持其衣，曰：“阮郎非桃花可伴，伴阮郎者，乃卿也。”遂曳以行，女虽拒之，而步已姗姗移矣。询之，即是阿惜，遂成眷属。

    Explanation

    178

    One day, a young maid came to the scholar and presented a slip of paper on which she had written, "I wish to have the 'Trickster Mieniang's Song' copied for reading."

    The handwriting was neat and beautiful. The scholar asked who had written it, and the maid smiled and replied, "It is the master's sister, Axi. She is seventeen years old and loves poetry and verse. This morning, the master went to visit relatives in the south of the city, so he sent me here." The scholar was extremely pleased and took out green paper to hastily write a response. He also attached a poem, saying:

    "A carefree traveler in a plain robe, a guest staying at the house of the jade maiden.
    Longing emotions tugging at dreams of travel, old phrases evoking the beauty of spring. Suddenly heard a talking bird, came to deliver the message of the speaking flower. Wishes to tune the zither of the Xiang River, play in harmony with the rosy clouds of Dongting Lake."

    He gave the maid to take it away. He was quite preoccupied with it and stretched his neck until dusk, but no word came.

    The next day, sitting alone by the window at noon, he heard faint recitation from outside the window. Stealthily stepping out to listen, he saw under a red peach tree a young lady standing with her back to the flowers. With her hand, she was picking a branch of flowers to smell, while smelling and murmuring. He heard the last two lines, which said:

    "Praising her beauty, can she accompany the scholar?"

    The scholar playfully said, "Who is this jade beauty who is blushing and asking for a branch from your hand? See if she can accompany the scholar." The young lady turned around, smiled with a sideways glance, and immediately threw the flower at him. She lowered her head and turned away. The scholar was in a state of wild abandon and went straight up to hold her clothes, saying, "The scholar is not someone who can be accompanied by a peach blossom. The one who accompanies the scholar is you." Then he dragged her along. The young lady resisted, but her steps were already slowly moving. He asked her, and it was Axi. Thus, they became a couple.

    179

    由是得闲即至，绸缪婉娈，恩情日新。偶语及媚娘之事，生问：“卿兄妹何以知之”惜曰：“媚娘乃吾从女兄。比来音问虽绝，彼处举动，未尝不知。”生因笑曰：“媚娘非媚，惜娘真有媚珠耳，”惜不觉愠见曰：“何相讥也？”从而谢之，犹未解。少年忽至，见之，怒曰：“相待不薄，何乱吾妹？”生惭伏不敢言，少年亟呼：“将吾拄杖来”惜前批其颊，曰：“但许尔卧榻上抱阿郎睡耶”少年笑，因谓生曰：“戏耳戏耳吾妹怜婿太甚，便以妹归君。”
    　　于是开正室，进丽服，焚香蓺烛，设五色氍毹，令行交拜之礼。美婢成行，诸姑毕至。开筵列宴，酒肴络绎，琴瑟铿锵。引至洞房，椒兰四壁，锦帷绣幞。衾枕既具，旧事新翻，愈觉欢洽。

    Explanation

    179

    Since then, whenever he had free time, he would come, and they would be affectionate and tender, with their affection and kindness growing anew each day. They happened to talk about the matter of Lady Mei, and the young man asked, "How do you and your sister know this?" Xie replied, "Lady Mei is my cousin. Though we have lost contact recently, I always know what happens there." The young man laughed and said, "Lady Mei is not charming; your mother truly has a pearl of charm." Xie did not realize she was angry and asked, "Why are you mocking me?" She then apologized, but he still did not understand. The young man suddenly arrived, saw them, and angrily said, "We treated you well, why are you messing with my sister?" The young man was ashamed and dared not speak, while the young man quickly called out, "Bring my cane!" Xie slapped him on the cheek and said, "Do you just allow you to sleep in bed with my brother-in-law?" The young man laughed and said to the young man, "It's just a joke. My sister is too fond of her husband, so she will give her to you."

    Then they opened the main room, brought in beautiful clothes, lit incense and candles, laid out a colorful carpet, and had them perform the exchange of bows. Beautiful maids lined up, all the aunts arrived. They set up a feast, with food and drinks flowing, and the music of the zither and lute was loud. They led them to the bridal chamber, where the walls were fragrant with cinnamon and orchid, and the curtains and pillows were embroidered. With everything prepared, they revisited old stories and found new joy, feeling even more happy.

    180

    生谓惜曰：“今日之事，可谓转败为功。但卿“抱郎”一语，使令兄前倨后恭，此何故也”惜笑而不答。生愈疑，诘之再四。惜曰：“今辛托丝箩，当不复以异类见摈。妾兄妹皆狐也，婢及诸姑亦皆狐也。兄亦能为女，我亦能为男。有李郎者，兄曾夫之，今已溘逝。妾所言，触其旧事耳。”生以情亲，竟不惧，乃更戏之曰：“卿试为男。”惜曰：“是何难但以被覆我，我呼乃启之。”如其言，果作翩翩娈童也，施双角髻，衣绿罗衫，浅绛吴绫裤，美如冠玉，楚楚动人。生抚之曰：“古所称奉馀桃、泣前鱼者，殆不子过。”惜曰：“是何足道！但犬子辈所为，每不屑耳。彼既具男子之形，复享妇人之奉，阴阳淆乱，雌雄倒置，莫此为甚。妾之以女见，不以男见者，诚羞耻而贱恶之也。欲以信君，姑为此态，固已辱矣。愿还本形。”生然之，覆被如前，复成阿惜。

    Explanation

    180

    The translator said to Ah Xi, "Today's events can truly be seen as turning defeat into victory. But your 'embrace the young master' remark made your elder brother act arrogantly at first and then respectfully later. Why is that?" Ah Xi smiled but did not answer. The translator became more suspicious and prodded her repeatedly. Ah Xi said, "Now that I have entrusted my life to you, I will no longer be treated as an outsider. My brother and I are both fox spirits, as are the maids and aunts. My brother can become a woman, and I can become a man. There was a man named Li Lang whom my brother once married, and he has since passed away. What I said was just a reminder of that past."

    The translator, being close to Ah Xi, did not fear and began to tease her, saying, "Why don't you try becoming a man?" Ah Xi replied, "That's not difficult. Just cover me with a blanket, and I will call out when you should uncover me." As instructed, she indeed transformed into a handsome young boy, with two small buns on his head, wearing a green silk robe and light red Wu-style brocade pants. He was as beautiful as jade, delicate and charming. The translator touched him and said, "The ancient saying of 'embracing the leftover peach' and 'weeping over the fish in front' seems to be no exaggeration."

    Ah Xi said, "That's not worth mentioning! But my children and their kind often do things that I find despicable. They have a male appearance but receive treatment typically associated with women, disrupting the natural order and inverting the roles of men and women. That's why I prefer to appear as a woman rather than a man. To prove my trust in you, I will change back to this form, which is already shameful enough. Please return me to my original form." The translator agreed, and as before, she covered her with a blanket, and she returned to her original form as Ah Xi.

    181

    翼日晨起，少年来揖，曰：“夜来吾妹漏言，惟君盛德，勿弃为感！”生指物矢心焉，且附惜耳曰：“尔能教若兄作女耶”惜因谓少年。少年笑而颔之，趋入帐中。须臾而出，花颜雪鬓，浅黛低颦，立于惜旁，莹然双璧。生因浼阿惜媒之，并妻焉，比于历戴。字之曰阿怜，因惜名也。闺门之内，颇称柔淑。
    　　生每偕两女出游，临雨花，渡桃叶，见者羡慕之，以为神仙携偶，下瞰尘寰也。有道士见之，引生私问曰：“君拥此，宁不惧乎”生色变，问其故，道士曰：“君妖气贼神，不治将死。”生固疑二女或害己，乃以实告。道士书一符与之，令持归，系于私处，交接之际，乃能胜之，则妖气可除而元气可复。言罢，飘然竟去。

    Explanation

    181

    Early the next morning, the young man came to greet him and said, "Last night, my sister accidentally revealed something. Only your great virtue, sir, should not be abandoned out of gratitude!" The narrator pointed to an object and pledged his heart, adding to the older sister's ear, "Can you teach your brother to be a woman?" The older sister then told the young man. The young man smiled and nodded, quickly entering the tent. After a short while, he emerged, with a face like a flower and hair like snow, a light frown, standing beside the older sister, as if they were two shining pearls. The narrator then asked the older sister to act as a matchmaker and married them, comparing it to the marriage of Li Dai. He named her Ah Lian, because of the name of the older sister. Inside the household, she was quite gentle and virtuous.

    The narrator often went out with the two women, visiting the Rainflower Garden and crossing the Peach Leaf Bridge. People who saw them admired them, thinking they were like a divine couple, looking down upon the mortal world. A Taoist saw them and privately asked the narrator, "Do you not fear having this?" The narrator's face turned pale and asked why. The Taoist said, "You have a malevolent and supernatural aura. If you do not treat it, you will die." The narrator was already suspicious that the two women might harm him, so he told the truth. The Taoist wrote a talisman for him, instructing him to take it home and tie it to his private part. During intimate moments, he would be able to overcome it, and then the malevolent aura could be dispelled, and his vital energy could be restored. After saying this, the Taoist departed gracefully and swiftly.

    182

    生信之，夜将寝，悬符于两股之间。二女已知，故诘之，不以告。惜怨怒曰：“久同枕席，何太无情，而使妖道窜入闺中乎”阿怜笑曰：“妹勿怒，当擒此野猪，迟则无及矣。”亟令生解符。符已不可解。令卧而烛之，则豕鬃蠕动著胯下，将啖其势矣。生大恐，乃亟呼“二卿救我”。阿惜口：“郎君负心，合受此祸。然我辈芳洁，安肯作乌将军妇哉”阿怜已袖刀，即胯下割之。生楚极昏寐，有倾而苏，见道士反缚于庭柱，二女指以问生曰：“是此物教尔否”生方怨道士卖己，起而挞之。道士低头不语。二女曰：“此野猪魅也潜以隐身符授郎君，欲作郎君胯下物犯吾姐妹，而饮其元精，淫狠极矣！当令复其形而宰割之，为郎君取酒。”道士哀求，二女以水浇其首，倏已化为一豕矣。命婢杀而烹之，味殊甘腴。

    Explanation

    182

    The man believed it and, as night fell, was about to sleep when he hung the talisman between his two thighs. The two women already knew and therefore questioned him, but he did not tell them. The elder sister angrily complained, "We have long shared the same bed and pillow. How heartless are you to allow this demon to sneak into the boudoir?" The younger sister smiled and said, "Sister, do not be angry. We will capture this wild boar; if we delay, it will be too late." She quickly ordered the man to remove the talisman. But the talisman could no longer be removed. She ordered him to lie down and light a candle, and the boar's bristles were wriggling against his thighs, about to devour his manhood. The man was greatly frightened and then hastily called out, "Two ladies, save me!" The elder sister said, "You have betrayed me; you deserve this misfortune. But we are virtuous and pure. How could we become the wives of a black general?" The younger sister had already hidden a knife in her sleeve and immediately cut off the boar's head from his thighs. The man was in great pain and almost unconscious, but after a while, he regained consciousness. He saw the Taoist tied up to the pillar in the courtyard, and the two women pointed and asked the man, "Was it this thing that taught you?" The man was cursing the Taoist for betraying him and beat him. The Taoist lowered his head and remained silent. The two women said, "This is a wild boar demon that secretly gave the man an invisibility talisman. It wanted to make you our sister's man and violate us, and drink your essence. It is extremely lecherous! We will restore its true form and slaughter it to get wine for you." The Taoist begged, and the two women poured water over his head, and he suddenly transformed into a pig. They ordered the maid to kill and cook it, and the taste was exceptionally sweet and rich.

    183

    生饮酒既醉，求二女与寝。二女曰：“郎体己为豕气所中，妾等义不可再辱，请从此辞矣。彼媚娘者，为淫媚过多，为神所怒，责令受生女体，堕入烟花，不复能自变化，竟失本来面目。此吾前车矣！”生闻言惨然，深自悔恨，并问此后能见否。二女凝思久之，曰：“三十年后，相见于少室山下，所以报伉丽之情也。”言罢洒泣，举家望空而去。

    Explanation

    183

    The person drank wine and became drunk, seeking to have relations with two women. The two women said, "Your body has already been affected by the pig's breath, and we, as women, cannot be dishonored again. We must take leave from you. That woman named Mianiang, because of excessive promiscuity, was angered by the gods, and was commanded to take on a human female form, fall into the world of courtesans, and could no longer change back, ultimately losing her true self. This is our example!" The person heard the words and became sad, deeply regretting their actions, and asked if they could see them again in the future. After long contemplation, the two women said, "In thirty years, we will meet again at the foot of Shaoshi Mountain, to repay our marital affection." They finished speaking, wept, and flew away into the sky.

    184

    生坐至天明，视所居，乃牛首山也。松柏荫翳，人迹杳然。
    　　向所熟游，故能识之。旋造媚娘述其事，媚娘亦罔然，不复记忆。
    　　荷袈裟
    　　从曾伯祖讳瞻，言其前身，邻村寺僧也。僧素持念，有修行。
    　　公父讳子惠，长者也，而家颇裕。僧甚慕之，尝语人曰：“吾敲木鱼、宣贝叶以种善果，惟愿来生为公嗣子足矣。”一日，余族有人遇僧于里门，问何之，答曰：“子惠公家去。”已而公生。族人偶过公家，问：“僧至何事”皆曰：“僧未尝来也。适举一子耳。”族人甚讶，亦不言，即造寺访僧，已捏槃矣。问僧气绝之时，即己见僧之时，亦即公降生之时也。盖公即僧转世也。僧之志愿于是乎遂。

    Explanation

    184

    The protagonist sat until dawn, looking around and realizing they were at Niu Shou Mountain. The pines and cypresses cast dense shade, and there were no signs of human presence.
    He had previously been familiar with this area, which is why he recognized it. Soon after, he visited Mei Niang and recounted the events, but Mei Niang seemed bewildered and could not recall them.
    Wearing a monk's robe,
    he spoke of his former life, saying he was a monk from a nearby village temple. The monk had always practiced mindfulness and was devout in his spiritual life.
    The respected elder, whose name was Zi Hui, was a well-regarded figure in the community, and his family was quite prosperous. The monk greatly admired him and once told others, "I strike the wooden fish and recite the palm leaves to sow good deeds, and my only wish is to be reborn as the son of Zi Hui in my next life." One day, a relative of the protagonist encountered the monk at the village gate and asked where he was going. The monk replied, "I am going to Zi Hui's house." Shortly after, the protagonist was born. The relative passed by the protagonist's home and asked, "What did the monk come to do?" Everyone replied, "The monk never came. He just gave birth to a son." The relative was greatly surprised but said nothing, and soon went to the temple to visit the monk, who had already attained nirvana. When asked about the time of the monk's passing, it was revealed that it coincided with the moment the relative saw the monk and also with the time of the protagonist's birth. It is said that the protagonist was reborn as the monk, fulfilling the monk's wish.

    185

    公浑厚有德，蔼然可亲。孩提孺子，莫不依之，相与戏笑狎呢也。席厚履丰，康宁安乐，年八十馀而卒。平生宽衣缓带，双履拖沓，不甚修边幅，人谓之“荷般裟”云。
    　　又，新城僧綖与涂翁相善。一日，翁坐堂上，僧忽至，径入其妇室。翁怪之，就问，则无见也。时妇方产儿，惟一足未下。急使人觇于僧寺，僧已死矣，唯一足尚屈，引而伸之，而儿之一足遽下。遂名世綖，存故名也。长举进士。——与公事甚类。
    　　又，余阅古人说部所记前身事，僧居十之七八。是知轮回因果说本释氏，故其验为特多。而叔子金环，大华念珠，其偶然者矣。

    Explanation

    185

    Translation:

    The gentleman was broad-minded and virtuous, appearing amiable and approachable. Even young children were drawn to him, enjoying his company and playing together. He lived a life of comfort and peace, passing away at the age of over eighty. Throughout his life, he wore loose clothing and had a relaxed demeanor, paying little attention to details of appearance. People called him "Hé Bānsā," symbolizing his carefree attitude.

    Furthermore, there was a close friendship between Monk Chan from Xin Cheng and Old Man Tu. One day, while Old Man Tu was seated in his hall, Monk Chan suddenly arrived and entered the woman's room without hesitation. Old Man Tu found this strange and went to inquire, but found nothing unusual. At that time, the woman was in the process of giving birth, and only one of the baby's feet had not yet emerged. Old Man Tu quickly sent someone to check on Monk Chan at the temple, and found that the monk had already died, but his one foot was still bent. When they pulled and straightened the foot, the baby's other foot immediately emerged. The child was then named Shizhi, preserving the monk's original name. The child later passed the imperial examination and became an official. — This incident was very similar to the gentleman's case.

    Additionally, upon reading ancient novels that recorded stories of past lives, I found that in ten cases out of eight, the subjects were monks. This shows that the doctrine of rebirth and karma originates from Buddhism, and thus its verifications are particularly numerous. However, the stories of Shu Zi wearing a golden ring and Da Hua using prayer beads are merely coincidental occurrences.

    186

    紫衣吏
    　　吾乡某翁，贾人也，饶于财而性吝，学者轻之。翁慨然叹曰：“所以不齿于诸君子者，不学故也。我老大，良已矣。我能教子，安见铜臭者之不书香乎”
    　　其子方七岁，性奇鲁。援师教之，礼隆意盛。师感其厚，训牖百端。十年不能卒一册，虽寻常字义，莫能解也。师不得意，辞去。复请他师，亦未就。
    　　不得已，哲自督之，不住少懈。每夜三更，自携粥及果饵，往助技勤。至则书声琅琅，或对书默视，翁窃幸无师而其勤如此，虽愚可教也。后颇闻人言，其于实欺父，每夜假寐，闻父至则觉，父去则仍隐几耳。翁初不信，细察其故，盏缘住宅与书舍稍隔，未至数十步，有石当道置未安，翁经其上，辄占沓作声，子乃闻而觉之也。其夜密从他道行，掣棒伺窗外，子果昏睡几上。翁气塞，谓“不肖如此，留之且辱己”，痛决之。夜探无救者，其子竟毙于杖下。翁返内，亦不告其妻。

    Explanation

    186

    The Purple-Clothed Official
    　　In my hometown, there was an old man who was a merchant, rich in wealth but stingy in nature. Scholars looked down on him. The old man sighed deeply and said, "The reason I am not held in esteem by the gentlemen is because I lack learning. I am too old now, and it is too late. If I can teach my son, how can I not see that a merchant's son can become a scholar?"
    　　His son was just seven years old and was unusually dull. The old man hired teachers to educate him, treating them with great respect and enthusiasm. The teachers were moved by his kindness and tried many methods to teach him. After ten years, the son could not finish even one book, and even common words and meanings were beyond his comprehension. The teachers were dissatisfied and left. The old man tried other teachers, but they also failed.
    　　Unwillingly, the old man had to supervise his son himself, never relaxing his efforts. Every night at three o'clock, he would carry his own porridge and snacks to encourage his son's study. When he arrived, the son would either read aloud or silently look at the book. The old man secretly hoped that without a teacher, his son's diligence could still make him teachable, even if he was stupid, he could be taught. Later, he heard that his son was actually deceiving him. Every night, the son would pretend to sleep until he heard his father and then pretend to be asleep again.
    　　The old man initially did not believe it but then carefully investigated the reason. Because the house and the study were slightly apart, not reaching dozens of steps, there was a stone in the path that was not placed securely. When the old man walked over it, it made noise, alerting the son. That night, he secretly took another path, hid outside the window, and found the son sleeping on the table. The old man was filled with anger and said, "How unworthy he is, leaving him would disgrace myself," and severely punished him. No one could save him, and his son died from the beating. The old man returned home without telling his wife.

    187

    经两日，将使埋之，忽其子趋入内，叩首翁前自陈：“架上万卷书已尽读，大人何责之深也”翁惊其复苏，叱曰：“尔不死，犹敢妄言”取数册试之，背诵如流，不失一字，皆平生未尝诵及者。翁喜极，复令出与诸儒试，则皆莫能难，反难诸儒。并角文，文亦莫能及。于是皆惭伏流汗，匍匐称弟子。群知其天授，非人力也。向时轻翁者，皆愿纳交焉。
    　　翁详叩其由，子对曰：“是日杖死后，便有二神来相引。至一处，室宇如宫阙，中座一神如王者，指儿谓紫衣吏曰：“是为某翁子，其父教子颇笃，惜是子太愚，宜为易其心。”史乃剖儿心出之，别以一心置儿胸，仍引还。故儿得复见天地，书亦无所不记也。”

    Explanation

    187

    After two days, the old man was about to have his son buried, but suddenly, the son rushed inside, knelt before his father, and confessed, "All the ten thousand volumes on the shelves have been read; why do you blame me so harshly?" The old man was surprised by his recovery and shouted, "You’re not dead, yet you dare to speak nonsense!" He took several books to test him, and the son recited them fluently, not missing a single word—books he had never read before. The old man was extremely pleased and again sent him out to be tested by the scholars. None could challenge him, and instead, he challenged the scholars. In composing essays, none could match him. So all were ashamed, cowed, and sweating, crawling and calling him a master. Everyone knew it was a gift from heaven, not human effort. Those who had previously looked down on the old man now all wished to befriend him.

    The old man then asked him in detail about the cause, and the son replied, "After being beaten to death that day, two gods came to guide me. To a place where the buildings were like palaces, in the central seat was a god like a king. He pointed to me and said to the purple-clad official, 'This is the son of a certain old man; his father was very diligent in teaching his son, but this son is too foolish; it is best to change his heart.' The official then removed my heart and placed a new one in my chest, then guided me back. So I was able to see the world again and remembered all the books."

    188

    后举进士，登仕籍，天下仰之。盏吾乡先辈之擅名于世者。
    　　以传闻异辞，不能确定其为谁。
    　　胭脂娘
    　　王氏为云林巨族，家畜名书古画，累世宝之：美人一辐，化工笔也，——妖姬数人，倚阑扑蝶，——挂于斋壁。王氏子韶，年十六，盖风韵之士，而骛于情。每注画神移，向壁痴语，殆有叫活真真之想，乃题二绝于橙首云：
    　　“何处花间扑螺姝，芳姿宁许画工摹桃源女伴寻夫婿，走入滕王尺五团。”
    　　“立望姗姗来未来，云踪留滞楚阳台。
    　　东风谁道能轻薄，罗縤衣裳吹不开。”
    　　题罢，书款曰：“二八王郎题赠美人”。诸姊妹一粲。父见之而晒，取藏之，韶不敢问。
    　　父死。家稍落。韶舌耕于他姓。有族子无赖，尽窃其家书画卖之。美人图卷，亦未知流落谁手。韶嗒然懊恨，如丧拱壁。

    188

    After passing the imperial examination and becoming a government official, Wang Shao became renowned throughout the land. However, the identity of the woman he admired remains uncertain due to differing accounts.

    The story of "Yan Zhi Girl" is as follows:

    The Wang family was a prominent clan in the Cloud Forest region, and they cherished famous books and ancient paintings, which had been treasured for generations. Among these was a painting of a beautiful woman, a masterpiece of fine brushwork. The painting depicted several bewitching beauties leaning on a railing and chasing butterflies, hung on the wall of their study. Wang Shao, the son of the Wang family, was sixteen years old, a man of refined taste, but driven by his emotions. He would often be so absorbed by the painting that his spirit was transported, and he would talk foolishly to the wall, almost having the idea of calling the painting to life. He then inscribed two quatrains at the top of the painting:

    "Where in the flower garden does a graceful maiden chase butterflies? Her beauty is so exquisite that it cannot be captured by a painter. The women of Peach Blossom Spring seek their husbands, entering the lifelike scenes of Wu Daozi."

    "Standing and waiting, she slowly approaches but never arrives. Her trace lingers on the Cloudy Terrace in Chu. The east wind, often associated with spring and renewal, is said to be gentle, but it cannot blow open her light silk clothing."

    After writing the poem, he signed it as "A sixteen-year-old Wang Shao inscribed this poem as a gift to the beautiful woman." His sisters laughed, and his father saw it and smiled, taking it and putting it away. Wang Shao dared not ask about it.

    After his father's death, the family's fortunes declined somewhat. Wang Shao became a teacher for another family. A wayward relative stole all the family's paintings and books and sold them. The painting of the beautiful woman also ended up in unknown hands. Wang Shao was deeply disappointed and regretful, feeling as though he had lost a precious jade disc.

    189

    他日客洪都，馆于许氏西斋。其东紊，主人之所偃息也，通于内室，客不得入。
    　　一夕月明，松下若有红裳素蓵、倚而招之者。就视之，一十七八丽女也。与至西斋，低鬟无语，而情意殊厚。数叩其名，始答曰“胭脂娘”。质末明，别去。韶意许氏姬妾帷薄不戒者。次夜又偕两女来，皆靓妆丽服，妖娆非常，——一曰绛花，一曰云碧，——缱绻而去。次夜绛花复送粉怜至，亦丰韵天然。前后共四人，承值无虚夕。相见之际，恍若熟识，终不记会遇何所。意四姬曾或共游，相见于柳堤花径间，未可知也。一夕以问胭脂娘，胭脂娘曰：“郎向者赠妾等珠玉，何乃忘之”韶懵然不省，亦弗深究。久之，四姬情益密。韶期以昼见，则皆不可，曰：“无使射工伺影也。”韶信之。后微以叩之旁人，则未闻主人曾有所谓四姬者。心颇疑而不敢问。

    Explanation

    189

    One day, Shao traveled to Hongdu and stayed in the west study of the Xu family's house. The east side of the house, where the owner rested, was connected to the inner room, but guests were not allowed to enter.

    One moonlit night, under the pines, there seemed to be a woman in a red robe and white skirt, leaning and beckoning. Shao approached her and saw that she was a beautiful young lady of seventeen or eighteen. She accompanied him to the west study, lowered her head in silence, and their feelings were unusually deep. Shao asked her name several times, and she finally answered, "Liangzhi." It was still dark when she left. Shao thought that the Xu family's concubines were not cautious about their privacy.

    The next night, two more women came along, all beautifully dressed and extremely charming—named Jiahua and Yunbi. They lingered affectionately before leaving. The following night, Jiahua brought Fenlian, who was also naturally graceful. In total, there were four women, and there was no night without a visit. When they met, it seemed as if they were familiar, but Shao could not remember where he had encountered them. He wondered if the four women had met before, perhaps on a willow-lined path or a flower-lined walk, though he was unsure.

    One night, Shao asked Liangzhi about a previous gift, and she said, "You once gave us jewels and precious stones, how could you have forgotten?" Shao was completely unaware and did not delve deeper. Over time, the feelings of the four women became even closer. Shao hoped to see them during the day, but they all said, "Do not let the shadow hunters watch us." Shao believed them. Later, when he discreetly asked others, no one had heard of the master having four such women. Shao was quite suspicious but dared not ask.

    190

    一夜，四姬并至，皆锁眉敛态，有愁怨之容，韶怪之。曰：“与郎缘分尽此矣”韶惊问其故，不肯言，因泣下，韶亦泣。四姬曰：“妾等各有新诗，愿酬佳什。”云碧诗曰：
    　　“恨杀画眉人，将侬作年少。
    　　凝妆晓夜新，不向青荷照。”
    　　粉怜诗曰：
    　　“素靥低含笑，弓鞋左右看。
    　　碧霞裙上蝶，犹自避齐纨。”

    Explanation

    190

    One night, the four concubines arrived together, all frowning and repressing their expressions, with faces of sorrow and resentment. Zhaohui found it strange and asked them why. They refused to speak, and they wept. Zhaohui also wept. The four concubines said, "Each of us has a new poem, and we wish to present them." Yunbi's poem said:
    　　"Resentful of the person who painted my brows, mistaking me for a young man.
    　　My makeup is fresh in the early morning and night, but I do not face the green lotus."
    　　Fenlian's poem said:
    　　"A pale smile lowly contains a smile, looking left and right at my bow shoes.
    　　Butterflies on my greenish-blue skirt still avoid the fine silk from Qi."

    191

    胭脂娘诗曰：
    　　“晓起伪红栏，口香花上唾。
    　　迟回不启唇，怕弄樱桃破。”
    　　绛花曰：“阿姊辈愁思艳语，诗虽佳，失酬和之意矣。妾当补之。”诗云：
    　　“共得萧郎顾，崔徽写照真。
    　　明晨尊酒畔，凄断卷中人。”
    　　韶曰：“诸卿妙才，团香镂雪，今夕始露。鄙人方寸已乱，不能属和矣。但末识此后犹得相见否？”四姬曰：“在相见不相见之间。”韶不解其语，问之，仍不肯言，遂洒泪诀去。
    　　次日，主人谓韶曰：“君居此久矣，未尝一至吾东斋。”遂置酒其中，邀韶饮。韶入东斋，举头周览。忽见向所题诗美人图，悬于斋中之西壁。而卷中人俨然所遇四姬也，脸晕消红，眼波送碧，犹是夜来带笑含颦之态，——呼之欲出也。韶始而惊，继而悟，久之凄然泪落，累累然和于酒樽中。主人怪之，韶秘不敢言，但言：“此画吾家旧物，其上小诗，盖韶作也。抚今追昔，是以悲耳。”主人亦豪士，毅然还之。

    Explanation

    191

    Yan Zhi Nü's poem reads:

    "In the morning, she pretends to touch the red railing, her breath perfuming the flowers.
    She hesitates, not opening her lips, afraid to break the cherry."

    Jiang Hua remarked, "My elder sisters' poems filled with sorrowful thoughts and beautiful words are indeed excellent, but they miss the spirit of mutual response. I shall compose a poem to complement them." She wrote:

    "Together, we gain the gaze of Xiao Lang, and Cui Hui truly captures our likeness.
    Tomorrow morning, beside the wine cup, the person in the scroll will be heartbroken."

    Shao said, "All of you have wonderful talents, like fragrant incense and carved snow. Tonight, you have finally revealed yourselves. My heart is already in chaos, and I cannot respond with a poem. But I wonder if we will meet again in the future?" The four courtesans replied, "Between seeing and not seeing each other." Shao did not understand their words and asked them, but they still would not speak, and so he shed tears and bid farewell.

    The next day, the host told Shao, "You have been here for a long time, but you have never visited my eastern study." He then set up a feast there and invited Shao to drink. Shao entered the eastern study and looked around. Suddenly, he saw the painting of the beautiful women he had once met, hanging on the west wall of the study. The figures in the painting were clearly the four courtesans he had encountered, their faces flushed with a faint red, their eyes sending forth green waves, still bearing the smiles and frowns of the night before—as if they were about to come alive. Shao was initially startled, then understood, and after a while, he wept bitterly, his tears falling into the wine cup. The host was surprised and asked him, but Shao kept it secret and did not speak, only saying, "This painting is an old family heirloom. The small poem on it was written by me. Reflecting on the present and the past, that is why I am sad." The host, being a bold man, returned the painting to Shao.

    192

    韶拜谢，持之归，供之于衾帷之侧，将之以神明之敬，而祷之以夫妇之私。花月之朝，风雨之夕，饮食未尝不祝，梦寐未尝不怀，而楚楚相对，卒亦无有心痛而从者。韶自是感疾，咏青莲诗曰：“相见不相亲，不如不相见。”遂大恸而卒，时年二十一岁。命以美人图殉葬焉。
    　　衣工
    　　彭君坦斋翔履述一事，余恍然曰：数之定也，岂不巧哉！
    　　杭州吴山，俗呼城隍山。上有八卦石，倚城瞰江，风涛千里。凤皇、秦望而外，此其大观也。初，坦斋从其尊人南昌相国衡文两浙时，画舫青骢，探奇剔胜，而独未尝一至所谓八卦石者。

    Explanation

    192

    韶拜谢，持之归，供之于衾帷之侧，将之以神明之敬，而祷之以夫妇之私。花月之朝，风雨之夕，饮食未尝不祝，梦寐未尝不怀，而楚楚相对，卒亦无有心痛而从者。韶自是感疾，咏青莲诗曰：“相见不相亲，不如不相见。”遂大恸而卒，时年二十一岁。命以美人图殉葬焉。

    The person named Shao bowed and expressed gratitude, took it back home, placed it beside his bed or curtains, treating it with the reverence due to a deity, and praying to it as a private matter between spouses. On beautiful mornings and stormy nights, he always offered blessings during meals and always thought of it in his dreams. Despite their graceful appearance, there was no one who was deeply moved or affected. Shao then fell ill, reciting a poem by Qinglian (a reference to Li Bai): "Meeting without affection, it is better not to meet." He died after a great emotional outpouring, at the age of twenty-one. He ordered a beautiful woman's portrait to be buried with him.

    衣工
