    A tailor or seamstress.

    彭君坦斋翔履述一事，余恍然曰：数之定也，岂不巧哉！

    Mr. Peng Tanzai Xiangli recounted a story, and I suddenly realized: Fate is determined, how skillfully!

    杭州吴山，俗呼城隍山。上有八卦石，倚城瞰江，风涛千里。凤皇、秦望而外，此其大观也。

    Wu Mountain in Hangzhou, commonly known as the City God Mountain. There are eight trigram stones on top, leaning against the city walls and overlooking the river, with rolling waves for thousands of miles. Besides Phoenix Hill and Qin Wang Hill, this is a magnificent view.

    初，坦斋从其尊人南昌相国衡文两浙时，画舫青骢，探奇剔胜，而独未尝一至所谓八卦石者。

    Initially, when Mr. Tanzai followed his father, the Prime Minister of Nanchang, who was balancing the literary affairs of Zhejiang, they traveled in ornate boats and rode green horses, seeking out wonders and distinguishing the best, yet he had never visited the so-called Eight Trigram Stones.

    193

    丙午归自京师，将赴豫章秋闱，取道于杭。阻舟西湖坝，逆旅孤闷。薄暮兴发，径携一仆，求八卦石而登焉。烟景苍茫，方图纵日，忽见深树中隐隐有人。使仆迹之，则一人解带系树，为投缳之计，亟救出之。问其所苦，其人自诉：“本姑苏人，向业衣工。有中表某谓之曰：“于工贱而利微，非长策。倘倾子囊橐，得金若干，与子居货馀杭，当获数倍利。”吾甘其言而从之，遂为所绐，金尽攫去。质衣而食，且尽矣。入云栖寺求祝髪，寺僧不许。计无所出，独来此山。见波涛澎湃、山林杳冥，益增悲涕。箐莽之际，适有带荔衣萝、出而揶揄者，不觉入其彀中。非仁人引手，已尝夜台滋味矣。然涸鱼炉蚁，苟延何为早离水火坑，末为非计也！”坦斋恻然，予之金而劝之归。其人再拜而去。

    Explanation

    193

    On the day of Bingwu, I returned from the capital and was about to go to the autumn examination in Yuzhang, taking a route through Hangzhou. I stayed at the West Lake ferry and felt lonely and depressed while staying at an inn. Late afternoon, a sudden mood arose, and I directly took a servant and went to seek the Eight Trigrams Rock and climbed it. The scenery was dim and hazy, and I just planned to enjoy the day. Suddenly, I saw someone faintly in the deep trees. I sent the servant to follow, and it turned out to be a man who had tied his belt to a tree, planning to hang himself. I quickly rescued him. I asked what he was suffering from, and he spoke for himself: "I am originally from Suzhou and previously worked as a tailor. A cousin told me, 'The work is cheap and the profit is small, not a good strategy. If you empty your purse and get some money, and I help you trade in Yuhang, you will make several times the profit.' I agreed with his words and followed him, but was deceived and all the money was taken away. I sold clothes to eat, and almost all was gone. I went to Yunxi Temple to request tonsure, but the monks did not allow it. Having no plan, I came to this mountain alone. Seeing the surging waves and the dark mountains, I increased my sadness. In the dense forest, just happened to meet someone dressed in wild vines, who mocked me, and I unconsciously fell into his trap. If not for the benevolent person's help, I would have already experienced the taste of death. But like a dried fish and an ant in a stove, prolonging life is better than being in a pit of fire and water, it is not a bad plan!" Tan Zhai felt pity, gave him money and advised him to return home. The person bowed twice and left.

    194

    夫坦斋文洒豪华，虽耽情游览，必朋簪客履，照耀山谷间，非一人一仆徙倚空山者。况素不治其屐齿之地，乃于夕阳暮蔼中仓卒领取哉坦斋之游，为衣工来也，数之巧者也。虽未竟其趣，山灵喜焉。

    Explanation

    194

    The writings of Tan Zhai are luxurious and expansive. Though he indulges in travel, he is always accompanied by companions and guests, who shine brightly in the valleys, not wandering alone with just one servant. How could he, who usually does not manage the places he visits, suddenly take them up in the evening mist? Tan Zhai's travels are for the sake of a tailor, a skillful and ingenious act. Though the interest or fun is not fully realized, the mountain spirits are pleased.

    195

    绿云
    　　福山刘生，假馆乡僻，为童子句读师。盛夏晚凉，散步门外。暮霞层叠，残照满山，眺望间忘其远近。旋有柳车飞至，车中人搴箔语曰：“油壁苦迟，劳君久伫。”视之，十七八好女子也，言词泠泠，如娇簧韵笛。刘愕然半晌，答之曰：“某实不候卿，得毋误否”女子颜赦，微愠曰：“甫读数行讲章，坐破毡，作牧牛儿，两目乃遽无珠耶？”言已，趣车疾去。暮色苍茫，顷刻不见，刘深怪之。
    　　夜渐黑，逡巡而返，顿迷前路。方疑虑间，有数人囊灯而至，相谓曰：“寻着先生矣！”刘意馆人迎己，漫从之。导行乱山中。入一巨宅，讶非故址，欲问之。一人前启曰：“主君候堂下矣。”一老妇立灯光中，绿纱韬髻，短髪星星。见刘，熟视曰：“婶子眼故慧，果不误也。”既就坐，从容曰：“向别尊府时，君犹总角，不意岐嶷若此。堂上人俱无恙耶”刘故朴纳，且末审是何世旧，踌躇不知所对，唯唯谦谢而已。

    Explanation

    195

    Green Cloud

    In the remote village of Fushan, there lived a young man named Liu, who served as a teacher for the children, instructing them in reading and writing. On a warm summer evening, he strolled outside his dwelling. The evening sky was layered with clouds, and the last rays of sunlight bathed the mountains, making him lose track of distance as he gazed out. Suddenly, a willow-decorated carriage flew into view. A woman inside the carriage lifted the curtain and said, "The oil-lacquered carriage is too slow; I've kept you waiting for so long." Upon seeing her, Liu noticed she was a beautiful young woman in her late teens, her words as clear and melodious as a delicate flute. Startled for a moment, Liu replied, "I was not expecting you, have I perhaps mistaken you?" The woman's expression softened, but she spoke with a hint of displeasure, "I just read a few lines of the lesson, sitting on a worn-out mat, acting like a herdsman, and now you've suddenly lost your eyesight?" After speaking, she urged the carriage to depart swiftly. In the dimming twilight, it vanished in an instant, leaving Liu deeply puzzled.

    As night fell, Liu hesitated before returning, suddenly finding himself lost. While still in doubt, several people carrying lanterns arrived and said to each other, "We've found the master!" Thinking they were his hosts welcoming him, Liu followed them casually. They led him through a chaotic mountain range until they reached a large mansion. Surprised by the unfamiliar location, Liu wanted to ask questions. A person stepped forward and said, "The host is waiting in front of the hall." An elderly woman stood in the light of the lantern, her hair covered by a green silk scarf, with strands of white hair visible. Seeing Liu, she looked at him carefully and said, "Your eyes are still sharp, indeed you are correct." After taking a seat, she spoke calmly, "The last time we parted at your respected residence, you were still a child, but I did not expect you to be so mature. Are all the members of your family well?" Liu, being simple and reserved, and unsure of who this woman was, hesitated, unsure of how to respond, and merely nodded humbly in thanks.

    196

    次日辞归，老妇敛容曰：“弱雏失教，欲以西席相屈，幸毋谓棒栗不修，弃其孤婺，实惟盛德！”刘以旧馆为辞，老妇强之，遂留焉。
    　　越日，洁治馆舍，缥缃满室，文具精良。老妇引二女出拜。
    　　长绿云，翠衿碧衫，丰态憨韵，目刘而笑。刘谛视之，即车中人也，心愈蓄疑，然不敢问。次素云，甫垂髫，眉目明秀，衣裳如雪。二女天颖并绝，书一过即了了。刘初授以兔园之学，辄置不一览。喜诵佛经，不假师授，虽格磔钩辀，而梵音清越。间摘奇字叩刘，刘莫能答。刘姿质奇鲁，爱读制科文字，竟日夜咿唔不绝，二女每窃笑之。刘虽惭怒，无如何也。侍女窅儿，亦令佐读，聪悟稍逊二女，而苗条婉媚，便捷可怜；性好嬉戏，柳堤花圃，乘间窃游。刘禁之，不可，以告老妇。老妇曰：“是婢天性固然，姑听之。”

    Explanation

    196

    The next day, Liu returned home. The old woman, with a serious expression, said, "Our young and inexperienced daughter has not been properly educated. We wish to invite you as our tutor, please do not think that we lack discipline and abandon our orphaned daughter. This is truly an act of great virtue!" Liu declined the invitation by citing a previous residence, but the old woman persisted, and he eventually stayed.

    The following day, the study room was cleaned and prepared, and books filled the room. The writing materials were of high quality. The old woman introduced the two daughters. The older daughter, Green Cloud, wore green sleeves and a blue robe, and with a full and charming appearance, she smiled at Liu. Liu looked at her carefully and realized she was the same person he had met earlier, but he became even more suspicious and did not dare to ask. The younger daughter, White Cloud, was just a child, with bright and clear eyes, and her clothes were as white as snow.

    The two daughters had extraordinary natural talent and insight, and they understood what they read at a glance. Liu initially taught them the basics of elementary education, but they ignored it entirely. They enjoyed reciting Buddhist scriptures without the need for a teacher, and even though the pronunciation was complex and difficult, their chanting was clear and resonant. Occasionally, they would ask Liu about unusual characters, and he could not answer them. Liu, with his awkward and unrefined appearance, loved to study standardized examination texts, reading them day and night without stopping, and the two daughters would often laugh at him. Although Liu felt embarrassed and angry, he could do nothing about it.

    The servant girl, Yáo, was also asked to assist in reading, but her intelligence was slightly inferior to that of the two daughters. However, she was slim and charming, and her movements were graceful and endearing. She enjoyed playing and would secretly go out during breaks to the willow-lined embankments and flower gardens. When Liu tried to stop her, it was unsuccessful, and he told the old woman. The old woman said, "This is her natural disposition, let it go for now."

    197

    一日，有陈家姨来，称曰阿锦，华妆袨服，类金屋娇。二女令谒刘，将使授业。叩问家世，刘具告之。阿锦艴然，谓二女曰：“是吾仇也。老母左臂箭瘢犹在，今既相值，庸勿报乎”怀庭下石将投之。二女喻之曰：“怨毒虽甚，不在后嗣。”力劝而止。刘骇然，强谢之，始恨恨去。
    　　又数日，索云从刘受书，背诵如流。刘戏拍其背，遂喑哑。绿云泣曰：“中其要害矣！”驰告老妇。老妇至，亦泣曰：“是儿夙根太慧，宜获此报。非得菩萨杨枝露饮之，不能瘳也。”刘惶恐，因求去。老妇亦不复坚留，命绿云脱金约指付刘曰：“此君家故物，今特归赵。”临行，酌杯酒告曰：“太夫人之惠，末之敢忘。弱息又辱门墙，藉君牖迪。此酒所以报也！”刘立饮之：觉胸中如涤刮，下气大泄，神悟顿开，喉舌亦便利，无复期艾之苦。老妇命窅儿护车，进刘还家，须臾而至。

    Explanation

    197

    One day, an aunt from the Chen family came, calling herself Ah Jin, dressed in splendid and luxurious attire, resembling a beautiful woman in a golden house. The two girls ordered her to visit Liu, intending to have him instruct her. When she inquired about his family background, Liu informed her in detail. Ah Jin was suddenly displeased and told the two girls, "He is my enemy. My mother's left arm still bears the scar of an arrow. Now that we have met, why not seek revenge?" She held a stone in the courtyard, ready to throw it at him. The two girls tried to persuade her, saying, "Though our grudge is great, it should not be passed on to the descendants." They strongly advised her to stop. Liu was frightened and reluctantly thanked her, and she finally left with deep resentment.

    A few more days later, Ah Jin asked Liu to teach her, and she recited the lessons fluently. Liu playfully patted her back, and she suddenly became mute. Green Cloud cried, saying, "She hit her vital point!" She hurried to tell the old woman. When the old woman arrived, she also wept and said, "This child is too intelligent from birth, and this is her retribution. Unless she drinks the elixir from the Bodhisattva's branch, she cannot recover." Liu was frightened and asked to leave. The old woman no longer insisted on keeping him and ordered Green Cloud to take off her golden ring and give it to Liu, saying, "This is your family's old item, and I now specially return it to Zhao." As Liu was leaving, she poured a cup of wine and said, "I dare not forget the kindness of the respected lady. My young offspring also disgraced your family, relying on you to guide and enlighten. This wine is to repay you!" Liu drank it immediately: he felt as if his chest was cleansed and scraped, his lower energy greatly released, his spiritual insight suddenly opened, his throat and tongue became convenient, and he no longer suffered from worry. The old woman ordered Yǎo to guard the carriage and sent Liu back home, arriving shortly thereafter.

    198

    家人方觅刘。既见，皆欣忭。刘具述前事，井出约指观之、刘母识之，曰：“异哉昔尔父蓄二鹦鹉，一母一雏。母白色，雏绿色，并能言。一日悲鸣求去，余怜而放之，系以金戒环，而约之曰：“他日倘相见，以此为信。”即此物也。”急视窅儿，已化为燕子，呢喃而去，门外之车亦杳。复论阿锦之事，盖刘父尝射雉于郊，中其左翼云。
    　　耳食录卷九
    　　王方伯
    　　辽东王公某，少应童子试，自郡归，值日暮。使奴子策马先行寻旅店，王按平辔于后。过一第宅，甚闳敞，有数人侯于门外，叩马而请曰：“姑娘待公子久矣，”王讶然不识，下马入宅。既见，则一少女也，序世次甚洋，乃王之姑之夭死者。王忆果有是，然殊忘其死，遂以从子之礼见。坐言别后事，辞色凄惋

    Explanation

    198

    The family was searching for Liu. After seeing him, they were all delighted. Liu recounted his previous experiences and took out the ring finger to show it. Liu's mother recognized it and said, "Strange indeed, your father once kept two parrots, one mother and one chick. The mother was white, and the chick was green, and both could speak. One day, they mournfully called out and asked to be released. I pitied them and let them go, tying them with a gold ring, and promised them, saying, 'If we meet again someday, this will be proof.' This is that very thing." Quickly looking at the child, it had already transformed into a swallow, singing softly and flying away. The carriage outside also disappeared. They then discussed the matter of Ajin, it seems that Liu's father once shot a pheasant in the suburbs, hitting its left wing, so it is said.

    Ear-Shaped Record, Volume Nine
    Wang Fangbo
    A nobleman from Liaodong, at a young age, took the imperial examination for young scholars and returned from the prefecture as dusk fell. He sent a servant to ride ahead and find an inn, while he rode calmly behind. Passing by a grand mansion, several people waited at the gate, and one of them dismounted and asked, "The young lady has been waiting for the young master for a long time." Wang was surprised and did not recognize her, so he dismounted and entered the house. After seeing her, it was a young girl who recounted the family lineage in great detail, and she was Wang's aunt who had died young. Wang remembered that there was such a person, but he had completely forgotten about her death, so he greeted her with the etiquette of treating her as a younger relative. They sat and talked about what had happened since their last meeting, and her words and expressions were filled with sorrow and melancholy.

    199

    已闻有贵官至门、驺从呵叱声，姑曰：“尔姑丈归欠，可暂避内室。”姑死时，年方二八，实未字人，盖死后匹配者。王亦忘之。潜于壁穴间窥之。俄而姑丈入，面黝黑而貌狰狞。甚可憎畏。忽以手探面，则皮壳顿落，类今梨园中面具，以付其从者收之，则翩翩然美丈夫矣，而年亦少，与姑龄上下。问姑曰：“其人来否”姑曰：“来矣。”呼王出见之。戚谊其殷勤，不啻王导之待何充也。少顷，设饮食，酒馔丰备。王素不胜杯杓，姑强之，王勉为尽觞焉。
    　　旋有书吏呈一牒，令共观之，内书王姓名籍贯，胪列其科笫及事行，体例似年谱。有一页大书六字曰：“承宣布政使司”。姑丈取笔，下注一酒字。审阅未尽数页，遽付吏持去藏之。不令见。酒罢，引之就寝，帷榻茵席亦华焕非常。

    Explanation

    199

    Upon hearing the sound of a high-ranking official's attendants shouting at the door, the woman said, "Your brother-in-law is late in returning, you may temporarily retreat to the inner room." The woman, who died at the age of sixteen, was actually not yet married, as she was matched in death. Wang also forgot about this. He hid in a hole in the wall and spied on them. Soon after, the brother-in-law entered, his face dark and his appearance fierce. He was very terrifying. Suddenly, he touched his face, and the skin fell off, resembling a mask used in the theater. He gave it to his attendant to collect, and then he became a handsome young man, also young, about the same age as the woman. He asked the woman, "Has that person come?" The woman replied, "He has come." She called Wang out to meet him. Their relationship was very cordial, no less than the close relationship between Wang Dao and He Chong. A short while later, they arranged food and drink, with plenty of wine and food. Wang usually couldn't drink much, but the woman forced him, and Wang reluctantly finished his cup.

    Soon, a scribe presented a document, ordering them to look at it together. It listed Wang's name, origin, and family background, detailing his achievements and deeds, with a format similar to an annual chronicle. One page had six large characters written on it: "Chancellor of the Board of Civil Affairs." The brother-in-law took a pen and wrote the character for "wine" below. He reviewed only a few pages and quickly gave it to the scribe to take away and hide it, not allowing it to be seen. After the wine was finished, he led Wang to bed, where the curtains, bed, and bedding were also very luxurious.

    200

    天明视之，乃空山之冢上耳。王自是遂善饮酒，厥后仕至方伯，以事伏法死。其所未见数页内，殆书其犯事当诛之状，故不令见欤
    　　邓生
    　　新城邓生暮行，道逢一人。与语，甚相得。其人曰：“先生至家尚远，何不过舍下一宿，明日再行”邓自意交浅，托言有要事，坚却之。其人拽邓衣，强之行。至则广厦高墉，巍然华屋。主人礼意殷渥，酒肴洊至。女乐杂陈，率皆姝丽婉娈，柔声媚态，奔注于邓。邓素诚谨，略不为动，然渐觉沉醉，初不知身之在魅乡也。
    　　次日天明，有荷担者过，见泥淤中有物蠕动，就视，乃邓也。魔语含糊，不复可辨。亟援之出，则耳目口鼻皆为泥所塞，命在呼吸矣。急去其塞，掌掴其面而后苏。盖其所食者，皆土羹尘饭也。幸不为丽鬼所动，不然死矣。

    Explanation

    200

    When it was light, he looked at it and saw it was just a tomb on the top of an empty mountain. From then on, Wang became skilled at drinking alcohol. Later, he rose to the position of a high-ranking official but was executed for a crime. The several pages he had not seen probably contained a record of his crimes and the sentence of death, which is why he was not allowed to see them.

    New City's Cleng walked at dusk and met a man on the road. They got along very well. The man said, "Sir, your home is still far away. Why don't you stay overnight at my place and continue your journey tomorrow?" Cleng felt that their acquaintance was still shallow and made an excuse about having important matters to attend to, firmly declining. The man dragged Cleng's clothes and forced him to go. Upon arriving, they found a grand house with spacious halls and tall walls. The host was deeply courteous, and wine and food continuously arrived. Female entertainers were variously displayed, all beautiful and charming, with soft voices and alluring gestures, rushing to Cleng. Cleng was usually honest and cautious, barely moved by them, but gradually he felt deeply intoxicated, not realizing that his body was in a place of enchantment.

    The next day, when it was light, a man carrying a load passed by and saw something wriggling in the mud. He looked closer and saw it was Cleng. Cleng's speech was vague and indistinct. They quickly pulled him out, and his ears, eyes, nose, and mouth were all blocked by mud, his life hanging by a thread. They quickly removed the mud and slapped his face, and he revived. It turned out that what he had eaten were all earth soup and dust rice. Fortunately, he was not moved by the beautiful ghosts, otherwise he would have died.

    201

    东仓使者
    　　金溪苏坊有周姓丐媪，年五十馀。夫死无子，独处破屋。忽有人于耳畔谓之曰：“尔甚可悯，余当助尔。”回视不见其形。颇惊怪。复闻耳畔语曰：“尔勿畏。尔床头有钱二百，可取以市米为炊，无事傍人门户也。”如言。果得钱。媪惊问何神，曰：“吾东仓使者也。”媪察其意，非欲祸己者，竟不复畏怖。自是或钱、或米。或食物，日致于庭，亦无多，仅足供一二日之费；费尽则复致之，亦不缺乏。间又或为致衣服数事，率皆布素而无华鲜。媪赖之以免饥寒，心甚德之，祝曰：“吾受神之泽厚矣！愿见神而拜祀焉！”神曰：“吾无形也。虽然，当梦中化形示尔。”果梦中见之，皤然一翁也。久之，颇闻东邻人言室中无故亡其物，其西邻之人亦云，媪乃知神之窃邻以贶己也。乡邻有吉凶美恶事，辄预以告媪，嘱以勿泄。自后验之，无不中。如是者数年。

    Explanation

    201

    East Granary Messenger

    In the village of Jinxi Su Fang, there was an elderly woman of the Zhou family, a beggar who was over fifty years old. Her husband had died, and she had no children, living alone in a dilapidated house. Suddenly, someone spoke to her beside her ear, saying, "You are very pitiable; I shall help you." She turned around but could not see the form of the speaker. She was greatly startled. Then she heard a voice in her ear again, saying, "Do not be afraid. There are two hundred coins by your bed; you may take them to buy rice for cooking, and you need not rely on others for shelter." She followed the words and indeed found the money. The woman was surprised and asked, "Who is this spirit?" The voice replied, "I am the East Granary Messenger." Observing that the spirit had no intention of harming her, she ceased to fear. From then on, whether it was money, rice, or food, it would appear daily in her courtyard, not in large quantities, but just enough to last for one or two days; when the supply ran out, it would be replenished again, and she never lacked for necessities. Sometimes it would also bring several pieces of clothing, all plain and simple, without any bright colors. Relying on this, she avoided hunger and cold, and deeply felt grateful. She wished, "I have received great blessings from the spirit! I wish to see the spirit and worship it!" The spirit said, "I have no form. However, I will transform into a form in your dream to show myself." Indeed, in her dream, she saw an elderly man with a white beard. After some time, she heard from her eastern neighbor that things had mysteriously disappeared from their home, and the western neighbor also reported the same. The woman then realized that the spirit was stealing from her neighbors to bestow upon her. The spirit would always inform her of good or bad events happening among the neighbors, instructing her not to reveal the source. After this, everything she heard proved to be true. In this way, it continued for several years.

    202

    初，邻人讶媪之不复丐也，即其家伺之，则所亡之物在焉；乃怒媪，将执以为盗。忽闻空中人语曰：“彼何罪我实为之。损有馀，补不足，复何害若犹不舍，将不利于尔！”言甫毕，而瓦砾掷其前矣。邻人惧而弃，一里传以为怪。往观者甚众，与之婉语，殊娓娓可听。语不逊者，辄被击。惟媪言是听，媪言勿击则止。

    Explanation

    202

    Initially, the neighbors were surprised that the old woman no longer begged, so they watched her closely at her home, and found the lost items there; they then became angry with the old woman and intended to arrest her as a thief. Suddenly, they heard a voice from the air saying, "What crime has she committed? I am the one who did it. By reducing the excess to supplement the deficiency, what harm is there? If you still refuse to let go, it will be detrimental to you!" As soon as the voice finished speaking, tiles and gravel were thrown in front of them. The neighbors were frightened and abandoned their pursuit. The news spread throughout the village as a strange and miraculous event. Many people came to see it, and they spoke to her gently, finding her words extremely engaging and pleasant to listen to. Those who spoke rudely were immediately struck. They only listened to the old woman's words, and if she said not to strike, they would stop.

    203

    一日，有诸生乘醉造媪所，大詈曰：“是何妖妄作祟不已，敢出与吾敌乎?”詈之再三，竟无恙而去。媪诘神曰：“何独畏
    彼?”曰：“彼读圣贤书，列身庠序，义当避之。且又醉，吾不与较。”生闻，益自负。数日，又往詈之，则空中飞片瓦掷其首，负痛而归。媪又以话神。曰：“无故詈人，一之为甚，吾且柔之，则曲在彼夫。又不戢而思逞，是重无礼也。无礼而击之，又何怪焉！”
    乡人颇患之，谋请符于张真人，辄为阻于途，不得往。一日，媪闻神泣曰：“龙虎山遣将至，吾祸速矣！”媪曰：“曷不逃?” 曰：“已四布罗网矣，将安之?”言罢复泣，媪亦泣。越翼日，果有邻人持符诣媪家，盖托其戚属潜求于上清，故神不知而未之阻也。径入卧内，悬之壁。媪怒，欲裂之。忽霹雳一声，一巨鼠死于床头，穴大如窗，向常行坐其处，勿见也。自是媪丐如故矣。

    Explanation

    203

    One day, a scholar, drunk, visited the old woman's place and shouted angrily, "What kind of evil spirit continues to cause trouble, daring to come out and face me?" He scolded her repeatedly, but she left unharmed. The old woman questioned the spirit, "Why do you fear him alone?" The spirit replied, "He reads the works of sages and is listed among the scholars, so he should be avoided by right. And he is drunk, so I will not contend with him." The scholar, upon hearing this, became even more confident. A few days later, he went back to scold her, and a tile flew from the air and struck his head, causing him pain. The old woman spoke to the spirit again. The spirit said, "Scolding someone without reason is already too much. I will treat him gently, so the fault lies with him. He does not restrain himself but instead seeks to show off, which is disrespectful. Attacking him out of disrespect is not surprising!"

    The villagers were greatly troubled by this and planned to obtain a talisman from the real person Zhang, but they were always obstructed on the way and could not go. One day, the old woman heard the spirit cry, "A general from Longhu Mountain is being sent, and my trouble is coming quickly!" The old woman asked, "Why don't you escape?" The spirit replied, "I am already surrounded and have nowhere to go." After speaking, the spirit cried again, and the old woman also wept. The next day, a neighbor brought a talisman to the old woman's house, having entrusted a relative to secretly obtain it from Shangqing. The spirit did not know and was not obstructed. The neighbor went straight into the bedroom and hung the talisman on the wall. The old woman was angry and wanted to tear it, but suddenly there was a thunderclap, and a large rat died at the foot of the bed. The hole was as big as a window, and it was usually where the old woman walked and sat, but she did not see it. From then on, the old woman continued to beg as before.

    204

    卜疑轩
    褚青，馀杭人，年少负才，跌宕不羁。从其舅氏马公官山左，每为狭邪游。马知之，召而切责，褚遁不敢归。薄暮将投逆旅宿，遥见驺从甚盛，呵叱而来。褚避立道左，一贵官坐车中，问曰：“是何少年?”褚以姓名告，官惊曰：“是褚先生耶?愿乞相过。”即吁马来载先生，褚漫从之。
    须臾，至其第，閈闳高敞，阀阅家也。主人下车，肃客入。曲栏洞室，不知几落。竹林花径，曲折数重。达一书斋，窗几精洁。西偏小室，匾曰“卜疑轩”。揖褚坐其中，曰：“久耳先生名，幸不交臂相失，可舍此以为东道主。一日之积，一宿之卫，不足道也。”褚逊谢，叩问姓名。主人笑曰：“咏于《诗》，系于《易》，杂见于百氏之书，先生何问焉?”竟不告。褚甚疑之。

    Explanation

    204

    卜疑轩

    Zhu Qing, a native of Yuhang, was a young man of great talent and an unrestrained, free-spirited nature. He accompanied his uncle, who held an official position in the area east of the Taihang Mountains, and often engaged in licentious behavior. When his uncle learned of this, he summoned Zhu Qing and severely reprimanded him. Zhu Qing fled and dared not return. As dusk approached, he was about to stay at an inn when he saw a large retinue approaching with shouts and commands. Zhu Qing stood aside on the road, and a high-ranking official sat in a carriage, asking, "Who is that young man?" Zhu Qing told him his name, and the official was surprised, saying, "Are you Mr. Zhu? I wish to invite you to join me." He immediately summoned a horse to take Zhu Qing, who reluctantly followed.

    In a short while, they arrived at the official's residence, which had a grand and open entrance, indicating a noble household. The host stepped down from the carriage and respectfully invited Zhu Qing inside. The interior featured winding corridors and rooms, with bamboo groves and flower-lined paths that curved around several times. They reached a study where the windows and tables were clean and tidy. On the west side was a small room with a sign above the door reading "卜疑轩" (Divination of Doubts). The host bowed and invited Zhu Qing to sit inside, saying, "I have long heard of your name, and it is fortunate that we have not missed each other. You can stay here as my guest. A day's accumulation and a night's protection are not worth mentioning." Zhu Qing humbly declined and asked for the host's name. The host smiled and said, "My name is found in the Shijing (Book of Poetry), linked to the Yijing (Book of Changes), and scattered in various books. Why do you ask?" He finally did not reveal his name, leaving Zhu Qing deeply puzzled.

    205

    既而设宴享客，水陆具陈。诸妓行酒，众乐并喧，繁音靡曼。褚素善音律，竟不知其为何曲也，询于主人。主人曰：“佳客在坐，安用旧曲？皆妮子辈自制新腔，不识中听否？”褚赞赏不已，请其曲名，则有《九尾醮》、《夜篝红》、《玉面娘》、《绎缯囊儿慢》之属，皆新奇诡异，莫晓其意。为诵《玉面娘》一阕云：
    　　“如孤洞，今夜月华云涌。东瓜棚侧犬初眠，北斗垣中星欲动。你莫西，我莫东。大家看看，大家拜拜，大家送送。青翰被，　与谁共？也则待掠鬓梳头，学那些颠鸾倒凤。帐钩正挂。灯影偏红。不管小夭娘指尖儿湿破窗缝，睡也么浓；怕则怕，晓鸟数声，啼断一林幽梦。”云云。
    　　乐既阕，一女前而歌曰：
    　　“张家阿姊赵家姨，同向春山学画眉。
    　　更抹樱桃唇一点，檀郎颊上印胭脂。”
    　　一女继歌曰：
    　　“吴王宫柳醉春烟，阿姊腰肢二八年。
    　　昨夜伴郎郎未惯，今宵珍重向婵娟。”

    Explanation

    205

    Afterward, a banquet was set up to entertain the guests, with a wide array of dishes, both aquatic and terrestrial, laid out. Young women served wine, and various forms of music played, with complex and delicate melodies. Chǔ, who was skilled in music, did not recognize the tunes and asked the host. The host said, "With distinguished guests present, why use old tunes? All these new tunes are composed by the young women. Do you find them pleasing?" Chǔ praised them endlessly and asked for their names, which included "Nine-Tailed Invocation," "Night Gourd Red," "Jade-Faced Maiden," and "Silk Sack Slow," all of which were new and mysterious, their meanings unknown. One of the songs, "Jade-Faced Maiden," was recited as follows:

    "Like a solitary cave, tonight the moonlight and clouds surge. Near the gourd trellis, the dog is just beginning to sleep, while the stars in the Big Dipper constellation are starting to move. Don't go west, I won't go east. Let's all look, all bow, all send off. The blue feathered blanket, with whom do we share it? We'll just wait to comb our hair and prepare for those moments of passion. The curtain hook is hanging, the lamp shadows are red. It doesn't matter if the young maiden's fingertips wet the broken window frame, the sleep is deep; but I fear the morning birds will sing, breaking the dream of the secluded forest."

    After the music ended, one young woman stepped forward and sang:

    "My elder sister from the Zhang family, my aunt from the Zhao family, both went to the spring mountains to learn to paint their eyebrows. They also smeared a cherry lip, imprinting rouge on the cheeks of the beloved."

    Another young woman followed and sang:

    "The willows of King Wu's palace are drunk with spring smoke, my elder sister is in her second or eighth year. Last night, she was not accustomed to being with the beloved, so tonight she cherishes the beautiful moon."

    206

    又一女向主人歌曰：
    　　“元邱校尉太风魔，漫使佳人斗艳歌。
    　　斗柄栏干天欲曙，须防华表照双娥。”
    　　主人笑曰：“褚先生非其人也。虽然，先生醉矣，可扶先生睡。吾与东城君闲话去矣。”别褚径出。
    　　时褚己被洒，神飞目眩，形骸都非。诸女引入卧室。即拥一姬与狎，诸女顾之而笑。有顷而褚已颓矣，诸女以次嬲淫，俱觉梦寐中交融欢洽，非复人道之常也。
    　　次日梦觉，体不胜惫。开目瞪视，乃卧丛薄间，宛转细思，盖狐所为也。卜疑轩者，狐性善疑也。其语言词曲，皆狐隐语也。狼狈归舅所，卧不能起者数月。
    　　亦若公
    　　族祖亦若公，为邑诸生。一日病甚，觉其气自口出，遂离形飞于窗外，但见云霞丽天，长空万里，意所欲到，身即随之。飘飘然，泠泠然，乘云御风不逾其乐也。既而身在极西，见阳乌入于虞渊。日暮徘徊，欲归不识路。忽有数鸟东还，因与俱飞，疾乃倍于鸟。

    Explanation

    206

    Another woman sang to the host:
    "The Marshal Yuan Qiu is too mad,
    recklessly making beautiful women compete in singing.
    The handle of the Big Dipper is at the railings, dawn is approaching,
    beware the ornamental pillar illuminates the two beauties."
    The host smiled and said, "Mr. Chu is not the right person. Although, Mr. Chu is drunk, you can help him sleep. I will go have a casual conversation with Master Dongcheng." He left Chu and went out.
    At that time, Chu was already drunk, his spirit was flying, his eyes were dazed, and his body was no longer himself. The women led him into the bedroom. He immediately embraced one of the ladies and engaged in playful intimacy, and the other women looked at him and laughed. After a while, Chu was already exhausted, and the women took turns teasing and seducing him, all feeling a state of pleasure in a dream, which was no longer the usual human conduct.
    The next day, he woke up from a dream, his body was too weak. He opened his eyes and stared, lying among the grass, pondering carefully, it seemed the fox's doing. The Pavilion of Doubt is where the fox nature is good at doubting. Its words and phrases are all the foxes' hidden meanings. He returned in a sorry state to his uncle's place, lying unable to get up for several months.
    Also like the gentleman
    Ancestor also like the gentleman, was a student in the county. One day, he was very ill, felt his breath coming out of his mouth, then left his body and flew out of the window, only seeing clouds and rosy light in the sky, vast expanse of sky, wherever the mind desires, the body follows. Light and airy, cool and refreshing, riding the clouds and controlling the wind cannot surpass the joy. Then he was in the far west, saw the sunbird enter the abyss of the sun. At sunset, he lingered, wanting to return but not knowing the way. Suddenly several birds returned east, so he flew with them, the speed was twice that of the birds.

    207

    少选到家，见己身僵卧室中，而飞入者又一身也。妻、子环卧身而哭，己从旁慰止之，妻、子略不顾。乃大声叱喝之，即又不问。始悟己为异物。犹忆气从口出，因以首触口，试入焉；豁然两身合为一体，呻吟而苏，弥觉滞重，不复如向者之翱翔自得矣。
    　　后三十年卒，公之始苏也，谓人曰：“死为极乐所。吾今始悟庄生‘决疣溃痈’之说。”
    　　田卖鬼
    　　有田乙，素不畏鬼，而尤能伏鬼，遂以卖鬼为业。衣食之需，妻孥之供，悉卖鬼所得。人颇识之，呼为“田卖鬼”云。
    　　年二十馀时，尝夜行野外，见一鬼肩高背曲，头大如轮。田叱之曰：“尔何物？”鬼答言：“我是鬼，尔是何物？”田欲观其变，因绐之曰：“我亦鬼也。”鬼大喜跃，遂来相嬲抱，体冷如冰。

    Explanation

    207

    Translation

    Soon after, he arrived home and saw himself stiff in bed, while another version of himself had flown in. His wife and children were sitting around him in bed, weeping. He tried to comfort them from the side, but they barely paid attention. He then shouted loudly at them, and they ignored him again. Realizing he was an unusual being, he remembered breathing through his mouth and tried to breathe through his head, testing if he could return to life. Suddenly, the two versions of him merged into one, and he groaned and woke up. He felt increasingly heavy and sluggish, no longer feeling the same freedom and self-satisfaction as before.

    Thirty years later, he died. When he first regained consciousness, he told people, "I died in a place of extreme joy. Now I understand Zhuangzi's metaphor of removing a pimple and draining an abscess."

    Tian the Ghost-Seller

    There was a man named Tian Yi who had always been fearless of ghosts and was particularly skilled at subduing them. He made a living by selling ghosts. His family's needs, including food and clothing, were all met by the money he earned from selling ghosts. People knew him well and called him "Tian the Ghost-Seller."

    When he was in his twenties, he once walked alone in the countryside at night and saw a ghost with a high shoulder, hunched back, and a head as large as a wheel. Tian Yi shouted at the ghost, "What are you?" The ghost replied, "I am a ghost. What are you?" Tian Yi wanted to see how the ghost would react and then lied to the ghost, "I am also a ghost." The ghost was very happy and jumped, coming to playfully embrace Tian Yi. The ghost's body was as cold as ice.

    208

    鬼惊疑曰：“公体太暖，恐非鬼。”田曰：“我鬼中之壮盛者耳。”鬼遂不疑。田问鬼有何能，鬼曰：“善戏，愿呈薄技。”乃取头颅著于腹，复著于尻，巳复著于胯，悉如生就，无少裂拆。又或取头分而二之，或三四之，或五六之，以至于十数，不等。掷之空，投之水，旋转之于地，已而复置之于项。奇幻之状，摩不毕贡。既复求田作戏，田复绐之曰：“我饥甚，不暇作戏，将觅寻绍兴市，尔能从乎？”鬼欣然愿偕往，彳亍而行。
    　　途次，田问曰：“尔为鬼几年矣？”曰：“三十年矣。”问：“住何所？”鬼言：“无常所，或大树下，或人家屋角，或厕旁土中。”亦问田，田曰：“我新鬼也，趋避之道，一切未谙。愿以教我。”盖欲知鬼所喜以诱之，知鬼所忌以制之也。鬼不知其意，乃曰：“鬼者阴属也，喜妇人髪，忌男子鼻涕。”田志之。

    Explanation

    208

    The ghost was startled and suspicious, saying, "Your body is too warm, perhaps you are not a ghost." The narrator replied, "I am just a strong and vigorous ghost." The ghost then asked, "What can you do?" The narrator said, "I am good at performing tricks and would like to show you my modest skills." He then took his head and placed it on his stomach, then on his buttocks, and then on his hips, all as if it were a living person, without any tearing or splitting. Sometimes he would split his head into two, sometimes into three or four, sometimes into five or six, up to ten, in various numbers. He would throw it into the air, drop it into the water, spin it on the ground, and then place it back on his neck. All these fantastic shapes, none of which were missed. He then asked the narrator to perform a trick. The narrator replied to him in a deceitful manner, "I am very hungry and have no time to perform a trick. I am going to look for the city of Shaoxing. Can you accompany me?" The ghost happily agreed to go with him and they walked slowly together.

    On the way, the narrator asked, "How many years have you been a ghost?" The ghost replied, "Thirty years." The narrator then asked, "Where do you live?" The ghost said, "I have no fixed place to live. Sometimes I live under a big tree, sometimes in the corner of a house, sometimes in the soil beside a toilet." The narrator also asked the ghost about himself, saying, "I am a new ghost. I do not know the ways of avoiding danger. I wish you could teach me." This was because he wanted to know what the ghost liked to entice him and what he feared to control him. The ghost did not understand his intention and said, "Ghosts belong to the yin realm. They like women's hair and fear men's snot." The narrator recorded this information.

    209

    方行间，又逢一鬼，癯而长，貌类枯木。前鬼揖之曰：“阿兄无恙？”指田示之曰：“此亦我辈也。”癯鬼乃来，近通款洽焉，亦与惧行。
    　　将至市，天欲晓，二鬼行渐缓。田恐其隐遁，因两手捉二鬼臂，牵之左右行。轻若无物，行甚疾。二鬼大呼：“公不畏晓耶？必非鬼。宜速释手，无相逼也。”田不听，持愈急。二鬼哀叫，渐无声。天明视之，化为两鸭矣。田恐其变形，乃引鼻向鸭喷嚏。持入市卖之，得钱三百。
    　　后每夜挟妇髪少许，随行野外索鬼，鬼多来就之，辄为所制。或有化羊豕者，变鱼鸟者，悉于市中卖得钱以市他物。有卖不尽者，亦自烹食之，味殊甘腴。
    　　非非子曰：机智之能卖人者，人咸谓之鬼，谓其吊诡有似于鬼也。似鬼者若此，真鬼当何如？而世更有卖鬼之人也，然则鬼之诡亦乌能及人之诡哉？当以鬼之似人者为鬼之诡耳。

    Explanation

    209

    While walking, he encountered another ghost, who was lean and tall, with a withered appearance like a dead tree. The previous ghost bowed to him and said, "Is my elder brother well?" He pointed to Tian and said, "This is also one of us." The lean ghost then approached and engaged in friendly conversation, walking together with Tian.

    As they neared the market, the sky was about to dawn, and the two ghosts' pace gradually slowed. Tian feared they might disappear, so he held onto both their arms and led them to walk back and forth. They felt light as if they were nothing, and they moved very quickly. The two ghosts shouted, "Do you not fear the dawn? You must not be a ghost. Release your hands quickly, do not force us." Tian did not listen and held them more tightly. The two ghosts cried out in despair, and gradually became silent. When it became light, he saw that they had transformed into two ducks. Tian feared they might change again, so he blew his nose at the ducks. He took them to the market and sold them for 300 coins.

    Later, every night, he carried a small amount of his wife's hair and went out into the fields to find ghosts. Many ghosts came to him, and he controlled them. Some transformed into sheep and pigs, others into fish and birds, and all were sold in the market for money to buy other things. If there were any left over, he cooked and ate them, finding the taste exceptionally sweet and rich.

    Nononzi said: People who use their intelligence to deceive others are often called ghosts, because their tricks are similar to those of ghosts. Ghosts like these, what about true ghosts? There are also people in the world who sell ghosts, so how could the tricks of ghosts compare to those of humans? The tricks of ghosts should be compared to those of humans.

    210

    红纱灯笼
    　　陶生训蒙本里，每夜自塾中归宿。妻辄知之，使婢预启关以待。陶以其常然，谓臆揣之也，亦不问其由。
    　　一夜，陶归而门阖，呼之。其妻讶然曰：“殆非郎君也，何其异乎？”审声而后纳之入。其妻见之，熟视而无言，若有不怿之色。
    　　陶怪之。妻曰：“今日何所为？必有损德事。不然，何以君至而妾不知也？”陶愈怪之，问其故。妻曰：“妾每夜倚楼盼君，君归，或囊火，或步月，或暗中彳亍，然必有红纱灯笼二檠前行导引，及门然后灭。妾知君未尝自见，故久不敢泄。此诚非常之兆也。今夕君归而红灯不见，妾是以讶之。敢问致此者曷故？”
    　　陶默然久之，瞿然汗下，曰：“有是哉？吾过矣。——邻人之嫂今将嫁，使我作婚帖，吾漫为作之。鬼神怒我，其是故乎？”妻惊曰：“然矣。过莫大于破人节，而文书为凭。君不熟筹。

    Explanation

    210

    Red Silk Lanterns

    Taosheng, who taught children in the village, returned home from school every night. His wife always knew about it and had the maid open the door in advance to wait. Taosheng assumed this was normal and did not ask why. One night, Taosheng returned and found the door closed. He called out, and his wife, surprised, said, "It's not you, how strange it is?" After listening to his voice, she let him in. When his wife saw him, she looked at him carefully and remained silent, with an expression of displeasure.

    Taosheng was puzzled. His wife said, "What did you do today? There must be something that harms one's virtue. Otherwise, how could you arrive without me knowing?" Taosheng was even more puzzled and asked why. His wife replied, "Every night, I wait for you on the balcony. When you return, you either carry a lamp, walk under the moonlight, or move slowly in the dark, but there are always two red silk lanterns guiding you ahead, and they only go out when you reach the door. I know you never see yourself, so I have kept this secret for a long time. This is indeed an unusual sign. Tonight, when you returned, I didn't see the red lanterns, which is why I am surprised. May I ask what caused this?"

    Taosheng remained silent for a long time, then broke out in a cold sweat and said, "Is that true? I have made a mistake. — My neighbor's sister-in-law is now getting married, and I was asked to write a wedding invitation. I carelessly agreed to do it. The spirits are angry with me, isn't this the reason?" His wife exclaimed, "Yes, indeed. No fault is greater than breaking someone's chastity, and the document serves as proof. You did not think carefully."

    211

    肩此过矣。然速往视之，若犹未行，尚可饵也。”
    　　陶即造邻人之室，问婚帖去否，答言其期在明日。陶乃诡曰：“宰甚幸甚！是有误，当改作。”邻人以为信，因出帖。陶即于灯上焚之，拂袖出门外。邻人大骇，追而诘之。陶正色曰：“公嫁嫂已不义，吾岂助公为不义者乎？”邻人愧而返。其嫂竟以无人作婚帖，事不谐。
    　　陶自是每夜归，红灯之见如初矣。后仕宦，屡历清显之职。
    　　非非子曰：高明之家，鬼瞰其室。《春秋》之书，贤者为重。甚哉，神明之可畏，而士君子之宜自惕也，陶生以不知慎微之道，几遭冥冥之谴而贻士林羞。然即能悔咎自省，泯其过于终食间，君子称之。乃其妻者，深心远识，亦岂寻常巾帼哉？昔乐羊子捐遗金于野，激于其妻之一言，陶生之事近之矣。

    Explanation

    211

    Final Translation

    He shouldered this blame. Yet, he hurried to see it, and if it had not yet been done, it could still be lured."
    Tao immediately went to the neighbor's house and asked if the wedding invitation had been sent. The neighbor replied that the date was set for the next day. Tao then pretended to say, "The magistrate is very pleased! There is a mistake; it should be corrected." The neighbor believed him and took out the invitation. Tao promptly burned it on the lamp, waved his sleeves, and left the house. The neighbor was greatly startled and chased after him to question him. Tao sternly said, "You have already committed an injustice by marrying your sister-in-law. How could I help you in doing an unjust act?" The neighbor felt ashamed and returned. His sister-in-law eventually, because no one made the wedding invitation, the matter did not work out.

    From then on, every night when Tao returned, the red light appeared as before. Later, he entered officialdom and often held high-ranking positions.

    The author, Feifeizi, said: "In a noble household, spirits watch over the house." The Spring and Autumn Annals give priority to the virtuous. How terrible it is to fear the divine, and the scholars and gentlemen should be cautious. Tao did not know the way of being cautious about small matters and almost suffered punishment from the unseen, bringing shame to the scholarly community. But he was able to repent and self-examine, erasing his mistake in a short time, and gentlemen praised him. His wife had deep insight and foresight, how could she be an ordinary woman? Just as Le Yangzi threw away a bag of gold after his wife's words, Tao's story is similar to this.

    212

    揽风岛
    　　有粤贾，浮舶入南海。至一岛，见桑黮纂纂，上岸摘啖之。味逾常黮，怀数枚欲遗同舟。俄而风作，舶已离岸去，顷刻不见。海波汹涌，山林杳冥。独立叫号，凄苦万状。宛转至暮，虑逢豺虎蛇虺之族，欲赴海中死。转念身无生理，复何所畏惧？不如且穷其境。
    　　初行蓁莽梗路，趁趲欲踣。逾里许，渐觉平坦。复前三四里，见远灯甚明，似有村落。窃喜身入人境，寻灯而往，乃闻人语声自茅屋中出也。
    　　叩门呼之，一老人启关问曰：“客何来？”贾具告以故，且求寓宿。老人曰：“夙缘也。此地名揽风岛，惟有仙缘者能至，居此者三人，皆昔乘舟入海。遗于岸上者也。今与子而四矣。”

    Explanation

    212

    Windcatcher Island

    A merchant from Guangdong sailed into the South China Sea. Upon reaching an island, he saw lush mulberry trees and climbed ashore to pick and eat some. The fruit tasted better than the common variety, so he carried a few to give to his fellow travelers. Soon after, the wind began to blow, and the boat left the shore, disappearing in an instant. The sea grew turbulent, and the mountains and forests became distant and obscure. Standing alone, he shouted in anguish, feeling countless forms of sorrow and misery. Winding his way until evening, he feared encountering jackals, tigers, snakes, and vipers, and even considered rushing into the sea to die. But upon realizing there was no chance of survival, he thought, "What have I to fear?" He decided to explore the place thoroughly.

    At first, the path was obstructed by dense vegetation, and he almost fell. After traveling a little over a li, the path gradually became flat. He then went forward for three to four li and saw a very bright light in the distance, which seemed to indicate a village. Secretly delighted at entering a human settlement, he followed the light and heard human voices emerging from a thatched cottage.

    Knocking on the door and calling out, an old man opened it and asked, "Where do you come from, guest?" The merchant told him the reason in detail and requested to stay overnight. The old man replied, "It is a fateful connection. This place is called Windcatcher Island. Only those with a fate to meet the divine can reach it. Three people live here, all of whom were left behind on the shore after being on a boat in the sea in the past. Now, with you, there are four."

    213

    言罢，复有老人自内出。道骨仙风，衣冠潇洒，谓贾曰：“尔识我乎？吾，尔十九世从祖也。”挽以入室，指中坐一老人曰：“此为元邱公，先我来此七百年。”指启关者曰：“此最后至，亦三百馀岁矣。”
    　　视其室。无器量，亦无床榻。壁间悬灯，非膏非火。老人曰：“此万年脂也。昼则无光，夜则自燃。吾三人者，不饮不食，亦不梦寐。尔初至，或饥，则山果皆可食；或渴，则西涧有泉，味如醇酒，就而饮之，可已渴而不醉；或倦困，则陆地可眠，安于衾枕。睡或十馀日。或数月而后觉，久之，俱不复须矣。”贾闻言甚乐，以为遇仙，顿忘世虑。
    　　又问何名揽风岛，老人曰：“风起必过此，从而揽之，顷刻可以游六合、蹑太虚。然足迹所遍，山水景物，视此岛多不及焉，不幸为世尘听撄，反数日不宁，是以常不愿往也。”

    Explanation

    213

    After speaking, another old man emerged from inside. With a hermit-like appearance and a graceful demeanor, he said to Jia, "Do you recognize me? I am your nineteenth-generation ancestor." He led him into the room and pointed to an old man sitting in the middle, saying, "This is Yuan Qiu Gong, who arrived here seven hundred years before me." He then pointed to the one who opened the door and said, "This is the last to arrive, and he is also over three hundred years old."

    Looking around the room, there were no utensils or furniture. On the wall hung a lamp, neither oil nor fire. The old man said, "This is oil that lasts for ten thousand years. It has no light during the day but burns itself at night. We three do not drink or eat, nor do we dream. When you first arrive, if you are hungry, all the fruits in the mountains can be eaten; if you are thirsty, there is a spring in the western ravine, its taste like fine wine, drink it, and it can quench your thirst without intoxicating you; if you are tired, you can sleep on the ground, as comfortable as a bed. Sleep for more than ten days. Or for several months, and after a long time, none of us need it anymore." Jia was very happy upon hearing this, thinking he had met a fairy, and suddenly forgot about worldly concerns.

    He then asked what the name of the island that catches the wind was. The old man said, "When the wind arises, it must pass through here, and by catching it, one can travel the universe and tread the void in an instant. However, the places we have visited, with their landscapes and scenery, are often not as good as this island. Unfortunately, it is disturbed by worldly affairs, and it takes several days to recover. Therefore, we often do not want to go there."

    214

    次日，三老人引贾登小邱。遥望海波，想见飞旆大纛，簇拥一人，危冠广袖，鬚髪戟张，身骑青虎，凌空而过。老人曰：“是为风伯，即《山海经》所谓折丹者也，主天下雄风。凡鸣穷扬波，卷尘飞石，触物暴猛，皆彼为之。”果见巨浪楮天，海水皆立，而老人衣袂不少动，即贾亦不觉其风之冲拂也。
    　　巳而笙簧低奏，一少女跨白鸢曳纨扇，婀娜而来，从以曲盖，护以长斿，有香气袭人甚烈。贾不觉昏沉仆地，臣久始苏，老人笑曰：“封姨信虐也！”贾问何故，老人曰：“封姨年少夭斜，主天下雌风，名行柳堤花径、轻烟细雨间，习习飘飘，柔而善入。其挠人甚于风伯。顷者袭人香气，皆摄百花之精也。自非道力素定舌，鲜不为所中。尔之仆焉，宜矣！须经受此香三四千日，则不复畏。又数千日，始可以揽之而游。”

    Explanation

    214

    The next day, three elderly men led Jia to a small hill. Gazing far into the sea, they imagined seeing fluttering banners and large flags, surrounding a single figure with a tall hat and wide sleeves, his beard and hair bristling, riding a green tiger as he soared through the air. The elder said, "This is the God of Wind, also known as the 'Zhedan' from the Classic of Mountains and Seas, who governs the mighty winds of the world. All the roaring waves, rolling up dust and flying stones, and striking objects with sudden violence are his doing." Indeed, they saw giant waves crashing against the sky, the sea standing still, yet the elder's robes remained undisturbed, and Jia did not feel the wind's force.

    Shortly after, soft music played on wind instruments, and a young girl approached gracefully, riding a white eagle and holding a silk fan. She was accompanied by a round parasol and long streamers, and a strong fragrance filled the air. Jia suddenly felt dizzy and collapsed to the ground. After a long time, he regained consciousness, and the elder laughed and said, "The Goddess of Wind is indeed mischievous!" Jia asked why, and the elder explained, "The Goddess of Wind, though young, is capricious and governs the gentle winds that blow through willow-lined paths and flower-lined roads, light and delicate, yet adept at penetrating. Her mischief is more subtle than that of the God of Wind. The fragrance that just enveloped you was a blend of the essences of all flowers. Unless one has a strong and steadfast spirit, few can resist its influence. Your collapse was expected! You must endure this fragrance for several thousand days before you will no longer be affected. After several thousand more days, you will be able to harness and travel with it."

    215

    贾乃日于海上候其过，久之，渐不仆，然心摇神眩，每不自持。又久之，乃少定。亦渐不饮食，不梦寐矣。
    　　一日，老人谓之曰：“自尔来此，尔家人以尔为死，今日建道场度魂，吾携尔往观之。但既至家，见家人，慎勿声！否则，不利。”贾应诺。顷之风至，三老人令贾闭目，共挟之行。
    　　须臾，果至其家。方建坛设供，因共坐坛上。人皆莫之见，数僧鸣铙振锡，拜伏坛前，口宣梵呗吒婆，不可辨。贾顾之窃笑，老人掩其口而止之。既而妻子缟素而出，抢地哀恸，贾不觉心动泪零，亟下坛抚之曰：“我固在此！”妻子惊走。回视三老人，　已失所在。悔不可追，遂以故告其家。与妻子相处，饮食梦寐如常人。

    Explanation

    215

    On that day, Jia waited by the sea for him to pass. After a long time, he gradually lost his balance, feeling dizzy and disoriented, unable to control himself. After even longer, he became slightly more stable. He also gradually stopped eating and no longer dreamed or slept.

    One day, an old man told him, "Since you came here, your family has thought you were dead. Today, we are building a Buddhist temple to conduct a soul-release ceremony. I will take you to watch it. But once we arrive at your home and see your family, be careful not to speak! Otherwise, it will bring misfortune." Jia agreed. Soon after, the wind arrived, and the three old men told Jia to close his eyes and carried him along.

    In a moment, they indeed arrived at his home. They were just setting up the altar and preparing offerings, then sat on the altar together. Nobody could see them. Several monks rang the cymbals and shook their staffs, bowing and kneeling in front of the altar, chanting Buddhist chants that were unintelligible. Jia looked at them and smiled secretly, but the old men covered his mouth and stopped him. Soon after, his wife and children came out wearing white clothes, hitting the ground and sobbing in grief. Unconsciously, Jia's heart moved, and tears fell. He quickly came down from the altar and comforted them, saying, "I am here indeed!" His wife and children fled in shock. Looking back, the three old men had already disappeared. Regretting it too late, he then told his family about it. He lived with his wife and children, eating, drinking, sleeping, and dreaming like a normal person.

    216

    蒋氏女
    　　有富翁洪氏，一子甚聪秀。年十五，出就外傅，离家五六里。一日自塾归，过蒋氏之门。忽有自内倾盂水湿其衣履者，视之，婉然好女子也。生不为忤，笑而去。他日过之，女子复倾水向生，盖亦适然。而生甚疑其有意，遂狂惑。抵暮，径入女室，而女及父母皆坐于堂中，殊未之见也。
    　　是夜，女阖户搴帷，忽见男子横眠其榻，大惊欲呼，生遽起掩其口，胁之曰：“吾两过卿门，卿两以水浇吾衣，是诲我来也。吾有辞矣，复何畏？”女大冤苦，而莫能设辩也。生拥之共枕，誓以山河。女亦心动，弛衣昵就。时新秋，残暑未退，恣情欢狎，狂荡中宵。生渴甚，向女求浆，女以夜深，顾无由得，忆床下有西瓜一枚，剖以食之。既尽，昏然就睡。

    Explanation

    216

    Jiang's Daughter
    There was a wealthy man named Hong, who had a son who was very intelligent and handsome. At the age of fifteen, he went out to a private tutor, living about five or six miles away from home. One day, returning from school, he passed by Jiang's house. Suddenly, water was spilled from inside, wetting his clothes and shoes. He looked up and saw a graceful and beautiful girl. The boy was not angry, smiled, and left. On another day, passing by again, the girl spilled water towards him again, which was also by chance. However, the boy became very suspicious that she had an intention, and soon became crazily obsessed. By evening, he directly entered the girl's room, while the girl and her parents were all sitting in the hall, and he did not see them.
    That night, the girl closed the door and drew the curtains. Suddenly, she saw a man lying across her bed, greatly surprised and about to call out. The boy quickly got up and covered her mouth, threatening her and saying, "I passed by your gate twice, and you twice poured water on my clothes, which is teaching me to come. I have my reasons, what do I fear?" The girl was greatly wronged and suffering, but could not argue back. The boy embraced her and shared the same pillow, swearing by the mountains and rivers. The girl's heart was also moved, and she relaxed her clothes and approached closely. It was early autumn, and the heat had not yet dissipated. They indulged in unrestrained pleasure, wild and unrestrained in the middle of the night. The boy was very thirsty and asked the girl for water. She said it was deep into the night and there was no way to get it. She remembered there was a watermelon under the bed, cut it open, and ate it. After finishing, they fell asleep drowsily.

    217

    天将曙，女推之使去，则已死矣。女彷徨无策，掩袂幽咽，至午而门不启。父母怪之，破关而入。得其状，且怒且惧，曰：“洪翁继三妻、纳数妾，惟此儿，爱护若掌珠。今毙于此，奈何奈何！”驰告之。
    　　洪哀愤，诉于邑宰，言蒋女诱杀其子。宰览其词，颇疑惑，谓恶有少年处子钟情所欢而复杀之于床者乎？庭鞠之，女具诉本末。至食瓜之事，宰笑曰：“是矣，犹战斗甫息甲，盗贼入其室而刺之，恶能不死哉？”
    　　时女已受聘李氏，生亦缔婚宁氏，宰遂判以宁配李，而以蒋归洪守节焉。女已有身，遗腹生一子为洪后。
    　　方伯娈童冢
    　　有武人猎山谷中，得双兔，系之马后。时日已昏黄。过松桧之林，忽有物攫双兔以去。索之不得，　且怒且怖。前行数十武，遥见山角宿莽中一物甚白，隆起二尺许。瞪视久之，辨有双脚拄地，状如耸臀。武人知为鬼物，引弓射之，正中其窍。

    Explanation

    217

    As dawn approached, the girl pushed him away, and he was already dead. She was at a loss, covered her sleeves while sobbing quietly, and the door remained closed until noon. Her parents, puzzled by her behavior, broke through the gate and entered. Upon understanding the situation, they were both angry and afraid, saying, "Old Man Hong has had three wives and taken several concubines, but only this child, whom he cherished like a pearl in his palm. Now he has died here, what shall we do, what shall we do!" They rushed to inform the authorities.

    Hong, grieving and furious, reported to the local magistrate, claiming that Jiang had lured and killed his son. The magistrate read the report and was puzzled, asking, "How could there be a young maiden who is devoted to her beloved and then kills him in bed?" In court, the girl explained the whole story. When it came to the incident with the melon, the magistrate laughed and said, "That's it, it's like a battle just ended, and a thief enters the room and stabs him. How could he not die?"

    At the time, the girl was already engaged to Li, and the boy was engaged to Ning, so the magistrate decided that Ning would marry Li, and Jiang would return to Hong to remain chaste. The girl was already pregnant, and she gave birth to a son who became Hong's heir.

    In the tomb of a favorite concubine of a high-ranking official, there was a hunter who caught two rabbits and tied them to his horse. As he passed through a pine forest, something grabbed the rabbits and ran away. He could not find the creature and was both angry and afraid. He continued on for dozens of paces, then saw something white and swollen in the distance, about two feet high. After staring at it for a long time, he realized it had two legs and was shaped like a raised buttock. The hunter knew it was a ghost and drew his bow, shooting it right in the vital point.

    218

    有声呋然，带羽而没。遂驰马而归。
    　　次日至其处，得箭于小冢上，已半折矣。询之居人，言某方伯一娈童三年前葬于此。
    　　黄衣丈夫
    　　村人林某偶行河畔，见一浣衣女子容态殊绝，因访其姓氏里居，以厚币娶之。女子贤且慧，事舅姑颇孝谨，处诸姑娣姒间，性甚和柔，各得其意。惟待其夫，乃过于悍戾之妇。梳冼饮食，必其夫进巾栉、奉匕箸，少不如意便挞之。夫惟顺受不敢敌，亦不敢逃。甚或令长跪榻下，以火烙其面，锥刺其体；虽至于焦烂疮痍身无完肤，而妇怒犹未已也。舅姑奔救，则少止，既去，则复虐之。惨毒百端，莫可名状。而妇家兄弟，无赖若狼虎，不可理喻，故舅姑欲治以官，而卒不敢也。他人见之，每为不平。以故询其夫，则答言不知。或教以宜自振奋，不当为妇人所制，则惟俯首长叹而已。

    Explanation

    218

    A loud roar echoed, followed by a being with feathers vanishing into thin air. I then rode my horse back home.

    The next day, I went to the place where I had seen the strange occurrence and found an arrow on a small tomb, already half-broken. I asked the local people, and they told me that three years ago, a high-ranking official's young male lover was buried there.

    One day, a villager named Lin was walking by the riverbank when he saw a woman washing clothes who had an extraordinary appearance. He then asked about her family name and place of origin and married her with a large sum of money. The woman was both virtuous and intelligent, very filial and respectful to her in-laws, and gentle and harmonious in dealing with her sisters-in-law and other female relatives, each getting along well with her. However, towards her husband, she was like an extremely fierce and violent woman. In matters of grooming and eating, she required her husband to bring her towels and combs and serve her utensils; if he did not meet her expectations, she would beat him. The husband could only submit and dared not resist or escape. Even to the point of making him kneel on the floor, scorching his face with fire, and piercing his body with needles, his body being scorched, festering, and having no intact skin, but the wife's anger was not yet satisfied. When the in-laws rushed to save him, she would temporarily stop, but once they left, she would resume her abuse. The cruelty was in countless ways, beyond description. And the brothers of the wife were as worthless as wolves and tigers, unreasonable and impossible to reason with, so the in-laws wanted to punish them legally but ultimately dared not. Others who saw it often felt it was unfair. Therefore, when asked about it, the husband would say he did not know. Some advised him to be more self-reliant and not be controlled by a woman, but he could only bow his head and sigh.

    219

    一日，有黄衣丈夫，形容魁伟，至妇家而谓妇曰：“君虞固薄幸，然数世以来受玉娘之凌折，亦可以己矢。浪泡无檠，落花自好，尽可释然。胡不归郑曲，而频此与愚物较乎？”妇笑而颔之。
    　　家人怪其言。黄衣曰：“此唐李益、霍小玉数世业也。净持夫人使我迓玉娘，今与俱去耳。”家人以为诡词，将呵击之。黄衣抚掌大笑，须臾风起，云雾迷空，妇与黄衣俱失所在。
    　　非非子曰：余读蒋防所为《霍小玉传》，至李生负心之际，未尝不怒髪上冲也。及观长洲尤太史判有“扑杀此獠”之语。且妆点其事入《钧天乐传奇》中，心甚快之。既又闻此事，乃恍然于天道好还，而忍人之不可为也。书罢浮一大白。

    Explanation

    219

    One day, a man in yellow robes, of majestic appearance, came to the woman's home and said to her, "Lady Yu, you have indeed been treated unjustly, but for generations you have suffered at the hands of Yu Niang. Now you can stand up for yourself. Like a bubble without a frame, or fallen flowers that are still beautiful, you can let go. Why not return to the right path, rather than continuing to contend with foolish things?" The woman smiled and nodded in agreement.

    The family members found his words strange. The man in yellow robes said, "This is the legacy of Tang Dynasty figures Li Yi and Xiao Yu. Lady Jingchi sent me to bring Yu Niang away, and now she is leaving with me." The family thought his words were deceitful and were about to punish him. The man in yellow robes laughed heartily, and in an instant, the wind rose, the sky filled with clouds and mist, and the woman and the man in yellow robes disappeared.

    Feifeizi said, "I read Jiang Fang's 'Biography of Xiao Yu,' and when I reached the point where Li Sheng betrayed her, I could not help but be filled with righteous anger. Later, I learned that Changzhou You Taishi had said, 'Beat and kill this demon.' Furthermore, the story was adapted into the legendary work 'Juntian Music Legend,' and I found it very satisfying. After hearing this story, I realized the truth of the saying 'the way of heaven favors retribution,' and the inhumanity of certain actions is indeed intolerable." After finishing his writing, he raised a large cup of wine in a toast.

    220

    耳食录卷十
    　　大 王
    　　某乙雄于力，其侪共推之。众言某宅素凶，约：敢下榻者，当醵酒相劳。乙故酒徒，闻之欣然，曰：“请以麯君为息壤，愿往见魑魅为城下之盟。倘辱命，任公等遗我巾帼。”众壮其言。乙亦自觉无畏，遂腰剑幞被，夜往宿焉。众反鐍其户而去。空房寂谧，烛泪荧荧。乙本无胆勇。徒以酒故，忘身犯难。至是风鹤皆兵，渐生恇怯，下帷枕剑，耿不成寐。
    　　二更之后，遥见室四隅一妇人闪出，白面斓斑，黑衣蓬头，嘻笑向壁，若有所思。少焉，昂鼻四嗅，宛转而前，启唇发声，愿荐枕席。乙大怖，急起拔剑，手惊，剑落于床下。妇人睨之而笑，曰：“新弦可续，故剑何来?”遂下拜，求帐神也。乙力握帐门，帐不得开。良久，妇人怒曰：“尔不纳我耶?我请大王来主婚，看尔不作脔婿否?”即启户而出。

    Explanation

    220

    Record of Gullible Ears, Volume Ten
    The King

    A certain person named Yi was strong in strength, and his peers all recommended him. The people said that a certain house was usually ominous. They agreed: whoever dares to stay overnight, they will gather wine to entertain him. Yi was a habitual drinker. Upon hearing this, he was delighted and said, "Please make the wine god my sworn ally. I wish to go and see the ghosts and make a pact under the city walls. If I fail, let you all mock me with a woman's dress." The people admired his words. Yi also felt fearless. He then girded his sword and wrapped his bedding, and went to stay there at night. The people locked the door and left. The empty room was quiet and peaceful, with the candle tears dimly glowing. Yi originally had no courage. He only forgot himself and risked danger because of alcohol. At this point, even the wind and cranes seemed like soldiers, and he gradually became timid. He lowered the curtain and rested his head on the sword, unable to sleep soundly.

    After the second watch of the night, he saw a woman suddenly appear from one of the corners of the room. She had a pale face with spots, black clothes, and disheveled hair. She laughed and smiled at the wall, as if thinking about something. A little later, she raised her nose and smelled all around. She turned and came forward, opening her mouth to speak, offering her bed. Yi was greatly frightened. He quickly got up and drew his sword. His hand trembled, and the sword fell to the floor. The woman looked at him and laughed, saying, "The new string can be continued, what does the old sword have to do with it?" She then bowed and asked to be the spirit of the curtain. Yi tightly grips the curtain door, and the curtain cannot be opened. After a long time, the woman angrily said, "Do you not accept me? I will ask the king to come and marry us, see if you won't be a minced bridegroom!" She then opened the door and left.

    221

    乙窘甚，大呼。夜深地僻，卒无应者。因念魍魉大至，支解必矣。彷徨无策，视床头有酒瓮一具，大可容身，乙乃祝曰：“麯君麯君，向以尔为息壤，今以尔为朱家矣。幸乞相庇，毋使酒国中失一壮士也！”祝毕急入内，以盖覆瓮口。旁有一穴，容一目，遂蹲身屏息窥之。
    　　须臾鬼众坌至，以妇人为导，貌皆丑黑，装束类皂役。又有数人，以交椅舁一人至，头大身短，语言函糊，——众鬼惟其命是听，若主仆然，——即妇人所谓大王者也。喓嘟数声，不辨作何语，众鬼争前，裂帐翻被，索之不得，佥报口：“逃矣！”复索室中遍，及于酒瓮。乙战粟，齿叩有声。妇人曰：“有声自内，可索之。”一鬼前，即仆地。又一鬼前，复仆。于是诸鬼相顾不敢近。大王怒，自下椅，傫然而前。忽若有人掌其胸，大王仰首颠连作筋斗，哀呼曰：“康侯贷我!”群鬼亦为之跽请。良久，掖大王登椅，一哄而散。乙心知酒神卫巳，而口噤不能言，心感而已。

    Explanation

    221

    乙 was in dire straits and shouted out loud. Deep into the night in a remote place, no one responded. Thinking of the large number of spirits approaching, he was sure to be dismembered. Wandering aimlessly and without a plan, he looked at the bed and saw a large earthenware vessel that could fit his body. He then prayed, saying, "Lord Qu, Lord Qu, I previously considered you as a place of refuge, now I consider you as Zhu Jia. Please protect me, and do not let a brave warrior be lost in the realm of wine!" After finishing his prayer, he quickly went inside and covered the mouth of the jar with the lid. There was a small hole next to it, enough for one eye to see through, so he crouched down, held his breath, and peeked inside.

    Soon, a group of ghosts rushed in, led by a woman. They all had ugly and black appearances, dressed like low-ranking officials or servants. There were also several people who carried someone on an armchair. The person had a big head and a short body, and his speech was unclear. The ghosts listened only to his commands, acting like master and servant. This was the great king the woman referred to. He made several indistinct sounds, and the ghosts could not understand what he was saying. They rushed forward, tore the canopy, and turned over the bedding, but they did not find him. They all shouted, "He has escaped!" They searched the room thoroughly, including the wine jar. 乙 shivered with fear, and his teeth chattered audibly. The woman said, "There is a sound coming from inside. You can search for it." One ghost approached and immediately fell to the ground. Another ghost approached and also fell. Then, all the ghosts looked at each other and dared not approach. The great king became angry, got off the chair, and limped forward. Suddenly, it seemed as if someone slapped his chest. The great king looked up, stumbled, and fell backward, crying out in distress, "Lord Kang, forgive me!" The ghosts also knelt and begged for him. After a long time, they helped the great king back onto the chair and dispersed in a hurry. 乙 knew in his heart that the wine god had protected him, but he could not speak. He felt grateful in his heart.

    222

    比晓，其侪启关而入，至卧处见其状，大骇，莫知乙所在。晨后得之瓮中，急出之，已垂绝矣。灌之半日，乃得苏。一人戏谓曰：“君无胆如斗大，奈何自取困顿?方谓稳坐中军帐，谁请公入瓮者乎?”乙缕述其状，且曰：“不得，麯君攘臂，已消受无盐况味矣，可无一斗压惊乎?不宁唯是，亦所以报知己也！”众大噱，为置酒轰饮至暮。

    Explanation

    222

    Before dawn, his companions opened the door and entered, finding him in a dire state, greatly shocked, and unable to locate 乙. After sunrise, they found him in a jar, quickly pulled him out, and he was on the verge of death. They gave him water for half a day, and he finally revived. One of them joked, "You have a courage as small as a bowl, why did you put yourself in such a dangerous situation? You thought you were safely ensconced in the main tent, but who asked you into the pot?" 乙 explained his situation and said, "Without麯君's decisive action, I would have endured a difficult and unpleasant experience. Can't you give me a cup to steady my nerves? Not only for myself, but also to repay a true friend!" The crowd laughed heartily, and they set up a feast and drank heartily until dusk.

    223

    非非子曰：世之断杯中物者，每以麯醾为酖毒，使此公在座，当令人寡欢。而营糟邱、掉觥船之徒至依之为命，意谓天下无杜康，则大千世界直不可一朝居者。二者皆非也。予谓“成礼”“生祸”之语，皆出于圣贤之书，惟其适而已。余量似公荣而不持洒戒，风慕元亮而不逃醉乡，愁则饮之，乐则勿饮；亦或乐则饮之，愁则勿饮。其于酒也无怨无德，谁毁谁誉哉?坡公有言，无酒学佛，有酒学仙。末闻酒在有无之间而愿与鬼为徒者。然麯生风味，几如度索山桃树下人，其变化亦不可胜道者哉?
    　　萧点云
    　　东吴柳生悦邻女萧点云，思之甚笃。一日，过其门，见点云倚扉而立，生时被酒，挑之曰：“云娘真如飞云缥缈，乍见使人眼纈不能分明。今愿熟睹芳姿，归而摹画，作水月观音供养也。”遂逼视之，云微笑，掩门而去。生徘徊户外，至暮始归。

    Explanation

    223

    Final Translation

    Non-Nonzi said: Those who judge the contents of cups in the world often regard moutai as poison. If this person were present, it would make others less happy. Yet those who indulge in brewing and drinking, relying on it as life, believe that without Du Kang, the vast world would be unbearable to live in for even a day. Both views are incorrect. I say that the words "fulfilling a ritual" and "causing trouble" come from the writings of sages and virtuous individuals, and they are simply as they are. My capacity is like Gong Rong's, but I do not hold a ban on alcohol. I admire Yuan Liang's style but do not escape into drunkenness. I drink when sad and abstain when joyful; or I drink when joyful and abstain when sad. I have no complaint or praise for wine, who criticizes and who praises it? Su Shi once said, without wine, one should learn Buddhism; with wine, one should learn Daoism. I have never heard of anyone who wishes to be a disciple of ghosts when wine is neither present nor absent. Yet the flavor of the wine god is like that of the legendary figure under the peach tree on Dusuo Mountain, and its changes are beyond description.

    Flower Point Cloud
    A young man from Dongwu named Liu, who lived near Lady Xiao Dianyun, thought of her very deeply. One day, passing by her door, he saw her leaning against the door, and at that moment, he was drunk. He teased her, saying, "Lady Cloud is truly like a fleeting cloud, and at first sight, it makes one's eyes dazzled and unable to distinguish clearly. Now I wish to see her beauty up close, and upon returning, I will draw her likeness to worship her as the Water Moon Guanyin." He then looked at her closely, and she smiled, shut the door, and left. Liu wandered outside her door until evening before returning home.

    224

    其夜云忆生言，亦颇情注，孤灯愁对，不复就枕。闻有弹指于窗外者。静听之，其人微吟曰：
    　　“情痴伏情痴，情痴不可说。
    　　魂断楚峰云，尚绕梅花月。
    　　梅花复不开，魂兮真断绝！”
    　　云素娴吟咏，低问：“吟诗者谁?”答曰：“供养大士者也，今来侍香案耳。”云方念生，且怜之，不暇问生所由入之故，遂纳之。情好燕婉，遂约终身。由是往来，殆无间夕。
    　　一夜，云母刘来云所，突见生，亟呼其父共执之。生叩头乞舍。萧、柳素亲睦，两家门第亦显贵，不欲绝好而彰其丑也，许以女妻焉。且戒曰：“宜速以媒来。”因纵之出。
    　　数日杳然，刘乃造柳母私语其事。母诧曰：“何诬也？?吾儿久病床褥，垂毙者数矣，安能与贤女期邪?”生闻之，跃然起曰：“诚有是。昏愦之际，以为梦焉。不知游魂之为变也。”两家闻之，共讶以为天合，缔姻好焉。

    Explanation

    224

    On that night, Cloud, recalling the words of Life, was deeply moved and filled with emotion. She sat alone with a lamp, grieving, and could not sleep. Suddenly, she heard a snapping of fingers outside the window. Listening intently, she heard a faint recitation:
    　　"Obsessed with love, I am obsessed with love, but love cannot be spoken.
    　　My soul is broken by the clouds over the peaks of Chu, still lingering around the moonlit plum blossoms.
    　　The plum blossoms will not bloom again, my soul truly is severed!"
    　　Cloud, skilled in poetry, asked softly, "Who is reciting these verses?" The voice replied, "I am a devotee of the bodhisattva, here to serve at the incense table." Cloud, thinking of Life and feeling pity for him, did not have the time to inquire how he had entered, and thus accepted him. Their affection grew deep and harmonious, and they promised a lifetime together. From then on, they met frequently, almost without a break.
    　　One night, Cloud's mother, Liu, came to Cloud's place and suddenly saw Life. She quickly called her father to seize him. Life bowed and begged for mercy. The families of Xiao and Liu had long been friendly, and both families were of high social status. They did not want to break their good relations and expose their shame, so they agreed to marry their daughter to him. They also warned, "You should quickly send a matchmaker." And so, he was released.
    　　After several days, Liu went to see Cloud's mother-in-law and privately told her about the matter. The mother-in-law was surprised and said, "What lies! My son has been bedridden and near death for several times, how could he possibly have made an appointment with your daughter?" Life, upon hearing this, leaped up and said, "It is truly so. In my delirious state, I thought it was a dream. I did not know that my wandering soul had transformed." The two families, upon hearing this, were both astonished and believed it was a heavenly match, and they established a marital alliance.

    225

    李 公
    　　金溪尚庄李公某，偕数人府试而还。会日暮曛黑，迷路，至一山，不辨何地。荆棘四塞，溪涧环之，不可得前，遂共坐树下待旦。及明观之，乃其中一人之村后山也，甚平坦而无所阻，知迷魑魅矣，分道而归。而李亦旋至馆所，觉神情恍惚，卧起不舒。
    　　晚膳后，忽思归宿，而去家稍远，主人止之。殊勿听，遽袖火以行，天晓不能至。过一村郭，旧游处也，茫然似初经也者，以问人，曰：“是某村。”固立而熟视，果某村也。薄暮始至家。程不二十里，凡行一夜及一日。见其妻，若不识，问曰：“谁欤?”妻笑曰：“李某之妻也。何目谬至此！”固立而熟视，果妻也。既同寝好合，乃豁然而悟，谓我数日行止乃如梦中也，爽然自失者良久。
    　　是夜妻有娠，明年举一子，体貌怪异，不甚类人，亦不啼，亦不笑。怀抱乳哺，三岁而夭折。

    Explanation

    225

    Li Gong

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a man from Shangzhuang, Jinchxi, named Li, returned from a local examination with several others. As dusk fell and it grew dark, they became lost and reached a mountain, unable to recognize where they were. Surrounded by thorns and brambles, and streams and ravines, they could not proceed, so they sat together under a tree waiting until dawn. When it became light, they looked around and realized it was the mountain behind the village of one of the people. It was very flat with no obstacles, and they knew they were lost in the realm of evil spirits. They separated and returned home. Li also soon arrived at the inn, feeling mentally confused and uneasy when lying down or getting up.

    After dinner, he suddenly thought of returning home, but it was a bit far from home. The host stopped him. He strongly did not listen and hastily carried a firebrand to go. He could not reach his destination when it became light. He passed a village, a place he had visited before, and looked blankly as if he were seeing it for the first time. He asked a person, "Is this a certain village?" The person replied, "Yes, it is a certain village." He stood there and looked closely, and indeed, it was the village. He finally arrived home in the late afternoon. The distance was not twenty miles, but he traveled for one night and one day. He saw his wife, as if he did not recognize her, and asked, "Who are you?" His wife laughed and said, "I am Li's wife. How could you mistake me?" He stood there and looked closely, and indeed, it was his wife. After they slept together, he suddenly realized that his actions over several days were like a dream, and he was greatly disappointed and lost for a long time.

    That night, his wife became pregnant, and the next year, she gave birth to a son. The boy had a strange appearance, not very human-like, and did not cry or smile. He was held and fed, but died young at three years old.

    226

    盖魅之将托生其家也，故迷之于路，迫之以归也。嗟乎!将欲父之，而先魅之，鬼之无赖亦甚矣！靦然者一鬼，亦蠢然者一人，又曷怪焉！
    　　芙蓉馆扫花女
    　　一士人，——忘其地与姓矣，——名谷，下帷攻苦，罕与外交。舍旁溪水清洁，奇花绚烂，心爱之，日暮低徊焉。有一丽女子提瓮来汲，谷见惑之。由是谷每至，女子辄来，遂有玉洞桃花之约。
    　　女子行而谷从之。西行至一山，危峰连亘，奇峭插云。遥闻鸡犬之声，而不见村落。时已昏暮。谷惶遽不行。女子故绐曰：“闺人眼疏，迷失故路。山君且至，奈何亲何！”忽林中冲出—虎，咆哮而前，谷惊而仆，女子以手挥之曰：“去！毋惊我郎也。”虎吼而奔。女子扶谷起。又一虎继至，眈眈视谷。女子又挥之去。谷神情慌乱，请返者再。女子以袖拂谷面，调之曰：“年少书生，怯弱乃尔，今已至此，隔此一岭耳。”

    Explanation

    226

    When a ghost intends to reincarnate in their family, they lead them astray on the road and force them to return home. Alas! Intending to father them, but first bewitching them, the ghost's recklessness is indeed great! One shameless ghost, and one equally foolish human, how could one not be surprised!

    Fuhong Pavilion Flower Sweeper

    There was a scholar, —— whose place and surname are forgotten, —— named Gu, who studied diligently under a curtain and rarely interacted with others. The stream beside his abode was clean, and the flowers were exquisite and vibrant. He loved it in his heart and lingered at dusk. There was a beautiful woman who came to fetch water with a jar. Gu was bewitched by her. From then on, whenever Gu came, the woman would appear, and thus they had a secret rendezvous like a jade cave and peach blossom.

    The woman walked, and Gu followed her. They traveled west to a mountain, where dangerous peaks stretched endlessly, strange and steep, piercing the clouds. In the distance, they heard the sounds of chickens and dogs, but no village was in sight. It was already dusk. Gu was too frightened to move. The woman intentionally deceived him, saying, "My lady has poor eyesight and has lost the old path. The mountain god is coming, what can we do?" Suddenly, a tiger rushed out of the forest, roared, and approached. Gu was startled and fell down. The woman waved her hand and said, "Go away! Don't scare my husband." The tiger roared and ran off. The woman helped Gu up. Another tiger followed, staring at Gu. The woman waved it away again. Gu was in a state of panic and asked to return twice. The woman wiped Gu's face with her sleeve and teased him, saying, "A young scholar, so timid, now that we are here, there is only one mountain ridge left."

    227

    谷虑险峻无由上，女子取带束其腰，以系于己臂，前行牵挽。扪萝扳木，拾级以登。猿径侧出，鸟道斜连，胁息增欷，乃始得下，则一川浩淼，横截山跟，明月澄波，深浅莫测。谷又欲返，曰：“济则无楫，渡则无粱，长江岂能飞渡耶?”女子曰：“无虑。”乃摘一梧叶，浮之水上，须臾便大如舟，篷樯桡柁悉具。携谷登舟，挂帆西渡。暴风大作，舟覆中流，谷及女子俱落水，水及于眉，命在呼吸。女子奋立于波上，提谷臂出之，遂携手同行。如履平地。少焉登岸，笑曰：“我戏阳侯，阳侯亦复戏我也。”谷衣履尽湿，女子略不沾濡，谷深讶其神，女子谓略习水性耳，何是异?因令谷尽脱其衣履，飏之风中，须臾干燥。

    Explanation

    227

    The valley was too dangerous to climb, so the man was unable to ascend. The woman took a belt, tied it around her waist, and fastened it to her own arm, then pulled him forward. She climbed vines and grabbed onto trees, stepping on stairs to ascend. The path was like one a monkey would take, branching off to the side, and like one a bird would take, winding diagonally. She held her breath and sighed heavily, and only then did they begin to descend. Below them was a vast expanse of water, cutting across the base of the mountain. The moon reflected on the clear waves, and the depth was impossible to measure. The man wanted to return, saying, "How can we cross without a boat? How can we cross without a bridge? How can we fly across the Yangtze River?" The woman replied, "Don't worry." She then picked a leaf from a Chinese parasol tree, floated it on the water, and in a short time, it grew as large as a boat, with a sail, mast, oars, and rudder all in place. She took the man aboard, hoisted the sail, and crossed to the west. A strong wind blew, and the boat capsized in the middle of the stream. Both the man and the woman fell into the water, with the water reaching their eyebrows, and their lives hanging by a thread. The woman stood up bravely on the waves, lifted the man's arm out of the water, and they held hands and walked together, as if walking on flat ground. Soon they reached the shore, and she laughed and said, "I played with the Yang River god, and the Yang River god also played with me." The man's clothes and shoes were all wet, but the woman was slightly not wet. The man was deeply surprised by her divine power, and she said she had slightly practiced water skills, asking, "What is so unusual?" She then told the man to completely remove his clothes and shoes and throw them into the wind. In a short time, they became dry.

    228

    前行数武，又隔一岭。谷视之，崔嵬千仞，壁立如削，仰视股栗，疑巨鬼欲来扑人，不觉变色，抚膺长叹。女子笑曰：“郎乃畏劳乎?不历险阻艰难而成好事古，盖亦寡矣!既畏之，当另觅坦途，无怠厥志也。”乃抽簪于石壁画之，石随簪而中辟，望如深巷，幽暗不见人，不知其远近。曳谷而行，略无踬碍，随过而石巷随合。
    　　约二里许，忽然开旷，石壁乃在其东矣。一村如画，台阁参差。乃度疏林，达近郭，历重门，经曲榭。于时明月西斜，楼鼓三报，人语寂静，睡声唏然。掩息幽房，悄语相对。女子出酒果款谷，情态甚殷。谷忽自思谈道有年，奈何效长卿薄行?因正襟危坐，庄语酬答，凛然有不可犯之容。女子笑曰：“风流薮泽中，何处容道学腔调?宜向皋比座前去演也。”遂起，引谷与就榻。衾枕焕烂，兰麝熏人。

    Explanation

    228

    As they advanced a few steps, they encountered another ridge. Gu looked up and saw a towering peak, thousands of feet high, with cliffs standing sheer as if cut by a knife. Looking up made his legs tremble with fear, and he suspected that a giant ghost was about to attack, unconsciously changing color and sighing deeply. The woman laughed and said, "Are you afraid of hard work? In ancient times, it was rare to achieve good things without going through dangers and difficulties. If you fear it, find another easy path, but do not slacken your resolve." She then drew a hairpin on the stone wall, and the stone split open along the line, appearing like a deep alley, dark and empty, with no one in sight and its distance unknown. Dragging Gu along, they moved through the alley with no obstacles, and the stone path closed behind them as they passed.

    After about two li, the landscape suddenly opened up, and the stone wall was now to their east. A picturesque village appeared, with pavilions and towers arranged unevenly. They crossed a sparse forest, reached the outskirts of the village, passed through heavy gates, and walked along winding pavilions. At this time, the moon was slanting west, and the drum of the watchtower had struck three times, with people's voices silent and the sound of sleep permeating the air. They entered a quiet room and whispered to each other. The woman offered wine and fruit to Gu, showing great enthusiasm and sincerity. Gu suddenly thought about the years he had spent discussing the Dao, and wondered why he should imitate Sima Xiangru's light behavior. He sat upright, answered with serious words, and had a stern and unapproachable demeanor. The woman laughed and said, "In the marshes of romance, where can there be room for the pretentious airs of Confucian scholars? You should go to the seat of the tiger skin to perform." She then stood up, led Gu to the bed. The bed and pillows were splendid, and the scent of orchid and musk permeated the air.

    229

    将欲交颈，忽闻有女子叩门，连呼“七娘子”。女子失色，低语曰：“冤业至矣!此吾家四娘，最悍戾，向与吾有隙，今来伺吾短矣。”急藏谷于复壁中，然后启关而问曰：“夜深已寝，姊来何为?”四娘径入，见酒果狼藉，双盏宛然，指之笑口；“欲分鸳鸯杯中馀滴耳。”巧词敲击，女子不能掩，乃怒曰：“人家夫婿，何与尔事?”四娘亦怒曰：“果尔夫婿耶?”进出而寻刀，，女子亦掣剑而出，各呼其群婢助战。婢皆锦袄狭柚，手执短兵，捷如猿。猛如虎，轻如燕，艳如花，共十馀人，格斗庭中。逾时出门外，声渐远。谷壁缝潜窥，汗流浃背。

    Explanation

    229

    About to embrace, suddenly heard a woman knocking on the door, continuously calling out "Seventh Sister." The woman turned pale and whispered, saying, "The karmic retribution has arrived! This is my sister Fourth, who is the most fierce and violent. She has been at odds with me in the past. Now she is here to find fault with me." Quickly hid Gu in the double-walled room, then opened the door and asked, "It's late, and I'm already asleep. What brings you here?" Fourth Sister walked straight in, saw the wine and fruits scattered about, and the two cups were still there. She pointed at them and laughed, "Wants to share the remaining drops in the鸳鸯杯 (double-cup)." Used clever words to provoke, the woman could not hide it. Then angrily said, "My husband is my affair, what do you have to do with it?" Is that really your husband? Went in and out looking for a knife, the woman also drew her sword and came out. Each called their group of maids to help in the fight. The maids were all wearing brocade coats and holding short swords, as agile as apes, as fierce as tigers, as light as swallows, as beautiful as flowers, a total of more than ten people, fought in the courtyard. After some time, they went out of the door, the sounds grew faint. Gu peered through the crack in the double wall, sweat poured down his back.

    230

    忽闻群女笑而反，欢呼曰：“七娘子一军北矣！”谷为怆然，益恐惧。旋闻四娘云：“赃当在室中。”命搜索。群婢挟谷出复壁。谷俯伏于地。四娘怒曰：“何物邪魔？坏我闺门!”命二婢牵出斩之。谷哀求数四，终不允。忽传六娘子至。四姬告之故，六娘曰：“罪固不兔，然念其初犯，姑舍之。”因命扶谷起。六娘责以礼义之正，间杂以嘲谑之辞。谷惊悸既定，转复羞惭，双颊凝红，低头无语。

    Explanation

    230

    Suddenly, the group of women laughed and returned, shouting with joy, "The army of the Seventh Lady has been defeated!" Gu was filled with sorrow and fear. Soon after, the Fourth Lady said, "The stolen goods should be in the room." She ordered a search. The group of maids dragged Gu out from the hidden wall. Gu prostrated himself on the ground.
