The Fourth Lady angrily said, "What kind of demon are you? You have ruined my family!" She ordered two maids to drag him out and kill him. Gu pleaded with her several times, but she finally refused. Suddenly, it was said that the Sixth Lady had arrived. The Fourth Lady informed her of the situation. The Sixth Lady said, "The crime is indeed unforgivable, but considering it was his first offense, I will let him go." She then ordered someone to help Gu up. The Sixth Lady taught him about proper conduct and righteousness, mixed with teasing and playful words. Once his shock and fear subsided, Gu became even more ashamed, his cheeks turning red. He lowered his head and remained silent.

    231

    有顷，六娘去，四娘笑而抚谷曰：“我见犹怜，何况妮子?慎毋作闺态向人。”谷不敢应。群婢皆粲然而笑，四娘叱之使退。谷知其无他，因谛视之，始惊其艳，殆与七娘伯仲矣。然心念七娘登山涉水，险难备尝，不获一当，安忍负之，反颜作仇人藁砧?且又不敢问其消息，凄然泣数行下。
    　　将近五更，群鸡乱叫，四娘不自持，说以同寝。谷辞不获，将从之。一婢踉跄来告曰：“七娘子提兵至矣!”四娘骇而奔，诸婢从之。遂闻户外马蹄声、戈戟声、士卒喧呼声。七娘戎妆而入，执谷手泣曰：“阿郎阿郎，几落贼妇之手矣。今贼妇安在?”谷曰：“逃矣。”七娘曰：“若是，不可缓，纵虎贻患。妾当了却之，再与郎欢聚。”即上马扬鞭，领众而去。

    Explanation

    231

    In a short while, Sixth Sister left, and Fourth Sister smiled and patted Gu, saying, "I find her even more pitiful, how much more so for a young girl? Do not act coyly towards others." Gu dared not respond. The maidservants all laughed brightly, and Fourth Sister scolded them and sent them away. Gu knew there was no other intention, so he looked at her closely and was surprised by her beauty, which was almost as beautiful as Sister Qi's. However, he thought of Sister Qi, who had endured all kinds of hardships by climbing mountains and crossing rivers, and had not been able to meet her, how could he bear to betray her, treat her as an enemy? And he dared not ask about her news, so he wept bitterly.

    As it was nearly midnight, the roosters crowed chaotically, and Fourth Sister could not control herself, saying they should sleep together. Gu excused himself but was unable to refuse, and was about to follow her. A maid servant stumbled in and said, "Sister Qi has brought her troops!" Fourth Sister was startled and ran away, and the other maids followed her. They then heard the sound of horse hooves, the clashing of weapons, and the shouting of soldiers outside.

    Sister Qi entered in military attire, took Gu's hand and wept, saying, "Master, master, I almost fell into the hands of the enemy woman. Where is the enemy woman now?" Gu said, "She has fled." Sister Qi said, "If so, it cannot be delayed, to let a tiger loose and cause trouble. I will deal with it, and reunite with the master again." She immediately mounted her horse and cracked the whip, leading the troops away.

    232

    谷倚户目送，顷刻寂然。树间晓鸟啁啾，东方已白矣。凝视立处，乃荆棘之丛，所倚者枯树也，大数十田，高数丈，空中而露杪。四围松柏苍苍，杳无人迹。惊绝呼号，山灵响答。
    　　良久，寻至一村，见其居人室庐、衣冠、言语皆不类今世。见谷皆大骇。一白髪叟引谷至其家，以笔墨问答，惟文字尚同。乃知离家不计程，已渡大海而西矣。其地乃西方绝国，向尝有人至京师，三年乃能达。谷遥望乡国，挥涕伤心。叟问故。谷具言其状。叟曰：“危哉！此飞天夜叉也，我国多有。不劘其齿牙，幸矣！”谷且恐且悲，知不可骤返。

    Explanation

    232

    Gǔ leaned against the door and watched, and in an instant, silence fell. Sparrows chirped in the trees, and the east had already turned white. Gazing intently at where he stood, he realized it was a thicket of thorns, and what he leaned against was a dead tree, tens of acres wide and several zhang tall, with its top exposed in the air. Surrounded by dark green pines and cypresses, there was no trace of human presence. He was so surprised that he shouted, and the mountain spirits responded with echoes.

    After a long while, he found a village. He saw that the inhabitants, their houses, clothes, and speech were all unlike the present world. They all were greatly startled to see Gǔ. An old man with white hair led Gǔ to his house, and they communicated through written questions and answers, only the writing was still the same. He then realized that he had left home without counting the distance, having already crossed the great sea to the west. The place was a remote country in the west, where people had previously traveled to the capital, taking three years to reach. Gǔ looked far back at his homeland, shedding tears and feeling sorrow. The old man asked the reason. Gǔ explained the situation in detail. The old man said, "Dangerous! This is a flying sky demon, and there are many of them in our country. It's fortunate if you don't get bitten by its teeth!" Gǔ was both afraid and sad, knowing that he could not return immediately.

    233

    居数月，当事者知之，闻于国王。王状貌丑恶可怖，相见甚敬礼。见其爱女，女悦之，达意于王。王喜，乃纳谷为婿。女貌颇类其父，而赋性善淫。成婚三月，谷不堪命，因念为异域鬼。不如葬夜叉之腹。
    　　遂私寻其故处，空山如故，枯木依然，徘徊浩叹，谓人面终不可覩矣，忽村郭房舍见于当前，悉符尔夜所见，七娘袨服华妆，旖旎而出，见谷即掉头欲入，若不相识。谷急前相呼，致词白表。七娘曰：“闻已知我形迹，那得不畏而复来耶?”谷曰：“虽知之，亦何害?情好固在，愿终怜我耳。”七娘笑曰：“真无赖之徒也。”遂纳之。

    Explanation

    233

    After several months, those in charge learned about the situation and reported it to the king. The king, with his ugly and terrifying appearance, greeted them with great respect and courtesy. When he saw his beloved daughter, she found Gu pleasing and conveyed her feelings to the king. The king was pleased and then took Gu as his son-in-law. The princess's appearance was somewhat like her father's, but she had a nature inclined to lust. After three months of marriage, Gu could no longer endure it and thought of himself as a ghost in a foreign land. It would be better to be buried in the belly of a night demon.

    He then secretly searched for the old place. The empty mountain remained the same, and the withered trees were still there. He wandered around, sighing deeply, thinking that he would never see a human face again. Suddenly, the village houses appeared before his eyes, all matching what he had seen that night. Qiqi was dressed in splendid attire and appeared gracefully. When she saw Gu, she turned her head and wanted to enter, as if she did not recognize him. Gu hurried forward to call out to her and expressed his feelings. Hearing that you already know my appearance, how could you not be afraid and come back? Even if you know, what harm is there? Our affection is still there, I hope you will continue to love me. Qiqi smiled and said, "You are truly a shameless fellow." And then she accepted him.

    234

    谷问：“四娘及群婢安在?”七娘笑而不答。叩之不已，乃谓曰：“实告君：吾乃芙蓉馆扫花女也。以误触花根，谪汲水人间，浇花三年，故与君遇于溪畔。君灵台不戒，牵绪巫云，本当斥逐遐陬，永沈异壤，然好学可嘉，得从未减，故为此以相戏耳。一切所见所历，皆吾幻设。此地本非绝国，亦无国王，王亦无女，女亦不嫁君，况乎四娘，群婢顷剡之现示者哉?孽海茫茫，回头是岸耳!”谷骇然状地谢罪。
    　　七娘引与登楼，遥指楼下曰：“此去君家不远矣。”复缘梯而下，则人物最光，悉失所在，乃立于舍旁之溪上耳。恍如梦觉，趋归书帷，砚水未乾，炉烟初烬，止顷刻之间耳。自是闭户读书。谨身寡过，识者以为有道之士焉。

    Explanation

    234

    谷问道：“四娘和她的侍女们在哪里？”七娘笑着没有回答。谷不断追问，七娘才说：“我告诉你实话吧：我是芙蓉馆的扫花女。因为不小心触碰了花根，被贬到人间汲水浇花三年，所以才在溪边与你相遇。你的心灵不加防范，陷入了虚幻的云雾中，本当被流放到遥远的地方，永远沉沦在异乡，但因为你热爱学习值得称赞，所得未曾减少，所以我才这样戏弄你。你所见所经历的一切，都是我幻化的景象。这里原本不是偏远之地，也没有国王，国王也没有女儿，女儿也不会嫁给你，更何况四娘和刚才显现的侍女们呢？罪孽的海洋无边无际，回头就是岸。”谷吓得在地上谢罪。

    七娘领着他上了楼，远远地指着楼下说：“离你家不远了。”他们又顺着梯子下来，只见人物最明亮的地方，全都消失了，他们站在屋旁的溪边。恍如从梦中醒来，赶紧回到书斋，砚水还未干，炉烟刚熄灭，只是一瞬间的事。从此他闭门读书，修身寡过，有见识的人认为他是有道之士。

    235

    非非子曰：以求女而摈绝国，以好学而恕狂且，则谷不能效刘、阮之尤，而女能推石、丁之意也。夫在昔已有子高瑶英共游事，则芙蓉片土，亦花里送郎之地矣。而谷竟山是获谴，岂其不幸欤?呜呼！士君子守身克念，暗室无欺，一念之差，岂不远哉?岂不危哉？
    　　哭笑疾
    　　昔吾乡一人有笑疾，视人颜色举动少异，即大笑不止。复一人有哭疾，与笑者殆称合璧。每两人相遇，便各发其疾。笑者见哭者之哭，则大笑。哭者见笑者之笑，则大哭。愈哭愈笑，愈笑愈哭。闻者往观，填衢塞巷。数十里之外，多有至者，亦或笑或哭。各因其哀乐之所感，而从旁骇叹者，亦十之五六。群狗闻人声鼎沸，又从而狺狺不休。于是笑声、哭声、众哗声、千百狗吠声，哄然数里。两家亲朋劝慰，言语皆不闻。笑者至于痰喘气索，哭者至于泪尽咽乾，犹相对张口拭目不少止、但无声而已。日暮，观者渐散。家人强牵其臂归，闭之室中，乃已。

    Explanation

    235

    Feifeizi said: In order to seek a woman, he was rejected by the country; in order to love learning, he was forgiven for his rashness. Gu could not imitate Liu and Ruan's exemplary behavior, but the woman could understand the meaning or intent of Shi and Ding. In the past, Zigao and Yaoying had been on an outing together, and this place is also where lovers are sent off amidst flowers. However, Gu was blamed or condemned at the end of the mountain. Is this not unfortunate? Alas! Gentlemen should maintain their integrity and control their thoughts, even in secret places, and should not deceive. A single thought can lead to a significant difference, is it not far? Is it not dangerous?

    Laughing and Crying Illness

    In my hometown, there was once a person with a laughing illness. Whenever they saw someone's expression or actions slightly different, they would laugh uncontrollably. Another person had a crying illness, and the two almost perfectly matched. Every time the two met, they each expressed their illness. The more they cried, the more they laughed, and the more they laughed, the more they cried. Those who heard about it went to see, filling the streets and blocking the alleys. Many people arrived from dozens of miles away, either laughing or crying. Each person felt what they felt because of their sadness and joy, and about half of the people from the side were shocked and amazed. The dogs heard the people's voices and then barked incessantly. Thus, the laughter, crying, crowd noise, and thousands of dog barks resounded for dozens of miles. The relatives and friends of the two families tried to comfort them, but their words were all unheard. The person who laughed coughed and wheezed, short of breath, and the person who cried exhausted their tears and dried their throats, still facing each other, open-mouthed and wiping their eyes, not stopping but silent. As the sun set, the onlookers gradually dispersed. The family forcibly dragged them by the arm home, locked them in the room, and only then did they stop.

    236

    妇失裈
    　　有男子御其妻归宁者，及山僻之间，妇欲小遗。时远顾无人，即大树下溺焉。忽若有捉臂而褫其裈者，竟失裈，惊怪而归。
    　　洎夜分，夫妇方寝，闻剥啄声甚急，且呼其名。夫起应之，归止之曰：“日间事甚怪，今谁何夜呼?姑勿出。”夫不可，遽往启关。妇愈疑，尾而烛之。骇然一声，而夫踣地上，不复作声。急视，已无首矣。大哭呼邻里，须臾毕集。验其创。殊无点血，非斧刃所剒，若石磨去者然。俱莫明其故，以闻于令。
    　　令鞫知妇溻失裈事，曰：“是必触妖物作祟也。”遂命导肩舆至其处，周视良久。复命就溺处掘地验之，深丈馀，得大石。绠石出之，则妇裈裹其夫首宛然在也。碎其石，血涔涔滴，腥闻数里。
    　　非非子曰：闺中人少溲野外，取祸之道也，否亦取辱。噫！孰使之然哉?君子盖无恶于石。
    　　捕鱼仙
    　　捕鱼仙者，不知何许人也，出捕鱼得仙，故云。

    Explanation

    236

    The Woman Who Lost Her Underwear

    There was a man who attended to his wife on her return home. They passed through a remote area, and the woman wanted to urinate. Seeing no one around, she went to a large tree and relieved herself. Suddenly, someone grabbed her arm and stripped her of her underwear. She lost her underwear and returned home in shock.

    By nightfall, the couple was about to go to bed when they heard someone knocking on the door and calling her name. The husband got up to answer the door and told her, "What happened during the day was very strange. Who is calling at night? Just don't go out." The husband didn't listen and hurriedly opened the door. The wife was even more suspicious and followed him with a lit candle. Suddenly, the husband fell to the ground and made no sound. She looked closely and saw that he had no head. She cried out and called the neighbors, who soon gathered. They examined the wound and found no trace of blood, suggesting that it was not caused by an axe but rather as if ground off by a stone. No one understood what had happened, so they reported it to the magistrate.

    The magistrate investigated and learned about the woman's lost underwear. He said, "This must be the work of a supernatural creature." He ordered a sedan chair to be brought to the site and inspected it thoroughly. They then dug at the spot where the woman had urinated and found a large stone. When they pulled out the stone, her husband's head was wrapped in her underwear. They broke the stone, and blood flowed out, with the smell spreading for several miles.

    Non-Non said: Women rarely urinate in the wild, which is a way to invite disaster, if not disgrace. Who made it so? Gentlemen generally do not hate stones.

    The Fishing Immortal

    The Fishing Immortal is a man whose origin is unknown. He became an immortal while fishing, hence the name.

    237

    仙性憨而诚。家近大溪，捕鱼为业。兄某甲客于边地，十馀年末归。仙竭力本母，未尝缺乏。
    　　一夜宿溪畔，闻鬼语曰：“明午铁拐仙人过此，吾辈当远避，”仙尝闻里老谈说，知铁拐乃八仙之一，并识其状类丐者，因跪而俟之。
    　　次日午晌，果见一丐者蓬头跣足，蹒跚而来，疮秽臭恶，不可向迩。仙候其过，抱其足，乞大仙度世。丐者笑曰：“尔知我仙乎?视尔缘分，”因探葫芦中得药一丸，如樱桃大，谓仙曰：“是尔造化，尚带得此丸，可将去。”仙拜受之，视丐者，忽不见。
    　　仙得丸，喜极玩弄。归以告母及邻人，人咸嗤之。后偶以丸置鱼瓮巾，鱼死者尽活，仙益喜。每鱼死，辄以丸活之，用是卖得数倍利，乡之人始闻其异矣。有谲者私计是丸殆莫仙丹也，以投鱼，活鱼，若以咽人，当不死；谋夺之。仙觉而吞之，忽点首大悟曰：“原来如此！”自是言未来事，无不奇中，而神施妙用不可测识矣。

    Explanation

    237

    Xian was simple and sincere. His home was near a large river, and he made a living by fishing. His brother, Mr. Ji, had been a guest in a distant land for over ten years and had not returned. Xian worked hard to support his mother, and they never lacked for anything.

    One night, Xian stayed by the riverbank and heard ghosts talking: "At noon tomorrow, the Iron Crutch Immortal will pass this way. We must stay far away." Xian had often heard the elders talk about the Eight Immortals and knew that Iron Crutch was one of them. He also recognized his appearance as that of a beggar. Therefore, he knelt and waited for him.

    The next day at noon, sure enough, a beggar appeared, bald-headed and barefoot, limping along. He was covered in sores and smelled terrible, making it unbearable to be near him. Xian waited until he passed and grabbed his foot, begging the Immortal to save him. The beggar smiled and said, "Do you know I am an Immortal? Look at your fate." He then reached into his gourd and took out a pill as big as a cherry, saying to Xian, "This is your fate. You still have this pill. Take it." Xian bowed and accepted the pill. When he looked up, the beggar had disappeared.

    Xian was overjoyed and played with the pill. He returned home and told his mother and neighbors, but they all laughed at him. Later, he accidentally placed the pill in a fish basket, and all the dead fish came back to life. Xian was even more delighted. Whenever fish died, he would revive them with the pill, and as a result, he made several times more profit. The villagers began to notice his unusual abilities.

    A cunning person suspected that the pill was an elixir of immortality. He tried it on a dead fish, and it came back to life. He thought that if he swallowed it, he would not die. He plotted to steal the pill. Xian sensed this and swallowed the pill. Suddenly, he nodded and realized, "Ah, I see!" From then on, he could predict future events with uncanny accuracy, and his use of divine power and subtle methods was beyond comprehension.

    238

    一日，谓母曰：“儿当迎兄归，计明日可到。”母虽知其术，犹未甚信。仙别去，明日兄果归。母惊问其得归状，兄曰：“弟昨暮省我。我以离家久，初犹不识，叩其姓字，乃知弟也。大怪其来，弟乃言来迎我。我辞以程远费乏，不然，且早归。弟笑曰：“无虑也，顷刻即至耳。”我深嗤其妄。弟言：“试一行，何害?”固令我附肩上，坚戒闭目。时已四更馀，但觉风声贯耳，云气侵肌，迅鸟奔马莫喻其速，殆凌虚也。终食之间，倏已投地，令我开目，曰：“兄可步行以归，吾访青城山人去，不日亦返。”言讫不见。我视其地，依稀记是某村，去家十馀里，遂寻路得归。今见阿母，犹疑梦中也。”母告以吞丸之事，于是咸知仙盖已仙云。

    Explanation

    238

    One day, the younger brother told his mother, "I will go and bring my brother back, it is estimated that he can arrive tomorrow." Although the mother knew of the younger brother's magical abilities, she still did not fully believe him. The fairy departed, and the next day, the older brother indeed returned. The mother was surprised and asked how he managed to return. The older brother said, "The younger brother visited me last night. I was surprised by his coming because I had been away from home for a long time and did not recognize him at first. I asked him his name, and then I knew it was the younger brother. I was greatly surprised by his coming, and the younger brother then said he came to fetch me. I declined due to the long distance and lack of funds, otherwise, I would return early. The younger brother laughed and said, 'Do not worry, it will be here in a moment.' I deeply ridiculed his nonsense. The younger brother said, 'Try it once, what harm can it do?' He strictly warned me to climb onto his shoulder and close my eyes. It was after the fourth watch of the night, and I only felt the wind rushing past my ears, the mist touching my skin, the speed incomparable to that of swift birds and galloping horses, it seemed to be flying in the air. Within the time it takes to eat a meal, suddenly, we landed on the ground. He told me to open my eyes and said, 'You can walk back home, I will go to visit the people of Mount Qingcheng, and will return soon.' After he finished speaking, he disappeared. I looked at the place and vaguely remembered it as a certain village, about ten miles from home, and found the way and returned home. Now that I see Mother, I still doubt it was a dream." The mother told him about the matter of swallowing the pill, and thus everyone knew that the fairy had indeed become a fairy.

    239

    数日，仙归，曰：“母于兄弟阔绝多岁，今得聚首，良可乐也。当大会宾客亲朋，以贺斯遭。第舍宇太隘，当少谋之。然明日便可召客，毋迫于事。”至明日，舍外甲第大启，杰阁重门，雕栏曲榭，广十馀亩。供帐之盛，埒于公侯。酒肴歌舞，莫不极精尽妙。鲜衣盛饰，进母及兄。客至，罔不骇异，见所未见也。庆宴三日，远近来观。
    　　先是，溪旁有高塔，名会仙塔。新宅甫成，适当门庭之要。仙漫言当移之他所，乃不碍车马辐辏。言罢，伏几而寐。有顷，客且至，兄乃呼之。仙醒而愠曰：“适移塔南海之岸，安置末正，兄乃促我归，亦缺事也。”兄趋视门外，塔已不见。后有乡人游南潜，见塔于岸上，“会仙”二字依然，虽欹侧而终不倾塌，盖仙故为之，以留奇迹也。

    Explanation

    239

    Several days later, the immortal returned and said, "Mother has been separated from her brothers for many years, and now they can reunite, which is truly joyful. We should invite guests, relatives, and friends to celebrate this occasion. The house is too narrow, so we should make slight plans. However, you can invite guests tomorrow, without being rushed." The next day, the mansion outside was grandly opened, with majestic towers and heavy doors, carved railings, and winding terraces, covering more than ten acres. The splendor of the furnishings rivaled that of dukes and marquises. Food, wine, music, and dance were all extremely exquisite and perfect. Guests dressed in fine and elaborate attire were introduced to the mother and brothers. When the guests arrived, they were all astonished, seeing things they had never seen before. The celebration lasted for three days, and people from far and near came to watch.

    Previously, there was a tall tower by the stream, named the Meeting of Immortals Tower. The new mansion was just completed and was conveniently located at the entrance. The immortal casually said it should be moved to another place, so it would not obstruct the flow of carriages and horses. After saying this, the immortal lay down and fell asleep. After a short while, guests were about to arrive, and the brother then called the immortal. The immortal woke up and was annoyed, saying, "Just now I moved the tower to the shore of the South Sea, but the placement was not quite right, and you hurried me back, which is also a bit of a bother." The brother rushed to look outside, and the tower was no longer there. Later, a local person traveling to the South Sea saw the tower on the shore, with the words "Meeting of Immortals" still there, though the tower was leaning, but it never collapsed, because the immortal had done it to leave a miracle.

    240

    后辞家人入武夷山，遂不返。其捕鱼敞笱，一日风雨之际，忽化为青龙，腾空而去。
    　　髑 髅
    　　余偕数君子看花丰台，饮于卖花翁，座中相与说鬼。罗两峰述一髑髅事，亦可发一噱也。
    　　扬州有狂夫，从数人行郭外。道有髑髅甚夥，或侮之，辄被祟，詈骂有声。于是相戒无犯。
    　　狂夫大言曰：“咄，是何敢然!”就一髑髅之口溺焉， 巳戏曰：“吾洒汝！”溺毕，疾行数步。夸于众曰：“田舍奴，我岂妄哉”旋闻耳后低呼曰：“拿酒来！”狂夫愕然，诘于众，众未之言也。行数武，又呼如前，众亦未闻。少顷，又呼曰：“顷云洒我，何诳也?”声渐厉。始信为髑髅之祟，漫应之曰：“汝欲酒，第随以来。”髑髅曰：“诺！”于是寂然。

    Explanation

    240

    After bidding farewell to his family, he entered the Wuyi Mountains and never returned. One day, while fishing with a wide-mouthed basket, during a storm, he suddenly transformed into a green dragon and soared into the sky.

    Skulls

    I accompanied several gentlemen to admire flowers at Fongtai, drinking with an old man selling flowers. In our conversation, we discussed ghosts. Luo Liangfeng recounted a story about a skull, which also provoked laughter.

    In Yangzhou, there was a madman who, accompanied by several others, walked outside the city walls. Along the way, they encountered many skulls. Some of them insulted the skulls, and as a result, they were plagued by evil spirits, with loud curses. They then warned each other not to offend them.

    The madman boasted, "What audacity! How dare you?" He then urinated into the mouth of one skull and playfully said, "I am sprinkling you!" After finishing, he hurriedly walked a few steps. He bragged to the others, "Rustic fool, am I lying?" Suddenly, he heard a low call behind him, "Bring me some wine!" The madman was startled and questioned the others, but they had not heard anything. After walking a few steps, the call came again, and the others still did not hear it. A short while later, the call came again, "A moment ago, you said you would sprinkle me, how can you deceive me?" The voice grew louder. He then believed it was a skull haunting him and carelessly replied, "If you want wine, just follow and come." The skull said, "Okay!" And then there was silence.

    241

    既入城，共登酒家楼，列坐呼酒，虚其一位，设匕著杯杓，以飨髑髅。众每饮一觞，则以一觞酹之。酒注楼下，泛滥如泉。叩其“醉乎”，则应曰：“死且不朽，卮酒安足辞哉!”髑髅饮既无算，众皆厌之，次第散去，惟狂夫不能自脱，颇为所苦。久之，髑髅且醉，狂夫绐以如厕，急下楼，取金质酒家，不暇论值，悄然而遁。
    　　已闻楼上索酒甚急，酒家保往应，杳不见人，大骇，以为妖。空中喧呶曰：“我何妖?奴辈招我来饮。乃避客而去耶?须为我召来！”意甚怒，酒家谕之曰：“招汝者谁?避汝者谁?酒徒千百，我乌知之?汝既相识，曷弗自寻?索之于我，汝殊愦愦！”于是髑髅语塞，忿恨而去。
    　　尝见杂剧中扮一嗜酒鬼，挂壶于襟，出杯于怀，且哭且饮，亦髑髅之流亚也。

    Explanation

    241

    After entering the city, they all climbed to the wine house's upper floor, sat in a row, and called for wine. They left one seat empty, set out chopsticks, bowls, and ladles, and entertained the skull. Each time the crowd drank a cup, they would pour one cup as an offering. The wine flowed down to the lower floor, overflowing like a spring. They asked the skull, "Are you drunk?" It replied, "Even if dead, I remain unperishable; how can I refuse a cup of wine!" The skull drank without counting, and the crowd grew tired of it and gradually dispersed. Only the madman could not free himself and suffered greatly. After a while, the skull became drunk. The madman deceived by saying he needed to go to the toilet, quickly went downstairs, took gold to pay the wine shop, without time to discuss the price, and quietly fled.

    They heard that upstairs, there was an urgent demand for wine. The wine shop owner went to respond, but found no one, and was greatly startled, thinking it was a ghost. From the air came a loud voice: "What ghost am I? You people invited me to drink. Now you avoid me and leave? You must call me back!" The shop owner angrily said: "Who invited you? Who avoided you? There are countless wine drinkers, how can I know? Since you recognize them, why don't you find them yourself? To ask me is very unreasonable!" Thus, the skull was speechless and left in anger.

    I have seen in a variety play a role of a wine-loving ghost, wearing a wine flask on his chest, taking out cups from his sleeves, crying and drinking, which is also a kind of skull.

    242

    耳食录卷十一
    　　二皂役
    　　族祖某公，处邻村王氏西席。一夜，梦自家赴馆，道逢二皂役，以黑索牵二妇人。妇人哀哭，役呵之曰：“此何时，犹效楚囚耶?”公前问故，役曰：“奉公拘人，何与尔书生事!”公视二妇人，大骇：其一主人之母，其一主人之佣媪，老于其家者也。亟言此为某某，今何罪而见拘?役曰：“亦无甚大罪，但二妇好诅詈人，口角太多。”公辩曰：“此亦妇女常态，官长奈何察此细故耶?”役曰：“闺门诟谇，风化攸关，那得不察?”即牵以行。公复追之，为之缓颊曰：“是固有罪，然于我曾有葭莩，乞纵之。”井取囊中钱二百以畀役。役初不听，言之至再三，二役乃私相谓，其语不可闻。既而指主人母谓公曰：“是本当拘去，今为先生舍之。”遂解索，纵之去。公称谢，役反其钱曰：“吾曹得此无所用，但求归语主人，以后无相忘，足矣。”佣媪啜泣向公，公欲更为之请，役已挟之奔而去。公度不能及，太息而行。

    Explanation

    242

    Ear Food Record, Volume Eleven
    Two Bailiffs

    My distant ancestor, a certain public, was serving as a tutor in a neighboring village for the Wang family. One night, he dreamed that he was going to his post, and on the road, he encountered two bailiffs who were using a black rope to tie two women. The women were weeping and lamenting, and the bailiffs shouted at them, "What time is this? Are you still acting like captives from Chu?" The public approached and asked why they were being detained. The bailiffs replied, "We are acting on official orders to detain people. What business is this of you, a scholar?" The public looked at the two women and was greatly alarmed: one was the mother of the Wang family, and the other was an old servant who had worked in the Wang household for a long time. He quickly said, "These are such and such, what crime have they committed to be detained?" The bailiffs said, "They don't have any major crimes, but the two women are prone to cursing and arguing too much." The public argued, "This is also a common trait of women. Why should the officials concern themselves with such trivial matters?" The bailiffs said, "Disputes within the family concern the moral and social order. How could they be ignored?" They then took the women away. The public followed them and tried to intercede on their behalf, saying, "They do have a fault, but they are related to me through distant kinship. Please let them go." He then took out two hundred coins from his bag and gave them to the bailiffs. The bailiffs initially refused, but after the public pleaded with them repeatedly, the two bailiffs privately discussed something that was inaudible. Eventually, one of the bailiffs pointed to the mother of the Wang family and said to the public, "This one should have been detained, but for you, I will let her go." He then untied the rope and let her go. The public thanked them, and the bailiffs returned the money, saying, "We don't need this money. We just ask you to tell the owner when you return, and we will not be forgotten in the future." The old servant wept and looked at the public. The public wanted to intercede on her behalf again, but the bailiffs had already taken her and ran away. The public knew he could not catch up and sighed as he continued on his way.

    243

    忽残柝鸣窗，倏然惊觉，深讶所梦之异，披衣而起，敲石燃缸，坐以达旦。旋闻书童来报：昨夜主人之母及佣媪同时暴卒，主人之母复苏云。

    Explanation

    243

    Suddenly, the sound of the watchman's gong rang out through the window, and I was abruptly startled awake, greatly surprised by the strangeness of my dream. I got up, put on my clothes, struck a flint to light a lamp, and sat there until dawn. Soon after, the page boy came to report that last night, the master's mother and the servant lady had both died suddenly at the same time. The master's mother regained consciousness and spoke.

    244

    碧 桃
    　　马晴湖为余言：
    　　其乡赵梦虚，字云友，性倜傥，好为汗漫游。年二十馀，客杭州，爱西湖之胜，流连近半载，为山水所醉矣。而春夏之间，花柳争发，靓妆袨服，香车画肪、为湖上游者四望如云，赵往来寓目，亦不觉其情之移也。
    　　一日，舣舟断桥，独步至花神庙。向一神注视良久，戏谓曰：“花神年少，得毋寂乎?”因吟一绝云：
    　　“彩云堆垛眩双睛，欲 向罗浮梦里行。
    　　今夜月明横翠羽，玉梅花下待卿卿。”
    　　惘然返旅舍，买酒夜酌。朦胧就枕，梦一少女搴帘而入，衣五铢缟素之衣，拖六幅绉碧之裙，足系五色云霞之履。耳垂明珰，鬓朵珠翘，行步姗姗，丰神旷世。遥坐谓赵曰：“感君挚意，不避崔苑之嫌，来与君共谈风月。”言词渊雅，谑浪波生。赵颇惑之，挑以微词。不答而起，辞曰：“坐久更深，恐风露侵入衣袂，妾行矣！”赵急挽之，已冉冉出户去，莫知所之。遗素帕一方于榻上，取视之，有诗曰：

    Explanation

    244

    Bai Tao
    Ma Qinghu told me:
    His hometown's Zhao Mengxu, courtesy name Yunyou, was a man of free spirit who enjoyed wandering aimlessly. At the age of twenty, he traveled to Hangzhou and fell in love with the beauty of West Lake. He stayed there for nearly half a year, enchanted by the natural scenery. During the spring and summer, flowers and willows competed to bloom, with women dressed in elaborate and colorful attire, riding in ornate carriages and boats, making the area bustling with activity. Zhao observed these scenes and found himself captivated by the beauty around him.

    One day, he docked his boat at the Broken Bridge and walked alone to the Flower Goddess Temple. He gazed at a statue of the goddess for a long time, playfully saying, "The Flower Goddess is young, isn't she lonely?" He then recited a quatrain:
    "Clouds of color dazzle my eyes, I wish to dream on the fairy mountain.
    Tonight, under the bright moon, I shall spread my green feathers,
    Waiting for you under the white plum blossoms."

    Disoriented, he returned to his inn and bought wine to drink at night. As he lay down to rest, he dreamed of a young woman pulling up the curtain and entering. She wore a five-zhu white silk robe, a six-fold pleated green skirt, and shoes adorned with colorful clouds and rosy hues. Her ears were adorned with bright earrings, and her hair was decorated with jade hairpins. She moved gracefully and had an extraordinary appearance. She sat down and said to Zhao, "I am moved by your sincere feelings. I do not fear the scandal of Cui Yingying, and I come to share the moon and breeze with you." Her words were refined and elegant, and she spoke with a playful and lively manner. Zhao was captivated by her, and he provoked her with subtle words. She did not respond but stood up and said, "It is getting late, and the cold wind and dew might seep into my clothes. I must leave now!" Zhao tried to stop her, but she gradually left through the door, disappearing into the night. She left a white handkerchief on the bed. Zhao took it to read, and there was a poem that said:

    245

    “琼楼深处片幡遮，久别孤山处士家。
    　　自有碧桃开洞口，不须惆帐向梅花。”
    　　览毕，倏然惊觉，辨色而兴，佩声香气犹堪想象，洵哉花神之来也！
    　　次日至庙，祷请再见，花神若相视而笑，有意无意，宛然搴裳夜过时也。低徊而返，徜徉湖上。水光山色，尽入愁肠。花外鸟语叮咛，若与愁人浩叹相应答。
    　　有老翁见而问之曰：“少年何叹！此非叹所矣。”赶曰：“人各有心，翁岂知之?”翁曰：“若是，何不过我?我有敝庐在武林门内，桑园十亩，颇称闲适。朝夕与老夫晤对，或可以破寂寥乎?”盖翁实心仪赵之风采，而未知其才藻也，欲有以试之，故仓卒相邀。赵坚辞不获，勉从之，而心亦颇讶。
    　　既至，馆赵于桑园。园与内室相联接，盖幽居而华构者也。款接之殷，供侍之盛，实愈凡分。而宾主觞咏之间，亦称劲敌焉。

    Explanation

    245

    "Deep within the jade tower, a banner shields the way, long separated from the hermit scholar's abode on Solitary Mountain.
    　　There are naturally beautiful peach blossoms opening at the cave entrance, no need for sorrow toward the plum blossoms."
    　　After finishing, suddenly startled awake, distinguishing the colors and arising, the sound of the pendant and the fragrance still imaginable, indeed the flower god has come!
    　　The next day, arriving at the temple, praying to see again, the flower god as if looking at each other and smiling, intentionally or unintentionally, as if lifting the skirt at night. Lingering and returning, wandering on the lake. The water light and mountain colors, all entering the sorrowful gut. Beyond the flowers, bird talk and admonitions, as if responding to the deep sighs of the grieving person.
    　　An old man saw and asked, "Why is the young man sighing! This is not a place to sigh." Zhao replied, "Each person has their own heart, how could the old man know?" The old man said, "If so, why not come to me? I have a humble abode within Wulin Gate, ten acres of mulberry orchard, quite suitable for leisure. Morning and evening in conversation with me, the old man, perhaps to break the loneliness?" It is said that the old man truly admires Zhao's appearance, but does not know his literary talent, wants to test him, therefore hastily inviting. Zhao firmly refused but could not, reluctantly followed, and was also quite surprised.
    　　Having arrived, lodging Zhao in the mulberry orchard. The garden and inner room are connected, it is a secluded yet splendidly constructed place. Warm reception, abundant service, indeed exceeds ordinary standards. During the host and guest's drinking and singing, also considered strong rivals.

    246

    一日方赋诗，微闻帘间芗泽，银钩动处，半露烟鬟；赵频目之，翁觉之而不语。如是凡十馀次，赵相思之情见于颜色，翁乃笑指帘间曰：“此弱女也，请以侍君子箕帚。”赵惶恐，谓翁诮己，半晌乃辞曰：“已婚邬氏，曷敢辱命?”翁复请以女备小星之选，赵避席再拜曰：“一介鄙人，辱长者置之门舍，已幸矣!奈何复以兰惠下匹蒿艾乎?”翁固言之，乃卜吉成礼焉。
    　　定情之夕，女丰艳非常，才亦婉丽。问名，曰“碧桃”，赵忽触悟花神“碧桃开洞口”之句，遂为碧桃言之。碧桃嗟叹曰：“是矣是矣，花神乃撮合山矣。妾向见君而弗能释也，则频梦一女郎来谓曰：“郎在卿家，不可失。”因是而情愈不禁。妾父母怜妾过甚，不欲违其意。丝箩之托，有自来矣！”赵问所梦之状，则容颜服饰一一与己梦相符，洵哉花神之来也！

    Explanation

    246

    On one day, while composing poetry, Zhao faintly smelled the fragrance of incense between the curtains. Where the silver hook moved, half revealing her hair like smoke. Zhao frequently glanced at her, and the old man noticed it but did not speak. Like this, more than ten times, Zhao's longing was evident in his expression. The old man then smiled and pointed to the curtain, saying, "This is my frail daughter, please let her serve you as your wife." Zhao was alarmed, thinking the old man was mocking him. After a while, he declined, saying, "I have already married Wu, how dare I disgrace your command?" The old man then asked to have his daughter serve as a concubine. Zhao got up from his seat and bowed twice, saying, "A humble person like me, disgraced by the elder placing me in his home, is already fortunate! How could I compare to a noble lady with a lowly person?" The old man insisted, and they chose a good omen to complete the marriage.

    On the night of their engagement, the girl was exceptionally beautiful and graceful. When asked her name, she said "Bixiao." Zhao suddenly remembered the flower spirit's line "Bixiao blooms at the cave entrance," so he told Bixiao about it. Bixiao sighed and said, "Yes, yes, the flower spirit is the matchmaker. I have seen you and cannot forget you, and I often dreamed of a young lady who told me, 'You are in my family, don't lose me.' Because of this, my feelings became even stronger. My parents love me too much and do not want to go against their wishes. The trust of a silk rope, has always been there!" Zhao asked about the details of the dream, and her appearance and attire matched his dream exactly. Indeed, it was the flower spirit who came.

    247

    遂共焚香，诣庙中谢蹇修焉，而以沉香肖其像，祀于家。
    　　我来也
    　　昔京师一偷儿奇绝莫测，每入人家窃财货，虽高墉坚壁，弗能防也。临去，必粉书“我来也”三字于门壁间。被盗之家岁以百计。官吏罗而捕之，久不获。
    　　一日，近县获一盗，云是“我来也”，解以来京，系之郡狱。怨家共诣守言之，皆欲得而甘心焉。盗闻之，谓狱卒曰：“我固尝作贼，然实非“我来也”。今既诬服，有死而已。子幸善视我，当即有以相报。”卒遂善待之。
    　　数日，盗谓曰：“我有白金三百，在某塔下，盍往取之?”卒以为诳，盗曰：“子夜半绕塔三匝，当有应者，金可得也。”如言果得之，卒喜甚，待之益厚。盗又曰：“某桥下水中有金器数事，亦以赠子。”卒曰：“桥道行者甚众，何术取之?”盗曰：“不难。以竹篮盛衣往浣，沉之水底，下而取衣，因以金置篮中，谁能见耶?”果又得之。

    Explanation

    247

    Together they burned incense and went to the temple to thank the matchmaker, and then they carved an image of him from agarwood and worshipped it at home.

    "I Come"

    In the capital city, there was a notorious thief who was extraordinarily elusive. He would enter people's homes to steal valuables, and even high walls and strong fortifications could not prevent him. Before leaving, he would always write the three characters "I Come" in powdered ink on the door or wall. The number of families he robbed was in the hundreds each year. Officials set traps to catch him, but they were unsuccessful for a long time.

    One day, a county near the capital captured a thief who claimed to be "I Come." He was brought to the capital and imprisoned in the local jail. The victims, who were known as "resentful people," all went to the governor to report him, and they all wanted to get their revenge. The thief, upon hearing this, told the jailer, "I have indeed been a thief, but I am not 'I Come.' Now that I have been falsely accused, I am only afraid of death. If you treat me well, I will have something to repay you." The jailer then treated him kindly.

    A few days later, the thief said to him, "I have three hundred taels of silver under a certain pagoda. Why don't you go and get it?" The jailer thought he was lying, but the thief said, "At midnight, go around the pagoda three times, and you will find someone who will give you the gold." Following his instructions, the jailer indeed got the gold, and he was very happy. He treated the thief even better. The thief then said, "Under a certain bridge, there are several pieces of gold hidden in the water. I will also give them to you." The jailer said, "The bridge is a busy thoroughfare; how can I retrieve them?" The thief said, "It's not difficult. Take a basket, put your clothes in it, and go to wash them. Sink the basket to the bottom of the water, take out your clothes, and then put the gold in the basket. Who will see it?" The jailer indeed got the gold.

    248

    是夜盗谓卒曰：“有所求于子，而无损于子，其许我乎?”卒问云何，盗曰：“求为我哲脱枷钮，出狱勾当一事，五更即至，决不子累。”卒颇难之，盗作色曰：“子受吾千金之赠，我出即不返，子以此受薄谴，亦无负也。矧不至此耶?若必不获命，诚不难破网而逃，于子反恐不利耳。”卒不得已，纵之去，心甚惶惑。比五更，盗倏从檐际投下，复就系。卒大喜过望。
    　　次日，有富民诣府投状云；“夜来大亡其财，门上大书，‘我来也’。”守诧曰：“‘我来也’固未获耶?狱中盗之诬明矣。”遂薄责而释之。
    　　卒归家，妻迎谓曰：“夜来一事颇奇。有谁何呼门甚急，我启门问之，其人突入，掷一布袋于地而去。其中累累者不知何物，我不敢发也。”卒亟启视之，则皆黄白物。乃悟盗即“我来也”，为此以求免，且以报己也，秘不告人。

    Explanation

    248

    That night, the thief said to the guard, "I have something to ask of you, and it will not harm you. Will you grant my request?" The guard asked what it was, and the thief replied, "I ask you to wisely remove the lock and let me out of the jail to handle one matter. I will return by the fifth watch, and it will not cause you any trouble." The guard found it quite difficult, and the thief became stern and said, "You received my thousand gold coins as a gift. If I do not return, you will only suffer a light punishment, and it will not be a burden. How much less so in this case? If you absolutely refuse, it would not be difficult for me to break free and escape. It would be more disadvantageous to you." The guard, having no choice, let him go, feeling greatly troubled. By the fifth watch, the thief suddenly dropped from the eaves and returned to his original state. The guard was greatly overjoyed beyond expectations.

    The next day, a wealthy person went to the government office to file a complaint, saying, "I lost a lot of money last night. There was a large sign on the door that read ‘I am coming.’" The official said in surprise, "It is clear that the thief in jail is falsely accused." He gave a light reprimand and released him.

    The guard returned home, and his wife greeted him, saying, "There was a strange event last night. Someone called very urgently, and when I opened the door to ask who it was, the person suddenly entered, threw a cloth bag on the ground, and left. I did not dare to open it because I did not know what was inside." The guard quickly opened and looked inside. There were a lot of items whose nature was unknown. He then realized that the thief was ‘I am coming,’ and he did this in order to avoid punishment and to repay me. He kept it a secret and did not tell anyone.

    249

    ——此事似出宋人说部，姑记于此。
    　　董 公
    　　乐安董公，绍兴时人，传者隐其名。未遇时，从师读书，他姓，为同学士所不齿。弗与共饮食，诮让侮辱，靡所不至，以其贫也。同学窃主人园蔬，为所觉，嫁之于董。董弗能平，他日试亦窃之，猝遇主人，甚惭恧。主人曰：“无害也。先生果缺于供，当日奉不腆之饵，蔬宁足道哉?”董谓其诮己，辞谢而返。主人果遣奴于馈饮食，甚丰备，礼意有加焉。董惊其异数，屡辞弗获，深不自安。同学亦莫不疑骇，稍稍亲昵之。
    　　主人复言于师，请以女妻董。师讶曰：“公真不有其女。”主人曰：“先生休矣，乌有董生而长贫贱者?”师固素器董，闻言甚喜，乃更私问曰：“董生固可妻，但公何以识之?”主人曰：“以盗蔬识之。前夜吾偶窥园，见二红灯笼照耀菜畦间。迫而视之，董生耳，灯固无见也。是必大贵，盖鬼神为之役矣。”师亦深以为异，遂媒而妻之。

    Explanation

    249

    This story appears to be from a collection of anecdotes or short stories from the Song dynasty, and I will temporarily record it here.

    Mr. Dong from Le'an

    Mr. Dong, a man from Le'an during the Song dynasty, is mentioned in a text that does not reveal his name. Before he achieved success, he studied under a teacher, but he was not well-regarded by his classmates due to his poverty. They did not share food or drink with him and often insulted and humiliated him.

    One day, his classmates secretly stole vegetables from the owner's garden, and they were caught by the owner, who blamed Dong for the theft. Dong was not pleased, and on another occasion, he tried to steal vegetables as well, but he was caught by the owner. Dong felt deeply ashamed. The owner said, "No harm done. If you truly lacked food, I would have provided you with my meager fare. How could stealing vegetables be a big deal?"

    Dong misunderstood the owner's words and thought he was being mocked, so he returned home. However, the owner sent a servant to provide Dong with abundant food and drink, showing him great respect. Dong was surprised by this unexpected treatment and felt uneasy. His classmates were also puzzled and began to treat him with more kindness.

    The owner then approached the teacher and suggested that he marry his daughter to Dong. The teacher was surprised and asked, "Do you really want to give your daughter to someone like Dong?" The owner replied, "Master, you are mistaken. How could someone as great as Dong remain poor and lowly forever?"

    The teacher had always admired Dong and was pleased by the owner's words. He then privately asked the owner, "Dong is indeed worthy of marriage, but how do you know this?" The owner said, "I know because of the stolen vegetables. Last night, I happened to see two red lanterns shining in the vegetable garden. Upon closer inspection, I realized it was Dong, but the lanterns could not be seen. This must mean that he is destined for great things, as if he is being served by supernatural forces."

    The teacher found this very strange and agreed to arrange the marriage.

    250

    董后果居显秩，有闻于时。惜尝附秦桧主和议，为平生之玷云。明时犹传其画像，邹忠介见之，戏曰：“胡澹庵请斩桧，近等。著老夫在廷，恐公亦不免耳!”言毕，其画划然而裂，尤可异骇云。
    　　香囊妇
    　　袁州某生年十六，美丰姿。偶自塾归，过一人家，见绣罗香囊挂于两壁，兰麝袭人，心爱之。就门内呼问曰：“香囊市否?”有美妇出见之，笑曰：“妾家业此，云胡不市?”生问值几何，妇诡曰：“妾夫携此，日卖于乡里间，实未识其值。君欲之，第持以去，无须问价也。”解一以赠生，生受以归。盖妇实悦生，假此通意，而生不知也。后同学者见之，诂所自，生略不讳。咸以为诳，难之曰：“倘能更取数具分予诸人，则信；不然，且夺尔所佩。”胁而诺之。

    Explanation

    250

    Dong eventually attained a high rank and became renowned in his time. Unfortunately, he once aligned himself with Qin Hui, who advocated for peace with the Jin dynasty, which was a stain on his character. In the Ming dynasty, his portrait was still being passed down. When Zou Zhongjie saw it, he jokingly said, "Hu Dan'an requested the execution of Qin Hui, and you are similar. If only I were in court, I fear you would also be in trouble!" After he spoke, the painting suddenly cracked, which was particularly strange and alarming.

    The Perfumed Sachet Woman

    A youth from Yuzhou, sixteen years old and handsome in appearance, happened to return from school. Passing by a house, he saw perfumed sachets embroidered with silk hanging on both walls, their fragrance permeating the air, and he loved them. He called out from inside the gate and asked, "Are the perfumed sachets for sale?" A beautiful woman came out and saw him, smiling and saying, "My family deals in these, why wouldn't you buy them?" The youth asked how much they cost. The woman lied, "My husband carries these around to sell in the village, and I don't know their value. If you want one, just take it, there's no need to ask for the price." She gave him one as a gift, and he accepted it and returned home. It turned out that the woman was actually attracted to the youth, using this as a way to convey her feelings, though the youth was unaware. Later, his classmates saw the perfumed sachet and asked where it came from. The youth did not hide the truth. They all believed it was a lie and challenged him, saying, "If you can get more and share them with everyone, then we'll believe you; otherwise, we'll take the one you're wearing." They forced him to agree.

    251

    他日归，因重造妇室。妇见生，以为搴裳来就也，急引入内寝。具茗饮已，复设酒馔。生愕然致谢，谓主人款客何太厚，欲辞去。妇坚留之，而挑以微词，生亦不解。妇复整鬟易服，为华艳之妆。以手抓生面，而摩其痕曰：“郎君郁李粉桃，几为我弹破。”因取镜共照，以颊相偎，若两玉之相倚也。妇不自持，竟搂生就榻，笑而求欢。生大骇曰：“此何事，乌可为?”固求去，妇搂之愈力。时床头有剑，吷然作声， 自跃出匣三四寸。生挣脱，掣剑顾之，曰：“不畏剑锋耶?”妇笑曰：“尔忍杀我，即使杀，终不教尔去。”遂引颈向生，生竟挥之，奔而出。

    Explanation

    251

    On another day, when he returned, he rebuilt the wife's room. The wife saw the guest and thought he was coming to her, so she quickly led him into the inner chamber. After preparing tea and drinks, she then set up wine and food. The guest was startled and thanked the host, saying that the hospitality was too much, and wanted to leave. The wife strongly kept him and flirted with subtle remarks, but the guest did not understand. The wife then neatened her hair and changed her clothes, putting on elegant and beautiful makeup. She grabbed the face of the guest and rubbed the mark, saying, "Young master, your face is as red as pomegranate and peach blossoms. I almost broke it with my laughter." She then took a mirror and they looked at each other, their cheeks leaning against each other like two pieces of jade. The wife could not control herself and embraced the guest onto the bed, laughing and seeking pleasure. The guest was greatly startled and said, "What is this, how can it be done?" He insisted on leaving, but the wife held him more tightly. At that moment, there was a sword at the head of the bed, which made a sound and leaped out of its sheath by several inches. The guest struggled to free himself, drew the sword, and looked at her, saying, "Are you not afraid of the sword's edge?" The wife laughed and said, "Are you willing to kill me? Even if you kill me, I will never let you go." She then stretched her neck toward the guest, and the guest finally struck her, and she fled out.

    252

    洎暮夫归，见而骇恸。有邻父者，妇之假父也，尝出入其家。大疑之，执而讼之官，迫于刑，遂诬服。狱具矣，生闻之，急诣县自陈，具述本末，愿为死囚偿妇命。夫闻之，慨然曰：“有妇若此，宜死久矣。感君盛德，为我杀之，不然，其辱我滋甚。今何敢复相累乎?愿息讼以归。”县宰义之，允其请，而加礼于生。
    　　其年学使按郡，生进入泮。
    　　非非子曰：生真伟丈夫乎哉!其事，鲁男子之所不及，而柳季之所难也。然既已受美人之贻，入之子之室，与偷香钻穴者何以异?则慎始之道已失。及其抱璧将投、按剑相盼，亦何难翻然高逝、与彼长辞?完其节而复全其躯，斯盛德之至也。而必加刃焉，君子以为忍矣。论法；杀人者死。即原情末减，亦自有应得之罪，其得脱然无累也，幸矣!至观其毅然自投，无少违避，则不谓之伟丈夫不可也。

    Explanation

    252

    By evening, the husband returned home and saw the scene, greatly shocked and wept. There was a neighbor who was the woman's stepfather, and he often came and went from their house. The husband greatly suspected him, seized him, and took him to court. Under pressure from torture, the neighbor confessed falsely. The case was settled, and the man heard about it and hurriedly went to the county to explain himself. He fully recounted the beginning and end, and was willing to take the place of the dead prisoner to save his wife's life. The husband heard this and sighed, saying, "To have a wife like this, he should have died long ago. Moved by your great virtue, kill him for me. Otherwise, the shame I would suffer would be even greater. Now how dare I burden you again? I wish to end the lawsuit and return home." The county magistrate found him righteous and granted his request, treating him with respect.

    That year, the provincial examiner visited the prefecture, and the man entered the school. The Non-Non Son said: "Is this man truly a great man? His actions surpass those of the man from Lu and are difficult for Liu Ji to match. However, since he has already received the gift from the beautiful woman and entered her house, what is different from those who secretly steal fragrances and burrow through walls? Then the principle of caution at the beginning has been lost. When he held the jade and was about to throw it, and pressed his sword and looked at each other, why couldn't he suddenly fly away and bid farewell to them forever? Preserve his integrity and complete his body, this is the highest form of great virtue. But he insisted on being killed, and the gentlemen considered it cruel. According to the law, the killer must die. Even if mitigated by circumstances, there is still a deserved punishment. That he could escape without burden is fortunate. To see him resolutely throw himself into it, without any avoidance, then it cannot be denied that he is a great man."

    253

    青 青
    　　昔有相士，僦居京师虎坊桥，术颇验。往来士大夫家，往往礼为上客。车马辐辏，殆无虚日。
    　　一日，有乘白骡造其庐以求相者，衣冠都雅，从者数人。相士相之曰：“君神姿爽异，气色方新，必贵人也。”其人唯唯。
    　　复命相其从者，则或言当得财，当得寿，当有小疾厄，小伤损，不一其词。从者亦唯唯。其人复间曰：“吾寿几何？”相士又周视，久之，谓当得八十九岁，位至卿贰。其人曰：“恐不能也。”相士曰：“吾寄迹京华四十馀年，阅人多矣，所决穷通寿夭，鲜有不验，何独爽于君?”于是历数其生平应验之多，侈陈其交游声势之盛，意气扬扬，词色轩舞。其人曰：“先生神鉴，仆所素倾。仆有友人，尤为仰慕，惜今不能来。屈尊趾同过，可乎?”相士欣然命驾，连骑而行。

    Explanation

    253

    Green and Blue

    Once upon a time, there was a fortune-teller who rented a residence in Tiger Bridge, the capital city. His art was quite effective, and he was often treated as a top guest in the homes of scholars and officials. Carriages and horses converged like spokes of a wheel, and he was rarely without visitors.

    One day, a man arrived riding a white donkey, dressed in elegant attire and accompanied by several people. He came to the fortune-teller's residence to seek a reading. The fortune-teller examined him and said, "Your divine presence is striking and unusual, your complexion is fresh, and you are surely a person of high status." The man replied respectfully.

    The fortune-teller then examined the man's entourage and predicted that one would gain wealth, another would live a long life, and others would face minor illnesses or injuries. The predictions varied. The entourage also replied respectfully. The man then asked, "How long will my life be?" The fortune-teller looked around carefully for a long time and said that he would live to be eighty-nine years old and reach the rank of a vice-minister. The man said, "I doubt that will be the case." The fortune-teller responded, "I have lived in the capital for over forty years and have seen many people. My predictions about wealth, poverty, and lifespan are rarely wrong. Why should they fail for you?" He then recounted many instances of his accurate predictions and boasted about the influence and reputation of his social circle, speaking with high spirits and a dignified demeanor.

    The man said, "Master, your insight is truly remarkable; I have always admired you. I have a friend who is especially admired, but he is unable to come today. May I humbly request your presence? Would it be possible for you to visit him?" The fortune-teller happily agreed to go, and they rode together.

    254

    至宣武门内，一宅颇幽曲。既通刺，便有一人便衣出迎，各道相见之意。相士先以言聒之，知其好谀，乃虚词称美。其人大喜，出酒肴款之。席间因言有舍亲，居市西门外，愿同往一相。相士诺之。乃以后儅车载相士，而自策马与乘骤者从。
    　　既至， 日已昏黄。灯烛辉煌，出于华屋之内。有老人灯下相见，鬚髪如银，神韵孤迥。相土捧手迎谓曰：“何得此千岁鹤也?”老人大笑。坐谈有顷，相士请郎君相见。老人频顣曰：“老夫衰迈，尚寡嗣息。小妾方有身，愿先生相之，辨其男女。”遂引入内室，一少妇含羞而出。相士既相，揖老人曰：“夫人久当产麟。当由尊相某处末佳，故应少晚。今细观贵宠，必是弄璋之喜。”老人颜色顿悦。

    Explanation

    254

    Arriving at the inner gate of Xuanwu, they came to a residence that was quite secluded and winding. After presenting the business card, a person in casual attire came out to greet, and each expressed the intention of meeting. The fortune-teller first spoke to him in a loud, persistent manner, knowing that he enjoyed flattery, and then used empty words to praise. The man was very pleased and brought out wine and dishes to entertain. During the meal, he mentioned having a relative living outside the western gate of the city, and he wished to go together for a fortune-telling session. The fortune-teller agreed. He then had a sedan chair prepared to carry the fortune-teller, while he himself rode a horse and was followed by a rider.

    After arriving, it was already dusk. The lamps and candles were shining brightly, and they were inside a magnificent house. An old man met him under the light of the lamp, with a beard and hair as white as silver, and a spirit and demeanor that were solitary and distinct. The fortune-teller bowed his hands to greet and said, "How did you get this thousand-year-old crane?" The old man laughed loudly. They talked for a while, and the fortune-teller asked to see the young man. The old man repeatedly said, "I am old and feeble, and I still have few descendants. My concubine is pregnant. I wish for you to tell me about it, to distinguish whether it will be a boy or a girl." He then led them into the inner room, and a young woman came out shyly. After the fortune-teller had looked at her, he bowed to the old man and said, "Madam will soon give birth to a dragon. It is because your face in a certain part is not good, therefore, it should be a bit late. Now, after carefully observing your concubine, it must be the joy of a son." The old man's face brightened immediately.

    255

    忽闻屏后有人吱吱而笑，意似哂其妄者。老人呼之曰：“青青儿，汝可出，令先生相尔好否。”一妇人迟回而出，年三十许，颜色颇丽，双颊羞红，笑容犹未尽敛也。相士复揖老人曰：“此位娘子，目下亦有玉燕投怀之兆。”言甫毕，妇人大怒而唾，就屏后索梃逐之。老人亦神色顿异，急挽相士出，谓之曰：“此是老夫寡女，性最贞烈，先生奈何失言？”同来二人亦谓：“先生信口而谈，此事全无影响，诬青娘甚矣!”相士大惭，赤颈汗面，无言可答。老人又曰：“虽然，劳先生远行，岂可空返?当奉薄谢。”即入内取金钱赠之，二人亦各有所与，相士皆辞而后受。共议留宿书斋，忽闻青青率数婢持梃而出，大骂曰：“贼奴犹敢逗留受赏耶?须令吃吾棒!”相士奔逃，女追之半里，及城乃返。

    Explanation

    255

    Suddenly, a soft and continuous laughter was heard from behind the screen, as if mocking his absurdity. The old man called out, "Qingqing, you may come out. Let the master look at you and see if he has good fortune." A woman hesitated before emerging, around thirty years old, with a beautiful appearance and rosy cheeks. Her smile had not yet fully faded. The fortune-teller bowed to the old man and said, "This lady here also has the omen of a jade swallow in her bosom." As soon as he finished speaking, the woman became furious and spat, then went behind the screen to fetch a stick to chase him away. The old man's expression suddenly changed, and he quickly pulled the fortune-teller out, saying, "This is my widowed daughter, who is the most chaste and fierce. How could you have made such a mistake, Master?" The two men who had come with him also said, "Master, you spoke without thinking, and this matter has no basis at all. You have greatly slandered Qingniang!" The fortune-teller was greatly ashamed, with a red neck and sweating face, unable to respond. The old man then said, "Even so, it would be a waste to have you travel all this way and return empty-handed. I should give you a small token of appreciation." He immediately went inside to fetch some money and gave it to him, and the two men also each gave something, which the fortune-teller accepted after declining. They then discussed staying overnight in the study. Suddenly, Qingqing led several maids with sticks and came out, scolding loudly, "You scoundrel still dare to linger and accept rewards? I will make you eat my stick!" The fortune-teller fled, and the woman chased him for half a mile before returning to the city.

    256

    时城门已合，坐以待旦。天明，视金钱，皆纸耳。往迹所居，杳非人境。知逢鬼物，怖骇而归。事闻于街衢，后遂无问津者。
    　　奎章道士
    　　乡人之始死者，其家必召道士竖符。有奎章道士以术鸣于乡，凡建醮设法，道士无不与者。
    　　一日，于人家竖符归，夜已初更，星光昏黑。迤逦行田野中西风嗖嗖扑面。忽有一物迎至，面阔一尺，长仅数寸，色黝黑，似笑似哭，两耳大如葵扇。与道士交臂过，两肩相触，冷气射人入肌骨。道士立而凝视，物亦屹立相向。道士初不甚辩，久之乃审见其状，始知非人；惊怖失措，亟以米囊提之，物亦欻灭。

    Explanation

    256

    The city gates were already closed, and he sat waiting for dawn. At daybreak, he looked at the money, which had all turned into paper. He followed the trail to where he had been, finding it a desolate place far removed from the human world. Realizing he had encountered a ghost, he returned terrified. The story spread through the streets, and afterwards, no one came to inquire about it.

    Kui Zhang Daoist

    When a villager first dies, their family must call a Daoist to erect talismans. There was a Daoist named Kui Zhang, renowned for his magical skills in the village. Whenever rituals and spells were performed, he was always involved.

    One day, after erecting talismans at a household, he returned home as the first watch of the night had begun, the stars dim and the sky dark. As he walked through the fields, a cold wind blew against his face. Suddenly, an object approached him, a face a foot wide and only a few inches long, dark black in color, looking as if it were laughing and crying, with ears as large as sunflower fans. It passed by the Daoist, their shoulders touching, and a cold air penetrated his bones. The Daoist stood and stared, and the object also stood facing him. At first, the Daoist did not recognize it clearly, but after a while, he realized its form and understood it was not human. Terrified and at a loss, he quickly picked it up with a rice bag, and the object also suddenly disappeared.

    257

    李氏妇
    　　余客郡城时，谈席间，闻客述一事甚怪：
    　　有张姓娶李姓妇，道稍远，亲迎之日，中途而新妇不见。香车绣箔，女家封键依然也。于是张疑李氏未尝以女来，而媒者及舆夫固见其以女来也。李闻之，亦疑张杀其女，故责我不以女至，而媒者及舆夫诚未见其女至也。两不能释，以闻于宰。宰不能明，于是遂疑媒者及舆夫，或利其奁，或淫其色，中道共图之。而媒者则两家之亲旧，乡里之所谓端人也。而舆夫则未尝逃一人，妆奁则未尝亡一物也。以闻于守，守不能讞。以闻于诸司两台，诸司两台皆不能决。
    　　延滞三年，遂成艇狱。两姓之民，咸愿息焉。忽空中掷下一人正新妇也，枯槁死矣。其事遂白。

    Explanation

    257

    The Wife of the Li Family

    When I was staying in the county town, during a conversation, I heard a guest recount a very strange matter:

    There was a man surnamed Zhang who married a woman from the Li family. The journey to the Li family's home was somewhat distant, and on the day of the wedding procession, the bride was not seen halfway. The fragrant carriage with embroidered curtains remained sealed and locked as it had been at the Li family's home. Zhang suspected that the Li family had not sent the bride, while the matchmaker and the bearers had indeed seen the bride being brought along. When the Li family heard this, they also suspected that Zhang had killed their daughter, accusing him of not bringing her. However, the matchmaker and the bearers had truly seen the bride being brought along. Unable to resolve the matter, they reported it to the county magistrate. The magistrate could not clarify the situation, so he then suspected the matchmaker and the bearers, thinking they might have been motivated by the dowry or indulged in the bride's beauty, and had conspired against her along the way. But the matchmaker was a close relative of both families and was considered an upright person in the village. The bearers had not escaped, and nothing from the dowry was missing. The matter was then reported to the governor, but the governor could not resolve it. It was also reported to various government departments and the imperial court, but none of them could make a decision.

    The case dragged on for three years, becoming a floating prison. The people of both families all wished to end the conflict. Suddenly, a person fell from the sky, who was the bride, but she was dead and emaciated. The matter was finally resolved.

    258

    噫！此何怪欤?
    　　胡夫人墓
    　　分宜某塾师，聚徒山中。门人某生，聪颖而好学，师绝爱之。而见其体渐赢瘦，神色尫然，谓攻苦所致，戒以少辍，然殊不闻诵读之声，窃疑之。夜就其窗外伺之，则闻有人喁喁私语，音似妇人，而听不能彻。意其私人闺阁也，甚怒。
    　　明日，使他徒诘之，生不能讳，遂告之曰：“向暮行山下，逢一女子含笑而来，姿容旷世，款语通情，遂订私约。夜分乃来，入自侧门，会于灯下。比寝，则幽香软玉，宛转衾席间，复以径寸明珠置我口中，戒勿吞咽，将晓则仍取之去。盖已如是两月矣。叩其居址姓氏，乃山下前村之女。如此佳人，如此密约，吾非石人。诚不能遣，直愿为斯人死耳！”
    　　徒以告师，师益疑之，度人家闺女。恶能晓夜独行，踪迹不露如此?果若所云，其殆妖魅也。且明珠必取精之具，若再来，当吞之，以观其变。乃召生而教之。

    Explanation

    258

    Alas! What is this to be wondered at?
    The Tomb of Mrs. Hu
    In a certain place in Fenyi, there was a private tutor who gathered students in the mountains. One of his students, a certain young man, was intelligent and studious, and the teacher loved him greatly. However, he noticed that the young man's body was gradually becoming emaciated, and his complexion was wan. He believed it was due to the hard work of studying and advised him to take a break, but he did not hear any sound of recitation or reading, which made him suspicious. At night, he approached the window to spy on him and heard someone whispering softly, the voice sounding like a woman's, but he could not understand what they were saying. He suspected that the young man was having an affair with a woman from her inner chamber, and he became very angry.
    The next day, he sent another student to interrogate him. The young man could not hide the truth and told him, "In the evening, I was walking down the mountain and met a woman who came smiling. Her beauty was unparalleled, and we spoke intimately, so we made a secret agreement. She came at midnight, entered through the side door, and met me under the lamp. When we lay down, she was fragrant and soft like jade, moving gracefully between the sheets, and placed a pearl about the size of an inch in my mouth, advising me not to swallow it, and took it away when dawn came. This has been going on for two months now. I asked her about her address and surname, and she said she was a woman from a village near the foot of the mountain. Such a beautiful woman, such a secret agreement, I am not a stone person. I truly cannot bear it, and I would rather die for this person!"
    The student told the teacher, who became even more suspicious. He thought that a woman from a household could not walk alone at night, and her movements were not revealed like this. If it is as he said, it is probably a demon or ghost. Moreover, the pearl is likely a symbol of fertility. If she comes again, you should swallow it, to see what happens. He then summoned the young man and gave him instructions.

    259

    是夜，女子复来，纳珠如故。及其将取，则吞之。女子跌足面泣曰：“垂成之功，堕于一旦矣!”生歉然不安，叩其故。女子曰：“此珠已历五百年。死于此珠苕，凡九十九人，皆聪明、富贵，寿考主人，其精气尽在于是。若经百人，则成正果，不谓乃败于君也！邪道求仙，终归无益，安敢怨君？君后日福祚且不可量，幸念枕席之情，明日求吾尸于东山下，棺椁而葬之，得比于君之姬妾，岁时以杯酒浇冢上，不使游魂弱魄寂寞泉台，君主惠也，死亦无憾!”遂相持恸哭而去。同室之人莫不闻之，惟之见形耳。
    　　次日至东山之下，见一大狐死焉。生伏而哭之，甚哀。殡葬如礼，为文而祭之。夜乃梦女子来谢。
    　　生自吞珠之后，精神智慧尽倍于前。居显秩，登上寿，皆珠所益云。乃题狐墓曰：“胡夫人之墓”。

    Explanation

    259

    On that night, the woman returned, presenting the pearl as usual. When he was about to take it, she swallowed it. The woman stamped her foot and wept, saying, “A nearly completed achievement, ruined in a single day!” The man felt uneasy and asked her why. She replied, “This pearl has existed for five hundred years. Ninety-nine people have died by it, all of them intelligent, wealthy, and long-lived masters, whose essence and vitality are all contained within it. If it passes through a hundred people, it will achieve its true form, but it was ruined by you! Seeking immortality through evil means is ultimately of no benefit; how could I blame you? Your blessings and longevity are immeasurable; please remember the affection we shared in bed. Tomorrow, seek my body at the foot of East Mountain, bury me in a coffin, treat me as you would your concubines, pour a cup of wine on my grave every year, so that my spirit and soul are not left lonely in the netherworld. It is your kindness, and even in death, I have no regrets!” They held each other and cried bitterly before parting. Everyone in the room heard her, but only her form was visible.

    The next day, he went to the foot of East Mountain and found a large fox dead there. He knelt down and wept for it, deeply moved. He buried it according to proper rites, wrote an inscription to commemorate it, and that night he dreamed of the woman coming to thank him.

    After swallowing the pearl, the man's spiritual wisdom and intelligence were greatly enhanced. He held a high-ranking official position and attained great longevity, all due to the pearl. He then inscribed the fox's grave, naming it “The Tomb of Lady Hu.”

    260

    古 瓶
    　　金溪邮路亭胡姓，有甲乙二人。入山游猎，见一白兔自草间逸出，急引弓追而射之。兔忽不见，相与惶惑。甲谓乙曰：“兔也而白，必义也。”盖里巷以得窖镪为义，谓其利以义取也，故谓之义。亦间闻有见白物而得白金者，以其色同而幻化也，故甲意及此。乙亦以为然，谨志其处。
    　　伺人静，往发之，则古冢也。椁槥无存，唯断砖残碣可验。旁得一大缸，中贮古瓶二、古砚一。二人本图大获，见此爽然。甲恚甚，举畚碎其一瓶，乙曰：“止！取此聊为养花器，不庸愈于空返乎?”因提一瓶及砚以归。
    　　砚乃泥砚，甚平平。瓶置几上数日，觉有气自内浮出，氤氲若云气之蒸，不测其故。试折花木贮其中，无水而花木不萎，且抽芽结实，着附土盘根者然。始讶瓶盖宝物也。
    　　一日，风雨大作，雷轰电闪，震耀室中。忽霹雳一声起于柱侧，破屋穿瓦而去。举室皆惊，惊定视瓶，已为雷裂碎矣。

    260

    The Ancient Vase

    In Jinxi, at the post station, there were two men surnamed Hu. They went into the mountains to hunt and saw a white rabbit escape from the grass. They quickly drew their bows and shot it. The rabbit suddenly disappeared, and both were in a state of confusion. Man A said to Man B, "The rabbit is white, so it must be a sign of good fortune." In the neighborhood, finding buried treasure is considered good fortune, because it is believed to be obtained through good fortune, so it is called good fortune. They also heard of people who saw white objects and found white gold, because the colors are the same and it is a transformation, so Man A thought of this. Man B also agreed, and they carefully noted the location.

    They waited until people were quiet, then went to investigate, and found an ancient tomb. The coffin and inner coffin were no longer there, only broken bricks and remnants of stone tablets could be used to verify. Nearby, they found a large jar containing two ancient vases and one ancient inkstone. They originally intended to find great wealth, but were disappointed when they saw this. Man A was very angry and lifted the basket to break one of the vases. Man B said, "Stop! Take this and use it as a vase for flowers; isn't that better than returning empty-handed?" So he took one vase and the inkstone and returned home.

    The inkstone was a clay inkstone, very ordinary. They placed the vase on the table for several days and felt a vapor rising from inside, like the rising steam of clouds, but they could not understand the reason. They tried to place cut flowers and plants inside, and without water, the flowers and plants did not wilt, and they even sprouted leaves and bore fruit, as if rooted in the soil. They began to suspect that the vase contained a treasure.

    One day, a violent storm with thunder and lightning struck the room. A sudden clap of thunder sounded near the pillar, breaking through the roof and tiles and flying away. Everyone in the house was startled. After calming down, they looked at the vase and saw that it had been shattered by the lightning.

    261

    非非子曰：瓶出冢中，明器耳，何宝之足云?然而云气生焉，植物花实焉，不可谓非宝也。宝矣，而雷殛之者何也?岂其陆离炫耀，竟干造椅之忌欤?抑有妖物凭之，而受池鱼之殃欤?殆非也。盖既为宝物，则隐见之间，倍宜珍重。当世无博物君子，抱瓮全真可也。藉朴渥以为先容，引猎徒以为知己，宝之自待不已亵乎?
    　　猎者不知而碎其一，宜也，猎者无罪也。即猎者不知而收其一，幸也，猎者无奇也。且一瓶既碎，则一瓶岂忍复完?兔死狐悲，芝焚蕙叹，凡物且然，而况宝乎?雷之击之，殆瓶之自悔其误、自伤其孤，而假手于丰隆以为蜕化者也。则瓶虽不慎始，犹善其终也。嗟乎！张雷逝而剑化矣，隋和死而珠沉矣。瓶即邀大雅之鉴，登博古之堂，而策后人之不能伤斯人之已往，终亦人琴俱亡，殉知己于地下。安能转移市侩之手，徒消受三斗尘战！

    Explanation

    261

    Non-Non Zi said: A bottle emerged from a tomb, a ritual object, what is there to be considered a treasure? Yet vapor arises from it, plants, flowers, and fruits grow from it; it cannot be called not a treasure. It is indeed a treasure, but why was it struck by lightning? Was it because its dazzling appearance offended the gods? Or was it because an evil spirit possessed it, leading to its destruction? Probably not. Since it is a treasure, it should be especially cherished during its appearances and disappearances. If there are no learned gentlemen in the world, one can remain pure by carrying a jar. Relying on simplicity and sincerity to gain initial favor, and attracting hunters as friends, treating it as a treasure does not make it too precious?

    The hunter unknowingly broke one of them, which is appropriate, the hunter is innocent. If the hunter unknowingly collected one of them, it is fortunate, the hunter is not extraordinary. Since one bottle is already broken, how could one bear to keep the other intact? When one dies, the other mourns; when one is burned, the other laments. All things are like this, let alone treasures. The lightning striking it was probably the bottle's own regret for its mistake, its self-harm due to its loneliness, and its transformation through the agency of the thunder god. So although the bottle was not careful at the beginning, it still had a good end. Alas! When Zhang Lei passed away, his sword transformed; when Sui He died, his pearls sank. If the bottle were to invite the judgment of the refined, it would enter the hall of ancient knowledge, but to prevent future generations from harming those who have passed. In the end, both the person and the instrument would perish, sacrificing themselves to the knowledge of their friends in the afterlife. How can it be transferred to the hands of a market vendor, only to suffer three dou of dust and strife?

    262

    或者且为猎者惜，以为非常之物既得而复失之也。夫谀墓得金，昔人犹讥，矧于启其墓而取其物哉?吁！掘地得金，何以便谓之义?使果无心掘之，无心得之，犹可言也。今则为义而因以掘地，掘地而因以掘墓，不义孰甚焉?利由义生也，既不义矣，又何利焉?然则雷之击瓶，固不仅为瓶计也。
    　　耳食录卷十二
    　　婉 姑
    　　龚生者，浮薄于也。年二十馀，读书开元寺。先是，某典史一女死，殡寺中，与龚隔院，有二门通焉。女名婉姑，有殊色，能诗。年十六，未嫁，以情死。龚闻而慕之，凭其棺，戏谓曰：“生为有情人，死亦有情鬼。柳生丽娘之事，宁不可嗣徽音乎?”寺僧笑之，而龚不顾也。他日又戏之曰：“卿青春佳丽，寂处泉台，宁可无郎?又宁不念鳏鱼永夜乎?”
    　　是夜挽抽空阶，月华浸影。微闻隔院娇歌，声如莺燕，深怪僧徒那得容此摩登女?倾听久之，非歌也，乃吟诗耳。时微风贯耳，字字清越可辨。诗曰：

    Explanation

    262

    Perhaps one might pity the hunter, thinking that an extraordinary thing was obtained and then lost again. Flattering the tomb to gain wealth, even ancient people still criticized it; how much more so to open the tomb and take its contents? Alas! Digging up gold from the ground, how can that be called righteous? If it were truly done without intention, and obtained without intention, it could still be justified. Now, in the name of righteousness, digging up the ground and opening the tomb, which injustice is greater? Profit arises from righteousness; since it is not righteous, what profit is there? Then, the lightning striking the bottle is not just for the sake of the bottle.

    Ear Feeding Records, Volume Twelve

    Wan Gu

    Gong Sheng, a shallow and frivolous person, was in his twenties, reading at the Kaiyuan Temple. Previously, a daughter of a certain magistrate died, buried in the temple, and Gong was in a separate courtyard connected by two doors. The girl was named Wan Gu, with exceptional beauty, and could compose poetry. At sixteen, unmarried, she died of emotional distress. Gong heard of her and admired her, leaning on her coffin, and playfully said, "Living as a lover, dead as a ghost of affection. The story of Liu Sheng and Li Niang, how can it not be a continuation of such a narrative?" The temple monk laughed at him, but Gong did not care. On another day, he playfully said again, "You, in your youthful beauty, in the quiet of the underworld, how can you not have a lover? How can you not think of the lonely fish in the eternal night?"

    That night, the wind blew through the empty courtyard, and the moonlight bathed the shadow. A faint sound of a sweet song from the other courtyard, like the chirping of orioles and swallows, deeply wondering how the monks could allow such a modern girl. Listening for a long time, it was not a song, but a recitation of poetry. The slight wind carried the words, each one clear and distinct. The poem reads:

    263

    “棠梨花老杜鹃残，玉磐凄凉翠袖单。
    　　不耐潇潇连夜雨，断肠明月又添寒。”
    　　龚愀然曰：“噫！安得此凄恻之音也?”又闻吟诗曰：
    　　“紫玉多情忽化烟，曲中谁唱《想夫怜》?
    　　镜台长挂葳蕤锁，小小眉弯画不全。”
    　　龚太息曰：“词愈好而心愈悲，何处佳人，愁怨乃尔?”
    　　忽阴气砭肌，毛髪皆立，见一女郎由墙角旖旎而前，画颊仙庞，亭亭玉立，笑谓龚曰：“屡蒙相忆。今来矣!”龚失惊，猛悟阶下香魂即棺中玉骨也。急唾而奔，女亦踵逐不舍。龚大呼，寺僧尽起。烛之，见龚仆地上，神已痴矣，口中呼“婉姑”不止。僧知为女鬼所魅，急告其家，载之归。痴情魔语，逐日而增。其家恐甚，召道士作符呪，不治；召医师进汤药，不治。龚气息奄奄，犹言，“我与婉姑百年情好，义不独生。但求为我作鸳鸯冢可矣。”
    　　其友人白云生，风雅之士，善属文。闻其故，乃作书焚于婉姑之柩，龚数日顿愈。书曰：

    Explanation

    263

    "Plum blossoms wither, and cuckoos grow weary; the jade zither sounds mournful, and the green sleeve stands alone.
    Unable to bear the continuous rain all night, the bright moon adds to the sorrow of a broken heart."
    Gong sighed, saying, "Ah! How can I find such a sorrowful sound?" Then he heard someone reciting poetry:
    "The purple jade, full of emotion, suddenly turns to smoke; who sings 'Thinking of My Husband with Sympathy' in the melody?
    The mirror stand hangs with a lush lock, and the small curved eyebrows cannot be fully drawn."
    Gong sighed deeply, saying, "The words are more beautiful, but my heart is more sorrowful. Where is this fair maiden, whose grief is so profound?"
    Suddenly, the cold air pierced his skin, and his hair stood on end. He saw a young lady gracefully approaching from the corner of the wall, with a painted face and a celestial beauty, standing tall and graceful. She smiled and said to Gong, "I have often been remembered by you. Now I have come!" Gong was startled and suddenly realized that the fragrant spirit on the stairs was the jade bones in the coffin. He spat in disgust and ran away, but the woman followed him relentlessly. Gong shouted, and the temple monks all woke up. When they lit the candles, they saw Gong lying on the ground, his mind gone mad, calling out "Wan Gu" without stopping. The monks knew he had been bewitched by a female ghost and quickly informed his family, who took him home. His delirious and magical words grew worse each day. His family became very frightened and summoned a Taoist priest to perform spells, but it did not help. They also called in a physician to give him medicine, but it was ineffective. Gong lay there, barely breathing, and still said, "I have a hundred-year affection with Wan Gu. It is not right to live alone. I only ask for you to build a double grave for me."
    His friend Bai Yunsheng, a refined and cultured man skilled in writing, heard of the incident and wrote a letter, burning it at the coffin of Wan Gu. Gong recovered suddenly after several days. The letter read:

    264

    盖闻阴阳事重，姻缘簿必订三生：伉俪情深，温柔乡何难一死?誓坚皦日，室虽异而穴必同，心托清尘，形已销而诚不泯。然皆同牢合卺，共枕连衾，结大义于山河，写素心于琴瑟。一朝离别，孤镜里之青鸾，中道解携，落钗头之白燕。是以神伤旧梦，甘殉倾城；意绝新欢，愿图合冢。疑冤禽其解语，比翼千秋；借拱木以还魂，相思百尺。
    　　其或已偕风卜，末驾鱼轩，花含蕊以先凋，玉无瑕而遂葬。想云雨之末试，欲遂幽欢，伤蒲柳之无依，爰求故偶。红丝犹在，重牵己断之魂；黄土难乾，长作同眠之梦。斯皆义在倡随，是以情无生死也。
    　　又或曾谋数面，久许同心，倩侍女以代冰人，托短笺而申索约。誓鸳鸯之不独宿，愿蝴蝶之必双飞。未卜他生，倏成隔世。望佳人兮不见，泪溅梅花，思公子兮无言，歌残桃叶。此黄衫所以挺身于生前，紫玉所以延颈于没后者也。

    264

    It is said that matters concerning yin and yang are significant, and fated connections are destined for three lifetimes: deep affection between husband and wife, how difficult is it to give up one's life for the comfort of a blissful state? Vows as firm as the sun, though the rooms may be different, the burrows must be the same; heart entrusted to the pure dust, though the form may have perished, the sincerity remains. Yet, they share the same dish and cup, sleep together under the same quilt, bind great righteousness to the mountains and rivers, write pure hearts on the zither and lute. One day, separated, the solitary figure reflected in the lonely mirror, halfway through the journey, the white swallow falls from the hairpin. Thus, the spirit grieves for old dreams, willingly sacrifices for the sake of the beautiful; cuts off new joys, wishes to be buried together. Doubting that the grieving bird can speak, the mandarin ducks fly together for a thousand autumns; borrowing a beam to return to life, longing reaches a hundred feet.
    　　Perhaps they have already consulted the wind, not yet driven the fish carriage, the flower with its bud has already withered, the flawless jade has been buried. Thinking of the untried clouds and rain, wishing to fulfill the hidden joy, grieving for the willow and elm without support, seeking the former partner. The red thread still exists, rekindling the broken spirit; the yellow earth is hard to dry, forever dreaming of sleeping together. All these are about righteousness in following and leading, thus there is no distinction between life and death in emotion.
    　　Or perhaps they have met several times, long promised to be united in heart, asking a maid to act as a matchmaker, entrusting a short note to express the desire for a pact. Vowing that the mandarin ducks will not sleep alone, wishing that the butterflies will always fly together. Not knowing about future lives, suddenly becoming separated by a lifetime. Looking for the beautiful lady but not seeing her, tears spilling onto plum blossoms, thinking of the young man but remaining silent, the song of the peach leaf has ended. This is why the yellow robe stands up for itself in life, the purple jade stretches its neck in the afterlife.

    265

    乃婉姑以碧玉破瓜之年，抱绿珠捐粉之恨。人皆惜玉，畴弗伤心?我不偷香，亦为茹叹！然而绛雪无丹，莫驻蛾眉之寿，彩云竟散，仍还鹤驭之班。既已归清净真缘，当勿念风流旧债。况乎身犹柳嫩，曾无忤臼婚姻；期未桃夭，宁识藁砧滋味?而于龚生，又陌路之不如，云霄之迥隔者矣。良缘悭梧叶，沟无顾况新诗；扑面隔桃花，洞绝刘晨旧路。玉钗尘葬，岂挂臣冠；宝椁云封，谁感子蜕?未闻温郎玉镜，徒聘丽影于泉台，石氏珍珠，犹买娇鬟于地府。纵情根已断而未断，不甘荳蔻胎含，然色界本空而又空，谁为牡丹花死?且贞魂稍荡于地下，则秽史遂流于人间，白骨其犹包羞，青山何能止谤?女也不爽，应教清白长存，魂兮归来，那使门庭受玷?姑诚念此，庸独安乎?
    　　呜呼!金钿盒空，金碗非定情之物；玉钩斜冷，玉箫乏再世之缘。崔罗什事本无稽，杜丽娘尤安可效?吾故特为友诉，不避瓜李生嫌；卿宜亟放郎还，毋谓丝萝可托。

    Explanation

    265

    In her sixteenth year, like the green jade breaking the melon, she bore the sorrow of Green Pearl's sacrifice. Everyone grieves for the jade, who would not be saddened? I do not steal fragrance, but I still sigh for her. However, the red snow lacks the red pigment, unable to preserve the longevity of the eyebrows. The colorful clouds finally disperse, returning to the ranks of the immortal's chariot. Since she has returned to the pure and true destiny, one should not remember the old debts of romance. Moreover, her body is still tender like willow, and she has never been involved in a marriage that went against her will. She is not yet at the age of peach blossom, how could she know the taste of the chopping block? As for Gong Sheng, she is even less than a stranger, and far removed like the sky and earth. Good fortune is scarce like the leaves of the phoenix tree, and there is no new poem in the ditch. The face is separated by peach blossoms, and the cave has cut off the old path of Liu Chen. The jade hairpin is buried in dust, how can it hang on the official's hat. The treasure coffin is sealed by clouds, who would feel the transformation of the butterfly. I have not heard of Wen Lang's jade mirror, only to hire the beautiful shadow in the underworld. Shi's pearls, still buy the beautiful hairpin in the underworld. Even if the root of passion is broken but not completely, she is unwilling to contain the bean sprout. However, the realm of color is empty and void, who would die for the peony flower. Moreover, if the chaste soul is slightly disturbed in the underworld, then the filthy history will flow into the world. The white bones still bear shame, how can the green mountains stop the slander? The woman is not at fault, she should be taught to maintain her purity. Soul, come back, how can the door be defiled? If you truly consider this, how can you remain alone and at peace?

    Alas! The golden box is empty, the golden bowl is not a token of love. The jade hook is cold, the jade flute lacks the chance for a second life. The story of Cui Luo Shi is baseless, how can Du Liniang be imitated? I specially plead for my friend, not avoiding the suspicion of being involved in the affair of melon and plum. You should quickly let the young man return, do not say that the silk and lichen can be relied upon.

    266

    从此瑶台浣露，世间知有许飞琼；蓉苑看花，灭上岂无丁文雅！
    　　王侍御
    　　饶州景德镇，江右一大都会也。商贾辐辏，士大夫亦往往税驾焉。
    　　同里王石林侍御为孝廉时，尝客其地，旅于抚州公寓之西偏。其正室素传不靖。先有同郡某君者居之，魑魅昼见，童仆夜惊，大为所嬲，畏而他徙。某既去，侍御命下榻其中。众咸以为言，侍御夷然。久之，略无所异，遂为吉宅。
    　　又，侍御所居地向有所谓三圣公王者，素能祸人。众为小石龛栖其主，岁时奉牲盛惟谨。或犯其龛旁树石，及语言不庄者，辄被射立死，轻亦疾废，如是不一人。侍御令子典华孝廉昆仲时尚少，闻而恶之。诣龛前数其罪，取其主劈而焚之，以灰置溷中。众咸恐，谓神怒且不测，言于侍御。侍御笑而置之，卒无咎。后神梦于邻人，求为新其主。邻人重为立之，至今奉祀不衰，然虐焰则已熄矣。

    Explanation

    266

    From the Jade Terrace, where the dew is washed, the world knows of Xu Feiqiong; in the Lotus Garden, admiring flowers, is there no Ding Wenyi on top?
    Wang Shi Lin, the Censor,
    Jingdezhen in Raozhou is a major metropolis of Jiangxi Province. Merchants gather like spokes in a wheel, and scholars and officials also often stay here.
    When Wang Shi Lin, the Censor, was a xiao lian, he once traveled to this place and stayed at a guesthouse in the western wing of Fuzhou. The main hall was said to be unpeaceful. Previously, a certain gentleman from the same county who lived there saw spirits during the day, and his servants were terrified at night, greatly harassed, and fearing, moved elsewhere. After the gentleman left, the Censor ordered that he stay there. Everyone spoke against it, but the Censor remained calm. After a long time, there was no significant change, and it became a lucky residence.
    Furthermore, the Censor's residence had a place known as the Three Holy Kings, who had always been able to harm people. The crowd built a small stone shrine to house its master, regularly offering sacrifices with great reverence. If one offended the trees and stones near the shrine or spoke disrespectfully, they were immediately shot and killed, or even lightly, it caused rapid disability, not just one person. The Censor's son, Dianhua, and his younger brothers were still young, heard of it and detested it. They went to the shrine and listed its crimes, took its master, split it, and burned it, placing the ashes in the latrine. Everyone was afraid, thinking the god was angry and unpredictable, and spoke to the Censor. The Censor laughed and dismissed it, and finally had no harm. Later, the god appeared in a dream to a neighbor, asking to be given a new master. The neighbor rebuilt it, and it is still worshipped today without decline, but the cruel flames have already been extinguished.

    267

    非非子曰：谅哉左氏之言，妖由人兴也!观于侍御桥梓之事，亦从可见矣。其正气之所慑欤?抑贵达之徵也?三圣公王者，不审为何神，其称号亦殊僭妄。吾郡乡里处处有之，多能祟人。迹其所为，殆厉鬼之属。要其所祸者，皆其衰败者也。又能为神灯，灯色浅碧异常火，去地尺许，隐隐见脚跟，若有人擎之以行者，明灭多寡不常。夜行者多见之。余髫时侍太夫人板舆，自信州归里。未至家数里，日暮道黑，无从觅炬。忽见神灯起于前，相隔近一丈，导舆夫而行。迫之莫及，赖以识途。甫及里门而隐。则家僮已捧椠道左矣。灯灭之处。有本里三圣龛云。噫！三圣于我，是为有施。吾亦神之而已矣。若孝廉昆仲所为，则动于公愤矣，能令人壮。
    　　东岳府掌薄

    Explanation

    267

    Nononzi said: Indeed, the words of Zuo's commentator are true; supernatural phenomena arise from human actions! Observing the matter of the Shiyu Qiaozizi, one can also see this clearly. Is it due to the power of righteousness? Or is it a sign of nobility? The Three Holy Kings, whose identities are unclear, are considered kings or deities, and their titles are quite presumptuous. In my county and village, they are everywhere, and they often harm people. Tracing their actions, they are likely of the malevolent spirit category. The victims of their harm are those who are in decline or decay. They can also manifest as divine lamps, which have a light blue color unlike ordinary fire, hover about a foot above the ground, and faintly reveal the heel, as if someone is carrying them while moving. Their brightness flickers and varies in intensity. Travelers at night often see them. When I was a child, I served my mother in a sedan chair, returning from Xinzhou to my hometown. Not yet home, several miles away, at dusk when the road was dark, I could not find a torch. Suddenly, I saw a divine lamp appear in front, about a yard away, guiding the bearers of the sedan chair.
