I could not catch up to it, but I relied on it to recognize the path. Just as we reached the village gate, it disappeared. Then my servant had already carried a tablet on the left side of the road. The place where the lamp went out was the village's Three Saints Shrine. Ah! The Three Saints to me, are beneficial. I also treat them as deities. If the actions of the scholars who passed the imperial examinations were driven by public anger, they could invigorate people.
    　　Eastern Mountain Temple Clerk

    268

    明末，成都有仕宦至卿贰者。其子年十九，忽暴病死。逾数月，其父梦之，云：“儿生簪缨之家，长纨袴之窟，席丰履厚，固已久矣。近为东岳府君皂役，既贱且劳，备诸苦况。而某吏部之子亦与儿共事。前数月，有新任判官，生时与吏部有旧，即转其子为内班，今复转为录事矣。吾父姻党多在当路，独不能嘱托，使为儿免此役乎?”父觉而伤之，百计图营，然明冥异路，无可为也。于是作书千馀言，焚于东岳庙，大约求东岳君照拂其子也。
    　　复梦子来告曰：“吾父书为门者所格，幸未达于东岳君；不然，且获罪。东岳君岂可干以私者乎?儿今探知寅伯父张虹当为东岳府掌簿，于某日受事。宜速往，赂以钱二万，求其转斡，则儿获免此役矣。”父觉而忆之，因思：“张，吾至交也。些须之求，庸必贿乎?且事属虚渺，恐徒费无益。”第造张言之，而不复以赂往。张愕然曰：“吾其死乎?果有是，不烦叮嘱矣。”父再拜而退。至期而张果死。

    Explanation

    268

    At the end of the Ming Dynasty, there was an official in Chengdu who had risen to the rank of a high-ranking minister. His son, at the age of nineteen, suddenly fell gravely ill and died. After several months, the father dreamed of his son, who said, "I was born into a family of nobility, grew up in a life of luxury, and have long enjoyed comfort and abundance. Recently, I have been serving as a lowly laborer for the Lord of Mount Tai, a position that is both humble and laborious, filled with various hardships. Another son of a Ministry of Personnel official serves alongside me. Several months ago, a new judge was appointed, who had known the Ministry of Personnel in his previous life. He transferred his son to a higher position, and now he has been promoted again to a clerical role. My father has many relatives and associates in high places, but can you not find someone to intercede and exempt me from this labor?" The father woke up, deeply grieved, and tried countless ways to help his son, but realized that the realms of the living and the dead were separate, and there was nothing he could do. He then wrote a letter of several thousand words and burned it at the Temple of Mount Tai, requesting the Lord of Mount Tai to look after his son.

    The father dreamed again, and his son appeared to him, saying, "Father, your letter was intercepted by the gatekeeper and did not reach the Lord of Mount Tai; otherwise, I would have incurred his wrath. The Lord of Mount Tai cannot be approached with private requests! I have discovered that my uncle Zhang Hong will be the recorder of the Mount Tai office on a certain day. You should go to him quickly, bribe him with twenty thousand coins, and ask him to intervene. Then I will be exempted from this labor." The father woke up and remembered the dream. He thought to himself, "Zhang is my close friend. Is it necessary to bribe him for such a small request? Moreover, this matter seems too far-fetched, and I fear it would be a waste of money with no benefit." So he went to Zhang and told him about the matter, but did not bring any money. Zhang was startled and said, "Am I going to die? If this is true, I don’t need to be reminded." The father bowed twice and left. On the appointed day, Zhang indeed died.

    269

    十馀日后，子复于梦中告父曰：“吾父吝费，不惟无益，且受其虐矣。儿屡谒张公，求以犹子之礼见，俱为阍者所叱。最后见之，张公怒言：“吾与尔父偶尔同官，声势依倚，初无天伦骨肉之爱，复鲜金珠币帛之交。即使阳世请托，亦不能耽无贿之令名而为之委曲，况幽冥之间，时异势殊。吾既与尔父无情，则尔亦于我何与，与众役等耳。而冥法不可轻干，公门不可私谒，尔屡犯焉，法不可贷。不然，府君闻之，且谓我门如市也。”杖儿三十而逐之。儿前者之言，盖逆知有今日也。倘赂之，何以至此?即吏部子之于判官，亦非托诸空言者也。”遂掩泣而去。父为之大恚而觉，遂郁结成疾，数月亦死。

    Explanation

    269

    After more than ten days, the son again told his father in a dream, saying, "My father is stingy with expenses, which is not only futile but also harmful to me. I have repeatedly visited Mr. Zhang, seeking to see him with the respect due to a nephew, but all were rebuked by the gatekeeper. Finally seeing him, Mr. Zhang angrily said, 'I and your father occasionally served together in the same office, relying on each other's influence, but we had no familial affection or financial dealings. Even in the mortal world, if someone asks for favors, I cannot indulge in the reputation of being incorruptible and do favors, let alone in the underworld where circumstances and situations are different. Since I have no affection for your father, you have no connection to me either; you are just like any other servant. The laws of the underworld cannot be lightly violated, and private visits to official places are not allowed. You have repeatedly violated these rules, so the law cannot be lenient. Otherwise, if the Lord of the Underworld hears about it, he will think my place is like a marketplace.' He beat the son thirty times and drove him away. My earlier words were a prediction of this outcome. If I had bribed him, how could this have happened? Even the son of the Minister of Personnel to the Judge is not something to be taken lightly.' The son then cried and left. The father became very angry upon waking and, as a result, fell ill and died after several months."

    270

    段 生
    　　段生者，——逸其地与名，——年十七八，神姿秀朗，时有潘岳。卫玠之目。自少失怙恃，家计贫乏，然乡邻戚友多器之，每所助其金钱，得不甚困。生既自负英特，锐意进取，亦念非毛锥颖脱。终不免穷鬼揶揄，因是而名心甚炽。应童子试，补诸生。乡荐不售，乃从诸戚好醵金入太学。赴都应顺天乡试，复落解。贫不能返，遂止京师，以图再举。

    Explanation

    270

    段生者，——其地与名皆不详——年十七八，神姿秀朗，时有潘岳、卫玠之目。自少失怙恃，家计贫乏，然乡邻戚友多器之，每所助其金钱，得不甚困。生既自负英特，锐意进取，亦念非毛锥颖脱，终不免穷鬼揶揄，因是而名心甚炽。应童子试，补诸生。乡荐不售，乃从诸戚好醵金入太学。赴都应顺天乡试，复落解。贫不能返，遂止京师，以图再举。

    271

    城东有小宅一区，素不靖，主人以是故，取值甚廉。生固不知也，税居之。自夏徂秋，略无所异，惟纸窗石炕，孤闷无聊耳。
    　　一夕，灭烛而寝，少顷而觉。乃在绡帐绣被中，兰麝芬芳扑鼻。生惊起四顾，则漆几银缸。人影在壁。一女郎背灯而坐，钗光鬓影，隐跃撩人，而明珰玉佩，时姗珊作声。生不禁毛磔，亟问；“此何地?尔何人?”女郎回眸斜盼，半露芳姿，少焉发声如流莺语燕，曰：“君自至此，君顾不知?吾不尔诘，反诘我耶?”言罢，仍转靥背灯，微闻叹息之声。生故恇怯，不敢复问，但蠕缩衾中，汗出如蒸，不觉昏然，竟成熟睡。既寤而残月射窗，晓钟切枕，依然独眠孤馆耳。遂以为梦。

    271

    In the eastern part of the city, there was a small house that was always restless. The owner rented it out at a very low price because of this. The protagonist, unaware of the house's reputation, rented it. From summer to autumn, there was nothing particularly unusual, only paper windows and stone beds, making it a lonely and boring place.

    One night, after extinguishing the candle and going to sleep, the protagonist awoke a short while later. He found himself in a silk canopy with an embroidered quilt, and the fragrance of orchids and musk filled the air. Startled, he sat up and looked around. There was a lacquered table and a silver lamp, and a shadow was cast on the wall. A young lady was sitting with her back to the light, her hairpins and hair gleaming, subtly enticing. Her bright jade pendants and ornaments made a rustling sound as she moved. The protagonist felt his hair stand on end and asked urgently, "Where is this place? Who are you?" The young lady turned her eyes sideways, partially revealing her beautiful figure. After a moment, her voice, like that of a nightingale or swallow, replied, "You came here yourself, yet you don't know? I am not questioning you, yet you are questioning me?" She then turned her face away from the light, and a faint sigh could be heard. The protagonist, naturally timid, dared not ask again. He crawled back into the quilt, sweating profusely, and fell into a deep sleep. When he awoke, he saw a crescent moon shining through the window and the morning bell striking against the pillow. He was still alone in a solitary inn. And then, he thought it was all a dream.

    272

    次夜既寐，忽有人摇之醒，则前背灯女郎也，微笑而无言。生谛视之，有倾城冠世之姿，疑惧顿消，因推枕拥衾而坐，展问邦族姓氏。女低应曰：“天下固有如此仓卒客，两次造访，犹自不识主人氏族。儿姓杜氏，名兰秋，本贯洛阳。初从父母，移家于此。”生因请谒其父母，女曰：“复移去五载矣。惟儿与婢子小铃居此耳。”复问有伺亲串往来，女曰：“无之。惟异姓姊妹数人皆别宅而居。”生窃喜，稍以游词侵之。女赤颊无言，俯颈捻双带而已，削玉纤纤，类麻姑手爪。生心大动，前握其腕，求与欢。女虽微拒，而冶荡之态，渐觉不支，乃低骂曰：“何物小郎，作剧太恶，使世间无复贞姜。”遂解衣登榻，成眷属焉。抱璞含苞，依然处子也。

    Explanation

    272

    On the following night, after sleeping, he was suddenly shaken awake by someone—it was the woman who had lit the lamp from behind earlier. She smiled without speaking. He looked at her closely and saw that she possessed a beauty that could captivate the entire city and surpass the world. His doubts and fears vanished, so he pushed aside the pillow and wrapped himself in the quilt, sitting up, and asked about her family and surname. She replied softly, "There are indeed such impromptu guests in the world. I visited twice and still don’t recognize my host’s lineage. My surname is Du, and my given name is Lanqiu. Originally from Luoyang, I first followed my parents and moved here." He then asked about her parents, and she said, "We have moved again for five years. Only I and my maid Xiao Ling live here now." He asked if there were any relatives or acquaintances who visited, and she replied, "None. Only several sisters-in-law live in different houses." He secretly delighted in this, and tried to flatter her slightly. She blushed and remained silent, lowering her head and playing with her double ribbons. Her slender hands were like carved jade, resembling the hands of Lady Magu. His heart was greatly stirred, and he reached out to take her wrist, seeking her favor. Although she slightly resisted, her licentious demeanor gradually overcame her resistance, and she muttered softly, "What kind of young man are you? You’re being too mischievous, making the world devoid of virtuous women." She then undressed and climbed onto the bed, and they became a couple. She was pure and untouched, still a maiden.

    273

    已而一婢款关入，手捧一器，置桌上，曰：“酪奴来矣。”青衣窄袖，夭冶殊常。既见生，作色曰：“谁家秀才不守法度，亟当使受水厄，以惩其拂墙花影之罪。”生闻言惶惑，莫知所对。女睨之而笑，以手挥婢曰：“去去！狡狯婢子，故以险语骇人，不顾书痴破胆耶?”婢徐徐匿笑而出。

    Explanation

    273

    After a while, a maid knocked on the door and entered, holding a vessel in her hand. She placed it on the table and said, "The milk servant has arrived." She wore a simple, short-sleeved garment and was exceptionally graceful and beautiful. Upon seeing the young scholar, she changed her expression and said, "Which scholar does not adhere to rules and regulations? He should be made to suffer the water ordeal to punish his crime of brushing against the wall and casting a shadow." The young scholar was confused and did not know how to respond. The woman glanced at him and smiled, waving her hand to dismiss the maid, saying, "Go, go! You sly and mischievous maid, deliberately using alarming words to frighten people, without regard for the scholar's courage?" The maid sneaked a smile and left.

    274

    女谓生曰：“此即小铃，儿之私人也，姑弗恐。”生始敢纵息，徐悟水厄之说，叩女曰：“卿好茗饮乎?”女戏抚其腮曰：“亏杀小郎聪明颖悟到此，儿生平实有此癖， 自谓女中卢仝，故婢子辈习闻此说。不识小郎枵腹，能为左纨素劲敌否?”生故嗜茶，应声曰：“黄九之穷，半为车声羊肠，七碗之技岂足道哉?”女曰：“书生大言恐众，是其故智。姑试之。”遂揽衣下榻。
    　　生从后戏捉其足曰：“新花著雨，莫眼撷否?”女回顾，怒之出目，脸潮忽生，无语可措，乃掣生衣衫睨之，掷地曰：“苏季子貂裘敝矣，下第鲰生一寒至此，犹漫作丑态向人！”生不觉赧然踧踖，叹息而起。女慰之曰：“前言亦戏耳。大丈夫不耻抱，宁惭败絮?”即拾衣起，为生著之。

    Explanation

    274

    The girl told the boy, "This is the little bell, my private possession, so please don't be afraid." The boy then dared to relax, gradually realizing the saying about water disaster, and asked the girl, "Do you like tea?" The girl playfully patted his cheek and said, "It's a pity that you, the young master, are so clever and insightful to this point. I have always had this habit, considering myself a Lu Tong among women. So the maidservants have often heard this saying. Don't you know that the young master is hungry? Can you be a strong opponent for the left-side white silk?" The boy, who was fond of tea, replied, "The poverty of Huang Jiu is half due to the sound of carriages and the winding roads. The skill of seven bowls is not worth mentioning." The girl said, "The scholar makes grand statements to frighten the crowd. It is his old trick. Let's try it." She then took off her clothes and got off the bed.

    The boy playfully grabbed her foot from behind and said, "New flowers in the rain, won't you pluck them?" The girl turned around, her eyes flashing with anger, and her face suddenly flushed. She had no words to respond and pulled the boy's clothes, looking at him sideways. She threw them to the ground and said, "Su Qin's fur coat is worn out. A failed candidate is as cold as this. Still making ugly gestures towards people!" The boy blushed and became uneasy, sighing and getting up. The girl comforted him, "What I said before was just a joke. A true man does not despise holding, but is ashamed of being like rotten cotton." She picked up the clothes and put them on the boy.

    275

    旋取碗倾茶对啜，生童稍解，且啜且叹曰：“贫非病，何足怏怏！良以主司冬烘，致使书生眊矂，胸中枨触，殊觉懊然!”女曰：“是尤不然。儿闻曹沫不耻三败之辱，卞和不惧再刖之刑，忍以成之，艰以得之。观其所为，志念深矣。即谓千金之弩，耻于再试，则摧撞折牙，永息机用，效妙手空空可也。士诚有才，何适不然，而自令若是?”生曰：“此老生常谈，谁则不知?终身入其中，乍动于不及觉耳。且吾向者视卿，背灯太息，壹似重有忧者。以卿玉腕珠颜，妙龄芳齿，有何不释?亦愁苦若此，岂其为郎憔悴乎?”女不觉愀然，曰：“儿之愁苦，甚于下第秀才。人孤似月，命薄如云。回首当年，春风几度，抱卫姬之深痛，比倩女之离魂，人世零丁，孰甚于此！”言讫，玉筋双垂，娇啼呜咽。生亦凄然泪承，睫不能已，频以袖为女拭面，慰以宽言。小铃闻之，亦来劝勉。乃始止泣，泪痕纵横粉颊，惨黛长眉尚压盈盈秋水也。

    Explanation

    275

    Quickly taking a bowl, pouring tea, and drinking it together, the young student began to understand. While drinking and sighing, he said, "Poverty is not a disease; why should one be so gloomy! It is indeed due to the examiners being dull and rigid, causing the scholars to be confused and disoriented, stirring feelings in the heart, and one feels very disappointed!" The woman replied, "This is not the case. I have heard that Cao Mo did not disgrace himself by suffering three defeats, and Bian He did not fear the punishment of having his feet cut off twice. Endure to achieve it, and work hard to obtain it. Observing what they did, their intentions were deep. If one says a thousand-gold crossbow is ashamed to be tested again, then destroy the crossbow, break the teeth, and permanently stop its use. It is better to emulate the skillful hands that are empty-handed. If a scholar truly has talent, why should it not be the case, and why should they put themselves in such a state?" The man said, "This is a common saying; who does not know it? One spends a lifetime in it, and suddenly feels it without realizing it. And I saw you before, sighing with your back to the lamp, as if you had a heavy worry. With your beautiful wrists and face, in your prime and with lovely teeth, what is there to be unreleased or unsolved? You are also suffering so much; could it be that you are thinning out for me?" The woman unconsciously felt sad, saying, "My suffering is more than a failed scholar. One is as lonely as the moon and as thin as a cloud. Looking back at the past, how many times has the spring breeze blown? I bear the deep sorrow of Wei Ji and the wandering soul of Qian Nu. Loneliness in the world, who can surpass this!" After finishing her words, tears streamed down like jade threads, and she sobbed softly. The man also felt sad, tears streaming down, unable to stop. He frequently wiped the woman's face with his sleeve, comforting her with gentle words. Xiao Ling heard this and also came to encourage her. She finally stopped crying, with tears streaking across her powdered cheeks, her dark eyebrows still pressing over her clear, watery eyes.

    276

    于是倾茗复啜，各飏愁襟，喁喁絮语间，已尽数器。女双脸挑晕，若被酒然，愈觉妍媚撩人。生兴狂不禁，辞言茶已不胜，欲饮蓝桥琼液矣。女哂曰：“觓茗瘕已取盈耶?未便筑受降城，且再赐金茎露一杯。”使小铃捧茶向生，生勉尽一碗，曰：“胸有奇渴，非苦蕣所能消也。”小铃置茶笑而出，女亦笑而止。遂复就寝。生戏谓曰：“温柔乡中宿，死不为枉。”女曰：“本与君有夙缘，不图于今夕了之。”生闻夙缘之说，转滋疑抱，曰：“逆旅孤眠，不审何以有此?犹恐趾离戏我，非有夙缘，祗成幻梦，向梅花惆怅而已！”女曰：“天下恐无此梦，梦中恐无此人，人中恐无此景也。郎既垂涎于梦，则与郎同梦何如?”

    Explanation

    276

    After pouring and drinking tea again, they dispelled their sorrows and engaged in soft, continuous conversation, finishing all the tea. The woman's cheeks blushed flirtatiously, as if she were under the influence of alcohol, making her even more beautiful and charming. The man's excitement could not be contained; he said that he could no longer bear the tea and wished to drink the nectar of the gods from the Blue Bridge. The woman smiled and said, "Have you already had your fill of tea? It's not yet time to build a city of surrender. Let me give you another cup of the precious dew from the golden stem." She had the maid, Xiao Ling, bring the tea to the man. He tried hard to finish the bowl and said, "I have an unusual thirst that cannot be quenched by bitter flowers." Xiao Ling placed the tea and laughed as she left, and the woman also laughed and stopped. They then returned to bed. The man playfully said, "Sleeping in the land of gentleness and tenderness is not in vain." The woman replied, "I have a predestined connection with you, and I did not expect it to be fulfilled tonight." Hearing this, the man became more doubtful and said, "Sleeping alone in a temporary lodging, I wonder why this is happening. I fear it might be a mockery or a trick, and if there is no predestined connection, it will only be an illusionary dream, leaving me melancholic about plum blossoms!" The woman said, "The world fears there is no such dream, and in a dream, one fears there is no such person, and among people, one fears there is no such scene. Since you are eager for this dream, how about sharing it with you?"

    277

    生为解颐，然终疑之，乃伺女睡，私于茵下搜得绣履一只，藏之腰间，俟明日验其真幻。初欲不寐达旦，缘与女狎，倦甚不支，因复沉睡。至晓，女失所在。而履故存也，纤小类新月，花样精巧殊伦，不异人间美人步莲物。叹诧久之，悟蕉鹿之非梦矣，而亦知其非人，疑惧填膺。凡三夕，目不交睫，独寝如故也。既而心味其美，思念颇挚，日玩弄绣履，珍若连城，以为美人之贻，物在人亡，倍可惜也。
    　　越四日，黄昏，无聊偃卧，恍惚复眠绣榻。见女郎搴帏凝视，微批其颊，曰：“书生绣虎雕龙，乃盗人亵物何为?所谓穿窬之类，非子也耶?”生惊喜，应曰：“刺史不能守鞋，从者岂其窃屦?会须以此要盟耳！”时小铃在侧，佯怒曰：“酸子作贼，三日逋逃，今舌强犹尔，请令长跪捉跽，用惩厥后。”直前牵生下床，按令屈膝，女笑曰：“郎亦可怜矣，姑贷之。使出力自赎。”生亦笑曰：“武库戈矛，随身而具，奈无用武之地何?

    Explanation

    277

    He lived to amuse himself, yet he remained doubtful, so he waited for the woman to sleep, secretly searching and finding a embroidered shoe, which he hid in his waistband, planning to verify its reality the next day. Initially, he intended to stay awake all night, but due to exhaustion from being intimate with the woman, he could not bear it and fell back into a deep sleep. By dawn, the woman had vanished. Yet the shoe remained, delicate and small like a crescent moon, with exquisite and unique patterns, no different from the shoes of a beautiful woman. He marveled for a long time, realizing that the dream of the banana and deer was not a dream, and he also knew that she was not an ordinary person, filled with doubt and fear. For three nights, he did not sleep, lying alone as before. Later, he savored her beauty, his thoughts filled with deep affection, playing with the embroidered shoe every day, valuing it as if it were worth more than a city, believing it to be a gift from a beautiful woman, lamenting that the object remained but the person was gone, feeling it all the more precious.

    Four days later, at dusk, feeling bored and lying down, he suddenly fell asleep on the embroidered bed again. He saw the young lady pull back the curtain and gaze at him, lightly slapping his cheek and saying, "A scholar who embroiders tigers and carves dragons, why did you steal someone's intimate belongings? Aren't you the kind of person who digs through walls and climbs through windows?" The scholar was surprised and replied, "The governor could not guard the shoe, so his attendant must have stolen it. We must use this as a bond or pledge!" At that moment, Xiao Ling stood by, pretending to be angry and said, "You thief, after three days of escape, you still have the nerve to speak, please kneel and bow, to punish you for the future." She stepped forward, pulled the scholar off the bed, and made him kneel, saying with a smile, "You are also pitiful, I will forgive you. Let you redeem yourself by effort." The scholar also smiled and said, "My arsenal of literary skills is always with me, but where is the place to use them?"

    278

    ”小铃曰：“昨高七姑折柬，约为秉烛游，想又负彼诗债，盇与小郎俱往，一角楚汉?”女曰：“微尔言，吾几忘之。宁馨雅集，彼细酸技痒，当鍪弧先登，犹烦劝驾耶?”生笑曰：“既欲乞师解围，乃用激将法，真智囊也。虽然，夜深矣，心旌不定，庸敢他出?彼倘问韩寿何来，将何以应?不且鸿离鱼网乎?”女曰：“桃源女伴，皆司空见惯，无虑唐突。儿亦自有袖里兵，用不著捉刀人。所以相屈者，诚虑小郎向隅耳。”生乃许之。小铃前导。

    Explanation

    278

    "Xiao Ling said, 'Yesterday, Gao Qigū sent an invitation, and we agreed to enjoy ourselves after dark. I suppose I have another poetic debt to pay. Perhaps you and Young Master should go together and see who comes out on top in this little rivalry?' The woman replied, 'If not for your words, I would have almost forgotten. This is a delightful gathering of scholars. He is so eager to show off his skills, he will surely take the lead. Should I still try to persuade him?' The young man laughed and said, 'Since you want to ask for help to relieve a difficult situation, you are using the strategy of provocation. You are truly a wise advisor. However, it is late at night, and my heart is unsettled. How dare I go elsewhere? If he asks where Young Master came from, what should I say? Won't I just escape like a bird from a net?' The woman said, 'The female companions from Peach Blossom Spring are all familiar to me, so there is no need to worry about causing offense. I have my own hidden resources and don't need a substitute. The reason I invited you is that I truly worry that Young Master might feel left out.' The young man then agreed. Xiao Ling led the way."

    279

    甫出门，西风砭骨，谅露侵肤。片月东来，松阴桧影，萧疎满地。约三四矢地，灯光隐隐，射于林表。少顷而至，则甲第一区，缭以茅屋土室。叩环数声，一女使启关接入，径造其堂。女向内呼曰：“不速之客来，主人何避之深耶?”俄有美人自内出，年可十八九，姱容情态，直与女埒。笑曰：“我谓是何嘉宾，乃红拂妓携偶偕奔耳。”生跼踖谦让，唇舌无措。美人曰：“乌衣子弟，固如此哉?”生私询小铃，始知美人即七姑也。
    　　七姑请入内，生惶恐称不敢。女曰：“我郎即彼郎也，何遂以形迹拘拘?”即挽生先行。七姑骂曰：“妮子唇锋刺人乃尔！”生至是已不复畏，回首睨七姑，笑而谢之。七姑低鬟，略不应。既入内，素壁文窗，斐几华榻；兽炉蕴香，一缕烟袅；图书笔砚，位置骈罗。膏烛光中，遥见阶前盆花比列，绿叶纷披。生问：“何花浓丽若此？”女使告曰：“断肠花也。”生令秉烛观之，胭脂点点，娇楚可怜。回视诸女，皆有凄惋之色。

    Explanation

    279

    As he stepped out, the west wind pierced his bones, and the dew felt like a chill on his skin. A crescent moon rose in the east, casting sparse shadows of pine and cypress trees across the ground. After about the distance of three to four arrows, faint lights could be seen through the trees. Soon he arrived at a place surrounded by thatched cottages and earthen rooms. He knocked on the door several times, and a female servant opened it and let him in. He directly entered the hall. The woman called out to the inside, “Unexpected guests have come, why is the host hiding so deeply?” Shortly after, a beautiful woman emerged from inside, around eighteen or nineteen years old, with a graceful appearance that matched the other woman. She smiled and said, “I thought who this esteemed guest might be, but it turns out to be the courtesan Hong Fu, accompanied by her lover.” The protagonist was nervous and at a loss for words. The beautiful woman said, “Are you really so formal, a well-bred young man?” The protagonist privately inquired about “小铃” and learned that the beautiful woman was actually “七姑.”
    　　“七姑” invited him inside, but the protagonist hesitated and claimed to be unworthy. The woman said, “My husband is also your husband, why are you so formal?” She then took the protagonist’s hand and led him in. “七姑” scolded, “Your sharp tongue is quite cutting, girl!” By now, the protagonist was no longer afraid. He turned to look at “七姑” and smiled, thanking her. “七姑” lowered her head and barely responded. Once inside, the room had plain walls and elegant windows, a carved table, and a luxurious bed. A furnace emitted fragrance, with a wisp of smoke rising. Books, writing tools, and other items were neatly arranged. In the candlelight, he could see pots of flowers lined up on the steps, with green leaves spread out. He asked, “What kind of flower is so rich and beautiful?” The female servant replied, “It’s the heart-wrenching flower.” He asked for a candle to view it, and saw spots of red, delicate and pitiable. Looking back at the other women, they all had sad and pitiful expressions.

    280

    旋闻剥啄声，令女使往应。顷之，偕数女而入。一年四十馀，面枯瘦，一年三十许；一年二十馀。各相见通款，则皆左右邻女赴七姑之招者也。叙坐毕，仍设茗饮，供山果数品，殊雅洁。生始详叩七姑家世，答曰：“儿高县尹之女也。父官福州，留老母与儿居此。一月前，阿姐家迎老母去，犹未还也。”生曰：“大家风范，固自非常。”七姑伪词酬对，吐纳生新。诸女游词浪语，无所不至。惟七姑正襟端坐，庄语不佻，然眉影眼波属童于生，生颇惑之。

    Explanation

    280

    Soon after, he heard the sound of someone knocking, and he sent a female servant to answer. In a moment, several women entered together. One was over forty years old, with a gaunt face; another was around thirty; and another was over twenty. They exchanged greetings, and it turned out that they were all neighbors invited by Qiqi. After they sat down, tea was served, along with several kinds of wild fruits, which were very elegant and clean. The narrator then inquired in detail about Qiqi's family background, to which she replied, "I am the daughter of a high-ranking county magistrate. My father serves in Fuzhou, and he left my elderly mother and me to live here. A month ago, my sister brought our mother away, and she has not yet returned." The narrator said, "Your noble demeanor is indeed extraordinary." Qiqi responded with polite and fresh words, while the other women engaged in idle and varied conversations. Only Qiqi sat upright and spoke seriously, though her eyes and eyebrows conveyed a subtle interest in the narrator, who found himself quite captivated by her.

    281

    巳而年四十馀者曰：“七姑见召何为?”七姑捧茗碗而起，曰：“酒军觞政，连日棼如。今玉郎在坐，不可以俗事败意，但宜剪烛烹茗，各赋新词。如诗不成，罚依玉川茶数。”年三十许者笑曰：“主人不欲破悭，徒以苹蘩昭其忠信，乃侈然欲执牛耳耶?”年二十馀者曰：“亦可谓所持者小，而所求者大矣。”女曰：“良夜苦短，无事以口舌杀风景，便可从七姑之议，留为身后佳话。毋酒食是议，使舞文之士又赋《老饕》也。”众皆发粲。

    Explanation

    281

    After a while, a man in his forties said, "Why was the Seventh Aunt summoned?" The Seventh Aunt, holding a bowl of tea and rising, replied, "The rules and regulations of drinking games have been chaotic for several days. Now that the young man is here, we should not spoil the mood with trivial matters. Instead, let us trim the candle and brew tea, each composing new verses. If a poem is not completed, the penalty will be based on the number of cups of tea." A man in his thirties smiled and said, "The host does not wish to break his frugality, merely to show his loyalty and trustworthiness through the offering of simple offerings, yet he greedily wants to take the lead?" A man in his twenties said, "He holds onto something small while seeking something great." The Seventh Aunt said, "The good night is short, let us not waste it with idle talk. Let us follow my suggestion and leave behind a good story for afterlife. Let us not discuss food and drink, lest the literary scholars compose another 'Gourmand' poem." Everyone smiled at her suggestion.

    282

    生意属七姑，乃起而请曰：“群芳毕集，安用远觅旧题?庭中秋海棠盛开，殊可赋咏。闻此花乃少女怀人、洒泪于地而生，真情花也！某已占就拙句，不识可呈观否?”诸女皆曰：“善。”因授笺笔使书之。生书毕，径授七姑，诗曰：
    　　“岂让无香种，芳名况复同。
    　　夜深花不睡，应为怕秋风。”
    　　年四十馀者阅之，睇女而笑曰：“尔家阿郎意别有在矣。”女曰：“得陇望蜀，薄幸郎类多如此，曷足怪哉！”生支词以解之。
    　　既而女与七姑诗皆成。七姑诗曰：
    　　“秋日看花最可怜，碧鸡空见梅棠颠。

    Explanation

    282

    The idea belonged to the seventh aunt, who then got up and asked, "With all the flowers gathered here, why search far for old topics? The autumn begonia in the courtyard is in full bloom and especially worthy of poetry. I've heard that this flower grew from a young girl missing someone, shedding tears on the ground, and is truly a flower of true feelings! I have already composed some poor verses, do not know if they can be presented for viewing?" All the girls said, "Good." Then she gave them paper and pen to write it. He finished writing and directly gave it to the seventh aunt, the poem says:
    　　"How can it be allowed that a flower without fragrance has the same fragrant name.
    　　Deep into the night, the flower does not sleep, should be because it fears the autumn wind."
    　　The woman in her forties read it, looked at the girl, and smiled and said, "Your young master has something else in mind." The girl said, "Having achieved one thing, one desires another, fickle men often like this, how can it be surprising!" He offered excuses to explain it.
    　　Afterward, both the girl and the seventh aunt's poems were completed. The seventh aunt's poem says:
    　　"On an autumn day, looking at flowers is most pitiful, only seeing the green rooster on top of plum and crabapple trees.

    283

    玉腰未识花成泪，误到香霏小阁前。”
    　　女诗曰：
    　　“当年珠泪阶头溅，化作秋来花片片。
    　　玉骨长埋夜独眠，柔肠断尽无人见。”
    　　年二十徐者曰：“七姑悲谅太甚，兰娘愁怨无端，读之使人感慨凄然，那复有愉词赴笔?不如且巳。”生视诸女，眉黛间皆有恨色，谓己佻达所致，急自引咎。七姑曰：“薄命之人各有心事，非小郎之过也。”于是邻女皆辞去。女亦谓生曰，“七姑与君有缘，君亦有心，盍止此以毕夙愿?儿亦去矣。芙蓉镜下，当复相见。”七姑亦无语推拒，生心摇意夺，留止不能自主，而女已呼小铃出户矣。
    　　女使旋即闭关，引生入七姑卧室，华焕与女室无异。少焉七姑亦入，背灯痴坐，不发一语。女使徐出，反为阖扉焉。生前揖七姑曰：“漏探矣，百无所求，唯乞早寝为幸！”七姑初不应，生逼不巳，乃低骂曰：“兰秋陷我矣！”忽闻女使惊报曰：“夫人归矣！”七姑变色，亟伏生于床而出。

    Explanation

    283

    A Beautiful Woman's Tears Turned to Flowers

    She arrived at a fragrant, secluded pavilion, her jade-like waist still unaware that her tears had turned into flowers. The woman recited a poem:

    "Years ago, her tears splashed at the steps, transforming into petals falling in pieces as autumn came.
    Her pure and beautiful body was long buried, sleeping alone at night, with her tender intestines broken and no one to see her suffering."

    A young man in his early twenties said, "Seven Aunt's sympathy is too excessive, and Lan娘's grief and resentment seem unjustified. Reading this makes one feel sad and melancholic. How can there be any joyful words to write? It would be better to stop now." The young man looked at the women, seeing expressions of resentment between their brows, and blamed himself for his carefree behavior, taking responsibility quickly. Seven Aunt said, "Each person has their own troubles, and it is not the young man's fault." Thus, the neighboring women all left. The woman also told the young man, "Seven Aunt and you have a connection, and you also have a heart. Why not stay here to fulfill your long-held wish? I will leave too. Under the mirror of lotus flowers, we will meet again." Seven Aunt silently refused, the young man's mind wavering, and he could not control his desire to stay, while the woman called the little bell to leave.

    The maid quickly closed the door and led the young man into Seven Aunt's bedroom, which was as luxurious as the woman's room. Shortly after, Seven Aunt entered, sitting quietly with her back to the light, not saying a word. The maid slowly left and closed the door behind her. The young man bowed before Seven Aunt and said, "It is late, and I have no requests, only asking to sleep early!" Seven Aunt initially did not respond, but the young man persistently urged her, and she finally softly cursed, saying, "Lan Autumn has trapped me!" Suddenly, the maid reported, "The lady has returned!" Seven Aunt changed color and quickly hid the young man under the bed before leaving.

    284

    俄闻有老妇声自外而内，间曰：“茶具纵横，何客至此?”七姑曰：“邻姓姊妹也。”老妇又曰：“尔适见我，神色不定，何故?”七姑曰：“别阿娘久，故喜形于色耳。”老妇又曰：“非喜色，实惧色也。”旋见老妇入卧室，年近六十，面目凛然有怒色，诘七姑曰：“何得有男子气？”七姑不能隐，遂告之。老妇大恚曰：“尔父居官不洁，故责报于尔耶?何中篝之丑如此！”七姑羞惭伏地，女使启帷，提生耳出于衾中，长跪谢罪。老妇气塞，半晌乃骂曰：“好秀才，禽行至此耶！”徐操梃挞女使数十，命逐生于大门外。门随闭，犹闻内哗不已。生倚于檐下听之，闻老妇曰：“不看渠是进士，肯令出耶?”馀语嘈杂不可辨。遥闻群鸡乱鸣，语声遂寂。生亦倦甚，就檐下宿焉。

    Explanation

    284

    Soon, the voice of an old woman was heard coming from outside to inside, gradually saying, "The tea utensils are scattered everywhere; what guest has come here?" The Seventh Aunt replied, "She is a sister from a neighboring family." The old woman then said, "You just saw me, and your expression was uncertain; why?" The Seventh Aunt said, "It has been a long time since I last saw Mother, so I was overjoyed and it showed on my face." The old woman then said, "It was not a joyful expression; it was actually a fearful one." Soon, the old woman entered the bedroom. She was nearly sixty years old, with a stern and angry expression. She questioned the Seventh Aunt, "How could you have a man's demeanor?" The Seventh Aunt could not hide it and told her. The old woman was greatly enraged and said, "Is it because your father was corrupt in his official duties that he is punishing you? How could you be so unseemly and disgraceful!" The Seventh Aunt, ashamed and embarrassed, prostrated herself on the ground. The maidservant opened the curtain, pulled the man's ear out from under the quilt, and he prostrated himself in a kneeling position to apologize. The old woman was filled with anger and, after a while, shouted, "Fine scholar, how could you behave like an animal!" She slowly took a stick and beat the maidservant several times, then ordered that the man be chased out of the main gate. The door closed immediately, but the noise inside continued. The man, also very tired, stayed under the eaves. He heard the old woman say, "If I had not seen that he is a successful candidate in the imperial examinations, would I have let him go?" The rest of the words were too chaotic to understand. Far away, he heard the roosters crowing in disorder, and the noise inside then fell silent. The man was also very tired and stayed under the eaves for the night.

    285

    比醒而东方白矣，此风露满衣，虫螀聒耳，仰见树木萧疎。群鸟啁哳，夜来房舍化为乌有，乃卧乱冢之间耳。惊愕而起，踉跄而归。距所居已二里许，不识夜来何自而出，又若此其近也。
    　　亟以所见语主人。主人不能讳，告之曰：“此宅实河南杜某所居。闻其有一女，末嫁而卒，一婢亦旋死。客所遇，其必此也。至高七姑云云，官地冢多，不知伊谁也。”生又问杜氏之墓，主人亦不知，但言客宜去，不然，且祸客。生细思他处房值必昂，无从措置，而心恋兰秋之美，将殉而甘心；又念青娥皓齿，软玉温香，当非祸人者。仍坚请不去，主人无如之何，亦听之。
    　　入室，将取绣履示主人。履旧置枕下，则已不知所在矣。徘徊眷念，冀其复见，久之杳然。因忆老妇“进士”之语，知必捷南宫，心转安之。遂下帷攻苦，果于次科领乡荐，明年复成进士。

    Explanation

    285

    After waking, the east had turned white. The wind and dew filled my clothes, the sound of cicadas and crickets disturbed my ears, and I looked up to see sparse trees. Flocks of birds chirped, and last night, the buildings had vanished, leaving me lying among disordered graves. Startled, I got up and staggered back home. I was already two or so li away from my residence, and I did not know where I had come from last night, yet it felt so close.

    I quickly told the master what I had seen. The master could not conceal the truth and said, "This house was actually lived in by Mr. Du of Henan. I heard that he had a daughter who died unmarried, and a maid who also died shortly after. What you encountered must be this. As for High Auntie Seven and others, there are many tombs on official land, but I do not know whose they are." The guest asked again about the Du family's tomb, but the master also did not know. He just said that the guest should leave, otherwise, he would be harmed. The guest thought carefully that rent elsewhere must be high and could not arrange for accommodation, but he longed for the beauty of autumn and would die for it, being content. He also thought of a beautiful maiden with white teeth, soft jade with warm fragrance, and must not be harmful. He still firmly requested not to leave, and the master could do nothing and also listened to the guest.

    He went into the room to take the embroidered shoes and show them to the master. The shoes that were previously placed under the pillow were now missing. He lingered and longed for them, hoping to see them again, but after a long time, they disappeared without a trace. Because of remembering the old woman's words about the imperial scholar, he knew he would certainly win the imperial examination, and his heart became more at ease. He then devoted himself to studying diligently, and indeed, he passed the provincial examination in the next year and became an imperial scholar the following year.

    286

    将理归装，一夕艨胧之际，见兰秋姗姗而前，再拜曰：“知郎君垂注颇殷，非不相念，奈缘尽于此。所以复见者，欲求念枕席之思，下及枯骨耳。儿向者不慎，逢怒于父母，迫而殒其生，婢亦坐是致死。未谋葬地，遂瘗于此室。郎君卧榻之下，即儿与婢之幽宫也。郎向者无力改葬，故不敢言。今则贵人矣，幸为别择爽垲，更筑新居，俾近七姑牛眠之地，则弱魄得所依倚，小婢亦受及乌之爱也。衔环之报，其敢不申！”因于袖中出一玉环授生曰：“此儿婴年所弄，父母因以为殉，今以赠郎。虽不足以报德，亦金碗蕤锁之意也。郎其毋辞，使儿抱歉九原！”生欲答之，脚中竟如物压，不能出声，手足亦不能运，但以首颔之而已。忽砉然一声，惊寤而起，兰秋已失所在。凝思久之，始悟“芙蓉镜下复见”之说。

    Explanation

    286

    Upon returning home, one night in a state of drowsiness, he saw Lan Qiu approaching gracefully and bowed twice, saying, "I know that you have been greatly concerned about me, but I am not unthinking of you; the bond has ended here. The reason I have come back is to express my longing for intimate thoughts, even down to the bones. My child once acted carelessly and incurred the anger of my parents, leading to my death, and the servant also died as a result. We were not able to plan for a burial site, so we were buried in this room. Beneath your bed is the resting place of my child and the servant. You were unable to change the burial location before, so you did not speak of it. Now that you are a person of high status, I hope you will choose a dry and elevated place, build a new residence, and make it near the good burial site of the Seventh Aunt. Then the weak souls will have a place to rely on, and the small servant will also receive the care and respect due to the dead. I dare not fail to repay the favor!" She then took out a jade ring from her sleeve and gave it to him, saying, "This ring was played with by my child when he was young, and my parents sacrificed it. I give it to you now. Although it is not enough to repay your kindness, it carries the meaning of a precious object. Please do not refuse, so that my child will not feel ashamed in the underworld!" He wanted to respond, but suddenly something seemed to press on his feet, preventing him from speaking or moving his hands and feet. He could only nod his head in agreement. Suddenly, there was a sharp sound, and he woke up in a start, only to find that Lan Qiu had vanished. After pondering for a long time, he finally realized the meaning of the phrase "seeing again under the mirror of the lotus."

    287

    次日，遂告主人，于炕下掘之，果得二棺焉。舁至官地，并瘗于向者所卧高七姑冢旁。立石碣二，一题曰：“洛阳美人杜氏兰秋之墓”。一题曰：“杜兰秋侍女小铃之墓”。浇奠恸哭而归。果于茵下得玉环焉，匣而藏之，珍为异宝。生后官至二千石。

    Explanation

    287

    The next day, he informed the owner and dug under the kang, and indeed found two coffins. He carried them to the official burial ground and buried them together beside the tomb of Gao Qigugu where the person had previously lain. He set up two stone tablets, one of which was inscribed as "the tomb of the beautiful Du Lanqiu from Luoyang," and the other as "the tomb of Xiao Ling, the servant girl of Du Lanqiu." He poured libations, wept bitterly, and returned home. And indeed, he found a jade ring under the bedding, placed it in a box and kept it, treasuring it as a rare treasure. Later in life, he attained the rank of Erqianshi.

    288

    【初编完】

    《耳食录》 二编 [清] 乐钧 著 [l4z5 整理]
    作者:沉思曲 发表时间: 2004/08/09 08:56 点击:848次 修改 精华 删除 置顶 来源 转移 收藏
    　　耳食录二编 清 乐钧 著
    　　自　序
    　　余既梓前编，诸君子竞来说鬼。随而记之，复得八卷，犹前志也。
    　　夫常人之生，寿者百年。过此以往，不复名人矣。百年之中，梦寐半之，孩稚十之一，衰老十之三四，无贤愚，一也。
    　　我生二十九年矣，不幸忧劳侘傺又十之七八，人之不暇而鬼是亟乎！然人能说鬼，鬼乃不能说人，若之何饱吾耳者不以餍吾舌而飫吾笔也？庸讵知余之说鬼之不如他日之人之说我乎哉？庸讵知余之说鬼之不即所以自说乎哉？鬼耶？人耶？我耶？乌乎辨？

    Explanation

    288

    Ear Food Records: Second Compilation
    [Qing Dynasty] By Le Jun
    [Compiled by l4z5]

    Author: Sinking Melody
    Published: August 9, 2004, 08:56
    Views: 848

    Self-Introduction

    I have already published the first edition, and various gentlemen are eager to tell ghost stories. I have recorded them as they came, and I have now obtained another eight volumes, still in the same spirit as before.

    The life of an ordinary person: those who live for a hundred years. Beyond this, they no longer count as a person. Within a hundred years, half is spent in dreams, one-tenth in childhood, and one-third to one-fourth in old age. Whether wise or foolish, it is the same.

    I have lived for twenty-nine years, unfortunately spending seven-eighths of my life in worry and hardship. If people have no time, why not ghosts? Yet people can tell ghost stories, but ghosts cannot tell human stories. How can those who satisfy my ears not satisfy my tongue and my pen? Who knows if my telling of ghost stories will not be surpassed by future people's telling of my story? Who knows if my telling of ghost stories is not also a way of telling my own story? Ghost? Human? Me? How can one distinguish?

    289

    乾隆甲寅岁十二月，乐宫谱元淑自序于京邸芳阴别业。
    　　耳食录二编卷一
    　　韩布衣
    　　布衣韩生，豪宕好奇。出游则累年不返。万里不远也。尝曰：“吾平生所善有四：一为会稽老道士，一为湘江女子，一为太仓泥塑皂隶，一为粤西驿亭之鬼。”或徵其由，曰：“吾向游会稽，路遇道士。银髯雪顶，足下恍惚有云气。异而尾之，则入一茅庵，踞坐石上。问之不应，礼之不答，怒而呵之，亦不报也。余无奈之何，亦坐其旁，默然相守两昼夜，饥甚而呻。道士大笑起，出门，余又尾之。行里许，道士反顾曰：“无赖子，缠扰何为？欲作饿殍耶？”余曰：“师不死，我亦不死。”道士睨余。复大笑，遂出一物啖余，类莲实而大。腹顿果。

    Explanation

    289

    In the twelfth month of the Qianlong 13th year (1744), Yuan Shu wrote the preface in his garden retreat in Beijing.

    Ears and Mouth Record: Second Compilation, Volume One

    Han Sheng, a man of humble attire, was bold and unrestrained, full of curiosity. When he traveled, he would often not return for years. A thousand miles was not far to him. He once said: "There are four things I excel at in my life: one is an elderly Taoist priest from Kuaiji, one is a woman from Xiangjiang, one is a clay statue of a low-ranking official from Taicang, and one is a ghost at a post station in western Guangdong."

    When asked for the reason, he replied: "I once traveled to Kuaiji and met an elderly Taoist priest. He had a silver beard and white hair, and his feet seemed to be surrounded by clouds. I found him strange and followed him. He entered a thatched hut and sat cross-legged on a stone. I asked him questions, but he did not respond. I greeted him respectfully, but he did not answer. I became angry and scolded him, but he did not reply. I had no choice but to sit beside him, silently keeping company for two days and nights, until I was very hungry and groaning.

    The Taoist priest laughed loudly and stood up, leaving the hut. I followed him. After walking a short distance, he turned back and said, 'Good-for-nothing, why are you bothering me? Do you want to starve to death?' I replied, 'If you don't die, I won't die either.' The Taoist priest looked at me sideways and laughed again. He then took out something to feed me, which was like a large lotus seed. My stomach felt full immediately."

    290

    引至一处，云馆星横，环以流水。仆妾数辈出迎，道士指余谓之曰：　‘是尔辈主人，宜善事之。吾往欠！”余掣其袂，辞不可，道士曰：　“无庸！余索替人不得，而属之子也。”绝袂而去。余遂入居之。数日，有伟丈夫卧门外。余意其饥，使饭之。不受，坚卧弥日。问其故，曰：　‘居无家室，出无舆马，不如死也，将栖魂于子之门矣。”余哂曰：“何必尔。”拉之入室，即以道士之所赠赠之，出门径行。复遇道士揖余曰：“子难取易舍，心迹洒然，道根植矣。后五十三年，相见于天都峰下，勿忘也！”遂去。异哉道士，仙乎仙乎！此余方外之交也。

    Explanation

    290

    Led to a place, the clouds and stars stretched across the sky, surrounded by flowing water. Several servants came out to greet me. The Taoist priest pointed to me and said to them, "This is your master, treat him well. I am leaving!" I tried to stop him, but he refused. The Taoist priest said, "No need! I couldn't find a substitute, and so I have entrusted him to you." With his sleeves torn apart, he left. I then entered and took up residence. After several days, a tall man lay at the gate. I thought he was hungry and had him served food. He refused it and continued to lie there all day. I asked him why, and he said, "I have no home, no carriage or horse, and would rather die. I will rest my soul at your gate." I smiled and said, "Why so?" I pulled him into the room and gave him the gifts the Taoist priest had given me. He left, walking straight ahead. Later, I met the Taoist priest again, who bowed to me and said, "You find it hard to take and easy to give up, your heart and actions are clear and pure, the roots of the Dao are deeply planted. In fifty-three years, we will meet again at the foot of the Heavenly Peak, do not forget!" He then departed. How extraordinary was the Taoist priest, truly a divine being! This is my friend from the other realm.

    291

    ‘南游于楚，晓行湘水。遇童子涉草而来，手握马蹄香一束。聊从乞之。答云：“是我姑姑命采以簪鬓者也。不欲更与人。”径去不顾，余亦置之。旋过一山居，嘉树当门，　小憩其下。忽见前童子启门，熟视曰：　‘是乞花人也。”因招余进。具茗饮。有女子窥帘间，若甚姣好，鬓旁马蹄香犹依稀赴息也。余辞出门，女使童子追余反。诂其故，曰：　‘姑姑年少气高，不旨适人，栖遁于此。人稀地僻，虎狼如麻。视君有奇骨，将邀福于君，惟邻是卜，缓急有赖。惟君之赐！”余许诺。馆余于舍旁之次，供具甚备。

    Explanation

    291

    Traveling south to Chu, I walked at dawn along the Xiang River. I encountered a child wading through the grass, holding a bundle of horse hoof fragrance. I asked for it casually. The child replied, "It is what my aunt ordered me to pick to adorn her hair. I do not wish to give it to anyone else." The child walked away without looking back, and I left it aside as well. Soon, I passed a mountain dwelling, where fine trees stood by the door. I took a short rest under them. Suddenly, I saw the previous child open the door and looked closely, saying, "It is the person who asked for the flower." He then invited me to enter. They prepared tea for me. A woman peered through the curtain, appearing very beautiful, with the faint scent of horse hoof fragrance still lingering by her temples. I departed from the door, and the woman sent the child to follow me back. I asked for the reason, and the child said, "My aunt is young and spirited, does not intend to marry, and has retired here. The place is remote and sparsely populated, with as many wild animals as hemp. I see you have a peculiar bone, and will bring blessings to you. We only seek a neighbor, relying on each other in times of need. Only your favor!" I agreed. They accommodated me in a room next to the house, and the provisions were very ample.

    292

    童子馈资脯，越一日至。一夕，暴客大集。余横槊而山，遇女于门，短衣提剑，剑脊血光殷然，己斩其魁而连其馀矣，翩然竟入。乃知女习剑术也。余故善槊，思一角以究其能，使请于女。女不可，屡请乃许，余舞槊，女飞剑削槊，槊靳如朽。余大惊而退。童子乃言女父本豪客，女传父术。固无敌也。女多技能，工笔札，尤娴于琴。一日，忽抱琴诣余，纨衣茜捃，姽婳无俦，而风范矜肃，据床弹《思归之引》，音响凄激，往而不反。曲终，语余曰：　‘吾逝矣，’推琴而绝。余出其资，厚葬之，馀以付童子，封树而去。此闺阁之交，盖神交也。

    Explanation

    292

    A Boy Brought Provisions, and the Next Day Arrived. One Night, a Sudden Gathering of Robbers Came. I Stood with a Spear, and Met the Woman at the Gate. She Wore Short Clothes and Held a Sword, with Blood Dripping from the Blade. She Had Already Cut Down the Leader and Connected with the Rest, and Gracefully Entered. I Then Knew She Was Skilled in Swordsmanship. I Was Also Skilled in Using a Spear, and Thought to Challenge Her to See Her Abilities. I Asked Her to Do So, but She Refused. After Repeated Requests, She Finally Agreed. I Waved My Spear, and She Flew Her Sword, Cutting My Spear as If It Were Rotten Wood. I Was Greatly Shocked and Retreated. The Boy Then Told Me That Her Father Was Originally a Robber, and She Had Inherited His Skills. She Was Indeed Invincible. She Was Skilled in Many Things, Particularly in Calligraphy and Playing the Qin. One Day, She Suddenly Brought Her Qin to Me, Wearing Fine Silk Clothes and Being Graceful and Beautiful. She Had a Dignified and Solemn Demeanor, and Sat on a Bed, Playing the "Thoughts of Returning" Piece on the Qin. The Music Was Sorrowful and Intense, with No Return. When the Piece Ended, She Said to Me, "I Am Leaving," and Pushed the Qin Away, and Died. I Buried Her with Great Care, and Entrusted the Remaining Provisions to the Boy, Sealed the Tomb, and Departed. This Was a Relationship Between Women, a Spiritual Connection.

    293

    ‘又尝宿太仓古庙。泥塑诸神像面日不完，颠倒颓墙断瓦之中。独廊下一皂隶像，执杖岿然。余戏曰：“穷途倦客，藉尔为东道主人，携有壶酒，当以酹尔。”因举壶灌之，问曰：　‘佳乎？”隶忽应曰：“佳！”余不觉惊跃却立，叱之曰：“鬼子敢尔，立斩尔首！”隶置杖叩头乞命曰：　‘素嗜麴蘖。且遇知己。不觉失言。幸宽假之，使得侍杯杓，亦不岑寂。”余佞其言，笑许之，对坐而饮。苦无饮器，各引颈就壶吸之，须臾酒涸。隶曰：“君与我钱，我能行沽。”解杖头付之，果沽酒至。又饮。

    Explanation

    293

    Once, I stayed overnight in an ancient temple in Taicang. The clay statues of the deities were incomplete, their faces worn and weathered, amidst the ruins of fallen walls and broken tiles. Only one servant figure stood alone in the corridor, holding a staff upright. I said to myself, "A weary traveler at the end of his journey, relying on you as a host, I bring a pot of wine and pour it as an offering to you." I then asked, "Is this good?" The servant suddenly replied, "Good!" I was startled and leaped back, shouting, "You dare to do this! I will behead you immediately!" The servant placed his staff down and knelt, begging for mercy. He said, "I have always loved alcohol, and now encountering a true friend, I unconsciously spoke out of turn. Please forgive me. Allow me to serve the cups and ladles, and it won't be so lonely." I found his words charming and laughed, agreeing to let him stay. We sat together and drank. There were no drinking vessels, so we each extended our necks to drink from the pot. Soon the wine was finished. The servant said, "Give me some money, and I can buy more wine." I gave him the money from the staff, and he indeed bought wine. We drank again.

    294

    隶先醉，哆口刺刺，谈幽冥荒怪之事。已而距跃曲踊，揶揄不已。余曳其髪以虐之，塌然卧地。余亦旋醉，遂卧其旁。比晓酒醒，隶执杖如故。余是日郊游至暮，复止庙中。隶欣然迎门，问：“有酒乎？”余答以钱尽，隶攒眉良久，　曰：　‘当谋之。”遂出。既而抱一瓮、置两爵以归。酌之，佳醖也。穷所自来，悄语曰：“盗诸酒肆耳。”余赏其解事，纵饮大醉。由是日以为常。一夜，求出盗酒，久而不反。余闷且倦，枕肱而卧。隶见于梦曰：“余盗发被执，投诸浊流矣。感恋高情，姑来相诀。”言讫而觉，凄然伤之，盖酒徒于是亡矣！
    　　“又尝探奇粤西，晚宿驿亭。长夜荒阒，星河到枕，徘徊而兴。遥闻吟咏声，清越宛委。细听之，其词曰：

    Explanation

    294

    The servant was already drunk, drooling and chattering incessantly, talking about strange and supernatural matters. Then, he jumped up and down, teasing and mocking without end. I pulled his hair to torment him, and he collapsed flat on the ground. I, too, soon became drunk and lay down beside him. By morning, when the wine had worn off, the servant held the stick as usual. That day, we went on an outing until dusk and returned to the temple. The servant greeted me with joy at the door, asking, "Do you have any wine?" I replied that I had spent all my money, and the servant furrowed his brow for a long time before saying, "We should plan it." He then went out. Soon after, he carried a jar and placed two cups before returning. When we tasted it, it was excellent wine. I inquired about its origin, and he whispered, "I stole it from the wine shop." I admired his cleverness and drank freely until very drunk. From then on, it became a daily routine. One night, he asked to go out and steal wine, and stayed away for a long time without returning. I felt bored and tired, lying with my head on my arm. In a dream, the servant appeared and said, "I was caught stealing and thrown into the muddy river. Grateful for your kindness, I come to bid farewell." After speaking, I woke up, feeling sad and mournful. Thus, the wine companion passed away!

    Once again, I ventured to explore the western region of Guangxi and stayed at an inn in the evening. It was a long, desolate night, and the Milky Way reached my pillow. I wandered and became inspired, hearing the sound of recitation far away. The voice was clear and melodious. Listening carefully, the words said:

    295

    ‘月黑万山迷，西风吹我出。
    　　平生短剑青，秋气侵人骨。”
    　　余诧曰：　‘鬼也！”又闻鬼吟曰：
    　　‘栖鹘叫层崖，乱星堕林莽。无入夜转深，岩洞孤泉响。”
    　　声渐逼近；徐步逆之，一鬼颀而膻，贸贸然来。余心颇慑，鬼笑曰：“余虽陈人，然不为俗大轻出，非魑魅之比。子何畏焉？生时雅好孤游，致死山谷，魂魄恋此不能去。向有萧生过此，与余交最笃。萧生去，寂寞至今，始得吾子，愿少留也！，余颔之，与坐亭础上，谈论甚畅，濒曙乃隐。余游山至暮，期于亭下，鬼又至，曰：“壮哉游也！虽然，蹊径不习，末穷幽险。今夜月辉竟天，某请为导，必有以娱子。”余欣然从之。鬼授双履，著之足轻，登陟巉岩，易若平地。每经峰峦洞壑，指示其名胜缕缕。至一古洞，中有老猿见客作礼。洞外小猿数十，争献山果，饱啖而反。如是十馀夕，乾糇告匮。始辞别。鬼恸哭送余。逾年复往，三宿亭下，鬼不至，其交遂绝。

    Explanation

    295

    On a moonless night, the mountains were shrouded in mist, and the west wind guided me out.
    With a short sword of green all my life, the autumn air penetrated my bones.
    I was startled and said, "A ghost!" Then I heard the ghost sing:
    "The perched falcon calls from the layered cliffs, stars fall into the dense forest. As night deepens, there is no one around, and the lone spring echoes in the rocky cave."
    The sound gradually approached; I walked slowly towards it, and a tall, clumsy ghost came forward. My heart was filled with fear, but the ghost laughed and said, "Though I am an old soul, I do not lightly venture into the mundane world; I am not comparable to ghosts or demons. Why should you fear me? In life, I had a preference for solitary travel, and I died in the mountains and valleys. My spirit lingers here unable to leave. There was once a man named Xiao who passed through here, and he was the closest friend I had. Since Xiao left, I have been lonely until now, and now I have met you. I wish you would stay a while!" I nodded in agreement, and we sat on the base of the pavilion, talking very pleasantly until dawn, when the ghost disappeared.
    I continued my mountain journey until dusk, meeting the ghost at the pavilion. The ghost said, "What a grand journey! However, the paths are unfamiliar, and the dangers are not yet explored. Tonight, the moonlight fills the sky; I will be your guide, and we will surely have a pleasant time." I happily followed. The ghost gave me a pair of shoes, and wearing them made me feel light on my feet. We climbed steep cliffs as easily as walking on flat ground. Every time we passed through peaks, valleys, and caves, the ghost pointed out the names and wonders of each place. We arrived at an ancient cave, where an old monkey greeted us with a bow. Outside the cave, dozens of small monkeys competed to offer mountain fruits, and we ate our fill and returned. For more than ten nights, our provisions ran out. We finally said our goodbyes. The ghost cried bitterly to see me off. More than a year later, I returned and stayed at the pavilion for three nights, but the ghost did not come, and our friendship thus ended.

    296

    “道士隐其姓，名希客。女子姓阮，名西娘。驿亭之鬼，姓练名嵌。”
    　　逆旅少年
    　　陈孝靡奇圣自京师之金陵制府，道遇骑使，一一亦往制府勾当公事者，——因同行。
    　　一日，解装逆旅，则北寝已有宿客，遂止东偏。顷见少年自外入，深笠宽衣，面掩于笠，不甚可辨，即北寝客也。趣召主人，使治具待客，必丰必洁。主人诺，去。少年入北寝，曲肱支颐，凝灯宴坐。陈亦遂就榻。而骑使素好选事，秣马储刍已，潜往窥之，伏于梁间。

    Explanation

    296

    "The Taoist monk concealed his surname, his name being Xi Ke. The woman's surname is Ruan, her name being Xi Niang. The ghost at the post station, whose surname is Lian and name is Jian.
    The young man at the inn. Chen Xiaomimi Qisheng, traveling from the capital to Jinling Prefecture, encountered a messenger on the road, each also went to the prefecture to handle official matters, thus they traveled together.
    One day, they dismounted and stayed at the inn, but the northern room already had a guest staying, so they stopped in the eastern side. Soon, they saw a young man from outside enter, wearing a deep hat and a wide robe, his face obscured by the hat, not very visible, he was the guest in the northern room. They quickly summoned the innkeeper, instructing him to prepare the best and cleanest accommodations for guests, it must be abundant and clean. The innkeeper nodded and left. The young man entered the northern room, bent his arm as a pillow, and sat in contemplation with the lamp burning. Chen also lay down on the bed. The messengers, who were always fond of observing, after feeding their horses and storing hay, secretly went to spy on them, hiding in the beams."

    297

    旅柝再鸣，欻见一物飞入，状类鵰鹗，乃觉室中鞺鞳，势若斗兵，灯影散乱，莫可端倪。顷之，有一人踣地，乃胖僧也。少年挥剑拟之曰：“若不自量，欲犯乃公耶？”僧固称‘不敢’，卑辞乞命。少年曰：“若虽不竞，然亦吾属也，羽翼可任，安肯相遇？”遂释之起。
    　　复仰视粱间，谓骑使曰：“君为壁上观，亦殊勇敢。宜下相见。”骑使方怖，闻言骇甚，趋下拜伏。少年并携就坐，呼主人将酒看来，相与飞觞大嚼。少年与僧语，骑使略不解，危坐倾听而已。
    　　酒酣，少年谓僧曰：“吾有尺书寄于某所，能为我鳞鸿乎？”僧曰：“能。”少年又曰：“三日后，会我某山，能不愆期乎？”僧曰：“谨如命。”少年即袖中出书付僧。僧便乞辞，复如鵰鹗飞去。
    　　骑使亦谢出，往卧东偏。天明迹之，北寝空矣。叩主人，亦弗知也。以状语陈，相与嗟异。计僧所寄书及所期之地，殆三四千里。骑使又言少年形貌，“玉立美如曲逆”云。

    Explanation

    297

    The bell sounded twice, and suddenly a creature flew in, resembling a large predatory bird. The room was filled with a resonant noise, as if a battle were taking place, and the light from the lamps scattered chaotically, offering no discernible clue. Soon after, a person fell to the ground, and it was a fat monk. The young man drew his sword and said, "If you do not know your limits, do you wish to challenge me?" The monk insisted, "I dare not," and pleaded humbly for his life. The young man replied, "Though you are not strong, you are also of our kind, and your support can be relied upon. How could I meet you?" He then released the monk and stood up.

    Looking up at the beam, he said to the rider, "You have been watching from the sidelines, showing remarkable bravery. You should come down and meet me." The rider was terrified and, upon hearing this, was even more alarmed. He hurried down and bowed. The young man took him by the hand and led him to sit, calling the host to bring wine. They drank and feasted together. The young man spoke with the monk, while the rider barely understood, sitting upright and listening intently.

    When the wine was flowing freely, the young man said to the monk, "I have a letter to send to a certain place. Can you carry it for me?" The monk replied, "I can." The young man then said, "In three days, meet me at a certain mountain. Can you not delay?" The monk replied, "I will follow your command." The young man took the letter out of his sleeve and gave it to the monk. The monk then asked for leave and flew away like a large predatory bird.

    The rider also thanked and left, going to sleep on the eastern side. The next morning, they searched for him, but the northern bed was empty. They asked the host, but he did not know. They told Chen about the situation, and they all marveled at it. Considering the place where the monk was to send the letter and the location of the meeting, it was likely thousands of miles away. The rider also described the young man's appearance, saying, "He stood tall and beautiful, like Zhang Liang."

    298

    影　娘
    　　青莲山秀削如花，清泉出其阴，淳而为沼，滃然澄沏。昔有士人春日陟山，倦憩水上，拾得一玉钗，把弄冥想。忽水中见丽女子影出其肩后，若相偎倚。惊而反顾，无有也。俄而微风皱波，滉漾久之，影遂失，叹诧而归。
    　　试一览镜，则女在镜中，倩辅流睐，士人悦之。出钗问之，曰；“此卿所贻耶？”女摇首微笑，徘徊却去，环佩珊珊作声。士人大惑，入以游语，女面颊发赤，敛然遂隐。士人急索视镜背，垂首怅惘。闻空中吃吃笑，微语曰：“左矣！”其声如箫管从风，微婉清妙，莫知其所自发也。
    　　士人彷徨四顾，神志散乱。又闻空中语曰：“苟无相谑，当见镜中，日一度。第焚沈水香，供钗其上，妾即至矣。”如教，果至，即相对琐琐语他事。朱唇微动，则声出镜中，词旨殊妙。其初一两时许便去。久之，语渐狎，女亦稍稍见答，迁延镜中不忍去。

    Explanation

    298

    Shadow Maiden
    In the spring, a scholar ascended the Green Lotus Mountain, which was as graceful and sharp as a flower. From the shaded side of the mountain, a clear spring flowed, becoming a marsh or pond, vast and clear. One day, weary from his climb, he rested by the water and picked up a jade hairpin, toying with it and lost in thought. Suddenly, he saw the reflection of a beautiful woman appear behind his shoulder, as if leaning against him. Startled, he looked back, but there was nothing there. Soon, a gentle breeze rippled the water, and for a long time, the reflection disappeared. He returned home, bewildered and astonished.

    He tried looking into a mirror, and there she was, in the mirror, with a charming gaze that captivated him. He took out the jade hairpin and asked, "Is this a gift from you?" The woman shook her head and smiled, then hesitated and departed, her jade ornaments making a soft, rhythmic sound. The scholar was deeply perplexed. When he recounted his experience to others, the woman's cheeks turned red, and she vanished. The scholar quickly turned to look at the back of the mirror, hanging his head in disappointment. He heard a soft laugh from the air, and a faint voice said, "You're wrong!" The sound was like a flute carried by the wind, delicate and enchanting, with no clear source.

    The scholar wandered around, his mind scattered. He heard another voice from the air say, "If you do not tease me, you will see me in the mirror once a day. Simply burn incense of agarwood, place the hairpin on it, and I will come." Following the instructions, the woman indeed appeared, and they conversed about trivial matters. Her lips moved slightly, and her voice came from the mirror, her words and intentions uniquely charming. At first, she would leave after a couple of hours. Over time, their conversation became more intimate, and the woman began to respond more, lingering in the mirror, reluctant to leave.

    299

    家人异其状，疑镜为妖，夺镜摔之地，镜裂，士人惊惋失声，曰：“伤我丽人！”亟取他镜注视，乃色喜曰：“幸无恙！”家人愈异甚，尽藏其镜，不使复得窥。士人忧闷，嗒焉如丧魂魄。
    　　偶于案上得芍药一枝，不知所从来，闻耳畔语曰：“君颇识此花名否？请西如圃中池上，与君别矣！”士人凄然，趋诣之，见女在水中，揽悌而歌曰：
    　　涓涓流泉，潋滟清池。
    　　灼彼镜光，影合形违。
    　　斯影斯幻，复能几时？
    　　春风告行，赠子将离。
    　　子不我思，思我其谁？
    　　子即我思，我胡能为。
    　　悠悠天地，两心知之。
    　　水流西东，永以为期！
    　　遂不复见。
    　　士人由是卧疾，废饮食，治以巫医，弗效也。有道士款关求见，自言知隐疾。家人见之，道士问士人：“玉钗安在？”士人瞿然曰：“公焉知此？”道士微笑，袖中取绛丹一粒，令吞之，病良已。

    Explanation

    299

    The family found his appearance strange and suspected the mirror was an evil spirit. They took the mirror and threw it on the ground, causing it to shatter. The scholar was shocked and lamented, losing his voice, and exclaimed, "It hurts my beautiful one!" He quickly took another mirror to look at it, and upon seeing that it was unharmed, he said with relief, "Fortunately, it is unharmed!" The family became even more suspicious and hid all of his mirrors, preventing him from seeing them again. The scholar fell into a deep depression, as if his soul had left his body.

    One day, he chanced upon a branch of peony on the table, not knowing where it came from. He heard a voice beside him say, "Do you recognize the name of this flower? Please go west to the garden and the pond, where I will bid you farewell!" The scholar was filled with sorrow and hurried to the garden and pond, where he saw a woman in the water. She clasped her hands and sang:

    "Softly flows the spring water, shimmering over the clear pond.
    The bright light of the mirror, its reflection and form apart.
    This reflection, this illusion, how long can it last?
    Spring breeze whispers of departure, I give you this farewell.
    If you do not think of me, who will think of me?
    If you think of me, what can I do?
    Vast are the heavens and earth, only our hearts know.
    The water flows east and west, forever our bond!"

    Afterward, she was no longer seen.

    The scholar fell ill thereafter, stopped eating and drinking, and was treated by a shaman and a doctor, but it was ineffective. A Taoist priest knocked on the door to request an audience, claiming to know his hidden illness. The family saw him, and the priest asked the scholar, "Where is the jade hairpin?" The scholar was startled and asked, "How do you know this?" The priest smiled and took a red pill from his sleeve, instructing him to swallow it. The illness was cured.

    300

    道士谓曰：“君前身为诸生，过邻家，值其女影娘坠钗帘下。瞰其无人，径拾之不还，由是相慕悦。女死，念钗坠人间，业缘未了，求得之，转以贻君。而格于形迹，弗能合，又惧为君害，故去之。行而丐我，我怜其情挚，故来。”因出一小瓶授之，曰：“当以某日如青莲山，见梅花树上有翠鸟千百飞翔，乃捧瓶西向，立呼‘来来来’者三。当有所遇。”遂辞去。
    　　至期，士人如言往呼，乃见紫烟一缕入瓶中。闻瓶中语曰：“来矣！”即怀瓶趋归，置室中。顷刻，瓶大数抱，中辟一户，有丽人姗姗而出，即昔之水中镜中人也。道士旋来抚瓶曰：“几坏我器。”瓶即小如初，纳袖中，倏然已杳。女谓士人曰：“道士盖申元之也。”
    　　朱　克
    　　宛平朱克，感疾昼寝，梦游河滨，眺望甚乐。有四马服车东来，车中五人，御者一。御者曰：“某贵家张乐召客，但有知者皆得往。汝盍俱焉？”五人者亦呼“亟登”。克不觉已在车上。

    Explanation

    300

    The Taoist priest said, "In your previous life as a scholar, you passed by a neighbor's house and saw the girl Yingniang drop her hairpin under the curtain. Seeing no one, you picked it up and did not return it. From then on, you admired and liked each other. After the girl died, she remembered the hairpin still on earth, and their karmic connection was not yet complete. She sought to retrieve it and give it to you. Bound by physical constraints, you could not be together, and feared that it would harm you, so she left. She went and asked me, and I was moved by her sincere feelings, so I came." He then took out a small bottle and gave it to you, saying, "On a certain day, go to Qinglian Mountain, see the green birds flying in thousands and hundreds on the plum tree, then hold the bottle facing west and call 'come, come, come' three times. You will meet someone." And then he left.

    On the appointed day, the scholar went as instructed and called, and saw a wisp of purple smoke enter the bottle. Hearing the bottle say "I am here," he immediately held the bottle and hurried home, placing it in the room. In a moment, the bottle grew to several times its size, with a door in the middle, and a beautiful woman emerged gracefully, who was the same as the one he saw in water and in the mirror. The Taoist priest came back and touched the bottle, saying "I almost broke my vessel." The bottle immediately shrank back to its original size, was put into his sleeve, and vanished suddenly. The woman told the scholar, "The Taoist priest is really helping us."

    Zhu Ke of Wanping, feeling ill, slept during the day and dreamed of walking by the riverbank, enjoying the view greatly. A four-horse carriage came from the east, with five people inside and one driver. The driver said, "A noble family is hosting a musical gathering and inviting guests. Anyone who knows about it can attend. Why don't you join us?" The five people also called out, "Hurry up and get on!" Unconsciously, Zhu Ke found himself already on the carriage.

    301

    疾驱而行。
    　　抵一处，嘈杂类市镇。西有崇台，优伶演剧其上，其下列席焉。宾客甚盛。克与五人亦共据一席，酒馔随至，亦不知主人谁也。克素不习酒，又念抱病未瘳，绝不敢饮澉。而五人饕餮弥甚，杯盘尽罄。御者促曰：“可归矣。”复上车驰行。
    　　少焉皆下，入一家，则克之姨之家也。御者直拥至犬窝。递推五人仆，皆成小犬。次至克，克恐，欲走避，亦为所推，大呼昏去。比苏，则身亦犬矣。遍体痛楚，母犬为舔之，痛顿止。姨至，见之，曰：“犬产六子矣。”克呼姨，弗应。复诉曰：“我，尔甥也，非犬也。”姨亦弗闻，遂入内。群狗皆争食母乳。
    　　克亦觉乳香，饥欲食，复念：“我人也，奈何食狗乳？”欲趋归，足弱小能运。强行数步，母犬辄衔至故处。中心凄然自伤，遂为异物也。

    301

    Rushing and traveling, they arrived at a place that was noisy like a market town. To the west, there was an elevated platform where actors were performing plays, and rows of seats were arranged below. The guests were very numerous. Kè and five others also occupied a seat together, and wine and dishes arrived shortly, though they did not know who the host was. Kè had never been accustomed to drinking alcohol, and thinking of his ongoing illness, he absolutely dared not drink. Meanwhile, the five others greedily ate even more, and their plates and cups were all empty. The driver urged, saying, “We can go back now.” They got back on the carriage and rushed again.

    After a short while, everyone got off and entered a house, which was the home of Kè's aunt. The driver directly led them to the dog's den. One by one, the five people were pushed down and turned into small dogs. Then it came to Kè, who was afraid and wanted to run away, but he was also pushed by them, shouted loudly, and fainted. After regaining consciousness, he found that he too had become a dog. All over his body was in pain, and the mother dog licked him, and the pain suddenly stopped. The aunt arrived and saw him, saying, “The dog gave birth to six puppies.” Kè called out to his aunt, but she did not respond. He then said, “I am your nephew, not a dog.” The aunt also did not hear, and she went inside. All the dogs competed to eat the mother's milk.

    Kè also felt the milk was fragrant and was hungry and wanted to eat, but he thought, “I am a human, how can I eat dog milk?” He wanted to rush back, but his feet were weak and unable to move. He forced himself to walk a few steps, but the mother dog would carry him back to the original place. Heartfelt sorrow and self-pity filled him, and thus he became a different creature.

    302

    忽见御者踉跄来，颇惶恐。旋有二役追至，以锒铛系其颈，叱曰：“何物小鬼，敢私诱人为畜耶？”一役牵御者奔去，一役举克而掌之。昏痛之际，倏已复故身。即随役至家，为所仆，瞿然而悟。
    　　母、妻环之哭，盖死焉而复苏也。亟述其事，使验诸姨家。犬果产六子，其一牡犬者死矣，黑首而赤身，与克冠服之色相符也。克取死犬归，裹以布而埋之。
    　　非非子曰：跬步不谨，即堕畜生道，微乎危哉！幸地府敏察，复其故我。不然，虽欲不为犬于也得乎？
    　　六脚骨
    　　三江口富翁某，延师教其子。授以《三字经》首句，依师读之，亦能成诵。试使独诵，则函胡，似云‘六脚骨”。使诵次句亦然。以至他书字句不符者，皆曰‘六脚骨”，盖奇鲁也。娶妇甚艾，竟不能人道，姒续以斩。然善博善窃，曲尽其妙。卒倾巨万之家。殆夙业云。

    Explanation

    302

    Suddenly, the charioteer staggered and came, quite frightened. Soon, two officers arrived, chaining his neck with shackles, and shouted, "What kind of little ghost, dare to privately entice people into beasts?" One officer dragged the charioteer away, while another raised Kè and struck him. In the midst of dizziness and pain, suddenly he returned to his original body. He immediately followed the officers home, fell unconscious, and suddenly woke up.

    His mother and wife surrounded him and cried, for he had died and then revived. He quickly recounted the story, to verify at the aunt's house. The dog indeed gave birth to six puppies, one male dog died, black head and red body, matching the color of Kè's clothes. Kè took the dead dog back, wrapped it in cloth, and buried it.

    Feifei said: "A single step not careful, one falls into the animal realm, how perilous it is! Fortunately, the underworld was quick to investigate and restored his original self. Otherwise, how could one avoid becoming a dog?"

    Six Leg Bones

    A rich man in Sanjiangkou invited a teacher to educate his son. Taught the first line of the Sanzi Jing, followed the teacher's reading, and could also recite it. When tested to recite alone, it was unclear, as if saying 'six leg bones.' When reciting the second line, it was the same. Until other books' phrases did not match, all said 'six leg bones,' for it was a strange and clumsy recitation. He married a wife who was very beautiful, but could not perform sexual intercourse, the sister-in-law continued to divorce. But he was good at gambling and theft, mastering all the tricks. Finally, he depleted a household worth millions. Perhaps it was his karma.

    303

    刘　生
    　　刘生，三十游庠序，遂得狂疾。逢人辄谩骂，人无有伍之者。
    　　偶访远亲，归迷失道，遇瞽叟坐山下，交手而倚杖于肩，意甚得也。刘漫叩之，叟挥其杖曰：“南。”刘乃南行三四里，幽篁苍翠，溪水埩淙鸣，讶非故道。
    　　于时日既曛矣，颇惶惑，遥见三四丫头女子戏竹林间，——眉目竟秀，不可描画。——取竹叶扭结作舟，投溪水中，共叉手憨笑。刘以失道告，一女子敛笑睨视曰：“客何为者！闺中人岂孤竹马哉？”刘心好之，故故请指迷，迁延不去。一女子谓众曰：“归耳归耳，莫听烦絮。”遂相携穿竹林中去。
    　　刘尾之，数十武，见小村落。众女子入一白板屋，返身见刘，相与嬉笑耳语，扉遽阖。刘径叩之，意甚肆。良久，有妪出应，问：“伺处恶郎子挝门欲碎？”刘答以求宿。妪曰：“家有主翁，请自见之。”言已自去。

    Explanation

    303

    Liu Sheng
    Liu Sheng, at thirty, roamed the schools, and thus contracted a mad illness. Encountering people, he would curse them, and no one would associate with him.
    Once visiting distant relatives, he returned lost and off the path, and met an old blind man sitting at the foot of the mountain, with his hands crossed and leaning on his staff, seeming very content. Liu casually asked him, and the old man waved his staff and said, "South." Liu then walked south for several miles, through deep bamboo groves that were lush, and the stream murmured, and he was surprised to find it was not the old path.
    The sun had already set, and he was quite confused, seeing a few young girls playing in the bamboo grove. Their eyebrows and eyes were so beautiful, they could not be described. They took bamboo leaves and twisted them into boats, threw them into the stream, and laughed together. Liu told them he was lost, and one girl suppressed her laughter and said, "What kind of guest are you! Are you some sort of solitary bamboo horse?" Liu liked her, so he repeatedly asked for directions and lingered. One girl said to the others, "Go home, go home, don't listen to his chatter," and they then went together through the bamboo grove.
    Liu followed them for several steps and saw a small village. The girls entered a white board house, turned back to see Liu, and laughed and whispered among themselves, then quickly closed the door. Liu knocked directly on the door, his intentions quite bold. After a long time, an old woman came out and asked, "What scoundrel is pounding the door to break it down?" Liu answered that he was seeking shelter. The old woman said, "There is a master at home, please see him for yourself," and then left.

    304

    刘登其堂，粉壁纸窗，甚雅洁。一老人拥杖枯坐，灯影中就视，即向所遇瞽叟也。怪而问之，三问而不答。刘怒骂曰：“瞽奴给我失道，何得复尔？”叟曰：“我奚瞽哉？迷道者真瞽耳！尔目诚在，且何为问我？”刘语塞，忿然径出。
    　　时月弦云翳，万星隐曜，俯不见地，举步辄绊踬。遽返，则门己扃矣。颇悔前倨，而耻于再通，便就檐下倚坐。少焉细雨刺面，衣絮渐寒。闻门内莺语间关，笙簧满耳。徐察之，乃女子读书声，念欲一进观，且便图寄宿，计亦良得。
    　　遂复叩门，前妪启门问，刘掩入，诡辞以对，而讳言前客。妪曰：“止止！君适至此，何诳也？须重启主翁。”乃趋上堂。刘从户下窥见叟南向据案坐，两眼碧光与灯烛相映射。女子十馀人，罗坐左右诵书，溪上数女亦厕其列。始知叟盖非瞽者，心窃窃惊讶。妪前白叟，叟曰：“是故为求宿来也。东厢一席地，姑为之所。”

    Explanation

    304

    Liu ascended to the hall, where the walls were whitewashed and the windows papered, creating a very elegant and clean atmosphere. An old man leaned on a cane and sat motionless. In the light of the lamp, Liu looked closely and recognized the blind old man he had previously encountered. Finding it strange, he asked him three times, but received no answer. Liu angrily shouted, "You blind servant, how could you lead me astray again?" The old man replied, "Why am I blind? Those who are lost are truly blind! Your eyes are truly there, so why are you asking me?" Liu was speechless and angrily left.

    At that time, the moon was in its crescent phase, the sky was cloudy, and the stars were hidden. He could not see the ground below, and every step caused him to stumble. He hurriedly returned, but the door was already locked. He felt somewhat regretful for his previous arrogance and ashamed to return again, so he sat leaning against the eaves. Soon after, a fine rain pricked his face, and his clothes became increasingly cold. He heard the chirping of birds inside the door, and the sound of flutes and bells filled his ears. He carefully observed and realized it was the sound of women reading. He thought about entering to see and also planned to stay overnight, thinking the plan was quite good.

    So he knocked on the door again. The old woman opened the door and asked, and Liu entered stealthily, lying in response and avoiding mentioning the previous guest. The old woman said, "Stop! You just arrived here, why are you lying? It must be reopened to the master." So he hurried upstairs. Liu peeked through the door and saw the old man sitting facing south at a table, his eyes glowing green and reflecting the light of the lamps. Over ten women sat around reading books, and several women from the stream were also among them. He then realized the old man was not actually blind, and he was secretly amazed. The old woman went forward to inform the old man, who said, "He came to ask for lodging. There is a place in the east wing, let him stay there."

    305

    刘因登堂，欲自陈谢。叟便闭其目，书声亦遽止。视叟，瞆眊如前矣。刘益怪之，问叟曰：“丈人之目何其异也？”叟曰：“老朽失明，何足为异？”刘诣观诸女所读书，则皆掩之。问何书，叟曰：“此不足为子道也。昔山人遗我此笈，惟妮子辈能读之。请便栖止，勿复见诘。”语次，女子皆已趋入内，叟亦策杖起。刘不得已，诣东厢，而堂上之烛灭矣。
    　　东厢寝具颇设，便昏然就睡。中夜闻虎啸声，忽复惊觉，起，窥见堂上光明如昼，一虎蹲阶下，有大鸟群舞庭中。女子皆华妆，次第乘鸟，从檐间翔去。最后妪出，骑虎将行。闻叟笑谓曰：“诸妮子游戏甚乐，奈老夫何？”妪曰：“勿复作龙钟态，且偕往耳。”叟因掷其杖，化为白龙，天矫而下。叟跃登龙背，乃朗目修眉，面如傅粉，少年人也。妪亦少女耳。龙虎相逐，凌空而逝。

    Explanation

    305

    Liu ascended the hall, intending to present himself for explanation or apology. The old man closed his eyes, and the sound of reading stopped abruptly. Liu looked at the old man and saw that he was still blind. Liu became more puzzled and asked the old man, "What is so strange about your eyes, sir?" The old man replied, "I am old and blind; what is there to be strange about that?" Liu went to see what the girls were reading, but they all covered their books. When he asked what they were reading, the old man said, "This is not something I can tell you about. In the past, a recluse gave me this book, and only the young girls can read it. Please settle down and don't ask any more questions." As they spoke, the girls had already gone inside, and the old man stood up with his staff. Liu, having no choice, went to the east wing, and the hall's candle went out.

    The sleeping arrangements in the east wing were quite set up, and Liu fell asleep. In the middle of the night, he heard the sound of a tiger roaring and woke up suddenly. He got up and peeped, seeing the hall as bright as day, with a tiger crouching on the steps and large birds dancing in the courtyard. The girls, all beautifully dressed, one by one rode the birds and flew away from the eaves. Finally, the old woman came out, riding a tiger and about to depart. Liu heard the old man laugh and say, "The girls are having so much fun; what can I do about it, old man?" The old woman replied, "Don't act like an old man; let's go together." The old man then threw his staff, which turned into a white dragon and soared down gracefully. The old man leaped onto the dragon's back and transformed into a handsome young man with bright eyes, long eyebrows, and a face like powdered white. The old woman was also a young girl. The dragon and tiger chased each other and flew away into the air.

    306

    刘汗浃如蒸，始敢纵息，乃知叟亦并非叟。而视己身，乃在破舟中，并无篙楫。烟波空蒙，惟其所之，肃然悲恐。倏有名而呼之者，则舟已触岸。舍舟亟登，杳然无人。仰视疎星缀天，残蟾未堕，树杪群鸦，犹在梦中也。徐有光一缕，起于林腰，望而即之，砰然下坠，若在陷穽。举目如漆，俯仰略无所见。以手四扪，杳无崖壁。且扪且行，亦无窒碍。大呼叫绝，亦无应者。历时甚久，历路甚远，不知其为南北而为东西也，不知其为昼而为夜也，不知其为明而为幽也。既困且饥，念必无生理。而困极饥极，卒亦不死。颓然僵卧，不复能行，因默念瞽叟神人，何乃困我？

    Explanation

    306

    Drenched in sweat like steam, he finally dared to take a deep breath, then realized that the old man was not actually an old man. Looking at himself, he found himself in a broken boat, with no oars or paddles. The misty waters were hazy, and he drifted wherever they took him, suddenly filled with fear and sorrow. Suddenly, someone called out his name, and the boat had already hit the shore. He quickly got out of the boat and found himself in a deserted place, completely empty of people. Looking up, he saw sparse stars scattered across the sky, and the waning moon had not yet set. Crowds of crows on the tree branches were still asleep. Gradually, a faint ray of light appeared from the middle of the forest. He looked toward it and felt a thud as if he were falling into a pit. His vision was as dark as pitch, and he saw nothing above or below. Feeling his way around, he found no cliffs or walls. As he continued to feel his way and move forward, there were no obstacles. He called out loudly, but there was no response. He wandered for a long time, traveling a great distance, not knowing whether it was north and south or east and west, not knowing whether it was day or night, not knowing whether it was bright or dark. Both tired and hungry, he thought there was no chance of survival. But even in his extreme exhaustion and hunger, he did not die. He collapsed and lay motionless, unable to move, and silently reflected on the blind old man, a divine being, and wondered why he had been put in such a plight.

    307

    旋觉有物上拂其顶，引手探之，乃一垂绳，急捉绳结于带间，蛛挽而上。挽且千寻，绳卒不尽，即又惴惴，不敢释手。缒悬良久，力竭握弛，昏然复堕，遂闻人语其旁曰：“是刘生也，何以在此？”刘因张目，则天地云日、山川人物，昭然复见，若矇之始发而梦初觉也。其语者刘之邻人，其地则刘所居郭外耳。掖之至家，计失道时已三十馀日。刘自是恂恂谦让，乡里称之。

    Explanation

    307

    Suddenly, he felt something brushing the top of his head, and he stretched out his hand to feel it. It was a hanging rope. He quickly grabbed the rope and tied it to his belt. A spider then pulled him up. They ascended for what seemed like a thousand fathoms, but the rope finally ran out. He became anxious and dared not let go. He was suspended for a long time, his strength failing and his grip loosening. He fell unconscious and dropped. Then he heard someone speaking beside him, saying, "It's Liu, why are you here?" Liu opened his eyes, and then the sky, clouds, sun, mountains, rivers, and people became clearly visible again, as if waking from a long sleep and just emerging from a dream. The person speaking was Liu's neighbor, and the place was just outside Liu's village. They led him home, and he calculated that he had been lost for more than thirty days. From then on, Liu became modest and yielding, and the villagers praised him.

    308

    借金人
    　　康熙中，有图公某者，厚于赀财。其母氏八十初度日，置酒张乐，召客为寿。
    　　是夕客散，图归私室，将寝，而屋瓦作声，声及于地。俄有一人排扉入，躯貌侈伟，巾服皆青，露刃于腰间，屹立于灯下。图骇怖失色，请问所欲。其人曰：“闻公多精金，姑以百两贷我，旬日当还也。”图曰：“敬诺。”顾侍儿取金授之，其人乃出门升屋而去。图惊甚，嘱家人秘之。
    　　越数日，灯下独饮，其人忽复至。图亟问何来，曰：“来还金耳。”遂出金。图曰：“金已赠壮士，不欲还也。”其人笑曰：“吾亦有少金，不知其品高下，故借公好金较量耳。非剠公金者。”图因曰：“壮士能饮乎？”曰：“能。”即满引奉之。立竭数觥。乃辞去，曰；“感公厚谊。公此时无所用我，他日当相报。”言讫已逝。图异而叹曰：“真奇士也！”

    Explanation

    308

    Lending Gold to a Man
    　　During the reign of Emperor Kangxi, there was a man named Tu Gong, who was wealthy. On the day of his mother's eightieth birthday, he set up wine and music, invited guests, and celebrated her life.
    　　That night, after the guests had left, Tu returned to his private room, ready to sleep, when he heard a sound from the roof tiles, which echoed down to the ground. Soon, a man pushed open the door and entered, his figure tall and imposing, dressed entirely in blue, with a blade visible at his waist, standing firmly under the lamp. Tu was terrified and pale, asking what the man wanted. The man said, "I heard that you have much fine gold. I would like to borrow a hundred taels from you, and I will return it in ten days." Tu replied, "I agree." He asked the maidservant to fetch the gold and give it to the man, who then left the room, climbed onto the roof, and disappeared. Tu was greatly shocked and instructed his family to keep it a secret.
    　　After several days, while drinking alone by the light of the lamp, the man suddenly appeared again. Tu quickly asked why he had come, and the man said, "I have come to return the gold." He then handed over the gold. Tu said, "I have already given the gold to the brave man and do not wish to take it back." The man smiled and said, "I also have some gold, but I do not know its quality, so I borrowed your fine gold to compare it. I did not intend to steal your gold." Tu then asked, "Can the brave man drink?" The man replied, "Yes." Tu immediately poured a full cup and offered it to him. They drank several cups in quick succession. The man then bid farewell, saying, "I am grateful for your kindness. At this time, I have no use for you, but I will repay you in the future." With that, he disappeared. Tu was amazed and exclaimed, "Truly a remarkable man!"

    309

    后数年，图被罪在狱，资财藉入官，妻孥各食其力，相随一老仆而己。一夜，有搴帘入者，负一囊置地，拜曰：“公尚识某否？”图诧曰：“甚忆，然忘之。”曰：“畴昔贷金主人也。”即解囊，出衣裘二袭。白金五百两相授，曰：“知公困乏，聊以相助，且酬旧德。”图感泣称谢，曰：“义士惠我于穷，幸不死，宜何如报？敢问姓字？”其人答曰：“吾无姓名久矣。向尝借公金，即呼钱‘借金人’可耳。”又曰：“圄扉严邃，吾往来幸无觉者，公宜密之。倘泄于左右，此金非公有矣。”遂别而去。

    Explanation

    309
