    Several years later, Tu was imprisoned for a crime, and his wealth was confiscated by the government. His wife and children supported themselves, accompanied only by one old servant. One night, a person entered by lifting the curtain, carrying a bag and placing it on the ground. He bowed and said, "Do you still remember me?" Tu was startled and replied, "I remember very well, but I forgot his name." The visitor said, "I am the person who lent you money in the past." He immediately opened the bag and took out two sets of clothes, handing over five hundred taels of silver, and said, "Knowing you were in distress, I came to help, and also to repay your kindness." Tu was moved to tears and thanked him, saying, "The righteous man helped me when I was in dire straits. I am fortunate to be alive, so how should I repay him? I dare to ask for your name?" The visitor replied, "I have no name for a long time. I once borrowed money from you, so just call me 'Borrowed Gold Man.'" He then added, "The prison is strict and secluded. I come and go without being noticed. You should keep this secret. If it is revealed to others, this money will no longer be yours." He then parted and left.

    310

    图后经赦出狱，卒衣食其金以终。
    　　虎
    　　圆明园离宫外，虎圈三区，坎地为之，覆以铁网。有户，可梯而下也。旁各为小柙，限以木闸。上为辘轳抽屉之。虎奴将粪除，则启闸；俟虎入小柙，则闭之。故虎奴下上卒不与虎遇。
    　　有某奴役此有年矣，亦渐狎易。一日启闸，虎入，闭闸不尽者去地尺有咫，弗觉也。既下而虎出，将咥之。奴震慴流汗，跽而请曰：“若啖我，即啖。苟念喂养之恩，毋啖我，若即入，毋恐我。”虎闻言，踌躇不决。奴又哀乞，于是虎竟入柙，不复顾。奴乃缘梯上，几堕者三四。

    Explanation

    310

    After being pardoned, he was released from prison and eventually lived off the money until the end.

    Tiger

    Outside the imperial garden of the Summer Palace, there were three enclosures for tigers, dug into the ground and covered with iron nets. There were doors that could be descended by ladder. On the sides were smaller cages, separated by wooden gates. Above were pulleys that operated the gates. When the tiger attendants cleaned, they would open the gates; after the tigers entered the smaller cages, the gates would be closed. Thus, the tiger attendants never encountered the tigers.

    There was a certain attendant who had been working there for many years and gradually became more familiar with the tigers. One day, when the gate was opened, a tiger entered, and the gate was not fully closed, leaving a gap of about a foot. The attendant did not notice. As he descended, the tiger emerged and was about to attack him. The attendant trembled with fear, sweating profusely, and knelt down to beg. "If you eat me, then eat. If you remember the kindness of feeding you, do not eat me. If you do eat me, do not be afraid of me." The tiger, upon hearing this, hesitated. The attendant pleaded again, and the tiger finally entered the cage, no longer looking back. The attendant then climbed the ladder, nearly falling several times.

    311

    官日供羊一头，为诸虎俸。于是奴德此虎，饲之往往倍他虎。遇有事杀虎，奴率以他虎应。此虎以奴庇，老且寿焉。后圈虎且尽，将杀虎，无代者，不得已，槛此虎以往。奴痛哭送之，谓虎曰：“命也夫！奈何？”虎既殪，奴亦寻毙，
    　　靖安有虎，夜跃上人家屋。虎重屋弱，四足陷椽中，大吼，栋宇撼动。其处者为二女子，盖姑嫂也。闻屋上虎声，仰见虎足，走且恐。久之，虎不能去。姑嫂相与谋曰：“此易制也，不图且有患，盍决其蹯？”乃共梯上粱间，以绳絷虎足，其姑持而引之，搜斫以伐薪之斧。四足次第解下，不及天明，虎竟卒。
    　　皮先生
    　　有皮先生者，鲁而好奇。慕愚公之移山也，而曰：“愚公年九十，我始半之。太行、王屋，若是其高且广也，而可移，我直试其少者？”

    Explanation

    311

    The government provided a daily supply of one sheep as a form of "salary" for the tigers. The slave favored this particular tiger and often fed it double the amount given to the other tigers. When it was necessary to kill a tiger, the slave would usually substitute another. This tiger benefited from the slave's protection and lived long and old. Later, when the tigers in the enclosure were almost all gone, and it was time to kill the remaining tiger, there was no one to replace it. Reluctantly, they put this tiger in a cage and took it away. The slave cried bitterly as he saw it off, saying to the tiger, "Fate! What can be done?" After the tiger had died, the slave soon died as well.

    In Jing'an, there was a tiger that leaped onto a house at night. The weight of the tiger caused the weak structure of the house to give way, and its four paws got stuck in the rafters. The tiger roared loudly, causing the building to shake. The occupants of the house were two women, who were aunt and niece. They heard the tiger's roar and saw its paws from above, running away in fear. After a while, the tiger could not move. The aunt and niece discussed what to do, saying, "It would be easy to control the tiger, but if we don't act, we might face danger. Let's cut off its paw." They climbed up to the beams and used ropes to bind the tiger's paws. The aunt held and pulled the tiger while they used an axe to chop and cut. The four paws were removed one by one. Before dawn, the tiger finally died.

    There was a man named Mr. Pi, who was simple-minded but curious. He admired the story of Yu Gong moving the mountains and said, "Yu Gong was ninety years old, and I am only half that age. The Taihang and Wangwu mountains are so high and wide, yet they can be moved. I will just try to move the smaller ones."

    312

    野有丘。率二子荷畚操箕，旦夕垦之，而欲以徙之。或笑止之曰：“愚公事，《列子》寓言耳。且彼为其塞出入之迂也，故欲平其险。今此培塿者，处于广漠之墟，居焉而不障于庐，行焉而不壅于途，葬焉面不窒干墓，种莳焉而不浸子之田壤，亦既避子之巧矣。子何为者？且是丘也，高仅可隐。广不及数亩之宫，平之不足以夸力，去之不足以鸣能，过之者如去毛髪焉。而曾莫之知也，子何为者？”弗听。垦之期月，运土于溪中，溪为之塞。
    　　是岁旱，溪涸。溪右之农，其田弗溉焉，苗且槁。以先生　绝其源也，相与哄诸其门。先生惧，亟出溪中土置溪上，复成一丘。见者笑谓先生曰：“此夸娥氏之力也！”
    　　王大胆
    　　康龙山言：有王大胆者，习爰书。佐某廉访于苏州，豪于胆，群呼“王大胆”。同舍诸客欲试之，恐以鬼，弗畏也。又伪为鬼冠服面目者，伺诸幽而乘诸忽，王遇之，言笑自若。人以是信王之果大胆也。

    Explanation

    312

    There was a mound in the wilderness. He led his two sons, carrying baskets and shovels, diligently cultivating it day and night, wishing to move it away. Someone laughed and stopped him, saying, "The story of Yu Gong, a fable in Liezi. And he wanted to level the winding path in and out, so he wished to level the danger. Now this small mound, situated in the vast wilderness, living there without obstructing the house, walking there without blocking the road, burying there without obstructing the grave, planting there without flooding your fields, it has already avoided your cleverness. What are you doing? Moreover, this mound is high enough to hide, wide enough to cover several acres, leveling it is not enough to boast of strength, removing it is not enough to show ability, passing by it is like removing a hair, and no one knows about it. What are you doing?" He did not listen. He cultivated it for a month, transporting the soil into the stream, which was blocked as a result.
    That year was dry, and the stream dried up. The farmers on the right side of the stream, their fields were not irrigated, and the crops were about to wither. Because the master blocked its source, they shouted at his door together. The master was afraid, quickly took the soil from the stream and placed it back, restoring the mound. Those who saw it laughed and said to the master, "This is the power of Kua E!"
    Wang Dàndǎn
    Kang Longshan said: There was a man named Wang Dàndǎn who was familiar with legal documents. He assisted a censor in Suzhou, was courageous, and everyone called him "Wang Dàndǎn." His fellow guests wanted to test him, fearing ghosts but not fearing them. They also disguised as ghosts with hats, clothes, and faces, lurking in the dark and taking advantage of the sudden moment. Wang met them and spoke and laughed as usual. People thus believed Wang was indeed very brave.

    313

    他日，诸客游郊外。见丐者状绝丑恶，皆笑曰：“此真鬼，盍致之出试王大胆？”佥曰：“善！”召丐者与归。赐之钱而告之谋。丐者辞曰：“丐小人也，胡敢然？惧获罪焉。”再三喻之而后许。
    　　是夜，王归寝，犹阅牍灯下，不遽寝。诸客亦已伏丐者床下，隙窗而伺之。夜将半，丐者出，则左手持牌，右锒铛，如世皂隶状，前而咄王曰：“去！去！”王见则大嘶，仰而踣。诸客亦惊，趋视，王大胆已绝矣。而丐者亦忽失所在。乃皆诧曰：“此真鬼！”
    　　罗台山
    　　罗台山有高，江右人。尝察孝廉，不汲汲仕进。抗志读书，以博雅闻海内。能拳勇，善击剑，风流隽爽，殊有奇气。好购　买古器。鼎彝、权律之属，充列几案。又好蓄奇石，有奇章南宫之癖。数千里外，辄独行无仆从。

    Explanation

    313

    One day, the guests were traveling outside the city. They saw a beggar whose appearance was extremely ugly and repulsive, and they all laughed and said, "This is truly a ghost. Why not bring him out to test Wang Da Dan?" Everyone agreed, saying, "Good!" They summoned the beggar and brought him back. They gave him money and told him their plan. The beggar declined, saying, "I am a beggar, how dare I do such a thing? I fear I will be punished." After repeatedly explaining to him, he finally agreed.

    That night, Wang returned to his room, still reading documents by the lamp, not immediately going to sleep. The guests had also hidden under the beggar's bed, peering through a crack in the window to watch. When the night was halfway through, the beggar came out, holding a tablet in his left hand and a chain in his right, looking like a world-renowned constable. He approached and shouted at Wang, "Go! Go!" Wang saw him and let out a loud cry, faced upwards, and fell. The guests were also shocked, rushing to see, and found that Wang Da Dan was already dead. The beggar also suddenly disappeared. They all exclaimed, "This is truly a ghost!"

    Luo Taishan

    Luo Taishan, a man from Jiangxi, once passed the examination for孝廉 but was not eager to advance in his career. He upheld lofty aspirations while reading, known throughout the land for his broad learning and erudition. He was skilled in martial arts and proficient in swordsmanship, graceful and spirited, with a unique and extraordinary quality. He enjoyed collecting ancient artifacts, such as tripods, ritual vessels, and other such items, which filled his tables. He also enjoyed collecting strange stones, with a passion for rare stones like those of Qi Zhang and Nan Gong. He would travel thousands of miles away, always alone without servants.

    314

    尝慕蜀中瞿塘、滟滪、峨眉、剑阁山水之雄险，束装独游。比返，载石盈舟，如百万金宝，压舟欲沉坠。处石以囊，其上者囊以缣帛。坐舟中捧运摩挲，终日不体。
    　　舟子窃窥视，以为财也，夜相与谋议杀客而分其有。舟子四：一老翁，其二为翁子，一为佣者。二子及佣者窃窃语，翁入问，初皆秘之。翁诘不已，始以所谋告。翁惊曰：“噫！恶乎可？”二子曰：“厚利也，且易而无祸，伺葸焉？”翁不能止，叹息去。
    　　时罗己寝，忽惊觉，袖而占之，知有杀机，潜起，属耳察之审，还就枕。
    　　后数日薄暮，舟泊荒江丛苇间，其侪请曰：“今者享神介福，愿以馂馀为客寿，客其无辞！”罗曰：“甚善。”舟子喜，以酒肴进。罗知其酒鸩也，置不饮。舟子阴异之，然欺其独夜，度无所避匿，亦不固强。罗自出绍兴酿一瓮，倾杯大嚼。瓮几罄，伪醉，据榻灭烛寝。

    Explanation

    314

    Luo had long admired the majestic and perilous landscapes of the Chongqing region, including the Qutang Gorge, Yaniyu, Emei Mountain, and Jianmen Pass, and decided to pack his belongings and travel alone. Upon his return, he loaded the boat with stones, which weighed as heavily as a million pieces of gold and treasure, pressing down on the boat and almost causing it to sink. He stored the stones in bags, with the finer ones kept in silk and brocade. Sitting in the boat, he held and handled them, all day without feeling tired.

    The boatmen secretly peered at the cargo, thinking it was treasure, and at night they plotted together to kill the guest and divide his possessions. There were four boatmen: one old man, his two sons, and one hired worker. The two sons and the hired worker whispered together. The old man entered and asked, but at first they all kept it secret. The old man persisted in questioning, and only then did they reveal their plan. The old man was shocked and said, "Ah! How could this be done?" The two sons said, "It's a great profit, and easy with no danger, what's there to fear?" The old man could not stop them and sighed as he left.

    At the time, Luo had already gone to bed, but suddenly he woke up, put his sleeve to his ears, and realized there was a killing intent. He quietly got up, listened carefully, and then returned to bed.

    Several days later, as evening approached, the boat docked in a desolate river among reeds, and his companions asked, "Today we are enjoying the blessings of the gods, we wish to offer you the remaining food as a toast to your health, please do not refuse!" Luo said, "Very good." The boatmen were pleased and brought out the wine and dishes. Luo knew the wine was poisoned and refused to drink it. The boatmen secretly found him strange, but they took advantage of his being alone at night, thinking he had nowhere to hide, and did not insist. Luo took out a jar of Shaoxing wine, poured himself a cup, and drank heartily. The jar was almost empty, and he pretended to be drunk, lying on the bed with the candle extinguished.

    315

    顷之，三人各秉炬持刀入，刃晃晃如霜雪。一人举刀就枕下悉力斫之，觉有异，验之，非人，盖卷被为之如酣卧状，相与大骇。搜索，闻罗在别舱吁曰：“余在此！”一人奔之，忽飞一石起，中腕，腕伤刀落。二人次至，亦如之。遂突起击三人，俱仆，拽而叠之，拾刀拟其项，笑曰：“余能前知，安得犯余？余无金，亦无点金术，尔曹何利焉？姑与尔曹戏，故不泄也。亦藉以歼厥败类，聊逞余志。”
    　　三人哀呼乞命。翁亦来跽请，罗麾之起，曰：“翁无罪也，毋恐。”翁泣曰：“三人者罪固不宥，然老朽之嗣斩于是矣，幸仁人宽假之！”罗从容掷刀曰：“为翁故，贷尔曹死。亟革乃心，脱复创难，必血吾刃矣！且孤踪远涉者，类能自保，如某犹其季指耳。遇之悉当善视，毋自取戾，摇尾态不足常恃也！”众唯唯。

    Explanation

    315

    After a while, three men entered, each holding a torch and a knife, their blades gleaming like frost and snow. One of them raised his knife and struck the bed with all his might, feeling something unusual. Upon inspection, it was not a person but someone pretending to be asleep under a blanket, and they were all greatly frightened. They searched and heard Luo shout from another cabin, "I am here!" One of the men rushed to the sound, and suddenly a stone flew up, hitting his wrist and causing the knife to fall. Two others arrived next, and the same thing happened to them. Luo then suddenly rose and struck the three attackers, knocking them all down, dragging them together, and picking up a knife to threaten their necks. He laughed and said, "I can predict the future; how could you dare to attack me? I have no gold, nor do I have the ability to turn base metals into gold; what benefit do you gain from attacking me? I was just playing with you, so I didn't reveal my powers. I also used this opportunity to eliminate your kind, satisfying my own will."

    The three of them cried out in despair, begging for mercy. The old man also came and knelt to plead. Luo waved him up and said, "Old man, you are innocent; do not be afraid." The old man wept and said, "The three of them are certainly not to be pardoned, but the old man's son will be killed because of this. Please show mercy, kind person!" Luo calmly threw the knife and said, "For the sake of the old man, I will spare your lives. Change your hearts quickly; if you create trouble again, I will surely spill your blood! Those who travel alone can usually take care of themselves; I am just one of them. Treat them all well; do not bring trouble upon yourselves; the submissive attitude is not always reliable!" They all nodded in agreement.

    316

    于是共疑罗神人，奴仆事之，讫于既，不敢有贰。
    　　非非子曰：癸丑夏，与李介夫太史剪灯语。介夫述台山轶事，相与壮之。夫台山自有其远者、大考，不以此表见，然已难能己。又言台山尝为友人戏闭室中，三日不与食饮，无饥馁色，殆能辟谷耶？而闻其已死，其真死耶？惜余不及见之。
    　　偷饭翁
    　　偷饭翁者，以偷饭得名。曷为乎偷饭？乃以母故。翁之贫也，母病，无以食也，闻邻之炊香，涎而梯。翁伤之，则乘间窃诸其釜。有竖子见之，窃告邻人，迹而至。至则翁方捧饭、跪进母，母拭泪尝之。邻人以是知其孝也。
    　　是时翁年始十有二岁。及长，客河南，未娶也。有女子自来为翁妻，育二予，归其家而奉母焉。母终而去。翁后以稗贩起家，纳粟为县吏，有廉声。
    　　耳食录二编卷二

    Explanation

    316

    They all doubted the divine person of Luo and served him as slaves, until the end, without any doubt. Feifei said: In the summer of the year of Gui-Chou, I was talking with Li Jiefu, the Grand Historian by the lamp. Li Jiefu recounted anecdotes about Taishan, and we found them impressive. Taishan had its own far-reaching and great achievements, which were not displayed through this, but it was still difficult to achieve. He also said that Taishan was once locked in a room by a friend as a joke, and for three days without food or drink, he showed no signs of hunger or starvation, perhaps he could practice辟谷? And I heard that he had died, did he really die? It is regrettable that I did not get to see him.

    The thief of rice was a person who was known for stealing rice. Why steal rice? It was because of his mother. When the old man was poor, his mother was ill and had no food, he heard the aroma of cooking from the neighbor, salivated and climbed. The old man was moved by this, so he took the opportunity to steal from his pot. A young boy saw this, secretly told the neighbors, and they followed the trail. When they arrived, the old man was holding the food, kneeling to offer it to his mother, who wiped her tears and tasted it. The neighbors knew of his filial piety from this.

    At that time, the old man was just twelve years old. When he grew up, he became a guest in Henan, and did not marry. A woman came to be his wife, bore two sons, and returned to his home to serve his mother. After his mother died, he left. Later, the old man became wealthy through small-scale trade, donated grain to become a county official, and had a reputation for integrity.

    Second Volume of Ears and Food Record

    317

    揭　雄
    　　明季有揭雄者，——遣其郡邑矣。——貌椎鲁，寡言笑，然门以内无违行，肫肫如也。年十馀，不识冬夏，适于途，迷所向。乡人愚之。
    　　每为人佣役，任负不及常儿，然不敢值，人亦利其用。里之豪右争役之，雄椎移其间，亦无忤也。
    　　久之，贫甚，或劝之服贾，雄曰：“诺。”与族商练事者数人俱西。众欺其愚懦，齿之仆数，榷执役不倦。中途遇盗御其装，诸客辟易，雄徐曰：“是何敢然！”即拳一盗仆。他盗复进，雄巧夺其刃，连殪数人，馀盗奔去。众始惊其异矣，待之有加礼。既共诸客贾，使视利数四，丧失其资。人皆曰：“是健儿而愚者也。”复易之。
    　　其年所居地雨甚，浃日，河涨啮堤。乡人修之，堤成而复决者三，莫能为计。雄谓众曰：“是其下有暴物焉，将鱼我村里，某请除之。”乃拔剑没水，移时斩一鼋，提其首，奋波而出。水威顿杀，而堤得不溃。众复惊曰：“子固若是之能耶！何向者示人弱也？”

    Explanation

    317

    Revealing the Strength of Jie Xiong

    During the late Ming dynasty, there was a man named Jie Xiong, who lived in his county. He had a simple and unrefined appearance, rarely spoke or laughed, but within his household, he was sincere and earnest in his actions. As a child, he was barely ten years old, did not recognize the seasons, and often got lost on his way, confusing the locals who thought him foolish.

    He often worked as a hired laborer, unable to carry loads as well as other children, yet he did not dare to ask for fair compensation, and people still found value in using him. The wealthy and powerful in his village competed to employ him, and he was moved around among them, but did not resent it.

    After a long time, he became very poor, and someone advised him to engage in commerce. Jie Xiong agreed and traveled west with several experienced traders from his clan. The group treated him as a servant, but he persevered in his duties without complaint. On the way, they encountered bandits who robbed their belongings, and the other travelers fled in fear. Jie Xiong calmly said, "How dare you do this!" and immediately knocked down one of the bandits with a punch. Another bandit advanced, and Jie Xiong skillfully seized the bandit's weapon, killing several more in succession. The remaining bandits fled. The group was astonished by his bravery and began to treat him with greater respect. They then let him look at the profits several times, but he lost his capital. People began to say, "He is a strong but foolish person," and their attitude towards him changed again.

    That year, the area where he lived experienced heavy rain for a whole day, causing the river to swell and erode the embankment. The villagers repaired the embankment, but it broke three times, and no one could come up with a solution. Jie Xiong told the villagers, "There is a violent creature below, and it will flood our village. I will deal with it." He then drew his sword and plunged into the water. After a while, he emerged with the head of a large turtle, lifting it above the waves. The water's force suddenly subsided, and the embankment was saved from breaking. The villagers were surprised and said, "Do you really have such ability? Why did you show weakness earlier?"

    318

    于是士大夫好事者亦渐与往来。遇宴会，群口哆然，雄默坐而已。他日有两文士论文，席间奋争至攘臂，雄劝之曰：“文可不必论，论文亦不易。”众以其言夸，诧曰：“公亦解此耶？”雄笑曰：“惟稍识之，故知其堆耳。”众欲徵其蕴，争摘古义诘难之。雄随问酬对，机悟深微，超超平玄著，累累然如贯珠也，论辨若悬江河、骤风雨，而不可遏也，疏故实如数家珍，如举东西南朔岁月日之无误也。而平生未尝一问字、一执卷呻吟也。皆帖然服曰：“神人也！”
    　　舍岁饥，流民相聚，剽掠乡里，势若蜂屯。里人走且号，谋窜山谷。雄曰：“无庸，此易制耳！”乃聚丁壮，使于境南之泽中斩劲竹为兵仗，昼夜训练之，增高垒濬沟，分守要害，妇人老弱治饷，转徙馈饮食。寇至，鼓之，令之曰：“尔有家，尔庐，尔墓，毋以畀贼，其奋毋怠！不用命者众有诛！”邻里闻之，相趋皆来，守益众。贼至，无所利，竟引去。数十里之内，赖以安全。

    Explanation

    318

    As the scholars and officials who were enthusiastic about literature began to interact with him more frequently, he attended banquets where many people talked excitedly, yet he remained silent. One day, two literati engaged in a heated debate on literature, raising their arms in passion. Xiong advised them, "Literature may not always need to be discussed, and discussing it is not always easy." The audience was impressed by his words and asked, "Do you also understand this?" Xiong smiled and replied, "I have only a little knowledge, so I understand its complexity." The audience wanted to delve deeper and challenged him with ancient texts. Xiong responded with deep and subtle insights, connecting ideas smoothly. His arguments were powerful and unstoppable, like a river or a storm. He recounted historical facts with ease and accuracy, as if they were familiar household items. Despite his extensive knowledge, he had never studied or read extensively. Everyone submitted respectfully and called him a "divine person."

    In a year of famine, refugees gathered and plundered the countryside, gathering like bees. The villagers ran and shouted, planning to flee to the mountains. Xiong reassured them, "There is no need to panic, this situation is easily controlled!" He organized able-bodied men to cut strong bamboo and train continuously. They built higher walls and dug deeper ditches, guarding strategic points. Women and the elderly prepared food and transported it. When the enemy arrived, he rallied and commanded them, "Protect your homes, houses, and graves. Be brave and do not tire! Those who do not follow orders will be punished!" Neighbors heard and came together, increasing the number of defenders. The enemy found no advantage and eventually left. The area relied on these measures for safety.

    319

    雄晚岁无妻子，闭居茅屋中，焚香诵佛经，旦夕不彻。罕与外人相见。遂有一远方僧来访之，跏趺对语，三日夜乃去。
    　　其后辄数月一来，但相对枯坐，不复作一语。久之僧去，临行谓雄曰：“可矣！”雄点首者再，僧遂不复来。
    　　雄自是祝髪为僧，乃不复诵经。已谓其所亲曰：“吾前身劳山僧也，好攻文籍，及一切经世之务。师以结习特重，不可化，使出世，戒之曰：“慎勿逞也，将迷不复！”每念师言，深自韬秘，不意数数与人事，不免炫露，亦不得已耳。师趋召我，我去矣！”遂合掌坐逝。

    Explanation

    319

    In his later years, Xiong Jing had no wife or children and lived in isolation in a thatched hut, burning incense and reciting Buddhist scriptures day and night without interruption. He rarely met with outsiders. One day, a monk from a distant place came to visit him, and they sat cross-legged and conversed for three days and nights before the monk left.

    Afterward, the monk would come every few months, but they would just sit facing each other silently, not speaking a word. After a long time, the monk left, and on his way out, he told Xiong, "It is done!" Xiong nodded twice, and the monk never came again.

    From then on, Xiong Jing took vows and became a monk, and he no longer recited scriptures. He then told his close relatives, "In my previous life, I was a monk from Mount Lao, who loved studying literature and all worldly affairs. The teacher said my attachments were particularly strong, making it impossible to transform me, and sent me into the world, warning me, 'Be careful not to indulge, or you will be lost and unable to return!' Whenever I recall the teacher's words, I deeply hide them within myself, but I did not expect to be involved in so many worldly matters, which inevitably led to my exposure. It was unavoidable. The teacher is calling me, I am going!" Thus, he sat with hands joined and passed away peacefully.

    320

    非非子曰：余往闻故老言雄之软事，韪而识之。或曰：雄董姓邓，江西人，所传亦不尽与此合。异哉所闻，故采入稗说，未足以为信也。要其负才藏用，不激不随，济物下人，无所失于已，有足纪者。
    　　双　玉
    　　庆佑之员外述其第十兄琴川之卒也，有姬双玉者，病绝数四，怀小刀，将自为计，家人见而夺之。姬慷慨自矢，坚不欲生。或绐之曰：“从公诚善。然公始死，魂魄且迷惘，子乌乎遇公？负初志矣。七日将复焉，以死，其可。”盖冀其日远哀杀而志可回也。姬许诺。由是晏然食息，绝不复悲泣。既逾期，家人防少懈，竟投缳死。时癸丑八月十六日也，距琴川卒盖十日，年二十有六岁。

    Explanation

    320

    Feifeizi said: I have heard from old sayings about the gentle deeds of Xiong, and I approve and remember them. Some say: Xiong was surnamed Dong and named Deng, from Jiangxi, and the accounts do not entirely agree with this. Strange is what I have heard, so I have included it in trivial sayings, but it is not sufficient to be taken as true. In summary, he was talented but kept his abilities hidden, neither impulsive nor conforming, helping others and humbling oneself, nothing was lost to himself, and there is enough to be recorded.

    Shuang Yu

    The magistrate of Qingyou described the death of his tenth brother Qinchuan. There was a concubine named Shuang Yu who was on the verge of death several times, carried a small knife, intending to take her own life. Her family saw and took the knife from her. She was resolute and determined not to live. Someone deceived her by saying, "Following him is indeed good. But since he has just died, his spirit is still confused. How can you meet him? You would be betraying your initial resolve. In seven days, he will return. To die now would be inappropriate." This was perhaps to delay her grief and change her mind. The concubine agreed. From then on, she ate and slept calmly, never cried again. After the deadline had passed, the family's vigilance slightly relaxed, and she finally hanged herself to death. It was the sixteenth day of the eighth month in the year Gui Chou, ten days after Qinchuan's death, and she was twenty-six years old.

    321

    其后二日，有鬼凭其佣媪，索钱楮甚急。或曰：“其双姑乎？”曰：“然。我死，人不我援，又不我祭，一何忍乎？我实馁而，故来耳。”家人以为信，将焚镪焉，佑之斥曰：“否否！谁欤？厉鬼而伪托者也。恶有贞烈如双姑而不神者、而索币者哉？”弗听。鬼良久叹息，曰：“奈何奈何！诈不售矣！”媪忽仆。霍然良愈。
    　　初，琴川夫人金氏性绝颖，读书如夙记，工笔札，雅善鼓琴。其他艺事，习焉辄妙，殆天授也。甲辰某月，病且革，绝而复苏。娓娓处分后事已，语琴川曰：“勿悲，与君十年别耳。若欲亟见，西山天台山水莲洞有童女子，衣粉红、捧绿瓶者，即我也。”言讫而瞑。他日诣洞觌神貌，果肖焉。及是琴川卒，十年之语亦验。亦并足志矣。
    　　琴川名蕙，镶黄旗人，尹文端公子也。官銮仪卫冠军。

    Explanation

    321

    Two days later, a ghost possessed her servant woman and urgently demanded money and paper for burning. Someone said, "Could it be your aunt?" She replied, "Yes. After I died, no one helped me or offered sacrifices, how could they be so heartless? I am truly hungry, so I came here." The family believed her and was about to burn the money, but the guardian rebuked them, saying, "No, no! Who is this? It's a malevolent ghost pretending to be someone else. How could a virtuous and heroic person like Aunt not be a spirit, and yet demand money?" They did not listen. After a long while, the ghost sighed, "What can I do? My deception has failed!" The servant woman suddenly fainted and then suddenly and completely recovered.

    At first, Lady Jin of Qinchuan had an extremely sharp intellect and read books as if they were memorized from the start. She was skilled in writing letters and was very good at playing the zither. In other artistic pursuits, whenever she practiced, she became excellent, which was probably a gift from heaven. In a certain month of the Jia Chen year, she fell seriously ill and died but then revived. She carefully arranged her after-death affairs and told Qinchuan, "Do not be sad; we will be separated for ten years. If you want to see me soon, there is a young girl in the Water Lily Cave on the Water Lotus Mountain on West Mountain, dressed in pink and holding a green vase; that is me." She finished speaking and then closed her eyes. On another day, when he visited the cave and saw the spirit's appearance, it indeed resembled her. When Qinchuan died, the prediction of ten years also came true. It is worth recording as well.

    Qinchuan's name was Hui. She was a person from the Yellow Banner of the Eight Banners system and the son of a prominent official, serving as a champion in the Imperial Court's Ceremonial Guard.

    322

    明　绡
    　　浙江卢七，以衣工起家，家富巨万。不衣帛，不御酒肉，以俭著。子重，性绝豪侈，略不类其父。尤睨志狭邪，青楼珠箔间多识其名者。顾严于择配，议婚多门，卒鲜所当意。年二十馀，犹鳏鱼也。
    　　或说之曰：“姑苏佳丽之冀北，必有施旦其人者。”重乃挟重资游吴门，棹钱塘。达于笠泽。
    　　湖风度耳，遥闻歌吹声。少焉，片席拂天，双桡剪水，须臾而至，岸然官舫也。歌喉尚啭，曼响如丝。重心醉，张帆随之，昼则同流，夜则同岸。历两日，两舟僮仆稍稍通问讯。官舫人语曰：“严州顾刺史徙居金阊，先行矣。此夫人舟也。”重使人饮其舟师，窃叩歌吹者为谁，舟师曰：“夫人女奴数人，皆善丝竹，奏曲者其女公子耳。”问：“公子字乎？”曰：“闻尚未。”问：“见乎？”曰：“公子甚简出，尝一见，真仙人也！”重乃赂舟师，使缓桨迂行，终日华冠冶服，徙倚舵楼，冀一遇。

    Explanation

    322

    A Tale of the Ming Dynasty: The Silk Weaver's Son

    Lu Qi, a man from Zhejiang, rose to wealth through the clothing trade and amassed a fortune of millions. Known for his extreme frugality, he did not wear silk or indulge in alcohol and meat. His son, named Chong, was exceptionally extravagant, unlike his father in every way. Chong was particularly fixated on immoral pursuits and was well-known among the courtesans of Suzhou. Despite his high standards for marriage, he proposed to many women but found none to his liking. At the age of twenty, he remained unmarried.

    One day, someone advised him, "In the northern part of Suzhou, there must be a woman named Shi Dan." Inspired, Chong set out with a large sum of money, traveling to Wu Gate and paddling the Qiantang River, eventually reaching Lize.

    As the lake wind passed by, he heard the distant sound of music. Soon, a small boat rose to the sky, its two oars cutting through the water. In no time, a dignified official boat appeared. The singing voice still trilled, and the soft sound was like silk. Chong's heart was intoxicated, and he sailed after it, sharing the same river during the day and the same shore at night. After two days, the servants of the two boats began to communicate slightly. The person on the official boat said, "The governor of Yanzhou, Mr. Gu, has moved to Jin Chang and has already gone ahead. This is the boat of the lady." Chong sent someone to drink with the boatman and secretly asked who the singer was. The boatman replied, "The lady has several female servants who are all skilled in music. The one playing the music is her daughter." Chong asked, "Has she been given a name?" The boatman said, "I heard she has not." Chong then asked, "Have you seen her?" The boatman replied, "The daughter rarely goes out. I have seen her once, and she is truly a fairy!" Inspired, Chong bribed the boatman to slow down the paddling and take a detour. All day, he wore splendid hats and beautiful clothes, leaning on the steering house, hoping to meet her.

    323

    次日晚泊，暮霭横波，顾舟篷窗半启。粉黛数人，肤光粲发，措画遥山，仰睇樯乌，相与喃喃漫语。一翠衣茜袖者最丽，回首见重，遽命掩窗。重以问舟师，舟师曰：“翠衣者是也。”
    　　重益惑乱，求舟师而啖之金。舟师却之曰：“郎君亦痴矣！某何能为？”重固请，舟师踌躇曰：“若是，为郎君策之。”已而曰：“得之矣。夫人有弟赵，刺史所任也。今在舟中。试以乡谊修半刺，先结其欢心，申礼而求娶可也。”重从之。赵来答拜，因留之饮洒，语颇洽。酒酣，重微露其意，赵曰：“公固未室耶？某甥女尚待字，如公者诚佳婿也！”重即下拜称谢，赵曰：“容返舟与姊言之。”少选，赵来，色喜曰：“事谐矣！姊闻公高义，甚愿浼我为执柯，然亟须纳采为定，虑刺史性梗，或有变易耳。”重曰：“礼不备，奈何？”赵曰：“随所有可也。”

    Explanation

    323

    On the following evening, the boat anchored at dusk, the evening mist spreading over the waves. The narrator looked out through the half-open window of the boat and saw several beautiful women. Their radiant skin and lustrous hair gleamed, and they were depicting distant mountains, looking up at the black crow perched on the mast, and talking idly together. Among them, the most beautiful was a woman in green and red attire. She turned around and, seeing the narrator, hastily ordered the window to be closed. The narrator then asked the boatman about the woman, and the boatman replied, "The one in green is her."

    The narrator became more confused and bribed the boatman with gold. The boatman refused, saying, "You are also foolish! What can I do?" The narrator persisted, and the boatman hesitated before agreeing to help. After a while, he said, "I have it. The lady has a brother named Zhao, who is appointed by the governor. He is on the boat now. Try to use our hometown connection to send a half-letter, first win his favor, and then formally request marriage." The narrator followed the plan. Zhao came to bow in response, and the narrator kept him and drank with him. The conversation was very pleasant. After drinking heavily, the narrator subtly revealed his intention to marry Zhao's sister. Zhao said, "You haven't married yet, have you? My niece is still unmarried. You would be a good husband for her!" The narrator immediately bowed and expressed gratitude. Zhao said, "Let me go back to the boat and talk to my sister." After a short time, Zhao returned with a happy expression, saying, "It's settled! My sister heard of your noble character and is very willing to let me act as a matchmaker. But we need to send a betrothal gift to finalize it. I'm concerned about the governor's stubbornness, and there might be changes." The narrator said, "The rituals are not complete, what should I do?" Zhao replied, "Whatever you have is fine."

    324

    是日舟已近姑苏，遂止不行。重乃出千金为聘，复以百金为赵寿。旋登顾舟，执贽见夫人，年四十许人也。慰赉良厚。诸婢皆窃窃戏笑。　　
    　　既返舟，约翌日同发。抵吴门，夫人及诸女皆先乘行，装资随往。赵留宿重舟，曰：“俟姊归，少屏当，当往谒妇翁。不意萍水之交，竟成丝萝之托。”重谢曰：“长者之赐也！”
    　　越三日，有使者来迎。重留二仆守舟，随赵往。造一大宅，类官阀。主人出迎，赵曰：“刺史也。”重再拜，执子婿礼甚恭。赵遂趋入内。刺史颜颇庄，训辞严简。重侍坐，局促良苦。已而设席，重不敢纵饮，愁愁数杯而已。

    Explanation

    324

    That day, the boat had approached Suzhou and stopped without proceeding further. Zhong then offered a thousand gold as a dowry and, in addition, presented another hundred gold as a gift to Zhao. Immediately, he boarded the Gu boat and presented a gift to meet the lady, who was in her forties. She received generous comfort and gifts. The maidservants all whispered and laughed.

    After returning to the boat, they agreed to depart the next day together. Upon arriving at Wumen, the lady and her daughters went ahead, while their belongings followed. Zhao stayed overnight on Zhong's boat and said, "Wait for my sister to return, and then we will go to see my father-in-law. Never expected a casual acquaintance to become a lifelong alliance." Zhong thanked, saying, "It is a favor from a senior person!"

    After three days, an envoy came to welcome them. Zhong left two servants to guard the boat and followed Zhao. They reached a large house, resembling an official residence. The host came out to welcome them, and Zhao said, "He is the prefect." Zhong bowed twice, performing the son-in-law's respectful rituals with great reverence. Zhao then hurriedly went inside. The prefect's expression was quite solemn, and his words were strict and concise. Zhong sat by, feeling quite uneasy. Afterward, a feast was set up, but Zhong dared not drink freely, only a few cups of wine.

    325

    及罢，日已晏，遂馆之外舍。赵来，笑谓曰：“余不耐拘拘，故失陪奉。想君亦复不畅也。”遂命剪灯取酒，欢然更酌。重放怀飞觥，狂饮大醉。赵辞去。
    　　沉睡至午晌始醒，悄然无一人，异而迹之，宅乃空。问之邻人。乃言：“此朱氏新构别业，昨一人来，暂赁请客耳。”始知被诓。仓皇至舟中见二仆，则箱箧衣装，亦皆为赵赚去矣。急索顾舟，舟亦杳。其舟师者，亦党同设局者也。
    　　重惭恧，固僦原舟返浙江。至家而偿其值，嘱二仆秘之。时卢七已老，家柄悉以委重，故重得自专。重忿顾赵之卖己也，而恋女不置，时时使人物色之。忽有李乙者来言；“予我金，当言顾所在。”重喜曰：“第言之。”李曰：“顾实返严州。试同往踪迹，聊信吾言。”重然之，偕之严州。

    Explanation

    325

    After the meeting, it was already late, and he was then housed in an outer room. Zhao came, smiling and saying, "I couldn't bear to be confined, so I couldn't accompany you. I suppose you didn't enjoy it either." He then ordered the lamp to be lit and wine to be fetched, and they enjoyed themselves freely, drinking wildly and getting drunk. Zhao took his leave.

    He slept deeply until noon when he finally woke up, finding no one around. Strange, he traced the situation and found the house empty. He asked the neighbors, who said, "This is a new property of the Zhu family. Yesterday, one person came, rented it temporarily to entertain guests." Only then did he realize he had been deceived. Hurriedly going back to the boat, he saw the two servants, and found that all his boxes, clothes, and belongings had also been taken by Zhao. He urgently sought the boatman, but the boat was nowhere to be found. The boatman was also part of the same gang.

    Chong felt ashamed and rented the original boat to return to Zhejiang. Upon arriving home, he paid the boatman and instructed the two servants to keep it secret. By then, Lu Qi was already old, and the household affairs were entrusted to Chong, so he could act independently. Chong was furious that Zhao had betrayed him, but he was unable to let go of the girl, and he often sent people to search for her. Suddenly, someone named Li Yi came and said, "Give me some money, and I'll tell you where Zhao is." Chong was delighted and said, "Just tell me." Li Yi said, "Zhao actually returned to Yanzhou. If you go there together, you can verify my words." Chong agreed, and they went to Yanzhou together.

    326

    至一村郭中，长杨高阁，画槛周遭。闽上一女于苗条夭冶，李蹑重足，悄语曰：“此即顾居也。请隐树间，伺其出。然慎毋轻动，虞其反噬耳。”重颔之，因审视女子，亦颇类舟中所见。
    　　于时夕照红黄，炊烟四迸，一人酩酊入其门，谛之，果顾也。李曰：“信乎？”重曰：“信也。虽然，何以处之？”李曰：“君欲得女耶？得金耶？”重曰：“能兼得甚幸，不然，则得女而甘心焉。”李曰：“欲得金，讼之可也。讼其父，是仇之也，则女必不从，或有他变，可惜也。欲得女，则戚也。顾诚居女为奇货，更挟以求公，公能应之乎？”重曰：“君意谓何？”李曰：“某请说之，明日当报命。”

    Explanation

    326

    Upon arriving at a village, they found a tall elm tree and a high pavilion surrounded by painted railings. A slender and graceful woman from Fujian was there. Li tiptoed and whispered, "This is Gu's residence. Please hide in the tree and wait for her to come out. But be careful not to move lightly, fearing she might turn around and bite." Zhong nodded in agreement and then looked at the woman carefully, finding her quite similar to the one he had seen on the boat.

    At that time, the sunset was red and yellow, and cooking smoke rose everywhere. A man staggered into the door, and upon closer inspection, it was indeed Gu. Li asked, "Is it true?" Zhong replied, "Yes, it is." Li then said, "Even so, how should we handle it?" Li asked, "Do you want the woman or the money?" Zhong replied, "If both can be obtained, it would be very fortunate; otherwise, obtaining the woman and being content with that would be enough." Li said, "If you want the money, you can sue for it. Suing her father is to make an enemy of him, and the woman would certainly not agree, or there might be other complications, which would be regrettable. If you want the woman, it would be a pity. Gu indeed treats his daughter as a rare commodity and uses her to extort you. Can you handle it?" Zhong asked, "What do you mean?" Li said, "I will explain it to you, and I will report back tomorrow."

    327

    明日，李乃引顾来，惶恐谢罪曰：“内兄误我以及此，悔也。愿更修前好。”重喜甚，顾去。李曰：“假我千金，则佳人为君有矣。”重许诺，与之归，而授之金。复偕往。
    　　既至，挛请先入报，使重待门外。久不出，心疑之。入其门，无人焉。登其堂，无人焉。窥其闺，镜奁钗盒颠倒凝尘，益讶其无人。排扉甫进，则闻人呼曰：“贼！贼！”俄有数人。共执重，数之曰：“入人闺闼，欲何为？”重力申前说，人曰：“此方氏，非顾氏，何诬也！”遂系之栋而鞭之。重不胜楚，请所欲。人曰：“以万金来，当宥汝。”重哀吁，请半之，且无扬于众。初，皆不听，再三而后许。重即遗书所亲，致五千金赎之。

    Explanation

    327

    The next day, Li brought Gu over, who was filled with fear and apologized, saying, "My brother-in-law has misled me to this point; I regret it. I wish to renew our former friendship." Zhong was very pleased and Gu left. Li said, "Lend me a thousand gold pieces, and the beautiful woman will be yours." Zhong agreed and accompanied him home, where he gave him the money. They then went together.

    Upon arriving, Gu asked to enter first and report, leaving Zhong to wait outside. After a long time, Gu did not come out, and Zhong began to doubt him. He entered the gate and found no one inside. He went up to the main hall, but there was still no one. Peering into the inner chambers, he saw cosmetics and hairpin boxes scattered with dust, and he was even more surprised that no one was there. Just as he pushed open the door and stepped in, he heard someone shout, "Thief! Thief!" Suddenly, several people appeared and seized Zhong, accusing him, "What did you want to do by entering someone's private rooms?" Zhong vigorously defended himself, but they said, "This is the Fang family, not the Gu family, how can this be a lie?" They then tied him to a beam and whipped him. Zhong could not bear the pain and asked for what he wanted. They said, "If you come with ten thousand gold pieces, we will forgive you." Zhong pleaded, asking for half the amount and requesting that they not spread it among the people. At first, they did not listen, but after several requests, they finally agreed. Zhong then wrote a letter to his relatives, sending five thousand gold pieces to redeem himself.

    328

    归，羞悔不自容，念乃寝。由是家渐替，行亦少敛。逾年，闻里中张丞女好，遂委禽焉。将醮，张女外遇，忽逃去。丞羞窘无策，适买一媵婢，绝姣丽，因厚遇之，嘱婢伪如己女者，以归重。
    　　婚夕无语，重诘之，婢忽垂梯曰：“妾亦安忍复秘哉？虽然，惟君宽假之，乃敢言。”重曰：“姑言之。”婢曰：“妾非张氏女，所谓顾女者也。亦非顾女，特章台柳耳，宋十三娘明绡者是也。顾亦非顾，全五耳，赵乃李八也。奸辈买妾以诱君，得金而鬻妾。败叶随风，孤英落溷，已三易婿矣；而卒得归君，亦夙分也！”重骇然，因述严州事，绡曰：“此事妾不知，要亦诳楚故智耳。”重问张女亦安在，绡备以情告，且解曰：“非张公之得已也。君将兴中篝之狱，于君实有阙。”重既虑宜秽于外，又嬖绡美遂安之。

    Explanation

    328

    Upon returning, he was overwhelmed with shame and regret, unable to bear it, and his thoughts then ceased. From that point on, his family gradually declined, and his behavior also became somewhat restrained. After a year, he heard that a woman in his neighborhood, the daughter of an official named Zhang, was virtuous, so he arranged a marriage. On the eve of the wedding, Zhang's daughter had an affair and suddenly fled. Zhang Gong was ashamed and at a loss, so he just happened to buy a concubine servant who was extremely beautiful. He treated her well and instructed her to pretend to be his own daughter in order to restore his reputation.

    On the night of the wedding, there was no conversation. He questioned her sternly, and the concubine suddenly said, "I also cannot bear to keep it hidden anymore. Although, only if you give me some leniency, I will dare to speak." He said, "Go ahead and say it." She replied, "I am not Zhang's daughter, but the one called Gu. Nor am I Gu, but just a woman from the Changtai Pavilion, Cao San's daughter, Ming Xiao. Gu is also not Gu, but Quan Wu, and Zhao is actually Li Ba. The villains bought me to lure you, took your money, and sold me. Like fallen leaves following the wind, and solitary flowers falling into the latrine, I have already changed husbands three times; but finally, I was able to return to you, which is also my fate!" He was startled and then recounted the matter of Yanzhou. Xiao said, "I don't know about this matter, but it was just an old trick to deceive Chu." He asked where Zhang's daughter was, and Xiao told him everything, and further explained, "It was not Zhang Gong's doing. You were about to launch a case against him, which would have been a fault on your part." He was worried about being tainted by outsiders, but enamored with the beauty of Xiao, he then settled down.

    329

    居无何，重归自友家，过林间，有少妇投缳，救之而苏。问其故，妇不胜羞泣，自言偶逾闺诫，乃为抱布氓所诱，今复见弃，无所归，故自决耳。重觑妇亦颇有姿，喻以情，妇乃言感德相援，愿为箕帚妾，遂窃从垂归。征其氏族，即张丞女也。
    　　重怒其前瑕，欲弃之。绡力劝重，且以嫡让妇，重不可，遂妾之。使人告张丞，修甥贝礼焉。
    　　毛　人
    　　硝商数人，穴蜀山取硝。林间遇毛人，以为怪，欲杀之。毛人拜且泣，若欲言而不克者。愍其意，乃舍之，推与饮食。数日，遂能言，曰：“我崇祯时人也。献贼屠蜀，避山中，食草根本实，得不死。久之，偶下山。闻金鼓声，其恐僳，遂不敢复出。今见灯光，故来耳。并不知今经几年月也。”
    　　又数日，毛尽脱落，携以出山，逾月而死。

    Explanation

    329

    After a while, Zhong returned from a friend's house and passed through the forest, where he found a young woman attempting to hang herself. He rescued her and she revived. He asked her the reason, and the woman could not bear the shame and wept, saying that she had accidentally violated the rules of propriety and was lured by a peddler. Now she is abandoned again and has no place to go, so she decided to end it. Zhong looked at the woman and found her quite attractive, so he explained the situation to her. The woman then said she was grateful for the help and was willing to be a servant. She secretly followed Zhong home. When he inquired about her family lineage, it turned out she was the daughter of Zhang the Magistrate.

    Zhong was angry about her past flaw and wanted to abandon her. Xiao strongly persuaded Zhong and offered to let her be the legitimate wife. Zhong did not agree, so he made her a concubine. He sent someone to inform Zhang the Magistrate and arranged a marriage ceremony for the niece.

    A mao ren (a mythical or supernatural being with human-like features) was encountered.

    Several nitre merchants dug into the mountains of Shu to mine nitre. In the forest, they met a mao ren, thinking it strange and wanting to kill it. The mao ren knelt and wept, as if wanting to speak but unable to. They pitied its intention, so they spared it and gave it food. After several days, it could finally speak, saying, "I am a person from the Chongzhen era. The Xian rebels massacred Shu, so I hid in the mountains and survived by eating grass roots and fruits. After a long time, I occasionally went down the mountain. I heard the sound of drums and was terrified, so I dared not come out again. Now that I see the light, I have come. I don't know how many years and months have passed."

    After several more days, all the hair fell out. They took it out of the mountain, and it died after a month.

    330

    ——有商于蜀耆言之。
    　　壁　虱
    　　某氏女子，梦黑甲人为祟。其家患之，问所自来，女曰：“自楼来。”楼久不登。旦日索之，见故柜有物，大与柜等，舁下焚杀之，——壁虱也。怪遂绝。
    　　又，某甲宿斋中，日就赢尪。家人疑其故，夜烛之。见壁虱大如碗，伏甲胸，小者万计，周身而集，无隙地。见灯即引去，入础旁穴中。灌而掘之，尽死。病寻愈。
    　　吁！斯真阴贼之尤者！
    　　文　寿
    　　文上舍寿，累举不第。其仲弟某者，一战而捷。父谓寿不学，责而逐之。母亦时时挫辱其妇，妇故贤，终无怨色。
    　　寿之初逐也，诀妇曰：“父母以不第逐寿，寿不笫，终不返。寿不肖，既长违父母，又以累卿。卿年少，宜自为计。”妇泣曰：“异哉，君之言及此也！君才妾所知，何有于一第？然得罪堂上，复何言！幸早有以慰之，妾之愿也，敢有他志！”寿亦泣曰：“谨受教。”寿行无资，妇尽取衣饰付质库，为戒行李，使应京兆举。

    Explanation

    330

    A rumor from the elderly of Shu.
    Wall Louse
    A woman from a certain family dreamed of a black-armored figure causing harm. Her family was troubled by this, and they asked her where it had come from. She said, "It came from the upper part of the house." The upper part had not been entered for a long time. On the next day, they searched it and found an object in an old chest, as large as the chest itself. They carried it down and burned it— it was a wall louse. The strange phenomenon ceased.
    Another time, a man named Mr. A stayed in his study, gradually becoming thin and weak. His family suspected the cause and lit a candle at night to investigate. They saw a wall louse as large as a bowl, lying on Mr. A's chest, with countless smaller ones surrounding him, covering every inch of his body. Upon seeing the light, they immediately retreated into a hole beside the foundation. They poured water into the hole and dug it out, killing all of them. His illness soon recovered.
    Alas! This was indeed the most sinister of all!
    Wen Shou
    Mr. Wen Shou, a scholar who had failed his exams multiple times, had an elder brother who passed in one attempt. Their father blamed Wen Shou for not studying and expelled him. Their mother also often belittled his wife, who was virtuous and never showed resentment.
    When Wen Shou was expelled, he said to his wife, "My parents expelled me for failing the exam. If I fail again, I will never return. I am unworthy, having already disobeyed my parents, and I will also burden you. You are young, you should make your own plans." His wife wept and said, "Strange, for you to mention this! Your talent is known to me, what does a single examination matter? But if you offend the elders, what can I say? Please find a way to comfort them soon, this is my wish, dare I have other intentions?" Wen Shou also wept and said, "I will follow your advice." He had no money for the journey, so his wife sold all her jewelry to the pawnshop, prepared their belongings, and sent him to take the exam in Jingzhao.

    331

    寿乃至京师，税居宣武坊某寺中，重理故业。已闻仲弟来赴春官试，趋往见之，问父母安否，泣言别后事。仲笑曰：“伯氏夙昔自负，今孰与仲多？”寿凄然而退。及仲捷南宫，授官部曹，乞假归，寿既惭复往，仲亦竟不诣寿所。
    　　仲至家，亲朋来贺者踵相接。召客张乐，门庭如火。寿妇体舅姑童，不敢向隅。仲妻诮之曰：“姒亦良乐。”妇佯为不闻。数日，问仲曰：“叔见而兄乎？”仲漫应曰：“曾一见。”即旁顾漠漠说他事。妇知其意，不复问，闭门掩泣而已。仲又言寿实怨亲之逐己也，而以为我罪，故在都常避我，不得数数见。父母既以仲贵而听信其言。益怒骂寿。寿书数至，辄焚齐不览。益爱仲妻而憎妇，婢畜之。妇固知仲之谮寿而不敢辩也。每食，以食馀食妇，妇常不饱。岁时饮宴游戏之事，妇未尝获与。寿有子甫三岁，与仲子争栗而啼，仲子亦啼。母怒，抚仲子而笞寿之子，且谓妇教之，诟詈累日。妇因垂涕长跪谢。

    Explanation

    331

    寿到了京城，租住在宣武坊某寺中，重新开始旧业。听说弟弟仲来参加春官试，急忙去见他，询问父母安好与否，哭着说起离别后的事情。仲笑着说：“哥哥以前总是自夸，现在谁比我强？”寿凄然退去。等到仲在南宫考试中取得成功，被任命为部曹，请求假期回家，寿感到惭愧再次前往，但仲最终也没有去寿那里。

    仲回到家，亲朋好友络绎不绝前来祝贺。他召集客人演奏音乐，门前热闹非凡。寿的妻子侍奉公婆，不敢面向角落。仲的妻子讽刺她说：“你的姐姐也很愉快。”寿的妻子假装没听见。过了几天，她问仲：“你见到哥哥了吗？”仲漫不经心地回答：“见过一次。”随即旁顾漠然地说着其他事情。寿的妻子知道他的意思，不再追问，只是关门哭泣。仲又说寿实际上怨恨家人把他赶走了，而认为这是自己的错，所以在京城常常避开他，不能经常见面。父母因为仲的地位而相信他的话，更加愤怒地责骂寿。寿多次写信来，都被仲烧掉不看。仲更加宠爱自己的妻子而厌恶寿的妻子，把她当作奴婢。寿的妻子知道仲在诬陷她，却不敢辩解。每次吃饭，仲只给寿的妻子剩饭吃，她常常吃不饱。每逢节日宴饮游戏，她从未得到参与的机会。寿有一个三岁的儿子，与仲的儿子争栗子而哭泣，仲的儿子也哭了。母亲生气，安慰仲的儿子并鞭打寿的儿子，还说寿的妻子教唆的，连续几天责骂。寿的妻子因此泪流满面，长跪道歉。

    332

    寿父病笃，妇日夜忧叹，既痛其夫之不遇，而深恐舅之不及待也，夜焚香吁天，私为舅乞算。寿父病良已。仲妻见之，乃谗于姑曰：“姒实祝诅！”姑怒甚，官于寿父，将讼之。邻里多知妇冤，而畏仲妻且怨己，不敢言。妇无以自明，饮恨呕血数升卒，年未三十。闻者伤焉。

    Explanation

    332

    The husband was gravely ill, and his wife mourned day and night, grieved by her husband's misfortune and deeply afraid that he might not survive long enough for her to see him recover. She burned incense and called upon heaven, secretly praying for her husband's longevity. Her husband's illness was truly cured. The wife of the younger brother saw this and slandered her sister-in-law to their mother-in-law, saying, "She has actually cursed him!" The mother-in-law became very angry and planned to sue her son, intending to take legal action. Many neighbors knew of the wife's innocence but feared the younger sister-in-law and resented themselves for not speaking up, so they remained silent. The wife had no way to clear her name and died after spitting up several liters of blood out of resentment, not yet thirty years old. Those who heard of it were saddened.

    333

    是时寿举京兆，复报罢。止京师，不敢归。资用乏绝，为寺僧佣书自给。偶薄暮步寺外，有少妇徘徊立松下，貌类其妇。就问，果妇也，大骇。问何以至此，妇哽咽不能答，已而告曰：“妾已鬼矣！”寿闻言大恸，妇止之曰：“勿悲。今来就君，不异生时之乐。且生时离逖。今乃聚晤，奈之何不欢？”寿乃收泪，亦不复畏怖，与妇俱入寺中。他人莫见妇，妇语亦莫之闻也。
    　　妇谓寿曰：“君食贫若此，愿策所以佐君者。”问何术，曰：“明日请署于门，设卜肆。妾颇预人事，当大获。”寿从之。卜焉辄验，声誉大起。都人士阗咽其门，出为君子复出也。
    　　无何，将复宾兴，寿问：“吾今获隽否？”妇曰：“此事神道所秘，妾不能知。但宜破釜以冀一当耳。”因劝寿避居谢客，购书盈案，日夜读之。妇故识字，亦展卷相对弦诵，敏乃过于寿。克日课经义及诗策之属。妇制常忧。寿叹曰：“惜卿乃不栉进士，又隔幽泉，擅此将安施乎？”妇笑而不答。

    Explanation

    333

    At that time, Shou was appointed as the governor of Jingzhao but was again reported to be dismissed. He remained in the capital city and dared not return home. His resources were completely exhausted, so he worked for a temple monk by copying texts to support himself. One evening, as he walked outside the temple, he saw a young woman standing under a pine tree, hesitating. She looked like his wife. He approached her and asked why she was there. She choked up and could not answer, but after a while, she said, "I am already a ghost!" Upon hearing this, Shou was greatly moved. The woman comforted him, saying, "Do not grieve. Now I come to you, it is no different from the joy of life. And since we were separated in life, now we are reunited, how can we not be happy?" Shou stopped crying and no longer feared. He went into the temple with the woman. Others could not see the woman, nor could they hear her words.

    The woman said to Shou, "You are living in such poverty, I wish to contribute to help you." He asked what method she suggested. She replied, "Tomorrow, please set up a divination booth. I participate in human affairs and will have great success." Shou followed her advice. Divination was always accurate, and his reputation greatly increased. People from the capital city crowded his door, thinking that a virtuous person had returned.

    Soon, Shou was about to be recommended for official service again. He asked, "Will I succeed?" The woman said, "This matter is known only by the divine. I cannot know. But you should break the cauldron and hope for a single chance." She advised Shou to avoid guests and stay away, buying books that filled his desk and reading day and night. The woman knew how to read and write and opened the book to read aloud together with Shou, being more agile than Shou. They set a specific day to study the meanings of classics and poetry strategies. The woman often worried, and Shou sighed, saying, "It is a pity that you are an unmarried scholar, and now separated by the dark waters, where will you apply your talents?" The woman laughed without answering.

    334

    寿既入闱，比夜，妇亦至，谓寿曰：“妾向勤学如应举秀才，乃以君故也。当并力图之，庶其济乎！”遂为寿捉刀，文成，寿朗诵之。邻号舍某生，知名士也，闻之索现，大快赏，谓有神助，且曰：“必冠南军。”寿因告以故，生亦欷嘘叹诧。
    　　及揭晓，寿竟复落解，妇不胜悲，曰：“已矣！奈何？”寿反慰谕之。妇曰：“非也。科名诚何足蒂芥？所悲者堂上人老矣，旦夕望君之贵，而君卒不副其志也，命也夫！命也夫！”
    　　时仲已携妻子供职司曹。寿意亲且就养，驰往问讯，则亲固未来。仲耻见之，戒门者不为通。盖自妇卒后，仲妻倚夫贵，益骄，往往肆悖慢舅姑之前，略无子妇礼，箕帚谇语，殆有甚焉。舅姑反曲意下之，职是，仲官京师，不愿与仲俱来也。寿得其故，益自恨不第，与妇痛哭竟日夜。
    　　未几，仲秩满出守外郡。妇窃谓寿曰：“非福也。仲氏寡恩而好货，将不免。”

    Explanation

    334

    After Shou entered the examination hall, by night, his wife also arrived. She said to Shou, "I have been diligently studying as if preparing for the imperial examinations, all because of you. We should work together to achieve our goal, perhaps we can succeed!" She then wrote on his behalf. When the essay was completed, Shou recited it aloud. A well-known scholar in the adjacent examination room, upon hearing it, sought to see the work and greatly praised it, saying that it seemed divinely inspired, and added, "You will certainly be the top candidate in the southern region." Shou then explained the reason to him, and the scholar sighed and admired the work.

    When the results were announced, Shou failed to pass the examination again. His wife could not bear the sorrow and said, "It is over! What can be done?" Shou comforted her. She replied, "No, it is not about the examination itself. What is truly sad is that our parents are old and every day and night they hope for your high rank, but you ultimately fail to meet their expectations. It is fate, indeed! Fate, indeed!"

    At this time, Zhong had already taken his wife and children to serve in the capital. Shou thought that his parents would soon come to live with him, so he hurriedly went to inquire. However, his parents had not yet arrived. Zhong was ashamed to see them and instructed the gatekeeper not to allow them entry. Since his wife's death, Zhong's wife relied on his status and became increasingly arrogant, often acting disrespectfully toward her in-laws, showing no filial or marital respect, and even speaking rudely. Her in-laws, on the other hand, treated her with excessive courtesy, which led to Zhong's decision not to bring his family to the capital. Upon learning this, Shou felt even more regretful for not passing the examination and cried with his wife day and night.

    Not long after, Zhong completed his term and was reassigned to govern a distant prefecture. His wife secretly told Shou, "This is not a good fortune. Zhong lacks kindness and is greedy for wealth, and he will not escape the consequences."

    335

    寿有季弟名秩生，天性仁淑，父母绝怜爱之。寿出时尚幼，既长，就外傅。颇知寿被逐无罪而嫂以谗死，垂涕请于父母曰：“伯兄不第，庸有罪乎？命也！且科第亦何爱之有？如仲氏富贵，大人奚与焉？长嫂贤孝，邻里无不知，不幸冒不韪之名，衔屈泉壤，遗孤茕茕，可为伤心！愿大人少宽假之，使伯兄复侍庭闱，而酬酒嫂氏之墓，以伸其魂灵，实天地之德！”时寿父母亦渐悔悟，稍稍知妇冤，闻秩生言，不觉泣下，曰：“儿大仁孝，吾从汝。”于是作书召寿归，使人祭妇冢，而抚恤其子。
    　　书未至，妇已知，喜动颜色，谓寿曰：“贺君归矣！舅姑比以季子言，旦夕召君。妾志亦蒙昭雪，且赐酒食矣。宜治装待，与君偕返耳。”
    　　后十馀日，书果至，至则启行。关河风雨，舟车馆驿之间，妇末尝不在。将至家，谓寿曰：“妾欲登堂重拜尊嫜，恨身为异物，不能明修榛栗，恐骇听闻，君幸为我陈白之！”寿泣曰：“诺！”

    Explanation

    335

    Shou had a younger brother named Zhi Sheng, who was naturally kind and virtuous, and his parents loved him dearly. When Shou was young, he was still a child, but as he grew older, he went to school. He learned that his eldest brother was expelled without fault, and his elder sister-in-law died due to slander. He tearfully pleaded with his parents, saying, "Is the eldest brother guilty of failing the examination? It is fate! Why should one cherish the examination success? If the second brother becomes wealthy, what does it matter to you, sir? The elder sister-in-law was virtuous and filial, and everyone in the neighborhood knew this. Unfortunately, she bore the name of wrongdoing, suffered injustice in the netherworld, and left behind an orphaned child. This is truly heartbreaking! I wish you would show some leniency, to have the eldest brother return to serve in the household, and to offer wine at the elder sister-in-law's grave, to honor her spirit. This is truly a great virtue of heaven and earth!" At that time, Shou's parents gradually came to regret and understand, began to understand the woman's injustice, and hearing Zhi Sheng's words, they could not help but shed tears, saying, "You are truly a kind and filial son, I will follow your advice." Thus, they wrote a letter to summon Shou back, sent someone to offer sacrifices at the woman's grave, and to comfort her child.

    Before the letter arrived, the woman already knew, and her face lit up with joy. She told Shou, "Congratulations, you are returning home! Your father-in-law and mother-in-law have been speaking highly of your younger brother and will call you home soon. My innocence has also been vindicated, and soon you will be offered food and drink. You should prepare to return with me." After more than ten days, the letter indeed arrived, and Shou set out. Throughout the journey, through the mountains and rivers, in the rain and wind, on boats and in carriages, in inns and at stations, the woman was never absent. As they approached home, she said to Shou, "I wish to enter the main hall and pay respects to your parents-in-law again, but I regret being an outsider, and cannot openly offer offerings, fearing to cause alarm. Please explain this to them on my behalf!" Shou wept and said, "Yes!"

    336

    既至，有少年俟门外，见寿乃趋而前曰：“公谁耶？”寿曰：“亡人寿也。”少年潸然下拜，问之，秩生也，盖度寿将至，企望者累日矣。旋有垂髫而号于地者，寿子也。方妇之卒也，父母待寿子不慈。秩生悯之，　日取食饲寿子，夜则视其寝，一果饵、一什物弄具，未尝不分。逮父母重拂秩生意，亦不复过虐寿子。故寿子虽失王父母欢，而以育以长，不以饥寒疾困死，秩生之力也。至是并出迎寿，而寿去家已八年，故各不相识。于是秩生驰入门内报父母，寿亦趋入拜父母堂下，曰：“寿不孝，久缺温清，卒不能有所成立、少慰高厚之心。面大人慈爱无已，使得复望见颜色。”言未既，哭失声。父母亦涕泗呜咽，掖之起，怜惜慰劳之甚厚。邻人闻之，无少长皆来观，多泣下者。

    Explanation

    336

    Upon his arrival, a young man waited outside the door. Seeing Lord Shou, he hurried forward and asked, "Who are you, sir?" Lord Shou replied, "I am the deceased Lord Shou." The young man tearfully bowed, and upon inquiry, he was identified as Rank Sheng. He had guessed that Lord Shou would arrive, and for several days he had been looking forward to it. Soon there was a child with hanging hair crying on the ground; this was Lord Shou's son. When his mother died, his parents treated him poorly. Rank Sheng pitied him, bringing food to feed Lord Shou's son daily and checking on his sleep at night, sharing a piece of fruit and a toy with him without fail. Until his parents became angry with Rank Sheng, they no longer excessively mistreated Lord Shou's son. Even though Lord Shou's son lost the favor of his grandparents, he was raised and grew up, not dying from hunger, cold, illness, or hardship—all thanks to Rank Sheng's efforts. At this point, they both came out to greet Lord Shou, but Lord Shou had been away from home for eight years, so they did not recognize each other. Rank Sheng then rushed inside to inform his parents, while Lord Shou also hurried in and bowed to his parents at the hall, saying, "I am unworthy, having long neglected my duties, and have failed to achieve anything to comfort my parents' deep love." Before he finished speaking, he burst into tears. His parents also wept bitterly, supporting him up and showing great compassion, pity, and comfort. Neighbors heard of it and all, young and old, came to see, many of them weeping.

    337

    寿欲言妇偕归事，虑亲惊怖，踌躇不敢发。父母则以妇卒而寿末之知，且初至，姑讳之。洎暮，寿趋出门外，见妇于墙隅泪涔涔目尽赤矣。妇曰：“言之乎？”寿曰：“未也。卿且入。明发寿当言之。”妇曰：“无舅姑命，安敢入？”寿为叹息，遂人，请命于父母，具陈始末。室中闻者皆相顾咄咄。秩生曰：“请无疑畏嫂非祸人者。嫂贤且孝，以神明其身，何为乎不然！”父母感其意，命之入。
    　　寿乃即门外呼曰：“妇入矣！”徐闻堂下有泣而言者，衣裳簌簌热若跪起者。寿白父母曰：“妇拜。”母踧躇曰：“向有间新妇于我者，我实不德，重负新妇，今固知其诬矣！新妇毋怨我。”则闻妇对曰：“何敢！”母又曰：“新妇语可得闻，形不可得见，何也？”妇曰：“妇人不饰，不敢见舅姑，况敢以朽化之身惊尊者？诚不以诡异见摈，得供使令于冥漠中，而防卫其不及，以少补生前未尽之职，何幸如之？奚以见为？”言已，复拜谢秩生，词致哀婉。

    Explanation

    337

    Shou wanted to speak about the matter of his wife returning home together, but hesitated due to concern about frightening the relatives. The parents thought the wife had died and Shou did not know about it, and since he had just arrived, the widow avoided mentioning her deceased husband. By dusk, Shou went outside and saw his wife standing in a corner of the wall, her tears streaming down and her eyes red with weeping. The wife asked, "Shall I speak it?" Shou replied, "Not yet. You should enter. At dawn, I will speak about it." The wife said, "Without the permission of the husband's parents, how dare I enter?" Shou sighed and then went inside, asking his parents for permission, and fully stated the beginning and end. Those in the room who heard all looked at each other in surprise. Ranker Sheng said, "Please do not doubt or fear that my sister-in-law is not a harmful person. She is both virtuous and filial, having purified herself through spiritual means. Why should it be otherwise!" The parents were moved by his words and allowed her to enter.

    Shou then called out from outside, "The wife has entered!" Gradually, he heard someone weeping and speaking below, their clothes rustling as if they were kneeling and rising. Shou informed his parents, "The wife is bowing." The mother said, "Previously, someone accused the new wife to me, but I was indeed unkind and heavily burdened her. Now I know the accusations were false! New wife, do not blame me." Then the wife responded, "How dare I?" The mother added, "New wife, your words can be heard, but your form cannot be seen, why is that?" The wife replied, "A woman without adornment, dare not show herself to the husband's parents, how dare I show my withered body to the respected ones? If I am not rejected for my strange appearance, I can serve and obey in the unseen world, guarding against what cannot be done, and slightly fulfilling duties that were not completed in life. How fortunate is that? Why should it be seen?" After speaking, she bowed again and thanked Ranker Sheng, her words filled with sorrow and tenderness.

    338

    寿子号眺而请曰：“儿幼未识母，母弃儿去。日夜思母不得见。今幸母来，愿使儿得见母也，”妇亦哭曰：“汝依妆祖父母、汝父、汝叔父甚善，吾虽汝母，不复能抚汝，不忍更汝见也！”子固请，妇为一见，曰：“聊为他日验。”旋隐去。自是，寿之外无得见妇者。妇虽死而言词恭慎，循循执妇道维谨，捆以内咸安之。
    　　母诞辰将至，妇谓寿：“请为我市束帛及少缯采来，作针黹数事，为介眉之献。”寿曰：“善。”即市与之。妇将去，置邻妇绣筐中。邻妇讶所从来，闻空中言曰：“吾西家文氏妇也，欲制衣履为姑寿。腕指苦弱，烦夫人代操刀尺、缝鉃而成之，感且不朽！”邻妇欣然许之，不逾日，成衣一袭、女舄一双，见者啧啧，咸谓针神。邻妇亦自讶巧捷，他日女红不能如也。妇持归付寿，则凭几支颐，若倦绣状，盖妇恐物出己手，疑于不祥，故附女成之。至日，寿进母。母且喜且悲，以为死妇乃胜生妇也。

    Explanation

    338

    寿子号眺而请曰：“儿幼未识母，母弃儿去。日夜思母不得见。今幸母来，愿使儿得见母也。”妇亦哭曰：“汝依妆祖父母、汝父、汝叔父甚善，吾虽汝母，不复能抚汝，不忍更汝见也！”子固请，妇为一见，曰：“聊为他日验。”旋隐去。自是，寿之外无得见妇者。妇虽死而言词恭慎，循循执妇道维谨，捆以内咸安之。

    母诞辰将至，妇谓寿：“请为我市束帛及少缯采来，作针黹数事，为介眉之献。”寿曰：“善。”即市与之。妇将去，置邻妇绣筐中。邻妇讶所从来，闻空中言曰：“吾西家文氏妇也，欲制衣履为姑寿。腕指苦弱，烦夫人代操刀尺、缝鉃而成之，感且不朽！”邻妇欣然许之，不逾日，成衣一袭、女舄一双，见者啧啧，咸谓针神。邻妇亦自讶巧捷，他日女红不能如也。妇持归付寿，则凭几支颐，若倦绣状，盖妇恐物出己手，疑于不祥，故附女成之。至日，寿进母。母且喜且悲，以为死妇乃胜生妇也。

    339

    里有悍妇，夫服贾于外，素虐其姑。窃市美食自食，使姑茹聋，蘖，终岁不知肉味，浣衣涤器若服役，终日不得休。而悍妇惺卧游戏，犹诟谇不已。及闻妇事，深自惭悔，事姑有加礼。其感化如此。
    　　父母已知寿贤而数奇不遇，遂使主家政，不复令与进取。妇乃教寿倾囊饯易粟，积贮连囷。属岁洊饥，劝寿平粜而赈其贫者，所全活甚众。里中饥民群起掠食富户，德寿之尝恤己也，相戒不入其门，以故文氏父子兄弟饔飧晏然，无劫夺流亡之患。

    Explanation

    339

    In the village, there was a domineering wife whose husband was engaged in commerce outside the home. She habitually mistreated her mother-in-law, secretly buying fine food for herself to eat while forcing her mother-in-law to consume coarse and bitter fare, leaving her unaware of the taste of meat all year round. The mother-in-law washed clothes and cleaned utensils as if serving, never able to rest. Meanwhile, the domineering wife would lie awake idly playing around, continuing to scold and berate. Upon learning of the wife's actions, she deeply felt ashamed and regretful, treating her mother-in-law with increased respect. Her transformation was so profound.

    The parents, aware of her husband's virtue and the misfortunes he had faced, decided to let her take charge of the household, no longer allowing him to pursue further advancement. The wife then taught her husband to empty his purse to buy grain, accumulating large stores. During a year of severe famine, she advised him to sell grain at a fair price and to help the poor, saving many lives in the process. The starving villagers in the neighborhood banded together to rob wealthy households, but they warned each other not to enter the house of the couple, for they had shown compassion in times of need. As a result, the Wen family, father and sons, brothers, ate their meals peacefully and calmly, without the danger of robbery, plunder, or flight.

    340

    已而得仲宫中耗，仲以赇败，削籍戍边，宦橐投入官。妻子穷困不得归。寿白父母，使秩生往迎仲妻子。秩生薄仲为人，不欲往，妇劝之曰：“伯与仲，均之兄也。小郎孝友，何独间于仲？”秩生叹曰：“闻命矣！”乃行，以仲妻子归。仲妻习于安乐，骤丁困苦，颇不任。寿父母以仲得罪实辱己，而仲妻素不敬己，其爱亦寝衰，久更恶之，若向之恶妇。邻里初以仲贵，腹非不敢言，至是亦多不齿之。寿子怨仲妻之谮死其母也，虽遏于父母命，不敢发，而时有报志。仲子随宦失业，既愚且骄，好博塞饮酒，数数盗财物亡去，从里中无赖少年游，鞭笞莫能禁。而仲屏死荒徼，无邱首之日。故仲妻虽归，前后如隔世，惭沮诎辱，几不得比于人数。独妇遇之如常，不以前卻为憾焉。

    Explanation

    340

    After obtaining news about Zhong from the palace, it was learned that Zhong had fallen due to bribery, was stripped of his position and exiled to the frontier, and his belongings were turned over to the government. His wife and children, now poor and suffering, were unable to return home. Shou informed his parents, and they sent Zhi to bring back Zhong's family. Zhi, however, looked down on Zhong as a person and did not want to go. His wife encouraged him, saying, "Brother Bo and Brother Zhong are both your brothers. Young Master is filial and friendly, why should he distance himself from Brother Zhong?" Zhi sighed and said, "I have received the order!" He then went and brought back Zhong's family.

    Zhong's wife, accustomed to a life of ease, found it difficult to cope with sudden hardship. Shou's parents felt disgraced by Zhong's downfall and, since Zhong's wife had never shown them respect, their affection for her gradually diminished. Over time, they came to dislike her as much as they had previously disliked her. Neighbors, who had once respected Zhong, now often spoke ill of him. Shou's son harbored resentment toward Zhong's wife for spreading false accusations that led to his mother's death. Though he was restrained by his parents' orders, he sometimes harbored thoughts of revenge.

    Zhong's son, following in his father's footsteps, became unemployed. He was both foolish and arrogant, enjoying gambling, drinking, and often stealing property to run away, associating with ruffians in the neighborhood. Despite being whipped, he could not be restrained. Zhong died in exile on the remote frontier, never to return home. Thus, although Zhong's wife was brought back, the contrast between her past and present life felt like two different worlds. She was filled with shame, humiliation, and disgrace, almost not considered equal to others. Only Zhi's wife treated her as usual, without regretting the changes that had occurred.

    341

    顷之，秩生补弟子员，登乙科，将议室。妇私告寿曰：“季子有宿业，当晚娶，且谐怨藕，妾已请而易之矣。符氏有女妍而德，其前身比丘尼也，行甚苦，偶涉香色界，遂结世缘，故尚无定匹。请以念珠为聘，事必谐。”初，符媪梦少年尼来至其家，乃生女子。女稍长，常常梦至佛寺中：长松翠忤，黄鸟缗蛮；蒲团空而金经掩，炉烟袅袅，犹萦绕璎珞幢幡之间，徘徊一两时便返。后有老尼谓之曰：“伽楠一百八，是汝委身处也。”女述于符媪，媪异而志之。妇所言合其冥契，果一媒而定。
    　　婚夕，有蜥蜴长二尺许，来瞰青庐。家人欲杀之，妇曰：“不可。即所谓怨耦者也，宜亟藏其镜，而祭以季子之履，则无咎矣。”如言，蜥蜴见履若甚怒，跃下啮履，裂之，跂跂缘壁间，回首顾秩生者再，遂登屋而隐。知妇言之非妄矣。

    Explanation

    341

    After a short while, the scholar was appointed as a student-official and passed the second-level examination, and he was about to discuss his marriage arrangements. His wife privately informed Shou, saying, "The younger son has past karma, and he should marry soon, and it will be a difficult match. I have already requested and changed it. The Fu family has a daughter who is both beautiful and virtuous; her previous life was as a female Buddhist nun, whose conduct was very arduous, and she occasionally involved herself in the realm of sensory pleasures, thus forming worldly connections, so she still has no fixed match. Please use prayer beads as the dowry, and the matter will surely be harmonious." At first, Fu the woman dreamed that a young nun came to her home, and then she gave birth to a daughter. As the daughter grew older, she often dreamed of going to a Buddhist temple: tall pines were green and lush, yellow birds chirped; the mat was empty and the golden sutra was covered, incense smoke curled, still winding around jewelry and banners, and she would wander for a short while before returning. Later, an old nun told her, "Sandalwood one hundred and eight is the place where you entrusted yourself." The daughter told Fu the woman, who found it strange and recorded it. What the woman said matched her inner agreement, and indeed, they were settled with one matchmaker.

    On the wedding night, there was a lizard about two feet long that came to look at the green tent. The family wanted to kill it, but the woman said, "No. This is the so-called difficult match. You should quickly hide her mirror and offer a sacrifice with the younger son's shoes, then there will be no harm." As she said, the lizard saw the shoes and seemed very angry, jumped down and bit the shoes, tore them, moved along the wall, turned back and looked at the scholar twice, then climbed onto the roof and disappeared. She knew that the woman's words were not false.

    342

    符女贤柔颇类妇，其事舅姑处门内，一以妇为法。妇每形见，宴坐相款语，相得弥至。仲妻心窃忮之，然卒无能复毒也。
    　　居数年，寿父母相继卒，寿，秩生居丧哀毁，妇亦衰絰，哭泣如生人。寿问曰：“舅姑安在？”妇曰：“此不可言，言则存亡皆获罪。故不敢言也。”妇自是颜色常不豫，见亦甚疎。诂其故，终不肯告。
    　　一日，闻檐际箫管杂蘧，则家人咸得见妇。妇惨然雪涕曰：“别矣！上帝悯妾志而旌其愚，使得位神灵之末，叨庙祀之享。获邀此命，己历五年。妾为舅姑在，不忍行，今不能复却矣！”乃谓寿曰：“诸人禄命方长，惟君侘傺，人间福祚莫能一朝享，然君毋苦也。越数日，当相告。”复谓其于曰：“儿且贵，后十二年还见我。今勿悲，善事汝叔父母，他日竭力报国家，无忝祖、父、汝母之志也！”言讫，遂冉冉而去。

    Explanation

    342

    Fu's daughter was virtuous, gentle, and resembled a wife. She treated her in-laws inside the door, following the example of a wife. Whenever she appeared, they would sit together and converse, growing closer with each encounter. Zhong's wife secretly envied her, but she could not ultimately harm her again.

    After several years, Shou's parents died one after another. Shou, ranked as a son, was in mourning and showed signs of grief, and his wife also wore mourning clothes and cried like a living person. Shou asked, "Where are my in-laws?" The wife replied, "This cannot be said; if spoken, both the living and the dead would be punished. Hence, I dare not speak of it." From then on, the wife's face was often gloomy, and she rarely saw her. When questioned about the reason, she refused to reveal it.

    One day, hearing flutes and pipes in the eaves, all the family members saw the wife. She sadly wiped away her tears and said, "Farewell! The Heavenly Deity has pitied my will and honored my foolishness, allowing me to hold a position among the spirits and to receive offerings at the temple. Receiving this command, it has been five years. I was reluctant to leave because my in-laws were still alive, but now I cannot refuse!" She then told Shou, "Others' fortunes are long, but you are lonely. Human blessings cannot be enjoyed for a moment, yet do not be distressed. In a few days, I will inform you." She also spoke to her son, saying, "You will soon be honored, and in twelve years, you will see me again. Do not grieve now. Serve your uncles and aunts well, and in the future, work hard to serve the country, and do not disgrace the aspirations of your ancestors, father, and mother!" After speaking, she slowly departed.

    343

    寿寻病，弥留之际，忽语家人曰；“妇来！”乃卒。后十二年，其子官某所，过一庙谒神像，酷肖其父母。
    　　——妇盖姓裴。席间闻某客谈其事甚详，独遣其郡县，惜哉！
    　　耳食录二编卷三
    　　沈　髯
    　　夏店人沈髯者，赴都门访亲。途遇少妇独行，衣缟素，姿致明冶。髯悦之，而玩其孤弱，因与接语。妇曰：“妾夫婿客死，远葬烟郊。昨提麦饭一盂，亲奠其冢。今欲往京师，大归母家。忽值群马奔至，与童子相失，日昃矣，鞋弓不速，惧有遇焉，惟君携挈之。”髯窃喜曰：“幸辱同逮，敢先步武？”妇称谢，遂与同行。

    Explanation

    343

    Shòu, upon falling ill, was in his final moments when he suddenly spoke to his family, saying, "Bring the woman!" and then passed away. Twelve years later, his son, serving in a certain place, passed by a temple and paid homage to a statue of a deity. The statue was extremely similar to Shòu's parents.

    —The woman was likely surnamed Péi. At a gathering, someone mentioned the story in great detail, but only mentioned the county, alas!

    Ears Eating Record, Second Edition, Volume Three
    Shěn Rán
    A man from Xià Diàn named Shěn Rán traveled to the capital to visit relatives. On the road, he encountered a young woman walking alone, dressed in white, with a beautiful and graceful appearance. Shěn Rán was pleased by her and, playing with her loneliness and weakness, began to speak with her. The woman said, "My husband died as a guest in a foreign land and was buried far away in a desolate place. Yesterday, I carried a bowl of millet porridge to personally offer at his grave. Now, I wish to go to the capital to return to my mother's family. Suddenly, a group of horses rushed by, and I lost sight of the young boy. It is already midday, and I am hurrying with my shoes on, fearing something might happen. Only you can accompany me." Shěn Rán secretly rejoiced and said, "It is fortunate to be caught up with you. Dare I take the lead?" The woman thanked him, and they set off together.

    344

    洎暮，抵通州。髯曰：“逆旅诘问，何以应之？盍兄我，以塞众口？”妇笑曰：“即婿君，亦复何伤，翼必兄也？”髯喜甚，既就舍，遂同食寝。
    　　迟明，髯寤，则见妇纸衣泥首，乃刍灵耳，惊而呼。众至，询得其故。发被视之，精秽流濡纸裈间，莫不诮谑髯。楫髯惭极，遁去。主人取刍灵焚之。
    　　他日髯返，出故道，复遇妇来。笑谓曰：“沈郎归去，不携我行乎？”髯骇，亟避之。至家，寻病卒。
    　　并蒂莲
    　　某太守致仕归绍兴。一子年十二，甚聪秀。太守女兄者，适萧山某氏，来视太守，留数月。有婢年十四，随以来。子一见悦之，饮食寝起，率须婢为理，非婢辄不乐。婢亦窃乐为之用，不自离也。

    Explanation

    344

    By nightfall, they arrived at Tongzhou. The bearded man said, "If the innkeepers ask me questions, how should I respond? Why not treat me as your elder brother to satisfy everyone's expectations?" The woman smiled and said, "Even if you marry me, it wouldn't hurt, and hopefully, you will become my elder brother." The bearded man was very pleased. After settling in, they ate and slept together.

    Just before dawn, the bearded man woke up and saw the woman dressed in paper with a mud-covered head, realizing she was a straw figure. He was startled and called out. When the others arrived, they learned the reason. Upon uncovering her, they saw filthy fluids flowing between her paper undergarments, and no one failed to mock the bearded man. The bearded man was extremely ashamed and fled. The innkeeper then burned the straw figure.

    Another day, the bearded man returned, taking the old path, and encountered the woman again. She smiled and said, "Señor, are you not taking me along?" The bearded man was startled and quickly avoided her. Upon returning home, he soon fell ill and died.

    Twin Lotus

    A certain governor retired to Shaoxing. He had a son who was twelve years old and very intelligent and handsome. The governor's elder sister had married into a family in Xiaoshan and came to visit her brother, staying for several months. There was a maid who was fourteen years old, who came with her. The son fell in love with the maid at first sight. For his meals, sleep, and daily routines, he relied entirely on her, and he was unhappy if she was not around. The maid also secretly enjoyed serving him and did not leave his side.

    345

    会清明，女兄欲展墓萧山，将携婢俱去。两人相对殊恫惘。以为童子也，恋其所习，亦忽之。濒行之前夕，丙夜风起，扉辟阖有声。家人疑有盗，起视，门启矣。迹至河干，于双履在焉，讶而返，验于卧室，则媪在而子亡矣。趋视婢所，婢亦亡。知其溺矣，急拯之。乃得两人尸，相抱持甚固，钮相结也，带相束也。大守哀痛，合葬之。
    　　所沉水中，故有莲花，自是皆并蒂。
    　　非非子曰，宋咸淳末，海寇犯扬州。有曹璧、张丽春夫妇，相搂溺池中。逾年生并蒂莲花。又有男女相慕，赶水死，陂中姑婢。瓜期末及，已解怜郎，藤蔓相牵，乃同聘妇。惊青衣之窃窕，见即魂消；感白扇之凄凉，怜因而乞。固知沉檀既藕，一气无分；胶漆相黏，两形俱化矣。然犹藏驱斗草，晦迹于痴顽；洗砚煎茶，假名于役使。银鱼尚觅，竹马仍驰，佯缠丱角之红，乱贴丫头之翠。

    Explanation

    345

    Coinciding with the Qingming Festival, my sister wished to visit the gravesite in Xiaoshan and take the maid along. The two of us stood before each other, deeply sorrowful. Thinking of the children, we believed they were attached to what they had learned and neglected them. On the eve of their departure, during the third watch of the night, the wind rose, and the door opened and closed with a sound. The family suspected a thief and got up to look, finding the door open. They traced to the riverside, where a pair of shoes was found, and returned surprised to examine the bedroom, discovering the maid was there but the children were gone. Rushing to the maid's place, they found she was also gone. Knowing they had drowned, they hurriedly rescued them, finding the two bodies, tightly embraced, with buttons tied together and belts bound together. The local official mourned deeply and buried them together.

    Where they were submerged in water, lotuses grew, and from then on, they were all twin-headed.

    The author, Feifeizi, said: At the end of the Xianchun era of the Song dynasty, sea bandits attacked Yangzhou. There was a couple named Cao Bi and Zhang Lichun who embraced and drowned in a pond. The following year, twin-headed lotuses grew. Another pair of lovers, mutually fond, drowned in water in a pond. By the time of the melon harvest, they already understood to love the young man, with vines intertwined, and were then betrothed to the same woman. Startled by the graceful young man in green clothes, they immediately lost their souls; feeling the sadness of the white fan, they pitied and begged. Certainly knowing that sandalwood and camphorwood were already like lotus roots, one breath, no division; glue and lacquer stuck together, both forms were transformed. Yet still, they hid and drove away the fighting grass, hid their traces among the foolish and stupid; washed the inkstone and boiled tea, took on false names for service. Still searching for silver fish, still galloping on bamboo horses, pretending to tangle in the red of the young boy's topknot, messily pasting the green of the young girl's hairpin.

    346

    若其夜深私语，春暮长愁，焚石叶以分烟，照菱花而比玉。待阙鸳鸯社，悄悄安排；穿花蛱蝶图，闲闲点染。芳情不露，密约谁知？是以桐叶无凭，蓬根不固。时则禁烟节近，濡露心悲。家家焚榆荚之钱，处处奠梨花之酒。女媭欲去，人种难留。坐上鹧鸪，送佳人兮不能语；檐前鹦鹉，思公子兮未敢言。此意同缄，旁人讵解？皆曰鸦雏燕觳，犹有童心，宁知凤侣鸾俦，竟非儿戏？
    　　于是牵衣掩泣，解佩吞声，神已全痴，梦常半醒。杨花杨树，明日分离；桃叶桃根，何时迎接？卿虽念我，其如雁杳鱼沉？侄不从姑，便看云飞雨散。红笺写恨，菱叟难逢；香楮祈神，潮王不管。事已如此，计无复之。与其相望于天河，孰若同游于水府？烟波十里，是双鱼比目之乡，木柝三更，乃孤月伤心之候。罗衣纽结，尚愁死后分开；绣舄留遗，早向生前解脱。遂乃灯昏绮阁，风戛朱扉，一室皆惊，两人长逝矣。

    Explanation

    346

    In the depths of night, they whispered secrets, long sorrow lingering at the end of spring. They burned stone leaves to release fragrant smoke, and compared her beauty to a lily or jade. They awaited the gathering of the swans, quietly making arrangements; they painted butterflies among flowers, leisurely adding strokes. Their hidden emotions remained unexpressed, their secret agreements known only to those involved. Thus, their promises were without foundation, their roots not firmly established. As the Qingming Festival approached, their hearts filled with sorrow as the dew fell. Households burned willow leaves, and everywhere they offered wine to the ancestors. The woman wished to leave, and it was hard to keep the people. On the seat, the partridge, unable to speak, sent off the beloved; before the eaves, the parrot, thinking of the young lord, dared not utter a word. This meaning was kept secret, and others could not understand. All said that young birds and sparrows still had a childlike innocence, how could they know that noble pairs were not mere play?

    Then, they held onto their clothes and wept, took off their jade pendants and swallowed their words, their spirits completely entranced, their dreams often half-awake. The willow flowers and trees, tomorrow they would part; the peach leaves and roots, when would they welcome each other? Though you may miss me, what can be done when geese and fish are lost? The nephew does not follow the aunt, and the clouds fly, the rain disperses. They wrote their grievances on red paper, but the elderly man of the lily pond was hard to find; they burned incense to pray to the gods, but the King of the Tides did not intervene. The matter was already as it was, there was no way to change it. Rather than look across the Milky Way, how much better to travel together in the underwater realm? Ten miles of misty water, it was the land of paired fish; the wooden watchman's stick at midnight, it was the hour of the lonely moon causing sorrow. The buttons on the woman's robe, still fearing they would be separated after death; the embroidered shoes left behind, early released from the bonds of life. Then, the lights dimmed in the luxurious room, the wind blew against the red door, everyone in the room was surprised, and both had passed away.

    347

    于斯时也，波臣为之动色，泉客为之怆心。湘灵鼓瑟以来迎，太乙浮槎而赴吊。郁金玳瑁，栖燕咸惊；丽玉箜篌，枯鱼亦泣。况关骨肉，能保肝肠，爰含肌肤，以嘉魂魄。纵同棺椁，初无殉葬之名，曾抱衾裯，不背嫁殇之禁。天荒地老，水远山长。虽沉玉于黄沙，终埋香于青草。若耶溪浅，原邻郑旦之村，幼妇碑高，恰近曹娥之墓。泉台女伴，莫唱“大郎神”，乐府词人，应歌“孺子妾”。
    　　呜呼！姻缘有恨，今古同悲。儿女何知，死生不变。

    Explanation

    347

    At this time, the spirits of the sea were moved to color, and the spirits of the springs were made sorrowful. The Lady of Xiang played the zither to come and welcome, and the deity Taiyi floated on a raft to come and mourn. Precious materials like saffron and tortoiseshell, and the perching swallows were all startled; the beautiful jade konghou, and the dried fish also wept. How much more so for close family members, who could protect their inner feelings, and hold their flesh and skin to honor the soul and spirit. Even if buried in the same coffin, there was no practice of burying people alive as sacrifices, and even if holding bedding or clothing, it did not violate the prohibition against burying a bridegroom who died before marriage. Heaven and earth grow old, waters are far and mountains are long. Even if the jade sank in the yellow sand, it would ultimately be buried in the green grass. The Ruo Ye Creek is shallow, originally neighboring the village of Zheng Dan, and the monument to a young wife is high, just near the tomb of Cao E.
