The female companions in the underworld should not sing "Great Lord God," and the musicians of the imperial court should sing "Young Wife."

    Alas! Marital ties are filled with sorrow, and this has been a shared lament throughout history. Children do not know, but love and death remain unchanged.

    348

    至使断肠名草，洒泪成花，梨著雌雄，兰开夫妇。墓上相思之梓，庭前蠲忿之棠，丛筠留妃子之斑，丽卉学美入之舞。
    　　如兹韵事，尤有祥徵。本图碧树之交枝，翻作红蕖之并蒂。情根既种，无奈丝长，意蕊虽开，依然心苦。然而人间露水，岂卜长圆？无上星河，犹愁远隔。曷若痴魂自在，千年翡翠之巢，艳福同消，十亩荚蓉之馆。童男雨迸，错落成珠，少女风香，玲珑透玉。听《江南》‘采莲”之曲，尽游戏于月白风清，歌《子夜》‘同藕’之词，莫感怀于水流花谢！
    　　河东丐者
    　　有新鬼者苦馁，往见旧鬼而问术焉。旧鬼怒其无贽，绐之曰：“子欲得食，易与耳。有樵于终南之旁者，于代为负薪，彼且享子。”新鬼信之，附樵者之担而致其力数日，樵者不知也。偶憩路旁，新鬼负其担而趋。樵见薪之自行也，且骇且驰。鬼恐失樵，迅逐之。樵至家，而薪亦至。疑薪妖也，燎诸大门之外，终不食鬼。

    Explanation

    348

    To the point where the grass, known for breaking hearts, weeps tears that turn into flowers; pears with male and female parts, and orchids that bloom as couples. On the tomb, the paulownia tree symbolizes longing, and in the courtyard, the catalpa tree represents the removal of anger. Bamboo retains the marks of a concubine, and beautiful flowers learn the dance of a beautiful woman.

    Such elegant events are accompanied by special signs. Originally intending for the intertwined branches of green trees, it turned into the paired blossoms of red lotuses. Once the roots of emotion are planted, it is inevitable for the threads to grow long, and though the buds of feeling may open, the heart remains troubled. However, the dew of human life, how can one predict its permanence? The Milky Way, even the highest, still worries about being far apart. Why not let the soul of a fool be free, in a thousand-year nest of emeralds, sharing the beauty and fortune, in a ten-acre pavilion of lotuses. Boys splash water, forming pearls, and girls' fragrance permeates like jade. Listening to the songs of lotus picking from the Jiangnan collection, enjoying the clear moonlight and gentle breeze, singing the words of Ziye about the same lotus, do not feel sad about the passing of water and flowers.

    A Beggar from Hedong
    There was a new ghost suffering from hunger, who went to see an old ghost to ask for advice. The old ghost was angry that the new ghost had no offering, and tricked him, saying, "If you want to get food, it's easy. There is a woodcutter near Mount Zhongnan, who carries firewood for others, and he will share with you." The new ghost believed him, and for several days, he helped the woodcutter carry the load, but the woodcutter did not know. One day, they stopped by the roadside, and the new ghost took the woodcutter's load and ran away. The woodcutter saw the firewood moving by itself and was both startled and ran after it. The ghost was afraid of losing the woodcutter, and quickly chased after him. When the woodcutter reached home, the firewood also arrived. Suspecting that the firewood was a supernatural entity, he burned it outside the main gate, and it never consumed the ghost.

    349

    鬼由是竟饥且惫，复往见旧鬼，咎其无验。旧鬼笑曰：“向亦戏子耳，是固恶可得食也？山下某氏将祭，请与子俱。”
    　　既至，有衣冠而拜于墓者，鱼肉在俎，果实在笾，爵有酒，盂有浆。墓中有鬼出。避其拜，涕泣而不忍尝食。新鬼馋甚，径前掬嘬之。忽有狞鬼扼其喉，执而系之树。讫于其既，以馂馀分啖诸鬼，独新鬼以攘食故，怒不与，且鞭而后释之。
    　　冤苦无所诉，忽大悟曰：“物各有主，固不以幽明异也。吾独无家乎？”乃梦诸其子，语以前事。于泣而祭之，然贫不备物，莱羹葁粥而已。鬼不暇择，餍饱而去。既饥，复走告其子，子淅疑之曰：“父已死矣，而频频索食，当不其然，其有伪而托者欤？”竟不祭。

    Explanation

    349

    The ghost was thus starving and exhausted, and he returned to see the old ghost, blaming him for not proving true. The old ghost laughed and said, "I was just joking with you. This is inherently evil and cannot be eaten. A certain family in the mountains below is about to perform a sacrifice. Please go with me."

    After arriving, they saw someone in formal attire bowing at the tomb. Fish and meat were on the cutting board, and fruits were in the basket. The wine vessel had wine, and the bowl had water. Suddenly, a ghost emerged from the tomb. The new ghost, avoiding the bow, wept and could not bear to eat. Being very hungry, he went straight forward and scooped and sucked the offerings. Suddenly, a fierce ghost choked his throat, grabbed him, and tied him to the tree. Afterward, he distributed the leftovers to the other ghosts. Only the new ghost, because of his greedy eating, was angry and refused to give, and then beat him and released him.

    Feeling wronged and having nowhere to appeal, he suddenly had a great realization and said, "Each thing has its owner, and this does not change whether it is visible or invisible. Do I not have a home?" He then dreamed of his son and told him about the previous events. He wept and performed a sacrifice for him. However, he was poor and could not provide the necessary items, only offering a simple vegetable soup and porridge. The ghost did not have time to choose, and after eating his fill, left. After becoming hungry again, he went to tell his son. His son hesitated and doubted, saying, "Father has already died, yet he frequently asks for food. This does not seem right. Could there be someone pretending and deceiving?" Finally, he did not perform the sacrifice.

    350

    鬼窘甚无策，而视他鬼不常食，亦不甚苦饥，走谒主者叩其故。主者曰：“此业报也。予生时烹鲜割肥，极口腹之奉，物以珍，家以贫，故身死而肠胃犹生，不尔更也。”鬼大觉悟，哀主者而告之悔，请得自比于众鬼，弗许。
    　　乃痛恨闭居墓中，不复出，竟饿死为聱，而饥弥甚。不得已，复崇诸鬼间得一饱。忽自讼：“术有穷而饥无已，何为自苦？且既为聱矣，宁忧复死？”忽金光中现一人如菩萨状，以指弹之。则身已在墓中，复转生为鬼，俯仰甚乐。念向者望为鬼不可得，一念之复，幸得至此，得长为饿鬼足矣。于是仍坐墓中，不出，亦渐不甚饥。深悔生时之饕餮，欲补过而道无由也。
    　　久之，主者召之去，曰：“尔能白责，可以为人，但宰杀过多，膏腴之福尽矣。残羹榺炙，其可饱也。”使往生丐者之家，宿因了了。遂持戒不茹荤腥，乞食河东。往来郡县里党间，辄自述之。常言鬼多于人，聱又多于鬼，又言人不尽生，鬼亦不尽死。

    Explanation

    350

    The ghost was in dire straits with no strategy, and he observed that other ghosts did not often suffer from hunger, nor did they experience great distress from it. He went to the master and inquired about the reason. The master said, "This is the result of your karma. In my life, I cooked fresh and fatty foods, indulging in the pleasures of the palate. Treating things as precious led to poverty, so even after death, my intestines remain alive, and I cannot change this." The ghost had a profound realization and, grieving before the master, confessed his remorse. He asked to be treated as one of the other ghosts, but the master did not permit it.

    Enraged, he closed himself in the tomb and did not come out again. Ultimately, he starved to death and became a spirit of hunger, and his hunger grew even more intense. Unable to bear it, he was reborn among the ghosts and managed to eat his fill. Suddenly, he began to reflect, "There is a limit to my skills, but my hunger knows no end. Why should I torment myself? And since I am already a spirit of hunger, why should I fear death again?" Suddenly, a person appeared in golden light, resembling a bodhisattva, and touched him with a finger. Then, he found himself back in the tomb, reborn as a ghost, and found great joy in his new existence. Reflecting on how he once longed to be a ghost but could not, he now finds happiness in his rebirth, and he is content to remain a hungry ghost forever. Thus, he stayed in the tomb, not emerging, and his hunger gradually diminished. He deeply regretted his gluttonous ways in life and wished to make amends, but found no way to do so.

    After some time, the master summoned him and said, "If you can confess and repent, you can be reborn as a human. But because you killed too many animals, the blessings of rich and fatty foods are gone. Only scraps and leftovers can satisfy you." So he was sent to be reborn in the home of a beggar, and his past karma was clear. He kept the precepts and did not eat meat, begging for food in Hedong. He traveled between counties and villages, often recounting his story. He often said that there are more ghosts than people, and more spirits of hunger than ghosts, and he also said that not all people are born, and not all ghosts are dead.

    351

    宝　剑
    　　李介夫言：浙江赵君长人，豪迈好侠。所携剑，宝剑也以慑鬼怪，往往验。
    　　常佐某公幕。署后有室一堵，华且敞，而蓬蒿蔽之。以问胥吏，曰；“居者辄死，或竟失所在，用是鞠为茂草。”赵笑曰：“恶有是！”以避喧良便，遂翦草，解装其中，使二仆共卧对室。
    　　其夜，二仆纵饮他所，赵方燃烛阅官牍，闻屏外啄门声，呼问无应。少焉门大启，觉有物入，喷息甚厉。俄焉及寝门，触帘，帘动。赵异之，乃仗剑秉烛挑帘出，则见有口大如牖，其身崔嵬若山，几与椽梁。急以剑掷口中，物竟吞剑。复以烛并锡具掷之，物似负痛，乃转首急去。屏门尽倾。有顷，二仆来，赵呼语之，亟移榻去。

    Explanation

    351

    The Precious Sword
    Li Jiefu said: Zhao Junchang, a man from Zhejiang, was generous and fond of chivalry. The sword he carried was a precious one, capable of intimidating ghosts and monsters, and it often proved effective.
    He often served as an aide to a certain official. Behind the office, there was a room with one wall, which was both beautiful and spacious but overgrown with grass. He asked the clerks about it, and they said, "Anyone who stays there either dies or disappears, so it has become overgrown with dense grass." Zhao laughed and said, "How could there be such a thing!" Considering it a good place to avoid noise, he cut the grass, unpacked his belongings, and had two servants sleep in the adjacent room.
    That night, the two servants went to drink elsewhere. Zhao was reading official documents by candlelight when he heard a tapping sound outside the screen door. He called out, but there was no response. Soon, the door opened wide, and he felt something enter, breathing heavily and fiercely. It soon reached the bedroom door, touching the curtain and making it move. Zhao was surprised and, holding his sword and candle, pulled the curtain aside. He saw a mouth as large as a window, and its body was towering like a mountain, almost reaching the rafters. He quickly threw the sword into its mouth, and the creature swallowed it. Then he threw the candle and tin utensils at it, and the creature seemed to feel pain, turning its head and hurrying away. The screen door completely collapsed. Soon after, the two servants returned, and Zhao called out to them, urging them to quickly move the bed away.

    352

    次日，见血于门外淋浪相属。迹之十馀里，得穴于山旁，有巨蟒死焉。剖腹求剑，不得，烛具乃无恙。
    　　蛟
    　　乾隆癸卯二月，金谿北鄙祟岭崩，蛟也。大雨雹，风霆怒甚，山下村几墟、民几鱼，其暴如此。
    　　郡中故多蚊。某年小山出九蚊，得九穴，然不为暴。某年夏雨甚，邻里陈坊桥涨及于梁。有田父荷锄过桥上，见两巨蛇黄色，队行水中。随以锄击之，毙其一，致之桥上。闻者皆来观。已见上流有浮滓如席，去梁数丈，盘旋不前。——浮滓者，相传蚊属行水中，用以自覆者也。——于是观者皆走避，浮滓乃奔下，势若山裂，浪沸起，高丈许，粱不尽榻，涨亦顿落，而人无损者。若此皆不为民暴者也。
    　　闻古老言：唐太守在吾郡时，选材官攸飞，教之伐蛟，其法不传矣。验蚊之法：于大雪时四山望之，无雪处，其下乃蚊窟。

    352

    The next day, blood was seen spilling out of the door, flowing continuously. Tracing the source over ten miles, they found a cave by a mountain where a giant python had died. They cut open its body in search of a sword, but did not find it, and the candle and other items were unharmed.

    Regarding the蛟, in the second year of the Qianlong reign period, the northern outskirts of Jinxī were devastated by a崩, which was caused by the蛟. There was a heavy hailstorm, with fierce wind and thunder, and the village at the foot of the mountain was almost destroyed, with the people in great danger. The蛟's destructive power was evident in this manner.

    In the county, there were many mosquitoes. In a certain year, nine mosquitoes emerged from a small mountain, and nine nests were found, but they did not cause any harm. In another year, during a heavy summer rain, the bridge in the neighborhood of Chenfang was submerged. A farmer carrying a hoe walked over the bridge and saw two large yellow snakes moving in a line in the water. He followed with his hoe and killed one, placing it on the bridge. Those who heard about it all came to watch. Soon, they saw floating debris resembling a mat, several feet away from the bridge, moving back and forth. ——This floating debris, it is said, is what the mosquitoes use to cover themselves when they move in the water. ——As a result, the spectators all fled in fear, and the floating debris rushed downstream, with the force as if the mountain were splitting. The waves rose and boiled, reaching a height of several feet, but the bridge was not completely destroyed, and the water level dropped suddenly, with no harm done to the people. Such events did not cause harm to the people.

    Hearing from ancient people, during the time when the prefect of Tang was in our county, officials who could fly were selected, and they were taught to hunt down the蛟. However, the method was not passed down. The method of verifying the presence of mosquitoes: during a heavy snowfall, look at the four mountains. The places without snow are the mosquito nests.

    353

    平阳生
    　　平阳生，无能举其姓名者。或曰平阳人，或曰平阳其郡望也，世为清门，多隐德，至生稍凌夷矣。生有奇表，末龀，言语辄惊人。稍长，出而亡焉。至十五始归，竟喑哑。不栉不沐，首不冠。足不履，腰不束带。敝衣一袭，常服之，冬不裘，夏不葛。住无常所，亦不常饮食。所行类有道，又类依隐玩世者。
    　　有士人阴异之，辄与之游，欲以观其私，醉之酒，以观其真，遗之钱，以观其节，激之使怒，以观其度。生或饮或不饮，或醉或不醉，或受或不受，或怒或不怒，卒莫得而测也。
    　　他日者霪雨既霁，士人偶出于野，日垂暮矣。窃见生行泥淖中，不汙不陷，犹犹然。奇而尾之，则入古庙中。蔽门。隙窥之。庙中土偶见生，皆起迓，生与抗礼，坐石上共语，为洸洋恣肆之言甚隐跃。或可闻，或不可闻，或可解，或不可解。始知生非真哑者，大讶趋归。

    Explanation

    353

    Pingyang's Native
    Pingyang's native, whose surname and given name cannot be named. Some say he is from Pingyang, others say Pingyang is his ancestral home. His family has been of high moral standing, with many hidden virtues, but by the time of this person, the family's status had declined somewhat. This person had a remarkable appearance, even before the age of eight, his speech was often astonishing. As he grew older, he disappeared. By the age of fifteen, he returned, but he was mute. He did not comb his hair or bathe, did not wear a cap, did not wear shoes, and did not wear a belt. He wore a single tattered robe, which he always wore, did not wear fur in winter, and did not wear light clothing in summer. He had no fixed place to live and did not eat regularly. His actions were similar to those of a person who follows the Way, and also similar to those of a person who lives in seclusion and plays with the world.

    A scholar found him strange and followed him, wanting to observe his private life, he got him drunk to see his true nature, gave him money to see his principles, provoked him to anger to see his composure. This person sometimes drank, sometimes did not drink, sometimes got drunk, sometimes did not get drunk, sometimes accepted money, sometimes did not accept money, sometimes got angry, sometimes did not get angry, and ultimately, no one could measure him.

    One day, after a heavy rain had stopped, the scholar happened to be out in the wild, and the sun was setting. He secretly saw this person walking through the muddy ground, without getting dirty or sinking, and he walked calmly. He found him strange and followed him, and then he entered an ancient temple. He hid behind the door and peered through a crack. The clay figurines in the temple saw this person and all stood up to greet him. This person treated them as equals, sat on a stone and talked together, speaking in expansive and unrestrained words, which were very obscure or enigmatic. Some things could be heard, some could not; some things could be understood, some could not. He began to realize that this person was not truly mute, and he was greatly surprised and hurried back home.

    354

    明日生来，士人为勿知也者，而强留之宿。比夜，再拜而请曰：“吾知公仙人也，幸有以教之！”生笑曰：“我何仙？幻术耳。子既侦而识之，姑勿泄。当有以娱子。”因袒而示之，胸有方孔寸许，谓士人曰：“盍进之？”士人笑未信，试举踵焉，则超超然已升，绰绰焉而行，盖入生之胸中矣。
    　　其初，如驷马之门。其既，如九轨之途，百雉之城。万井之邑。
    　　苍然而高者天也，蔚然而叠者山也，渟然而流者江河也。有耕于野者，有负于途者，有往来游戏者，有呵道队仗行者，有追逐者、喧笑者。其物有木，有草，有石，有飞鸟，有鸡，狗、马、羊、豕。适于其市，杂然而陈者，无所不有。其人，男女犹是也，服饰犹是也，言语犹是也，居处饮食犹是也。士人行焉，止焉，食焉，息焉。心廓然而舒，神焕然而畅，亦几忘其身之在生之胸中也。

    Explanation

    354

    The next day, the guest arrived, and the scholar pretended not to know, but insisted on keeping him for the night. By evening, the scholar bowed twice and respectfully asked, "I know you are a celestial being; I hope you will instruct me!" The guest smiled and said, "What makes me a celestial being? It's just magic tricks. You have already observed and recognized it; do not reveal it for now. I will find a way to entertain you." He then undressed to show his chest, which had a square hole about an inch in size, and said to the scholar, "Why don't you step into it?" The scholar, still skeptical, tried stepping into it. He rose up swiftly and moved gracefully, having entered the guest's chest.

    At first, it was like a gate for four horses. Later, it became like a road for nine carriages, a city a hundred zhi high, and a town with ten thousand wells. The high and vast expanse was the sky; the layered and lush formations were mountains; the tranquil and flowing bodies were rivers and lakes. There were people farming in the fields, carrying loads on the roads, coming and going, playing, and walking with guards and attendants. There were people chasing each other and laughing loudly. The things there included trees, grass, stones, flying birds, chickens, dogs, horses, sheep, and pigs. In its markets, everything was mixed together, and there was nothing that was not there. The people, men and women alike, wore the same clothes, spoke the same language, lived in the same way, and ate the same food. The scholar walked there, stopped there, ate there, and rested there. His mind was broad and relaxed, and his spirit was bright and free; he almost forgot that he was inside the guest's chest.

    355

    三日，至一所，其郊原如绨锦，城郭如云霞，宫室如珠贝。入焉，见其人皆衣绣而冠玉，餐香而饮雪。翠竹蔽其墉，瑶草环其阶，绎花拂其檐，孔翠鸾鹤舞于庭户之间。无风而神籁韵于耳者，笙璈琴管之音也。旗旌摇摇，帘幞垂垂。栏楯纵横，窗扉四开。彝鼎几研之属，陈不一处。于是意迷神眩，仿徨焉而莫知所向。
    　　俄有童子搴帷出，谓之曰；“观止乎？未也。盍随以来？”士人欣然武其后，历数闼，曲折洞达。行其左，奉佛之堂寂以高，经其右，祀仙之馆净以广，升其中，庋书之阁光明而邃奥。进抵其内，有巨人焉，坐圆台之上。士人敬且畏，仰而瞻，俯而拜，旁而伺，就而问；巨人若弗见焉、弗闻焉，不言不动，寂然如止水。槁然如枯木，屹然若顽石。俄有捧衣进履，陈牛羊、具酒浆而进者，巨人如故也。

    Explanation

    355

    After three days, they arrived at a place where the countryside was like brocade, the city walls like clouds and dawns, and the palaces like pearls and mother-of-pearl. Upon entering, they saw that the people were all dressed in embroidered clothes and wore jade hats, eating fragrant food and drinking snow. Lush green bamboo surrounded the walls, jade-like grass circled the steps, and embroidered flowers brushed against the eaves, while peacocks, phoenixes, and cranes danced in the courtyards. Even without wind, the sounds of flutes and zithers echoed in the air. Flags and curtains swayed gently, railings crisscrossed, and windows were open on all sides. Ritual objects and writing implements were arranged in different places. Thus, the scholar was bewildered and dazzled, wandering aimlessly without knowing where to go.

    Soon, a young boy pulled back the curtain and said, "Have you seen enough? Not yet. Why not come along?" The scholar happily followed behind, passing through many doors, navigating a winding and open path. On the left, there was a quiet and lofty Buddhist hall; on the right, a clean and spacious hall for worshipping immortals; and ahead, a bright and deep library. They entered the innermost part of the place, where a giant sat on a round platform. The scholar was both respectful and fearful, looking up to gaze at him, bowing down, and observing him from the side, then approaching to ask. The giant seemed neither to see nor hear, remaining silent and motionless, still as still water, dry as dry wood, and solid as a rock. Soon, servants brought clothes, shoes, and offerings of cattle, sheep, and wine, but the giant remained unchanged.

    356

    又有赍金怀玉及舁货贝而入者，巨人如故也。于是皆退。少焉，人报曰：“患至矣！”乃闻戈马汹涌，破门而入，环而攻之者皆张弓露刃之夫，复有狞鬼群魔跳踉乎其前，鸷兽毒蛇盘踞乎其后。士人股栗噤伏，魂魄震慑，而巨人亦如故也。顷之，纷纷者欻然俱灭。士人欲趋出，乃有女子旖旎而来，花颜而霞衣，云烟五色，缥缈护之，若隐若见，且前且却。忽有红树生台下，须臾寻丈，状类珊瑚，巨人乃震荡欲堕。俄空中一剑飞出，断树砉然。女子隐去，巨人始定焉。

    Explanation

    356

    There were those who entered carrying gold and jade, as well as goods and shells, and the giant remained as before. Then, they all retreated. Shortly afterward, someone reported, "Trouble has arrived!" Then, they heard the sound of swords and horses surging, breaking through the door and entering. Those surrounding and attacking were all armed with drawn bows and exposed blades, and there were also fierce ghosts and demonic hordes leaping and bounding before them, with fierce beasts and venomous snakes crouching behind them. The scholar trembled and cowered in silence, shocked to the soul, and the giant remained as before. Soon, the tumultuous ones suddenly vanished. The scholar wanted to hurry out, but then a woman approached gracefully, with a flower-like face and a rosy garment, surrounded by five-colored clouds and mists, shrouded in a misty veil, partly hidden and partly visible, advancing and retreating. Suddenly, a red tree grew beneath the terrace, in a moment it grew to several feet tall, resembling coral, and the giant shook as if about to fall. Soon, a sword flew out from the air, snapping the tree with a crack. The woman disappeared, and the giant finally settled.

    357

    士人睹之，且喜且怵，忽念此固生之胸中也，何为乎不归？前童子在侧，笑曰：“故道不可复识矣。”引之出自旁门，指画其途曰：“由此其可达也。”士入遵面行，行不计程，宿不计处，朝而暮者不计日，朔而晦者不计月，寒而暑者不计岁。向之来也，草木青青而华也，而今黄落矣，未几则又甲坼矣，水波溶溶也，而今潦降潭清矣，未几而川复涨矣，风暖而日喧也，而今霜雪载途矣，未几而阴谷之冰凘矣，燕则再雏矣，雁则再宾矣，月弦而璧者不知其度。时倏然其速也，途杳然其长而无极也。于是感怆悲涕，苦远不得归。疑其梦也。而非梦也，疑其死也，而非死也。遂大呼生曰：“公戏我，公戏我！”

    Explanation

    357

    The scholar saw it and felt both joy and fear. Suddenly, he thought, "This must be something that has always been in my heart. Why haven't I returned?" The young boy standing beside him smiled and said, "The ancient path can no longer be recognized." He led the scholar out through a side door and pointed out the way, saying, "This path can lead you there." The scholar followed and walked without counting the distance, stayed without counting the places, from morning to evening without counting the days, from the beginning to the end of the month without counting the months, from cold to hot without counting the years. When he first came, the grass and trees were lush and blooming, but now they are yellow and fallen. Soon after, they sprouted again. The water was calm and clear, but now it has risen and the pond is clear. Soon after, the river swelled again. The wind was warm and the sun was bright, but now frost and snow cover the road. Soon after, the ice in the valley has melted. The swallows have nested again, and the geese have returned as guests. The moon waxes and wanes, and its phases are unknown. Time passes swiftly, and the path is long and endless. Thus, he felt sorrowful and cried, suffering from the distance and inability to return. He doubted it was a dream, but it was not a dream; he doubted it was death, but it was not death. Then he shouted to the living one, "You are playing with me, you are playing with me!"

    358

    忽自生左耳中落。视残红犹明焉，墙外之柝四声耳。生则酣寝榻上，推而起之，乃大笑趋出门外。自是不复见。
    　　——士人者，姓周，失其名。
    　　爱　驴
    　　某翁富而吝，善权子母，责负无虚日。后以年且老，艰于途，遂买一驴代步，顾爱惜甚至，非甚困惫，未尝肯据鞍。驴出翁胯下者，岁不过数四。

    Explanation

    358

    Suddenly, he emerged from the left ear. Seeing the remaining red still bright, it was only the sound of four beats of a drum from outside the wall. He then slept soundly on the bed, and when pushed up, he laughed loudly and hurried out of the door. From then on, he was no longer seen.
    —The scholar, surnamed Zhou, whose name is unknown.
    Love for a Donkey
    A certain old man was rich but stingy, skilled in manipulating capital, and had no day without debt. Later, because he was old, it was difficult for him to travel, so he bought a donkey to use as transportation. He loved and cherished it very much, and unless he was very tired, he would not sit on the saddle. The donkey came out from under the old man's legs only a few times a year.

    359

    值天暑，有所索于远道，不得已，与驴俱。中道翁喘，乃跨驴。驰二三里，驴不习骑，亦喘。翁惊，亟下，解其鞍。驴以为息己也，望故道逸归。翁急遽呼驴，驴走不顾，追之弗及也。大惧驴亡，又吝于弃鞍，因负鞍趋。归家，亟问驴在否，其子曰：“驴在。”翁乃复喜，徐释鞍，始觉足顿而背裂也，又伤于暑，病逾月乃瘥。
    　　吴　生
    　　荆州田舍翁何某，乡里称长者。乾隆四年春，有葛衣人来访之，自云：“家江右，吴姓。途穷求助。”何辞焉，而视其人，虽敝衣赢尪，神采特异，乃复问之，曰：“君颇识字否？”吴曰：“我固诸生。”何曰：“儿辈方求师傅，敢以辱先生。俟秋获所入，敬戒行李。可乎？”吴曰：“诺。”遂洁馆舍，　卜日使二子煜、熥及从子燧受业焉。既严且勤，颇尽师职。自夏徂秋，亦殊不言去。岁时修脯，悉却不受，曰：“但求吃饭处，奚以金为？”

    Explanation

    359

    On a hot day, the old man had to fetch something from a distant place and, out of necessity, took his donkey with him. Halfway there, the old man became out of breath and climbed onto the donkey. After riding for two or three miles, the donkey, not accustomed to being ridden, also became out of breath. The old man was startled and quickly dismounted, removing the saddle. The donkey thought the old man was resting and ran back to the old road. The old man urgently called out to the donkey, but the donkey ignored him and ran away, making it impossible for the old man to catch up. Greatly afraid that the donkey would escape, and reluctant to discard the saddle, the old man carried the saddle and hurried back. Upon returning home, he urgently asked if the donkey was still there. His son replied, "The donkey is here." The old man was relieved and slowly removed the saddle, only then realizing he had a sore foot and a split back. He was also injured by the heat and took over a month to recover.

    Feng Sheng

    An old man from the countryside in Jingzhou named He, respected by his village, was visited in the spring of the fourth year of the Qianlong reign by a man wearing a coarse cloth robe. The man introduced himself, saying, "I am from Jiangxi and my surname is Wu. I am in dire straits and seek your help." He politely declined, but upon seeing the man, despite his ragged clothes and emaciated appearance, he noticed something peculiar about his demeanor. He asked, "Do you know how to read and write?" The man replied, "I am a scholar." He said, "My sons are looking for a teacher. I dare not trouble you, but if you agree, I will provide you with a place to stay and a stipend after the autumn harvest. Would that be acceptable?" The man agreed, "Yes." He then prepared a clean room and chose a good day for the man to begin teaching his two sons, Yu, Teng, and his nephew, Sui. The scholar was both strict and diligent, fully fulfilling his role as a teacher. From summer to autumn, he never mentioned leaving. The annual stipend was offered, but he completely refused to accept it, saying, "I only seek a place to eat. Why need gold?"

    360

    既三年，何有姻娅许某者，夜经何宅后，见一人裸身被髪，拜月于丛树之间。审之，吴生也，大骇急去。及返诣何，侦吴生。吴生方午餐，愈疑之。乃以状告何，且曰：“诡异若此，不遣且为患。”先是，何以吴衣葛无以御冬，制缊袍赠之。吴生笑不受，而衣葛如故，亦未见其寒也。心窃疑侄，至是闻许言，深然之。乃谓吴生曰：“先生固有归志，为儿辈羁留久矣。敢具刍粮，为君启途。”即袖中出十金为赆，吴笑而受之，曰：“即今日行矣。”何请暂留，为杯酒之饯。吴坚谢，遂长揖别去。

    Explanation

    360

    After three years, Mr. He had a relative by marriage named Mr. Xu. One night, as Mr. Xu passed by Mr. He's residence, he saw a man naked with his hair loose, worshipping the moon among the trees. Upon closer inspection, he realized it was Wu Sheng. Mr. Xu was greatly frightened and fled quickly. Upon returning to Mr. He, he investigated Wu Sheng. Wu Sheng was having lunch, which only increased Mr. He's suspicion. He then reported the situation to Mr. He, saying, "So strange and unusual, if not dealt with, it will become a problem." Previously, Mr. He had given Wu Sheng a cotton robe because he couldn't protect himself from the cold. Mr. He made a cotton robe and gave it to Wu Sheng, but Wu Sheng laughed and refused, continuing to wear his usual clothes and showing no signs of feeling the cold. Mr. He secretly suspected his nephew. Upon hearing what Mr. Xu said, he deeply agreed with it. He then said to Wu Sheng, "You have always had the intention to return home, but have been held back by my children for a long time. Dare I prepare some provisions to start your journey?" He immediately took out ten gold pieces as a farewell gift. Wu Sheng laughed and accepted them, saying, "I will leave today." Mr. He asked him to stay temporarily to have a farewell drink, but Wu Sheng firmly declined and took a long bow before leaving.

    361

    他日，何策卫而出，复遇吴灌綐溪边，因问曰：“先生未归耶？”吴曰：“方授徒东村李氏，未有行期。”款叙数语，别而行。前二三里，复见吴坐大树下，颇疑诧。吴见而笑曰：“引避不及，幸恕之。”何唯唯驰去。过李氏之门，却见吴生与李立门外，乃大诧。李固何旧识，乃前诘吴曰：“顷两遇先生，遽已在此，何间道之速也？”吴不答。李笑曰：“先生固未出，公焉得见之？得毋误否？”何默然，曳李于内，问吴所自来，且述其异。李曰：“适主于我四栽矣。”计其在李氏时，即其在何氏时也。相与惊绝。趋出视吴，吴已去，后遂不复至。

    Explanation

    361

    One day, He Cewei went out, and on the way, he met Wu Guan by the stream. He asked, "Has the Master returned yet?" Wu Guan replied, "I am currently teaching at the Li family in Dongcun and have no set departure date." They chatted briefly and then parted ways. A few miles ahead, He Cewei saw Wu Guan sitting under a big tree, which surprised him greatly. Wu Guan saw him and laughed, saying, "We couldn't avoid each other, please forgive me." He Cewei nodded and rode away. Passing by the Li household, he saw Wu Guan and Li standing outside, which surprised him even more. Li, being an old acquaintance of He Cewei, confronted Wu Guan, asking, "You just met me twice, and now you're already here. How fast did you take the shortcut?" Wu Guan did not answer. Li laughed and said, "The Master hasn't left yet, how could you have seen him? Could there be a mistake?" He Cewei remained silent, dragged Li inside, and asked about Wu Guan's origins, recounting the strange events. Li said, "I just arrived at my place." Calculating the time, it was the same time as when Wu Guan was at the Li household. They were both shocked. Rushing out to see Wu Guan, they found him already gone, and he never came back again.

    362

    ——何燧官武清令，尝为人言之。
    　　猫　言
    　　某友言：某公夜将寝，闻窗外偶语，潜起窥之。时星月如昼，阒不见人，乃其家猫与邻猫言耳。邻猫曰：“西家娶妇，盍往觇乎？”家猫曰：“其厨娘善藏，不足税吾驾也。”邻猫又曰：“虽然，姑一行，何害？”家猫又曰：“无益也。”邻猫固邀，家猫固却，往复久之。邻猫跃登垣，犹遥呼曰：“若来若来！”家猫不得已，亦跃从之，曰：“聊奉伴耳。”某公大骇。

    Explanation

    362

    He Sui, the magistrate of Wucheng, once told someone about this.
    The Cat's Words

    A friend of mine said: One night, as Mr. So-and-so was about to go to bed, he heard some casual conversation outside his window. He quietly got up to look. The stars and moon were as bright as daytime, and there was no one to be seen. It turned out to be a conversation between his cat and a neighbor's cat. The neighbor's cat said, "The house to the west is getting married. Why not go and see?" The cat at home replied, "The kitchen maid is good at hiding things. It's not worth stopping my carriage." The neighbor's cat then said, "Even so, why not go once, what harm is there?" The cat at home answered, "It's not beneficial." The neighbor's cat insisted on inviting, and the cat at home firmly refused. They back and forth for a long time. The neighbor's cat leaped onto the wall and still called out from afar, "If you come, if you come!" The cat at home, unable to refuse, also leaped over and followed, saying, "Just to accompany you." Mr. So-and-so was greatly startled.

    363

    次日，执猫将杀之，因让之曰：“尔猫也，而人言耶？”猫应曰：“猫诚能言，然天下之猫皆能言也，庸独我乎？公既恶之，猫请勿言。”某公怒曰：“是真妖也！”引槌将击杀之，猫大呼曰：“天乎冤哉！吾真无罪也。虽然，愿一言而死。”某公曰：“若复何言？”猫曰：“使我果妖，公能执我乎？我不为妖，而公杀我，则我且为厉，公能复杀之乎？且我尝为公捕鼠。是有微劳于公也。有劳而杀之，或者其不祥乎？而鼠子闻之，相呼皆至，据廪以糜粟，穴簏而毁书，揓无完衣，室无整器，公不得一夕安枕而卧也。妖孰甚焉？故不如舍我，使得效爪牙之役，今日之惠，其宁敢忘？”某公笑而释之，猫竟逸去，亦无他异。

    Explanation

    363

    The next day, the man seized the cat and was about to kill it, saying to it, "You are a cat, but you speak like a human?" The cat replied, "I truly can speak, but all cats in the world can speak. Why should I be the only one? Since you dislike me, the cat asks not to speak." The man became angry and said, "This is truly a demon!" He took up a mallet and was about to kill it. The cat cried out, "Heaven, this is injustice! I am truly innocent. However, I wish to speak before I die." The man said, "What more can you say?" The cat said, "If I am truly a demon, can you catch me? If I am not a demon and you kill me, then I will become a ghost, can you kill me again? Moreover, I have often caught mice for you. This is a small service to you. To kill someone who has served you, is that not ominous? And the mice, hearing this, all come together, filling the granary with grain, burrowing into baskets and destroying books, tearing clothes to shreds, and leaving the room without any intact items, so you cannot sleep peacefully for a night. Which is more demonic than this? Therefore, it would be better to let me go, allowing me to continue serving as your guard, today's kindness, I will never forget." The man laughed and released the cat, which then escaped and there were no further strange occurrences.

    364

    《东阳夜怪录》记苗介立事，猫之能言，古有之矣。而此猫滑稽特甚，足为捧腹。
    　　瓜　异
    　　某公尝市一瓜，大可如斗，置案下。夜半地上有声，嘤咛如鸡雏千百。察之，自瓜中出也。视瓜，乃洞灼有光，如万星烂然射目。惊语左右，闻见皆同。使燃烛谛观，则声寂而光弢矣。置屋外，旦日剖之，戛然有声，然亦常瓜也。或啗之，味甚甘冽，无他验。

    Explanation

    364

    Translation:

    The Dongyang Night Strange Record recounts the story of Miao Jielì, a man who encountered a cat capable of speech, a phenomenon that has existed since ancient times. This particular cat was exceptionally humorous, enough to make one laugh heartily.

    Melon Anomaly

    Once, a gentleman bought a melon, which was as large as a winnowing basket, and placed it under the table. At midnight, there was a sound on the ground, like the chirping of thousands of chicks. Upon investigation, it was found that the sound was coming from the melon. When he examined the melon, he saw that it had a hole and emitted light, as if thousands of stars were shining brightly. He was shocked and told those around him, and they all heard and saw the same thing. He ordered a candle to be lit and carefully observed the melon, but then the sound ceased and the light was extinguished. He placed the melon outside the house and cut it open the next day, and there was a sudden noise, but it was just an ordinary melon. Some ate it, and it was very sweet and refreshing, with no other unusual effects.

    365

    秦　某
    　　宋某者，直隶玉田人。尝举进士，后以事被罪，配乌鲁木齐。白香幼随其考为边吏，尝从宋受书。时有秦某者，亦配军也，山西徐沟人，与宋特相善。每过宋斋，说往事，故白香闻而熟焉。白香告余曰：
    　　宋初至配所时，谒关庙，阶下立一人，年界五六十，疏眉而秀髯，　目宋而笑，若曾相识者。巳而呼宋曰：“子吾故人也，而不识几？”宋愕然，唯唯莫对。其入乃能道宋郡县族里氏名及事状甚悉，且曰：“田国荣来欤？然当至。”田国荣者，宋之家仆也，时果在道矣。于是宋闻之讶甚，曰：“仙乎哉，仙乎哉！何以能然？”亟叩其姓氏，则曰秦某也。秦谓宋曰：“吾所以识子，乃以梦故。虽然，言长矣，非立谈可讫。”遂与归其所居，述其详。

    Explanation

    365

    During the reign of Emperor Qianlong, there was a man named Song, who was from Yutian in Zhili (now part of Hebei Province). He once passed the imperial examination for the Jinshi degree and later, due to some matter, was punished and sent to Urumqi for labor service. White Xiang, who was young, followed his father as a border official and studied under Song. At that time, there was also a man named Qin, who was also sent to labor service. Qin was from Xugou in Shanxi Province and was especially close friends with Song. Every time he passed by Song's study room, Qin would talk about past events, and so White Xiang heard it and became familiar with it. White Xiang told me:

    When Song first arrived at his place of labor, he went to worship at the Guan Temple. On the steps below, there stood a person in his fifties or sixties, with sparse eyebrows and a beautiful beard. He looked at Song and smiled, as if they had met before. Soon, the person called out to Song, saying, "You are my old friend, but I don't remember when." Song was startled and nodded but did not respond. The person then told Song about his county, family, and other details with great accuracy, and added, "Is Tian Guorong coming? Indeed, he will arrive." Tian Guorong was Song's servant, and at that time, he was indeed on his way. Song was very surprised and said, "Truly a divine being, truly a divine being! How can you do this?" He quickly asked for the person's name and surname, and the person replied, "I am Qin." Qin said to Song, "I recognized you because of a dream. Although, the story is long, it cannot be finished in a brief conversation." Then, Qin invited Song to his residence and told him the details.

    366

    秦谓宋曰：“吾向在京师。某年除夕，忽梦为有司逮去，鞫于刑曹。狱成，配于此。亲旧相吊而别也，吏卒相拘而行也，山川关塞道里之所经，水陆舟车步马程宿之所身受，无不尽历。至乌鲁木齐乃惊觉，则献岁而贺客来也。心疑而恶之。至上元夜，忽又续前梦，则至配所以讫于身终而觉。遂信其有以。越三日而事果作矣。得罪以来，事无巨细，率与梦符，莫能强异。适遇子于庙，亦遵梦而行者也，而果遇子。请更言其后，以试吾梦。如子某年某月吉，某年某月当小蹇；某事如志，某事当无成。某月且疾病，某月当愈，某月有获，某月当有所失，某某当来，某某当去。此皆吾梦中事也。梦境渺茫，每不自信，又恐迹涉诡怪，惑人听睹，重取罪戾，深秘不敢言。子厚于我，姑言之，以试吾梦。”

    Explanation

    366

    Qin said to Song, "I was in the capital city before. On a certain New Year's Eve, I suddenly dreamed of being taken by officials and interrogated in the Criminal Department. The case was decided, and I was assigned here. Friends and relatives came to console me and then said goodbye, while officials and soldiers escorted me. I passed through mountains, rivers, passes, and distances, and experienced water and land travel, boats, carriages, walking, and riding, with all the stops along the way. I went through everything. Upon arriving in Urumqi, I woke up, and the New Year had arrived, with guests coming to congratulate. I was suspicious and disliked it. On the Lantern Festival night, I suddenly had another dream continuing from the previous one, until I was assigned here and it ended with my death, and I woke up. I then believed that there was some truth to it. Three days later, the event indeed occurred. Since I was disgraced, everything, no matter how big or small, matched my dream, and nothing could be forced to differ. I happened to meet you in the temple, and you also followed your dream, and indeed met you. Please tell me more about what happened afterward, to test my dream. If you are fortunate in a certain year and month, you will face a small obstacle in another year and month; if you achieve your goal in one matter, you will not succeed in another. In a certain month, you will fall ill, and in another month, you will recover; in a certain month, you will gain something, and in another month, you will lose something. Certain people will come, and others will leave. All these are things that happened in my dream. The dream is vague, and I often do not believe it. I also fear that it might seem strange and confuse people. I am afraid of bringing more guilt and keep it secret, not daring to speak of it. You are kind to me, so please tell me, to test my dream."

    367

    宋于是遂与定交。然疑其或有他术，久而察之，然后知其果梦也，而非术也。识其所言，无弗验者。梦既久，事以千万计，秦亦不能尽记。事至辄忆之，则娓娓而竟其后，无弗验者。
    　　秦谓宋曰：“无弗验者。独一事弗验。吾梦居此地时，乃三城品列，今独一城耳，此为不验。”乾隆三十年后，屯田既广，商贾云集，更筑新城以处之。后又移陕西满兵驻防于此，复筑满城。三城之说亦验。

    Explanation

    367

    Song thus established a formal alliance with Qin. However, it suspected that Qin might have another method. After a long time, upon careful examination, it was finally realized that it was indeed a dream, and not a method. Everything in the dream came true without fail. The dream had lasted for a long time, with countless events, and Qin could not remember them all. When the events occurred, Qin recalled them in detail and brought them to their conclusion, and nothing failed to come true. Qin said to Song, "Nothing fails to come true. Only one thing does not. When I dreamed of living in this place, there were three cities arranged in order. Now there is only one city, and this is not verified." After the thirtieth year of Emperor Qianlong, the land reclamation efforts had become extensive, and merchants gathered in large numbers. A new city was built to accommodate them. Later, the Manchu troops from Shaanxi were relocated to garrison here, and a Manchu city was built. The saying about three cities is also verified.

    368

    宋有母年老，岁时诞节，未尝不瞻望涕泣。秦慰之曰：“无伤也。子行遇释，且终养。”后数年，宋果释归，奉母以终，乃卒。秦卒于配所。
    　　噫嘻！除夕一梦也异矣！越十五日续梦之，其后数十年复履而行之，以至周旋琐杂，语言纤碎，目见而耳闻，无异于己。
    　　而不相谋者，莫不如印同符合。比于他人，盖不啻两世，亦古今以来仅有之事也。然岂非数哉！白香在乌鲁本齐，年尚少不能具悉，比述于余，又忘其四五。余记之，又逸其四五。盖非其详矣。白香又言：宋晚岁颇明于卜筮，推占后事，往往与秦梦符也。然耶？否耶？
    　　武侯碑
    　　卒制军莅成都日，掘城下得古碑，勒五‘火’字，旁有武乡侯款识，因移置武侯祠。是日大蛙见于市，旁有穴焉，或倾以盂水，蛙避入穴，穴亦塞。次日，蛙数万队行，诣武候祠，填塞门巷。众祷焉，已而不见。顷之，成都火起者五处，延毁万家。乃悟所掘者武侯镇火碑也。复埋之，遂不复火。

    Explanation

    368

    There was an elderly mother named Song, who during festivals would always look forward with tears. Qin comforted her, saying, "There's no need to be sad. Your son will be released and can take care of you until your passing." After several years, Song was indeed released and returned home to care for his mother until her death, and then he passed away. Qin, however, died in exile.

    Alas! A strange dream on New Year's Eve! Fifteen days later, the dream continued, and over the next few decades, it was relived in detail, including intricate and complex interactions, minute descriptions of speech and actions, all seen and heard firsthand, no different from reality.

    Despite not having conspired, everything matched perfectly. Compared to others, it was not only two lifetimes, but also a rare event in history. Was it not fate? Bai Xiang, when young in Urumqi, did not fully understand the details, but when recounting them to me, he forgot a few. I remembered, but also forgot a few. It's not a complete account. Bai Xiang also said: In his later years, Song was quite skilled in divination and often predicted future events, which often matched Qin's dreams. Was it true? Or not?

    The Stone Tablet of Zhuge Liang
    When the general arrived in Chengdu, he dug beneath the city walls and found an ancient stone tablet with five characters for "fire" engraved on it, along with an inscription identifying it as belonging to Zhuge Liang. The tablet was then moved to Zhuge Liang's temple. That day, a large frog appeared in the market, with a hole nearby. Someone poured water from a bowl into the hole, and the frog retreated into it, sealing the hole. The next day, thousands of frogs marched in formation to Zhuge Liang's temple, blocking the entrance and alleys. The crowd prayed, and then the frogs disappeared. Soon after, five fires broke out in Chengdu, destroying ten thousand households. They realized that what they had dug up was Zhuge Liang's fire-preventing stone. They buried it again, and no more fires occurred.

    369

    姚子英
    　　闽人桃子英，少而俊。随父商淮阴，父命挟资之京师，谋为小吏。渡黄河而北，忽得寒疾，甚困。税居莱庙中，久之，药焉而愈。出游庙旁村里间，见之者目相环也。
    　　既还庙，夜中忽有丽女子入室。姚惊曰：“汝人耶？”曰：“否。”“鬼耶？”曰：“否。”‘狐耶？”女子笑不答。遂同欢寝，旦则倏然去，不户不庭，信狐也。夜复来，则授丹数粒，使服之，盖媚药也，益健于人道。
    　　居无何，姚父至，盖闻其疾来视状。见其愈也，乃促之就道。姚不得已，行。流连数十里外，待女子，女子不至。数日，姚度父已归，复还庙中。女子至，喜曰：“妾知郎，情者也，必返，故至。”姚戏曰：“卿狐矣，乃不能随我，曷故？岂非忘我哉？”女曰，‘无须见责，明旦随郎俱去耳。”

    Explanation

    369

    Yao Ziyi
    A young man named Tao Ziyi from Fujian was handsome and talented. Following his father's business to Huaiyin, he was tasked with taking money to the capital to seek a minor official position. After crossing the Yellow River and heading north, he suddenly contracted a severe cold, becoming very ill. He rented a room in the temple of Lai and stayed there for a long time, eventually recovering after taking medicine. When he went out to explore the village near the temple, those who saw him looked at each other in surprise.

    After returning to the temple, one night a beautiful woman suddenly entered his room. Yao was startled and asked, "Are you a human?" She replied, "No." "Are you a ghost?" She answered, "No." "Are you a fox spirit?" The woman smiled but did not respond. They spent the night together, and in the morning she vanished without closing the door or leaving any trace, confirming her identity as a fox spirit. That night, she returned and gave him several pills to take, which were likely an aphrodisiac, making him even more vigorous in his human affairs.

    Not long after, Yao's father arrived, having heard of his illness and come to check on him. Seeing that Yao had recovered, he urged him to continue on his journey. Yao had no choice but to leave. He lingered for several miles, waiting for the woman, but she did not appear. After several days, Yao estimated that his father had returned home and went back to the temple. The woman appeared and said happily, "I knew you, a man of feeling, would return, so I came." Yao teased her, "You're a fox spirit, yet you can't follow me, why is that? Have you forgotten me?" The woman replied, "There's no need to blame me; I will follow you tomorrow."

    370

    是夕漏三下，闻檐际鸡声喔喔，女急推姚起，曰：“亟窜！老父迹至矣！”即以长衾裹姚，挟之破扉出，登垣而奔。如乘马蹑虚，昏然听之，不知身在何所也。旋闻女呼曰：“醒！醒！”姚如梦中觉，擘衾启目，曙色苍凉，乃卧野亭中，女在旁喘吁，睨之而笑。姚顾己身尚裸处，不禁腆然。女指衾下曰：“忍辱铠固在。”姚索得衣裈著之，始从容问故。
    　　女曰：“无须问，当图其亟者。装资尽弃，无以具糇粮，奈何？”姚忧窘无策，女笑曰：“不嫌绿头巾为累，贫不足忧也，’姚亦笑曰：“事在卿，不在我。”女前拊其颊曰：“幸不至此。郎饥，第言之。”乃于衾下出馎饦数枚，授姚曰：“有少饵，可啖也。”自是姚欲食，女辄供之。

    370

    That night, when the third watch of the night had passed, the sound of chickens crowing from the eaves could be heard. The woman hurriedly pushed Yao awake and said, "Flee quickly! The old father has arrived!" She immediately wrapped Yao in a long quilt, carried him out through a broken door, and they climbed over the wall and ran. It felt as if they were riding a horse through empty space, and Yao was in a dazed state, unaware of where they were. Soon, he heard the woman call out, "Wake up! Wake up!" Yao awoke as if from a dream, tore open the quilt, and opened his eyes to see the cool and desolate dawn light. He found himself lying in a wild pavilion, with the woman beside him, panting and smiling at him. Yao looked at himself and realized he was still naked, feeling embarrassed. The woman pointed to the quilt and said, "The armor of endurance is still here." Yao searched and found his clothes, putting them on. Only then did he calmly ask about the situation.

    The woman said, "There's no need to ask, just focus on what's urgent. We've lost all our travel funds and have no provisions for food, what shall we do?" Yao was worried and at a loss for what to do, but the woman laughed and said, "Don't worry about being embarrassed by a green headband, poverty is not something to worry about." Yao also laughed and said, "It's up to you, not me." The woman patted his cheek and said, "Fortunately, it's not that bad. Are you hungry, just say so." She then took out a few flat noodles from under the quilt, gave them to Yao, and said, "Here's a little food, you can eat it." From then on, whenever Yao wanted to eat, the woman would provide for him.

    371

    行数日，女曰：“此亦非长计。”姚曰：“若何？”女曰：“妾幼习角牴戏，请同游市麈，少展薄技，醵金而戒途，可乎？”姚曰：“甚善。”同行至一肆，女选敞地，画中矩，忽于袖中出碧纱步障，围四际，中垂绣幔，隔障为内外。一切供具悉取给袖中。坐姚于幔下，曰：“但观之。诸无与郎事。”姚唯唯。
    　　女亦遂坐于左，扬袖一挥，则障辟如门者四五，观者蚁集矣。忽一小鬟搴幔出，年可十三四，裙杉楚楚，向女拜曰：“姊姊好！”又拜姚曰：“姊夫好！”姚欲起答之，女曰：“止。”小鬟复对众敛衽，展白折叠扇索钱，顷刻得数贯，献于姚。徐举扇挥空，则万紫千红纷纷坠地，皆桃杏花也。咸讶九秋气候，不宜有此。俄有牡丹一朵，大如盆，飞堕众花中。小鬟趺坐其上，凌风而起，馀花皆随之而去。观者莫不叹诧，以为奇绝妙绝也。

    Explanation

    371

    After walking for several days, the woman said, "This is not a long-term plan." Yao replied, "What should we do?" The woman said, "I learned wrestling when I was young. Let us travel together to the bustling market, and I will slightly display my skills, raise some funds, and prepare for our journey. Is that acceptable?" Yao said, "Very good." They traveled together to a shop, where the woman chose an open space and drew a square. Suddenly, she pulled out a green silk screen from her sleeves, surrounding all sides, with an embroidered curtain hanging in the middle, dividing the area into inside and outside. All supplies were taken from her sleeves. She seated Yao under the curtain and said, "Just watch. Nothing to do with the gentleman." Yao nodded in agreement.

    The woman then sat to her left, waving her sleeves, and the screen opened like several doors. Onlookers gathered like ants. Suddenly, a young girl emerged, about thirteen or fourteen years old, with a neat and attractive appearance. She bowed to the woman and said, "Sister, you're good!" Then she bowed to Yao and said, "Brother-in-law, you're good!" Yao wanted to stand up to respond, but the woman said, "Stop." The young girl then bowed to the crowd, unfolded a white folding fan, and began to collect money. In a moment, she collected several strings of coins and presented them to Yao. She slowly raised the fan and waved it in the air, and a myriad of colors, all peach and apricot flowers, fell纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠地纷纷坠

    372

    女曰：“鸦头无礼，竟舍我去乎？谁为我执以来？”便有老人应而出，长尺许，荷一梃控小马，伛偻嚏咳，惴惴伏鞍上，若欲坠下状。女哂曰：“汝欲追妮子，不顾闪折骨头耶？”老人忸怩而退，观者皆大笑。女曰：“鸦头既去，且听之。请为激楚舞以壮宾客。”遂呼曰：“良子来！”则有童于出于东，短衣双髻，执戟而舞。良久，女又呼曰：“阿延来！”复有童子出于西，装如前，持双剑。女曰；“斗！”二童子乃斗，剑戟迷离，终不相触。女曰：“搏！”二童子乃弃仗而搏。

    Explanation

    372

    The woman said, "Aya is being rude, is she going to leave me here? Who will come and take me back?" Then an old man responded and came out, about a foot tall, carrying a stick and controlling a small horse, bent over and coughing, nervously crouching on the saddle, as if he is about to fall off. The woman laughed and said, "Do you want to chase the girl, without worrying about breaking your bones?" The old man, embarrassed, retreated, and the spectators all laughed. The woman said, "Since Aya has gone, let's watch and see. Please perform the Ji Chu dance to entertain the guests." She then called out, "Come, good boy!" And a boy came out from the east, dressed in short-sleeved clothes with two braids, holding a halberd and dancing. After a long time, the woman called out again, "Come, Aoyan!" Another boy came out from the west, dressed similarly, holding two swords. The woman said, "Fight!" The two boys then engaged in a mock battle, their swords and halberds entangled, but they never touched each other. The woman said, "Grapple!" The two boys then threw away their weapons and wrestled.

    373

    忽一人拦二童子仆。厉声曰：“淫婢子尚不归耶？”女色变而奔，其人逐之，倏忽已杳，诸物皆化为纸矣。
    　　姚惊骇，急避入众中，遂走至京师，忆女甚苦。数年，落拓归。过故庙，风景凄然。留宿数日，寂如也。不得己。问庙中道士：“此地故有孤祟否？”道士曰：“客尝遇之乎？”姚具告之，道士笑曰：“伪狐也。有某侩者，无姓名，挟左道攫人财物。尤善房中术，能致妇人魂魄。好女子见之，辄病卧如丧，久之乃复；往往言自侩所来，盖非其璞矣。侩又能隐形易貌，甚诡秘。昔尝居此，今复移去矣。闻侩有女，传父术，亦用以媚男子，托名于狐。侩虽甚耻之，莫能禁也，客所遇得毋是乎？”

    Explanation

    373

    A sudden figure blocked the two young servants. He spoke sternly, "That promiscuous maid still hasn't returned?" The girl's face paled, and she fled. The man pursued her, but in an instant, he vanished, and all the objects around them transformed into paper.

    Yao was terrified, quickly evading into the crowd, and then made his way to the capital, deeply pining for his daughter. After several years of wandering, he returned home. Passing by the old temple, he found the scenery melancholy. He stayed there for several days, but it remained as quiet as before. Out of necessity, he asked the Taoist priest in the temple, "Has there ever been a haunting spirit here?" The priest replied, "Have you ever encountered such a thing?" Yao recounted his experience in detail. The priest laughed and said, "It was a false fox spirit. There was a certain man named Kui, though he had no known name, who used unorthodox methods to steal people's property. He was especially skilled in sexual techniques and could summon the spirits of women. Beautiful women who saw him would fall ill and lie in bed as if mourning, but after a long time, they would recover. They often claimed to have come from Kui, though it was not their true nature. Kui could also become invisible and change his appearance, making him very mysterious. He once lived here but has since moved away. I heard that Kui has a daughter who inherited his techniques. She also uses them to charm men, claiming to be a fox spirit. Though Kui is deeply ashamed of her, he cannot stop her. Could it be that you encountered her?"

    374

    耳食录二编卷四
    　　奎　光
    　　诸生某，锐意进取。岁当宾兴，往往梦中跃起，走叫出门外，曰：“中矣中矣！”已又作报喜人索采钱状，往复争竞。良久，复就床，鼾然睡去。次日忆之，惘惘然如不第者然。
    　　又闻人言，登科则奎光且见。一夜，有偷儿灶火耀窗间。某正拥被冥想，见之，喜曰：“殆奎光耶？果尔，当再见。”偷儿承意复耀之，某大喜，遂熟睡不疑。偷儿尽发其囊箧以去。
    　　燕
    　　豫章某节妇家，岁有双燕巢其堂。后雌燕独来，盖亦孀矣。或谓孤燕不祥，毁巢而逐之。燕旋毁旋茸，终不去。
    　　他日，忽有双燕者径来夺其巢。孤燕露处宇下，孑然悲鸣。而是夜双燕竟为鼠啮以死，孤燕乃复，闻者快之。

    Explanation

    374

    Ear Food Record II, Volume 4

    Kui Guang

    A certain scholar, intent on making progress, often dreamed of leaping up and running out of the door, shouting, "I've passed! I've passed!" He then acted out the scene of congratulators asking for gifts, going back and forth in competition. After a while, he returned to bed and fell asleep soundly. The next day, he remembered the dream and felt a sense of confusion and disappointment, as if he had not passed.

    Another story goes that if one passes the imperial examinations, the light of the North Star will appear. One night, a thief shone a light through the window. The scholar, wrapped in his quilt and lost in thought, saw the light and said, "Is it the light of the North Star? If so, I will see it again." The thief, understanding his intention, shone the light again, and the scholar was delighted and fell asleep soundly. The thief then took everything from the scholar's possessions.

    Yan

    In the home of a certain chaste widow in Yuzhang, there were always two swallows nesting in her hall each year. Later, only the female swallow came, implying that she too was a widow. Some people believed that a lone swallow was inauspicious and destroyed the nest to drive it away. However, the nest was repeatedly destroyed and rebuilt, and the swallow refused to leave.

    One day, two swallows suddenly came and took over the nest. The lone swallow was exposed under the eaves, crying out alone and sorrowfully. That night, the two swallows were bitten by mice and died, and the lone swallow returned. Those who heard the story were pleased.

    375

    节妇既贫，鬻其室他徙。明年孤燕至，讶主人已非，徘徊旧巢，已复去。卒访得其新居，构垒处焉，去来者十九年。
    　　周厚庵来都下，述于余。此与姚玉京及卫敬瑜妻事略相类。噫嘻！今孤燕尚无恙耶？可不谓贞且义习？夫鼠，黠而窃者也，乃亦能侠，善乎哉！
    　　葆　翠
    　　某生笃学，自少至长无交游，诵读之外，亦无他嗜好，泊如也。
    　　读书城中某寺。其邻以宅警他徒，有叟挈眷来，税居之。邻故有楼，俯临生书室，久扃钥。翌日，忽施帘幕，甚华焕。俄有女子自楼出，妙龄殊姿。生见而好之，木立移时。女凭槛他顾，略不垂盼。徐徐搴帘入，径阂其扉。生徘徊窃叹：“天下乃有斯人耶！”翘首楼上，冀女且复出。数日杳然。生意必叟眷属，即往谒叟，欲结比邻欢、为朱陈计。门者谓主人性介，不愿宴见宾客，置刺不为通。生怏怏而返。次日复往，则杜其门焉。由是益怅惘欲绝，　日对楼凝望冥想，而诵读之声不复作矣。

    Explanation

    375

    The Chaste Widow, having fallen into poverty, sold her home and moved elsewhere. The following year, a lone swallow returned, surprised to find the owner no longer there, and wandered around the old nest before leaving again. Eventually, it discovered her new residence and began coming and going over the course of nineteen years.

    Zhou Hou'an came to the capital and told me about this. This story is somewhat similar to those of Yao Yujing and Wei Jingyu's wife. Alas! Is the lone swallow still well? How can one not say that it is chaste and righteous? The rat, though clever and thieving, can also be chivalrous—how good is that!

    Bao Cui

    A certain student was diligent in his studies, having no social contacts from youth to adulthood. Beyond reciting and reading, he had no other hobbies; he was indifferent.

    He studied in a certain temple in the city. His neighbor used his house to warn others, and an old man came with his family and rented a place to live. The neighbor had an old building that overlooked the student's study room, which had been locked for a long time. The next day, suddenly, curtains were put up, and it looked very splendid. Soon, a young woman appeared, stepping out of the building, with a unique and beautiful appearance. The student saw her and liked her, standing motionless for a while. She leaned on the railing and looked elsewhere, barely glancing down. Slowly, she pulled the curtain aside and entered, directly blocking the door. The student wandered around, sighing to himself: "How could there be such a person in the world!" He looked up at the building, hoping the woman would come out again. After several days, she did not appear. The student thought she must be the old man's family, so he went to visit the old man, wanting to establish good relations with the neighbor and form a close friendship. The gatekeeper said the owner was aloof and did not want to entertain guests, putting the name card aside without passing it on. The student returned feeling disappointed. The next day, he went again, only to find the door closed. Therefore, he became even more disheartened and confused, every day facing the building, staring and pondering, and the sound of reciting and reading no longer resounded.

    376

    一日薄暮，微闻楼上步屐声，戽乃启，仿佛见红袖。生喜，注目待之。忽微风飕然，一箭出帘间，生惊闪避，已著地。拾视，蜡镞耳。少焉，女挟弓矢出见生，似甚怪怒，复射之。生神夺，不复知避，又以蜡镞无伤也，仰面受之。箭发，中生颊，甚痛楚。拔视箭端，易以绣针矣，流血及颊，女乃大笑趋入内。
    　　生既病创，数日偃卧不能起，终不怨女。念女戏而卖己，殆非无意者。且以投梭故，博倾城一笑，计亦良得，及瘥，则楼上帘幕无见，扃钥如故矣，凄然丧魄，讶叟伺故遽迁去。侦诸其门，则叟殊未迁。卑辞叩门者，乃知叟盖山西富人，有二子，外商，室中止老妇，及灶下婢耳。始悟女竟非人也，炽念顿灰。然犹时时忆其美不置，遂感疾，迁延卧榻上。

    Explanation

    376

    One day in the evening, the young man faintly heard the sound of footsteps on wooden clogs coming from upstairs. The curtain opened, and he saw a figure in a red sleeve. He was delighted and watched attentively, waiting for her. Suddenly, a light breeze blew, and an arrow shot out from behind the curtain. The young man startled and dodged, but the arrow had already hit the ground. He picked it up and examined it, realizing it was an arrow with a wax tip. After a while, the woman appeared, carrying a bow and arrows, and seemed both surprised and angry. She shot at him again. The young man was so frightened that he lost his wits and no longer knew how to dodge. Since the wax-tipped arrow was harmless, he looked up to receive it. The arrow struck his cheek, causing great pain. He pulled out the arrow and found that the tip had been replaced with a needle embroidered with silk. Blood flowed to his cheek, and the woman laughed loudly and rushed inside.

    The young man was injured and sick, lying down for several days unable to get up, but he never blamed the woman. He reflected that she had played a trick on him, likely with a purpose. He thought that her actions, like throwing a shuttle, had earned him a smile that could captivate the entire city, which was a good strategy. When he recovered, he found that the curtains and screens were no longer visible, and the doors and locks were as before. He felt sad and lost his spirit, surprised that the old man had suddenly moved away. He investigated at the door and found that the old man had not moved. He humbly knocked on the door and learned that the old man was originally a wealthy person from Shanxi, with two sons who traveled abroad for business. In the house, there were only an old woman and a kitchen maid. Finally, he realized that the woman was not a person, and his intense thoughts suddenly turned to ashes. However, he continued to remember her beauty without ceasing, and thus fell ill, lingering on the bed.

    377

    家人闻之，请医来问状，生但瞪目直视，不复作一语。举室惊悼，谓生且死也。无何，跃然从床起，神色怡然，若并无疾者。家人愈疑骇，环而守之。生辞焉，弗听三，始相引趋出，伺诸窗间。则闻生语曰：“退矣退矣！卿盍前？”少间，生又曰：“君太恶作剧，乃以人面为鹄耶？”则闻有吃吃笑者。笑已，乃答曰：聊相试耳，君乃不怒。既知为异物，乃复为畏，且念我且病，君良苦矣，而情亦至矣！是以来。”家人听其语，知其鬼也。急排闼入，共徙生以归。生悲怛无已，奄然复病，饮之药弗效。更为召巫。生乃见女来笑曰：“巫焉能驱我，适诱我耳。久欲视君，苦无间，可乘此一游。”生欣然从之出。略无阻隔。

    Explanation

    377

    The family heard about it and summoned a doctor to inquire about his condition. The man only stared fixedly and did not speak a word. The entire household was shocked and mournful, thinking he was on the verge of death. Soon after, he suddenly leaped up from his bed, his expression calm and composed, as if he had no illness at all. The family became even more suspicious and gathered around to watch over him. He tried to leave, but they refused him three times, and then they led him out, eavesdropping from the window. They then heard him say, "Step back, step back! Why don't you come forward?" A short while later, he said again, "You are too mischievous, are you taking a human face as your goal?" They heard a soft, suppressed laugh. After the laughter subsided, he replied, "I was just testing you; you didn't get angry. Now that I know you are an unusual being, I am afraid again. And I think of how I am ill, and you must have been very troubled, yet your feelings are genuine! That is why I came." The family listened to his words and realized he was a ghost. They hurriedly forced their way in and brought him back home. The man was overwhelmed with grief and suddenly fell ill again; the medicine they gave him was ineffective. They then summoned a shaman. The man saw the woman come laughing and said, "The shaman can't drive me away; it was just to lure me. I have long wanted to see you, but it has been difficult to find a chance. Let's take this opportunity for a visit." The man happily followed her out, and there were no obstacles at all.

    378

    倏忽间行入一室，绣帷香榻，壁有图，瓶有花，琴书玩好之属，位置疏雅。女辟旁闼引生出帘外，凭栏而指曰：“是何处？”生审视即己书室也。始觉身在女楼中，益自喜且幸，已于门侧得断弓及矢。生问：“此射我者耶？”女粲然曰：“然，为其射君，罚而折之矣。”生亦笑。生既与女处，因问女姓字，女曰：“妾前明张总帅女也，小字葆翠，遇疾夭逝，栖魂于此，茕守泉关，未敢自涴。感君意厚，故不避非礼，赴情申义。不幸形迹乖异，见防于人，迟迟至今。”生曰：“鬼真畏人乎？”女曰：“非也。

    Explanation

    378

    In a flash, he walked into a room, where embroidered curtains hung and a fragrant bed was set. Paintings adorned the walls, and vases held flowers. Musical instruments, books, and other items of enjoyment were arranged sparsely and elegantly. The woman opened a side door and led him out of the curtain, leaning on the railing and pointing. "Where is this place?" she asked. He looked around and realized it was his own study. Only then did he realize he was in the woman's room, and he became even more pleased and fortunate. He had already found a broken bow and arrow near the door. He asked, "Is this what shot at me?" She smiled brightly and said, "Yes, because it shot at you, I was punished and broke it." He laughed as well. After spending time with the woman, he asked about her name. She replied, "I was the daughter of a high-ranking military official during the Ming Dynasty, and my given name is Bao Cui. I died young due to illness, and my spirit resides here, guarding the underworld alone, not daring to defile myself. Grateful for your kind intentions, I did not avoid what was improper and fulfilled my feelings and expressed my righteousness. Unfortunately, my appearance and behavior are strange, and I am seen as suspicious by others, so I have been slow to act until now." He asked, "Do ghosts really fear people?" She said, "No."

    379

    桑濮之事，安得　然不避人目？彼虽不见，妾实耻之，非畏之也。”生曰：“吾与君今日殆夙缘耶！”女曰：“非缘也，情也！无缘者神魂不清，飞絮因风，飘萍逐水，偶合偶离耳。妾向遇君盖如此矣！若情之所结，自有而无，亦自无而有，由生而灭，亦由灭而生，山川不能间，死生不能隔，而天帝神明不能禁也。”生曰：“卿埋香已久，九原寂寞，盍为再世玉箫乎？”女曰：“鬼之不欲生，犹人之不欲死也。人不能不死，鬼则可以不生。且人之生也，饥寒伺其身，职役劳其形，嗜好攻其情，灾患怵其虑。妾尝为人备领之矣。而溘化以来，举不复有，此何乐如之？虽有绛雪神丹，还魂灵草，不愿服也。”生唯唯。

    Explanation

    379

    Matters of Sangpu, how could they be hidden from the eyes of others? Though he may not see them, I truly feel ashamed, not out of fear." The man said, "Is it not our destined fate that we meet today?" The woman replied, "It is not fate, but emotion! Those without a destined connection lack clarity of spirit. Like willow fluff carried by the wind and water plants drifting with the current, we meet by chance and part by chance. My previous encounters with you were like this! If it is the emotion that binds us, then it can exist and not exist, or not exist and come into existence, from birth to death, and from death to birth. Mountains and rivers cannot separate us, nor can death and life, and even the gods cannot prevent our connection." The man said, "You have been buried for a long time, and the Nine Graves are lonely. Why not be the jade flute of the next life?" The woman said, "The desire of a ghost not to be reborn is like the desire of a person not to die. Humans cannot escape death, but ghosts can avoid rebirth. Moreover, in life, one faces hunger and cold, labor and toil, desires and misfortunes. I have experienced all this as a human. Since my sudden transformation (death), I have not experienced any of this again. What joy is there in that? Even if there were the Red Snow Immortal Elixir and the Reincarnation Spirit Herb, I would not take them." The man responded with a nod of agreement.

    380

    历三日，女促生归。生恐失女不肯行。女曰：“无伤也。君归请宣言于众曰：“听我，我乃生；不听且死。孰与听我？”众必听，则使人迎我于楼，妾即至矣。不然，幽遘亦何可长哉！然须将币帛，具舆马，如婚娶礼，妾乃行。非以为文，礼不可废也。”生唯唯，遂归。

    Explanation

    380

    After three days, the woman urged the man to return. The man, fearing to lose her, refused to go. She said, "No harm will come. Please go back and make a public announcement to everyone: 'Listen to me, and I will be born; if you do not listen, you will die. Who would rather not listen to me?' They will surely listen, and then someone can come to fetch me from the tower, and I will come. Otherwise, how long can secret meetings last? But you must bring gifts of cloth and silk, prepare carriages and horses, and follow the wedding customs. Only then will I come. This is not for show, but because rituals cannot be abandoned." The man replied, "Yes, yes," and then returned.

    381

    忽从榻上起，则闻家人惊喜曰：“生矣！”问魂亦何之，生如女教。家人不忍违，为娶女以归，居室如夫妇。女性贞谧，不好冶媟，生之外虽三尺童子莫能觏也。
    　　生有外兄某，闻其事欲求见女，女不可。某坚坐不去，语渐亵嫚。女掩耳让生曰：“闺帏之中，安得容淫朋秽语？幸速遣之。”某闻惭而出，其严凛如此。女流相对，间亦形见，往往相谓，言目中闺秀无如新妇妍且慧者。
    　　居数年，帷薄甚修，遇有休咎，多因女决之。生家呼女为神娥。生感女意不复娶，女劝生纳姬。生二子，为父后，生卒，女亦去。女尝出生时小像，使生题识之，藏于家。
    　　蜡技
    　　时人有善蜡技者，团黄蜡作麒麟，成之指掌间，麟角毕肖，他物皆然，亦绝技也。人或求之作，万万不肯作。谲者知其癖，故握黄蜡诱之，又故故匿之，彼必索观，再三而后与之。乃随意团捏，酬对如常，俄顷而就。置之近身，意甚珍惜。伺间攫藏之，不然，少时便复毁之矣。

    381

    Suddenly, he got up from the bed, and he heard the family express surprise and joy, saying, "She has come to life!" He asked where the soul had gone, and she lived as if taught by a woman. The family could not bear to disobey, so they arranged for a wife to be brought home, and they lived together as husband and wife. The woman had a chaste and quiet nature, did not like to flirt or be intimate, and outside of him, not even a child of three feet could see her.

    He had a maternal uncle, who upon hearing the story wanted to meet the woman, but she refused. The uncle sat firmly and would not leave, and his language gradually became disrespectful and rude. The woman covered her ears and scolded the husband, saying, "In the inner chambers, how can there be room for lewd friends and filthy words? Please send him away quickly." The uncle, upon hearing this, felt ashamed and left, so strict and awe-inspiring was she. When they were together, sometimes she appeared, and they often spoke to each other, saying that among the women she knew, none were as beautiful and intelligent as the new wife.

    They lived together for several years, maintaining a very strict household. When there were good or bad omens, often the decision was made by the woman. The family called her 'Divine Swan.' Moved by her feelings, he did not remarry, and the woman advised him to take a concubine. He had two sons, who became his successors, and when he died, she also left. She once produced a small portrait of herself at birth, asked the husband to inscribe it, and kept it at home.

    Wax Craftsmanship
    There was a person skilled in wax craftsmanship who made a qilin (mythical creature) from yellow wax, completing it in the palm of his hand. The qilin's horns were perfectly shaped, and other objects were similarly made, making it a supreme skill. If someone asked for it to be made, he would absolutely not do it. A cunning person knew his obsession and held yellow wax to lure him, then deliberately hid it. He would surely ask to see it, and only after repeated requests would he give it. Then he would casually mold it, responding as usual, and in a moment, it was completed. He placed it close to his body, showing great care. He waited for an opportunity to secretly take it away, otherwise, it would be destroyed soon.

    382

    施　建　昌
    　　有卖药者施建昌，河南汤阴县人。顺治八年秋，归自湖湘，日暮失道，经花圃之侧，桃实方熟，纂纂出墙。施正饥，求墙缺处，逾而入，升树饱啖，即憩树上，倦而假寝。及觉，则明月既出，亭馆凄然。风露满衣，畏寒欲下。忽东阁门启，语笑有声，蔽树叶窥之。见三小人高尺许，一虎首者红衣，一马首者黄衣，一羊首者绿衣，共携酒肴，设亭前石上，席地对饮。虎首者中坐，羊马左右焉。俄而角门再启，复有四人出，一鹿首、一牛首、一狗首、一鬼首独角。皆衣缯癰，其长不及前三人，亦就石上坐，相语曰：“月佳哉！”施骇甚，伏树屏息。虎首者忽曰：“何故有生人气？”皆曰：“良然。”哗然而起，遍索圃中不得。羊首者谓曰：“大兄故善疑，生人安在？”众皆笑，遂列坐饮酒。

    Explanation

    382

    Shi Jianchang
    There was a medicine seller named Shi Jianchang, from Tangyin County, Henan Province. In the autumn of the eighth year of the Shunzhi era (1651), he returned from Hunan and Hubei provinces. As evening fell and he lost his way, he passed by the side of a garden where ripe peaches were hanging out of the wall. Shi Jianchang was hungry, so he looked for a gap in the wall, climbed in, and ascended a tree to eat his fill. He then rested on the tree and dozed off, feeling tired. When he woke up, he saw the moon had risen, and the pavilions and buildings looked desolate. The wind and dew were on his clothes, and he felt cold, wanting to come down. Suddenly, the eastern gate opened, and voices and laughter were heard. Shi Jianchang hid behind the leaves to look. He saw three small figures, each about a foot tall, with the heads of a tiger, horse, and sheep, respectively. They were dressed in red, yellow, and green clothes and were carrying wine and food. They set up the food on a stone in front of the pavilion and drank together on the ground. The tiger-headed figure sat in the middle, with the sheep- and horse-headed figures on either side. Shortly after, another door opened, and four more people came out, with the heads of a deer, cow, dog, and a one-horned ghost. They were all wearing silk and fur, and they were shorter than the first three figures. They also sat on the stone and talked, saying, "The moon is beautiful!" Shi Jianchang was very frightened and hid behind the tree, holding his breath. The tiger-headed figure suddenly asked, "Why is there a living person's breath here?" The others agreed, "That's true." They shouted and looked around the garden but did not find Shi Jianchang. The sheep-headed figure said to the tiger-headed figure, "Brother, you are always suspicious, where is the living person?" Everyone laughed, and then they sat down to drink again.

    383

    良久，虎首者又曰：“我终觉有生人气。”群起复大索不得。马首者曰：“夜良如此，姑饮酒，无忧自扰也！”众是之　。乃相与拇战，飞觥轰饮。已而虎首者呼曰：“此时生人气甚浓。须再细搜之。”时斜月穿树，人影堕地。虎首者见之，笑曰：“我岂妄言哉？固在此矣！”众共叹其卓识，遂趋至树下，仰而呼曰：“汝速下，否则祸汝！”施抱树恐栗，不复能言。众见其不答，则环树詈辱，摇撼攀跃，而人小树高，终不能及。无何鸡乱鸣，群小人乃敛迹而去，角门亦阖。

    Explanation

    383

    After a long time, the one with the tiger head said again, "I still feel a human presence." They all rose up again to search thoroughly but could not find it. The one with the horse head said, "The night is so good, let's just drink, there's no need to worry and trouble ourselves!" Everyone agreed with him. They then played a finger-counting game and drank heavily. Soon after, the one with the tiger head called out, "The human smell is very strong now. We must search more carefully again." At that moment, the moonlight filtered through the trees, and human shadows fell to the ground. The one with the tiger head saw this and laughed, "Am I speaking falsely? It is certainly here!" Everyone praised his keen insight. They then hurried to the tree, called out while looking up, and said, "Come down quickly, or else you will be in trouble!" The person hugged the tree in fear and trembling, unable to speak. Seeing that he did not answer, they circled the tree, cursed him, shook and climbed the tree, but the person was small and the tree was tall, so they could not reach him. Soon after, the roosters began to crow chaotically, and the group of miscreants then quietly disappeared. The side door also closed.

    384

    施俟日出始下树，欲穷其异，访主人而告之故。主人姓陈氏，明季尝官内翰，隐居于此。闻施言，未之信也。入告夫人，夫人曰：“其有焉，婢媪辈亦言东角门夜尝有声，盍察之？”同至角门内，谛视地上，有蹄迹焉，而甚小。其旁曲室，积薪草及败椟其中，尘埃蔽之，独一椟浮滑若常有物出入者。遂发其椟，中贮数土偶，为虎头马头诸形，杯盘之属亦在焉。夫人泫然曰：“此亡儿戏具，吾不忍见，姑弃于此。今二十余年，不虞其为怪也，令碎之。有声有血。

    Explanation

    384

    Shi waited until the sun rose to climb down the tree, wanting to thoroughly explore its strange nature. He visited the owner and told him the reason. The owner surnamed Chen had once served as an official in the Hanlin Academy during the late Ming period and was now living in seclusion there. Upon hearing Shi's account, he did not believe it. He went inside to inform his wife, who said, "It is true. The maids and servants also mentioned that there was a sound at the eastern corner gate at night. Why don't we investigate it?" They went together to the inner part of the corner gate and carefully examined the ground, where they found small hoof prints. Nearby, in a small room, there was a pile of firewood, grass, and broken boxes covered with dust, except for one box that was smooth and slippery, as if something frequently passed through it. They opened the box and found several clay figures inside, shaped like tiger heads, horse heads, and other forms, along with cups and plates. The wife said with tears in her eyes, "These are the toys of my deceased son. I could not bear to see them, so I left them here. Now, over twenty years have passed, and I did not expect them to become a nuisance. I had them broken. There were sounds and blood."

    385

    汤琇
    　　冯生者盖河南人，与同里汤琇，少时同学，最相善。两人皆名家子也，居相比，齿相若，才相并，顾志趣不甚合。冯，弱书生也，循循曲谨，不臧否人物；琇则慷慨激烈，殊有侠士肠，又多力善射，挽两石弓，百步辄命中。两人不相效，亦不相非。尝同游水曲，冯载酒，琇挟弓矢俱，选径僻处，踞坐丰草上，相与论志。冯因取片石，睨柳枝最高处掷之，祝曰：“以中否卜吾进退。”一掷而中。琇抚掌曰：“善。”适有鸟拂树过，琇即援弓射之曰：“吾亦以此卜！”矢既鸣，鸟竟飞去。矢落湾波中。琇折弓投地叹曰：“已矣！”冯惊，慰之曰：“皆戏耳！何遽出此？”琇曰：“吾他日百发不一失，而今失之，岂非数也，君善为之。吾志决于此矣！”

    Explanation

    385

    Feng, a man from Henan, was close friends with Tang Xiu, his fellow townsman. They studied together in their youth and were the closest of friends. Both came from distinguished families, lived close to each other, were roughly the same age, and had similar talents, but their interests and aspirations were not very compatible. Feng was a weak scholar, cautious and careful, not passing judgment on others; Tang Xiu, on the other hand, was generous and passionate, especially possessing the heart of a chivalrous warrior, and was strong and skilled in archery, able to draw a bow that required two dan of force and hit the target consistently at a distance of one hundred steps. The two did not emulate each other, nor did they criticize each other. They once walked together along a winding riverbank, with Feng carrying wine and Tang Xiu carrying bows and arrows with him. They chose a secluded path and sat cross-legged on lush grass, discussing their aspirations. Feng then took a small stone, aimed at the highest branch of a willow tree and threw it, wishing, "To predict my success or failure based on whether it hits the target." He hit it on the first throw. Tang Xiu clapped his hands and said, "Excellent." Just then a bird flew past the tree, and Tang Xiu immediately drew his bow and shot at it, saying, "I also use this to predict!" The arrow made a sound, but the bird flew away. The arrow fell into the bay. Tang Xiu broke his bow, threw it on the ground, and sighed, "It is over!" Feng was surprised and reassured him, "It was all just play! Why say such a thing?" Tang Xiu replied, "In the past, I never missed a shot, but now I missed. Isn't it fate? You should continue well. My resolve is made here!"

    386

    是岁冯果领乡荐，明年成进士。琇竟放浪山泽，有冥鸿之志。冯屡劝之，不顾也。冯既登仕版，远宦闽粤间，作书招隐者三四。琇率不报，弃家游四方，于是并踪迹不可得测矣。冯思之，往往至流涕。岁时存问其家，廉察十余年，卒无知琇消息者。
    　　冯尝买一僮，顽劣特甚，殆不堪任使，食已即卧，起乃复食。或请卖之，冯怜其少，不忍也，且曰：“卖之他人，宁能听之耶？”一夕，盗大至，从檐端下，皆雕面少年三十余人，露刃秉火，举室沸腾。僮徐徐从梦中起，振臂一叱，声若虎豹吼，群盗罻然伏地不能起。僮一一断其手腕，掷垣外令去。冯家以全，始异僮之能。呼而诘之，已逸矣。

    Explanation

    386

    In that year, Feng Guo was recommended by his local government and passed the imperial examination the following year, becoming a jinshi. Xiu, however, chose to wander freely in the mountains and rivers, harboring lofty aspirations. Feng repeatedly tried to persuade him, but Xiu paid no heed. After Feng entered the ranks of officialdom and served far away in Fujian and Guangdong, he wrote several letters inviting Xiu to return from his seclusion. Xiu consistently ignored these invitations, leaving his home to wander around the country. As a result, his whereabouts became completely unpredictable. Feng often thought of him, and his thoughts would sometimes lead to tears. He regularly visited Xiu's family and investigated for over ten years, but ultimately found no news of Xiu.

    Once, Feng bought a servant who was extremely lazy and mischievous, almost unable to be used. The servant would eat and then immediately lie down, and upon waking, he would eat again. Someone suggested selling him, but Feng pitied him because he was young and could not bring himself to do so. He said, "If I sell him to someone else, would they be able to manage him?" One night, a large group of thieves descended from the eaves, consisting of thirty or so tattooed youths, brandishing weapons and holding torches, causing the whole household to be in turmoil. The servant slowly woke up from sleep, raised his arms and shouted loudly, his voice like the roar of a tiger or leopard. The thieves were stunned and fell to the ground unable to move. The servant cut off their wrists one by one, threw them over the wall, and ordered them to leave. Feng's family was saved, and they began to marvel at the servant's abilities. When they called him and questioned him, he had already escaped.

    387

    未几，冯罢官，以妻子先归，独载一舟，溯江访亲旧。比归，维舟京口。夜中缆忽解，簸荡洪流中，行绝驶，不知几百千里。舟中号恐，谓必葬鱼腹。及晓，忽触岸而止。沙屿水岛之间，其僮在焉，惊而就之。僮笑曰：“先生候主人久矣。”冯益诧曰：“先生为谁也？”僮曰：“去当识之。”引冯行里许，有呼于断崖之上者曰：“故人无恙耶？”仰而视之，琇也。颜鬓如旧时，衣巾飘洒，若独鹤立于松上。冯不觉泣下，哽不能语。顾视峻壁，绝莫能登，则呼曰：“奈何？”俄有云梯下，倚崖而齐其巅。僮翼冯以升，冯乃持琇而哭。

    Explanation

    387

    Not long after, Feng was dismissed from his official post and sent his wife and children back home first. He then embarked alone in a boat, sailing upstream along the river to visit his old friends and relatives. Upon returning, he moored the boat at Jingkou. At night, the mooring rope suddenly loosened, and the boat was tossed and swirled in the great current, traveling at an extreme speed, without knowing how many hundreds of miles it had covered. The people in the boat cried out in fear, thinking they would surely be buried in the fish's belly. By dawn, the boat suddenly hit the shore and stopped. Between sandy islands and water islets, his servant was there, surprised and went to him. The servant smiled and said, "Master, you have been waiting for the host for a long time." Feng was even more astonished and asked, "Who are you, Master?" The servant replied, "You will recognize him when you see him." Leading Feng to walk about a mile, someone called out from the cliff saying, "Is my old friend well?" Looking up, Feng saw Xiu. His face and hair looked as they did in the old days, and his clothes and headwear were elegant and graceful, as if a solitary crane stands on a pine tree. Feng could not help but cry, choked and could not speak. Looking at the steep cliff, it was impossible to climb, so he called out, "What shall we do?" Soon, a cloud ladder descended, leaning against the cliff and reaching its peak. The servant helped Feng climb up, and Feng then held Xiu and wept.

    388

    徐视崖上茅屋一区，旁无邻，侍者二，其一僮也。屋中一石榻、一几、一炉、一拂，壁张一琴，无他物也。冯问别后事，琇曰：“吾倦游至此，爱其幽旷，止焉。虑君之不可见也，故挽舟以来。”冯叹曰：“君栖烟露，味芝木，齐肩羽流，而吾堕尘海中，且碌碌以死，悬去远矣！”琇曰：“不然，君富贵中人，济时润物，世之所赖也。如琇者亦何贵哉？名教之地，乐于三山；忠孝之流，长于四海；菽粟迷味，胜于丹药。忘身而利物，上也；遁世而全己，次也。吾亦不得已，姑出于此，君何羡焉？且君诚厌薄樊网，则饬而躬，一而志，凝而神，遵理而违欲，葆真而适道，安在其不仙也？敬扫白云，待君促坐，区区之意，尽于此矣！”冯唯唯。琇遂起，导冯出屋后，仄径秋豪，蜿蜒而下，俯见波涛，风樯满江，指一泊舟曰：“君登矣！”言已，忽不见。冯惘然而登，则故舟也。舟人乃言君去后，舟为疾风驱至此。于是鼓棹而西，已达淮泗矣。遂归洛中。

    Explanation

    388

    Feng slowly examined the thatched house on the cliffside, noting that there were no neighbors nearby. There were two attendants, one of whom was a child. Inside the house, there was one stone bed, one table, one stove, one whisk, and a lute hung on the wall. Nothing else was present.

    Feng asked about the events since their last meeting. Xiu replied, "I grew tired of traveling and came here, loving its seclusion and openness. I stayed here. Worried that I might not be able to see you, I pulled the boat here."

    Feng sighed, "You have retreated to the clouds and live in seclusion, enjoying the simple pleasures of herbs and wood, and you are on par with the immortals, while I have fallen into the sea of dust and will die in a routine and uneventful way, the distance between us is far apart!"

    Xiu said, "That is not the case. You are a person of wealth and status, benefiting the times and nurturing things, relied upon by the world. What value does someone like me have? In the realm of moral and social norms, I am happy in the three mountains; in the flow of loyalty and filial piety, I am spread throughout the four seas; grains and beans are sufficient for taste, better than elixirs.
