He mistakenly thought it was a pearl, and upon closer inspection, suddenly realized, "Things I have never seen before are naturally hard to guess. At first, I thought pearls were small things, but now I know pearls can move." He then asked, "Is it for sale?" Someone said it belonged to the owner. The owner deliberately raised the price, and he bought it for thirty thousand units of currency. He secretly rejoiced, thinking that since the text spoke of the high value of pearls, he had obtained one at a low price, a great profit. Thus, those who knew of the value of pearls began to sell pigs, and whenever he came across one, he would buy it, regardless of size, at the same price. His old servant strongly advised against it, but he would not listen. After a year, he had hundreds of pigs, the pens could not hold them, and the filth spread throughout the house. He hired several people to feed them, but they could not keep up. The sounds of the pigs were incessant, day and night, mingling with the sounds of reading. He gradually became unable to bear it and suddenly said, "Ancient people treasured pearls, which I find hard to understand." He ordered them all driven away. Calculating the expenses, they amounted to nearly several hundred thousand units of currency. The family was thus somewhat impoverished.

    429

    著年齿既壮，仆恐其斩嗣，劝之娶妻，著默然良久曰：“汝言良是。书固有之曰：“娶妻如之何？”但不知娶妻如何耳！”仆曰：“公读书，岂不闻‘不孝有三，无后为大’？”著曰：“此与娶妻何与？且何以言后耶？”仆恨其愚甚，乃笑曰：“姑依书为之何害？”著许诺。仆遂乞邻里为之媒定。迨吉，轺碪至，有赞于堂者曰：“拜！”著愕眙木立，问：“何为？”仆相之跪起，乃得成礼。著笑曰：“我知之矣，娶妻乃如此。”洎合卺。熟视新妇曰：“汝亦女子邪？”心惩前事，执礼甚恭。夜虽共寝，绝不敢复触其胸。久之，妇不能忍，私教以人道所在。著不觉畅言曰：“此大乐事，而书中略不及之，读之何为？”次日尽焚其书，不复览。

    Explanation

    429

    When Zhe's years had grown strong, I feared he would cut off his descendants, and thus urged him to marry. Zhe remained silent for a long time, then said, "Your words are indeed correct. The books certainly say, 'How to marry a wife?' But I do not know how to marry a wife!" I replied, "Sir, you read the classics. Have you not heard, 'There are three forms of filial impiety, and the greatest is not to have descendants'?" Zhe asked, "What does this have to do with marrying a wife? And what do you mean by descendants?" I was frustrated by his ignorance and laughed, saying, "What harm is there in following the book?" Zhe agreed. I then asked the neighbors to arrange the marriage. When the auspicious day arrived, the matchmaker came, and someone in the hall said, "Bow!" Zhe stood motionless, staring blankly, and asked, "What is this for?" I helped him kneel and rise, and the ceremony was completed. Zhe smiled and said, "I understand now, marrying a wife is like this." On the night of the wedding, he looked at his new wife and said, "Are you also a woman?" Reflecting on his previous experiences, he was very respectful and adhered to the proper etiquette. Although they slept together at night, he dared not touch her chest again. After a while, the wife could no longer bear it and privately taught him about the proper way of human conduct. Zhe exclaimed in delight, "This is a great pleasure, and yet the books barely mention it. Why read them?" The next day, he burned all the books and never read them again.

    430

    耳食录二编卷六
    　　张　碧　云
    　　有诸生应豫章举者，曰张碧云，税居观音寺。其邻父者，亦张姓，尝仕末僚，罢官居此。生以同姓故，往投刺焉。邻父喜，延之，礼数隆异。由是遂投分，往来甚密。已而生报罢，遄归其家，邻父送之曰：“郎君年少才俊，不鄙老夫，甚辱高义，今如此云别，何以为怀？”生曰：“会待次科，重承颜色耳！”邻父曰：“甚幸。”复执手叹曰：“惜哉惜哉！”生亦殊惘惘。别后浪迹吴越间，音问遂绝。

    Explanation

    430

    Volume 6 of Ershi Lu: Second Compilation

    Zhang Bihui

    There was a scholar named Zhang Bihui who participated in the Yuzhang examination. He stayed temporarily at Guanyin Temple. His neighbor, an elder also surnamed Zhang, had once served as a minor official before retiring and settling in the area. Zhang Bihui, due to their shared surname, visited the neighbor and presented his calling card. The elder was pleased and welcomed him, treating him with great respect. From then on, they became close friends, and their interactions were frequent. Later, when Zhang Bihui failed the examination, he hurriedly returned home. The elder, seeing him off, said, "Young sir, you are talented and well-mannered, not looking down on an old man like me. Your high regard has deeply honored me. Now that we part like this, how can I bear it?" Zhang Bihui replied, "I will wait for the next examination and look forward to seeing you again!" The elder said, "That is very fortunate." He then clasped Zhang Bihui's hand and sighed, "What a pity! What a pity!" Zhang Bihui also felt deeply melancholy. After their farewell, he wandered in the Wu and Yue regions, and their communication ceased entirely.

    431

    逮生归，重赴乡试，已越一科矣。及豫章之郊，见道旁小山一石碣，署曰：“张碧云之墓。”心甚诧异，己实未死，谁奠斯邱者？而姓名之符耶？低徊向暮，始趋车入城，侨寓城西。
    　　试事既毕，忽忆邻父，即访之。至则高柳婆娑，蒿莱没径。叩门良久，一老婢出应。向讯邻父，已下世，绝无子息，其妪尚存耳。生为凄恻。婢犹识之曰：“子非张郎者乎？”生曰：“然。”婢乃垂泣曰：“吾家惨苦，子尚未知，请见主母而说之。”遂领生进见。一老妪坐南床绩麻，伛偻昏聩。婢白客至，通姓名者再，妪乃惊起，旋复涕洟。坐生于破甑之旁，婢进茶一瓯，殆不可饮，生强为啜尽。妪哽咽而语，不能了了，婢为代述之。

    Explanation

    431

    Upon returning home, the protagonist took the provincial examination again, having already passed one cycle. When he reached the outskirts of Yuzhang, he saw a small hill by the roadside with a stone tablet inscribed with the words "The Tomb of Zhang Bihui." He was greatly surprised, for he was still very much alive, and wondered who had offered sacrifices at this grave. Could it be a coincidence that the name matched his own? He lingered until evening, then hurried into the city and took temporary residence in the western part of the city.

    After the examination matters were completed, he suddenly remembered his neighbor's father and immediately went to visit him. Upon arrival, he found tall willows swaying and wild grass overgrowing the paths. After knocking on the door for a long time, an old maid servant came out to answer. When he inquired about the neighbor's father, she told him that he had already passed away, leaving no children, but the old woman was still alive. The protagonist felt deeply moved. The maid, recognizing him, said, "Are you not Zhang Lang?" The protagonist replied, "Yes." The maid then wept and said, "Our family has been through great suffering, and you may not yet know. Please see the mistress and tell her." She then led the protagonist to meet the old woman, who was sitting in the south of the room spinning hemp, stooped and dull-witted. The maid informed the old woman that a guest had arrived, stating the protagonist's name twice. The old woman then startled up and soon started crying again. The protagonist sat by the side of a broken pot, and the maid brought him one bowl of tea, which was almost undrinkable. The protagonist forced himself to drink it all. The old woman spoke while choking with sobs, and her words were unclear. The maid relayed the old woman's words to the protagonist.

    432

    盖邻父有女，亦名碧云，年始笄矣，丽而文。父母怜受，恃女为性命，相攸数岁，卒鲜可妻者。方生之谒邻父也，邻父奇其姓名，及见生，又钦其器字。女闻其同姓名也，亦数数窥生。邻父常借览生所属文辞，女辄窃观读再三，未尝不称善。妪觉其意，告邻父，欲婿生，而格于同姓，且虑生已婚，莫可为计。会生亦告归。议遂寝，而女自是每不怿，微吟短叹，渐以流露，罗帏翠被之间，始多泪痕矣。又书己名而绣之，尽藏袖中，夜则置枕下。数月遂病，废饮食。又数月竟殁。家人皆知女之为生而死也，而生不知。邻父痛女死，未几亦卒。

    Explanation

    432

    In a certain village, there lived a neighbor whose daughter, also named Bìyún, had just reached the age of笄, a traditional coming-of-age ceremony for women. She was both beautiful and cultured, and her parents loved her dearly, depending on her as their life. They had been looking for a suitable match for several years, but ultimately found few suitable candidates.

    When the man, whom we will call Shēng, first visited the neighbor's father, the neighbor's father found his name and surname unusual. When he saw Shēng, he also admired his character and writing. The girl heard that they shared the same name and surname and often peeked at him. The neighbor's father often borrowed Shēng's writings to read, and the girl secretly read them repeatedly, always praising them.

    The old woman, noticing the girl's feelings, told the neighbor's father that she wanted Shēng to marry her. However, the plan was hindered by the taboo of marrying within the same surname, and the old woman worried that Shēng was already married, making it impossible to plan anything. Coincidentally, Shēng also announced his return home, and the plan was abandoned. Thereafter, the girl often felt unhappy, softly singing and sighing, gradually revealing her feelings in the silk curtains and green quilt, where she began to have many tears. She would write her name and embroider it, keeping it all in her sleeves, and at night she would place it under her pillow.

    After several months, she fell ill and stopped eating and drinking. After another few months, she died. Everyone in the family knew that the girl died because of the man, but the man did not know. The neighbor's father grieved over the girl's death, and soon after, he also died.

    433

    卒后三余年，生始至，闻婢言之，不禁哭之恸。婢又言女平生雅善笔札，病中悉火之，惟镜匣中偶遗数纸，得不焚。翁已死，家中无识字者，不知是何语，又不欲示外人，今尚贮故处。生乞取观之，得诗草二首，词半阕，及临写《黄庭经》一片，而尘湮鼠啮，字句断缺，盖不可读矣。生感女意，再拜于妪曰：“某庸愚贱子，又忝宗系，诚不敢妄托非爱，辱诬贤女，得罪名教。然铭心之惠，不敢忘也。请妹视贤女而母事夫人，迎归敝里，终身依侧，申万一之报而已。夫人幸无辞焉！”妪泣而许之。生又欲拜邻父墓及女之墓而后行，忽忆向所经过，乃即女墓也，益悲不自胜。遂返馆舍，为文以告女，明日往吊，而焚诸其墓。其文曰：

    Explanation

    433

    Three years after the death, the narrator finally arrived and, upon hearing the servant's words, could not help but cry bitterly. The servant then mentioned that the woman had always been skilled in writing, and during her illness, all her writings were burned, except for a few papers that had accidentally been left in the mirror box and thus were not destroyed. The husband had already passed away, and there were no literate people in the household to understand the contents of the papers, nor did they wish to show them to outsiders; they were still stored in their original place. The narrator asked to see the papers and found two drafts of poems, half of a verse, and a copy of the Huangting Jing (Yellow Court Scripture). However, the papers were dusty and gnawed by rodents, with many words and phrases missing, making them unreadable. Deeply moved by the woman's intentions, the narrator bowed twice to the old woman and said, "I, a humble and unworthy member of this family, truly dare not falsely claim affection, defame the virtuous woman, or offend the principles of propriety. Yet, the profound kindness I have received, I will never forget. I ask the sister to treat the virtuous woman as a mother and serve the lady, to welcome her back to our village, to remain by her side for life, and to repay even a tiny fraction of her kindness. Please do not refuse, madam!" The old woman wept and agreed. The narrator then wished to pay respects at the graves of the neighbor's father and the woman before leaving, but suddenly remembered that he had already passed by the woman's grave, and he became even more sorrowful, unable to bear it. He returned to his study, wrote an article to inform the woman, and the next day went to pay his respects, burning the article at her grave. The text of the article read:

    This translation aims to maintain the emotional depth and cultural nuances of the original Classical Chinese text while ensuring it reads naturally in English.

    434

    维年月日，张碧云焚香酹酒，敢告张张碧去之灵。卿即我耶？我即卿耶？夫阴阳者互结之根株，男女者同开之跗萼。引癶团土，既听娲皇；跃冶铸金，宁为干将？而拘拘造物，乃限以方隅；扰扰生民，复分其氏族。亿万姓要无二本，五百年谁是一家？且敬宗收族，不少卫仲卿；即别嫌明微，仍多吴孟子。然一源所发，百世不通。诚大义之所闲，孰深情之敢越乎？
    　　若夫事属怜才，分同知已。描眉黛笔，偷评罗隐之诗；绕指红丝，欲绣平原之像。闺中感遇，尤荣于流水高山；梦里相思，绝异乎朝云暮雨。素心如此，青眼非常。斯则性命之恩，不作形骸之论矣。况乎画阁藏春，香闺似海；衣缄琥珀，了不闻声；屏暗琉璃，何曾透影？徒以青衫作客，兰若偶卜芳邻；黄石论交，松门常寻宗竣。初不识王孙有女，琴瑟相求；更谁问小姑无郎，箜篌自叩？而红绡聚泪，竟少人知；紫玉成烟，乃由我死。尚安得拘牵典礼，恪守宗盟，反因瓜葛之亲，致绝芜蘅之爱哉！

    Explanation

    434

    On the day of the month of the year, Zhang Bihua burned incense and poured wine, boldly reporting to the spirit of Zhang Bihua. Are you I? Am I you? The yin and yang are the intertwined roots, and men and women are the co-opening petals and sepals. Nüwa molded the clay, and Gan Jiang forged the sword—yet the Creator limits with boundaries, and the common people are divided into clans. Billions of surnames share no common origin, and in five hundred years, who can claim to be of the same family? Respect for ancestors and the gathering of clans, not unlike Wei Zhongling; avoiding suspicion and clarifying subtle matters, still much like Wu Mengzi. Yet from a single source, a hundred generations are separated. Truly, these are the boundaries set by great principles—how can deep feelings dare to cross them?

    As for matters of compassion for talent, sharing the same understanding. Drawing eyebrows with delicate brushstrokes, secretly evaluating the poetry of Lu Yi; the red thread around the finger, wishing to embroider a portrait of Lu Buwei. Feelings of admiration within the confines of the inner chambers, especially honored by the profound and refined appreciation of flowing water and towering mountains; longing in dreams, utterly different from the fleeting clouds and rains of dawn and dusk. Such is the sincerity of the heart, and such is the unusual favor. This is the bond of life and death, not to be reduced to superficial considerations. Moreover, in the secluded and mysterious chambers, the fragrance of the inner chambers is like the sea; garments as precious as amber, silent and unheard; screens as transparent as glass, never revealing their shadows. Only as a guest in the blue robe, occasionally visiting a beautiful neighbor; in the pines, frequently seeking the lineage of the clan. At first, not knowing that the nobleman's daughter sought a harmonious and loving relationship; who would ask about the young woman without a husband, playing the konghou in solitude? And the tears gathered on the red silk handkerchief, known by few; the beauty fading like purple jade, due to my death. How can one be bound by ritual and tradition, strictly adhering to clan alliances, and thus sever the deep and enduring love due to distant kinship?

    435

    前者群鸦噪晚，匹马嘶秋，旁瞻堕泪之碑，宛是葬身之地。笙吹鹤背，岂王乔竟已入棺；锥处囊中，乃毛遂何曾堕井？宁知桃僵李代，蕙叹芝焚。漆灯未！于沈彬，金碗先从于崔女？盖连蜷青柱，当时未近姮娥；零落红梅，别后难逢驿使。故飘蓬千里，虚拟投金；宿草三年，未闻葬玉。今则谢家月冷，燕子重来；郑曲花残，鹦哥不语。青鸳如故，白鹤无归。愁深南浦之云，瘦损东墙之杏。访陈陶赋诗旧宅，始感啼乌；开吴女写韵遗轩，空悲冷翠。镜台尘掩，印粉留编。研匣云干，团香剩字。素旗丹旐，私招芳草之魂；桂酒椒浆，重拜小桃之墓。

    Explanation

    435

    Formerly, crows cawed at dusk, and a lone horse neighed in autumn; beside the tombstone that caused tears to fall, it seemed like a place of burial. The sound of the flute on the back of a crane, could Wang Qiao have already been laid in his coffin? The needle in the bag, did Mao Sui ever fall into a well? Who could have known that peach trees would die and be replaced by plum trees, or that orchids would lament and be burned? The lacquer lamp had not yet burned out for Shen Bin, but the golden bowl had already been taken by Cui's daughter. The green column, which was not close to the moon goddess, had missed its chance; the fallen red plum blossoms, after parting, were hard to find a messenger to bring news. Thus, the traveler wandered a thousand miles, virtually throwing gold; the grass grew over the grave for three years, and there was no news of the burial of jade. Now, the moon at Xie family's house is cold, and the swallows return; the flowers in Zheng's garden wither, and the parrot remains silent. The blue mandarin ducks remain unchanged, but the white cranes do not return. The clouds over the southern shore are deep with sorrow, and the apricot blossoms on the eastern wall have withered. Visiting the old house of Chen Tao, where poetry was written, one begins to feel the cries of crows; opening the old pavilion where Wu's daughter wrote poetry, one feels the cold greenness in vain. The mirror is covered with dust, and the traces of powder remain. The inkstone is dry, and the scent of incense lingers. White flags and red banners privately summon the soul of fragrant grass; wine and spices are offered again to the tomb of the young peach tree.

    436

    碧云碧云，卿耶我耶，如影随形，如响应声，如萧艾之同香，如笙簧之合韵。我宁作我，卿自怜卿。本异苔而同岑，亦求凰而得凤。杨加柳姓，树合消魂；鸟借花名，鹃应叫血。翠禽临水，乃辱号于鱼师；彩蝶穿云，幸蒙称于凤子。非男非女，何死何生？两美二难之合异，千秋万古之联结。彼绍威结江东之罗，正伦攀城南之杜。相如慕蔺，元叹名雍。但附声华，犹增光宠。矧袭名于琬玉，遂刻苕华；直同谱于金兰，长芬龄颊。

    Explanation

    436

    Blue clouds, blue clouds, are you me? Like shadow follows form, like echo responds to sound, like the scent of xiao and ai is the same, like the harmony of sheng and huang. I will be myself, and you will be yourself. Though different in appearance, we are the same in essence; even in seeking the phoenix, we find the phoenix. Adding the surname Yang to Liu, the trees merge into a soul-shattering union; the bird borrows the name of a flower, and the cuckoo calls with blood. The green bird near the water is called a "fish teacher," while the colorful butterfly flying through the clouds is honored as a "phoenix child." Neither male nor female, what death, what life? The union of two beauties and two difficult things, a bond that lasts for thousands of years. He established a strong connection in the east of the river, while she ascended to the high status of the city south. Sima Xiangru admired Lin Xiangru, and Yuan Tan achieved the name of Yong. Though attached to fame and glory, one still adds to one's glory and honor. Especially when inheriting the name of precious jade, thus carving the tiao hua; directly comparable to the jin lan, long-lasting fragrance on the cheeks.

    437

    方为我庆，宁为卿悲？纵复多情，将毋不达？然而身蒸火宅，委蜕仍难；骨冷泉台，回肠曷已！飞残绛雪，莫逢山叟之师；捣尽元霜，仅见云英之妪。卖珠侍婢，常苦牵萝；积玉邻翁，宁容扑枣。西州长恸，徒拜母而登堂；南郭奇贫，愿移居而共宅。且也雌雄既判，勿处嫌疑；昭穆虽遥，敢干名分？欲作蒹葭之依倚，宜联棠棣之班行。接木无痕，连枝有本。九原侍父，邓伯道不患无儿；四海皆兄，张君瑞何妨有妹？青阳主祀于弧宿，既殊句灵支流；红拂通谱于虬髯，尤胜朱陈嫁娶。

    Explanation

    437

    I rejoice for myself, but why should I lament for you? Even if one is deeply emotional, will it not lead to failure? Yet, being trapped in a fiery house, the process of transformation remains difficult; bones cold in the realm of the dead, the heart's anguish knows no end! The fleeting beauty like red snow never meets the wise teacher of the mountain hermit; striving for purity like white frost, one only encounters the frail old woman. Selling pearls as a servant, one often suffers the hardships of being entangled; a wealthy neighbor, hoarding jade, would not allow picking his fruit. Deep sorrow in the west, one merely pays respects to the mother and enters the hall; extraordinary poverty in the south, one wishes to move and live together. Moreover, once gender roles are determined, avoid ambiguity; even if distant in lineage, dare not violate the proper hierarchy? To rely on reeds, one should join the branches of the peach tree; the connection of branches is seamless, sharing a common root. Serving the father in the nine graves, Deng Boda is not concerned about having no children; all over the world are brothers, Zhang Junrui has no problem having a sister. The east governs the ancestral rites at the star of Arc, distinct from the branch of the river; Hong Fu connects the genealogy with the dragon's beard, surpassing the marriage of Zhu Chen.

    438

    呜呼！慈鸠泣雨，行就鹊巢；断雁鸣霜，将辞鸳冢。坟前玫瑰，讵感行人？门外批杷，应思故主。幸从亲于手足，聊铭德于肝肠。孤霜之感何穷？大雷之书莫寄。冬青树老，年年风雨清明；光碧堂深，日日烟霞伴侣。固识三生因果，当著《同姓名录》中；谁将一代容华，更入《续神仙传》里？
    　　是岁生复落解，遂与妪与老婢俱归。时人称其义焉。
    　　经数年，生游浔阳。舟既泊，忽一雉拂舵楼过，止于沙滩上。生上岸欲攫得雉，雉且飞且止，生漫逐之。行稍远，雉噢然一声，穿树而去。却见树间一老人呼生曰：“郎君何为？尚识老夫否？”生审之，则邻父也，惊喜曰：“公固无恙耶？”邻父曰：“　幸无他。暮眺江流，偶出于此，不谋相遇。”生问其所居，邻父曰：“循平林而西，敝居斯在。便请相过。”

    Explanation

    438

    Alas! The compassionate dove weeps in the rain, heading to a new home; the broken geese cry in the frost, about to leave the grave of the mandarin ducks. The roses before the tomb, do they feel the passerby? The loquats outside the door, should they recall the former master? Fortunately, I have found kinship in my siblings, and I shall engrave their virtues in my heart. How endless is the feeling of loneliness and coldness? The letter of the big thunder cannot be delivered. The holly tree grows old, year after year, enduring the winds and rains of Qingming; the bright green hall is deep, day after day, a companion to the mist and clouds. I understand the three-lifetime karma, and it should be recorded in the Tongxingminglu (Book of the Same Names); who will write about the beauty of this generation, and include it in the Xu Xiansheng Zhuan (Continuation of the Immortals' Biography)?

    In that year, the child fell ill again and was released from school, returning home with the old woman and the elderly maid. At the time, people praised his filial piety.

    After several years, the child traveled to Xunyang. When the boat docked, a pheasant suddenly flew past the steering house and landed on the sandy beach. The child got out of the boat and tried to catch the pheasant, but the bird flew and stopped, and the child followed it casually. As he walked a bit further, the pheasant gave a loud cry and flew through the trees and disappeared. Then he saw an old man calling out to him from among the trees: "Young master, what are you doing? Do you still remember me?" The child looked closely and realized it was his neighbor, and he was surprised and said: "Are you still well, sir?" The old man replied: "Thankfully, I am fine. I was looking out at the river in the evening, and happened to come here, not expecting to meet you." The child asked where he lived, and the old man said: "Follow the flat woods to the west, and my humble home is there. Please come and visit."

    439

    生从之半里许，乃至，因高墉而深宅也。生执子婿礼，从容展问。邻父曰：“别后携弱女，卜筑于此数年矣。感涌厚爱，未尝去怀。”生诧曰：“女公子亦无恙耶？”邻父曰：“固在。”使人谓女曰：“汝兄至矣，宜出见，勿复为嫌。”有顷，女迟回而去，眉黛凄然，无语，即退。生亦睨视叹惋而已。既灯，闻呼门曰：“夫人至！”门启而妪入，邻父与生起逆之。女闻妪至，亦趋出相见，各悲叙。生愕然讶妪在吾家，焉得至此？妪乃谓生曰：“吾本欲俟汝为别，仆御在门，不容稽延，怏怏就道，今反晤于此，实出望外。”遂述生家中事甚悉，且曰：“老婢尚留备役使，未与偕来。新妇且病，可即归，然终吉无患。”将曙，邻父促生行。各相涕泣，送生至故处而别。

    Explanation

    439

    The young man followed for about half a li, and then arrived at a place with high walls and a deep courtyard. He performed the rituals of a son-in-law, asking questions calmly and in detail. The neighbor's father said, "Since we parted, I have taken my frail daughter and settled here for several years. I have always felt deeply grateful for your kindness and have never forgotten it." The young man was surprised and asked, "Is your young lady safe and sound?" The neighbor's father replied, "Indeed, she is." He then had someone tell the girl, "Your brother has arrived; you should come out to see him, and do not hesitate." After a while, the girl hesitated and then went away, her eyebrows furrowed in sorrow, silent, and then retreated. The young man glanced at her and sighed in lamentation. As the lamps were lit, they heard someone calling at the door, "The lady has arrived!" The door opened, and the old woman entered. The neighbor's father and the young man stood up to greet her. The girl heard the old woman had arrived and also hurried out to meet her. They each shared their sorrowful stories. The young man was astonished and asked, "How did the old woman end up in my home? How did she get here?" The old woman then told the young man, "I originally wanted to wait for you to say goodbye, but the servants at the gate did not allow me to delay, so I reluctantly set off. Now I unexpectedly meet you here, which is truly beyond my expectations." She then recounted the details of what had happened at the young man's home and added, "The old maid is still there to serve, but she did not come with me. Your new wife is sick and can return soon, but there will be no worries in the end." Just before dawn, the neighbor's father urged the young man to leave. They each cried, and they sent him off to his original place, where they parted ways.

    440

    生恍然如梦觉，遽返舟。旦而迹之，野岸空林，固无村落，有社令祠焉。社令之旁，新塑夫人像，深为嗟异。度妪必已死。社令乃邻父也。亟棹舟还家，妪果以是日暴卒。生妇亦果病，亦寻愈。其他家事，悉如妪言。
    　　铁丸
    　　闻某公言：昔曾遇游客，类三齐年少，布衫广袖，腰系夹袋，内定二铁丸，别无长物。或问丸所用，答曰：“弄具耳！”或延拆字者于家，问叩纷然。客至，见之曰：“此亦拆字耶？”众问：“客能乎？”曰：“亦尝学之，然不能若是。请各心识一字，余乃能测之。”众异其言，竟试之，无弗符者。次至一人，客忽踌躇曰：“君之所识者‘天’也，而字不类。”乃取笔，画形作国书“阿补喀。”“阿补喀”者，国语，言天也。其人果识“阿补喀”。盖欲变文以验其术也。遂笑曰：“良然。”其神异如此。或曰：“人苟造意，子必知之，何以待子？”客曰：“不知也，此特以机相触耳！”

    440

    Translation:

    The man suddenly felt as if he had awakened from a dream and hastily returned to his boat. In the morning, he traced his steps and found himself on a wild riverbank surrounded by an empty forest, where there was certainly no village, only a shrine to the local deity. Beside the shrine, there was a newly carved image of a lady, which deeply amazed and puzzled him. He reasoned that the old woman must have died. The local deity was, in fact, the village elder. He quickly rowed the boat back home, and indeed, the old woman had died suddenly on that day. His wife also fell ill but soon recovered. All other household matters turned out exactly as the old woman had predicted.

    Iron Balls

    I heard from a certain official: Once he encountered a traveler who resembled a young man from the state of Qi, dressed in a cloth shirt with wide sleeves and a sash tied around his waist, carrying two iron balls inside, with nothing else of note. Someone asked what the iron balls were for, and he replied, "They are a toy or trick!" Someone else invited a fortune-teller to his home, and questions and inquiries were numerous. When the guest arrived, he saw and said, "Is this also fortune-telling?" The crowd asked, "Can you do it?" He replied, "I have also studied it, but I cannot do it like this. Please each think of a character, and I will be able to tell it." The crowd was amazed by his words and tested him, and none of their predictions failed to match. Then, another person came, and the guest hesitated and said, "What you recognized was 'heaven,' but the character does not resemble it." He took a pen and drew the shape as the national script "A bu ka." "A bu ka" is a term in a foreign language, meaning "heaven." The person indeed recognized "A bu ka." It seems he wanted to change the text to test his skill. Then he laughed and said, "Indeed so." His supernatural abilities were like this. Someone said, "If someone makes up an idea, you must know it, how do you treat me?" The guest replied, "I don't know, it's just a coincidence."

    441

    他日之郊外，逢弹鸟者数人，适人隼戾空，客使弹之，弹鸟者谢曰：“高矣疾矣，非弓徼所及。”客笑曰：“易耳！”即袋中铁丸抛之。隼应手翻堕，胸已洞矣，丸故在客手。
    　　庐　山　僧
    　　近有某人裹粮游匡庐，重趼不息，至紫霄峰下，石室轩然。有僧破衲枯坐，两目上睑覆下寸余，知其异人也。再拜而问，僧拨开一目视之，炅炅如碧玉有光，叹曰：“噫！尔奚以来？余，尔祖也。”某讶曰：“吾祖殁时，某尚未生。即于土五十年矣，安得在此？”僧曰：“向实蜕化，尔父不知也。”乃道其世族事状，悉符合。某泣拜曰：“果吾祖矣！闻祖平生好内典，不图竟证佛果。今幸遇祖，何以令之？”僧曰：“尔非此中人，宜便去！”某涕泣不舍，乞留一宿，许之。访以后事，悉不答。

    Explanation

    441

    One day in the outskirts, he encountered several bird shooters. Just as a hawk soared in the sky, the guest asked them to shoot it. The bird shooters replied, "It is high and fast, beyond the reach of a bow." The guest laughed and said, "It's easy!" He then threw an iron ball from his bag. The hawk fell over immediately, its chest already pierced, and the ball remained in the guest's hand.

    Mount Lu Monk

    Recently, a certain person traveled to Mount Lu with provisions, his feet heavy with calluses, and he did not rest until he reached the foot of Purple Cloud Peak, where a spacious stone room awaited. There was a monk sitting in meditation with tattered robes, his upper eyelids covering the lower part by a few inches. Recognizing him as an extraordinary person, the traveler bowed twice and asked, "What brings you here?" The monk opened one eye and looked at him, his gaze clear and bright like a piece of jade. He sighed and said, "Ah! Why have you come? I am your ancestor." The traveler was astonished and said, "My ancestor died when I was not yet born. He has been dead for fifty years, how can he be here?" The monk replied, "He actually transformed, and your father does not know." He then recounted the details of the family lineage and events, all of which matched perfectly. The traveler wept and bowed, saying, "Indeed, he is my ancestor! I heard that my ancestor was fond of Buddhist scriptures, and I did not expect him to achieve enlightenment. Now that I have met my ancestor, what should I do?" The monk said, "You are not one of us, you should leave!" The traveler, unable to bear parting, begged to stay for one night, and the monk reluctantly agreed. When the traveler asked about future matters, the monk did not answer.

    442

    比夜，千山皆暗，独石室光明如昼。僧曰：“尔畏乎？”某曰：“祖在，亦何畏？”僧徐举指弹坐旁石壁，壁忽辟一舍，使某处其中。戒之曰：“倘有所睹，勿畏，亦勿语，勿出。今夜适有事矣。”某应诺。俄闻风飕飕起林间，万木怒号，有鬼魅无数坌入石室。僧摇首者再，乃见小人百十，自僧两耳出，如连珠激箭，执兵刺鬼。鬼悉遁，小人复还耳中。顷之，复有奇鬼修修然参伍而至，蓝身巨吻，类夜叉。僧张口嘘这，则一石堕地分裂，尽化为力士击鬼。鬼又遁，力士亦隐。某战栗齿叩，僧曰：“未已也，无恐！”旋闻崩崖裂壑，砰訇不已，有二人屹立如山。僧大放两目，二人忽缩小，各入一目中，目即闭，声亦遽止。已而栴檀氤氲，仙乐锵然，幡幢队来，金光灼灼。僧即下座，作礼讫。便解衲衣，胸乃洞开，恍见一人端坐其中。僧遂冉冉升空，俄顷而杳，天亦曙矣。
    　　某出，拾得履一双。归白其父。父见履泣曰：“尔祖敛时所著也。”

    Explanation

    442

    On that night, thousands of mountains were shrouded in darkness, but the stone chamber alone was bright as day. The monk asked, "Are you afraid?" The narrator replied, "Since the ancestor is present, what have I to fear?" The monk slowly raised a finger and tapped the stone wall beside him. Suddenly, the wall opened to reveal a room, and the monk gestured for the narrator to enter. He instructed, "If you see anything, do not be afraid, do not speak, and do not leave. There is something happening tonight." The narrator agreed. Soon, the wind began to rustle in the forest, and thousands of trees roared. Countless ghosts and demons rushed into the stone chamber. The monk shook his head twice, and then the narrator saw hundreds of little people emerge from the monk's two ears, like linked pearls and arrows, holding weapons to pierce the ghosts. All the ghosts fled, and the little people returned to the monk's ears. Shortly after, strange ghosts arrived in a mixed and orderly manner, with blue bodies and large mouths, resembling night demons. The monk opened his mouth and exhaled, and then a stone fell and split, transforming entirely into warriors who struck the ghosts. The ghosts again fled, and the warriors also disappeared. The narrator trembled and his teeth chattered, and the monk reassured him, "It is not over yet, do not be afraid!" Soon, the sound of collapsing cliffs and splitting ravines continued without stopping, and two people stood upright like mountains. The monk greatly opened his eyes, and the two people suddenly shrank and entered one of his eyes each. His eyes closed, and the sound immediately stopped. Then, sandalwood incense filled the air, celestial music resounded, banners and flags came in procession, and golden light shone brightly. The monk immediately descended from the seat, completed the ritual, and then removed his robe. His chest opened up, and the narrator vaguely saw one person sitting in it. The monk then slowly rose into the air, disappeared in a moment, and the sky also became light.

    The narrator left and found a pair of shoes. He returned home and told his father. The father saw the shoes and cried, "It is what your ancestor wore at the time of his burial."

    443

    鼪
    　　某公子尝养鸽。所居宅五区，闳敞深邃。东偏有小门，达于长廓。廓有旁室，架木为鸽巢百十如窗棂，以卵以雏，鸽以蕃息。一夜，忽失鸽数十头。公子疑，夜持棒伺诸巢下。已见有鼪鼠长数尺，径来取鸽。公子突击之不中，鼠人立相向。跃登公子顶，啮其衣领。俄而群鼠纷至，共围公子。公子大哗，僮仆共操具来。　鼪乃引去。公了怒曰：“是何可耐？”
    　　旦日，偕数仆持兵赴之。将入旁室，闻门内言曰：“姑勿来，来且不利！”从者惧，皆止公了。公子曰：“鼠辈诈吓耳！”排扉径入，则见鼠睛睒然。如万道金星，纵横巢上，慑而退。
    　　是夜跳踉百端，若有大木从屋抛下。烛之，无有也。南壁圜窗广可逾尺，规以玻璃，乃有白面映窗，大与窗埒。而北户有物，触扉求入。仆辈大怖，共相抱持。公子拔剑奋臂曰：“吾将止于此耶！将焉避之，若犹未也，妖何能为？鼠辈无逃，吾与尔并命矣！”于是二物并隐去。后亦不复出。

    Explanation

    443

    A Rat

    Once upon a time, there was a young nobleman who kept pigeons. His residence consisted of five spacious and deep compounds. To the east of his main house, there was a small door leading to a long corridor. On the side of the corridor, there was a small room where he had built over a hundred pigeon nests with wooden frames resembling window grilles. The pigeons laid eggs and raised their young, multiplying in number. One night, however, he suddenly lost several dozen pigeons. Suspecting something was amiss, the nobleman took a stick and waited beneath the nests. Soon, he saw a long-tailed rat, several feet long, come directly to take the pigeons. The nobleman tried to hit the rat with his stick but missed. The rat stood upright facing him, jumped onto the nobleman's head, and bit his collar. Soon after, a group of rats came in a flurry, surrounding the nobleman. The nobleman made a loud noise, and his servants came with tools. The rats then led away. The nobleman angrily said, "How can this be endured?"

    The next day, he went with several servants holding weapons. As they were about to enter the side room, they heard a voice from inside the door saying, "Do not come in, for it will be unfavorable!" The servants were afraid and tried to stop the nobleman. The nobleman said, "These rats are just scaring us!" He pushed open the door and entered directly. He saw the rats' eyes shining like thousands of golden stars, swarming over the nests, and the servants were frightened and retreated.

    That night, the rats jumped and leaped in many ways, as if a large piece of wood was thrown from the house. When they lit a candle to look, there was nothing. On the south wall, there was a round window, decorated with glass. A white face appeared on the window, as large as the window itself. At the north door, there was something that touched the door and tried to enter. The servants were greatly terrified and held each other. The nobleman raised his sword and said, "Will I stop here? Where will I hide? If it is not yet, what can the demon do? The rats cannot escape, I will die with you!" The two mysterious figures then disappeared together. After that, they did not appear again.

    444

    韩五
    　　国初，宝应县捕役韩五者，贫不能炊。思常捕响马，姑试一效之。为计良处，乃从人假羸马，挟弓矢而出，伏于麦陇之幽。
    　　是时大乱初平，流亡未夏，道路之间，尚少人迹。亭午，始见一骑缓缓来，台笠深衣，挂囊于鞍，略类商旅。韩五私计盍一利市，引弓呼之曰：“速解尔橐，束且毕尔命！”矢乃发。客从容举鞭拂之，矢旁落。又发亦如之，五矢不中。矢尽，韩恐，策马走。客笑曰：“行劫者返避人耶？虽然，尔焉逃？”俄闻风声肃肃，客追已将及，则短衣执刀，叱韩曰：“毋走。”韩度不可脱，亟投马下，叩首乞命曰：“良以母老待哺，不得已出此。今乃出试，不图误犯壮士，幸宽宥之！”号泣战慄。客谛视之曰“尔尚谨朴，姑贷尔，随余来，勿恐！”韩惧，勉从之。

    Explanation

    444

    In the early years of the Ming Dynasty, there was a man named Han Wu, a constable from Baoying County, who was so poor that he could not even afford to cook. He often thought about capturing bandits, and decided to try his hand at it. He devised a good plan and borrowed a weak horse from someone, armed with a bow and arrows, and set out, lying in ambush in a secluded part of the wheat field.

    At that time, the great turmoil had just been quelled, and many people had fled their homes. The roads were still sparsely traveled. At noon, he saw a rider coming slowly along the road, wearing a straw hat and a long robe, with a bag hanging from his saddle, resembling a merchant. Han Wu privately thought, "Why not try to make a profit?" He drew his bow and called out, "Quickly take off your bag, and I will tie you up and end your life!" The arrow was released. The guest calmly raised his whip to brush it away, and the arrow fell to the side. He fired again, and it was the same result; five arrows were all missed. When the arrows were all used up, Han Wu was afraid and spurred his horse to run. The guest laughed and said, "Is a robber who runs away from people? Although, how can you escape?" Soon, he heard the sound of the wind rustling, and the guest was already close behind. The guest was wearing short clothes and holding a knife, shouting at Han Wu, "Do not run." Han Wu realized he could not escape, and quickly dismounted from his horse, kneeling down and begging for his life, saying, "Because my mother is old and needs to be fed, I had no choice but to do this. I am now trying it out, and I did not expect to offend a brave man. Please forgive me!" He was crying and trembling. The guest looked at him carefully and said, "You are still cautious and simple. I will forgive you temporarily. Come with me, do not be afraid!" Han Wu was afraid, but he mustered the courage to follow him.

    445

    行十余里，入一山，甚邃，丛树间得穴焉。进之甚暗。半里许有光。既而屋舍忽见，客引韩入内。则先有数十人在，多虬须彪眼，衣巾伟岸，见客起问：“十四兄来何暮也。”客滑稽数语，众目韩，皆大笑。已而酒馔堆垛，规地而会，序列兄弟之次，以韩殿席焉。饮啖甚壮，言论豪猾。比暮，华灯明炬。照烛林野。夜中纵横醉卧，手足相枕藉。韩憖憖然莫知所为，竟夕不成寐。明日众醒，客为韩请曰：“此人尚有母，贫不能养，殊可矜悯，宜少资润之。”皆曰：“诺。”各探囊出金钱珠贝不等，无虑数千金，以授韩。韩惶恐拜谢，客曰：“视尔马弱，焉能致此归，吾仍送尔行。”一人曰：“我曹亦散耳！后二年当期于山东。”皆曰：“诺。”遂拱手各去。或独行，或侣行，或东或西，顷刻都杳。

    Explanation

    445

    After walking several miles, they entered a mountain that was very deep, and among the dense trees, they discovered a cave. Inside the cave, it was extremely dark. After about half a mile, there was light. Soon after, houses suddenly appeared, and the guest led Han inside. There were already dozens of people there, many with curling beards and bold eyes, dressed in grand attire. They rose to greet the guest and asked, "Why did you, Brother Fourteen, arrive so late?" The guest spoke a few slippery words, and the crowd looked at Han, all laughing. Soon, the table was piled high with food and drink, and they gathered on the ground, arranging themselves in order of seniority, with Han at the end of the seating. They drank and ate heartily, speaking boldly and recklessly. By evening, brilliant lamps and bright torches illuminated the forest and fields. At night, they lay drunk and sprawled, their hands and feet intertwined. Han felt uneasy and did not know what to do, and he slept not at all that night. The next morning, when everyone woke up, the guest spoke on Han's behalf, saying, "This person still has a mother, poor and unable to support her, truly deserving of pity. It would be good to give him some financial support." All said, "Yes." Each reached into their pockets and took out money and pearls of varying amounts, no less than several thousand coins, and gave it to Han. Han was terrified and bowed in thanks. The guest said, "Seeing your horse is weak, how can it carry you home? I will still send you on your way." One person said, "We are also dispersing! We will meet again in two years in Shandong." All said, "Yes." They bowed and left, some going alone, some in pairs, some east, some west, and in a moment, they were all gone.

    446

    客乃与韩俱，谓韩曰：“盗岂易为哉！诸君皆技勇绝人。故驰骤绿林，鲜有失败。吾亦有薄技，使尔观之。”袖中出匕首，指前椿树第三株标枝东接者曰：“吾截其某杈。”掷之，杈落，匕首故在手。又别拟之，三掷而三中。韩咨嗟惊诧。客曰：“尔向者太孟浪，幸遇我，倘值吾兄弟之暴者，尔作此树杈久矣！”韩唯唯，请客示姓名，誓图报效。客笑曰：“无须尔！吾属虽肝人喋血，其实行云流水耳！散游天下，率二岁一期会，虽数千里不失约，此外无知姓名者，尔何问为？”
    　　既至韩门，解所赠囊掷地，曰：“吾往矣！”已纵马绝尘而去。韩赖其资，遂为富人。
    　　易内
    　　有甲乙相善者，乙妻有姿，甲通焉，乙知之而弗禁也。他日乙偶自外至，获甲于床，佯怒，甲叩头曰：“若不深责，愿以荆妇荐枕席，易内而处，亦犹行古之道也！”乙许之，与之期而纵之。

    Explanation

    446

    The guest then went with Han and said to him, "Is it easy to be a bandit? All of you are extraordinarily skilled and brave. That's why you live in the wilderness, rarely failing in your endeavors. I also have a small skill; let me show you." He took a dagger from his sleeve and pointed to the third branch of a tree in front of them, saying, "I will cut this branch." He threw the dagger, and the branch fell, but the dagger remained in his hand. He threw it again, and again the branch fell, with the dagger still in his hand. He threw it a third time, and it hit the branch again. Han was amazed and astonished. The guest said, "You were too reckless before. Fortunately, you met me. If you had met one of my more violent brothers, you would have been this tree branch for a long time." Han nodded in agreement and asked the guest for his name, swearing to repay him. The guest laughed and said, "There's no need for you. We, though we might cut throats and spill blood, in reality, it's just like flowing clouds and water. We wander around the world, meeting every two years. Even if it's thousands of miles away, we never miss the appointment. There are no others who know our names, why should you ask?"

    After arriving at Han's door, the guest threw the bag he had given Han to the ground and said, "I'm leaving!" He then rode off, leaving a cloud of dust behind. Han relied on the money from the bag and became a wealthy person.

    Exchange of Wives

    There were two friends, A and B, who were very close. B's wife was beautiful, and A had an affair with her. B knew about it but did not forbid it. One day, B happened to return home and caught A in bed with his wife. B pretended to be angry, and A knelt down and said, "If you don't severely punish me, I am willing to give you my wife in exchange for your wife, and we can live together, just as the ancients did!" B agreed, set a date, and let A go.

    447

    甲归告诸妇，妇不可。甲无以谢之，固强妇。时甲兄养疾于外，嫂不节，妇因献策曰：“请说嫂而代之。”乃诡言甲实他往，己苦畏，挽嫂共宿，故为媟语以荡之。伺其已寝，托如厕暂出，使甲以乙往，而己之嫂室宿焉。于是嫂竟与乙狎，甲亦趋乙家，寻其故欢。
    　　是夜甲兄忽归。甲妇已熟寝，其扉阖而弗遂。甲兄入，径登床，以为妻也，启衾而凭焉。既乱，乃知为弟妇，各秘之。
    　　其后甲乙隙于末，交相抉露，以为谤，事遂彰。
    　　非非子曰：以媸易妍，甲诚巧矣。以姒易娣，甲妻又巧矣。孰知冥冥中更有巧于易者哉！天道耶？鬼神之戏耶？吁，可畏也！
    　　石　先　生
    　　叶生者，文而绮者也。尝游楚，至襄汉之间，资匮不得返。不得已为卜者以市，间亦悬中。某氏神之，遂主于其家。主人故服贾，不常归。比邻类业樵牧者，非问卜，亦率不至。叶生既苦其旅而独也，短构微吟，以思以叹。

    Explanation

    447

    When "甲" returned and informed his wives, they refused. With no way to thank them, he persistently pressured his wives. At that time, "甲"’s brother was staying outside to care for an illness, and his sister-in-law was not virtuous. "甲"’s wife then suggested a plan: "Let me persuade the sister-in-law to take my place." She lied, saying "甲" had actually gone elsewhere, and claimed to be afraid, inviting the sister-in-law to stay with her. She used lewd talk to seduce her. When the sister-in-law was asleep, she pretended to go to the toilet and temporarily left. She sent "甲" to go to "乙"’s place while she stayed in her sister-in-law’s room. As a result, the sister-in-law ended up having an affair with "乙", and "甲" also went to "乙"’s house to resume their previous relationship.

    That night, "甲"’s brother suddenly returned. "甲"’s wife was already sound asleep, and the door was closed but not locked. "甲"’s brother entered, went straight to the bed, thinking it was his wife, and undressed. After realizing it was his sister-in-law, they both kept it secret.

    Later, "甲" and "乙" had a falling out, and they exposed each other, leading to scandal.

    "非非子" said: "By substituting ugliness for beauty, '甲' is indeed clever. By substituting the sister-in-law for the wife, '甲'’s wife is also clever. Who knows what more clever tricks are happening in the shadows? Is it the will of heaven? Or a game of the gods? Ah, it is truly frightening!"

    "石先生"

    "叶生" is a man who is both learned and eloquent. He once traveled to Chu, reaching the area between Xiang and Han, but due to lack of funds, he could not return. He had no choice but to become a fortune-teller to make a living, and sometimes he also did some divination. A certain family revered him, and he became the head of their household. The master of the house was a merchant and did not often come home. The neighbors were mostly engaged in cutting wood and herding, and they rarely came unless they were seeking a fortune. "叶生" felt the hardship of being a traveler and being alone, so he composed short poems and sighed deeply.

    448

    一夕，忽有排扉者，颜苍然而无髯，身儽然而短，正步端视，殊类有道。叶生起迓之，问其姓，曰：“石”。其居，曰：“邻。”且曰：“吾居此旧矣。闻子也才，故就子。”叶生谢焉，称曰：“石先生。”先生谢：“不敢。”揖之坐。再三，然后就宾位。与之言，颇根实，叶生窃喜，以为遭先生晚。将曙，先生辞去，送之出户，则固辞。请其庐而造焉，则又辞。且曰：“必若是，不复敢见矣。”叶生曰：“敬诺！”先生为反阖其扉，然后去。
    　　于是夜定辄来，来必让而坐，坐必肃。叶生容或不庄，误言或稍稍戏笑，辄不悦，然后知先生迂而固也。亦渐渐苦之，然始终敬之不敢懈。
    　　一夕对月赋小词遣意。先生来，遽索观之，未竟，艴然怒，抵词于地曰：“奈何为此耶？”遂起行。叶生惶骇，问其故，不答。追谢之，不顾。出户十余步，仆而灭。叶生惊，旦视其处，邻父系牛石在焉。

    Explanation

    448

    One night, there was a sudden knock at the door. The visitor had a pale complexion and no beard, a lean and short stature, walked straight and looked ahead, and appeared very much like a person of virtue. Le Sheng got up to greet him, asking his surname. The visitor replied, "Shi." When asked about his residence, he said, "Neighbor." He added, "I have lived here for a long time. I heard that you are talented, so I came to see you." Le Sheng thanked him and referred to him as "Mr. Shi." Mr. Shi responded, "I dare not." He invited Le Sheng to sit first, but after several times, he finally took the guest's seat. They spoke frankly, and Le Sheng secretly rejoiced, thinking he had met Mr. Shi too late. As dawn approached, Mr. Shi took his leave, and Le Sheng accompanied him out the door, but he firmly declined. Le Sheng asked to visit his home, but he again declined, saying, "If it must be so, I will no longer dare to see you." Le Sheng replied, "I respectfully agree!" Mr. Shi then closed the door behind him and left.

    From then on, he would come at night, and when he came, he would always let Le Sheng sit first. When sitting, he would always be serious. Le Sheng's appearance might not be solemn, and he might make mistakes or slight jokes, but Mr. Shi would always be displeased. Then Le Sheng knew that Mr. Shi was eccentric and stubborn. He also gradually found it difficult, but he always respected him and never slackened.

    One night, facing the moon, he composed a small poem to express his feelings. Mr. Shi came and quickly asked to see it. Before finishing, he suddenly became angry, threw the poem on the ground, and said, "Why did you do this?" Then he got up and left. Le Sheng was terrified and asked the reason, but he did not answer. Le Sheng tried to apologize to him, but he did not look back. After taking several steps out the door, he collapsed and disappeared. Le Sheng was shocked and in the morning, he looked at the place. There was a neighbor's father's ox-stone there.

    449

    疯　道　人
    　　有疯道人者，敝裘一袭，冬夏服之，忽哭忽笑。人问：“哭何悲？”曰：“无悲。”“笑何乐？”曰：“无所乐。”遇人辄拜，亦无所求也。语无伦次，如风雨之迷离，雷电之倏忽，往来齐赵间，人皆呼为疯道人。
    　　傅菊衣尝赴贵家宴，道人在焉，饮噉兼数人，杯盘俱为之罄。众颇鄙之，而菊衣独奇其量。他日，乃招道人饮，道人欣然来。菊衣为具豚肩羊胛各十簋，鸡凫这属称是，殽胾皆大脔，络绎竟日，至则尽之。酒亦无算爵，终不醉饱，及暮而止。菊衣问之曰：“道人日食几何？不常饥乎？”道人曰：“吾食亦不饱，不食亦不饥也。惟向在东海，罗氏姑遗酒二十斛，饮而甘之，飞斛三日，不觉径醉耳。尚有未尽者，来日当与君倾之。”遂别而去。

    Explanation

    449

    Mad Taoist Hermit

    There was a mad Taoist hermit who wore a tattered robe, which he wore in both winter and summer. He would suddenly cry and suddenly laugh. When people asked, "Why are you crying so sadly?" he replied, "There is nothing to be sad about." When asked, "Why are you laughing so joyfully?" he replied, "There is nothing to be happy about." He would bow to everyone he met, but he had no desires. His speech was incoherent, like the confusion of wind and rain, and the suddenness of lightning. He traveled between Qi and Zhao, and everyone called him the mad Taoist hermit.

    Fu Juyi once attended a feast at a noble's house, and the hermit was there. He ate and drank enough for several people, and the dishes and cups were all emptied. The guests generally looked down on him, but Juyi alone found his capacity remarkable. One day, he invited the hermit to drink, and the hermit came happily. Juyi prepared ten dishes of pork shoulder and lamb chops, and chickens and ducks were similarly prepared. All the meat dishes were large pieces, and the feast lasted throughout the day. When they arrived, they finished everything. The wine was also poured without limit, but the hermit never became drunk or full, and he stopped at dusk. Juyi asked, "How much does the hermit eat in a day? Does he not often feel hungry?" The hermit replied, "I do not eat until I am full, and I do not feel hungry if I do not eat." He continued, "Only recently, in the East Sea, Lady Luo gave me twenty dou of wine. I drank it and found it sweet, and I flew through three days of drinking, unconsciously becoming drunk. There is still some left, and I will pour it for you another day." Thus, he left.

    450

    次日亭午，道人始来，笑曰：“昨归逢故人，邀与共弈，竟忘宿约。棋罢，始忆之，真仓卒主人，可便行矣。”引菊衣至一废圃，坐空亭上，几榻之外，他无所有。菊衣意其诳，欲辞焉，未发也。顷之，见双鹭在霄。道人招之曰：“速来，客不耐矣！”鹭堕地，化为两童子，一捧壶，一执盏。道人酌客曰：“且润渴吻。”菊衣异而饮之，果佳酿也。既而珍馔殊品连翩而至，送觞者、行炙者、擘脯者、送果核者、具汤者，皆名姝妙选，供帐之盛，人间未有也。

    Explanation

    450

    The next day at noon, the Taoist priest finally arrived, smiling and saying, "Yesterday, I met an old friend and was invited to play chess with him, and I completely forgot our previous arrangement. After the game, I only remembered, truly a hasty host, you can leave now." He led Ji Yi to an abandoned garden, where they sat in an empty pavilion. Apart from a table and a couch, there was nothing else. Ji Yi thought he was being deceived and wanted to decline, but had not yet spoken. Soon, he saw two herons in the sky. The Taoist priest called out, "Come quickly, the guest is impatient!" The herons fell to the ground and transformed into two children, one holding a wine flask and the other a cup. The Taoist priest poured wine for the guest, saying, "Let's moisten your parched lips." Ji Yi, finding it strange, drank it and indeed it was fine wine. Soon after, exquisite and rare dishes arrived in quick succession, with attendants serving wine, hot food, meat, fruit and nuts, and soup, all of whom were beautiful and select, and the grandeur and splendor of the furnishings and decorations were such that they had never been seen on earth.

    451

    洎暮，菊衣起辞，道人挽留曰：“嘉宾既临，更当卜夜，但无烛奈何？”乃顾语双鬟最丽者往请明月来。须臾，双鬟反命曰：“来矣！”俄见白光起于东南，如玉山千仞。遥遥泛空。渐近，乃是一仙人，周身洞朗，蹑虚而至。仙风道骨，轩轩若霞举，而残醉未醒，衣中尚作酒气。女从数十人，皆具殊色。于时云气幕天，万星灭没，独圃中花草树石，尽在月明中。菊衣踧踖下拜。仙者亦抗礼入座，连引巨觥数十，舌本粲花，谈词英妙，间与道人论说，语多玄著不可解。夜将半，道人曰：“世无此乐千年矣，盍歌舞以尽欢乎？”仙者曰：“善。”一妓前席，捧玉盘，贮红筹数十，刻翠篆书各二字，有萦尘、集羽、双拂、合蝉、阳阿、结风、虚影、海眼、横影诸色目，盖舞筹也。使菊衣探之，得虚影，于是粲者数人，腾衣拂袂，飞翔空际。亭中丽影蹁跹，如锦水生波，轻云幻彩，觉一时风露苍凉，松竹动摇也。

    Explanation

    451

    By evening, Ju Yi rose to take his leave. The Daoist priest, seeing this, said, "Since our honored guest has arrived, we should stay up late to enjoy the night, but what if there is no candle?" He then turned and instructed the most beautiful of the girls with double buns to go and invite the moon. Soon, the girl returned and said, "The moon has come!" Suddenly, a brilliant white light appeared in the southeast, like a thousand-foot jade mountain. It floated far away in the sky, gradually approaching, and it turned out to be a divine being, whose entire body was luminous and who arrived on ethereal steps. The divine being had a graceful demeanor, as if rising like a rainbow, yet still appeared slightly intoxicated, with a faint scent of wine on his clothes. He was accompanied by dozens of women, all of extraordinary beauty. At that moment, clouds covered the sky, and all the stars had vanished, leaving only the flowers, grass, trees, and stones in the garden bathed in the moonlight. Ju Yi bowed humbly before the divine being. The divine being also greeted him with equal courtesy and took his seat, drinking repeatedly from large cups of wine. His words were eloquent and brilliant, and he engaged in profound and esoteric discussions with the Daoist priest, speaking in a way that was difficult to understand. By midnight, the Daoist priest said, "There has been no such pleasure in a thousand years; why don't we dance and sing to enjoy ourselves?" The divine being replied, "Very well." One of the entertainers stepped forward, holding a jade tray with several dozen red sticks, each inscribed with two characters in green seal script, representing various dance routines such as 'Winding Dust,' 'Gathering Feathers,' 'Double Wipe,' 'Combined Cicadas,' 'Yang Elegance,' 'Binding Wind,' 'Illusory Shadow,' 'Sea Eye,' and 'Horizontal Shadow.' These were the dance markers. The Daoist priest asked Ju Yi to choose one, and he selected 'Illusory Shadow.' Then, several of the most graceful women lifted their clothes and swept their sleeves, flying through the air. In the pavilion, their beautiful shadows danced gracefully, like waves rippling on a brocade surface, and the light and color shifted like fleeting clouds. Suddenly, a chill in the air was felt, and the pines and bamboos seemed to tremble.

    452

    仙者曰：“舞妙矣！谁为歌者？”一妓应命发声，歌曰：“春风东来忽相过，金樽缘酒生微波。落花纷纷稍觉多，美人欲醉朱颜酡。青轩桃李能几何？流光欺人忽蹉跎。”玉簌珠含，颇极悠扬纤婉之致。一妓继歌曰：“白兔捣药秋复春，嫦娥孤栖与谁邻？右人不见今时月，今月曾经照古人。”仙者笑曰：“误矣误矣！乃今人不见古时月也。”妓曰：“今人不见古时月，古人亦谁见今时月哉？”
    　　仙者叹息，因举杯属菊衣曰：“公听此语，犹不痛饮乎？”菊衣故不胜酒，为浮一大白。仙者拔侍者佩剑，起舞亭中，已而倚剑郎吟，顾侍女擘笺书之，以示菊衣。菊衣读而识之，诗曰：
    　　海风荡八表，云气低漫漫。

    Explanation

    452

    The immortal said, "The dance is exquisite! Who will sing?" One of the courtesans responded to the command and began singing, her voice like this: "The spring wind comes from the east, suddenly passing by; the golden cup, due to the wine, produces slight ripples. The falling flowers are numerous, slightly noticeable; the beautiful lady, about to be drunk, her rosy cheeks flush. How long can the peach and plum trees in the green pavilion last? The fleeting light deceives people, suddenly causing them to waste time." The jade flute and pearl strings played, extremely graceful and delicate. Another courtesan continued singing, her voice like this: "The white rabbit pounds medicine, from autumn to spring; Chang'e, the moon goddess, lives alone, who is her neighbor? People today do not see the moon of ancient times, but neither do ancient people see the moon of our time." The immortal laughed and said, "Mistaken, mistaken! It is that people today do not see the moon of ancient times." The courtesan replied, "People today do not see the moon of ancient times, but neither do ancient people see the moon of our time?"

    The immortal sighed and then raised his cup, saying to Ju Yi, "Sir, hearing these words, why not drink heartily?" Ju Yi, who is not good at drinking, drank a large cup of wine. The immortal drew the sword from the attendant's belt, danced in the pavilion, and then leaned on the sword and recited poetry. He looked at the attendant girl, who unfolded a scroll and wrote it, to show to Ju Yi. Ju Yi read and recognized it, the poem saying: "The sea wind sweeps the eight directions, the cloud mist is low and diffuse."

    453

    仰首睇飞鸿，宇宙何其宽。
    　　磨剑蓬莱顶，芙蓉开紫澜。
    　　侠累何足仇，壮气鸣心肝。
    　　谈笑杀两蛟，翻身跨孤鸾。
    　　道逢赤松子，饮我琼浆寒。
    　　一醉五百年，仍卧三神山。
    　　当时相识人，轮转沙尘间。
    　　十万紫宫女，大半非朱颜。
    　　双泪不可涸，下救溟渤者。
    　　却听云和笙，还求神鼎丹。
    　　朗然化片月，流光照人寰。
    　　仙者吟竟，复引十余觥，大醉辞去。夜复黑，道人更燃松节继之，谓菊衣曰：“此李青莲先生也。”菊衣讶曰：“顷言明月者非欤？”道人曰：“子未识乎？月者才人之化身，匝月而一代。盈亏出没，其气数然也。然古今以来，不过数人，循环相照，今适是青莲。吾与有旧，故延之来耳。”菊衣曰：“世传先生为长庚，又言为东华上清监清逸真人，又言掌笺奏于嵩山，今又为明月，不亦岐乎？”道人曰：“神仙星月，初无定位。不足疑也。”言次，东方白，菊衣乃归。道人自是不复见。

    Explanation

    453

    Gazing upward at the flying geese, the universe is so vast.
    Sharpening a sword at the peak of Mount Penglai, lotus flowers bloom in purple waves.
    Chivalrous deeds are not worth hating; the spirit of bravery resonates in the heart and liver.
    To kill two dragons with a smile, to turn over and ride a solitary phoenix.
    Meeting the Red Pine Immortal, drinking my cold ambrosia.
    To be drunk for five hundred years, still lying in the Three Sacred Mountains.
    Those who were once acquainted now cycle through the dust.
    Ten thousand Purple Palace maidens, most of whom are no longer young.
    Tears that cannot dry, to save the vast seas.
    To listen to the cloud and reed pipes, and to seek the divine elixir.
    To transform into a bright moon, its light shining on the human world.
    The immortal finishes reciting, then invites more cups, gets drunk, and departs. The night becomes dark again, and the hermit lights more pine resin to continue, saying to Mr. Chrysanthemum, "This is Mr. Li Qinglian." Mr. Chrysanthemum was surprised and said, "Wasn't it just said that it was the moon?" The hermit replied, "Do you not know? The moon is the incarnation of talented people, changing every month. Its waxing and waning, its appearances and disappearances, are due to its fate. Since ancient times, there have been only a few, cycling and illuminating each other. Now it is Li Qinglian. I have known him for a long time, so I invited him here." Mr. Chrysanthemum said, "It is said that the master is the Morning Star, and also the Immortal of Purity and Tranquility of the Eastern Palace, and also the one who writes documents in Songshan. Now he is the moon, isn't that contradictory?" The hermit said, "Gods, stars, and the moon have no fixed positions. There is no need to doubt." As he spoke, the east turned white, and Mr. Chrysanthemum returned home. The hermit was not seen again.

    454

    恶蝇
    　　昔有恶蝇者，日操砧杵击蝇。蝇集父者，大怒，槌之，父脑裂死，而蝇飞去。有司以弑父论，置极典。呜乎！彼非不爱其父也？恶而妨爱，君子愚之。
    　　宋　先　生
    　　江宁藩署，徐中山王故第也。署后一楼，扃钥甚固，莫敢登者。
    　　乾隆十五年，德方伯者在官，乘醉登之。凝尘尺许，而几榻甚净，略无纤埃。德异而退于户下，拾一纸巾箱，小方寸，内贮一冠，类梨园幞头，携下楼，置诸案间，。是夜梦中山王至。白皙长髯，金冠蟒玉，怒之曰：“宾客过我，以汝故解散。宋先生遗其冠。汝拾之，宜亟还之。否且祸汝！”言区即去。德遽觉。
    　　黎明出厅事，且谒长吏。将升舆，见一青衣前启曰：“奉主君命前来取帽。”德悟，急令取巾箱与之。青衣忽不见。德深骇异，意宋先生者，必景濂也。旋具牲醴，祭而谢焉。遂扃楼如初，莫敢登者。亦无他害。

    Explanation

    454

    The Evil Fly
    In ancient times, there was a man who daily wielded a pestle to hit flies. When the flies gathered around his father, he became greatly angry and struck them, causing his father's skull to crack and die, while the flies flew away. The authorities considered this act as patricide and imposed the harshest punishment. Alas! Was it that he did not love his father? Evil obstructs love, and the gentleman regards it as foolish.

    Mr. Song
    The Jiangning provincial office was the former residence of the Prince of Zhongshan. Behind the office stood a building with a very secure lock, no one dared to ascend it.

    In the fifteenth year of the Qianlong era, a magistrate named De was in office and, while drunk, climbed it. The dust settled about a foot thick, yet the tables and beds were very clean, with slightly no fine dust. De was surprised and withdrew to the door, where he picked up a small paper box, about a square inch in size, which contained a hat, resembling a hat of the Pear Garden. He carried it down the stairs and placed it on the table. That night, he dreamed that the Prince of Zhongshan came to him. The Prince had white skin and a long beard, wearing a golden crown and python jade. He angrily said, "Guests passed by me, and because of you, they dispersed. Mr. Song left his hat. You picked it up; you should quickly return it. Otherwise, I will bring harm to you!" He then departed. De woke up abruptly.

    At dawn, he left the hall and paid respects to the senior officials. As he was about to mount his carriage, he saw a blue-clad servant approach and say, "I come to fetch the hat on the master's command." De understood and urgently ordered to fetch the box and give it to him. The blue-clad servant suddenly disappeared. De was greatly terrified and amazed, thinking that Mr. Song must be Jinglian. He quickly prepared sacrifices and liquor, sacrificed and thanked. Then he locked the building as before, no one dared to ascend it, and there was no other harm.

    455

    金陵樵者
    　　靖安舒四长，好豢勇，阅数师矣，顾自谓弗善。去之金陵，登某甲之门。甲勇闻通国，生徒甚众。居数年，略尽其技。
    　　一日，师徒游大市，遇樵者负薪疾过，误裂甲衣。樵惶恐俯躬谢。甲马掴其面。樵愠曰：“误而谢焉，亦足矣，何遽掴我？”甲以己素力掴人，无不仆者，樵乃不仆，且抗言，愈怒，遂拳之。手未及樵，甲反仆。其徒皆骇，相顾莫敢近。市人无不笑者。樵责让数言，徐徐负薪去。
    　　舒异之，潜尾出城数里，得荒村茅屋一区，樵者入焉。舒拜于门外，求为弟子。樵反顾，讶曰：“子何为者？”舒曰：“公适所仆者，吾师也。知公神勇，故舍而从公，请卒为第子！”樵辞以无能，径入不出。舒徘徊门外。久之，询诸其邻：“樵者何人也？”邻人曰：“是尝徙此，莫知其姓名。有母焉，老矣。日给于樵，甚孝也。”舒遂归。

    Explanation

    455

    The Woodcutter of Jinling

    Shu, from Jing'an, was fond of cultivating bravery and had studied under several teachers, but he considered himself not good enough. He went to Jinling and entered the gate of a certain Mr. Jia, whose bravery was known throughout the country and who had many students. After several years, Shu had mastered most of his skills.

    One day, while Mr. Jia and his students were wandering in the great market, they met a woodcutter carrying firewood who hurriedly passed by, accidentally tearing Mr. Jia's clothes. The woodcutter was terrified and bowed to apologize. Mr. Jia slapped his face. The woodcutter angrily said, "Apologizing for the mistake is enough; why slap me so quickly?" Mr. Jia usually slaps people and they all fall down, but the woodcutter did not fall and spoke boldly, becoming even angrier and punching him. His hand did not reach the woodcutter, and Mr. Jia fell instead. His students were all shocked and looked at each other, daring not to approach. No one in the market did not laugh. The woodcutter scolded him with a few words and slowly carried his firewood and left.

    Shu found it strange and secretly followed him for several miles, finding a dilapidated village with a thatched cottage. The woodcutter entered it. Shu bowed at the door and asked to become his disciple. The woodcutter turned back and said in surprise, "Who are you?" Shu said, "The one you just knocked down is my teacher. Knowing you are divine and brave, I left my teacher to follow you. I ask to become your disciple!" The woodcutter refused, saying he is not capable, and went straight in and did not come out. Shu lingered outside the door. After a long time, he asked the neighbors, "Who is the woodcutter?" They said, "He has moved here before, no one knows his name. He has an old mother. He provides for her through woodcutting, and he is very filial." Shu then returned.

    456

    旦日复往。伺樵既出，登堂拜其母，出百金为寿。母亦诧不肯纳。舒具陈己意，欲母语樵。使卒为弟子。母许之。
    　　樵归，得母命。且感其意，谓舒曰：“苟有薄长，敢不以相授，然请兄我。毋师我。”舒从之。樵引至屋后，有石坡甚峻，轨辙如绳，下有硙，重三四百斤，使舒掇之，仅能举。樵以足蹴硙辊而上及于坡顶，　轱辘而下。又蹴之，如是十数，无困色，曰：“筋力久弛，聊以此当运甓耳。”饮舒以药，使习之，久而能焉。遂教以炼形摄气之法，周身如铁，巨梃扑之，皆反跃。以腹贴墙壁及屋梁，能行而不坠。
    　　积十余年，乃辞归，卖浆豫章城。遇人谦谨，若无能者。或言盖无敌矣。闻者多不信。群不逞诣之，请与角。舒谢曰：“诸公皆壮士，余何能？余何能？”请不已，乃曰：“虽尝学之，然甚劣，窃欲观诸公技勇，使习而进焉。幸甚！”众许之。

    Explanation

    456

    The next day, Shu returned. After waiting for the woodcutter to leave, he entered the hall and paid respects to his mother, presenting a hundred gold coins as a gift. The mother was surprised and refused to accept it. Shu fully explained his intentions, asking her to speak to the woodcutter. Eventually, the woodcutter agreed to become his disciple. The mother consented.

    The woodcutter returned home, receiving his mother's command. He was also moved by Shu's sincerity and said to Shu, "If I have any slight skill, I dare not withhold it from you. However, please treat me as a brother rather than a teacher." Shu agreed. The woodcutter led him to the back of the house, where there was a very steep stone slope with tracks as straight as a rope. At the bottom was a grinding stone weighing three or four hundred pounds. The woodcutter asked Shu to lift it, but he could barely manage it. The woodcutter then kicked the grinding stone up the slope with his foot, rolling it to the top. He then rolled it down again. He repeated this process ten or more times without showing any signs of fatigue, saying, "My muscles and strength have been relaxed for a long time, so I just use this as a substitute for carrying bricks." He gave Shu medicine to drink and instructed him to practice. After a long time, Shu was able to do it. The woodcutter then taught him techniques for refining the body and controlling breath, making his entire body as hard as iron. When struck with a large stick, it would bounce back. He could walk along walls and beams without falling.

    After more than ten years, Shu finally returned home, selling soy milk in Nanchang. He met people with humility and seemed incapable. Some said he was invincible, but many did not believe it. A group of unruly people came to him, challenging him to a contest. Shu replied, "All of you are strong men, what can I do? What can I do?" They kept asking, and he finally said, "Even though I have studied it, I am very poor at it. I secretly wish to see the skills and bravery of the gentlemen, so I can practice and improve. How fortunate!" Everyone agreed.

    457

    相与之野外，各呈其能。舒观而哂曰：“甚善！”从欲试舒。舒曰：“若欲试我者，则殴我。”一少年应声殴之，甫引拳，忽反仆。少年羞怒，出铁杵，悉力击之。舒挟持其杵，作色曰：“太恶剧，是欲死我乎？”乃弛衣服裸而立，曰：“来来，共攻我，我不畏！”于是手足器械，交至如雨，舒屹然受之，众纷纷堕跌。黠者乘虚击其肾，如击石焉。众始惧，罗拜请长其曹，乞勿扬于人，以败其誉。舒笑曰：“吾以自娱耳，岂欲与诸公竞短长哉？幸无虑此！”众益服其量。
    　　由是舒名噪一城。接见宾友，或反臂握手，当者则痛，器具入手，往往破碎。其力如此。今死矣。死时，遗紫血数斗，甚惨楚，药故也。尝曰：“吾能气行耳，樵乃能神行，不可及也！”樵盖秦人，尝为盗，已乃改行，变姓名，遁居金陵，奉母以终云。
    　　耳食录二编卷七

    Explanation

    457

    Together they went into the wild, each displaying their abilities. Shu observed and smiled, saying, "Very good!" One wanted to test Shu. Shu said, "If you want to test me, then strike me." A young man responded by striking him. Just as he was about to strike, he suddenly fell over. The young man, feeling ashamed and angry, pulled out an iron pestle and struck him with all his might. Shu grasped the pestle and said with a stern expression, "Too cruel, are you trying to kill me?" Then he loosened his clothes and stood naked, saying, "Come on, attack me, I'm not afraid!" Thus, hands, feet, weapons, all came down like rain, and Shu stood firm and endured it. The crowd fell over one after another. A clever one took advantage of the opening and struck his kidneys, like striking a stone. The crowd began to fear, knelt in a circle, and asked to be led by Shu, asking not to publicize it, so as to ruin his reputation. Shu smiled and said, "I did it for my own amusement, do I want to compete with all of you in terms of length or ability? Please don't worry about it!" The crowd admired his composure even more.

    Thus, Shu's fame spread throughout the city. When he met guests and friends, sometimes turning his arm and shaking hands, those who touched him would feel pain, and tools in his hands often broke. His strength was like that. Now he has died. At the time of his death, he left several dou of purple blood, very painful, due to the medicine. He once said, "I can only move by qi, but Qiao can move by spirit, which is beyond my reach!" Qiao, a person from the state of Qin, used to be a bandit, but later changed his ways, changed his name, and lived in seclusion in Jinling, serving his mother until his death.

    From the second compilation of "Ear-Feeding Records," volume seven.

    458

    龙某
    　　举人龙某，谒选都下，住樱桃斜衔。一夕，被酒卧，苦渴求浆。起呼僮，僮已熟睡。及门，见一女背阶，亭亭立月下。甚讶之，伏窗而觑。女闻人声，乃四顾，徐度西角门而去。觉罗袂弓鞋，形影俱丽，凝想忽忽，复就睡。觉而疑焉，以为醉也，殆梦也，然忆揣不置者累日，殊杳然。戏题一绝句焚之曰：
    　　两瓣莲花踏影行，全身都是可怜生。
    　　巫阳神女多情甚，偷到人家看月明。
    　　是夜既寝，有啄其门者，龙起延之，女子也。曰：“余为黄氏婢，特来召君。”龙愕然问故，婢愠曰：“小姑前夜偶来此看月，初不敢相闻，亦素不解吟诗是何生活。今大姑拾得一纸，读其前二十八字，云是一情诗。验其款识，乃君为小姑看月而作，白之县君，县君怒，诃诘小姑，疑有他涉。小姑涕泣诉其诬，意稍霁。君既以飞语陷人，宜往承之，且明月岂君家私物？辄云小姑‘偷看’耶？”龙惶恐谢过。婢牵其裾曳之，曰：“去去！”不觉随之行。

    Explanation

    458

    Mr. Long
    Mr. Long, a juren (a person who has passed the provincial examination), was staying in the capital, in a place called Cherry Slope Lane. One night, he was drunk and felt thirsty, so he called for his servant. However, the servant was already asleep. When he opened the door, he saw a woman standing by the steps under the moonlight. He was surprised and peered through the window. The woman heard the noise and looked around, then slowly passed through the western corner gate and left. He noticed her silk sleeve and bow-shaped shoes, and her graceful figure and shadow were both beautiful. He was lost in thought and then returned to sleep. The next day, he was puzzled, wondering if he had been dreaming, but he couldn't stop thinking about it for several days. He wrote a poem and burned it, saying:
    Two lotus petals tread the shadow, a figure of pity.
    The Wuyang goddess is very affectionate, stealing into someone's home to watch the moon.
    That night, after he had gone to sleep, there was a knock on his door. He got up to open it, and it was a woman. She said, "I am a servant of the Huang family, and I have come to summon you." Mr. Long was startled and asked why. The woman became angry and said, "My young mistress came here to watch the moon last night. I didn't dare to disturb her, and I didn't understand why she was writing poetry. Today, the elder mistress found a piece of paper and read the first twenty-eight characters, which turned out to be a love poem. When she saw your name on it, she was furious and scolded my young mistress, suspecting that she had done something improper. My young mistress cried and told her that it was a misunderstanding, and the elder mistress's anger subsided. You have spread false rumors to陷害 someone, so you should face the consequences. Besides, the moon is not your private property; why did you say that my young mistress 'stole' it?" Mr. Long was frightened and apologized. The woman pulled his sleeve and dragged him, saying, "Go, go!" Unconsciously, he followed her.

    459

    出一门，迥非熟径，历三四曲巷，乃达一第宅。始而闳敞，继而幽窔。处处有灯烛。至垂幕之下，婢止之，先入，俄传县君见客。龙踧踖进谒。一妇人上坐，年可四五十，辞色俱厉，曰：“何物狂且，造词媟冶，谤人家闺阁，不畏拔舌耶？”龙震慑流汗，长揖对曰：“某何敢然，特醉后戏笔，旋焚弃之矣，安得尘县君之目？”妇人曰：“汝诗吾所亲见，奈何遁饰？”龙曰：“才人绮语，类皆寄托耳！聘花媒月，何所不有？县君亦恶乎考之，乃欲以影响谈说，文致罪名，斯为冤矣！”妇人怒曰：“尚敢舌强，诸妮子为我挞之！”龙乃趋出，涉内霤，蹉跌，诸婢媪操鞭梃踵至。方恐迫间，前婢驰出，挥众曰：“止止，毋得动！县君宥之矣。命吾引还，命尔曹各去。”众诺而退。

    Explanation

    459

    Upon exiting the gate, he found himself on a path far from familiar, passing through several winding alleys before reaching a residence. Initially, the place was spacious and open, but it soon became dark and narrow. Everywhere, there were lights. He arrived at a spot under a hanging curtain, where a maid stopped him and went in first. Soon after, it was conveyed that Lady County would receive the guest. Dragon bowed deeply and entered to pay respects. A woman sat on the upper seat, around forty or fifty years old, with both her words and expression stern. She said, "What kind of madman are you, composing lewd and indecent words, defaming the family's inner chambers, do you not fear having your tongue pulled out?" Dragon was shocked and sweating, bowing deeply and replying, "How dare I do such a thing? I just wrote it as a joke after drinking, burned and discarded it immediately. How could it defile Lady County's eyes?" The woman said, "Your poem I have seen personally, why try to cover it up?" Dragon replied, "The words of talented people are often beautiful and ornate, they are all just expressions of feelings! Using flowers and the moon as intermediaries, what cannot be found? How can you verify it, Lady County? You want to fabricate a crime based on hearsay, this is a great injustice!" The woman became angry and said, "Still dare to speak defiantly, the maids will beat him for me!" Dragon then hurried out, stepping into the inner corridor, tripping and falling. Several maids and elderly women wielding sticks followed closely. Just as he was afraid of being caught, the maid who had stopped him earlier ran out, waving to the crowd and saying, "Stop, stop, do not move! Lady County has forgiven him. Order me to lead him back, order you all to leave." The crowd nodded and left.

    460

    婢导至小阁，悄语曰：“君之免，大姑力也。”龙称谢，婢曰：“未已。大姑言君既好吟诗，召此愆辱，宜更作数章，如佳，乃释尔。”龙曰：“幸不深罪，此何足辞？请给纸笔，并将韵本来。”婢去有顷，携文具至，设几上，复于袖中出韵本，笑曰：“大姑言此诗翁护身符箓，故万不可少。姑以此为题，试作一诗。”龙视韵本，牙签绣帕，装璜精丽，而脂香粉印，清溢行间，知为闺中习览物。意甚得，成五言律诗一首。婢持稿去，旋来曰：“大姑言，君讥议声韵，殊未允当，且诗语粘滞，不类才人吐属，须更作之。”龙不得已，伏几苦吟。

    Explanation

    460

    The servant led him to a small study and whispered, "Your escape was due to the efforts of the elder sister-in-law." He thanked her, and she said, "Not yet. The elder sister-in-law says that since you enjoy composing poetry, to atone for this disgrace, you should compose several poems. If they are good, then you will be released." He replied, "I am fortunate not to be severely punished; how could I refuse? Please provide me with paper, ink, and the rhyming book." After a short while, the servant returned with the writing materials, set them on the table, and took out the rhyming book from her sleeve, smiling, "The elder sister-in-law said this poem is like an amulet for the poet, so it cannot be missing. She has chosen this as the topic, and I invite you to compose one poem." He looked at the rhyming book, which was beautifully bound with ivory bookmarks and embroidered covers, and saw that it was filled with the scent of perfume and the marks of frequent use, knowing it was something the lady of the house often read. Feeling quite pleased, he composed a five-character regulated verse. The servant took the manuscript and soon returned, saying, "The elder sister-in-law said that your criticism of the sound and rhythm is far from appropriate, and the language is stiff, unlike what a talented person would write. You must compose another one." He had no choice but to bend over the table and struggle to compose the poem.

    461

    婢立于案头，拉杂漫语，亦频频流睇送情。龙思虑搅乱，不复能构思，因问婢：“汝宅中几人？”曰：“主翁亡矣，独县君携两女，并仆婢辈居此耳。”龙曰：“汝侍大姑乎？小姑乎？”曰：“侍大姑。”曰：“大姑何名，年齿几何矣？”婢笑曰：“汝亦作诗耳，奚用知此？此岂诗料耶？”龙亦笑，佯俯首作属思状，甫蘸笔，婢又催促之，龙因投笑长跪曰：“汝夺吾魂魄，虽李杜当此，不能更道只字矣，幸怜而拯我！”遂侵之。婢面赤，不甚拒，因成欢好。乃窃语龙曰：“吾视大姑，如春风飘荡，其情之隐跃久矣，今留君索诗，意岂为诗哉？君会心人，何不解此？”龙喜曰：“奈何？”婢曰：“但尾我行，保有良会。”随入复室中。

    Explanation

    461

    The maid stood by the desk, speaking in a disjointed and rambling manner, frequently casting flirtatious glances. The protagonist's mind was disturbed, and he could no longer concentrate on his thoughts. He asked the maid, "How many people live in your house?" She replied, "Our master has passed away. Only the noblewoman and her two daughters, along with the servants, live here." The protagonist asked, "Do you serve the elder sister or the younger sister?" She answered, "I serve the elder sister." He then asked, "What is the elder sister's name, and how old is she?" The maid laughed and said, "You're writing poetry too, why do you need to know this? Is this the subject of your poem?" The protagonist also laughed, pretending to bow his head in deep thought. Just as he dipped his brush, the maid urged him again. The protagonist then laughed and knelt before her, saying, "You've taken my soul; even Li Bai and Du Fu, when faced with this, could not write another word. Please pity me and save me!" And so, they engaged in intimacy. The maid blushed slightly but did not strongly resist, and they enjoyed a pleasant encounter. She then whispered to the protagonist, "I see the elder sister as if she were a gentle spring breeze, her feelings hidden and fluctuating for a long time. Now she has kept you here to write poetry—was it really for that? You are a person who understands the situation, why can't you see this?" The protagonist was pleased and asked, "What should I do?" The maid replied, "Just follow me, and we will have a good opportunity." She then led him into a private room.

    462

    乃见大姑者，坐镜奁之旁。貌颇妖冶，脉脉有思。婢乃伏龙于案下，前语曰：“彼人诗殊不能就，可若何？”大姑始觉，徐答曰：“姑纵之。”婢曰：“中门管钥，县君自掌之。天且曙矣，可若何？”曰：“姑留之。”婢曰：“婢子不敢宿客，已携至，须阿姑处分。”径趋出，反阖其扉。龙出而迫之，大姑惊曰：“贱婢乃陷我！”龙遽抚之，竟合焉。潜蓄之室中，每夕专对，婢欲图一私觌，不可得。

    Explanation

    462

    Upon seeing the elder sister-in-law, she was seated beside a mirror case. Her appearance was quite alluring, with a tender and lingering gaze. The maid lay down the man under the table and spoke up, saying, "That person's poem is especially incomplete; what should be done?" The elder sister-in-law only then noticed and calmly replied, "Let him go." The maid said, "The keys to the main gate are held by Lady County. It is almost dawn; what should be done?" The elder sister-in-law replied, "Keep him." The maid said, "I dare not accommodate guests overnight, but he has already been brought here; I need Lady Aunt's decision." She quickly went out, closing the door behind her. The man came out and approached her, and the elder sister-in-law was startled and said, "You wretched maid, you have trapped me!" The man quickly comforted her, and they finally united. He secretly harbored her in the room, and every night they conversed exclusively. The maid wanted to plan a private meeting, but she could not achieve it.

    463

    经数日，小姑出于庭，龙隙窗窥之，光彩妙丽，乃无伦比，丐大姑欲得一当，大姑咋舌摇手曰：“咄咄，此贞而烈者，何可犯也，且彼以前日之故，有憾于我，旦夕伺吾短，今授之以隙，必为所持，此间无侧足地矣！”龙意沮，然不能绝念，时时称羡其美，大姑不怿曰：“男子薄心肠，得陇复望蜀也。君既慕之，请自从之。”乃使婢引出。龙傍徨求解于婢，婢哂曰：“推贤让能，风徽远矣。君以古道遇今人，宜其龃龉。猜嫌既启，虽吾亦不复能谋。”龙曰：彼诚逐客。君亦不容耶？”婢曰：“吾虽鄙陋，不能为人容逐客。”再三迫之去。龙把其袖，贪赖不肯行，由是复与婢接。婢匿之积麦之囷。

    463

    After several days, the younger sister-in-law emerged from the courtyard. Long peered through a small window and saw her, her appearance splendid and beautiful, unmatched in her grace. He asked the elder sister-in-law if he could have a chance to see her, but she was speechless and shook her head, saying, "Oh, oh, she is a chaste and resolute woman; how could one dare to offend her? And because of something that happened before, she has a grudge against me, waiting for my faults day and night. If I give her an opportunity now, she will surely take advantage of it. There is no place for me here anymore!" Long's spirit was dampened, but he could not completely give up hope. He often praised her beauty. The elder sister-in-law spoke with displeasure, "Men have shallow hearts, having achieved one goal, one desires another. Since you admire her, please go to her yourself." She then had a maid lead her out. Long hesitated and asked the maid for help. The maid smiled and said, "To recommend the virtuous and yield to the capable, the influence is far-reaching. You meet modern people with ancient ways, which is why there is friction. Once suspicion arises, even I cannot help anymore." Long said, "She is indeed a guest who should be sent away. Can you not tolerate her either?" The maid replied, "Though I am humble and unimportant, I cannot tolerate being a guest who is sent away." She persistently urged her to leave. Long held her sleeve, reluctant to let her go, thus continuing to interact with the maid. The maid hid her in a granary filled with grain.

    464

    数日，大姑悔，思龙欲复见。问婢，婢对曰：“彼既去，谁能往召？且彼实怨怒阿姑，即召不复来。”明日大姑又言之，婢对如前。大姑乃怨婢，常假他故挫辱婢。婢以是亦怨大姑，乃诣小姑，语前事。阳为大姑谢小姑，实欲挑小姑怒，以倾大姑也。小姑性故和柔，殊不以介意。婢叹曰：“姑德惠若此，使人不忍复欺！”乃窃发大姑之覆，而自隐其私，且曰：“大姑禁我不得言，吾惧获罪，乃不敢不言。”小姑大惊曰：“姊素谈节义，不意反自越礼，为门户羞辱。脱县君知之，当奈何？”婢请白县君，小姑勿许曰：“吾冰清玉洁，岂乐与闻此事者？”婢请之至再，小姑察其奸，怒曰：“大姑，汝主也，何得无情？略不相护耶？且汝实左右大姑，即何事弗与？诱盗而保奸，皆汝之由。果白县君，当先治汝矣！”婢始惧，转求秘之。小姑曰：“欲吾庇汝，须亟绝私人之路，不然，不汝隐也。”

    Explanation

    464

    After a few days, the elder sister-in-law regretted her actions and wished to see her husband again. She asked the servant girl, who replied, "He has already left, who could go and call him back? Besides, he is truly resentful and angry with you, and if you call him, he will not come back." The next day, the elder sister-in-law asked again, and the servant girl responded as before. The elder sister-in-law then became resentful of the servant girl and often pretended to have other reasons to humiliate her. The servant girl, for this reason, also resented the elder sister-in-law and went to the younger sister-in-law to tell her about the previous events. She pretended to apologize on behalf of the elder sister-in-law to the younger sister-in-law, but in reality, she wanted to stir up the younger sister-in-law's anger in order to overthrow the elder sister-in-law. The younger sister-in-law, by nature gentle and mild, did not mind the matter at all. The servant girl sighed and said, "The younger sister-in-law has such virtue and kindness, making it impossible for me to deceive her again!" She then secretly revealed the elder sister-in-law's wrongdoing while concealing her own secret, and said, "The elder sister-in-law forbids me from speaking, and I fear punishment, so I dare not remain silent." The younger sister-in-law was greatly surprised and said, "My elder sister has always talked about virtue and righteousness, but I did not expect her to violate etiquette and bring shame to our family. If the county magistrate finds out, what shall we do?" The servant girl asked to report to the county magistrate, but the younger sister-in-law refused, saying, "I am as pure as ice and jade, how could I enjoy hearing about such matters?" The servant girl asked again, and the younger sister-in-law discovered her deception, becoming angry and saying, "The elder sister-in-law is your master, how can you be indifferent? Do you not protect each other at all? Besides, you actually assist the elder sister-in-law, so what matters do you not participate in? Luring thieves and protecting deceivers, all are your fault. If you report to the county magistrate, I will punish you first!" The servant girl then began to fear and sought to keep the matter secret. The younger sister-in-law said, "If you want me to protect you, you must quickly sever your private connections, otherwise, I will not conceal it for you."

    465

    婢恐，以语龙，求策之。龙亦恐，求去。婢不得已，导出门，从墙角绕行数十武，倏至寓室。婢仓皇弛去。次日寻之，不复识路。

    Explanation

    465

    The servant was frightened and told the dragon, seeking advice from him. The dragon was also frightened and sought to leave. The servant had no choice but to guide the dragon out, leading him around the corner for dozens of steps, and suddenly they arrived at the guest room. The servant hurriedly ran away. The next day when searching for it, they could not recognize the path again.

    466

    徐元直
