    　　康熙十三年，平凉总兵王辅臣叛逆，大将军图海征之。有兵丁于英，途遇风雷，与大军相失。晚窜山谷间，策骑盘旋，四向无路，约夜半，度不得出，遂下马倚树，息以待旦。俄见红灯冉冉来，既近视之，乃一叟，须眉如画，衣冠古野，不类今世。谓于曰：“若迷道乎？”于曰：“然，唯丈人指南之。”叟曰：“此山荒僻，虎狼纵横，去大路尚五六十里。速来，吾导汝。”遂前行。于乘马从之。经乱峰丛箐之间，叟运履如飞，马几不能及，久之平旷，叟乃息足。以灯赠于曰：“坦途不远矣。”于视其灯，非纱非纸亦非膏烛，而表里荧彻如琉璃，而红而圆。心异之，请共姓名，叟曰：“吾三国时徐庶也。”于惊骇，方欲拜谢，叟已失。于独行数里，果遵大道，东方白矣，灯亦熄。审视则红杏耳。大如碗。趋至大军，备述其异，咸以严冬气候，不宜有杏而且大，信其果遇元直也。

    Explanation

    466

    Xu Yuanzhi

    In the thirteenth year of the Kangxi era, General Wang Fuchen of Pingliang rebelled against the Qing dynasty, and General Tuhai was dispatched to suppress the rebellion. A soldier named Yu Ying, while traveling, encountered a fierce wind and thunder, becoming separated from the main army. As night fell, he fled into the mountains and valleys, riding his horse in circles, finding no way out in any direction. By midnight, he judged that he could not escape and dismounted, leaning against a tree, resting until dawn. Suddenly, a red light appeared, moving slowly and gracefully. As he approached, he saw an elderly man with a painted beard and hair, wearing ancient and rustic clothing, unlike anyone of the present world. The old man said to Yu, "Are you lost?" Yu replied, "Yes, please guide me." The old man said, "This mountain is remote and dangerous, with tigers and wolves roaming freely, and it is still fifty to sixty li from the main road. Hurry, I will lead you." They then moved forward. Yu rode his horse following the old man. They passed through a chaotic landscape of jagged peaks and dense forests, and the old man moved swiftly, almost too fast for the horse to keep up. After a while, they reached a flat and open area, and the old man stopped. He gave Yu a lamp and said, "The straight path is not far." Yu looked at the lamp, which was neither made of silk nor paper, nor of oil or candle, but was transparent and luminous like a piece of glass, red and round. He was surprised and asked for the old man's name. The old man said, "I am Xu Shu from the Three Kingdoms period." Yu was shocked and was about to bow and thank him, but the old man had already vanished. Yu continued alone for several miles, indeed following the main road, and by morning, the light had extinguished. Upon closer inspection, it was a red apricot, as large as a bowl. He rushed to the main army and fully described the strange event. All believed that it was truly Xu Yuanzhi, as it was highly unlikely for an apricot to grow so large and red during the severe winter climate.

    467

    沈璧
    　　沈璧者，秦人也。少迫于贫，辞母薄游。至文登，登台望诲，见波浪薄天，慨然舒啸。旋有少年至，紫衣绛帻，仪状光瑰，揖璧而笑曰：“君清兴不浅，能同游乎？”璧欣然诺之。
    　　俄见凫雁一点，破浪而来，乃大舟也。离岸数尺，忽止不进。少年遽挽璧下台，履水登舟，身不沉，袜亦不濡。舟中乃无人，亦更无一物。心颇异之，叩其姓名及所自，少年曰：“余，玉桑君之子也。”璧方欲再问，忽觉水声澎湃，耳不可胜。自窗间视之，则银涛　万丈，璧泻从天，不觉惊骇失色。问是何处，少年笑曰：“适去台下已万里矣！”璧眷念乡井，悲形于颜。顷之，涛声已寂，碧玉湛然。微风一拂，鳞鳞如玻璃万顷，恍惚有无数丽人滉漾清涟中。方疑诧间，冰上丝管作矣。举首凝睇，则彩舟一具，相去数尺，有数女倚棹瞰波，顷所见，盖其影也。少年携璧过彩舟，珍奇射目，不可具名。

    Explanation

    467

    Shen Bi
    Shen Bi was a man from the state of Qin. When he was young, he was driven by poverty and left his mother to travel lightly. Upon reaching Wenden, he climbed a platform to gaze at the sea, where he saw waves touching the sky and sighed deeply. Soon after, a young man arrived, dressed in a purple robe and wearing a red headband, with a resplendent and magnificent appearance. He bowed to Shen Bi and smiled, saying, "Your clear兴致 is not shallow; can you join me on a journey?" Shen Bi readily agreed.

    Suddenly, a single dot appeared, breaking through the waves, and it turned out to be a large boat. It stopped a few feet from the shore and did not proceed further. The young man quickly led Shen Bi down from the platform, walking on water to board the boat, neither sinking nor getting his socks wet. There was no one on the boat, and there were no other objects aboard. Shen Bi found this very strange and asked about the young man's name and origin. The young man replied, "I am the son of Lord Yu Sang." As Shen Bi was about to ask more questions, he suddenly heard a deafening roar of water. Looking out through the window, he saw silver waves towering ten thousand feet high, pouring down from the sky. He could not help but be shocked and pale. He asked where they were, and the young man laughed and said, "We have already traveled ten thousand miles from the platform!" Shen Bi felt a deep longing for his hometown, and his sadness showed on his face. Soon, the roar of the waves subsided, and the sea was as clear and still as a piece of jade. A gentle breeze brushed over it, and it shimmered like a vast expanse of glass. Suddenly, it seemed as if countless beautiful women were floating in the clear ripples. Just as Shen Bi was in disbelief, music played on the ice. He raised his head and looked, and there was a colorful boat a few feet away, with several women leaning on the oars, gazing at the waves. What he had just seen was their reflection. The young man led Shen Bi to the colorful boat, where exquisite and rare items dazzled the eyes, too numerous to name.

    468

    须臾席启，玉貌旁罗，奏具行觞，肴品络绎，亦非复人间芬苾。侍者进璧果一枚，大如瓜，紫色；啖之，味绝甘，度必世所传蟠桃之属。窃怀其余，欲归以奉母。酒酣，璧避席称谢。因便求归。少年叹曰：“孝子哉！虽然，此乃君所以仙也。今姑不强留，终当至此。”顾诸女曰：“何以送客？”诸女各摘鬓上花及钗钏之物为赆，曰：“持此鬻人间，吃著不尽矣！”一女俯而笑，解裳下绣舄一钩，投海中，少年目之曰：“妮子独狡狯，不虑堕落耶？”女面发赤，退去。璧视舄，已化小舟。少年曰：“君登矣！”

    Explanation

    468

    In a moment, the feast began, and beautiful women surrounded the scene. Drinks were served in sequence, and dishes came one after another, creating a feast that was not of this world. A servant presented a single jade fruit, as large as a melon and purple in color. Upon tasting it, the flavor was exceptionally sweet, and it was likely a legendary peach of immortality. The guest secretly kept some of the fruit, intending to take it back to honor his mother. After drinking deeply, the guest stood up and thanked his host. He then requested to leave. The young man sighed and said, "What a filial son you are! However, this is why you will become immortal. For now, I will not force you to stay, but you will eventually come to this place." Turning to the young women, he asked, "What shall we give the guest as a gift?" Each of the young women picked flowers from their hair and jewelry to offer as presents. "Hold these and sell them in the world; they will be enough for you to eat and use!" One girl smiled and, lowering herself, unfastened her skirt and removed one embroidered slipper, throwing it into the sea. The young man looked at her and said, "You are particularly mischievous, don't you worry about falling?" The girl's face turned red, and she stepped back. The guest looked at the slipper, and it had transformed into a small boat. The young man said, "You have boarded!"

    469

    遂辞众而登，则前舟已杳，惊涛湠漫，惘然自失。小舟中异香喷溢，神骨俱醉。少焉醒豁，已泊野岸，河水仅阔如衣带。舍舟登陆，甫数武，小舟已凌风去矣。信步独行，追想所遇，恍惚若梦寐，而怀中余果，俨然在矣。薄暮抵驿馆，语皆秦声，距家中才一舍耳。璧既归，出余果进母，兼逮妻、子，皆数月不饥。以核种庭中，亦不生也。后十余年忽甲拆，数年成树，又数年作花，花类莲而大，数年不谢。久之乃结实，由小而大。又数年色渐红紫，如曩舟中所食者，时璧已枯坐一室，不复与人事。一旦忽出至庭中，仰树大笑曰：“果熟矣！”即诀妻、子去。

    Explanation

    469

    Then he bade farewell to the crowd and ascended, only to find that the boat ahead had vanished into the distance. The waves roiled and surged, leaving him bewildered and lost. In the small boat, a strange fragrance wafted through the air, intoxicating both his spirit and his bones. A little while later, he awoke with clarity, having already reached the shore of a wild river, whose waters were as narrow as a ribbon. He disembarked and took just a few steps when the small boat had already sailed away, carried by the wind. Walking at his leisure, he recalled the strange encounter, feeling as though he were in a dream. Yet, the remaining fruit he had in his pocket was still there, as if nothing had changed. As dusk approached, he arrived at a post station, where everyone spoke in the dialect of the Qin region, indicating that he was only a day's journey from home. Upon returning, he presented the remaining fruit to his mother, and it also nourished his wife and children, who went several months without hunger. He planted the seed in the courtyard, but it failed to grow. After more than a decade, however, it suddenly sprouted and grew into a tree. Over the next few years, it blossomed, producing flowers larger than lotus, which bloomed continuously for years without wilting. Eventually, it bore fruit, starting small and gradually growing larger. After several more years, the fruit's color turned a deep red and purple, much like what he had eaten on the boat before. By then, the man named Bi had become so withered and motionless that he no longer engaged in worldly affairs. One day, he suddenly emerged from his room and went to the courtyard, looking up at the tree and laughing heartily. "The fruit is ripe!" he exclaimed. Then, he bid farewell to his wife and children and departed.

    470

    余　时　鏸
    　　余时鏸善写真，江南常州人。乾隆四十五年，客大梁。薄暮独坐，有青衣者持刺入，云主人奉迓，已牵马门外。遂乘之，行稍远，有城临河。进至一官署，青衣入。徐传主人出迎，乃旧交沈某，相见极欢。问此何地，曰：“赣州。”问其官，曰：“司马也。比以长官命，延君写真。”即具仪从送之往。
    　　复抵一署，青衣投刺入。有顷，传语云：“语矣。本延江西余时惠。非时也。请速归。”乃还至沈所，颇咎沈失言，有累远涉。沈曰：“吾已坐此罚俸四年矣。请便送君归汴梁，盍迂道毗陵，一视闾井？”余然之，各乘马行一炊许，闻城市人语操土音，果常州也，余凭望故居，凄然良久。沈曰：“宜行矣，十二年后复相见耳。”遂匆匆别去，余亦恍惚还旧馆。甫入门，闻哭声，遽然而寤，则家人已衰绖矣。盖死者二日，心尚温耳，故未敛。兰雪说。

    Explanation

    470

    Mr. Yu Shizhong was skilled in painting portraits and hailed from Changzhou, Jiangnan. In the forty-fifth year of the Qianlong era, he was a guest in Kaifeng. Late in the afternoon, he was sitting alone when a servant in blue clothes entered with a card, saying that the host respectfully received him and had already led a horse out to the gate. He then mounted the horse and rode a short distance, passing by a city by the river. They arrived at an official residence, where the servant entered. After a while, the host emerged to greet him, and it turned out to be his old friend Shen Mou, with whom he was extremely happy to meet. He asked where they were, and the host replied, "Ganzhou." He then inquired about the host's position, and the host said, "I am a Military Governor. Recently, I was ordered by my superior to invite you to paint portraits." They immediately prepared ceremonial escorts and sent him there.

    They then arrived at another official residence, where the servant entered with a card. After a short while, a message was conveyed that said, "It is said that the invitation was originally for Mr. Yu Shizhong from Jiangxi. It is not the right time. Please return quickly." So, they went back to Shen's place, and Yu blamed Shen for his mistake, which had caused him to travel so far. Shen said, "I have been penalized and had my salary reduced for four years. Please allow me to send you back to Kaifeng. Why not take a detour to Piling and take a look at your hometown?" Yu agreed, and they each rode their horses for about an hour. Hearing the city's people speaking in local dialect, he realized they were indeed in Changzhou. He gazed at his old residence and felt deeply sad for a long time. Shen said, "You should go now. We will meet again in twelve years." So, they parted hurriedly, and Yu also returned to his old residence in a daze. As soon as he entered the door, he heard crying sounds and suddenly woke up, only to find that his family was already in mourning attire. It turned out that the deceased had been dead for two days, but the heart was still warm, so they had not yet buried him. This was told by Lanxue.

    471

    章五
    　　琴师章五，宿邯郸。漏初下，有美人来就之，章疑为狐。美人曰：“妾平原君美人也。以笑躄者罹惨祸，诉于冥帝。帝怒，收躄者，置重典以偿，并逮平原。平原自辨：“实诸客迫胜，胜恐失士心，为赵国忧，不得已出此。”帝谓‘平原果好士，士果归平原，岂在乎杀妾？杀妾以媚士，所以待士者可知；杀妾而客来，客亦可知矣。”平原哑然，顿首请罪。帝以其素贤，仅从薄谴。愍妾无罪，复其元，使游人间。妾伤往事。

    Explanation

    471

    Chapter Five
    　　Qinshi Zhang Wu stayed in Handan. When the water clock just started, a beautiful woman came to him, and Zhang Wu suspected it was a fox. The woman said, "I am a beautiful woman of Marquis Pingyuan. Because of laughing at a lame person, I suffered a terrible misfortune, and I appealed to the God of the Underworld. The God was angry, arrested the lame person, and imposed severe punishment, and also arrested Marquis Pingyuan. Marquis Pingyuan defended himself: 'It was actually that the guests forced me, I feared losing the hearts of the scholars, and worried about the state of Zhao, so I had no choice but to do this.' The God said, 'If Marquis Pingyuan truly values scholars, then scholars will truly return to him. Why would it matter if I were killed? Killing me to please the scholars, one can see how he treats them; killing me and still having guests come, one can see what kind of guests they are.' Marquis Pingyuan was speechless and knelt down to ask for forgiveness. The God, considering his usual virtue, only gave him a light punishment. The God pitied me for being innocent, restored my original form, and allowed me to wander among people. I am saddened by past events.

    472

    故不愿复生人世。君亦平原君客矣，当时处门下，闻而伤之，喟然叹曰：“公子之贤，宾客之盛，何重其躄者之足而轻美人之头也？吾闻兰杜被焚，则松筠不茂；孔翠见弹，则鸿鹄高逝；哲女戕虐，则国士远引，吾何为于斯。”故诸客皆还，君反独去。及秦围邯郸，鲁连未来，晋鄙不救，诸客束手，莫能展一筹。或请于君，君以身既不预，竟不为设策。平原不知也，故史册亦阙书焉。君亦殆不复忆矣。妾感君义，求之数千载，今始相值耳。”

    Explanation

    472

    Therefore, unwilling to be reborn into the human world. You are also a guest of Lord Pingyuan. At that time, while serving under his roof, I heard it and was moved to sorrow, and exclaimed with a sigh: "The nobleman's virtue and the grandeur of his guests, why do you value the foot of the lame one and despise the head of a beautiful woman? I have heard that when orchids and laurels are burned, the cypress does not flourish; when the peacock and the parrot are shot at, the wild geese and cranes fly high; when a wise woman is mistreated, the statesmen withdraw, what business do I have here?" Therefore, all the guests returned, but you alone departed. When the Qin besieged Handan, Lu Buwei did not come, and Jin Bi did not rescue. All the guests were helpless, unable to offer a single plan. Someone asked you, but since you were not involved, you did not offer a strategy. Lord Pingyuan did not know, so it is not recorded in the historical records. You probably do not remember either. I am moved by your righteousness, seeking it for thousands of years, and now we finally meet.

    473

    章茫然，叹息不已。乃援琴作歌，美人取瑟和之。歌曰：“碧草油油兮，故国荒邱。房陵遂迁兮，谁遗之谋？贤士如云兮，惟妾之仇。临楼一笑兮，身命休。念公子兮，心惨忧！”于是相对欷歔，涕泣不可止。久之，美人拭泪扬袖复歌，歌曰：“寂寥兮山阿，灰飞兮绮罗。今夕何夕兮，与子婆娑。既见君子兮，我心则那。露冷冷兮泣复歌，千秋一息兮哀情多！明月坠西兮奈子何！”歌竟，群鸡胶胶，东方欲白矣。美人逡巡别去；章怅悒至曙，亦登道。

    Explanation

    473

    Zhang was filled with bewilderment and sighed continuously. He then picked up his qin and began to sing, while the beautiful lady took up her se to harmonize with him. He sang: "The lush green grass, the desolate ruins of the old country. The noble family was exiled, who devised such a plan? Virtuous scholars are as numerous as clouds, yet only my enemy remains. A smile from the balcony, the end of my life. Thinking of the noble young man, my heart is filled with sorrow and worry!" Thus, they wept together, their tears unable to stop. After a long while, the beautiful lady wiped her tears, waved her sleeves, and sang again. She sang: "The desolate mountains, the fading of beautiful things. What a wonderful night it is, dancing with you. Having seen the noble gentleman, my heart is at ease. The cold dew falls, tears and songs intertwine, and the sorrow of a thousand autumns is profound! The bright moon sets in the west, what can be done about it!" When the song ended, the roosters crowed loudly, and the east was about to turn white. The beautiful lady hesitated and then left; Zhang remained sorrowful until dawn and also departed.

    474

    王黄胡子
    　　有王黄胡子者，以髯黄得名。自诩剑仙。尝会饮贵家某公宅中，夸耀四座，递叩诸客所能，莫不谦让，王意益肆。末坐一客□□癃惫，衣冠了鸟。王颇轻之，乃揶揄曰：“公辱在婪尾，得毋善饭乎？抑有他长？”客笑曰：“仆诚不能与诸君子比数，然闻君论剑，颇触鄙好，愿各为戏剑，博诸君子欢。”王掀髯作色曰：“云何？”客曰：“剑之为用，上倚星汉，下披泉壤，旁行四极。剚蛟鳄于不测之渊，剪妖魅于幽暗之丛。虽脱兔逸林，疾隼赴霄，纵锷飞锋，罔有遗遁。今第试其易者，请伺飞鸟而刺之！”王心难之。适已日暮，乃诿曰：“阳乌匿矣，羽族息矣，何天有鸟，尚来迎吾刃者乎？”

    Explanation

    474

    There was a man named Wang Huang, whose yellow beard gave him his name. He boasted of being a sword saint. Once, he attended a banquet at the home of a wealthy noble and, in front of the guests, he boasted about his own abilities, asking each guest in turn about what they could do. Everyone was modest and yielded, but Wang Huang's intentions became more unrestrained. At the end of the table sat a weak and sickly guest, poorly dressed. Wang Huang looked down on him somewhat and teased him, saying, "You have the honor of sitting at the end of the table. Perhaps you are good at eating? Or perhaps you have other strengths?" The guest smiled and said, "I truly cannot compare with the gentlemen here, but I have heard your talk about swordsmanship and it touches my interest. I wish to each perform a sword dance to entertain the gentlemen." Wang Huang flared his beard and changed his expression, saying, "How?" The guest said, "The sword's use reaches up to the stars and down to the depths of the earth, extending to all directions. It can stab crocodiles in the depths of the unknown, cut demons and spirits in the dark and dense forest. Even if a rabbit has escaped the forest, a swift falcon flying to the sky, releasing the edges of the blades, no one can escape. Now let us just try the easy ones. Please wait for flying birds and stab them!" Wang Huang found the challenge difficult. Just then, the sun had set, and he said, "The sun has hidden, the feathered creatures have rested. Where are the birds in the sky, still come to meet my sword?"

    475

    客曰：“若是且已，盍试登高为二项之舞，一决楚汉。”王又辞曰：“休矣休矣。星月不曜，烛燎不辉，仰不瞻山，俯不察地，恶乎可登而角吾技？”客指主人厅事曰：“崇墉巍巍，飞甍冠之，上探云霞，嵩华可齐。顾不高耶？请灼尔目、誆尔足，仆虽跛躄，愿导先路。”于是搴袖离席，挟王以出，如提婴孺，履墙缘拱，如步康衢。径跨檐牙，升于屋脊。坐王于鸱吻之上而抚之曰：“此地平旷，聊可栖息。君请匡坐，仆便来迎。”言已复下，神气洒然。

    Explanation

    475

    The guest said, "If it is so, then let it be. Why not try climbing high and performing a dance of two items, to settle the matter between Chu and Han?" The king again declined, saying, "Enough, enough. The stars and moon do not shine, and candles do not light up. Looking up, one does not see mountains; looking down, one does not see the ground. How can one climb and display my skills?" The guest pointed to the hall of the host and said, "The grand and towering walls, with their soaring roofs, reach up to the clouds and mist, and can match the heights of Mount Song and Mount Hua. Is it not high? Please blind your eyes, deceive your feet. Even though I am lame, I am willing to lead the way." Thus, he pulled up his sleeves and led the king out, carrying him like a baby, walking along the wall and under the arch, as if on a broad and smooth road. He crossed the eaves directly and ascended to the roof ridge. Sitting the king on the ridge beast and patting him, he said, "This place is flat and open, suitable for resting. Please sit properly, and I will come to greet you." After speaking, he descended, appearing refreshed and composed.

    476

    堂下观者无不色飞飞吻，啧啧称神，客便索酒，连罄数觥，颓然就舍。主人命支梯树架，哄扰终宵，王始得下。天明视客，已豹隐矣。王甚惭，主人甚悔，海内奇士，交臂失之。
    　　猫犬
    　　康熙中，大兴县某媪家奉佛，佛前悬一灯。一日薄暮，闻佛舍小语。隙而窥之，见黄犬人立，以前两足承白猫；猫亦人立，盗饮佛灯油，猫吸油，转注犬口中，复吸之。稍缓，犬促之曰：“速饮速饮，人且至。”细视猫、犬，皆家所蓄也。媪惊，推门入，猫、犬皆奔出，索之杳然。
    　　越翌日，夜中，闻庭中有声，密起察之，复见猫乘犬背，犬彳亍而行。叱之，立隐。夜梦一黄衣男子，一白衣女子，来谓曰：“寄主人庑下久矣，豢养之恩，未知所报，顾形迹已彰，不可留矣。”乃相向再拜，卧地转身，忽成猫、犬；猫跃登犬背，骑之而去。

    Explanation

    476

    A Final Translation of the Source Text

    The Strange Guest

    The onlookers below were visibly impressed and repeatedly exclaimed in wonder. The guest immediately asked for wine, downed several cups in quick succession, and then slumped into the guesthouse. The host ordered the setup of ladders and scaffolding, causing a commotion all night long. The king finally descended. At dawn, he looked for the guest, but the guest had vanished like a leopard. The king was deeply ashamed, and the host was greatly regretful, having missed the opportunity to retain such a remarkable person within the realm.

    Cats and Dogs

    During the Kangxi era, in Daxing County, an elderly woman who venerated Buddhism had a lamp hanging before her Buddha statue. One evening as dusk fell, she heard soft whispers in the Buddha chamber. Peering through a gap, she saw a yellow dog standing on its hind legs, supporting a white cat with its front paws; the cat also stood on its hind legs, secretly drinking the oil from the Buddha lamp. The cat sucked the oil and transferred it to the dog's mouth, then sucked it again. After a moment, the dog urged, "Drink quickly, drink quickly, someone is coming!" Upon closer inspection, the woman realized that the cat and dog were both animals she kept at home. Startled, she pushed open the door, and both the cat and dog fled. Despite searching, she could not find them.

    The next day, at night, she heard a sound in the courtyard. She quietly went to investigate and saw the cat riding on the dog's back, and the dog walking slowly. When she shouted at them, they immediately disappeared. That night, she dreamed of a man in yellow robes and a woman in white robes, who came to her and said, "We have been staying under your eaves for a long time. We do not know how to repay your kindness, but our actions have already been exposed, and we cannot stay here anymore." They bowed to each other twice, lay down on the ground, and turned around, suddenly transforming into a cat and a dog. The cat leaped onto the dog's back and rode away.

    477

    仍吉
    　　越中郭生贫无家，去其乡，游于晋。鬻其文，无售者。困于旅食，则为人佣书。人劣之，薄其值。终日运管，不供一饱焉。念生不百年，劳且饥无穷期，信穷矣，不如死。复念以迟死，盍速？以忧死，盍乐乎？遂倾囊中钱，并质衣装，赴酒家痛饮至大醉，以馀钱散诸丐者。且歌且笑，见之者皆以为狂而乐也，而不知其将死也。
    　　既出，走入荒谷中，叹曰：“我其已夫！虽然，吾顷则陶焉而乐，且施于人矣，何忧而不死！”睨其旁有枯树，解束带挂焉。忽树中人语曰：“咄！已而，若亦浅之乎窥世，褊之乎其自处矣。”生叱曰：“魅乎莫余诮，余不尔畏，余将与若游！”树中人笑曰：“若将以余为鬼耶？否否！途适丐于尔者也，而知尔，故来。且余丐于人间者，今数百年矣，而不死，而尔曷以死？尔以亟死为达，余不以为能。

    Explanation

    477

    Translation:

    Still Auspicious

    A man named Guo from Yue, destitute and without a home, left his village and traveled to Jin. He tried to sell his writings, but no one bought them. Struggling to survive as a traveler, he took on the job of copying documents for others. People looked down on him and paid him poorly. He toiled all day, yet could not even afford a full meal. Reflecting on his short life, he thought that labor and hunger had no end, and truly, he was in a desperate situation, might as well die. Then he thought, why not die quickly? Why not die happily? So he emptied his purse, pawned his clothes, and went to a tavern to drink heavily until he was thoroughly drunk. With the remaining money, he distributed it among beggars. Singing and laughing, those who saw him thought he was crazy and happy, but they did not know that he was about to die.

    Afterward, he walked into a desolate valley and sighed, "Is this the end of me? Yet, I was just intoxicated and happy, and I gave to others. What have I to worry about that would make me die?" He glanced at a withered tree nearby and untied his belt, hanging it there. Suddenly, a voice came from the tree: "Go ahead! You only glimpse the world superficially and narrow your own position." Guo shouted, "You're a demon, don't criticize me! I don't fear you, I will travel with you!" The figure in the tree laughed and said, "Do you think I am a ghost? No, no! I was just passing by and begging from you, and I knew you, so I came. And I have been a beggar in the world for several hundred years now, and I do not die. Why should you die? You think dying quickly is noble, but I do not think so."

    478

    ”生曰：“子仙乎哉？何穷而寿也，且恶乎置余？”树中人曰：“回而志，作而气，将告尔。有虎于此，度其咥己也，因投之，孰与走而避之乎？济于水，惧不免于溺，因自沉，孰与方舟而纵其行乎？且若今日死，其余之年非若之有也？盍听我？听我而死，无加于死也，然视今日已后矣！脱听我而遇，则大幸，孰与听我？”生曰：“诺，谨受教！”瞬间树已失。有立于前者，蹙额而丛眉，目无光，鬓发蓬蓬然，垢腻满颊，衣鹑结不掩骭，双履不纳踵，生志夺于乍骇，而却蹜蹜而不敢前。其人笑曰：“愚哉，若且欲死也，而畏乎？随以来。”生意定，走从之。

    Explanation

    478

    The man replied, "Are you a sage? Why are you poor yet long-lived, and where will you place me?" The person in the tree said, "Turn your mind and act with spirit, and I will tell you. Suppose there is a tiger here, if it bites you, you might throw yourself into it; which is better, to run and avoid it? When crossing a river, afraid of not escaping drowning, you might drown yourself; which is better, to use a boat and let it go? And if you die today, the rest of your years are not yours. Why not listen to me? If you die after listening to me, it is no different from dying, but look at the days after today! If you listen to me and meet with good fortune, it will be a great blessing; which is better, to listen to me?" The man said, "Yes, I will follow your advice!" Suddenly, the tree disappeared. There was a man standing in front, furrowing his brows and clustering his eyebrows, with eyes without light, disheveled and messy hair, dirt and grease on his face, patched and torn clothes not covering his shins, shoes that did not cover his heels. The man's mind was overwhelmed by sudden shock and he stepped back, unable to move forward. The man laughed and said, "Foolish, if you want to die, why are you afraid? Follow me." The man's mind settled, and he followed him.

    479

    至断崖之下，其泉如帘，高落于长松之杪。披悬萝，得石窦，广可容身。其人蛇行入，生匍匐继之。倏旷朗，得方池，湛然而洁也。其人裸而浴。俄有送冠服者至，其人澡而登。衣绮绣，冠华冠，曳文履。视之，额丰矣，眉修矣，目顾盼朗矣，发韬矣，颜白皙而光泽矣。生复惊曰：“何变也？”其人笑曰：“然。若宁欲变乎？”生曰：“幸甚！”其人使生浴，顾来者为生具衣饰，生服之。即临池而知镜，讶而不类。其人笑曰：“亦变矣！既变，余可去！”言已，遂不见。

    Explanation

    479

    To the base of the precipice, the waterfall was like a curtain of water, falling high above the tips of tall pines. Parting the hanging vines, they found a stone cave, large enough to accommodate one's body. The person entered by slithering like a snake, and the other person followed by crawling. Suddenly, a bright and open space appeared, and they found a square pond, clear and clean. The person bathed naked, and suddenly, someone brought clothing. The person bathed and then ascended, wearing embroidered silk, a splendid hat, and ornate shoes. When looking at him, his forehead was full, his eyebrows well-formed, his eyes bright and expressive, his hair hidden, and his face white and lustrous. The other person exclaimed in surprise, "What has changed?" The person smiled and said, "Indeed. Would you rather change?" The other person replied, "It would be fortunate!" The person made the other person bathe and ordered someone to prepare clothes and decorations for him. Immediately, facing the pool, he saw his reflection, surprised and not like before. "It has also changed! Once changed, I can leave!" After speaking, he disappeared.

    480

    生甚悸，疾行，得小涧，循而西。涧止，得盘石，意倦憩其上。俄有男女数人来，见生若甚讶，相与指而目之，逡巡不遽去。生乃行，复遇数十人，讶如前，生亦讶。复行，得城郭，进之，其中男女见者，莫不环而迫之也。于是生所止，顷刻成市肆。生讶甚，问焉，皆曰：“悦子之都也。”生恶其扰，袖其面而趋。观者稍引去，然从者犹数十百人。有妪教生曰：“速自毁！”生乃土其面。会暮，众始解，独一人者留，则妪也，强曳生行。生惴惴焉。随至大第，达乎其内而帘启，有女子出，曰：“浴之。”妪导诸旁室，则汤沐具焉。已而入，与女子抗礼，女子乃言曰：“危乎哉，子也！是其人皆好色者，脱不自毁，将裂子。今秘子于此，乃违于难矣。”生惶恐谢，问：“此是何地？”女子曰：“五方杂处。不名一所。”由是共寝处无间。女子家世隐密，独知其姓邬，其字曰仍吉。

    Explanation

    480

    The man was greatly frightened and ran quickly. He found a small stream and followed it westward. The stream ended, and he found a large stone. Tired, he rested on it. Soon, several men and women came. They saw him and seemed very surprised. They pointed at him and stared at him, hesitating and not leaving immediately. The man continued walking and reached a city. As he entered, the people there saw him and none of them failed to surround and press against him. Thus, wherever he stopped, it became a market in an instant. The man was greatly surprised and asked what was going on. They all said, "This is the city of pleasure." Disliking the disturbance, he hurried away, covering his face with his sleeve. The onlookers gradually moved away, but some still followed him. An old woman advised him, "Destroy yourself quickly!" The man covered his face with dirt. As evening fell, the crowd dispersed, and only one person remained, who turned out to be the old woman, who forcibly dragged him along. Trembling with fear, he followed her to a large mansion. When they entered, the curtain was drawn, and a woman came out, saying, "Bathe." The old woman led him to a side room, where the bathing facilities were ready. He entered, and the woman treated him as an equal. She then said, "How dangerous you are! These people are all lustful. If you don't destroy yourself, they will tear you apart. Now, if you hide yourself here, you will be safe from harm." The man was greatly alarmed and thanked her. He asked, "Where am I?" The woman replied, "A place where people from all directions live together. It is not named after any specific place." From then on, they shared the same sleeping quarters without any barriers. The woman's family had always been secretive, and she alone knew her surname was Wu, and her name was Rengji.

    481

    久之妪来告，外人颇言邬氏有婿矣。仍吉惊且忧，谋匿生，乃烛而处之夹室，窃窃与生对。生虽嬖爱仍吉，而居如长夜，殊苦幽囚，亦怏怏不乐。无何，夜有斩关者，直趋夹室，状类昆仑奴，挟生越墙而出，行甚疾，置生于空庭而出。旋见灯檠出室中，女子队而前，见生皆笑。共推挽以入，交相接也。生惫极而卧，而闻女子曰：“敝矣。”共舁置门外，阖其扉。

    Explanation

    481

    After a long time, an old woman came to report, saying that people outside were saying the Wu family had a son-in-law. Rengji was both surprised and worried, and plotted to hide the son-in-law. She lit a candle and placed him in a side room, speaking in hushed tones to him. Although the son-in-law was favored by Rengji, living like this felt like a long night, and he was greatly distressed by being confined, so he was also unhappy. At night, there was someone who cut through the door, directly heading to the side room. He looked like a昆仑slave, carried the son-in-law over the wall and out. He moved very quickly, placed the son-in-law in an empty courtyard and left. Soon, they saw a lampstand come out of the room, and a group of women came forward. Seeing the son-in-law, they all laughed, and they all pushed and pulled him in. They interacted with each other. The son-in-law was exhausted and lay down, and heard the women say, "It's worn out." They all carried him out to the door and closed it.

    482

    及曙，有达官者过，见生曰：“病者也。”问所苦，生讳曰：“饥耳！”遂载之后乘。归其居而丰其室。寻复故，问所业，生对曰：“尝读书矣，而未竟。”达官悦，使传其二子。达官宪令文书，一切代于生。
    　　一日，达官听讼久不辍，生侦于厅事之侧，得数事：其一，某县城隍病，有为不道之诉者，胥辈利其镪，受而伸之，罚及良善。城惶觉，自揭其过。胥辈论罪有差，城惶褫禄一岁。其一，富民讼贫民之友。贫民尝贷富民金，莫偿也，富民夺其宅。计值为有馀，求鬻宅而益之金，富民弗与。质于乡父老，弗能正。鸣之宰，富民阻于途，且挞之，流血被踵。其友闻而怒，俟间杀富民，亡命去，卒逃于法。故讼之。达官曰：“若富而不仁，罪也，有召死之道。然贫民杀若，则怨也。

    Explanation

    482

    When dawn came, a high-ranking official passed by and saw the sick man, saying, "He is the sick one." The official asked what his ailment was, and the sick man evaded by saying, "I am just hungry!" The official then took him on his carriage and returned him to his home, enriching his household. Soon, the sick man recovered and was asked about his profession. He replied, "I once studied, but did not complete it." The official was pleased and ordered his two sons to be taught. The official's edicts and documents were all handled by the sick man.

    One day, the official listened to lawsuits for a long time without stopping, and the sick man observed from the side of the hall, learning several matters: one case involved a sick city god, and someone was accusing him of misconduct. The officials profited from the bribe and extended the accusation, affecting the innocent. The city god realized and confessed his own faults. The officials were punished differently, and the city god had his salary reduced for a year. Another case involved a wealthy person suing a poor person's friend. The poor person had borrowed money from the wealthy person and could not repay it, so the wealthy person seized his house. The value was more than enough, so he wanted to sell the house and add the money, but the wealthy person refused. They sought arbitration from the elders of the village, but they could not resolve it, leading to an appeal to the magistrate. The wealthy person blocked the way and beat the poor person, causing blood to flow down his feet. The poor person's friend heard about it and became angry, waiting for an opportunity to kill the wealthy person, then fled and escaped justice. Therefore, he sued the wealthy person. The official said, "If you are rich but unkind, it is a crime, and there is a way to summon death. However, if the poor person kills you, it is a grudge."

    483

    以怨杀人，不可训，当偿若命矣，其友杀若，则侠也，公也，则无罪。”富民语塞，泣而退。其一，两蛟出于山，伤山下禾稼百余亩。山神讼之，当论斩，捕两蛟于庭，乃两书生。各推诿，搒掠皆不承，遂并坐之。生叹曰：“善！”其一，女子讼男子，为其杀己也。男子辩，言与女盖相悦不遂，女乃病而死，非己实杀女。达官谓女曰：“若既悦之矣，复怨而诬之，曷故？”女诉曰：“我死，彼若弗闻者，又他娶，略不相念，其凉而忍焉若是，有死我之心，非杀我而何？”达官谓曰：“深文也。”竟置不理。其一，夫讼妇以为不节，乞绝之于庙。问妇，乃并无外遇。

    Explanation

    483

    Killing someone out of resentment cannot be corrected, and the killer should repay his life. If his friend kills him, then it is chivalrous; if it is public, then there is no crime." The wealthy man was speechless and wept as he left. In another case, two dragons emerged from the mountain and injured the crops of over a hundred acres in the valley. The mountain god sued them, and they should be sentenced to death. They were captured in court, and it turned out they were two scholars. Each shifted the blame, and all resisted interrogation, so they were both punished. The scholar sighed and said, "Good!" In another case, a woman sued a man, claiming he had killed her. The man defended himself, saying that he and the woman had once been lovers but the relationship did not work out, and she subsequently fell ill and died, not that he actually killed her. The official said to the woman, "If you were already pleased with him, why do you now resent and falsely accuse him?" The woman replied, "When I died, he acted as if he hadn't heard, then married someone else, barely thinking of me. His coldness and cruelty are so great, it's as if he wants me dead, not that he actually killed me." The official said, "It's a serious charge," and finally, it was ignored. In another case, a husband sued his wife for impropriety, seeking to dissolve their marriage in the temple. When asked, the wife denied any infidelity.

    484

    其夫曰：“吾视尔遇邻人之子，必目而送之，特阻于势分，其意岂可问也。”达官终以为罗织，斥其夫而直其妇。生意不能平，书一牍启达官曰：“明公听断如流，吏道精敏。下风观听，倾服何言？然前三事允臧矣，其后二谳，鄙见斯岐。询刍有义，伏惟裁察。窃谓德无不报，怨岂在明，国家有原情之律，君子有诛心之论。将正风俗，宜先人情。男女之狱，古今万端。闺房之内，事甚画眉。隐秘琐曲，莫可详诘，而要以治心为本。夫妃匹之际，虽君父尊亲，不能得之臣子者，不得其心也。然丈夫之心，所分者博，所向亦弗专。

    Explanation

    484

    The husband said, "I see you always look at your neighbor's son and wave goodbye, only restrained by social propriety. How could his intentions be questioned?" The official finally thought it was a trap, dismissed the husband, and vindicated the wife. The husband could not remain calm and wrote a letter to the official, saying, "The honorable official makes judgments as smoothly as flowing water, and his conduct is precise and sharp. Observing from below the wind, how can one not be impressed? However, the first three matters were indeed proper, but the next two cases, my humble opinion differs. Consulting the lowly has its merits; I humbly request your judgment. I believe virtue is never unrewarded, and resentment does not lie in the open. The state has laws based on the circumstances of the case, and gentlemen have theories that judge the heart. To rectify customs, it is appropriate to start with human nature. Cases involving men and women are numerous throughout history. Within the inner chambers, the matter is like painting eyebrows. Hidden and trivial matters cannot be fully questioned, but the key is to govern the heart. In the relationship between husband and wife, even if the emperor or father is respected and loved, they cannot obtain it from their subjects or children because they do not understand the heart. However, a man's heart is broad and his direction is not exclusive."

    485

    得不得，姑弗深论。女子之心，惟男子是寄，其心弗欲，虽金、张之显，卫、霍之贵，陶、猗之富，扬、马之才，莫能回也。名分虚耳，形骸迹耳。苟得其心，斯其夫也；不得其心，虽夫不夫。则得其心者，宜何如感；不得其心，怨怒亦宜。今女为男死，不能收滴泪之报，引致讼庭，犹急自煎洗，略无悔咎，獧薄之情，于斯为甚。宜听女言以男抵罪。至于夫之讼妇，情亦非过。其夫在侧，屡盼他人，其心逝矣。心之既逝，虽不妻可也。管仲感鲍叔之知，侔于生我，汉高论丁公之罪，以诫不忠。事虽不伦，其义一也。请取以断斯狱焉。”

    Explanation

    485

    Whether one succeeds or not, let us not delve deeply into it. A woman's heart is entrusted solely to a man; if her heart does not desire it, even the fame of the Jins and Zhangs, the nobility of the Weis and Hous, the wealth of the Taos and Yis, or the talent of Yang and Ma cannot change it. Titles are merely nominal, and physical forms are just traces. If one gains her heart, then he is her husband; if one does not gain her heart, then even a husband is not a husband. Then, those who gain her heart should feel how they should; those who do not gain her heart, anger and resentment are also appropriate. Now, a woman dies for a man, yet she cannot receive even a tear's worth of repayment. She drags him to court, still burning with self-purification, with no trace of regret or guilt. Such shallow and thin emotions are most extreme here. It is appropriate to listen to the woman's words and hold the man accountable. As for the husband's lawsuit against the wife, the emotion is not excessive. While her husband is beside her, she often looks at others, and her heart has already left. Once her heart has left, even not being a wife is acceptable. Guan Zhong was grateful to Bao Shu for his understanding, equating it to being reborn. Emperor Gao of Han discussed the crime of Ding Gong to warn against disloyalty. Although the events are not similar, their meanings are the same. Please use this to decide this case.

    486

    达官否之，生固争，达官不怿，谓人曰：“彼职为宾师。奈何强与吾官事？”于是礼渐疏，生亦不安。遂去达官而潜访仍吉。毁衣垢颜，用自韬晦。久之不得耗，游复困，乡思郁然，登高山以望越中，茫乎莫知其向也。忽见树中人来谓曰：“可返矣！”生惊喜未对，遽为所推堕。陡若梦觉，则身固卧枯树下，曾雉经矣，带绝而堕也。始悟身已久死，所遇皆鬼耳。然酒吻觉甚渴，乃走近市中乞浆。有识之者曰：“此昨日狂饮生也。”则又悟死而复活，才经一宿耳。生既以求死不得，益困不可支。走谒故所尝识者，间得一餐，人愈益贫之。每为人道死时事，多笑而不信。

    Explanation

    486

    <TRANSLATION> The high-ranking official rejected the protagonist's involvement in official matters. The protagonist insisted, and the official, displeased, told others, "His role is that of a guest teacher. Why should he interfere with our official duties?" As a result, their relationship became increasingly distant, and the protagonist felt uneasy. He decided to leave the official and secretly visit "仍吉."

    To conceal his identity, the protagonist wore tattered clothes and a dirty face. After a long time, he found no trace of "仍吉" and was once again in a difficult situation. His longing for his hometown grew, and he climbed a high mountain to look toward his homeland. The view was vague, and he could not determine its direction. Suddenly, he saw someone emerge from the trees and said, "You can return now!" Startled, the protagonist did not yet respond when the person pushed him down. It felt as if he had woken from a dream, and he found himself lying at the foot of a dead tree. He realized he had attempted suicide by hanging, but the rope had broken, and he had fallen instead. He understood that he had already died and that the people he had encountered were ghosts.

    However, his mouth still felt very thirsty after drinking alcohol, so he approached the marketplace to ask for water. Someone recognized him and said, "This is the person who was drinking heavily yesterday." The protagonist then realized that he had died and come back to life, having only been dead for one night.

    Feeling increasingly desperate, the protagonist went to visit former acquaintances he had met before. Occasionally, they provided him with a meal, but he was increasingly treated as poor. He often told people about the events surrounding his death, but many laughed and did not believe him.
    </TRANSLATION>

    487

    既乃为商贾负担者，去晋之京师，计力而食。足茧不能进，止于邺中，欲赴漳流以自葬。至河干，忽焉一舟过，倚舵而盼者，类仍吉之妪。尾而察之，果妪也。遂呼妪，妪不识，曰：“若谁欤？”生自陈姓名。旋于窗间见仍吉，生又呼，仍吉熟视曰：“非也，不类。”生窘，厉声曰：“不记夹室共述云娘事，为卿拭泪耶？”仍吉乃惊曰：“信也。”乃止舟于岸，呼曰：“登！”生登与仍吉相持哭，甚凄痛。云娘者，仍吉女兄也，弃于所欢，抱恨死。生在夹室时，仍吉尝述之，且谓曰：“子幸无类此！遂流涕不能止，生以衫袖硈其泪。因约曰：“不幸有破镜事！他日相遇如不识，请举此事为息壤之言。”故生貌虽变，仍吉信而纳之也。

    Explanation

    487

    After transforming himself into a merchant or laborer, he left the capital of Jin and lived by the labor he could perform. His feet blistered and he could not proceed further, so he stopped in Yecheng, intending to go to the flow of the Zhang River to bury himself. Upon reaching the riverbank, suddenly a boat passed by, and the person leaning on the rudder and looking out resembled an old woman named Reng Ji. Following and observing the boat, he confirmed it was indeed the old woman. He then called out to her, but she did not recognize him and asked, "Who are you?" The protagonist introduced his name. Then, through the window, he saw Reng Ji again and called out to her, but she looked at him closely and said, "No, you do not resemble him." The protagonist was embarrassed and shouted loudly, "Do you not remember when we were in the inner room and told the story of Yun Niang, and I wiped your tears?" Reng Ji was surprised and said, "It is true." She then stopped the boat at the shore and called out, "Come aboard!" The protagonist got on board and hugged Reng Ji, crying bitterly. Yun Niang was Reng Ji's elder sister, who had been abandoned by her lover and died with regret. When the protagonist was in the inner room, Reng Ji had once told the story and said, "May you never be like this!" Then she wept uncontrollably, and the protagonist wiped her tears with his sleeve. They then made an agreement: "If there is an unfortunate separation, if we meet again and do not recognize each other, please mention this as a sign of our agreement." Therefore, even though the protagonist's appearance had changed, Reng Ji believed and accepted him.

    488

    于是仍吉语生曰：“子向者为鬼，余亦邻处于鬼，鬼皆阴荡不可近。自子之见夺也，余亦旋徙去。今浮家至此，获遇子，永托于子矣！幸有薄积，可以归。”遂与生俱载归越中。仍吉盖狐云。
    　　石　孝　廉
    　　石孝廉某尝昼卧，觉鼻中奇痒，以小指捺之，因而大嚏。有物冲孔出，堕地作声。视之，一小人长寸行，游行地上。骇极欲呼，而胸间如压不能起。有顷，门外演盘铃傀儡，群呶在耳，小人皇遽出门去。石亦遽起，恍如梦觉。自是得奇疾，闻香物则掩鼻；遇矢溺腥膻之秽，嗅之以为香，心好之。履鲍鱼之肆，如入芝兰之室，流连不能去。

    Explanation

    488

    Translation

    Thus, still good at speaking, still good said to the scholar: "You were formerly a ghost, I also lived near the ghosts, all the ghosts were dark and chaotic, could not be approached. Since you were taken away, I also quickly moved away. Now, wandering household to this place, met you, will forever rely on you! Fortunately have a small savings, can return home." So with the scholar, together loaded and returned to Yue. Still good, it seems, said the fox.
    　　Scholar-Official Stone
    　　Scholar-Official Stone, a certain one, once lay down during the day, felt an extraordinary itch in the nose, with the little finger pressed it, and therefore sneezed greatly. Something rushed out of the hole, fell to the ground and made a sound. Looking at it, a small person one inch tall, walking, wandered around on the ground. Terrified to the extreme, wanted to call out, but felt as if pressed in the chest and could not get up. After a while, outside the door, a puppet show with bells and drums, many noises in the ears, the small person in panic ran out the door. The scholar also quickly got up, as if waking from a dream. From then on, got a strange illness, smelling fragrant things would cover the nose; encountering feces and urine, foul smells of fish and mutton, smelling it thought it was fragrant, liked it in the heart. Walking into a shop selling fish, as if entering a room filled with orchids, lingered and could not leave.

    489

    后举进士，官秦中。有道士来谒，自言能疗奇疾，异而见之。献一丸如李大，金光烂然，取大碗贮之，出囊中药一刀圭，糁其上。使熬之，须臾溶为液，五色陆离，然不堪其臭，左右皆欲呕哕。以进石，石乃觉芬罄逾常，立饮之，味殊甘酽。少顷，胸中热如蒸，移进如刀箭环簇心腑。急命缚道士，则已杳矣。
    　　昏痛这际，见道士在前，亟呼左右追执，乃悄无应者。道士笑曰：“此阴府也，何处觅左右耶？”石大号，道士曰：“无苦，此中不少佳境，盍往观之？”曳以同行。乃至狴犴间，见缧皃纵横，刀斧林簇，刑戮惨异，心甚恐怖。念世传地狱变相，竟有之也。末至一处，人皆残体，有无口鼻耳目者，或仅有口耳，余体不具者，或有五官倒置者，虎豹狗马羊豕诸物，杂处不相避。

    Explanation

    489

    After passing the imperial examination and becoming a Jinshi, Shi held office in the region of Shaanxi. One day, a Taoist monk came to visit, claiming he could cure strange diseases. Shi found this remarkable and invited him in. The monk presented a pill as large as a plum, which had a golden, radiant appearance. He took a large bowl to hold it, then took out a small amount of medicine from his bag and sprinkled it on top. He ordered the pill to be heated. In a short while, it melted into a liquid, which had various colors, like a rainbow. However, the smell was unbearable, and everyone around was about to vomit. Shi drank the liquid immediately, and the taste was unusually sweet and strong. A short while later, his chest felt as hot as boiling, and the sensation spread like arrows and knives piercing his heart. Shi quickly ordered to bind the Taoist monk, but the monk had already disappeared.

    In the midst of his dizziness and pain, Shi saw the Taoist monk in front of him. He immediately called out to those around to catch him, but nobody responded quietly. The monk laughed and said, "This is the underworld. Where can you find those around you here?" Shi shouted loudly, and the monk said, "Don't be afraid. There are not a few good places here. Why not go and see?" He pulled Shi along. They eventually arrived at a place with iron cages, where Shi saw people bound in chains all over, a forest of knives and axes, and cruel punishments. His heart was very terrified. He thought of the world's depiction of hell and realized that it indeed exists. They eventually arrived at a place where people were all mutilated, some had no mouth, nose, eyes, or ears, or some had only mouths and ears, with the rest of their bodies incomplete, or some had their five senses inverted. Various animals like tigers, leopards, dogs, horses, sheep, and pigs mixed together without avoiding each other.

    490

    石问其故，道士笑不答，亟携之返曰：“公欲生乎？”石因哀祈，谓：“妻妾子女犹在室相候，胡遽不欲生？”道士笑曰：“亦良易。”即袖中出一小人，长三四寸，曰：“此即公之故物也。公曩纵之，不知几何寻丈矣。赖吾收而制之，仅止于此，尚可纳也。”即以小人首向石鼻塞之，石大苦而嘶，倏然已活。冷汗浃背，出青紫痰液数斗，病遂失。
    　　耳食录二编卷八
    　　周　英　如
    　　昭武之东某市镇，尝张杂局。士女冶游，竟日连衽举袂，红雾幕衣。有少年姜某，仰见西楼一女子，凭窗凝望，与楼前矮屋蔷薇化绰约争丽，绚成妙彩，遂仰睇不移。女亦秋眸专注，目成焉而神痴也。比日斜人散，两人犹相对而望。有见者嗤之，始各避去。

    Explanation

    490

    The Stone asked the reason, and the Taoist laughed without answering, hastily taking him back and saying, "Do you wish to live?" The Stone then pleaded earnestly, saying, "My wife, concubines, children, and grandchildren are still waiting for me at home. Why suddenly don't I want to live?" The Taoist smiled and said, "It is also quite easy." He then took out a small person from his sleeve, about three to four inches long, and said, "This is your former self. You previously let it go, not knowing how far it had wandered. Thanks to me, I have caught and controlled it, and now it is only this size, still capable of being accommodated." He immediately placed the small person's head against the Stone's nose, causing the Stone great pain and a loud cry, and suddenly he came back to life. Cold sweat drenched his back, and he spat out several dou of greenish-purple phlegm, and the illness was cured.

    Ears Eating Records Volume Eight

    Zhou Yingru

    In the eastern part of Zhaowu, there was a certain town where a gathering was held. Young men and women went on outings, spending the entire day holding sleeves and raising their robes, creating a red fog-like atmosphere. A young man named Jiang saw a woman leaning against the window of a western building, gazing intently, and her beauty competed with the roses in front of the low house, creating a splendid and colorful scene. He looked up and did not move his gaze. The woman also had focused autumn eyes, and they fell in love with each other, becoming entranced. By the time the sun was slanting and people were dispersing, the two still looked at each other. Those who saw this laughed at them, and they finally went away separately.

    491

    次日姜复往，女已先在。楼高而屋隔，语不可闻。姜以手中素帨，裹约指金环掷诸楼上，女报以腕钏一枚，遂掩窗而入。女盖周姓名英如，依母以居。父某为茶商，远出矣。姜故悉其根荄，伺黄昏无人，伏其家寝门之右。既而鸟栖人定，潜窥英如之闺，闺已扃见英如独坐灯下，絮絮与灯语。姜以腕钏触窗棂，以声致英如。英如讶然，问曰：“谁？”姜曰：“我也，腕钟在此。”英如复大惊，趋至窗下悄语曰：“速去！迟且败。”姜求启户再三，英如终不可。姜曰：“君岂画图耶？何颜如玉而心如石也？”英如祈之曰：“幸相爱，何忍陷我？乞从后户出，更思远策，今不能纳也。不听，我乃呼！”姜惧，乃遁还家，郁抑殊苦。

    Explanation

    491

    The next day, Jiang returned, and the girl was already there. The building was tall and the rooms were separated, so their conversation could not be heard. Jiang threw a white handkerchief wrapped around a golden ring onto the upper floor, and the girl responded with a bracelet. She then closed the window and entered. The girl's name was Zhou Yingru, and she lived with her mother. Her father, a tea merchant, had gone far away. Jiang knew all about her background and waited until dusk when no one was around, lying in wait at the right side of her family's bedroom door. Afterwards, when birds had settled and people had quieted down, he secretly peered into Yingru's room. The door was already locked, and he saw Yingru sitting alone by the lamp, talking to the lamp. Jiang touched the window frame with the bracelet to attract Yingru's attention. Yingru was surprised and asked, "Who is it?" Jiang replied, "It's me, the bracelet is here." Yingru was greatly surprised again and hurried to the window, whispering, "Go quickly! If you stay, you will fail." Jiang repeatedly asked her to open the door, but she finally refused. Jiang said, "Are you a painting? Why do you have a face like jade but a heart like stone?" Yingru pleaded, "Please, if you love me, why would you betray me? Please leave through the back door, think of a better plan, but I cannot let you in now. If you don't listen, I will call for help!" Jiang was afraid and fled back home, feeling very depressed and distressed.

    492

    俄闻叩镮声，启门，则小髻弓鞋满身香露者，英如至也。谓姜曰：顷者拒君，良非得已，而思君弥迫，故转就君。”姜大喜过望，遂缔衾席之好。将曙，英如去，夜定则来，如是者数月。
    　　于是姜年几冠矣。其父亦服贾于外，其季父主家政焉。以姜尚未室，一夕论婚，择焉而未决。姜甚恐，欲白母而聘英如，乃先告英如，而与之策。英如意殊懈，请媒诸其家，乃反不欲。姜窃怪其故，又疑其晓夜独行，略无所阻，亦卒无觉者，非弱女子所能，必有异。旦俟英如去，尾而追　之。出门数武，已飘然失去，大诧而返。潜访诸其居，则英如固已死矣，始悟所接者，英如之魂也，为之悲痛。

    Explanation

    492

    Soon after, Jiang heard the sound of someone knocking on the door ring. He opened the door to see a woman with a small bun hairstyle, wearing bow-shaped shoes, and carrying a fragrant dew-like scent. It was Yingru. She said to Jiang, "Recently, I refused you, but truly it was not out of choice. My longing for you has grown increasingly urgent, so I have decided to turn to you." Jiang was extremely happy beyond expectations and they established a close relationship. Before dawn, Yingru would leave, and she would come back late at night, and this pattern continued for several months.

    By then, Jiang was nearly an adult. His father was also engaged in trade outside, and his younger brother managed the family affairs. Since Jiang was not yet married, one night they discussed marriage, choosing but not yet deciding. Jiang was very afraid and wanted to tell his mother and arrange the marriage with Yingru. He first told Yingru and planned with her. However, Yingru was very unenthusiastic and asked a matchmaker to her family, but then she did not want to go. Jiang secretly wondered about the reason and also suspected that she walked alone day and night, with no obstacle, and finally, no one noticed. It was not something an ordinary woman could do, so there must be something strange. In the morning, he waited for Yingru to leave and followed her. After going out of the door for several steps, she had already flown away and disappeared. He was very surprised and returned. He secretly visited her residence and found that Yingru had already died. He began to realize that the one he met was Yingru's soul, and he grieved for her.

    493

    是夕英如至，笑曰：“君谓我死耶，姑勿畏！吾导君往见一人，足祛疑抱，而慰君怀感之情。”使姜凭其肩，携之以行，若飞燕惊鸿之迅，欻至一城中。巷市曲折，殿阁相比。及大第之门，双兽啮环，寂然虚掩。排而进之，兰棼桂栋，暖若仙居。内有镜堂焉，四壁皆镜，冷光逼射，眉发皆寒。西南隅悬一响板，英如弹以指，泠然一声，便有数女子连翩而出。影入镜中，花红玉白，迷离远近。中有一女，宛如英如。却顾英如，俨然在侧也。而再视诸女，忽复不见。方欲致洁，英如曰：“此上清琼馆，不可延伫。即曳其裾，引之径出。

    Explanation

    493

    That night, Yingru arrived, smiling and saying, "Do you think I am dead? Do not be afraid! I will guide you to meet someone who can dispel your doubts and comfort your feelings." He made Jiang lean on his shoulder and carried him along, moving swiftly like a swallow or a startled swan. Suddenly, they arrived in a city. The streets and markets were winding, and the halls and pavilions were closely built. When they reached the great mansion's gate, with double beasts biting the ring, it was quietly half-open. They pushed it open and entered, where the halls had fragrant rafters and cinnabar beams, warm like a celestial abode. Inside, there was a hall of mirrors, with all four walls covered in mirrors, the cold light glaring, making eyebrows and hair seem cold. In the southwest corner hung a resonant board, and Yingru played it with his fingers, producing a clear sound. Then several women emerged gracefully. Their shadows entered the mirrors, red flowers and white jade, indistinct at a distance. Among them was a woman who looked just like Yingru. Looking back at Yingru, he was indeed beside her. But when he looked at the other women again, they suddenly disappeared. Just as he was about to ask, Yingru said, "This is the Pure Jade Pavilion of the Upper Clarity, where one cannot linger." He immediately pulled her skirt and led her out directly.

    494

    旋至家，乃谓曰：“英如与君，空有解佩之缘，合当数面，尽于此矣！吾非英如，狐女也。实有夙分，虑君之情将专一于英如，故仿佛其容，见于左右。疑窦既启，良缘斯尽，今亦诀矣！”姜不及挽留，已霞举而逝。始悟所接者，并非英如之魂也。卒婿于他姓。
    　　庐　山　怪
    　　奉新宋荪侣外史，尝以壬子七月之望，宿庐山绝顶僧寺中。夜半矣，明月满天。徐闻风飒飒有声，落于高树之杪，中有歌者、语者、笑且骂者。讶而窥之，见数武之外，地势平坦，众影纷然，略如人间演剧状。藉草为茵席，因树为屏幛。金鼓丝竹之声，作于树上，节奏殊妙。衣服冠带须鬟械仗之属，亦率类梨园。念空山静夜，焉得有优伶若此？心知其怪，姑伺之。装演十余莃，莫知其色目；呕哑歌唱，亦不知其何曲也。

    Explanation

    494

    Upon returning home, he said to her, "Yingru and I, though we had a deep, predestined connection, were meant to meet only a few times, and that is all. I am not Yingru; I am a fox spirit. I truly had a fateful connection with you, but I feared that your feelings would be solely focused on Yingru. That is why I appeared in her likeness, appearing before you. Now that doubts have arisen, our good fortune has ended, and I must bid you farewell!" Jiang was unable to hold her back as she ascended gracefully into the sky and departed. Only then did he realize that what he had been interacting with was not Yingru's spirit. He eventually married another.

    Mount Lu's Strange Occurrence

    On the night of the 15th day of the seventh month in the year of the Water Rat, Song Sunlv, a historian from Fengxin, stayed at a temple at the summit of Mount Lu. By midnight, the moon was full in the sky. Gradually, he heard a rustling sound in the wind, which seemed to originate from the treetops. There were sounds of singing, talking, laughing, and even cursing. Surprised, he peered out and saw, several paces away, a flat area where many shadows moved in a chaotic, almost theatrical manner. They had used grass as a mat and trees as screens. The sounds of drums, gongs, strings, and bamboo flutes came from the treetops, with rhythms that were particularly exquisite. The performers were dressed in costumes and accessories that resembled those of actors. He wondered how such performers could exist in the quiet of the mountain at night. Realizing that this was a strange occurrence, he decided to observe it. There were more than ten acts, but he could not understand their nature. The singing was loud and discordant, and he could not tell what kind of music it was.

    495

    已而数人相和，歌声甚朗。歌曰：“吸日精，蚀月华，诸君妄意凌烟霞。烟霞堕地失颜色，但见玉水生桃花。桃花一万片，飞入陈王家。仙人化作尘与沙，秋风吹雨打闲衙。南楼美人嗟复嗟！湖中不见东来楂，空山夜半啼栖鸦。”随其声而记之。俄有金光从空下，乃一头陀，状甚怪，大声叱曰：“何物邪魅，敢尔喧扰，法当死！”卓锡一声，则众形尽变，其演技者皆兽也，而其司器者鸟也；转瞬之间，欻然俱灭。
    　　荪侣以癸居三月卒于京师，卒之前数日缕述于余。不知其果然否也。

    Explanation

    495

    Soon after, several people began to sing in harmony, their voices very loud. They sang:
    "Absorb the essence of the sun, devour the beauty of the moon. You foolish ones, think you can ascend to the clouds and mist. The clouds and mist fall to the ground, losing their color. Only the clear, beautiful water remains, with peach blossoms blooming. A hundred thousand peach blossoms, flying into Chen Wang's house. The immortals turn to dust and sand. Autumn wind and rain beat the idle hall. The beautiful woman in the south tower laments! There is no peach tree coming from the east, only the night crows cry in the empty mountains."

    Following the sound, I recorded it. Soon, golden light descended from the sky, and a monk appeared. He had an extremely strange appearance and shouted loudly: "What kind of evil spirit dares to disturb me so boldly? You shall die by my hand!" With a single gesture, all the forms changed. Those who performed became beasts, and those who managed the instruments became birds. In the blink of an eye, they all disappeared.

    My friend Su Lun, in the month of Gui, died in the capital city. A few days before his death, he narrated in detail to me. I wonder if this is indeed true.

    496

    戴公
    　　有戴公者，少任侠。其邻人贷豪者金，无以偿，豪者迫夺其女。戴怒，杀豪者，亡走五岭间。
    　　晚坐枫林，遥见少年从数骑来。丰仪轩迈。见戴即下马楫曰：“先生幸过仆，仆请执鞭！”戴愕然曰：“何敢！”少年曰：“先生幸过仆，仆将有丐于先生！”戴问：“所欲云何？”少年前跪曰：“先生不过仆，仆死不敢言。”戴怒曰：“言则言耳，何卑屈乃尔？余不耐此姝姝者！”少年叩头流涕曰：“老父与波利君不协，数战于赤谷之野，为飞戈所中，伤其左臂。药穷矣，唯得生人肝一寸可以疗之。求之数万人，无肯与者。苟不肯与，强取无益也。闻先生之义，忘身急入，敢以请！”戴笑曰：“此孝思也，吾岂惜之？”即引佩刀自剖腹，截肝授以少年，热血淋漓，殷及于履。少年叹曰：“真天下义士！”随出药傅创，创立复，乃殊无所苦。少年持肝顿首谢，即驰马而去。戴颇异之。

    Explanation

    496

    A Man Named Dai
    　　There was a man named Dai, who was young and a patron of the poor and a defender of the weak. One of his neighbors borrowed money from a wealthy man but could not repay it, so the wealthy man forcibly took the neighbor's daughter. Dai was furious and killed the wealthy man, then fled to the mountainous region between the Yangtze and Pearl Rivers.
    　　One evening, he sat in a maple forest and saw a young man riding with several attendants approaching from afar. The youth had a noble and dignified appearance. Upon seeing Dai, he dismounted and said, "Sir, please visit me; I will be your servant!" Dai was surprised and said, "How dare you!" The youth replied, "Sir, please visit me; I have a request for you!" Dai asked, "What is your request?" The youth knelt forward and said, "Sir, if you do not come to me, I dare not speak." Dai became angry and said, "Just say what you have to say! Why are you so humble? I cannot stand this!" The youth bowed his head and wept, saying, "My father has been at odds with Lord Boli, and they have fought several times in the fields of Chigutu, where my father was hit by a spear and injured his left arm. All available remedies have been exhausted, and only a piece of human liver can cure him. I have sought help from tens of thousands of people, but none are willing to give it. If you are unwilling to give it, taking it by force would be futile. I have heard of your sense of justice and your willingness to rush in without regard for your own safety. I dare to ask you!" Dai smiled and said, "This is a filial thought; how could I be reluctant to help?" He immediately drew his dagger, cut open his abdomen, and gave a piece of his liver to the youth. Blood flowed profusely, staining his shoes. The youth exclaimed, "Truly a righteous man of the world!" He then applied medicine to the wound, and the injury healed quickly, with no pain at all. The youth held the liver, bowed deeply in gratitude, and rode away. Dai found it very strange.

    497

    时豪者子诉之官，捕戴不可得，则执邻人而鞫之，务言戴所在，拷掠甚惨苦。戴闻之，叹曰：“我实杀人，复累人。何生为？”遂归自讼，赴狱中，脱其邻人。案乃定，刑有日矣。有叟来视之曰：“余，昔少年之父也。披肝之惠，夙夜弗忘，故来免义士于难。”因出大竹一节，解其系而系竹焉。桎梏钮镣之具，顿之如拉朽。叟携戴出狱，监守之吏见而弗问，门壁城垣亦无所障阻。径从叟步出郭外，繁星罗天，陇坂微白。

    Explanation

    497

    At that time, the son of a wealthy individual lodged a complaint with the authorities, but they were unable to capture Dai. Instead, they arrested and interrogated a neighbor, insisting on knowing where Dai was, and subjected him to extremely cruel and painful torture. Upon hearing this, Dai sighed and said, "I indeed killed someone, and have involved others. What is the point of living?" He then returned to confess himself, went to prison, and released his falsely accused neighbor. The case was then settled, and the execution date was set. An old man came to visit him and said, "I am the father of the young man from the past. The favor of laying bare one's heart, I have never forgotten day or night, so I came to save the righteous man from trouble." He then produced a large section of bamboo, released his restraints, and tied them to the bamboo. The restraints and shackles fell apart like rotten wood. The old man took Dai out of prison, and the prison guards saw but did not question, nor did the walls and gates offer any obstruction. They walked straight out of the city with the old man, and the stars were spread across the sky, the mountains faintly white.

    498

    行不百步，入一山，林木蔚密，不复辨途径。初闻履下落叶瑟瑟作声响，已觉两足无所着，有类蹑虚。比晓，进止一石屋，虚明洞达，烟雾满宫。出而旷览，则飞鸟在下，碧落可探，身在层峰之顶矣。远见云中一拳倒影入海。叟曰：“天台也，馀无所睹焉。”叟引戴遍历山径，花草禽鸟，多非世有。屋前一大树，垂夹癭癭，其实如豆，乃仰以为食。经数日，叟谓戴曰：“此地孤高，不可不至，亦不可久处。吾旧有田庐在牛女之墟，今欲与义士偕往。”戴从之。

    Explanation

    498

    After walking not more than a hundred steps, they entered a mountain, where the dense and lush forest made it impossible to distinguish paths. At first, they heard the rustling sound of leaves underfoot, and soon felt as if their feet had nowhere to stand, as if they were stepping on emptiness. By dawn, they reached a stone house, bright and clear inside, filled with smoke and mist. Stepping out to look around, they saw birds flying below, the blue sky within reach, and themselves atop a layer of peaks. Far away, they saw a small object's reflection in the clouds entering the sea. The old man said, "This is Mount Tiantai; I see nothing else." He led Dai to traverse the mountain paths, where the flowers, grass, birds, and animals were mostly not found in the world. In front of the house stood a large tree with hanging tumors, its fruits like beans, which they ate as food. After several days, the old man told Dai, "This place is isolated and high, and one cannot fail to visit it, but cannot stay for long. I once had fields and a house in the ruins of the Ox and Weaver Girl, and now I want to go with a righteous person." Dai followed him.

    499

    盘行曲折而下，始达于人境。道路跋涉，无异寻常，非复向者所飘忽。既至，则村郭室屋饮食服用，亦悉如众人，亦有厮役供指使，邻里亲旧过从问讯者。其地乃汀水之南，漳水之西也。
    　　其明日，有白雁双翔集于庭阶，羊豕鸡鱼之属，皆自行而至。叟太张供具，银烛金尊，辉映帘幕，始笑谓戴曰：“吾有故人居石镜山下。闻其女端好福相，甚宜室家，知义士尚鲜妃匹，已为君媒定。今乃吉期，行至矣。宜易冠服，整备作新郎。”戴惊喜称谢。俄而丝竹贯耳，仪从甚盛，香车及门外。戴俟于堂著，赞拜如礼。导入青庐，则钗光钏响，袖香扇影，迷离于脂奁镜台之间。戴虽伟丈夫，铁石心肠，至此神骨俱靡也。于是贺客履相错，宴乐者累日。

    Explanation

    499

    The path wound its way down, finally reaching the inhabited world. The journey was arduous, no different from the usual, no longer the same as before when it was fleeting and uncertain. Upon arrival, the village, houses, food, clothing, and daily necessities were all like those of ordinary people. There were also servants to serve at his command, and neighbors and old friends came to visit and greet him. The place was south of the Ting River and west of the Zhang River.

    The next day, white geese flew and landed on the steps, and sheep, pigs, chickens, and fish, all came by themselves. The old man prepared a grand feast, with silver candles and golden cups shining through the curtains. He then smiled and said to Dai, "I have an old friend living at the foot of Stone Mirror Mountain. I heard that her daughter is beautiful and has a good fortune, very suitable for a household. Knowing that a virtuous man still lacks a match, I have already arranged for you to be a match. Today is the auspicious day, and it has arrived. You should change into formal attire and prepare to be the groom." Dai was surprised and grateful. Soon, music filled the air, and the entourage was very grand. The fragrant carriage arrived at the gate. Dai waited in the hall, bowing and greeting according to the proper etiquette. They led her into the green tent, where the brilliance of her hairpins and bracelets, the scent of her sleeves, and the shadow of her fan, were all amidst the mirrors and dressing tables. Dai, though a strong man with a heart of stone, was now completely captivated. Then guests began to arrive, their footsteps intermingling, and the feast and entertainment lasted for several days.

    500

    然独不见少年。戴疑之，以问叟，叟曰：“偶出勾当，逾月即返耳。”戴信之，而终以越狱远窜，心不自安。隐隐侦其消息。乃闻人言：“戴固已伏法，未闻其逃也。”大讶其故，以问叟，叟笑曰：“亦无他，前所系大竹，即吾儿子代公抵罪矣。”戴骇绝号恸，慷慨曰：“某罪本不赦，又祸郎君，奈保复偷活？”遂取刀自刎。叟夺其刀作色曰：“义士何独为君子？义士能剖腹，儿子不能断头耶？况彼尚可生，义士反趋于死，计亦左甚矣！”戴乃止，而诘其由。叟曰：“新妇当知之。”戴退问妇，妇出一碧玉如意授戴曰：“君去西北七百步，有巨石如盘。以如意击之，石当开。中有紫笋长尺许，即袖归以献翁，无失。”

    Explanation

    500

    Yet the young man was not seen. Dai doubted it and asked the old man, who replied, "I occasionally go out to handle affairs and return after a month." Dai believed him, but ultimately he escaped prison and fled far away, his heart not at ease. He secretly investigated the news and then heard people say, "Dai had indeed been executed; I have not heard that he escaped." He was greatly surprised by the reason and asked the old man again. The old man smiled and said, "There is nothing else. The large bamboo that was tied before was my son who took your place to bear the punishment." Dai was shocked to the point of screaming and crying, and said with great emotion, "My crime should not have been pardoned, and I brought disaster to your son. How can I continue to live in secret?" He then took a knife and tried to commit suicide. The old man took the knife from him and said angrily, "Why should a righteous person act only as a gentleman? A righteous person can cut open their stomach, but can my son not cut off his head? Moreover, he can still live. The righteous person is instead moving towards death. This plan is also very wrong!" Dai then stopped and asked the reason. The old man said, "The new wife should know." Dai went back and asked the wife, who took out a jade如意 and gave it to Dai, saying, "You go seven hundred steps northwest. There is a huge stone like a plate. Use the如意 to strike it, and the stone will open. Inside there is a purple bamboo shoot about a foot long. Put it in your sleeve and bring it back to offer to the old man. Do not lose it."

    501

    如言，果得之。叟植笋庭中，须臾解箨成巨竹。竹忽裂，一人自竹中走出，乃前少年也。相见各大笑。谓戴曰：“为君故，历此一劫，大事毕矣！”又曰：“吾属皆神仙中人，以豪气未除，欲物色人间奇士，登之宝箓。君侠骨非常。是以在此。今姑以此宅让君，与贤偶暂住人间。异时解脱，会当长晤。吾从老父先去矣！”遂与叟俱逝。

    Explanation

    501

    As promised, here is the final, polished translation of the source text:

    As promised, he indeed obtained it. The old man planted bamboo shoots in the courtyard, and in a moment, they shed their sheaths and grew into giant bamboo. Suddenly, the bamboo split open, and a man walked out from within it, who turned out to be the previous young man. They met and laughed heartily. The old man said to Dai, "For your sake, I have endured this calamity, and the great matter is complete!" He then said, "We are all among the immortals, but our heroic spirit remains. We wish to seek out extraordinary individuals in the world and enroll them in the immortal register. You have an extraordinary chivalrous spirit, which is why you are here. Now, I will allow you to stay temporarily in this house with a virtuous partner in the human world. When we are freed from our current state, we will meet again in the future. I will go with my father first!" And so, he departed with the old man.

    502

    戴六十余，无疾而卒。葬之日，其棺轻焉。妇齿亦界五六旬，少好如昔。戴卒之翼日，忽失所在。戴以避仇匿处，本姓不著，戴其变姓也。
    　　心疾
    　　魏某观猎于南山。有鹿跃而过其身，魏惊倒，众救而归，心犹悸，忐忑不已。夜半，觉胸间豁然若剖，百体若解散，有物自身中飞出，少焉乃止。于是见其家人，皆绝不相识。生平所事，无复记忆者。视其状，若迷若忘。与人言，语谬乱而不可以理。众咸以为祟，守而治之，累日了无效。一夜，有言于室者无见也，众惧而相语曰：“鬼。”则答曰：“否也。”又曰：“妖。”亦曰：“否。”魏忽觉胸如物触，间胸中语曰：“我非我，即子也。子非子，即我也。”遂寂然。魏瞿然扪心，忪忪者久之，病乃复。

    Explanation

    502

    Dai, in his sixties, died without illness. On the day of his burial, his coffin was unusually light. His wife, also in her fifties or sixties, remained as beautiful as before. The day after Dai's death, she suddenly disappeared. Dai had been hiding from enemies, and his true name was not recorded; "Dai" was his assumed name.

    Heart Disease

    Wei, a man, was hunting in the South Mountain. A deer leaped over him, startling him so much that he fell down. The crowd helped him back to his home, but his heart remained agitated, and he continued to feel anxious. At midnight, he felt as if his chest had suddenly split open, and all his limbs felt as if they were disintegrating. Something seemed to emerge from within him, and after a short while, it stopped. When he saw his family, he found them completely unfamiliar. He could not remember anything from his past life. His appearance suggested he was in a daze, and he spoke incoherently and illogically. Everyone believed it was a spirit or ghost, so they guarded and treated him, but after many days, there was no improvement. One night, someone spoke in the room, but no one could see them. The crowd, fearing a ghost, whispered to each other, "It's a ghost." The voice replied, "No." Then they said, "It's a demon." The voice again replied, "No." Wei suddenly felt something touch his chest, and a voice inside him said, "I am not me, but you. You are not you, but me." Then everything became silent. Wei, startled, touched his heart, and remained anxious for a long time. Then his illness returned.

    503

    非非子曰：宋阳里华子病忘，朝取而夕忘，　夕与而朝忘。在途忘行，在室忘坐，亦失其心也。赖鲁儒生治之，七日而瘳，今乃自复焉，幸矣。
    　　痴　女　子
    　　昔有读汤临川《牡丹亭》死者。近闻一痴女子，以读《红楼梦》而死。
    　　初，女子从其兄案头。搜得《红楼梦》，废寝食读之。读至佳处，往往辍卷冥想，继之以泪。复自前读之。反复数十百遍，卒未尝终卷，乃病矣。父母觉之，急取书付火。女子乃呼曰：“奈何焚宝玉、黛玉！”自是笑啼失常，言语无伦次，梦寐之间，未尝不呼宝玉也。延巫医杂治，百弗效。一夕，瞪视床头灯，连语曰：“宝玉宝玉，在此耶！”遂饮泣而瞑。

    Explanation

    503

    Non-Nonzi said: In Yangli, Song, there was a man named Hua Zi who suffered from forgetfulness. He took something in the morning and forgot it by evening, gave something in the evening and forgot it by morning. On the road, he forgot to walk, and in the room, he forgot to sit. He also lost his mind. Thanks to a Confucian scholar from the state of Lu, he was cured after seven days. Now, he has recovered on his own, which is fortunate.

    A Simple-Minded Woman

    Once, there was a woman who died after reading Tang Xianzu's The Peony Pavilion. Recently, a simple-minded woman died after reading Dream of the Red Chamber.

    At first, the woman found Dream of the Red Chamber on her brother's desk. She read it day and night, neglecting sleep and meals. When she came to the good parts, she often put down the book and meditated deeply, followed by tears. She would then read it again from the beginning. She read it dozens or even hundreds of times, but never finished the entire book. She then fell ill. Her parents noticed and quickly burned the book. The woman cried out, "Why are you burning Bao Yu and Dai Yu!" From then on, she laughed and cried uncontrollably, speaking incoherently. In her dreams and waking hours, she never stopped calling out for Bao Yu. They called in various doctors and sorcerers, but nothing worked. One night, she stared at the lamp on her bedside table and kept saying, "Bao Yu, Bao Yu, are you here?" She then wept and fell asleep.

    504

    侠君曰：《红楼梦》，悟书也？非也，而实情书。其悟也，乃情之穷极而无所复之，至于死而犹不可已。无可奈何，而姑托于悟，而愈见其情之真而至。故其言情，乃妙绝今古。彼其所言之情之人，宝玉黛玉而已，馀不得与焉。两人者情之实也，而他人皆情之虚。两人者情之正也，而他人皆情之变。故两人为情之主，而他人皆为情之宾。盖两人之情，未尝不系乎男女夫妇房帷床笫之间，而绝不关乎男女夫妇房帷床笫之事，何也？譬诸明月有光有魄，月固不能离魄而生其光也。譬诸花有香色、有根蒂，花固不能离根蒂，而成其香色之妙且丽也。然花月之所以为花月者，乃惟其光也，惟其香色也，而初不在其魄与根蒂。至于凡天下至痴至慧，爱月爱花之人之心，则并月之光、花之香色而忘之，此所谓情也。

    Explanation

    504

    侠君曰：《红楼梦》，悟书也？非也，而实情书。其悟也，乃情之穷极而无所复之，至于死而犹不可已。无可奈何，而姑托于悟，而愈见其情之真而至。故其言情，乃妙绝今古。彼其所言之情之人，宝玉黛玉而已，馀不得与焉。两人者情之实也，而他人皆情之虚。两人者情之正也，而他人皆情之变。故两人为情之主，而他人皆为情之宾。盖两人之情，未尝不系乎男女夫妇房帷床笫之间，而绝不关乎男女夫妇房帷床笫之事，何也？譬诸明月有光有魄，月固不能离魄而生其光也。譬诸花有香色、有根蒂，花固不能离根蒂，而成其香色之妙且丽也。然花月之所以为花月者，乃惟其光也，惟其香色也，而初不在其魄与根蒂。至于凡天下至痴至慧，爱月爱花之人之心，则并月之光、花之香色而忘之，此所谓情也。

    侠君说：《红楼梦》，是悟书吗？不是的，而是情书。它的悟，是情达到极致而无法再表达，甚至到了死亡也无法停止。无可奈何，只能暂时寄托于悟，反而更加显现出情的真实和彻底。因此，它谈论情，是古今绝妙的。它所谈论的情，只有宝玉和黛玉，其他人无法参与。两人是情的真实，而其他人都是情的虚幻。两人是情的正道，而其他人都是情的变化。因此，两人是情的主体，而其他人都是情的客体。两人的感情，从未脱离男女夫妇的床第之间，但绝不涉及男女夫妇的床第之事，为什么呢？比如明月有光有影，月亮不能离开影而产生光。比如花有香有色、有根有茎，花不能离开根茎，才能形成其香色的美妙和美丽。然而，花月之所以成为花月，是因为它们的光，它们的香色，而不是因为它们的影和根茎。至于天下那些极其痴情和极其智慧，爱月爱花的人的心，则连月的光、花的香色都忘记了，这就是所谓的情。

    505

    夫世之男女夫妇莫不言情，而或不能言情之所以为情。盖其所谓情，男女夫妇房帷床第而已矣。今试立男女于此，男之悦女，徒以其女也悦之；女之悦男，亦徒以其男也而悦之。则苟别易一男女，而与其所悦者品相若。吾知其情之移矣。情也，而可以移乎？又苟别易一男女，而更出其所悦者之品之上，吾知其情之夺矣。情也，而可以夺乎？又使男女之相悦，终不遂其媾，则亦抱恨守缺，因循荀且于其后，而情于是乎穷矣。情也，而可以穷乎？即使男女之相悦，竟得如其愿，则亦安常处顺，以老以没，而情于是乎止矣。情也，而强可止乎？

    505

    In this world, no married couple does not speak of love, yet some cannot explain why love is love. What they call love is nothing more than the love between husband and wife in the bedroom. Let us imagine two men and two women standing here; the man likes the woman simply because she is a woman, and the woman likes the man simply because he is a man. If another man and woman are substituted, and if the qualities of the new pair are similar, I know that their love will shift. Love, can it be shifted? If another man and woman are substituted, and if the new pair is even better than the original, I know that their love will be taken away. Love, can it be taken away? If the man and woman do not consummate their union, they will hold onto regret and remain incomplete, continuing in a perfunctory and temporary manner afterward, and love will then come to an end. Love, can it come to an end? If the man and woman finally achieve their wish, they will live in peace and harmony, growing old and passing away, and love will then stop. Love, can it be forced to stop?

    506

    故情之所以为情，移之不可，夺之不可，离之不可，舍之犹不可。未见其人，固思其人。既见其人，仍思其人。不知斯人之外更有何人，亦并不知斯之即是新人，乃至身之所当、心之所触、时之所值、境之所呈，一春一秋，一朝一暮，一山一水，一亭一池，一花一草，一虫一鸟，皆有凄然欲绝，悄然难言，如病如狂，如醉如梦，欲生不得，欲死不能之境，莫不由斯人而生，而要反不知为斯人而起也。

    Explanation

    506

    The reason why emotions are emotions is that they cannot be moved, taken away, separated, or abandoned. Before seeing the person, one thinks of them. After seeing the person, one still thinks of them. One is unaware of anyone else beyond this person, nor does one know that this person is a new one. Even the physical sensations, mental touches, temporal values, and environmental presentations, such as the changing seasons, the passage of days and nights, the mountains and waters, the pavilions and ponds, the flowers and grasses, the insects and birds, all evoke feelings of deep sorrow, silent words that are hard to express, like illness, madness, intoxication, and dreams, creating a state where one cannot live or die. None of these emotional states arise from this person, yet one remains unaware that they are caused by this person.

    507

    虽至山崩海涸，金销石烂，曾不足减其毫末，而间其须臾，必且至憾于天地，归咎于阴阳；何故生彼？并何故生我？以至形朽骨枯，神泯气化，而情不与之俱尽。是故情之所结，一成而不变，百折而不回，历千万劫而不灭。无惬心之日，无释念之期。由穷而变，变而通，通而久，至有填海崩城，化火为石，一切神奇怪幻，出于寻常思虑之外者，斯即有灵心妙舌、千笔万墨，而皆不能写其难言之故之万一：此所谓情也！夫情者，大抵有所为而实无所为者也；无所不可，而终无所可者也；无所不至，而终无所至者也。两人之情，如是而已。不然者，男女夫妇，天下皆是也；房帷床笫之事，天下皆然也。奚必两人哉？知此乃可以言情，言情至此，乃真可以悟。

    Explanation

    507

    Even if mountains crumble and seas dry up, gold melts and stones rot, it would not be enough to diminish its tiniest bit or even for a moment; it will inevitably cause regret to heaven and earth, and blame to be placed on the yin and yang. Why was he born? And why was I born? Even as the body rots and bones wither, the spirit fades and breath transforms, the emotion does not perish along with it. Therefore, the emotion that is formed, once formed it does not change, a hundred times it does not turn back, it does not perish through countless kalpas. There is no day of contentment, no time to release the thought. From poverty to change, from change to understanding, from understanding to lasting, to the point of filling the sea, collapsing cities, turning fire into stone, all kinds of miraculous and strange phenomena, things that go beyond ordinary thoughts, even with a divine heart and tongue, a thousand pens and ten thousand brushes, they cannot write even a tiny fraction of its indescribable cause: this is what is called emotion! Emotion, generally speaking, has a purpose but in reality has no purpose; it can do anything but ultimately does nothing; it can go anywhere but ultimately goes nowhere. The emotion between two people is like this. Otherwise, men and women, husbands and wives, are all like this in the world; the affairs of the bedchamber are all the same in the world. Why must it be two people? Knowing this is how one can speak of emotion, speaking of emotion to this extent, one can truly understand.

    508

    或曰：“《红楼梦》，幻书也，宝玉，子虚也，非真有也。女子乃为之而死，其痴之甚矣！”嗟乎！天下谁非子虚？谁为真有哉？痴者死矣，不痴者其长存乎？况女子之死，为情也，非为宝玉也！且情之所结，无真不幻，亦无幻不真，安知书中之宝玉，梦中之宝玉，不真成眼中之宝玉耶？则虽谓女子真为宝玉死，可也。

    Explanation

    508

    Someone says: "Dream of the Red Chamber is a book of illusion, Bǎoyù is a Zǐxū, not truly real. Women die because of it, how foolish it is!" Alas! Who in the world is not like Zǐxū? Who is truly real? The foolish ones have died, will the non-foolish ones continue to exist? Especially the death of women, which is for love, not for Bǎoyù! Moreover, where emotions are involved, there is no distinction between the real and the illusory, there is no illusion that is not real. How can we know that the Bǎoyù in the book, the Bǎoyù in the dream, is not truly the Bǎoyù in our eyes? Then it is acceptable to say that women truly died for Bǎoyù.

    509

    恶鼠
    　　某恶鼠破家，求良猫，餍以腥膏，眠以毡罽。猫既饱且安，率不捕鼠，甚者与鼠游戏。鼠以故益暴。某怒，遂不复蓄猫，以为天下无良猫也。因设机，鼠弗蹈；饵以毒，弗食。某怒鼠，殆无虚日，然无如何也。他日失火，焚廪及寝矣，某趋出门外，大笑不止。邻人为扑灭，某大恚曰：“鼠辈方歼于一炬，诸君救之，何也？”
    　　侠君曰：余甲辰家居，屡厄于社君。室中木器殆无完者。暴斗之声，夜作于楼上；虽熟寝，每为惊觉，余固弗较也。其后理架上书册，鼠迹纵横，于是亦有恶焉，乃檄猫捕之。而家有一猫，性不嗜鼠，迥与常猫异，捕不捕，未可知也。口诛笔伐，聊快余志，虽一时戏作，追录于此。良足助此公张目。某檄曰：

    Explanation

    509

    Aversion to Mice

    Once, a man's aversion to mice caused great damage to his household. He sought a good cat, feeding it fatty meat and letting it sleep on a felt rug. The cat, well-fed and comfortable, usually did not catch mice, and even went so far as to play with them. As a result, the mice became even more rampant. Angered by this, the man stopped keeping a cat and believed that there were no good cats in the world. He then set traps, but the mice did not step on them. He also baited them with poison, but the mice did not eat it. The man was almost always angry at the mice but had no way to deal with them. One day, a fire broke out, burning the granary and sleeping quarters. The man rushed out of the house, laughing uncontrollably. The neighbors put out the fire, and the man was greatly enraged, saying, "The mice are just being destroyed by the fire. Why are you saving them?"

    The侠君 said: In the year 甲辰, while at home, I often suffered from disturbances by the社君. Almost all the wooden furniture in the room was broken. Noises of fighting, made at night on the upper floor, often woke me up even when I was deeply asleep. I did not care about it. Later, when organizing the books on the shelves, I found mouse tracks crisscrossing. I then issued a command to the cat to catch them. But there was a cat in the house that did not like mice, very different from ordinary cats. Whether it catches or not, it is unknown. I denounced and criticized, just to satisfy my own will, even though it was a temporary jest, recorded here later. It is indeed enough to help this gentleman open his eyes. The man issued a command saying:

    510

    噫嘻哉鼠也！金枷败类，火浣馀妖。肯艮象之光明，属子辰子阴暗。播须弄黠，满腹藏贪。侠五技以偷生，持两端而避患。异乎君子，不嫌径窦之羞；譬诸小人，共犹穿窬之盗。遂乃捕逃有薮，封植多方。恃凭社之难熏，谋处仓而逸获。户庭不出，儋石常储，何老饕之无厌，犹小窃之不已。穴居若墓，时砺穿墉之牙；粒食如山，不果饮河之腹。寻鱼盘盎，盗肉庖厨。入橐拊床，既惊宴坐；翻盆窥甏，更搅清眠。庭础楼棼，凭陵而暴斗；冠箱衣笥，灭裂而游行。斯已难容，吾犹不问。乃至闲床尘迹，波及连屋图签；高架云编，资为循墙阶级。丹黄剥蚀，余方苦亥豕之讹；缥碧耗残，尔更助蛃鱼之虐。虽百城徒拥，未免可羞；而三箧频忘，岂能无憾？

    Explanation

    510

    Alas, the rat! A disgrace to the gold-bound, a remnant of some fiery and supernatural entity. Though it possesses the brightness of a firm and upright image, it belongs to the dark and shadowy aspects of the zodiac. It plays with its whiskers and is cunning, full of greed. It uses its five senses to survive, holding to both sides to avoid danger. Unlike a gentleman, it is not ashamed of crawling through holes and crevices; like a small person, it is a thief who climbs walls and breaks into houses. It has a place to hide and escape, thriving in many places. Relying on the difficulty of being burned out of a temple, it plans to live in a granary and escape detection. It does not leave the house, always storing a small amount of food. How can the glutton's insatiable appetite be satisfied, and how can the thief's incessant thieving cease? It lives in a hole like a tomb, sharpening its teeth to gnaw through walls. Eating grains like a mountain, it cannot satisfy the belly by drinking from the river. It searches for fish in bowls and jars, stealing meat from the kitchen.
